topics.title: Overview of Metal Cutting Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Metal Cutting Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metal Cutting** is defined as a controlled material removal process in which a harder, geometrically defined cutting tool is used to shear off unwanted material from a workpiece in the form of chips, thereby producing a desired shape, size, and surface finish. It is a subset of the broader family of **manufacturing processes** and is classified under **subtractive manufacturing**, as opposed to additive (3D printing) or formative (forging, casting) processes.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCIET402 – Machine Tools and Digital Manufacturing)**, *Module 1* introduces metal cutting as the foundational pillar of conventional machining. The mechanics of chip formation, the geometry of single-point and multi-point tools, and the energy conversion that occurs at the tool–workpiece interface form the analytical backbone for all subsequent modules on turning, milling, drilling, and grinding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The module specifically targets the *classification of metal cutting processes*, *chip formation mechanics*, *cutting tool geometry (rake, clearance angles)*, and the *Merchant's circle of forces* — all of which are recurrent 14-mark ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wood Chisel & Wood Block"

Imagine a carpenter planing a rough wooden plank. The carpenter pushes a sharp, wedge-shaped chisel across the surface of the wood. As the wedge advances:

- The **wood ahead of the chisel** is compressed and lifted.
- A thin **slice (chip)** curls upward and away from the surface.
- The plank becomes **smoother, flatter, and dimensionally smaller** with every pass.

A metal cutting operation is mechanically identical to this scene, except:
- The "wood" is **mild steel, aluminum, or titanium** (workpiece).
- The "chisel" is a **cutting tool** made of HSS, carbide, ceramic, or CBN (superhard materials).
- The "hand pushing" is replaced by a **machine tool** that delivers controlled linear (mm/rev) and rotational (rpm) motion.
- The "chip" is not loose sawdust but a **continuous, segmented, or discontinuous chip** whose morphology tells the engineer whether the cut is stable, efficient, or destructive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Metal cutting is fundamentally a *mechanical process of localized plastic deformation* followed by *shear separation*. The chip is the visible by-product of this controlled plastic failure.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Metal Cutting Processes

A high-level taxonomy, as mandated in the KTU syllabus, is presented below.

| S. No | Process | Tool Type | Motion | Typical Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Turning | Single-point | Tool feed, Work rotation | Shafts, rollers, disks |
| 2 | Milling | Multi-point (rotary) | Tool rotation, table feed | Slots, pockets, contours |
| 3 | Drilling | Multi-point (twist drill) | Tool rotation + axial feed | Internal cylindrical holes |
| 4 | Boring | Single-point (boring bar) | Tool rotation/feed, work rotation | Enlarging existing holes |
| 5 | Shaping/Planing | Single-point | Linear reciprocating | Flat surfaces, keyways |
| 6 | Broaching | Multi-point (linear) | Linear pull/push | Internal profiles (splines) |
| 7 | Grinding | Multi-point (abrasive wheel) | Wheel rotation + work feed | Finishing, hard materials |
| 8 | Sawing | Multi-point (band/circular) | Linear/rotary cut-off | Parting, blanking |
| 9 | Hobbing | Multi-point (gear hob) | Rotational + tangential | External spur/helical gears |

## 1.4 Orthogonal vs. Oblique Cutting

- **Orthogonal Cutting:** The cutting edge is *straight* and *perpendicular* to the direction of cutting velocity. Only **two forces** (cutting force and thrust force) act on the tool. Used in shaping, planing, and as a *theoretical model* for all analyses.
- **Oblique Cutting:** The cutting edge is *inclined* to the cutting velocity vector. A **third force** (radial force) appears. This is the *real-world* situation in turning, drilling, and milling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this distinction matters:** All KTU derivations (Merchant's circle, shear angle equation) are framed in *orthogonal cutting* for mathematical simplicity. The results are then generalized to oblique cutting via angle-transformation identities.

## 1.5 The Three Primary Motions in Machining

1. **Primary Motion (Cutting Motion):** The dominant, highest-magnitude motion that actually removes material — usually the **rotation** of the workpiece (turning) or the tool (milling, drilling).
2. **Secondary Motion (Feed Motion):** A slower, incremental motion that advances the tool relative to the workpiece so that fresh material is exposed.
3. **Tertiary Motion (Depth of Cut):** The motion that sets the *thickness* of the layer being removed in a single pass.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Chip Formation — Stage-by-Stage

When a sharp tool is pushed into a ductile metal under controlled conditions, the material near the cutting edge undergoes the following sequence:

1. **Elastic Deformation Stage:** As the tool approaches the free surface, the metal is first compressed elastically (Hookean region).
2. **Plastic Deformation Stage:** Beyond the yield point, the metal flows plastically. Dislocations multiply; grains elongate along the shear direction.
3. **Shear Localization:** Plastic strain concentrates along a narrow band called the **primary shear zone** (thickness ≈ 0.02 – 0.05 mm).
4. **Chip Separation:** The material beyond the shear zone loses cohesion and flows up the tool's **rake face** as a chip.
5. **Secondary Shear Zone:** Friction between the flowing chip and the rake face generates additional heat in a thin boundary layer (≈ 0.005 mm) — this is the **hottest zone** in metal cutting.
6. **Built-Up Edge (BUE) Formation:** Under certain combinations of low cutting speed and high friction, work-material atoms weld to the tool tip, forming a temporary wedge. BUE is *unstable* — it grows, breaks off, and is re-formed cyclically, deteriorating surface finish.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Anchor:** A 14-mark KTU question on chip formation demands that the student label the **primary shear zone, secondary shear zone, BUE, and the tool–chip contact length**. Missing even one label typically costs 2–3 marks.

## 2.2 Tool Geometry — The Nomenclature You Must Memorize

For a **single-point cutting tool** (used in turning, shaping, planing):

- **Rake Angle (α):** Angle between the **rake face** and a reference plane (XY plane). Positive rake reduces cutting force; negative rake strengthens a weak tool tip.
- **Clearance Angle (γ):** Prevents the tool **flank** from rubbing against the machined surface. Typically 6°–15°.
- **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ) / Side Cutting Edge Angle:** Controls the direction of chip flow.
- **Nose Radius (r):** Small radius (0.4 – 1.6 mm) at the tool tip; larger nose radius improves finish but raises radial forces.
- **Approach Angle:** Angle between the cutting edge and the feed direction (typically 15°).
- **Tool Signature (ASA / ISO):** Standardized written code, e.g., `0° 5° 7° 6° 15° 0.5 mm` — denoting **back rake, side rake, end relief, side relief, end cutting edge angle, and nose radius**.

## 2.3 Types of Chips — A Classification with Engineering Implications

| Chip Type | Conditions | Appearance | Practical Implication |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Continuous Chip** | Ductile metal, sharp tool, high speed, low feed, adequate coolant | Long, ribbon-like, no segmentation | Best surface finish; ideal for finish turning |
| **Discontinuous Chip** | Brittle metal (cast iron, bronze) or insufficient cutting edge strength | Small, separate segments | Easy chip disposal; rougher surface |
| **Continuous with BUE** | Low cutting speed, high friction, sticky work material | Saw-tooth-like edge with welded lumps | Poor dimensional accuracy; avoid in finish cuts |
| **Serrated (Saw-tooth) Chip** | Low thermal conductivity, high speed, high feed | Alternate thick/thin segments | Indicates high tool-tip temperature |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this table are exam-portable.** Memorize the symbols, the units (SI), and the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Cutting Speed (Turning) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $D$ = work diameter, $N$ = spindle rpm | m/min |
| 2 | Feed Rate | $f_{r} = f \times N$ | $f$ = feed per rev | mm/min |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Q = V \times f \times d$ | $d$ = depth of cut | mm³/min |
| 4 | Chip Thickness Ratio | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ | $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness, $t_2$ = chip thickness | dimensionless |
| 5 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | $\phi$ = shear angle, $\alpha$ = rake angle | dimensionless |
| 6 | Merchant's Shear Angle Identity | $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ | $\beta$ = friction angle at tool–chip interface | degrees |
| 7 | Shear Stress | $\tau_s = \dfrac{F_s \sin \phi}{b \, t_1}$ | $b$ = width of cut, $F_s$ = shear force | N/mm² |
| 8 | Cutting Force (Orthogonal) | $F_c = F_s \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}$ | — | N |
| 9 | Thrust Force (Orthogonal) | $F_t = F_s \sin(\beta - \alpha) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}$ | — | N |
| 10 | Friction Angle | $\tan \beta = \mu$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at tool–chip | degrees |
| 11 | Specific Cutting Energy | $U_c = \dfrac{F_c}{f \times d}$ | Energy per unit volume | N·m/mm³ |
| 12 | Cutting Power | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60\,000}$ | — | kW |
| 13 | Taylor's Tool Life Equation | $V T^n = C$ | $T$ = tool life, $C$ = constant, $n$ = exponent | min, m/min |
| 14 | Extended Taylor Equation | $V T^n f^a d^b = K$ | Includes feed $f$ and depth $d$ exponents | — |

> **Critical Notation Note:** The `|` symbol for absolute value is *forbidden* inside the table (would break markdown). Use $\vert$ in LaTeX instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Taylor's Equation** is the cornerstone of **CNC tool management** — modern CAM software embeds Taylor's constants to compute optimal spindle speeds for a desired tool life.
- **Specific Cutting Energy ($U_c$)** allows machine-tool selection without empirical testing. A machine tool's available power is divided by $U_c \times \text{MRR}$ to confirm the cut is feasible.
- **Chip Thickness Ratio ($r$)** is used in **force modeling** for chatter prediction in milling and in **adaptive control** systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Chip Thickness Relationship

**Given:** A single-point tool with rake angle $\alpha$ advances through a workpiece. The uncut chip thickness is $t_1$, and the deformed chip thickness is $t_2$. The shear angle is $\phi$.

**Geometric Setup:** Consider the primary shear plane as a straight line inclined at angle $\phi$ to the cutting velocity vector.

**Step 1:** The length of the uncut chip along the shear plane is:

$$
l_s = \dfrac{t_1}{\sin \phi}
$$

**Step 2:** The same shear-plane length expressed in terms of the deformed chip:

$$
l_s = \dfrac{t_2}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions for $l_s$:

$$
\dfrac{t_1}{\sin \phi} = \dfrac{t_2}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiplying and rearranging to obtain the chip thickness ratio $r$:

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 5:** Expanding the denominator using the cosine difference identity:

$$
\cos(\phi - \alpha) = \cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form:

$$
\boxed{\,r = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha}\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** As $\phi$ increases (shear plane becomes more horizontal), $r$ approaches 1 — meaning chips are *thin* and cutting is *easy*. As $\phi$ decreases (more vertical shear), $r \ll 1$ — chips are *thick*, force is *high*, and tool life *drops*.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Shear Strain in Chip Formation

**Objective:** Express the shear strain $\gamma$ in the primary shear zone in terms of $\phi$ and $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry of shearing:** The chip slides along the shear plane. The displacement along the shear direction relative to the shear-plane thickness gives the strain.

**Step 2 — Shear-plane thickness:** The undeformed chip thickness $t_1$ projected onto the shear plane has thickness:

$$
\Delta s = \dfrac{t_1}{\sin \phi}
$$

**Step 3 — Shear displacement (chip flow along shear plane):** The chip velocity $V_c$ slides along the rake face. The component of $V_c$ along the shear direction is:

$$
\Delta s_{\text{slide}} = \dfrac{t_1}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \cdot \cos\alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering shear strain** (ratio of slide to thickness):

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta s_{\text{slide}}}{\Delta s} = \dfrac{t_1 \cos\alpha}{\sin\phi \cdot \dfrac{t_1}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}} = \dfrac{\cos\alpha \cos(\phi - \alpha)}{\sin\phi}
$$

**Step 5 — Use identity** $\cos(\phi - \alpha) = \cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha$:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{\cos\alpha (\cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha)}{\sin\phi} = \dfrac{\cos^2\alpha \cos\phi}{\sin\phi} + \cos\alpha \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize** $\dfrac{\cos^2\alpha}{\tan\phi} = \cot\phi \cos^2\alpha$ and combine:

$$
\gamma = \cos^2\alpha \cot\phi + \sin\alpha \cos\alpha
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the half-angle identity** $\sin 2\alpha = 2 \sin\alpha \cos\alpha$:

$$
\boxed{\,\gamma = \cot\phi \cos^2\alpha + \dfrac{1}{2} \sin 2\alpha = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)\,}
$$

> **Note:** The final, exam-friendly form $\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ is widely cited and requires no proof in KTU answers — only statement of the result with brief geometric reasoning.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Cutting Force Equation (Orthogonal Cutting)

**Foundation — Merchant's Force Circle (geometric model):**

The tool face experiences a **friction force** $F$ along the rake face and a **normal force** $N$ perpendicular to it. Their resultant $R$ makes an angle $\beta$ (friction angle) with the normal to the rake face.

**Step 1:** Friction and normal force components:

$$
F = \mu N, \quad \tan\beta = \mu, \quad R = \sqrt{F^2 + N^2} = \dfrac{N}{\cos\beta}
$$

**Step 2:** $R$ can be resolved into the **shear force** $F_s$ (along shear plane) and the **normal force** $N_s$ (perpendicular to shear plane). The angle between $R$ and $F_s$ is $( \phi + \beta - \alpha )$.

**Step 3:** Resolving $R$ in the shear direction:

$$
F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha) = \dfrac{N \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\cos\beta}
$$

**Step 4:** Resolving $R$ into the cutting and thrust directions using Merchant's circle:

$$
F_c = R \cos(\beta - \alpha), \qquad F_t = R \sin(\beta - \alpha)
$$

**Step 5:** Eliminate $R$ using the shear force relation. From Step 3, $R = F_s \cos\beta / \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$. Substituting:

$$
F_c = \dfrac{F_s \cos\beta \cos(\beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the standard Merchant identity** $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$, hence $\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90° - \phi$, and $\beta - \alpha = 90° - 2\phi - \phi = \cdots$ After trigonometric simplification:

$$
\boxed{\,F_c = \dfrac{F_s \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Similarly for the thrust force:**

$$
\boxed{\,F_t = \dfrac{F_s \sin(\beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Variables Interact

| If this parameter increases | Shear angle $\phi$ | Chip thickness $t_2$ | Cutting force $F_c$ | Surface finish |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Rake angle $\alpha$ ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | Improves |
| Friction angle $\beta$ ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | Degrades |
| Feed $f$ ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ (∝ f^n) | Degrades |
| Depth of cut $d$ ↑ | — | — | ↑ (∝ d) | Slight effect |
| Cutting speed $V$ ↑ (ductile) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | Improves |
| Work material hardness ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑↑ | Degrades |
| Tool wear ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | Degrades |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition:** Larger $\alpha$ → larger $\phi$ → smaller $t_2$ → smaller $F_c$ → longer tool life. This is the *golden rule* of tool geometry optimization.

## 3.5 Sample Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** In an orthogonal cutting operation, the uncut chip thickness is **0.25 mm**, the deformed chip thickness is **0.45 mm**, and the rake angle is **+10°**. Calculate (i) chip thickness ratio, (ii) shear angle, and (iii) shear strain.

**Solution (i):**

$$
r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.45} = 0.556
$$

**Solution (ii) — Use the formula from §3.1, Step 4:**

$$
r = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.556 = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - 10°)}
$$

Trial: $\phi = 25°$ gives $\sin 25° / \cos 15° = 0.4226 / 0.9659 = 0.437$ (too low).
Trial: $\phi = 30°$ gives $\sin 30° / \cos 20° = 0.500 / 0.9397 = 0.532$ (close).
Trial: $\phi = 31.5°$ gives $0.5225 / 0.9336 = 0.560$ (excellent match).

$$
\boxed{\phi \approx 31.5°}
$$

**Solution (iii) — Apply §3.2, Step 7:**

$$
\gamma = \cot(31.5°) + \tan(31.5° - 10°) = 1.632 + \tan(21.5°) = 1.632 + 0.394 = 2.03
$$

$$
\boxed{\gamma \approx 2.03}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Orthogonal Cutting Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following block diagram maps the *interactions* of the orthogonal cutting model. It avoids risky geometric coordinates and focuses on the relationships between force, energy, and chip morphology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Rotational Motion V m per min] --> B[Single Point Tool Engagement]
    C[Tool Geometry rake alpha clearance gamma] --> B
    D[Work Material Property ductile vs brittle] --> B
    E[Cutting Parameters f d V] --> B
    B --> F[Primary Shear Zone phi deg]
    B --> G[Secondary Shear Zone tool chip contact]
    B --> H[Built Up Edge BUE unstable]
    F --> I[Chip Formation continuous or discontinuous]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Chip Thickness t2 mm]
    J --> K[Chip Thickness Ratio r t1 over t2]
    K --> L[Shear Strain gamma]
    F --> L
    C --> L
    I --> M[Cutting Force Fc N]
    I --> N[Thrust Force Ft N]
    I --> O[Shear Force Fs N]
    M --> P[Specific Cutting Energy Uc Nmm per mm3]
    Q[MRR mm3 per min] --> P
    P --> R[Power Requirement Pc kW]
    M --> R
    A --> R
    R --> S[Machine Tool Selection and Spindle Drive Sizing]
    M --> T[Taylor Tool Life VTn equals C]
    E --> T
    T --> U[Optimum Cutting Speed for Production Target]
    H --> V[Surface Finish Deterioration]
    M --> V
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Plane XY] --> B[Rake Face]
    B --> C[Rake Angle alpha]
    A --> D[Flank Face]

    D --> F

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tool Rake Face] --> B[Friction Force F mu N]
    A --> C[Normal Force N]
    B --> D[Resultant R on tool]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Resolve along shear plane]
    D --> F[Resolve along cutting direction]
    E --> G[Shear Force Fs N]
    E --> H[Normal to Shear Force Ns N]
    F --> I[Cutting Force Fc N]
    F --> J[Thrust Force Ft N]
    G --> K[Cutting Power Pc kW]
    I --> K
    L[Cutting Speed V] --> K
    K --> M[Machine Spindle Motor Sizing]
    G --> N[Shear Stress tau s MPa]
    H --> N
    O[Undeformed Chip Area A mm2] --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Study Tip:** The Merchant force circle is **not a Mermaid renderable geometry**. The block diagram above captures its logical structure and is acceptable in KTU answer sheets as a free-hand sketch. In exams, draw the actual circle with labeled $F$, $N$, $R$, $F_s$, $N_s$, $\beta$, $\phi$, and $(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "rake angle" in a single-point cutting tool. Differentiate between positive and negative rake angles with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **rake angle (α)** is the angle between the **rake face** of the tool and a reference plane (XY plane), measured in a plane perpendicular to the cutting edge.

- **Positive Rake Angle (α > 0):** When the rake face slopes downward toward the cutting edge. Common for **ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminum) at higher cutting speeds. Reduces cutting force and power consumption.
- **Negative Rake Angle (α < 0):** When the rake face slopes upward away from the cutting edge. Used for **hard, brittle materials** (cast iron, hardened steel) and for **interrupted cuts** to provide a strong cutting edge.

```
         Positive Rake                  Negative Rake
       \      alpha          /           /  alpha  \
        \   /                /           /  /        \
         \ /                /           / /          
          -----------------            /  --------
         (Workpiece top)            (Workpiece top)
```

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a "built-up edge" (BUE)? State the cutting conditions that promote its formation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** is a wedge-shaped deposit of work-material atoms that **weld onto the tool tip** due to high localized pressure and temperature in the secondary shear zone.

**Conditions that favor BUE formation:**
1. **Low cutting speed** (typically below ~30 m/min for steel).
2. **High friction** at the tool–chip interface (poor lubrication, sharp tool, sticky work material).
3. **High feed and depth of cut** (raises contact pressure).
4. **Ductile work materials** (e.g., low-carbon steel, aluminum) at moderate temperatures.
5. **Absence of coolant** or wrong coolant selection.

**Effects:** Poor surface finish, dimensional inaccuracy, and accelerated tool wear when BUE periodically breaks off carrying tool material with it.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** For every 14-mark question, the paper setter MUST provide an internal choice (either-or). Both choices test the same Course Outcome (CO1) but with different problem contexts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **mechanics of chip formation** in metal cutting. Differentiate between **continuous, discontinuous, and serrated chips** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Merchant's shear angle relationship** $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ for orthogonal cutting. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Sketch (3 marks):** Draw the orthogonal cutting model showing:
- Tool with rake angle $\alpha$ and clearance angle $\gamma$.
- Workpiece with uncut chip thickness $t_1$ and deformed chip thickness $t_2$.
- Primary shear zone inclined at angle $\phi$ to the cutting velocity.
- Secondary shear zone along the rake face.
- Built-Up Edge (BUE), if applicable.
- Cutting force $F_c$ and thrust force $F_t$ vectors.

**Mechanics of Chip Formation (2.5 marks):**

When the tool advances with velocity $V$ into the workpiece, the metal in front of the cutting edge is subjected to **compressive stress**. As the tool tip advances:

- The material first undergoes **elastic deformation** (recoverable).
- Beyond the yield point, the metal flows plastically.
- Strain is concentrated along the **primary shear zone** (thickness ≈ 0.02–0.05 mm) where the lattice structure disintegrates.
- The chip then slides along the tool's **rake face**, generating friction and forming the **secondary shear zone**.
- Heat is generated in *both* shear zones; temperatures can reach 600–900 °C at the tool tip.

**Chip Types (1.5 marks):**

| Chip Type | Description | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Continuous | Long, unbroken ribbon; smooth cutting | Turning mild steel at 120 m/min |
| Discontinuous | Segmented, broken; brittle fracture | Turning cast iron, machining bronze |
| Serrated | Alternate thick/thin segments; high temp | High-speed cutting of titanium |

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Statement of Merchant's Empirical Relation (0.5 marks):**

E. J. Merchant, in 1944–1945, conducted extensive orthogonal cutting experiments and proposed the relation:

$$
2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°
$$

**Assumptions (2 marks):**

1. Cutting is **orthogonal** — cutting edge is straight and perpendicular to the cutting velocity.
2. The tool is **sharp** (no wear land).
3. The **shear plane** is a single, well-defined plane, not a thick zone.
4. The **chip** does not slide on the rake face; i.e., the tool–chip contact is *sticking* (no relative motion at the interface), so the friction force equals $\mu N$.
5. The **work material is perfectly plastic** and homogeneous, with no work-hardening effect.

**Geometric Derivation (4.5 marks):**

Consider the tool–chip interface as a friction plane. The friction force $F$ and normal force $N$ act on the rake face, and their resultant $R$ makes angle $\beta$ (friction angle) with the normal to the rake face.

$$
\tan\beta = \dfrac{F}{N} = \mu
$$

The resultant $R$ can also be resolved along the **primary shear plane** into the shear force $F_s$ and the normal-to-shear force $N_s$. The angle between $R$ and $F_s$ in the force circle is:

$$
\theta = \phi + \beta - \alpha
$$

Merchant observed experimentally that this angle $\theta$ remains **constant at 90°** regardless of variations in cutting speed, feed, or rake angle. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°\,}
$$

This is the famous **Merchant's shear angle identity**. It allows the friction angle $\beta$ to be back-calculated from measurable quantities $\alpha$ and $\phi$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Merchant's Equation:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** Merchant's identity with the *force relation* $\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$. The latter is a different (and less universal) statement.
> 2. **Do not derive** this equation using a force-balance algebra — it is an *empirical* observation, geometrically interpreted. KTU examiners will deduct 2 marks if you present a force balance as a proof.
> 3. **Always list assumptions** before the derivation; skipping them costs at least 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Merchant's force circle** for orthogonal cutting. Resolve the resultant force $R$ into the **shear force $F_s$** and the **cutting force $F_c$**, deriving the relationship:

$$
F_c = \dfrac{F_s \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}
$$

**(b)** A turning operation on mild steel has the following data: cutting speed $V = 120$ m/min, feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, depth of cut $d = 2$ mm, rake angle $\alpha = +10°$, shear angle $\phi = 30°$, and friction angle $\beta = 35°$. If the cutting force $F_c = 1200$ N, calculate the **(i) Material Removal Rate**, **(ii) Specific Cutting Energy**, and **(iii) Cutting Power in kW**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Merchant's Force Circle (3 marks):**

A free-body diagram of the chip on the tool's rake face is drawn. Two forces act on the chip from the tool:
- **Friction force $F$** along the rake face (opposing chip flow).
- **Normal force $N$** perpendicular to the rake face.

Their resultant $R$ is inclined at angle $\beta$ to the normal of the rake face, with $\tan\beta = \mu$.

The force $R$ can be resolved along the **shear plane** (which is inclined at $\phi$ to the cutting direction):
- **Shear force $F_s$** along the shear plane.
- **Normal-to-shear force $N_s$** perpendicular to the shear plane.

The angle between $R$ and $F_s$ is $(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$.

**Force Resolution (4 marks):**

Resolve $R$ into $F_c$ (cutting direction) and $F_t$ (thrust direction) — first stage:

$$
F_c = R \cos(\beta - \alpha), \qquad F_t = R \sin(\beta - \alpha)
$$

Resolve $R$ into $F_s$ and $N_s$ — second stage:

$$
F_s = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)
$$

Equate $R$ from both stages:

$$
R = \dfrac{F_c}{\cos(\beta - \alpha)} = \dfrac{F_s}{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}
$$

Apply Merchant's identity $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$, which implies $\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90° - \phi$ and $\beta - \alpha = 90° - 2\phi$. Wait — this substitution gives a complex form. Instead, use the angle sum:

$$
\cos(\beta - \alpha) = \cos[(\phi + \beta) - (\phi + \alpha)] = \cos(\phi + \beta)\cos(\phi + \alpha) + \sin(\phi + \beta)\sin(\phi + \alpha)
$$

To obtain the standard KTU form, divide $F_s$ expression by $F_c$ expression and solve for $F_c$:

$$
\dfrac{F_s}{F_c} = \dfrac{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\beta - \alpha)}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\cos(\phi + \alpha)$ using the standard identity (the convention adopted in most textbooks):

$$
\boxed{\,F_c = \dfrac{F_s \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}\,}
$$

And for the thrust force:

$$
\boxed{\,F_t = \dfrac{F_s \sin(\beta - \alpha)}{\cos(\phi + \beta)}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- $V = 120$ m/min
- $f = 0.2$ mm/rev
- $d = 2$ mm
- $\alpha = +10°$
- $\phi = 30°$
- $\beta = 35°$
- $F_c = 1200$ N

**(i) Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d = 120 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 48 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

> **Unit Note:** Since $V$ is in m/min and $f$, $d$ in mm, MRR comes out in **1000 mm³/min**. Convert: $48 \times 1000 = 48{,}000$ mm³/min $= 48$ cm³/min.

**(ii) Specific Cutting Energy (2.5 marks):**

$$
U_c = \dfrac{F_c}{f \times d} = \dfrac{1200}{0.2 \times 2} = \dfrac{1200}{0.4} = 3000 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

> Equivalently, $U_c = 3.0$ GPa $= 3.0 \times 10^9$ N/m² $= 3.0$ J/mm³.

**(iii) Cutting Power (2.5 marks):**

First, express $V$ in consistent units — convert m/min to mm/s:

$$
V = 120 \text{ m/min} = 120{,}000 \text{ mm/min} = 2000 \text{ mm/s}
$$

Then:

$$
P_c = \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60\,000} = \dfrac{1200 \times 120}{60\,000} = \dfrac{144{,}000}{60{,}000} = 2.4 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> - MRR = **48,000 mm³/min** (or 48 cm³/min)
> - Specific Cutting Energy = **3.0 J/mm³**
> - Cutting Power = **2.4 kW**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Numerical Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit conversion of $V$** — failing to divide $V$ by 60,000 in the power formula is the #1 source of error. Students often write $P = F_c \times V$ directly, getting 144,000 (a meaningless number).
> 2. **Forgetting to square or cube** the units — $U_c$ must be in N/mm² (or J/mm³), *not* Newtons.
> 3. **Wrong MRR formula** — the correct order is $V \times f \times d$, *not* $f \times d \times V$ in some other unit basis. Be consistent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Metal cutting** is a *subtractive manufacturing* process based on *localized plastic shear failure* along a primary shear plane.
- **Orthogonal cutting** (edge perpendicular to velocity) is the *theoretical workhorse*; **oblique cutting** is the *real-world* condition.
- The **three primary tool angles** are **rake ($\alpha$), clearance ($\gamma$), and nose radius ($r$)**. Together with cutting edge angles, they form the tool signature.
- The **chip thickness ratio** $r = t_1/t_2 = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ is a *gateway identity* — derive it once and reuse throughout.
- **Shear strain** $\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ quantifies the *plastic severity* of the cut.
- **Merchant's identity** $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ is *empirical*, not derived from first principles.
- **Cutting force** $F_c = F_s \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha) / \cos(\phi + \beta)$ — the most-tested 14-mark derivation.
- **MRR** = $V \times f \times d$ — convert $V$ to mm/min (multiply by 1000) for consistent mm³/min.
- **Power** $P_c$ (kW) = $F_c \times V$ (N·m/min) ÷ 60,000.
- **Taylor's Tool Life** $VT^n = C$ — used to set optimum cutting speed for a target tool life.
- **Specific cutting energy** $U_c$ is material-dependent; e.g., aluminum ≈ 0.4 J/mm³, mild steel ≈ 2.5 J/mm³, titanium ≈ 3.5 J/mm³.
- **BUE** forms at low speed + high friction → poor surface → avoid in finish machining.
- **Coolant/lubricant** is essential for high-speed cutting to control secondary-zone temperature and reduce BUE.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *For a 14-mark question, always pair a labeled diagram (3–4 marks), a clear derivation with assumptions (5–6 marks), and a numerical worked example with units (3–4 marks).* KTU examiners reward *structure* and *unit discipline* as much as conceptual accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tool Nomenclature # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tool Nomenclature** is the **standardized system of designating and defining the geometric features of a cutting tool** — particularly a single-point cutting tool (SPCT) — using universally accepted reference planes, angles, and symbols. It provides a common engineering "language" so that a tool designer in Japan, a CNC programmer in Kerala, and a machine operator in Germany all interpret the tool geometry identically. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, tool nomenclature is governed by two parallel conventions: 1. **ASA System (American Standards Association, Old System)** – historically used in Indian workshops; still taught as a foundation in B.Tech curricula. 2. **ISO/ORS System (International Organization for Standardization / Orthogonal Rake System, Modern System)** – the contemporary, globally preferred method using *inclination angle* and *orthogonal rake*. A **Tool Signature** (or **Tool Code**) is the symbolic shorthand that lists all essential angles on a single line, e.g.: $$\text{ASA :}\;\; \alpha_b \; \alpha_s \; \alpha_e \; \alpha_s' \; C_e \; C_s \; r$$ $$\text{ISO/ORS :}\;\; \gamma_o \; \gamma_o' \; \alpha_o \; \alpha_o' \; K_r \; K_r' \; \lambda_s \; r_\varepsilon$$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Why We Need a "Naming System" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Doctor's Prescription Pad** > Think of tool nomenclature like a doctor's prescription shorthand. A doctor doesn't write the full name of every medicine and dosage in long sentences — they use a compact, standardized code understood worldwide (e.g., *Paracetamol 500 mg BD*). Similarly, a machinist cannot describe a cutting tool in paragraphs; they need a **compact code** that defines every geometric parameter of the cutting edge unambiguously. Without this naming system: - A toolmaker in Bangalore would send a tool to Coimbatore with no clear way to describe its geometry. - Two engineers would use different terms for the same angle — leading to **catastrophic tool failure** or poor surface finish. - CNC programmers couldn't translate blueprint specs into machine coordinates. The tool nomenclature system solves this by defining **reference planes first** (so we all agree on *where* to measure), then **angles** (so we all agree on *what* to measure), and finally a **signature** (so we all agree on *how to write it*). --- ## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners repeatedly frame tool-nomenclature questions as **"Explain the ASA/ISO tool signature with a neat sketch"** or **"Convert the given ASA tool angles to ISO/ORS system."** These questions test both **visualization** and **trigonometric conversion skill**, making them a perfect 14-mark Part B choice. **Key Physical Constants / Metrics used:** - Standard unit for all linear dimensions of tool geometry: **millimetre (mm)** - Standard unit for all angles: **degree (°)** — decimal degrees, never radians in workshop convention. - Standard nose radius range for turning tools: **0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.2 mm, 1.6 mm, 2.4 mm** (ISO 868). - Standard tool shank cross-section: **square (12×12, 16×16, 20×20, 25×25 mm)** or rectangular. --- ## 1.4 The Three Building Blocks of Tool Nomenclature Every tool nomenclature system, regardless of origin, rests on **three pillars**: | Pillar | What it Defines | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Reference Planes** | The imaginary planes in/around the tool | Provide a fixed 3-D coordinate framework | | **Tool Angles** | The angular measurements between planes/features | Describe tool geometry quantitatively | | **Tool Signature** | A short-hand code listing the angles | Compact communication across industries | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reference Planes — The "GPS Coordinates" of the Tool Before defining any angle, we must establish **six reference planes**. Each plane is named by its relationship to the **cutting velocity vector (V)** or to the **tool shank**. ### 2.1.1 Planes for a Single-Point Turning Tool | # | Plane Name | Symbol | Definition | Analogy | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Reference Plane** | $P_r$ | Plane passing through the tool point, parallel to the longitudinal and cross-feed directions (i.e., the horizontal plane containing the cutting velocity vector). | The "floor" on which the tool stands. | | 2 | **Longitudinal Plane** (Cutting Plane by ASA) | $P_f$ | Plane through the tool point, parallel to both the cutting velocity and the longitudinal feed direction. | The "front wall" the chip slides along. | | 3 | **Transverse Plane** | $P_t$ | Plane through the tool point, parallel to the cross-feed direction and perpendicular to the cutting velocity. | The "side wall" of the tool. | | 4 | **Cutting Plane** (ISO) | $P_c$ | Plane tangential to the cutting edge at the tool point, containing the cutting velocity vector. | The "blade face" of the tool. | | 5 | **Normal Plane** | $P_n$ | Plane perpendicular to the cutting edge (in 3-D), containing the cutting velocity vector. | Perpendicular cross-section of the cutting edge. | | 6 | **Orthogonal Plane** (ISO/ORS) | $P_o$ | Plane perpendicular to both the cutting edge's projection in $P_r$ AND to $P_r$ itself. | The "true working" plane for rake/clearance measurement. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Difference between ASA and ISO:** > - ASA uses **Cutting Plane** ($P_c$) as the primary angle-measurement plane. > - ISO/ORS uses the **Orthogonal Plane** ($P_o$) — which is geometrically the "true" plane of chip flow. This is why the ISO system is more physically meaningful. --- ### 2.1.2 Visual Reference — How the Planes Relate ``` Z-axis (cutting velocity V) ↑ | / Cutting Plane (Pc) | / | / | / | / | / Orthogonal Plane (Po) | / |/______→ X-axis (feed direction) /| / | / | Reference Plane (Pr) — horizontal / | ``` --- ## 2.2 Tool Angles — The "Personality" of the Cutting Tool The tool angles are classified into **four functional families**: ### Family 1: Rake Angles (Chip-flow controllers) | Angle | Symbol | Plane Measured | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Back Rake** (ASA) / Orthogonal Rake (ISO) | $\alpha_b$ / $\gamma_o$ | $P_c$ (ASA) / $P_o$ (ISO) | Controls chip flow direction **away from the workpiece**, vertically. | | **Side Rake** (ASA) / Side Rake (ISO) | $\alpha_s$ / $\gamma_o'$ | $P_t$ / $P_o$ | Controls chip flow **sideways**, along the cutting edge. | > **Positive rake** → thinner chip, lower cutting force, sharper tool (good for soft materials like aluminium). > **Negative rake** → stronger cutting edge, used for hard materials (e.g., hardened steel) and interrupted cuts. ### Family 2: Clearance / Relief Angles (Friction preventers) | Angle | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | **End Relief Angle** | $\alpha_e$ | Prevents the **end flank** (trailing surface) of the tool from rubbing against the workpiece's machined surface. | | **Side Relief Angle** | $\alpha_s'$ | Prevents the **side flank** from rubbing against the workpiece. | > [!WARNING] > If clearance angles are too small → **flank rubbing** → excessive heat, poor surface finish, rapid tool wear. > If too large → weak cutting edge → chipping. ### Family 3: Cutting Edge Angles (Geometry controllers) | Angle | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | **End Cutting Edge Angle** (ASA) | $C_e$ | Angle the **secondary cutting edge** (at the corner) makes with $P_r$. Typically $5°$–$30°$. | | **Side Cutting Edge Angle** (ASA) | $C_s$ | Angle the **major (side) cutting edge** makes with $P_r$. $C_s = 90° - C_e$ for a straight cutting edge. | | **Approach / Cutting Edge Angle** (ISO) | $K_r$ | Same as $C_s$ in ASA. ISO uses $K_r$ as the angle between major edge and feed direction. | | **Side Cutting Edge Angle** (ISO) | $K_r'$ | ISO's $K_r' = C_e$ in ASA terminology. | | **Inclination Angle** (ISO only) | $\lambda_s$ | Angle the cutting edge makes with $P_r$, measured in $P_f$. **Controls chip flow direction** along the edge. | ### Family 4: Nose Radius (The "smooth-finish" parameter) | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Values | |---|---|---| | **Nose Radius** | $r$ / $r_\varepsilon$ | 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6, 2.4 mm | > Larger nose radius → better surface finish (lower $R_a$), stronger tool, but increased radial force and chatter tendency. --- ## 2.3 The Tool Signature — Compact Code ### 2.3.1 ASA Tool Signature Format $$\boxed{\;\text{Signature} = \alpha_b \;\; \alpha_s \;\; \alpha_e \;\; \alpha_s' \;\; C_e \;\; C_s \;\; r\;}$$ **Example:** $0° \;\; 14° \;\; 6° \;\; 6° \;\; 15° \;\; 75° \;\; 0.8 \text{ mm}$ Means: back rake $0°$, side rake $14°$, end relief $6°$, side relief $6°$, end cutting edge angle $15°$, side cutting edge angle $75°$, nose radius $0.8$ mm. ### 2.3.2 ISO/ORS Tool Signature Format $$\boxed{\;\text{Signature} = \gamma_o \;\; \gamma_o' \;\; \alpha_o \;\; \alpha_o' \;\; K_r \;\; K_r' \;\; \lambda_s \;\; r_\varepsilon\;}$$ **Example:** $-5° \;\; -5° \;\; 6° \;\; 6° \;\; 75° \;\; 15° \;\; 0° \;\; 0.8 \text{ mm}$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **This table is exam-critical.** KTU questions on tool conversion directly use these formulas. ### Master Formula Table | # | Conversion / Relationship | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ASA back rake → ISO orthogonal rake | $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e$ | $C_e$ is end cutting edge angle | | 2 | ASA side rake → ISO side rake | $\tan \gamma_o' = \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e$ | Measured in transverse plane | | 3 | Inclination angle (ISO) | $\tan \lambda_s = \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e - \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e$ | Negative $\lambda_s$ → chip flows toward tool tip | | 4 | Approach angle relation | $K_r + C_s = 90°$ | For straight cutting edge | | 5 | Side cutting edge angle (ISO) | $K_r' + C_e = 90°$ | Equivalent to $C_s$ in ASA | | 6 | Orthogonal clearance from end + side relief | $\tan \alpha_o = \tan \alpha_e \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \sin C_e$ | Conversion of clearance angles | | 7 | Side clearance (ISO) | $\tan \alpha_o' = \tan \alpha_e \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \cos C_e$ | | | 8 | Cutting velocity relationship | $V = \pi D N$ (m/min) | $D$=workpiece dia, $N$=RPM | | 9 | Chip thickness ratio | $r = t_1 / t_2 = \sin \phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ | Merchant's circle analysis | | 10 | Shear angle (Merchant) | $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ | $\alpha$=rake, $\beta$=friction | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **CNC Programming:** Modern CNC lathes (e.g., FANUC, Siemens Sinumerik) accept tool-nomenclature parameters directly when defining the tool in the offset table. The ISO system is the default. > - **CAD/CAM Software:** Mastercam, Fusion 360, and CATIA use ISO tool geometry as input when defining custom tool shapes. > - **Tool Manufacturers:** Companies like Sandvik Coromant, Kennametal, and Walter publish tool catalogues in ISO nomenclature — every insert is described using $\gamma_o, \lambda_s, K_r$, etc. > - **Quality Inspection:** Tool presetters measure actual tool geometry against the declared signature to verify conformity before a production run. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ASA → ISO Rake Angle Conversion We will derive the formula $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e$ step-by-step. ### Setup Imagine the tool tip as origin $O$. The cutting edge lies along the **major cutting edge** in the reference plane $P_r$. The orthogonal plane $P_o$ is perpendicular to the projection of this cutting edge onto $P_r$. The cutting plane $P_c$ (ASA) is tangent to the rake face. The two rake angles are measured: - $\alpha_b$ in the cutting plane $P_c$ (vertical plane parallel to feed direction). - $\alpha_s$ in the transverse plane $P_t$ (vertical plane perpendicular to feed direction). - $\gamma_o$ in the orthogonal plane $P_o$ (perpendicular to the cutting edge's projection in $P_r$). Let the **rake face** have a unit normal vector. Its **slope** in 3-D can be expressed in two coordinate systems: one aligned with $P_c$ and $P_t$ (ASA), and another aligned with $P_o$ and the side-cutting-edge plane (ISO). ### Step 1: Express the rake-face orientation via slopes in ASA system In the ASA system, the slope of the rake face (i.e., its tilt from horizontal) is given by two components: - Slope along the **feed direction** (within $P_c$): $\tan \alpha_b$ - Slope **perpendicular to feed** (within $P_t$): $\tan \alpha_s$ ### Step 2: Identify the rotation angle between ASA and ISO measurement directions The orthogonal plane $P_o$ is rotated from the cutting plane $P_c$ by the **end cutting edge angle** $C_e$, because: - $P_c$ contains the cutting velocity and the side cutting edge direction. - $P_o$ contains the cutting velocity and the **normal to the cutting edge's projection** — i.e., it is rotated from $P_c$ by $C_e$ (measured in $P_r$). ### Step 3: Decompose the ASA slopes along ISO directions We need to project the rake-face slope components from the $\{P_c, P_t\}$ pair to the $\{P_o, P_{o\text{-side}}\}$ pair, where $P_{o\text{-side}}$ is the orthogonal plane rotated $90°$ from $P_o$. Using 2-D vector rotation in the horizontal plane by angle $C_e$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Component along } P_o &= \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e \\ \text{Component along } P_{o\text{-side}} &= -\tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e \end{aligned} $$ The first component IS the slope of the rake face in the orthogonal plane $P_o$, which by definition equals $\tan \gamma_o$. ### Step 4: Write the final result $$ \boxed{\;\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e\;} $$ This is the **fundamental ASA → ISO rake conversion formula**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Inclination Angle $\lambda_s$ The inclination angle $\lambda_s$ is the angle between the cutting edge and $P_r$, measured in the longitudinal plane $P_f$ (the vertical plane parallel to feed direction). The "rise" of the cutting edge along $P_f$ arises from the **difference** of slopes in the two horizontal directions, weighted by $\sin$ and $\cos$ of $C_e$: ### Step 1: Set up the slope decomposition The slope of the rake face perpendicular to the cutting edge's projection (i.e., the rate at which the rake face rises *out of* $P_r$ as we move sideways along the cutting edge) is: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\perp} = -\tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Recognize $\lambda_s$ as the angle whose tangent equals this slope The inclination angle is defined such that: $$ \boxed{\;\tan \lambda_s = -\tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e\;} $$ Equivalently: $$ \tan \lambda_s = \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e - \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e $$ **Sign convention:** Positive $\lambda_s$ means the **tool tip is the lowest point** of the cutting edge; the rake face slopes upward from tip to periphery. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — A Classic KTU-Style Problem > **Given (ASA tool signature):** > $\alpha_b = 5°,\; \alpha_s = 10°,\; \alpha_e = 6°,\; \alpha_s' = 6°,\; C_e = 15°,\; C_s = 75°,\; r = 0.8 \text{ mm}$ > > **Find:** Equivalent ISO/ORS tool signature. ### Step 1: Compute Orthogonal Rake Angle $\gamma_o$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \gamma_o &= \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e \\ &= \tan 5° \cdot \cos 15° + \tan 10° \cdot \sin 15° \\ &= 0.0875 \times 0.9659 + 0.1763 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.0845 + 0.0456 \\ &= 0.1301 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \gamma_o = \tan^{-1}(0.1301) = 7.41° \approx 7°24' $$ ### Step 2: Compute ISO Side Rake $\gamma_o'$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \gamma_o' &= \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e \\ &= \tan 5° \cdot \sin 15° + \tan 10° \cdot \cos 15° \\ &= 0.0875 \times 0.2588 + 0.1763 \times 0.9659 \\ &= 0.0226 + 0.1703 \\ &= 0.1929 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \gamma_o' = \tan^{-1}(0.1929) = 10.92° \approx 10°55' $$ ### Step 3: Compute Inclination Angle $\lambda_s$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \lambda_s &= \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e - \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e \\ &= \tan 10° \cdot \cos 15° - \tan 5° \cdot \sin 15° \\ &= 0.1763 \times 0.9659 - 0.0875 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.1703 - 0.0226 \\ &= 0.1477 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \lambda_s = \tan^{-1}(0.1477) = 8.40° \approx 8°24' $$ ### Step 4: Compute Orthogonal Clearance $\alpha_o$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_o &= \tan \alpha_e \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \sin C_e \\ &= \tan 6° \cdot \cos 15° + \tan 6° \cdot \sin 15° \\ &= 0.1051 \times 0.9659 + 0.1051 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.1015 + 0.0272 \\ &= 0.1287 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \alpha_o = \tan^{-1}(0.1287) = 7.34° \approx 7°20' $$ ### Step 5: Compute ISO Side Clearance $\alpha_o'$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_o' &= \tan \alpha_e \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \cos C_e \\ &= \tan 6° \cdot \sin 15° + \tan 6° \cdot \cos 15° \\ &= 0.1051 \times 0.2588 + 0.1051 \times 0.9659 \\ &= 0.0272 + 0.1015 \\ &= 0.1287 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \alpha_o' = \tan^{-1}(0.1287) = 7.34° \approx 7°20' $$ (Note: $\alpha_o = \alpha_o'$ here because $\alpha_e = \alpha_s'$ and the formula becomes symmetric.) ### Step 6: Compute Cutting Edge Angles $$ \begin{aligned} K_r &= C_s = 75° \\ K_r' &= C_e = 15° \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7: Write the Final ISO Signature $$ \boxed{\;\gamma_o \; \gamma_o' \; \alpha_o \; \alpha_o' \; K_r \; K_r' \; \lambda_s \; r_\varepsilon \;=\; 7°24' \;\; 10°55' \;\; 7°20' \;\; 7°20' \;\; 75° \;\; 15° \;\; 8°24' \;\; 0.8 \text{ mm}\;} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Functional Effect of Each Angle > [!NOTE] > Use this matrix as a rapid-revision tool. Each row tells you "what happens if I increase this angle." | Angle (Increase ↑) | Effect on Cutting Force | Effect on Chip Flow | Effect on Tool Strength | Effect on Surface Finish | |---|---|---|---|---| | Back Rake / Orthogonal Rake $\alpha_b, \gamma_o$ ↑ | **Decreases** | Chip flows more upward | **Decreases** | Marginal | | Side Rake $\alpha_s, \gamma_o'$ ↑ | **Decreases** | Chip flows sideways toward workpiece | **Decreases** | Marginal | | End Relief / Side Relief $\alpha_e, \alpha_s'$ ↑ | Slight decrease | Negligible | **Decreases** (weak edge) | Risk of chatter | | End Cutting Edge Angle $C_e, K_r'$ ↑ | Increases radial, decreases feed | Negligible | Increases | **Improves** (less nose contact) | | Side Cutting Edge Angle $C_s, K_r$ ↑ | Decreases radial, increases feed | Negligible | Increases | **Degrades** (more nose contact) | | Inclination Angle $\lambda_s$ ↑ | Negligible | Chip flows **toward tool tip** if positive | Negligible | Negligible | | Nose Radius $r, r_\varepsilon$ ↑ | Increases radial component | Negligible | Increases | **Strongly improves** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reference Plane Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram The following diagram maps the **3-D reference plane architecture** for a single-point turning tool, the conceptual backbone of tool nomenclature. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WORK["Tool Tip Coordinate System"] A["Tool Point (Origin O)"] end subgraph PLANES["Six Reference Planes"] P1["Reference Plane Pr
(horizontal, contains V)"] P2["Longitudinal Plane Pf
(vertical, parallel to feed)"] P3["Transverse Plane Pt
(vertical, perpendicular to feed)"] P4["Cutting Plane Pc
(ASA measurement plane)"] P5["Normal Plane Pn
(perpendicular to cutting edge)"] P6["Orthogonal Plane Po
(ISO measurement plane)"] end subgraph ANGLES["Tool Angles"] R1["Back Rake alpha_b
in Pc"] R2["Side Rake alpha_s
in Pt"] R3["Orthogonal Rake gamma_o
in Po"] C1["End Relief alpha_e
in Pf"] C2["Side Relief alpha_s
in Pt"] C3["Approach Angle Kr"] C4["Inclination Angle lambda_s"] NR["Nose Radius r"] end subgraph SIG["Tool Signature"] S1["ASA Code
alpha_b alpha_s alpha_e alpha_s Ce Cs r"] S2["ISO Code
gamma_o gamma_o alpha_o alpha_o Kr Kr lambda_s r"] end A --> P1 A --> P2 A --> P3 A --> P4 A --> P5 A --> P6 P1 --> C3 P4 --> R1 P3 --> R2 P6 --> R3 P2 --> C1 P2 --> C4 Pt --> C2 ANGLES --> S1 ANGLES --> S2 ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagram illustrates how the **six reference planes** form the geometric foundation, **eight tool angles** describe the geometry quantitatively, and the **two tool signatures** (ASA / ISO) provide the compact communication format. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — ASA-to-ISO Conversion Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Given ASA Angles"] I1["Back Rake alpha_b"] I2["Side Rake alpha_s"] I3["End Relief alpha_e"] I4["Side Relief alpha_s'"] I5["End Cutting Edge Ce"] end subgraph COMPUTE["Trigonometric Conversion"] F1["tan gamma_o = tan alpha_b cos Ce + tan alpha_s sin Ce"] F2["tan gamma_o' = tan alpha_b sin Ce + tan alpha_s cos Ce"] F3["tan lambda_s = tan alpha_s cos Ce - tan alpha_b sin Ce"] F4["tan alpha_o = tan alpha_e cos Ce + tan alpha_s' sin Ce"] F5["tan alpha_o' = tan alpha_e sin Ce + tan alpha_s' cos Ce"] end subgraph OUTPUT["ISO Signature"] O1["Orthogonal Rake gamma_o"] O2["Side Rake gamma_o'"] O3["Inclination Angle lambda_s"] O4["Orthogonal Clearance alpha_o"] O5["Side Clearance alpha_o'"] O6["Kr = 90 - Ce
Kr' = Ce"] end I1 --> F1 I2 --> F1 I5 --> F1 F1 --> O1 I1 --> F2 I2 --> F2 I5 --> F2 F2 --> O2 I2 --> F3 I1 --> F3 I5 --> F3 F3 --> O3 I3 --> F4 I4 --> F4 I5 --> F4 F4 --> O4 I3 --> F5 I4 --> F5 I5 --> F5 F5 --> O5 I5 --> O6 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to read this diagram for the exam:** > - Each **input angle** (left) feeds into the appropriate formula box (centre). > - Each **formula box** computes one **ISO angle** (right). > - The arrows enforce that **$C_e$** is required for *every* conversion — KTU examiners often omit $C_e$ to test whether students know the dependency. --- ## 4.3 Tool Geometry Cross-Section (Schematic Description) Since a true 3-D rendering cannot be embedded, here is the **schematic description** that KTU expects students to reproduce: ``` Rake face (where chip slides) / / ← Rake angle (alpha or gamma) /¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯ / Cutting edge /________________________→ / ↑ / | Side Relief / | (alpha_s') /____________________________ | |←——— Side Cutting Edge Angle Cs ———→| | | ↑ ↑ End Relief Nose Radius r (alpha_e) (curved corner) ─────────────────────────────────────── (Reference Plane Pr) ``` **Reading the schematic:** - The **rake face** is the upper sloped surface (positive slope = positive rake). - The **cutting edge** is the sharp line where the rake face meets the flank. - The **flank** has two parts: end flank (giving $\alpha_e$) and side flank (giving $\alpha_s'$). - The **nose** is the rounded corner between end and side flanks, with radius $r$. - All angles are measured between their respective feature and the **reference plane** $P_r$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"Define the tool signature of a single-point cutting tool as per ASA nomenclature. List all the elements with their typical ranges."** #### Model Answer (3 marks): A **tool signature** is a compact symbolic representation of the geometry of a single-point cutting tool, listing its essential angles and nose radius in a single line. As per the **ASA (American Standards Association)** system, the tool signature is: $$\boxed{\;\alpha_b \;\; \alpha_s \;\; \alpha_e \;\; \alpha_s' \;\; C_e \;\; C_s \;\; r\;}$$ **Typical ranges for finishing-grade tools on mild steel:** | Element | Symbol | Typical Range | |---|---|---| | Back Rake Angle | $\alpha_b$ | $0°$ to $+15°$ | | Side Rake Angle | $\alpha_s$ | $+5°$ to $+20°$ | | End Relief Angle | $\alpha_e$ | $5°$ to $8°$ | | Side Relief Angle | $\alpha_s'$ | $5°$ to $8°$ | | End Cutting Edge Angle | $C_e$ | $5°$ to $30°$ | | Side Cutting Edge Angle | $C_s$ | $60°$ to $85°$ | | Nose Radius | $r$ | 0.4 to 2.4 mm | The sum $C_e + C_s = 90°$ for a straight cutting edge. Each element describes a specific feature of the cutting tool, allowing any machinist to visualize and recreate the geometry. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"Distinguish between ASA and ISO (ORS) systems of tool nomenclature. Mention any three points of difference."** #### Model Answer (3 marks): | # | ASA System | ISO/ORS System | |---|---|---| | 1 | Rake angles measured in the **Cutting Plane ($P_c$)** and **Transverse Plane ($P_t$)**. | Rake angles measured in the **Orthogonal Plane ($P_o$)** and **Side Orthogonal Plane**. | | 2 | No inclination angle; uses **back rake** $\alpha_b$ and **side rake** $\alpha_s$. | Uses **inclination angle** $\lambda_s$ in addition to orthogonal rake angles $\gamma_o, \gamma_o'$. | | 3 | Clearance angles: end relief $\alpha_e$, side relief $\alpha_s'$. | Clearance angles: orthogonal clearance $\alpha_o$, side clearance $\alpha_o'$. | | 4 | Cutting edge angles: $C_e$, $C_s$. | Cutting edge angles: $K_r$, $K_r'$. | | 5 | Older American convention, still taught in Indian textbooks. | Modern global standard (ISO 3685); used in international tool catalogues. | The ISO system is preferred because the orthogonal plane represents the **true plane of chip flow**, giving physically meaningful rake angles. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]** > **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **ASA system of tool nomenclature** for a single-point cutting tool. List all angles and planes clearly. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** The ASA tool angles of a turning tool are given as: > $\alpha_b = 8°, \alpha_s = 12°, \alpha_e = 7°, \alpha_s' = 7°, C_e = 15°$. > Compute the equivalent **ISO/ORS tool angles** $\gamma_o, \gamma_o', \lambda_s, \alpha_o, \alpha_o'$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):** The ASA system (American Standards Association) is a convention for describing the geometry of a single-point cutting tool using standardized planes and angle symbols. It was historically used in American machine shops and is the basis of tool-nomenclature teaching in Indian B.Tech curricula. **Step 2 — Reference Planes (2 marks):** The four primary planes are: - **Reference Plane ($P_r$):** Horizontal plane through the tool point, parallel to the cutting velocity vector. - **Longitudinal Plane ($P_f$):** Vertical plane parallel to both the cutting velocity and the feed direction; also called the "cutting plane" in ASA usage. - **Transverse Plane ($P_t$):** Vertical plane perpendicular to the cutting velocity; parallel to the cross-feed direction. - **Cutting Plane ($P_c$):** Tangent to the cutting edge, containing the cutting velocity vector. **Step 3 — Angles with Sketch Description (3 marks):** | Angle | Symbol | Plane | Function | |---|---|---|---| | Back Rake | $\alpha_b$ | $P_c$ | Vertical chip-flow control | | Side Rake | $\alpha_s$ | $P_t$ | Sideways chip-flow control | | End Relief | $\alpha_e$ | $P_f$ | Prevents end-flank rubbing | | Side Relief | $\alpha_s'$ | $P_t$ | Prevents side-flank rubbing | | End Cutting Edge Angle | $C_e$ | $P_r$ | Angle of end cutting edge with $P_r$ | | Side Cutting Edge Angle | $C_s$ | $P_r$ | Angle of major edge with $P_r$; $C_s = 90° - C_e$ | | Nose Radius | $r$ | — | Rounded corner between end and side edges | **Step 4 — Tool Signature (1 mark):** The ASA signature is written as: $$\alpha_b \;\; \alpha_s \;\; \alpha_e \;\; \alpha_s' \;\; C_e \;\; C_s \;\; r$$ Example: $0° \; 14° \; 6° \; 6° \; 15° \; 75° \; 0.8 \text{ mm}$. **Sketch** (as described in Section 4.3) must be drawn showing all angles and planes clearly labelled. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Identify inputs (1 mark):** $\alpha_b = 8°, \alpha_s = 12°, \alpha_e = 7°, \alpha_s' = 7°, C_e = 15°$ **Step 2 — Orthogonal Rake $\gamma_o$ (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \gamma_o &= \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e \\ &= \tan 8° \cdot \cos 15° + \tan 12° \cdot \sin 15° \\ &= 0.1405 \times 0.9659 + 0.2126 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.1357 + 0.0550 \\ &= 0.1907 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \gamma_o = \tan^{-1}(0.1907) = 10.79° \approx 10°47' $$ **Step 3 — ISO Side Rake $\gamma_o'$ (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \gamma_o' &= \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e \\ &= \tan 8° \cdot \sin 15° + \tan 12° \cdot \cos 15° \\ &= 0.1405 \times 0.2588 + 0.2126 \times 0.9659 \\ &= 0.0364 + 0.2053 \\ &= 0.2417 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \gamma_o' = \tan^{-1}(0.2417) = 13.59° \approx 13°35' $$ **Step 4 — Inclination Angle $\lambda_s$ (1.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \lambda_s &= \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e - \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e \\ &= \tan 12° \cdot \cos 15° - \tan 8° \cdot \sin 15° \\ &= 0.2126 \times 0.9659 - 0.1405 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.2053 - 0.0364 \\ &= 0.1689 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \lambda_s = \tan^{-1}(0.1689) = 9.59° \approx 9°35' $$ **Step 5 — Orthogonal Clearance $\alpha_o$ (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_o &= \tan \alpha_e \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \sin C_e \\ &= \tan 7° \cdot \cos 15° + \tan 7° \cdot \sin 15° \\ &= 0.1228 \times 0.9659 + 0.1228 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.1186 + 0.0318 \\ &= 0.1504 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \alpha_o = \tan^{-1}(0.1504) = 8.55° \approx 8°33' $$ **Step 6 — ISO Side Clearance $\alpha_o'$ (0.5 marks):** Since $\alpha_e = \alpha_s'$ and $\tan \alpha_o' = \tan \alpha_e \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \cos C_e$, by symmetry $\alpha_o' = \alpha_o = 8°33'$. **Step 7 — Final ISO Signature (0.5 marks):** $$ \boxed{\;10°47' \;\; 13°35' \;\; 8°33' \;\; 8°33' \;\; 75° \;\; 15° \;\; 9°35' \;\; 0.8 \text{ mm}\;} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** > **(a)** Explain the **ISO/ORS system of tool nomenclature** in detail. List the reference planes, tool angles, and the tool signature format. Compare it briefly with the ASA system. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A single-point cutting tool has the following ISO angles: > $\gamma_o = -5°, \gamma_o' = -5°, \alpha_o = 6°, \alpha_o' = 6°, K_r = 75°, \lambda_s = 0°, r_\varepsilon = 1.2$ mm. > Convert these to the **ASA system** and write the ASA tool signature. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):** The ISO/ORS (International Organization for Standardization / Orthogonal Rake System) is the modern global standard for tool nomenclature. It uses the **orthogonal plane $P_o$** as the primary measurement plane because it represents the true plane of chip flow. **Step 2 — Reference Planes (2 marks):** The ISO system uses six reference planes: - $P_r$ — Reference plane (horizontal) - $P_f$ — Longitudinal plane (vertical, along feed) - $P_t$ — Transverse plane (vertical, across feed) - $P_c$ — Cutting plane (tangent to cutting edge) - $P_n$ — Normal plane (perpendicular to cutting edge) - $P_o$ — **Orthogonal plane (perpendicular to cutting-edge projection in $P_r$ AND to $P_r$)** — KEY ISO PLANE. **Step 3 — Tool Angles (3 marks):** | ISO Angle | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | Orthogonal Rake | $\gamma_o$ | Rake angle measured in $P_o$ | | Side Rake | $\gamma_o'$ | Rake measured in side orthogonal plane | | Orthogonal Clearance | $\alpha_o$ | Clearance in $P_o$ | | Side Clearance | $\alpha_o'$ | Clearance in side orthogonal plane | | Approach / Cutting Edge Angle | $K_r$ | Angle of major edge with $P_r$ | | Side Cutting Edge Angle | $K_r'$ | Angle of secondary edge with $P_r$ | | Inclination Angle | $\lambda_s$ | Angle of cutting edge with $P_r$, in $P_f$ | | Nose Radius | $r_\varepsilon$ | Rounded corner radius | **Step 4 — Tool Signature (0.5 mark):** $$\gamma_o \;\; \gamma_o' \;\; \alpha_o \;\; \alpha_o' \;\; K_r \;\; K_r' \;\; \lambda_s \;\; r_\varepsilon$$ **Step 5 — Comparison with ASA (0.5 mark):** Unlike ASA which uses $P_c$ and $P_t$, ISO uses the orthogonal plane $P_o$ for rake/clearance. ISO uniquely includes the **inclination angle $\lambda_s$**, which controls chip-flow direction along the cutting edge. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Inverse Conversion: ISO → ASA** We need to "un-rotate" the orthogonal rake back to back rake and side rake. The inverse formulas are: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_b &= \tan \gamma_o \cos C_e - \tan \gamma_o' \sin C_e \\ \tan \alpha_s &= \tan \gamma_o \sin C_e + \tan \gamma_o' \cos C_e \end{aligned} $$ Given $K_r = 75°$ and $K_r' = 15°$, we identify: $$ C_e = K_r' = 15°, \quad C_s = K_r = 75° $$ **Step 1 — Compute $\tan \alpha_b$ (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_b &= \tan(-5°) \cos 15° - \tan(-5°) \sin 15° \\ &= -0.0875 \times 0.9659 - (-0.0875) \times 0.2588 \\ &= -0.0845 + 0.0226 \\ &= -0.0619 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \alpha_b = \tan^{-1}(-0.0619) = -3.54° \approx -3°32' $$ (Negative back rake — characteristic of tools for hard materials.) **Step 2 — Compute $\tan \alpha_s$ (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_s &= \tan(-5°) \sin 15° + \tan(-5°) \cos 15° \\ &= -0.0875 \times 0.2588 + (-0.0875) \times 0.9659 \\ &= -0.0226 - 0.0845 \\ &= -0.1071 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \alpha_s = \tan^{-1}(-0.1071) = -6.12° \approx -6°07' $$ **Step 3 — Clearance Angles (1 mark):** Using the inverse clearance formula: $$ \tan \alpha_e = \tan \alpha_o \cos C_e - \tan \alpha_o' \sin C_e $$ With $\alpha_o = \alpha_o' = 6°$ and $C_e = 15°$: $$ \begin{aligned} \tan \alpha_e &= \tan 6° \cos 15° - \tan 6° \sin 15° \\ &= 0.1051 \times 0.9659 - 0.1051 \times 0.2588 \\ &= 0.1015 - 0.0272 \\ &= 0.0743 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \alpha_e = \tan^{-1}(0.0743) = 4.25° \approx 4°15' $$ By symmetry (since $\alpha_o = \alpha_o'$), $\alpha_s' = \alpha_e = 4°15'$. **Step 4 — Final ASA Signature (2 marks):** $$ \boxed{\;-3°32' \;\; -6°07' \;\; 4°15' \;\; 4°15' \;\; 15° \;\; 75° \;\; 1.2 \text{ mm}\;} $$ **Interpretation:** Both back and side rake are *negative*, indicating a heavy-duty tool suited for hardened steel or interrupted cuts. The modest $4°15'$ clearance angles and large nose radius ($1.2$ mm) suggest a roughing tool for tough machining conditions. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks on tool-nomenclature questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians/keep them as degrees consistently.** All trig functions in $\tan^{-1}$ take **degrees** here — do not feed radians by mistake. > 2. **Mixing up $C_e$ and $C_s$.** Remember: $C_e$ is the **end** (small) angle, $C_s$ is the **side** (large) angle. ASA convention: $C_e + C_s = 90°$. > 3. **Using the wrong conversion formula.** KTU papers often swap ASA and ISO labels. Always confirm *which system is given* before applying the formula. > 4. **Omitting the inclination angle $\lambda_s$.** ISO system has 8 elements; forgetting $\lambda_s$ or $r_\varepsilon$ loses full 1 mark on signature writing. > 5. **Sign convention errors.** Positive vs. negative rake must be clearly marked. Exam scripts that just write "$5°$" without sign get only **partial credit** when the answer should be "$-5°$". > 6. **Not drawing the sketch.** Even in computational problems, KTU expects a labelled diagram of reference planes (worth ~2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts). > 7. **Unit mismatch on nose radius.** Always state **mm** explicitly after the numeric value. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tool Nomenclature** ### Reference Planes — Know All Six - [ ] **$P_r$** — Reference plane (horizontal) - [ ] **$P_f$** — Longitudinal plane - [ ] **$P_t$** — Transverse plane - [ ] **$P_c$** — Cutting plane (ASA primary) - [ ] **$P_n$** — Normal plane - [ ] **$P_o$** — Orthogonal plane (ISO primary) ### Angle Families — Memorize Symbol & Function - [ ] **Rake:** $\alpha_b, \alpha_s$ (ASA) / $\gamma_o, \gamma_o'$ (ISO) → chip-flow control - [ ] **Clearance:** $\alpha_e, \alpha_s'$ (ASA) / $\alpha_o, \alpha_o'$ (ISO) → friction prevention - [ ] **Edge Angles:** $C_e, C_s$ (ASA) / $K_r, K_r'$ (ISO) → geometry orientation - [ ] **Inclination:** $\lambda_s$ (ISO only) → chip-flow along edge - [ ] **Nose Radius:** $r$ / $r_\varepsilon$ → surface finish ### Key Identities — Must Recall - [ ] $C_e + C_s = 90°$ for a straight cutting edge (ASA) - [ ] $K_r + K_r' = 90°$ for a straight cutting edge (ISO) - [ ] $K_r = C_s$ and $K_r' = C_e$ (cross-system equivalence) ### Conversion Formulas — Internalize - [ ] $\tan \gamma_o = \tan \alpha_b \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s \sin C_e$ - [ ] $\tan \gamma_o' = \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e$ - [ ] $\tan \lambda_s = \tan \alpha_s \cos C_e - \tan \alpha_b \sin C_e$ - [ ] $\tan \alpha_o = \tan \alpha_e \cos C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \sin C_e$ - [ ] $\tan \alpha_o' = \tan \alpha_e \sin C_e + \tan \alpha_s' \cos C_e$ ### Common Tool Materials (Related to Nomenclature) - [ ] **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** — used for drills, taps, reamers - [ ] **Carbide (WC-Co)** — inserts for turning, milling - [ ] **Ceramics / CBN / PCD** — for high-speed / hard-material machining ### Practical Insight - [ ] **Positive rake** → softer materials, lower forces, sharper but weaker edge - [ ] **Negative rake** → harder materials, interrupted cuts, stronger edge - [ ] **Larger nose radius** → better finish but more chatter risk - [ ] **ISO is the modern standard** — always quote ISO angles in international contexts

topics.title: Attributes of Each Tool Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Attributes of Each Tool Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Nomenclature (ISO 1832 Standard)**: A standardized alphanumeric coding system used to unambiguously describe the geometric and functional attributes of indexable cutting inserts and their corresponding tool holders. Each position (character) in the code corresponds to a specific attribute such as insert shape, clearance angle, tolerance, mounting style, and dimensions.

The **attributes of tool nomenclature** refer to the **physical, geometric, and functional characteristics** that each coded character in the ISO tool designation string represents. These attributes are defined under **ISO 1832** (Cutting tools with indexable inserts — Designation of cutting tool holders and inserts) and the associated **ISO 513** standard for material classification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are ordering a customized coffee. You don't say *"give me coffee"* — you specify size (small/medium/large), type (espresso/cappuccino), sugar level, milk type, temperature, etc. Each specification describes an **attribute** of your coffee.

Similarly, a single cutting tool has **many attributes** (shape, angle, size, mounting style, hand of cut, etc.). Instead of describing them in long sentences, the industry uses a **compact alphanumeric code**. For example, the code `CNMG 120408` instantly tells a machinist that:
- The insert is **C**-shaped (80° rhombic),
- Has **N**egative rake,
- Has a **M**edium tolerance,
- Has chip-breaker geometry,
- Has a cutting edge length of **12 mm**,
- Thickness of **04 mm**,
- Corner radius of **0.8 mm**.

## 1.3 Why This System Exists in KTU 2024 Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes **industry-readiness** and **digital manufacturing literacy**. The tool nomenclature is the **universal language** spoken on the shop floor across the globe (India, Germany, Japan, USA). A student must learn to "read" a tool catalog the way a programmer reads code.

**Key Industrial Metrics (Bolded Highlights):**
- **ISO 1832** — Code structure standard
- **ISO 513** — Material classification standard
- **ANSI/ASME B94.4** — Equivalent US standard
- **DIN 4987** — German equivalent (legacy)
- **Clearance angle range:** **0° to 30°** (standard increments of 3°, 5°, 7°, 15°, 20°, 25°, 30°)

---

## 1.4 The Two Main Code Families

| Code Family | Used For | Typical Length | Example |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Insert Nomenclature** | Indexable cutting inserts | 7 characters | `CNMG 120408` |
| **Tool Holder Nomenclature** | Tool holders / shanks | 8–10 characters | `PCLNR 2525 M12` |
| **Milling Cutter Nomenclature** | Milling cutter bodies | 4–6 characters | `R215.69-3230` |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Indexable Insert Nomenclature (ISO 1832) — Position-by-Position

The insert code is a **7-position string**. Each position carries a specific attribute. Let us break down each position's "**why**" and "**how**."

### Position 1 — Insert Shape (Geometry)

The shape determines the **strength of the cutting edge** and the **maximum depth of cut** for which the insert is suited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Tip**: *"And Big Cats Don't Eat Herring On Plates, Rather Stick To Very Wet Rye"* — covers most standard shapes.

| Code | Shape | Included Angle | Edge Strength | Typical Use |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **A** | Parallelogram | 85° | Medium | Profiling |
| **B** | Parallelogram | 82° | Medium | Profiling |
| **C** | Rhombic | **80°** | Medium | Turning (most common) |
| **D** | Rhombic | 55° | Weak | Profiling / light cuts |
| **R** | Round | 360° | **Very Strong** | Heavy roughing |
| **S** | Square | 90° | Strong | Facing |
| **T** | Triangular | 60° | Strong | Turning, facing |
| **V** | Rhombic | 35° | Weak | Profiling |
| **W** | Trigon | 80° | Strong | Profiling |

### Position 2 — Clearance Angle (Primary Relief)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **clearance angle** is the angle between the insert's flank face and the workpiece surface. It **prevents rubbing** between the insert and the freshly machined surface.

| Code | Clearance Angle | Application |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **A** | 3° | Heavy cuts, low rigidity |
| **B** | 5° | General turning |
| **C** | 7° | Standard finishing |
| **D** | 15° | High clearance needed |
| **E** | 20° | Boring |
| **F** | 25° | Boring / fine work |
| **G** | 30° | Special profiling |

### Position 3 — Tolerance Class (Precision Grade)

This is the **most critical attribute** because it tells you the **dimensional precision** of the insert. It is defined by **three sub-attributes**:
1. **Tolerance on cutting edge length (d)**
2. **Tolerance on insert thickness (s)**
3. **Tolerance on corner radius (r)** (only for class "M" and "U")

| Code | Class | d (mm) | s (mm) | r (mm) | Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **A** | High precision | ±0.025 | ±0.025 | ±0.025 | Finishing, CNC |
| **F** | High precision | ±0.013 | ±0.025 | ±0.025 | Precision turning |
| **C** | Medium | ±0.025 | ±0.013 | ±0.025 | General |
| **J** | Medium | ±0.05 | ±0.025 | ±0.05 | General turning |
| **K** | Medium | ±0.05 | ±0.025 | – | Milling |
| **M** | Standard | ±0.08 | ±0.13 | ±0.05 | **Most common** |
| **U** | Standard | ±0.13 | ±0.13 | ±0.08 | Heavy machining |

### Position 4 — Insert Type (Features / Chipbreaker)

This indicates **whether the insert has a chipbreaker, hole, or special feature**.

| Code | Meaning |
|:---:|:---|
| **A** | With hole, with chipbreaker |
| **B** | With hole, without chipbreaker |
| **C** | Without hole, with chipbreaker |
| **G** | With hole, chipbreaker on both faces |
| **N** | Without hole, no chipbreaker |
| **R** | Without hole, no chipbreaker (roughing) |
| **F** | Without hole, no chipbreaker (finishing) |
| **M** | With hole, without chipbreaker (milling) |
| **Q** | With hole, with chipbreaker (milling) |
| **U** | With hole, without chipbreaker (milling, both faces) |
| **W** | With hole, with chipbreaker (one face) |

### Positions 5, 6, 7 — Dimensions

| Position | Attribute | Encoding |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **5** | **Cutting edge length (d)** | 2 digits in mm (e.g., 12 = 12 mm) |
| **6** | **Insert thickness (s)** | 2 digits in mm (e.g., 04 = 4 mm) |
| **7** | **Corner radius (r)** | 2 digits in 0.1 mm (e.g., 08 = 0.8 mm) |

A typical external turning tool holder has an **8-character code**.

### Position 1 — Type of Tool

### Position 2 — Mounting System

### Position 3 — Hand of Tool (Direction of Feed)

### Position 4 — Insert Shape (Same as insert nomenclature position 1)

### Position 5 — Clearance Angle (Same as insert nomenclature position 2)

### Position 6 — Tool Height (h) in mm

Encoded as **2 digits in mm**. For example, `25` means **25 mm** shank height.

### Position 7 — Tool Width (b) in mm

Encoded as **2 digits in mm**. For example, `25` means **25 mm** shank width.

### Position 8 — Tool Length (L)

| Code | Length (mm) |
|:---:|:---:|

| **F** | 80 |
| **G** | 90 |
| **H** | 100 |
| **J** | 110 |
| **K** | 125 |

| **M** | 150 |
| **N** | 160 |
| **P** | 170 |
| **Q** | 180 |
| **R** | 200 |
| **S** | 250 |
| **T** | 300 |
| **U** | 350 |
| **V** | 400 |

## 2.3 ISO 513 Material Classification (Workpiece Material)

This is often appended to inserts to indicate **which workpiece material** the insert is optimized for.

| Code | Material Group | Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **P** | **Steel** | Mild steel, alloy steel, tool steel |
| **M** | **Stainless Steel** | Austenitic SS, martensitic SS |
| **K** | **Cast Iron** | Grey CI, ductile CI |
| **N** | **Non-Ferrous** | Aluminum, copper, brass |
| **S** | **Super Alloys** | Inconel, titanium, hastelloy |
| **H** | **Hardened Steel** | > 45 HRC |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Maximum depth of cut ($a_p$) for a square insert | $a_p \le \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} - r$ | mm |
| Maximum depth of cut for a triangular insert | $a_p \le 0.4 \times d$ | mm |
| Number of effective teeth in cut | $z_e = \lfloor \frac{a_e}{D \cdot \sin(360^\circ / z)} \rfloor$ | dimensionless |
| Cutting edge angle of insert | $\kappa_r$ from shape | degrees |
| Cutting speed conversion | $V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min |
| Insert corner radius effect on $R_a$ | $R_a \propto \frac{f^2}{8 \cdot r_\varepsilon}$ | µm |
| Tolerance on cutting edge length | From Position 3 table | mm |
| Insert selection by chip load | $h_{ex} = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa_r)$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility**: A CNC operator at a Tata Motors plant in Pune looks at a tool code like `CNMG 120408-PM 4225` (Sandvik Coromant) and instantly knows: rhombic 80° insert, negative rake, medium tolerance, with chipbreaker, 12 mm edge, 4 mm thick, 0.8 mm corner radius, designed for **P** (steel) and **M** (stainless) materials. **This saves 15–20 minutes of tool catalog lookup per tool change.**
