# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between continuous, discontinuous, and built-up-edge chips with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Continuous Chip | Discontinuous Chip | Built-Up-Edge Chip |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Definition** | Long, unbroken ribbon-like chip | Series of separated segments | Continuous chip with a wedge of material welded to the tool tip |
| **Material** | Ductile (e.g., mild steel, aluminum) | Brittle (e.g., cast iron, bronze) | Ductile (e.g., low-carbon steel) |
| **Conditions** | High speed, sharp tool, positive rake, adequate coolant | Low speed, small/negative rake, sharp tool | Low-to-medium speed, insufficient coolant, dull tool |
| **Surface finish** | Good | Rough | Poor (BUE leaves marks) |
| **Tool life** | Long | Long | Short (BUE causes wear) |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a chip breaker? List the different types of chip breakers used in single-point cutting tools.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A chip breaker is a small step, groove, or projection provided on the rake face of a single-point cutting tool, close to the cutting edge, to curl the chip into a tight radius and fracture it into small segments for easy handling and operator safety.

**Types of chip breakers:**
1. **Groove-type chip breakers** — Stepped groove, Curved (hump) groove, Oblique groove.
2. **Projection-type (or land-type) chip breakers** — Parallel land, Stepped land.
3. **Pin-type chip breakers** — A pin inserted into the tool body (rare, used in older HSS tools).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain Merchant's circle force diagram for orthogonal metal cutting with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship between cutting force ($F_c$), thrust force ($F_t$), shear angle ($\phi$), and friction angle ($\beta$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an orthogonal cutting operation, the following data are given:
- Uncut chip thickness, $t_1$ = 0.30 mm
- Chip thickness, $t_2$ = 0.45 mm
- Tool rake angle, $\alpha$ = 10°
- Cutting velocity, $V$ = 100 m/min

Calculate:
(i) Cutting ratio ($r$)
(ii) Shear angle ($\phi$)
(iii) Shear velocity ($V_s$) **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Merchant's Force Model:**

In Merchant's analysis, the chip is considered as a free body in equilibrium under the action of **four forces**:
1. $F_s$ — shear force along the shear plane,
2. $F_n$ — normal force perpendicular to the shear plane,
3. $F$ — frictional force along the tool face (rake face),
4. $N$ — normal force perpendicular to the tool face.

These four forces are balanced and form a closed quadrilateral (Merchant's force circle). The cutting force $F_c$ (along cutting velocity) and thrust force $F_t$ (perpendicular to $F_c$) are the **resultant components** of these four forces.

**Force balance along shear plane direction:**

$$F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Force balance perpendicular to shear plane:**

$$F_n = F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Force balance along tool face (friction):**

$$F = F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Force balance perpendicular to tool face:**

$$N = F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Friction angle** is defined as:

$$\tan\beta = \frac{F}{N} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Dividing equation (iii) by (iv):**

$$\tan\beta = \frac{F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha}{F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha}$$

Taking the force ratio $R = F_t / F_c$:

$$\tan\beta = \frac{\tan\alpha + R}{1 - R \tan\alpha} = \tan(\alpha + \arctan R)$$

This gives the key geometric relationship:

$$\beta = \alpha + \arctan\left(\frac{F_t}{F_c}\right)$$

From the force quadrilateral geometry, the angle between $F_c$ and $F_s$ is $\phi$, and the angle between $F_c$ and $F$ is $(\alpha + \beta)$. Using vector closure:

$$2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$$

This is **Merchant's fundamental equation**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given Data:**
- $t_1$ = 0.30 mm, $t_2$ = 0.45 mm, $\alpha$ = 10°, $V$ = 100 m/min

**(i) Cutting Ratio ($r$):**

$$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.30}{0.45} = 0.6667$$

**(ii) Shear Angle ($\phi$):**

Using the formula:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}$$

Substituting $r = 0.6667$, $\alpha = 10°$:

$$\cos 10° = 0.9848, \quad \sin 10° = 0.1736$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{0.6667 \times 0.9848}{1 - 0.6667 \times 0.1736}$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{0.6565}{1 - 0.1157} = \frac{0.6565}{0.8843} = 0.7424$$

$$\phi = \arctan(0.7424) = 36.59°$$

**(iii) Shear Velocity ($V_s$):**

$$V_s = \frac{V \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{100 \times \cos 10°}{\cos(36.59° - 10°)}$$

$$V_s = \frac{100 \times 0.9848}{\cos 26.59°} = \frac{98.48}{0.8946} = 110.08 \text{ m/min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $r = 0.667$
- (ii) $\phi = 36.59°$
- (iii) $V_s = 110.08$ m/min

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What are the different types of chips produced during metal cutting? Explain the conditions under which each type of chip is produced with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between shear angle ($\phi$), cutting ratio ($r$), and tool rake angle ($\alpha$) in orthogonal cutting. A turning operation has a cutting ratio of 0.6 and a tool rake angle of 15°. Find the shear angle. If the cutting velocity is 120 m/min, find the shear velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The three principal types of chips produced in metal cutting are:

**1. Continuous Chip:**
- Produced when machining **ductile materials** (e.g., mild steel, aluminum, copper).
- Conditions: **high cutting speed**, **sharp cutting edge**, **large positive rake angle**, and **adequate supply of cutting fluid**.
- The chip flows continuously along the rake face as a long, curled ribbon. The shear deformation occurs in a thin, narrow primary zone.
- The surface finish is good, and tool life is high.

**2. Discontinuous (Segmented) Chip:**
- Produced when machining **brittle materials** (e.g., cast iron, bronze) or when using **small or negative rake angles** at **low cutting speeds**.
- The chip consists of a series of small, separate segments, each formed by a fracture along the shear plane.
- Tool life is high (because chips don't rub on the tool), but the surface finish is poor and cutting is noisy.

**3. Continuous Chip with Built-Up Edge (BUE):**
- Produced when machining **ductile materials at low-to-medium cutting speeds** with **insufficient cutting fluid** and/or a **dull tool**.
- A small wedge of workpiece material (the built-up edge) gets welded to the tool tip due to high pressure and temperature at the chip-tool interface.
- The BUE periodically breaks off and embeds into the freshly machined surface, causing poor surface finish and accelerated tool wear.

(Sketches of each chip type should be drawn showing the chip shape and the primary shear zone — refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram for classification logic.)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Derivation:**

From the geometric construction of the cutting zone (a right triangle with hypotenuse as the shear plane):

The chip thickness $t_2$ and uncut chip thickness $t_1$ are related to the shear plane length $l_s$ by:

$$t_1 = l_s \sin\phi$$
$$t_2 = l_s \cos(\phi - \alpha)$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$

Since $r = t_1 / t_2$:

$$r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos\phi$:

$$r = \frac{\tan\phi}{\cos\alpha + \sin\alpha \tan\phi}$$

Solving for $\tan\phi$:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $r = 0.6$, $\alpha = 15°$, $V = 120$ m/min

$$\cos 15° = 0.9659, \quad \sin 15° = 0.2588$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{0.6 \times 0.9659}{1 - 0.6 \times 0.2588} = \frac{0.5795}{1 - 0.1553} = \frac{0.5795}{0.8447} = 0.6860$$

$$\phi = \arctan(0.6860) = 34.46°$$

**Shear Velocity:**

$$V_s = \frac{V \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{120 \times 0.9659}{\cos(34.46° - 15°)}$$

$$V_s = \frac{115.91}{\cos 19.46°} = \frac{115.91}{0.9428} = 122.94 \text{ m/min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\phi = 34.46°$
- $V_s = 122.94$ m/min

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Top 5):**
> 1. **Sign error in Merchant's equation** — Most students write $2\phi + \alpha - \beta = 90°$ (wrong). The correct form is $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$.
> 2. **Confusing $t_1$ and $t_2$** — Uncut chip thickness $t_1$ < Chip thickness $t_2$ (always). Reversing this gives $r > 1$ (impossible).
> 3. **Forgetting degree-radian conversion** — Most trigonometric functions in calculators default to degrees; ensure the calculator is in DEG mode for KTU problems.
> 4. **Skipping units in $V$** — Cutting velocity in m/min must be converted to m/s for power calculation ($P_c = F_c \times V$), or the answer will be off by 60×.
> 5. **Not showing force diagrams** — In Part B, a neat force diagram is worth 2 marks by itself. Skipping it costs at least 2 marks even if the derivation is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before KTU Exam):**

- [ ] **Three types of chips:** Continuous (ductile, high speed), Discontinuous (brittle, low speed), BUE (ductile, low speed, dull tool, no coolant).
- [ ] **Chip breaker purpose:** Curl and break chip into small pieces; prevent entanglement; improve safety.
- [ ] **Three chip breaker types:** Groove-type, Projection-type, Pin-type.
- [ ] **Chip breaker design rule:** Width $w$ > chip thickness $t_2$; ratio $w/t_2 = 5$ to $8$.
- [ ] **Merchant's equation:** $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ — **THE most important equation in the module.**
- [ ] **Cutting ratio:** $r = t_1 / t_2 < 1$ always.
- [ ] **Shear angle formula:** $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r\cos\alpha}{1 - r\sin\alpha}$.
- [ ] **Shear velocity formula:** $V_s = \dfrac{V\cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$.
- [ ] **Four forces on chip:** $F_s, F_n$ (on shear plane); $F, N$ (on tool face).
- [ ] **Two measured forces:** $F_c$ (cutting) and $F_t$ (thrust/feed).
- [ ] **Friction angle definition:** $\tan\beta = F/N$.
- [ ] **Cutting power formula:** $P_c = F_c \times V$ (note unit conversion).
- [ ] **Merchant's theory assumption:** Single shear plane; orthogonal cutting (2D); continuous chip formation; tool is sharp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Merchant's equation $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ has been asked in **almost every KTU end-semester paper** for the last 5 years. Memorize it, and you secure at least 2–3 marks guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Cutting Forces (Simple Problems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Cutting Forces (Simple Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Force Analysis** in metal cutting is the systematic study and mathematical evaluation of the forces generated at the tool–chip interface during machining operations. It employs the **Orthogonal Cutting Model (2-D)** to resolve the resultant cutting force ($F$) into its principal components — the **Primary/Tangential Cutting Force ($F_c$)**, the **Thrust/Feed Force ($F_t$)**, and the **Radial Force ($F_s$)** — along with the forces acting on the **Shear Plane (Shear Force $F_s'$ and Normal Force $F_n'$)** and the **Tool–Chip Interface (Friction Force $F_f$ and Normal Force $N$)**.

The analytical approach uses **Merchant's Circle Diagram** to geometrically and trigonometrically relate these forces, the **shear angle ($\phi$)**, the **rake angle ($\alpha$)**, and the **friction angle ($\beta$)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine using a **wood plane (hand tool)** on a wooden plank:

- You **push the plane forward** along the length of the wood → this is the **Cutting Force ($F_c$)**, the dominant force needed to slice off the chip.
- The plane **tries to lift away from the wood** due to the wood pushing back → this is the **Thrust Force ($F_t$)**, directed perpendicular to the cutting velocity.
- Inside the wood, the wood fibres are **shearing along an inclined plane** (much shorter and tilted compared to the tool face) → this is the **Shear Plane**, where most of the energy is consumed.

> [!NOTE]
> Just like a gardener cutting a hedge with shears experiences resistance from two angles — a forward push and an upward lift — the cutting tool experiences a similar two-component force system. **Merchant's Circle** is simply the graphical accountant that keeps track of all these forces in equilibrium!

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Rake Angle ($\alpha$):** $+15°$ (typical HSS tool, steel work)
- **Friction Coefficient ($\mu$):** **0.4 to 0.6** (typical for steel–HSS pair)
- **Shear Strength of Work Material ($\tau_s$):** **200 to 500 N/mm²** (mild steel)
- **Specific Cutting Energy ($U$):** **1.5 to 3.5 W·s/mm³** for steel
- **Chip Thickness Ratio ($r$):** **0.3 to 0.6** (compression ratio, always < 1)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Orthogonal Cutting Model — Setup

In the **orthogonal cutting model**:
- The cutting edge is **perpendicular** to the cutting velocity vector.
- Tool is **wide enough** so that end-edge effects are negligible.
- Only **2-D analysis** is needed (forces in the plane of cutting velocity and feed).

## 2.2 The Three Force Triangles (Merchant's Circle)

Merchant's circle contains **three interconnected force triangles** sharing the same resultant force $R$:

### Triangle 1: Horizontal–Vertical (Cutting & Thrust Plane)
- $F_c$ = horizontal cutting force (along velocity)
- $F_t$ = vertical thrust force (perpendicular to velocity)
- $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2}$

### Triangle 2: Shear Plane Forces
- $F_s$ = shear force (along shear plane)
- $F_n$ = normal force on shear plane
- $R = \sqrt{F_s^2 + F_n^2}$

### Triangle 3: Tool–Chip Interface (Friction Plane)
- $F$ = force normal to tool face
- $N$ = friction force along tool face
- $R = \sqrt{F^2 + N^2}$, and $\tan \beta = \mu = \dfrac{F}{N}$
- $\beta$ = friction angle

## 2.3 Why Merchant's Circle Works

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium Principle:** Since the chip is in dynamic equilibrium as it slides up the rake face, the **resultant force $R$** must be identical in magnitude and direction whether computed from the horizontal-vertical axes, the shear-plane axes, or the friction-plane axes. This gives us **3 triangles sharing one resultant $R$**, allowing complete force analysis from minimal input data.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is essential for every KTU problem on cutting forces.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ | Chip thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_s = F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi$ | Shear force | N |
| 3 | $F_n = F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi$ | Normal force on shear plane | N |
| 4 | $F = F_c \cos \beta \cos \alpha - F_t \sin \alpha$? | No — see below | N |
| 5 | $F = F_c \cos \alpha + F_t \sin \alpha$ | Force normal to tool face (alternate) | N |
| 6 | $N = F_c \sin \alpha + F_t \cos \alpha$? | No — see below | N |
| 7 | $N = F_c \sin \alpha - F_t \cos \alpha$ | Friction force along tool face | N |
| 8 | $\tan \phi = \dfrac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha}$ | **Merchant's Shear Angle Eq.** | rad/° |
| 9 | $\tan \beta = \mu$ | Friction angle definition | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{F_s}{A_s} = \dfrac{F_s}{b \cdot t_1 / \sin \phi}$ | Shear strength | N/mm² |
| 11 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V$ | Cutting power | W (Watts) |
| 12 | $A_s = \dfrac{b \cdot t_1}{\sin \phi}$ | Shear plane area | mm² |
| 13 | $\mu = \tan \beta$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| 14 | $F_f = \mu N$ | Friction force on tool face | N |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Signs:** Conventionally, $F_t$ is taken **positive when directed away from the workpiece** (pushes tool upward). If the actual thrust pushes the tool **into** the workpiece (negative rake effect), $F_t$ is negative — students often lose marks here.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Tool Design:** Determines the rigidity, power, and bearing sizing of lathes, mills, and drills.
- **Tool Life Prediction:** Higher $F_c$ → higher temperature → faster tool wear (Taylor's equation).
- **Surface Integrity:** Thrust force $F_t$ causes tool deflection → affects dimensional accuracy and chatter.
- **Energy Audit:** Specific cutting energy directly impacts factory electricity consumption bills.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shear Force $F_s$ from $F_c$ and $F_t$

**Starting Geometry:** Consider the lower force triangle of Merchant's Circle. The cutting force $F_c$ and thrust force $F_t$ are resolved along and perpendicular to the shear plane inclined at angle $\phi$.

**Step 1:** Project $F_c$ onto the shear-plane direction.
The angle between $F_c$ (horizontal) and the shear plane is $(90° - \phi)$. Therefore the component of $F_c$ along the shear plane is:

$$
F_c \cdot \cos \phi
$$

**Step 2:** Project $F_t$ onto the shear-plane direction.
The thrust force $F_t$ is vertical. The angle between $F_t$ and the shear plane is $\phi$. The component of $F_t$ along the shear plane is:

$$
-F_t \cdot \sin \phi
$$

(The negative sign appears because $F_t$ opposes the shear direction.)

**Step 3:** Add the two components to get the shear force $F_s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Normal Force on Shear Plane $F_n$

**Step 1:** Component of $F_c$ perpendicular to shear plane:

$$
F_c \sin \phi
$$

**Step 2:** Component of $F_t$ perpendicular to shear plane (both add):

$$
F_t \cos \phi
$$

**Step 3:** Total normal force on shear plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

**Given:**
- Chip thickness ratio $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$
- Rake angle $\alpha$
- Shear angle $\phi$

**Geometric Setup:** From the chip geometry, the uncut chip thickness $t_1$ and chip thickness $t_2$ are related through the shear angle and rake angle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \\
r \cos(\phi - \alpha) &= \sin \phi \\
r [\cos \phi \cos \alpha + \sin \phi \sin \alpha] &= \sin \phi \\
r \cos \phi \cos \alpha &= \sin \phi (1 - r \sin \alpha) \\
\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi} &= \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Shear Strength from Cutting Forces

The shear plane is an **inclined plane** of area $A_s$. The geometry gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{b \cdot t_1}{\sin \phi} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

(where $b$ = width of cut, $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness)

The shear force acts along this plane, so the average shear stress (shear strength) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_s &= \frac{F_s}{A_s} = \frac{F_s \cdot \sin \phi}{b \cdot t_1} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Simple Problem (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Style Numerical:** Solve using Merchant's Circle relationships. Always show the triangle diagram and verify dimensions.

### Problem Statement

In an orthogonal cutting operation on mild steel with a cutting tool of rake angle $\alpha = 10°$, the following data is observed:
- Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.30$ mm
- Chip thickness $t_2 = 0.55$ mm
- Cutting velocity $V = 120$ m/min
- Width of cut $b = 3.0$ mm
- Cutting force $F_c = 1100$ N
- Thrust force $F_t = 450$ N

**Determine:**
1. Shear angle $\phi$
2. Shear force $F_s$
3. Normal force on shear plane $F_n$
4. Coefficient of friction $\mu$
5. Cutting power $P_c$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute chip thickness ratio $r$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.30}{0.55} = 0.5455 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute shear angle using Merchant's Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \phi &= \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5455 \times \cos 10°}{1 - 0.5455 \times \sin 10°} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5455 \times 0.9848}{1 - 0.5455 \times 0.1736} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5373}{1 - 0.0947} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5373}{0.9053} \\
\tan \phi &= 0.5935 \\
\phi &= \tan^{-1}(0.5935) \\
\phi &= 30.68° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute shear force $F_s$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi \\
F_s &= 1100 \cos(30.68°) - 450 \sin(30.68°) \\
F_s &= 1100 \times 0.8597 - 450 \times 0.5108 \\
F_s &= 945.7 - 229.9 \\
F_s &= 715.8 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute normal force on shear plane $F_n$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi \\
F_n &= 1100 \sin(30.68°) + 450 \cos(30.68°) \\
F_n &= 1100 \times 0.5108 + 450 \times 0.8597 \\
F_n &= 561.9 + 386.9 \\
F_n &= 948.8 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute coefficient of friction $\mu$**

First find force normal to tool face ($F$) and friction force ($N$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= F_c \cos \alpha + F_t \sin \alpha \\
F &= 1100 \cos(10°) + 450 \sin(10°) \\
F &= 1100 \times 0.9848 + 450 \times 0.1736 \\
F &= 1083.3 + 78.1 \\
F &= 1161.4 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= F_c \sin \alpha - F_t \cos \alpha \\
N &= 1100 \sin(10°) - 450 \cos(10°) \\
N &= 1100 \times 0.1736 - 450 \times 0.9848 \\
N &= 190.96 - 443.16 \\
N &= -252.2 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Trap for KTU Valuation:** A negative $N$ here physically means the tool face contact is in a **different regime** (perhaps chip is not in full contact). Most textbooks take $N = \vert -252.2 \vert = 252.2$ N for computing friction. Always state this assumption!

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{N}{F} = \frac{252.2}{1161.4} = 0.2172 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Cutting Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 120 \text{ m/min} = 2.0 \text{ m/s} \\
P_c &= F_c \cdot V = 1100 \times 2.0 = 2200 \text{ W} = 2.2 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Tabulation

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Chip thickness ratio $r$ | 0.5455 |
| Shear angle $\phi$ | 30.68° |
| Shear force $F_s$ | 715.8 N |
| Normal force $F_n$ | 948.8 N |
| Friction coefficient $\mu$ | 0.217 |
| Cutting Power $P_c$ | 2.2 kW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Merchant's Circle Diagram — Force Triangle Architecture

The following Mermaid flow block represents the **functional architecture of Merchant's Circle** — the three force triangles that share the same resultant $R$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WORKPIECE AND CHIP SYSTEM AT CUTTING ZONE"] --> B["THREE FORCE RESOLUTION AXES"]
    
    B --> C["TRIANGLE 1: HORIZONTAL VERTICAL PLANE"]
    B --> D["TRIANGLE 2: SHEAR PLANE INCLINED AT PHI"]
    B --> E["TRIANGLE 3: TOOL FACE PLANE AT FRICTION ANGLE BETA"]
    
    C --> C1["Fc = Cutting Force along V"]
    C --> C2["Ft = Thrust Force perpendicular to V"]
    C --> C3["R = Resultant sqrt Fc sq plus Ft sq"]
    
    D --> D1["Fs = Shear Force along shear plane"]
    D --> D2["Fn = Normal Force on shear plane"]
    D --> D3["R is common to all three triangles"]
    
    E --> E1["N = Friction Force along tool face"]
    E --> E2["F = Normal Force on tool face"]
    E --> E3["tan beta = mu = N over F"]
    
    C3 --> RSHARED["COMMON RESULTANT R"]
    D3 --> RSHARED
    E3 --> RSHARED
    
    RSHARED --> OUT["FORCE ANALYSIS OUTPUT"]
    
    RSHARED --> OUT1["Shear Stress tau s"]
    RSHARED --> OUT2["Friction Coefficient mu"]
    RSHARED --> OUT3["Cutting Power Pc"]
    RSHARED --> OUT4["Tool Life Estimation"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Cutting Force Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["INPUT DATA"] --> P1["Uncut Thickness t1"]
    INPUT --> P2["Chip Thickness t2"]
    INPUT --> P3["Rake Angle alpha"]
    INPUT --> P4["Cutting Force Fc"]
    INPUT --> P5["Thrust Force Ft"]
    
    P1 --> CALC1["Compute r = t1 over t2"]
    P2 --> CALC1
    
    CALC1 --> CALC2["Compute phi via Merchant Eq"]
    P3 --> CALC2
    
    CALC2 --> CALC3["Compute Fs and Fn on shear plane"]
    P4 --> CALC3
    P5 --> CALC3
    
    CALC3 --> CALC4["Compute F and N on tool face"]
    P4 --> CALC4
    P5 --> CALC4
    
    CALC4 --> CALC5["Compute mu = N over F"]
    
    CALC3 --> OUT1["Shear Stress"]
    CALC5 --> OUT2["Friction Coefficient"]
    P4 --> OUT3["Cutting Power"]
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CUTTING_REGION["MACHINING ZONE"]
        TOOL["CUTTING TOOL WITH RAKE ANGLE ALPHA"]
        WORK["WORKPIECE MOVING AT VELOCITY V"]
        CHIP["CHIP FLOWING UP RAKE FACE"]
    end
    
    FC["Fc Horizontal Cutting Force"] --> WORK
    FT["Ft Vertical Thrust Force"] --> WORK
    FS["Fs Shear Force on Shear Plane at phi"] --> CHIP
    FN["Fn Normal Force on Shear Plane"] --> CHIP
    NFRIC["N Friction Force on Rake Face"] --> CHIP
    NFORCE["F Normal Force on Rake Face"] --> CHIP
    
    CHIP --> MERC["MERCHANT CIRCLE EQUILIBRIUM"]
    WORK --> MERC
    MERC --> RESULT["RESOLVED FORCES AND POWER"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the following terms in orthogonal cutting: (i) Shear angle, (ii) Rake angle, (iii) Chip thickness ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Shear Angle ($\phi$):** It is the angle made by the shear plane with the cutting velocity vector (or horizontal plane). It represents the inclination of the plane along which the work material shears to form a chip.

**(ii) Rake Angle ($\alpha$):** It is the angle between the tool face (rake face) and a reference plane measured in the plane of orthogonal cutting. Positive rake reduces cutting force and power; negative rake increases strength of tool.

**(iii) Chip Thickness Ratio ($r$):** Defined as $r = t_1 / t_2$, the ratio of uncut chip thickness to chip thickness. Always less than unity, indicating chip thickening due to compression.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: State and explain Merchant's circle diagram for orthogonal cutting.**

**Model Answer:**

Merchant's Circle Diagram is a graphical representation that resolves the resultant cutting force $R$ into components on **three different planes** (horizontal–vertical, shear plane, tool face), all in equilibrium. The diagram consists of three interconnected force triangles sharing the common resultant $R$.

The lower triangle gives $F_c$ and $F_t$ (measured values), the left triangle gives shear-plane forces $F_s$ and $F_n$, and the upper triangle gives tool-face forces $F$ and $N$. The geometric angles between triangles are the rake angle $\alpha$ and the shear angle $\phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Use:** From any two measured quantities (e.g., $F_c$, $F_t$, and $\alpha$), all other unknown forces and angles can be determined graphically or trigonometrically.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

In an orthogonal cutting test on mild steel with a HSS tool having rake angle $\alpha = 15°$, the following data is obtained:
- Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.25$ mm
- Chip thickness $t_2 = 0.45$ mm
- Cutting velocity $V = 90$ m/min
- Width of cut $b = 2.5$ mm
- Cutting force $F_c = 950$ N
- Thrust force $F_t = 400$ N

**(a)** Calculate the shear angle, shear force, and normal force on the shear plane. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the coefficient of friction at the tool–chip interface and the cutting power consumed. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Chip thickness ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.25}{0.45} = 0.5556 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Shear angle (Merchant's Equation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \phi &= \frac{r \cos \alpha}{1 - r \sin \alpha} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5556 \times \cos 15°}{1 - 0.5556 \times \sin 15°} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5556 \times 0.9659}{1 - 0.5556 \times 0.2588} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5366}{1 - 0.1438} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5366}{0.8562} \\
\tan \phi &= 0.6267 \\
\phi &= 32.07° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Shear force $F_s$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= F_c \cos \phi - F_t \sin \phi \\
F_s &= 950 \cos(32.07°) - 400 \sin(32.07°) \\
F_s &= 950 \times 0.8472 - 400 \times 0.5312 \\
F_s &= 804.8 - 212.5 \\
F_s &= 592.3 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Normal force $F_n$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_n &= F_c \sin \phi + F_t \cos \phi \\
F_n &= 950 \times 0.5312 + 400 \times 0.8472 \\
F_n &= 504.6 + 338.9 \\
F_n &= 843.5 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 5: Force normal to tool face $F$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= F_c \cos \alpha + F_t \sin \alpha \\
F &= 950 \cos(15°) + 400 \sin(15°) \\
F &= 950 \times 0.9659 + 400 \times 0.2588 \\
F &= 917.6 + 103.5 \\
F &= 1021.1 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Friction force along tool face $N$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= F_c \sin \alpha - F_t \cos \alpha \\
N &= 950 \times 0.2588 - 400 \times 0.9659 \\
N &= 245.9 - 386.4 \\
N &= -140.5 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking magnitude: $\vert N \vert = 140.5$ N.

**Step 7: Coefficient of friction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &= \frac{\vert N \vert}{F} = \frac{140.5}{1021.1} = 0.1376 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Cutting power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 90 \text{ m/min} = 1.5 \text{ m/s} \\
P_c &= F_c \times V = 950 \times 1.5 = 1425 \text{ W} \approx 1.43 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Results — Question A

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Shear angle $\phi$ | 32.07° |
| Shear force $F_s$ | 592.3 N |
| Normal force $F_n$ | 843.5 N |
| Coefficient of friction $\mu$ | 0.1376 |
| Cutting power $P_c$ | 1.43 kW |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative — KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

During orthogonal cutting of aluminium with a tool of rake angle $\alpha = 20°$, the following measurements were recorded:
- Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.40$ mm
- Chip thickness $t_2 = 0.65$ mm
- Cutting velocity $V = 150$ m/min
- Width of cut $b = 3.5$ mm
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.30$
- Friction angle $\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.30) = 16.7°$

**(a)** Compute the shear angle and the resultant cutting force $R$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the cutting force $F_c$ is observed to be 750 N, determine the thrust force $F_t$ and the power consumed. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Chip thickness ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \frac{0.40}{0.65} = 0.6154 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Shear angle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.6154 \times \cos 20°}{1 - 0.6154 \times \sin 20°} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.6154 \times 0.9397}{1 - 0.6154 \times 0.3420} \\
\tan \phi &= \frac{0.5783}{0.7895} \\
\tan \phi &= 0.7326 \\
\phi &= 36.21° \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Resultant force using Merchant's relation**

From Merchant's geometric relation, using $\beta = 16.7°$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{F_c}{\cos(\beta - \alpha)} \cdot \frac{\text{(using derived geometry)}}{}\\
\end{aligned}
$$

Direct geometric derivation: from the lower triangle $F_c = R \cos \theta$ where $\theta = (\phi + \beta - \alpha)$. Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \phi + \beta - \alpha = 36.21° + 16.7° - 20° = 32.91° \\
F_c &= R \cos \theta \\
R &= \frac{750}{\cos 32.91°} \\
R &= \frac{750}{0.8392} \\
R &= 893.7 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 4: Thrust force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= R \sin \theta \\
F_t &= 893.7 \times \sin 32.91° \\
F_t &= 893.7 \times 0.5438 \\
F_t &= 486.0 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Cutting power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 150 \text{ m/min} = 2.5 \text{ m/s} \\
P_c &= F_c \cdot V = 750 \times 2.5 = 1875 \text{ W} = 1.875 \text{ kW} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Results — Question B

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Shear angle $\phi$ | 36.21° |
| Resultant force $R$ | 893.7 N |
| Thrust force $F_t$ | 486.0 N |
| Cutting power $P_c$ | 1.875 kW |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cutting Force Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign of $F_t$**: When thrust force pushes the tool *into* the workpiece (negative rake or large $\alpha$), $N$ becomes **negative**. Students forget to take absolute value and get wrong $\mu$.
> 2. **Units of Velocity**: Forgetting to convert m/min → m/s when computing power. Always: $V \text{ (m/s)} = V \text{ (m/min)} / 60$.
> 3. **Merchant's Equation Argument**: Using $1 - r\sin\alpha$ but mistakenly using $\cos$ instead of $\sin$. Revise the trigonometric expansion $r\cos(\phi - \alpha) = \sin\phi$.
> 4. **Resultant $R$**: Many students confuse $R$ with $F_c$. Recall: $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2}$ is always greater than $F_c$.
> 5. **Forgetting $\phi + \beta - \alpha$ formula**: This is a one-line shortcut for finding $R$ from $F_c$ without computing $F_s$ and $F_n$ separately.
> 6. **Cutting Power Definition**: Only $F_c \cdot V$ gives cutting power — not $F_t \cdot V$ (thrust does no work).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Merchant's Circle** = 3 force triangles sharing one resultant $R$.
- ✅ **Merchant's Shear Angle Equation**: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}$.
- ✅ **Shear Force**: $F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi$.
- ✅ **Normal Force on Shear Plane**: $F_n = F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi$.
- ✅ **Force Normal to Tool Face**: $F = F_c \cos\alpha + F_t \sin\alpha$.
- ✅ **Friction Force on Tool Face**: $N = F_c \sin\alpha - F_t \cos\alpha$ (take magnitude if negative).
- ✅ **Friction Coefficient**: $\mu = \dfrac{\vert N \vert}{F}$.
- ✅ **Resultant**: $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2} = \dfrac{F_c}{\cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)}$.
- ✅ **Chip Thickness Ratio**: $r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} < 1$ (always).
- ✅ **Cutting Power**: $P_c = F_c \cdot V$ (only $F_c$ does work; $F_t$ is perpendicular to motion).
- ✅ **Quick Check**: Higher $\alpha$ → lower $\phi$ → lower $F_c$ → lower power consumption.
- ✅ **Shear Strength**: $\tau_s = \dfrac{F_s \sin\phi}{b \cdot t_1}$.
- ✅ **Assumption**: Tool-chip contact is full on rake face; cutting edge is perfectly sharp.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Done and Power Required. Cutting Tool Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Work Done and Power Required. Cutting Tool Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Work Done and Power Required in Metal Cutting

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
In orthogonal metal cutting, the **Work Done** during a machining operation is defined as the energy expended by the cutting tool to remove a unit volume of material from the workpiece, primarily overcoming the shear deformation of the metal in the primary shear zone and the friction at the tool-chip and tool-workpiece interfaces. **Power Required** is the rate at which this work is performed, mathematically expressed as the product of the tangential (cutting) force and the cutting velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Focus:** Examiners consistently test whether students can correctly identify the **three orthogonal force components** — $F_c$ (cutting/tangential), $F_f$ (feed/axial), and $F_p$ (radial/thrust) — and link them to the corresponding velocities. Marks are heavily penalized if the wrong force-velocity pair is multiplied.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Bread Knife Model:**
Imagine slicing a loaf of dense, fresh bread with a serrated knife. The harder you push *forward* (cutting velocity), the more *resistance* the loaf offers. The energy you burn is the **work done**, while the speed at which your hand tires is the **power required**. A sharper knife (superior tool material) reduces the resistance, just as a tougher knife edge (hot hardness) survives the heat of repeated slicing. The crumbs flying off represent the chip — and the energy that went into creating them is exactly the work you have to measure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Truth:** In turning, milling, and drilling, the **cutting velocity $V$ is always paired with the cutting force $F_c$** to compute instantaneous mechanical power. The feed velocity $V_f$ is paired with $F_f$, but its contribution to total power is usually negligible (typically $< 2\%$).

### 1.2 Cutting Tool Materials — Syllabus Definition

**Formal Definition:**
A **cutting tool material** is an engineering material specifically engineered to retain its hardness, wear resistance, and mechanical integrity at elevated temperatures generated during chip formation, enabling it to shear off metal from the workpiece economically and repeatedly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Mandatory Properties of a Cutting Tool Material (KTU Hot Topic):**
> 1. **Hot Hardness (Red Hardness):** Ability to retain hardness at cutting temperatures (often $> 800°\text{C}$).
> 2. **Toughness:** Resistance to fracture and chipping under mechanical shock.
> 3. **Wear Resistance:** Resistance to abrasion, diffusion, and chemical wear at the tool tip.

**Real-World Intuition:**
Think of cutting tool materials as a "performance pyramid." Plain carbon steel sits at the base — cheap, tough, but softens quickly when hot. As you climb the pyramid, materials become harder, more wear-resistant, and able to cut faster — but also more brittle and expensive. HSS is the workhorse, carbides are the industrial champions, and ceramics/CBN/diamond are the elite specialists for high-speed, high-precision, and exotic-alloy machining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cutting Speed Benchmark Values (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - Plain Carbon Steel: **$V \approx 10\text{–}20\ \text{m/min}$
> - HSS: $V \approx 30\text{–}60\ \text{m/min}$
> - Carbides: $V \approx 100\text{–}300\ \text{m/min}$
> - Ceramics: $V \approx 300\text{–}800\ \text{m/min}$
> - CBN/PCD: $V > 800\ \text{m/min}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Force System in Orthogonal Cutting (Theory Breakdown)

The single-point cutting tool experiences a three-dimensional force system resolved into three orthogonal components:

- **$F_c$ — Cutting Force (Tangential):** Acts in the direction of cutting velocity $V$. This is the **largest** force component and is responsible for **$99\%$ of the total power consumption**.
- **$F_f$ — Feed Force (Axial/Longitudinal):** Acts in the direction of feed motion. Opposes the feed drive.
- **$F_p$ — Thrust/Radial Force (Passive):** Acts perpendicular to the machined surface, pushing the workpiece away from the tool.

The **Resultant Force $R$** on the tool tip is:

$$R = \sqrt{F_c^{2} + F_f^{2} + F_p^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Force Ratios (Boulton & Cook-Type Observations):**
> - $F_p \approx 0.5\ F_c$ (for steel)
> - $F_f \approx 0.3\text{–}0.4\ F_c$ (for steel)
> These ratios are *not* universal but are excellent for KTU sanity checks.

### 2.2 Decomposition into Shear-Plane and Friction-Plane Forces

The cutting force $F_c$ and feed force $F_f$ can be resolved into:

- **Shear Force $F_s$** acting on the shear plane (in the direction of chip flow).
- **Friction Force $F$** acting on the tool rake face (along the tool-chip interface).

$$F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_f \sin\phi$$

$$F_n = F_c \sin\phi + F_f \cos\phi$$

Where $\phi$ is the **shear angle** and $F_n$ is the **normal force on the shear plane**.

Similarly, on the rake face:

$$F = F_p \sin\beta + F_f \cos\beta$$

$$N = F_p \cos\beta - F_f \sin\beta$$

Where $\beta$ is the **friction angle** and $N$ is the **normal force on the tool face**.

### 2.3 Work Done and Power Required — Logic Flow

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Volume of metal removed per second (MRR — Material Removal Rate):**
   $\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d$ (for turning), where $f$ is feed and $d$ is depth of cut.

2. **Work done to shear the metal:** The energy goes primarily into plastic deformation in the primary shear zone.
   - Work done per second = Power = $F_c \cdot V$

3. **Specific Cutting Energy $U_s$:** Energy consumed per unit volume of metal removed.
   - $U_s = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{V \cdot f \cdot d} = \dfrac{F_c}{f \cdot d}$

4. **Average Shear Stress $\tau_s$** on the shear plane:
   - Shear area $A_s = \dfrac{t_1 \cdot b}{\sin\phi}$ (where $t_1$ is uncut chip thickness, $b$ is width of cut).
   - $\tau_s = \dfrac{F_s}{A_s}$

5. **Frictional Stress on Rake Face:**
   - Tool-chip contact area $A_c = l_c \cdot b$, where $l_c$ is contact length.
   - Average normal stress $\sigma_n = N / A_c$
   - Average frictional stress $\tau_f = F / A_c$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\mid$ or \vert for absolute value, never the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a markdown table row.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Resultant Cutting Force $R$ | $R = \sqrt{F_c^{2} + F_f^{2} + F_p^{2}}$ | N (Newton) |
| Material Removal Rate (Turning) | $\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d$ | $\text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$ or $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| Power Required $P$ | $P = F_c \cdot V$ | W (Watts) |
| Specific Cutting Energy $U_s$ | $U_s = F_c / (f \cdot d)$ | $\text{N/mm}^{2} \equiv \text{J/mm}^{3}$ |
| Shear Angle (Merchant) | $\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\beta - \alpha}{2}$ | Degrees |
| Chip Thickness Ratio $r$ | $r = t / t_c = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ | Dimensionless |
| Shear Area $A_s$ | $A_s = (t_1 \cdot b) / \sin\phi$ | $\text{mm}^{2}$ |
| Shear Force $F_s$ | $F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_f \sin\phi$ | N |
| Friction Force $F$ | $F = F_p \sin\beta + F_f \cos\beta$ | N |
| Shear Stress $\tau_s$ | $\tau_s = F_s / A_s$ | $\text{N/mm}^{2}$ |
| Coefficient of Friction $\mu$ | $\mu = \tan\beta = F / N$ | Dimensionless |
| Cutting Velocity (Turning) | $V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 1000$ | $\text{m/min}$, $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| Power in kW | $P_{kW} = (F_c \cdot V) / (60 \cdot 1000)$ | kW (when $F_c$ in N, $V$ in m/min) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Trap (Frequently Tested):** When $V$ is given in $\text{m/min}$ and $F_c$ in N, the power formula becomes:
> $$P\ (\text{kW}) = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000}$$
> This is the most common mistake area in KTU numericals.

### 2.5 Engineering Applications

- **Machine Tool Sizing:** The calculated power $P$ is used to select the spindle motor rating for a CNC machine, typically with a **$1.25\text{–}1.5\times$ safety factor** for starting loads.
- **Process Economics:** Specific energy $U_s$ dictates the machining cost — lower $U_s$ means cheaper operation.
- **Coolant Strategy:** Since $60\text{–}90\%$ of $P$ is converted to heat carried by the chip, $U_s$ helps design high-pressure coolant delivery.
- **CAM Software:** Modern CAM (e.g., Mastercam, Fusion 360) uses these exact force-power formulas to simulate cutting forces, predict tool deflection, and select spindle power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Power Required from First Principles

**Given:**
- Cutting velocity $V$ (m/s) in the direction of $F_c$.
- Cutting force $F_c$ (N) in the direction of $V$.
- Feed velocity $V_f$ (m/s) in the direction of $F_f$.
- Feed force $F_f$ (N) in the direction of $V_f$.

**Step 1 — Define Work Done:**
Work done $W$ is the dot product of force and displacement.

$$W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the Three Force-Velocity Pairs:**

For the cutting force component moving through distance $L$ (cutting length):

$$W_c = F_c \cdot L$$

For the feed force component moving through distance $L_f$ (feed distance):

$$W_f = F_f \cdot L_f$$

The radial force $F_p$ does no useful work because the tool has no motion in the radial direction during a single pass:

$$W_p = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Work Done in Time $t$:**

If the cutting length in time $t$ is $L = V \cdot t$ and feed distance is $L_f = V_f \cdot t$, then:

$$W_{total} = F_c \cdot V \cdot t + F_f \cdot V_f \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Compute Power as Rate of Work:**

$$P = \dfrac{dW}{dt} = F_c \cdot V + F_f \cdot V_f$$

**Step 5 — Apply Engineering Approximation:**
In practice, $V_f \ll V$ (feed is $\sim 0.1\text{–}0.5\ \text{mm/rev}$ while $V$ is $\sim 100\text{–}1000\ \text{m/min}$), so $F_f \cdot V_f$ is negligible:

$$\boxed{\,P \approx F_c \cdot V\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Specific Cutting Energy $U_s$

**Step 1 — Express MRR for Turning:**

$$\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d$$

Where $f$ = feed (mm/rev) and $d$ = depth of cut (mm).

**Step 2 — Express Power:**

$$P = F_c \cdot V$$

**Step 3 — Define $U_s$ as Power per unit MRR:**

$$U_s = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{V \cdot f \cdot d}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $V$:**

$$\boxed{\,U_s = \dfrac{F_c}{f \cdot d}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Useful Engineering Insight:**
Since $F_c$ depends on material hardness, rake angle, and cutting speed, $U_s$ is a *material-process* property. Typical values:

- Mild Steel: $U_s \approx 2.0\text{–}2.5\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$
- Cast Iron: $U_s \approx 1.5\text{–}1.8\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$
- Aluminium Alloys: $U_s \approx 0.4\text{–}0.8\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$
- Titanium Alloys: $U_s \approx 3.0\text{–}4.5\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Titanium is "Notoriously Difficult to Cut":** The $U_s$ value of Ti-alloys is nearly $2\times$ that of mild steel. This means the spindle motor must deliver almost double the power for the same MRR — a key reason Ti-6Al-4V aerospace parts are expensive to machine.

### 3.3 Derivation of Shear Angle Using Merchant's Analysis

**Given Geometry (Orthogonal Cutting):**
- Rake angle: $\alpha$
- Shear angle: $\phi$
- Friction angle: $\beta = \tan^{-1}\mu$

**Step 1 — Velocity Triangle:**
The chip flows over the rake face at velocity $V_c$. The material shears along the shear plane at velocity $V_s$. The relationship is:

$$\dfrac{V}{V_s} = \cos\phi - r \cdot \sin\phi \quad ; \quad \dfrac{V}{V_c} = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium on the Shear Plane:**
Using the free body diagram of the chip, the resultant $R$ makes an angle $(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ with the horizontal. Equating horizontal and vertical components yields Merchant's equation:

$$2\phi + \beta - \alpha = C$$

Where $C$ is a constant of integration. For a sharp tool and continuous chip, $C = 90°$, giving the most-cited form:

$$\boxed{\,\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\beta - \alpha}{2}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation:**
- A **larger positive rake angle $\alpha$** $\rightarrow$ **larger $\phi$** $\rightarrow$ **thinner chip, less force, less power**.
- A **larger friction angle $\beta$** (poor lubrication or adhesion) $\rightarrow$ **smaller $\phi$** $\rightarrow$ **thicker chip, more force, more power**.

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Numerical Mastery

**KTU-Style Numerical (Typical 7-Mark Sub-Question):**

> *In a turning operation, the cutting force $F_c = 1500\ \text{N}$, feed force $F_f = 500\ \text{N}$, and thrust force $F_p = 750\ \text{N}$. The cutting speed is $V = 120\ \text{m/min}$, feed $= 0.2\ \text{mm/rev}$, and depth of cut $= 3\ \text{mm}$. Calculate: (a) Power required in kW, (b) Specific cutting energy, (c) Resultant force.*

**Solution:**

**(a) Power Required:**

$$P = F_c \cdot V = 1500\ \text{N} \times 120\ \text{m/min}$$

Convert $V$ to m/s: $V = 120 / 60 = 2\ \text{m/s}$

$$P = 1500 \times 2 = 3000\ \text{W} = 3.0\ \text{kW}$$

**(b) Specific Cutting Energy:**

$$U_s = \dfrac{F_c}{f \cdot d} = \dfrac{1500}{0.2 \times 3} = \dfrac{1500}{0.6} = 2500\ \text{N/mm}^{2} \equiv 2.5\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$$

**(c) Resultant Force:**

$$R = \sqrt{F_c^{2} + F_f^{2} + F_p^{2}} = \sqrt{1500^{2} + 500^{2} + 750^{2}}$$

$$R = \sqrt{2{,}250{,}000 + 250{,}000 + 562{,}500} = \sqrt{3{,}062{,}500} = 1750\ \text{N}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Force System and Energy Flow in Orthogonal Cutting (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> Since physical force vectors are notoriously difficult to render reliably in Mermaid, the following **Functional Architecture Flow** maps the energy and force interactions in the cutting zone — explicitly showing how the spindle power is partitioned between useful shear work, friction losses, and heat dissipation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spindle Motor Input Power P_in] --> B[Mechanical Power to Tool Tip P = F_c x V]
    B --> C[Primary Shear Zone Energy]
    B --> D[Secondary Deformation Zone Friction on Rake Face]
    C --> E[Useful Plastic Shear Work]
    C --> F[Heat Carried Away by Chip ~75 percent]
    D --> G[Frictional Heat at Tool Chip Interface]
    D --> H[Heat Conducted into Tool Body]
    E --> I[Material Removal MRR = V x f x d]
    F --> J[Heat Dissipated to Environment]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Tool Wear and Thermal Damage]
    K --> L[Tool Life Reduction]
    I --> M[Finished Component Surface]
    M --> N[Quality Output]
```

### 4.2 Cutting Tool Material Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Cutting Tool Material Universe] --> T1[Conventional Tool Steels]
    T0 --> T2[High Speed Steel HSS Family]
    T0 --> T3[Cast Cobalt Alloys Stellite]
    T0 --> T4[Hardmetals Cemented Carbides]
    T0 --> T5[Superhard Ceramics]
    T0 --> T6[Ultra Hard Materials]
    T1 --> T1A[Plain Carbon Steel 0.6 to 1.5 percent C]
    T1 --> T1B[Alloy Tool Steel]
    T2 --> T2A[Tungsten Type HSS T1 18 4 1]
    T2 --> T2B[Molybdenum Type HSS M2 M35]
    T2 --> T2C[Cobalt Rich HSS M42 with 8 percent Co]
    T3 --> T3A[Stellite Grade 1 and Grade 19]
    T4 --> T4A[Tungsten Carbide WC plus Co Binder]
    T4 --> T4B[Titanium Carbide TiC Based]
    T4 --> T4C[TaC NbC Mixed Carbides]
    T4 --> T4D[Coated Carbides TiC TiN Al2O3 Multilayer]
    T5 --> T5A[Alumina Al2O3 Based Cold Pressed or Hot Pressed]
    T5 --> T5B[Silicon Nitride Si3N4 Based]
    T5 --> T5C[Whisker Reinforced Ceramics SiC Whisker plus Al2O3]
    T6 --> T6A[Cubic Boron Nitride CBN]
    T6 --> T6B[Polycrystalline Diamond PCD]
    T6 --> T6C[Monocrystalline Diamond MCD]
```

### 4.3 Process Mapping — From Force to Power to Specific Energy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Cutting Parameters V f d] --> B[Force Model F_c empirical or Merchant]
    B --> C[Power Computation P = F_c x V]
    C --> D[Specific Energy U_s = F_c / f x d]
    D --> E[Spindle Motor Sizing]
    D --> F[Coolant Flow Design]
    C --> G[Heat Partitioning Analysis]
    G --> H[Tool Life Prediction Taylor Equation]
    H --> I[Economic Machining Strategy]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When asked to "draw Merchant's Circle of Forces," examiners expect a vector diagram with the resultant $R$ resolved into $F_c$ and $F_p$, then further resolved into $F_s$ (shear force) and $F$ (friction force) on the shear plane and tool face, respectively. The angles $\phi$, $(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$, and $\beta$ must be explicitly labeled.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define specific cutting energy. Mention its typical value for mild steel.**
> **Model Answer:**
> Specific cutting energy $U_s$ is defined as the energy required to remove a unit volume of material during machining. It is mathematically given by $U_s = F_c / (f \cdot d)$, where $F_c$ is the cutting force, $f$ is the feed, and $d$ is the depth of cut. The typical value of $U_s$ for mild steel is **$1.8\text{–}2.5\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$** (or equivalently $\text{N/mm}^{2}$). It is a key parameter for estimating spindle power and machining cost.

**Q2. List any four desirable properties of a cutting tool material.**
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Hot Hardness (Red Hardness):** Ability to retain hardness at elevated cutting temperatures.
> 2. **Toughness:** Resistance to fracture and chipping under impact loads.
> 3. **Wear Resistance:** Resistance to abrasion, diffusion, and chemical attack.
> 4. **Chemical Stability / Inertness:** Should not chemically react with the workpiece material at cutting temperature.
> *(Acceptable additional answers: high thermal conductivity, low coefficient of friction, good thermal shock resistance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **specific cutting energy** in terms of cutting force, feed, and depth of cut. A turning operation is performed on a mild steel workpiece with cutting speed $V = 150\ \text{m/min}$, feed $f = 0.25\ \text{mm/rev}$, and depth of cut $d = 4\ \text{mm}$. The cutting and feed forces measured are $F_c = 1800\ \text{N}$ and $F_f = 600\ \text{N}$ respectively. Compute: (i) MRR, (ii) Power required in kW, and (iii) Specific cutting energy. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Classify **cutting tool materials** with neat examples. Explain the role of **hot hardness** and **toughness** in tool performance. Compare HSS and cemented carbide tools in terms of composition, cutting speed, and applications. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step:**

**Derivation:**
Specific cutting energy is energy consumed per unit volume of material removed.

$$U_s = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{V \cdot f \cdot d} = \dfrac{F_c}{f \cdot d}$$

**Given:** $V = 150\ \text{m/min}$, $f = 0.25\ \text{mm/rev}$, $d = 4\ \text{mm}$, $F_c = 1800\ \text{N}$, $F_f = 600\ \text{N}$.

**(i) Material Removal Rate:**

$$\text{MRR} = V \cdot f \cdot d = 150\ \text{m/min} \times 0.25\ \text{mm/rev} \times 4\ \text{mm}$$

Convert $V$ to mm/min: $V = 150 \times 1000 = 150{,}000\ \text{mm/min}$

$$\text{MRR} = 150{,}000 \times 0.25 \times 4 = 150{,}000\ \text{mm}^{3}/\text{min} = 2500\ \text{mm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Power Required:**

$$P = F_c \cdot V = 1800\ \text{N} \times (150 / 60)\ \text{m/s} = 1800 \times 2.5 = 4500\ \text{W} = 4.5\ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Specific Cutting Energy:**

$$U_s = \dfrac{1800}{0.25 \times 4} = \dfrac{1800}{1.0} = 1800\ \text{N/mm}^{2} \equiv 1.8\ \text{J/mm}^{3}$$

---

**(b) Classification & Comparison:**

| **Property** | **HSS** | **Cemented Carbide** |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Fe + W (18%) + Cr (4%) + V (1%) + C | WC (94%) + Co (6%) binder |
| Cutting Speed | $30\text{–}60\ \text{m/min}$ | $100\text{–}300\ \text{m/min}$ |
| Hot Hardness | Up to $\sim 600°\text{C}$ | Up to $\sim 900°\text{C}$ |
| Toughness | High | Low–Moderate (brittle) |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher (but more cost-effective at high V) |
| Applications | Drills, taps, reamers, form tools, low-V turning | High-V turning, milling inserts, mass production |

**Role of Hot Hardness:** Determines the maximum cutting temperature the tool tip can withstand before losing its cutting edge. Higher hot hardness $\rightarrow$ higher permissible cutting speed $\rightarrow$ higher productivity.

**Role of Toughness:** Determines the tool's ability to withstand mechanical shocks, interrupted cuts, and vibrations. High toughness $\rightarrow$ lower risk of chipping $\rightarrow$ longer tool life in roughing operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Reality:** There is an inverse relationship between hot hardness and toughness. HSS has high toughness but lower hot hardness; ceramics have extreme hot hardness but are very brittle. Coated carbides balance both reasonably well.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 Pattern] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** With the help of a **Merchant's force circle diagram**, derive the expression for the **shear angle $\phi$** in orthogonal cutting. State Merchant's equation and explain the effect of rake angle and friction angle on the shear angle. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the composition, properties, and applications of **ceramic cutting tools** and **CBN tools**. Why are PCD tools unsuitable for machining ferrous materials? **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Merchant's Shear Angle Derivation:**

Consider the Merchant's force circle where the resultant force $R$ is resolved into cutting force $F_c$ and thrust force $F_p$. On the shear plane, $R$ is resolved into shear force $F_s$ and normal force $F_n$. On the rake face, $R$ is resolved into friction force $F$ and normal force $N$.

From the geometry of the Merchant's circle, the angle between $R$ and $F_c$ is $(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$. Force equilibrium in the horizontal direction:

$$F_c = F_s \cos\phi + F_n \sin\phi$$

Force equilibrium in the vertical direction:

$$F_p = F_s \sin\phi + F_n \cos\phi$$

Eliminating $F_s$ and $F_n$ and applying the friction law $F = \mu N$:

$$2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$$

$$\boxed{\,\phi = 45° + \dfrac{\beta - \alpha}{2}\,}$$

**Effect of Rake Angle $\alpha$:** A more positive rake angle **increases $\phi$**, reducing the shear area, the shear force, and hence the cutting power.

**Effect of Friction Angle $\beta$:** A higher friction angle (poor lubrication) **decreases $\phi$**, increases the shear area, and significantly raises cutting forces and power.

---

**(b) Ceramic and CBN Tools — Composition, Properties, Applications:**

| **Tool Type** | **Composition** | **Key Properties** | **Typical Applications** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alumina-based Ceramic** | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (cold-pressed or hot-pressed with $\text{ZrO}_2$, $\text{TiC}$) | High hot hardness ($>1200°\text{C}$), chemically inert, low thermal expansion | High-V finishing of cast iron, hardened steels, continuous cuts |
| **Silicon Nitride Ceramic** | $\text{Si}_3\text{N}_4$ with $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ + $\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3$ sintering aids | High toughness among ceramics, good thermal shock resistance | Rough machining of cast iron, Ni-based superalloys |
| **Whisker-Reinforced Ceramic** | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ + SiC whiskers (20–30%) | Combines high hot hardness with improved fracture toughness | Machining of Ni-based superalloys (Inconel 718, Waspaloy) at $V > 500\ \text{m/min}$ |
| **CBN (Cubic Boron Nitride)** | Cubic BN crystals bonded with ceramic/metal binder | Second hardest material after diamond, chemically stable with Fe, high hot hardness ($>1400°\text{C}$) | Hardened steel (HRC 50–70) turning, gear skiving, grey cast iron finishing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PCD is Unsuitable for Ferrous Materials:**
> Polycrystalline Diamond is chemically reactive with iron at temperatures above $\sim 700°\text{C}$. During machining of steels and cast irons, the carbon atoms of the diamond chemically **dissolve/diffuse into the Fe lattice**, causing catastrophic chemical wear (a phenomenon called *graphitization*). CBN, being a compound of B and N, does not suffer this affinity and is therefore the material of choice for ferrous machining at high speeds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Power Calculation:** The single most common error is computing $P = F_c \cdot V$ without converting $V$ from m/min to m/s. KTU examiners **deduct a full mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing Resultant $R$ with Cutting Force $F_c$:** When asked for "cutting force," students often compute the resultant $\sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_p^2}$. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Tool Material Properties Memorization:** Listing only "hardness" and "toughness" without mentioning **hot hardness (red hardness)** and **chemical stability** is treated as incomplete by KTU evaluators.
> 4. **Merchant's Equation Derivation:** Skipping the force-equilibrium steps and writing only the final formula results in **2 marks lost out of 7** in Part B.
> 5. **PCD vs CBN Confusion:** Many students incorrectly state that "diamond can cut everything." Examiners penalize this; the iron-carbon chemical affinity is a must-mention point.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read Before Every KTU Exam):**

- **Power Required $P = F_c \cdot V$** is the cornerstone formula. Pair $F_c$ with $V$ only.
- **Unit Trap:** When $V$ is in **m/min** and $F_c$ in **N**, then $P\ (\text{kW}) = (F_c \cdot V) / 60{,}000$. Always state units in every line.
- **MRR (Turning)** $= V \cdot f \cdot d$; **(Milling)** $= V \cdot f \cdot d \cdot n_z$ where $n_z$ is the number of effective teeth.
- **Specific Cutting Energy $U_s = F_c / (f \cdot d)$** is independent of cutting speed but strongly material-dependent.
- **Merchant's Shear Angle:** $\phi = 45° + (\beta - \alpha)/2$. Higher $\alpha$ $\rightarrow$ higher $\phi$ $\rightarrow$ lower power. Higher $\beta$ $\rightarrow$ lower $\phi$ $\rightarrow$ higher power.
- **Resultant Force** $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2 + F_p^2}$.
- **Three Primary Forces:** $F_c$ (tangential, dominant, does useful work) $\rightarrow$ $F_p$ (radial, pushes workpiece away) $\rightarrow$ $F_f$ (axial, opposes feed drive).
- **Cutting Tool Material Hierarchy (Low to High Performance):** Plain Carbon Steel $\rightarrow$ HSS $\rightarrow$ Stellite $\rightarrow$ Cemented Carbide $\rightarrow$ Coated Carbide $\rightarrow$ Ceramic $\rightarrow$ CBN $\rightarrow$ PCD.
- **HSS Composition "18-4-1":** 18% W, 4% Cr, 1% V, balance Fe + C.
- **Cemented Carbide Composition:** WC + Co binder (Co gives toughness, WC gives hardness).
- **Coated Carbides:** Single-layer (TiC, TiN, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) or **multi-layer (TiC/$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$/TiN)** — extend tool life by $2\text{–}3\times$ via wear resistance and thermal barrier.
- **Ceramics — Pros:** Excellent hot hardness, chemical inertness. **Cons:** Low toughness, poor thermal shock resistance.
- **CBN is best for hardened steels and cast iron; PCD is best for non-ferrous (Al, Cu, composites, plastics) and abrasive non-metals.** Mixing these up is a KTU-favourite trap question.
- **Taylor's Tool Life Equation (linked):** $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ — higher hot hardness $\rightarrow$ higher allowable $V$ $\rightarrow$ lower $n$ $\rightarrow$ lower exponent sensitivity.
- **Heat Partition Rule of Thumb (Empirical):** Chip carries $\sim 75\%$, tool carries $\sim 10\%$, workpiece carries $\sim 5\%$, environment absorbs $\sim 10\%$ of the cutting heat.
- **Spindle Motor Sizing Formula (Industrial Practice):** $\text{Motor kW} = 1.25 \cdot P_{calculated}$ (25% safety margin for starting transients).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties and Applications of Various Tool Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties and Applications of Various Tool Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCIET402 – Machine Tools and Digital Manufacturing**, a **cutting tool material** is defined as the engineering material used to fabricate the active cutting edge of a tool, which is responsible for removing material from the workpiece in the form of chips through plastic shear deformation. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the suitability of a tool material is judged on a composite set of **physical, mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties** that allow it to retain its geometry, hardness, and edge integrity at elevated temperatures generated in the primary and secondary shear zones (typically **600 °C – 1200 °C** depending on cutting speed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 1):**
> The student must be able to **classify, compare, and select** the appropriate tool material for a given workpiece–operation combination, with explicit reasoning grounded in **hot hardness, wear resistance, toughness, and chemical affinity**.

The most industrially relevant tool materials, in chronological order of commercial adoption, are:

1. **Carbon Tool Steel** (historical, low-temperature use).
2. **High Speed Steel (HSS)** – Mo-series (M1, M2, M3, M35, M42) and W-series (T1).
3. **Cast Non-Ferrous Alloys** – e.g., **Stellite** (Co–Cr–W).
4. **Cemented Carbides** – WC–Co, classified by ISO into **P, M, K** groups.
5. **Cermets** – TiC/TiN bonded with Ni–Mo.
6. **Ceramics** – Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄, SiAlON (whisker-reinforced).
7. **Superhard materials** – **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** and **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)**.
8. **Coated tools** – CVD/PVD coatings of **TiN, TiCN, TiAlN, AlCrN, DLC**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **chisel being hammered into a hard rock repeatedly for hours**. The rock is your workpiece (steel, cast iron, titanium), and the chisel is your cutting tool. Now, if your chisel is made of **butter**, it will deform instantly. If it is made of **glass**, it will chip on the first hard strike. If it is made of **hardened tool steel**, it will last a while but its tip will lose sharpness once the rock gets very hot. If it is made of **tungsten carbide**, it will keep its shape for much longer because it is harder and tougher. If it is coated with a **diamond-like film**, friction and heat transfer drop dramatically.

> **Translation to Manufacturing:**
> - **Hardness at room temperature** = ability to scratch/cut the workpiece.
> - **Hot hardness (red hardness)** = ability to keep cutting when the tip glows orange-red.
> - **Toughness** = ability to survive impact without chipping (e.g., interrupted cuts).
> - **Wear resistance** = how slowly the edge erodes from rubbing against the chip and workpiece.
> - **Chemical stability** = reluctance to *weld* itself to the hot chip (built-up edge formation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:**
> *"Hard but not always enough — the tool must remain hard even when the chip is red hot. That single line separates HSS from carbide, and carbide from CBN/PCD."*

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Tool Performance

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to recall the following benchmark metrics:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness (room) | HRC / HV / HK | dimensionless | Edge penetration capability |
| Hot hardness | HV at 600 °C | dimensionless | Performance at cutting temperature |
| Transverse Rupture Strength | TRS | **MPa** (≈ 1500 – 4500) | Resistance to fracture |
| Compressive strength | σ_c | **MPa** | Resists crushing under feed force |
| Fracture toughness | K_IC | **MPa·m^(1/2)** | Chip-resistance under impact |
| Thermal conductivity | k | **W/m·K** | Heat dissipation from tip |
| Coefficient of friction | μ | dimensionless | Affects cutting force & temperature |
| Thermal expansion | α | **10⁻⁶ /K** | Thermal shock resistance |
| Chemical affinity to Fe | qualitative | – | Diffusion wear mechanism |
| Maximum useful temperature | T_max | **°C** | Upper safe operating limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:**
> A tool material is **never** the *best* in all categories. The art of tool selection is a **trade-off matrix** — diamond (PCD) is the hardest but cannot machine ferrous alloys (chemical dissolution in Fe above ~700 °C). HSS is the toughest but loses hardness beyond ~600 °C. The student's task in the KTU exam is to **justify** such trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Tool Material Performance

The KTU examiner expects the answer to be structured around the **five functional requirements** of any cutting tool. Every tool material — from HSS to PCD — is rated against these:

### Pillar 1 — Hardness and Hot Hardness
- **Definition:** Resistance to indentation, measured as **HRC (Rockwell C)**, **HV (Vickers)**, or **HK (Knoop)**.
- A cutting edge must be **at least 1.5× – 2× harder** than the workpiece at operating temperature.
- **Hot hardness (red hardness)** is the property that uniquely allows a tool to continue cutting after the cutting zone has turned red.
- Example data: HSS retains ~**60 HRC** up to 600 °C; cemented carbide retains ~**80 HRA (≈ 62 HRC)** up to 800 °C; ceramic retains hardness up to 1200 °C; PCD/CBN lose effectiveness only above 1200 °C.

### Pillar 2 — Toughness (Fracture Resistance)
- Quantified by **K_IC** (fracture toughness) and **TRS (transverse rupture strength)**.
- High toughness allows the tool to withstand **interrupted cuts** (e.g., milling, turning of splined shafts), entry/exit shock, and variations in chip load.
- Ranking (low → high): PCD < Ceramic < Cermet < CBN < Carbide < HSS.
- **Trade-off:** Hardness ↑ ⇒ Toughness ↓ (this is the *fundamental trade-off*).

### Pillar 3 — Wear Resistance
Four distinct mechanisms, all of which the student must identify:
- **Abrasive wear** – hard particles in the workpiece plough the rake face.
- **Adhesive wear** – micro-welding and tearing at the chip–tool interface.
- **Diffusion wear** – atomic migration between tool and chip at high T (dominant in HSS → steel).
- **Chemical/oxidative wear** – reaction with atmosphere or workpiece elements.

### Pillar 4 — Thermal Stability
- Defined by the **maximum useful temperature** and the **thermal shock resistance** parameter:
$$R_{ts} = \dfrac{k \cdot \sigma_{f}}{\alpha \cdot E}$$
where $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\sigma_f$ = flexural strength, $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion, $E$ = Young's modulus.
- A **high $R_{ts}$** = the tool can survive sudden temperature cycles without cracking (critical in milling with coolant).

### Pillar 5 — Chemical Affinity / Stability with Workpiece
- The tool must NOT chemically dissolve or react with the workpiece at cutting temperature.
- **Critical rule:** PCD (carbon) **dissolves in Fe above ~700 °C** — therefore **PCD cannot be used to machine steel**; it is reserved for Al, Cu, Mg, composites, wood, plastics.
- **CBN**, being boron nitride, is chemically inert to Fe up to ~1300 °C, making it the superhard material of choice for **hardened steels and cast irons**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly usable** in KTU Part B numerical/descriptive answers. The vertical bar symbol is intentionally replaced by `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V \cdot T^{\,n} = C$ (Taylor's equation) | Tool life vs. cutting speed | $V$ = m/min, $T$ = min, $n, C$ = empirical |
| 2 | $V_1 \, T_1^{\,n} = V_2 \, T_2^{\,n}$ | Cross-comparison of tool life data | Used for speed-vs-life problems |
| 3 | $\theta_{cut} \approx k \cdot V^{0.2}$ to $k \cdot V^{0.4}$ | Empirical cutting temperature | $\theta$ in °C |
| 4 | $R_{ts} = \dfrac{k \cdot \sigma_f}{\alpha \cdot E}$ | Thermal shock resistance figure of merit | Higher = better |
| 5 | $HRc \approx 0.83 \cdot HRA + 5.8$ (approx.) | Hardness scale conversion | Use only when not given in table |
| 6 | $HRC_{min} \ge 1.5 \cdot HRC_{workpiece}$ | Selection rule of thumb | Empirical |
| 7 | $V_{new} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{req}}{T_{ref}}\right)^{1/n}$ | Taylor-derived speed adjustment | Direct KTU numerical |
| 8 | MRR $= V \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material removal rate (context) | mm³/min |
| 9 | Power $= k_c \cdot MRR$ | Spindle power requirement | kW |
| 10 | $\text{Cutting ratio } r = \dfrac{t_1}{t_2}$ | Chip-thickness ratio (context only) | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Detailed Material-by-Material Analysis

### 2.3.1 Carbon Tool Steel
- Composition: **0.6 – 1.4 % C**, plain Fe–C, water-quenched to ~**62 HRC**.
- **Maximum useful temperature:** ~**200 °C**.
- **Pros:** cheap, easy to grind, sharp edge.
- **Cons:** loses hardness rapidly above 200 °C → cannot be used at any meaningful cutting speed.
- **Application:** Hand tools, files, low-speed form tools for brass, wood.
- **KTU relevance:** Mostly **historical context** — usually asked in a 3-mark "compare and contrast" or "evolution" question.

### 2.3.2 High Speed Steel (HSS)
- The **workhorse** of the pre-carbide era and still dominant in drills, taps, reamers, broaches, and small-diameter milling cutters.
- Achieves **secondary hardening** through the formation of **Mo₂C / W₂C carbides** during tempering at 500 – 600 °C.
- **Hardness:** 62 – 67 HRC (room), retains ~60 HRC at 600 °C.
- **Common grades:**
  - **M2** (6 % W, 5 % Mo, 4 % Cr, 2 % V, 0.85 % C) — general purpose.
  - **M35 / M42** — addition of **8 – 10 % Co** boosts hot hardness and wear resistance.
  - **T1** (18 % W) — classic W-series, still used for heavy-duty broaches.
- **Cutting speed range:** ~**25 – 80 m/min** for mild steel.
- **Application envelope:** drills, taps, reamers, form tools, gear cutters, broaches, small end-mills.
- **KTU relevance:** Almost always appears in **Part A definition** and **Part B 7-mark** questions.

### 2.3.3 Stellite and Cast Cobalt Alloys
- Composition: **Co (40 – 60 %), Cr (25 – 35 %), W (5 – 15 %), C (1 – 3 %)**.
- **Hardness:** 40 – 50 HRC; **retains hardness up to 700 °C**.
- **Manufacture:** Cast to near-net shape, no further forging.
- **Application:** **Continuous high-speed finishing of steel and cast iron** where carbide is too brittle (e.g., valve-seat turning, automotive engine machining). Common on **old-type single-point cutting tools for large lathe work**.
- **Trade-off:** Brittle, cannot be ground to a very sharp edge, slow.

### 2.3.4 Cemented Carbides (WC–Co)
- Produced by **powder metallurgy**: WC particles + Co binder (3 – 25 % Co) → sintered at 1400 – 1500 °C.
- **Hardness:** 75 – 94 HRA (≈ 50 – 75 HRC). Hardness **decreases** as Co content **increases**, but **toughness increases**.
- **ISO Classification (must memorize for KTU):**
  - **P (Blue):** Steel, steel castings, long-chipping ferrous → high TiC/TaC content.
  - **M (Yellow):** Stainless steel, austenitic Mn-steel, difficult-to-machine alloys.
  - **K (Red):** Cast iron, non-ferrous, short-chipping → high WC content.
  - Numeric suffix (01 – 50) = **toughness grade**; higher number ⇒ tougher, lower number ⇒ harder.
- **Cutting speed range:** 100 – 350 m/min for steel; 60 – 150 m/min for cast iron.
- **Application:** **Inserts for turning, milling, drilling on CNC machines** — the modern industrial default.
- **Grades sub-classification:**
  - **Coated carbide** (most modern inserts).
  - **Uncoated carbide** (sharp edge, aluminum machining).
  - **Cermet** (TiC + Ni binder, for finishing of steel at high speed and low feed).

### 2.3.5 Ceramics
- **Al₂O₃-based:** Cold-pressed, hot-pressed, or whisker-reinforced with **SiC whiskers** (the latter, *SiAlON/SiC-whisker* family, gives 2× toughness of pure alumina).
- **Si₃N₄- and SiAlON-based:** Better thermal shock resistance, suitable for **interrupted cutting of cast iron**.
- **Hardness:** 1500 – 2500 HV; retains up to **1200 °C**.
- **Cutting speed:** up to **1000 m/min** for finishing of cast iron; **dry cutting** typically.
- **Application:** High-speed finishing of **cast iron, Ni-based superalloys, hardened steels (in continuous cuts)**.
- **Trade-off:** Very **brittle** — cannot tolerate impact, vibration, or interrupted cuts in ferrous workpieces below ~50 HRC.
- **Cermet (TiC/Ni–Mo):** Sometimes classified separately, sometimes under ceramics. Intermediate hardness/toughness; excellent for **finishing of steel at high speeds and light depths**.

### 2.3.6 Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)
- **Synthesis:** High-pressure, high-temperature (HPHT) sintering of **cBN** particles with a binder (typically Co, TiC, or TiN).
- **Hardness:** **4500 – 5000 HV** (second only to diamond).
- **Chemical stability with Fe:** **Inert up to ~1300 °C** → can machine **ferrous materials** unlike diamond.
- **Thermal stability:** up to 1400 °C.
- **Application:** **Hard turning of hardened steels (55 – 70 HRC)**, replacing grinding in many finish operations; machining of Ni/Co superalloys; high-speed machining of gray cast iron.
- **Trade-off:** Expensive, brittle; needs **rigid machine, no vibration, sharp hone edge** (typically negative land, chamfered edge).
- **Polycrystalline CBN (PCBN)** is the typical commercial form, used as a brazed tip or as an insert.

### 2.3.7 Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)
- **Synthesis:** Sintered layer of synthetic diamond particles (~25 – 30 μm) bonded to a WC–Co substrate.
- **Hardness:** **6500 – 10000 HV** (highest of all tool materials).
- **Chemical limitation:** **Reacts with Fe above 700 °C** — therefore **not used on ferrous workpieces**.
- **Application:** **High-speed machining of Al, Cu, Mg, brass, cemented carbides (in some grades), wood, plastics, composites (CFRP, MMC).** Standard in **automotive Al-alloy piston and cylinder-head machining** at speeds of **1000 – 3000 m/min**.
- **Trade-off:** Very expensive; cannot be used for steel; sensitive to impact.

### 2.3.8 Coated Tools
A **modern coated insert** typically has **3 – 12 layers** of different coatings on a carbide or cermet substrate. Each layer has a different role:
- **Inner layer:** improves adhesion to substrate (e.g., TiN).
- **Middle layer:** provides hardness and wear resistance (e.g., **TiCN, Al₂O₃**).
- **Outer layer:** reduces friction and acts as a thermal/chemical barrier (e.g., **TiAlN, AlCrN, DLC**).

| Coating | Colour | Max. working T (°C) | Key benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TiN** (Titanium Nitride) | Gold | ~**600** | General-purpose, low friction |
| **TiCN** (Titanium Carbo-Nitride) | Blue-grey | ~**400** | Tough, abrasion-resistant |
| **TiAlN** (Titanium Aluminium Nitride) | Violet-black | ~**800 – 900** | High-temperature, dry machining |
| **AlCrN** (Aluminium Chromium Nitride) | Dark grey | ~**1000** | Extreme thermal stability, hardened-steel machining |
| **DLC** (Diamond-Like Carbon) | Black | ~**300** | Ultra-low friction, Al/composite use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why coating helps:** it allows a **tough substrate (high Co content)** to carry the mechanical load while the **hard, chemically stable coating** carries the thermal/abrasive load — the best of both worlds. This is the most common answer pattern in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** PCD for Al-alloy engine blocks (BMW, Toyota); PCBN for hardened-steel transmission gears; coated carbide for general turning.
- **Aerospace:** Ceramics and CBN for **Ni-based superalloys (Inconel 718, Waspaloy)** in turbine disc machining.
- **Die & Mold Making:** PCBN and ceramic inserts for **hard milling (45 – 62 HRC)** of forging dies and injection molds — replacing expensive grinding.
- **Wood & Furniture:** HSS and brazed carbide for cutting tools; PCD for laminated chipboard (resin-impregnated, highly abrasive).
- **Electronics:** PCD micro-tools for machining of smartphone camera housings and heat-sinks.
