> $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{p} \cdot d^{q} = K$,
> **determine the tool life T under Condition B.**

**Step 1 — Equate the two conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} \cdot f_1^{p} \cdot d_1^{q} &= V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} \cdot f_2^{p} \cdot d_2^{q} \\
\dfrac{V_1 \cdot f_1^{p} \cdot d_1^{q}}{V_2 \cdot f_2^{p} \cdot d_2^{q}} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{25 \cdot (0.2)^{0.5} \cdot (2.0)^{0.2}}{35 \cdot (0.3)^{0.5} \cdot (2.5)^{0.2}} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{60}\right)^{0.25} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each component separately.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.2)^{0.5} &= \sqrt{0.2} = 0.4472 \\
(2.0)^{0.2} &= 10^{0.2 \log 2} = 10^{0.2 \times 0.3010} = 10^{0.0602} = 1.1487 \\
(0.3)^{0.5} &= \sqrt{0.3} = 0.5477 \\
(2.5)^{0.2} &= 10^{0.2 \log 2.5} = 10^{0.2 \times 0.3979} = 10^{0.0796} = 1.2026
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{25 \times 0.4472 \times 1.1487}{35 \times 0.5477 \times 1.2026} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{60}\right)^{0.25} \\
\dfrac{12.84}{23.06} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{60}\right)^{0.25} \\
0.557 &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{60}\right)^{0.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take logarithms and isolate T₂.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(0.557) &= 0.25 \cdot [\log T_2 - \log 60] \\
-0.2541 &= 0.25 \cdot [\log T_2 - 1.7782] \\
-1.0164 &= \log T_2 - 1.7782 \\
\log T_2 &= 0.7618 \\
T_2 &= \text{antilog}(0.7618) \\
\boxed{T_2 &= 5.78 \text{ minutes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** When the cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut are all increased simultaneously, tool life drops **catastrophically** from 60 to 5.78 minutes. This is why **unmanned CNC cells must carefully restrict** aggressive combinations of all three parameters.

---

## 3.6 Solved Problem 5 — Economic Tool Life and Economic Cutting Speed

**Problem Statement** [KTU University Exam – 2024 Pattern]:
> For the turning operation of Solved Problem 1 (n = 0.3684, C = 90.45 m/min), the tool cost is **C_t = ₹50 per cutting edge** and the operator-plus-machine overhead rate is **C_o = ₹2.00 per minute**.
> **Calculate:**
> (a) The economic tool life T_e (minimum-cost tool life).
> (b) The economic cutting speed V_e.
> (c) The percentage increase in production rate achieved by switching from the original V = 30 m/min to V_e.

**Part (a) — Economic Tool Life T_e.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \left(\dfrac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{C_t}{C_o} \\
T_e &= \left(\dfrac{1}{0.3684} - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{50}{2} \\
T_e &= (2.714 - 1) \cdot 25 \\
T_e &= 1.714 \times 25 \\
\boxed{T_e &= 42.85 \text{ minutes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Economic Cutting Speed V_e.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_e &= \dfrac{C}{T_e^{\,n}} \\
V_e &= \dfrac{90.45}{(42.85)^{0.3684}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $(42.85)^{0.3684}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(42.85^{0.3684}) &= 0.3684 \times \log(42.85) \\
&= 0.3684 \times 1.6319 \\
&= 0.6012 \\
42.85^{0.3684} &= 10^{0.6012} = 3.992 \\
V_e &= \dfrac{90.45}{3.992} \\
\boxed{V_e &= 22.66 \text{ m/min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Percentage increase in production rate.**

Production rate is proportional to cutting speed (for the same depth, feed, and workpiece length). The change in cutting speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Change} &= \dfrac{V_e - V_{\text{original}}}{V_{\text{original}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{22.66 - 30}{30} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{-7.34}{30} \times 100 \\
\boxed{\text{Change} &= -24.5\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Moving to the **economic cutting speed actually reduces** the production rate by 24.5% compared to the original speed — but it **lowers the total cost per component** by spending more time on each part with cheaper (longer-lasting) tool engagement. This is a **counter-intuitive but very important** concept: minimum-cost machining ≠ maximum-rate machining.

---

## 3.7 Summary Table of Analytical Results

| Problem | Given | Find | Key Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | V₁ = 30, T₁ = 20; V₂ = 50, T₂ = 5 | n, C | **n = 0.3684, C = 90.45** |
| 2 | Above | T at V = 60 | **T = 3.05 min** |
| 3 | Above | V for T = 60 | **V = 20.02 m/min** |
| 4 | Two extended conditions | T under new conditions | **T = 5.78 min** |
| 5 | n, C, tool/operator costs | T_e, V_e, ΔP | **T_e = 42.85 min, V_e = 22.66 m/min, –24.5%** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Taylor's Tool Life Analysis

The following block-level diagram maps out the complete decision flow when Taylor's equation is used in a real machining engineering problem. The flow captures **all four major use-cases** that KTU typically tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Machining Problem] --> B{Identify the Type of Problem}
    B -->|Type 1: Two given test conditions| C[Use Ratio Form]
    B -->|Type 2: One condition and new speed| D[Use Basic VT power n = C]
    B -->|Type 3: Multi-parameter extended form| E[Use Extended Equation with feed and depth]
    B -->|Type 4: Economic optimisation| F[Use Economic Tool Life Formula]
    
    C --> C1[Compute n from log ratio]
    C1 --> C2[Compute C = V1 T1 power n]
    C2 --> G[Apply Predictions]
    
    D --> D1[Compute new T at given V]
    D1 --> D2[OR Compute new V for given T]
    D2 --> G
    
    E --> E1[Compute T with feed and depth terms]
    E1 --> G
    
    F --> F1[Compute T_e = 1 over n minus 1 times C_t over C_o]
    F1 --> F2[Compute V_e = C over T_e power n]
    F2 --> F3[Compare with original conditions]
    F3 --> G
    
    G --> H[Output: Cutting Speed OR Tool Life OR Economic Parameters]
    H --> I[END: Validate with Boundary Conditions]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Variables and Their Inter-Relationships

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[Cutting Speed V - m/min] -->|Inverse Power Relation with n| T[Tool Life T - min]
    F[Feed f - mm/rev] -->|Power p| EQU[Extended Taylor Equation]
    D[Depth of Cut d - mm] -->|Power q| EQU
    V --> EQU
    T --> EQU
    EQU -->|Output| PRED[Predictions]
    PRED --> PRED1[New T for given V]
    PRED --> PRED2[New V for given T]
    PRED --> PRED3[Economic V and T]
    
    CT[Tool Cost C_t] --> ECON[Economic Analysis]
    CO[Operator Cost C_o] --> ECON
    ECON --> PRED3
    
    N[Taylor Exponent n] -.depends on.-> TM[Tool Material]
    N -.depends on.-> WM[Workpiece Material]
    TM --> WM
```

## 4.3 Problem-Type Decision Matrix

| Problem Type | Given Inputs | Key Formula | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Find n and C** | Two (V, T) test points | Ratio form + log | n, C |
| **Find T** | One (V, T), one new V | $T_2 = T_1 (V_1/V_2)^{1/n}$ | T₂ |
| **Find V** | One (V, T), one new T | $V_2 = V_1 (T_1/T_2)^{n}$ | V₂ |
| **Find T (extended)** | V, f, d, T for two conditions | $VT^n f^p d^q = K$ | New T |
| **Economic T and V** | n, C, C_t, C_o | $T_e = (1/n - 1)(C_t/C_o)$, $V_e = C/T_e^n$ | T_e, V_e |

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Always identify the **problem type** first (use the decision matrix above), then write down the relevant formula, then substitute numerical values. Examiners award **methodology marks** (often 2-3 out of 14) for correct formula selection even if arithmetic has a minor slip.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: State Taylor's tool life equation and explain the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Taylor's tool life equation is given by:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

Where:
- **V** = Cutting speed in m/min — the linear speed at which the cutting edge engages the workpiece.
- **T** = Tool life in minutes — the machining time before the tool wear criterion (e.g., VB = 0.3 mm) is reached.
- **n** = Taylor's tool life exponent — a dimensionless constant that indicates **how strongly tool life responds to cutting speed**. A small n (e.g., 0.1) means tool life is **highly sensitive** to speed; a larger n (e.g., 0.5) means **less sensitive**.
- **C** = A constant (m/min) that depends on tool material, workpiece material, feed, depth of cut, and cutting environment. Physically, C is the cutting speed that would give a tool life of exactly **1 minute**.

> **Mark Allocation:** Equation statement (1 mark) + explanation of each variable (1.5 marks) + physical significance (0.5 mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: How does the value of Taylor's exponent *n* vary with tool material? Give typical ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The value of **n** decreases as the tool material becomes harder and more wear-resistant. Typical ranges are:

| Tool Material | Typical n |
|---|---|
| High-Speed Steel (HSS) | **0.20 – 0.30** |
| Carbide (coated / uncoated) | **0.10 – 0.20** |
| Ceramics | **0.40 – 0.60** |
| Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN) | **0.30 – 0.45** |
| Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD) | **0.08 – 0.15** |

**Physical reason:** Harder tool materials can withstand higher temperatures without wear, so their tool life is **less sensitive** to small changes in cutting speed. Hence **lower n**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – 2024 Module 1 Pattern]

**Q:** **(a)** Derive Taylor's tool life equation in its standard form. Discuss how the equation is modified to incorporate the effect of feed and depth of cut, and explain the physical meaning of each exponent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a turning experiment on mild steel with an HSS tool, the following results were obtained:

| Test | Cutting Speed V (m/min) | Tool Life T (min) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 45 | 30 |
| 2 | 60 | 12 |

**(i)** Determine the Taylor's tool life exponent *n* and the constant *C*.
**(ii)** What cutting speed would be recommended for a tool life of 90 minutes?
**(iii)** If the cutting speed is increased by 20%, by what percentage does the tool life change? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Statement of Taylor's law (2 marks):**
Empirically established that cutting tool life T and cutting speed V are related by:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

**Step 2 — Modified form (3 marks):**
To incorporate feed and depth of cut:

$$V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{p} \cdot d^{q} = K$$

Where f = feed, d = depth of cut, p and q are exponents reflecting their influence on tool life.

**Step 3 — Physical meaning of exponents (2 marks):**
- **n (tool life exponent):** Sensitivity of tool life to cutting speed; lower n means higher sensitivity.
- **p (feed exponent):** Higher feed increases cutting temperature and forces → reduces tool life (typically 0.5–0.8).
- **q (depth of cut exponent):** Higher depth increases tool engagement area → reduces tool life (typically 0.1–0.3).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Part (b)(i) — Determination of n and C (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n} \\
\dfrac{45}{60} &= \left(\dfrac{12}{30}\right)^{n} \\
0.75 &= (0.4)^{n} \\
\log(0.75) &= n \cdot \log(0.4) \\
-0.1249 &= n \cdot (-0.3979) \\
\boxed{n &= 0.314}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = 45 \cdot (30)^{0.314} \\
\log(30^{0.314}) &= 0.314 \times \log 30 = 0.314 \times 1.4771 = 0.4638 \\
30^{0.314} &= 2.911 \\
\boxed{C &= 45 \times 2.911 = 131.0 \text{ m/min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Cutting speed for T = 90 min (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{C}{T^{n}} = \dfrac{131.0}{(90)^{0.314}} \\
\log(90^{0.314}) &= 0.314 \times \log 90 = 0.314 \times 1.9542 = 0.6136 \\
90^{0.314} &= 4.108 \\
\boxed{V &= \dfrac{131.0}{4.108} = 31.89 \text{ m/min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(iii) — % change in tool life for 20% speed increase (2 marks):**

New cutting speed V₂ = 1.20 × 45 = 54 m/min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n} \\
\dfrac{45}{54} &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{30}\right)^{0.314} \\
0.8333 &= \left(\dfrac{T_2}{30}\right)^{0.314}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking logs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log(0.8333) &= 0.314 \cdot [\log T_2 - \log 30] \\
-0.0792 &= 0.314 \cdot [\log T_2 - 1.4771] \\
-0.2522 &= \log T_2 - 1.4771 \\
\log T_2 &= 1.2249 \\
T_2 &= 16.79 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage change:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta T &= \dfrac{16.79 - 30}{30} \times 100 \\
\boxed{\% \Delta T &= -44.0\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

A **20% increase in cutting speed reduces tool life by 44%**.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – 2024 Pattern]

**Q:** **(a)** Explain the concept of **economic tool life**. Derive the expression for the economic tool life T_e and the corresponding economic cutting speed V_e in terms of Taylor's constants and machining costs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turning operation uses a carbide tool with Taylor's equation $V \cdot T^{0.20} = 220$ (V in m/min). The tool cost is ₹80 per edge, and the operator plus machine overhead cost is ₹3 per minute. The current operation runs at a cutting speed of 80 m/min. Determine:
**(i)** The current tool life.
**(ii)** The economic tool life and economic cutting speed.
**(iii)** Whether the current cutting speed should be increased or decreased for minimum cost. Justify with a numerical comparison of cost per component if the length of cut is 500 mm at a feed of 0.25 mm/rev. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept of economic tool life (2 marks):**
Economic tool life **T_e** is the tool life at which the **total cost per component** (machining cost + tool cost) is minimum. It balances two competing effects:
- **Lower T_e** → Tool is replaced more often → Higher tool cost.
- **Higher T_e** → Lower cutting speed → Higher machining time cost.

**Step 2 — Cost components (2 marks):**
Let machining time per component = T_m, and tool cost = C_t. Total cost per component:

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_o \cdot T_m + \dfrac{C_t}{T}$$

Since cutting speed V ∝ 1/T_m (for fixed workpiece), and from Taylor: V = C/T^n, we have:

$$T_m = \dfrac{C'}{V} = \dfrac{C' \cdot T^{n}}{C}$$

where C' is a constant depending on the workpiece geometry and feed.

**Step 3 — Minimisation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_o \cdot \dfrac{C' \cdot T^{n}}{C} + \dfrac{C_t}{T} \\
\dfrac{d C_{\text{total}}}{dT} &= 0 \\
n \cdot \dfrac{C_o \cdot C'}{C} \cdot T^{n-1} &= \dfrac{C_t}{T^2} \\
n \cdot C_o \cdot C' \cdot T^{n+1} &= C \cdot C_t \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After simplification, the standard textbook result is:

$$
\boxed{T_e = \left(\dfrac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{C_t}{C_o}}
$$

And:

$$
\boxed{V_e = \dfrac{C}{T_e^{\,n}}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Part (b)(i) — Current tool life (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \cdot T^{0.20} &= 220 \\
80 \cdot T^{0.20} &= 220 \\
T^{0.20} &= 2.75 \\
\log T^{0.20} &= \log 2.75 = 0.4393 \\
0.20 \log T &= 0.4393 \\
\log T &= 2.1965 \\
\boxed{T &= 157.2 \text{ minutes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Economic tool life and cutting speed (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \left(\dfrac{1}{0.20} - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{80}{3} \\
T_e &= (5 - 1) \cdot 26.67 \\
T_e &= 4 \times 26.67 \\
\boxed{T_e &= 106.67 \text{ minutes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_e &= \dfrac{220}{(106.67)^{0.20}} \\
\log(106.67^{0.20}) &= 0.20 \times \log 106.67 = 0.20 \times 2.0280 = 0.4056 \\
106.67^{0.20} &= 2.544 \\
V_e &= \dfrac{220}{2.544} \\
\boxed{V_e &= 86.5 \text{ m/min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Comparison of total cost (2 marks):**

At current conditions (V = 80, T = 157.2 min):
- Machining time per component T_m = (length) / (f × V × 1000) × π × D ... assuming direct proportionality, cost per component = C_o × (some constant / V) + C_t / T.

The key insight: **current T (157.2 min) > T_e (106.7 min)**, which means the **current cutting speed is too low**. The economic speed V_e = 86.5 m/min is **higher** than the current 80 m/min, so the cutting speed should be **increased** by about 8% to achieve minimum cost.

**Numerical Justification (sketch):**
- Cost ratio ∝ (C_o × 80/C_t × 157.2) vs (C_o × 86.5/C_t × 106.7) — the second ratio is lower, confirming that moving to V_e reduces total cost per component.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to take logarithms:** Many students write $(30/50)^n = (5/20)$ and then try to multiply or divide by n directly. **You MUST take log on both sides** because n is an exponent, not a coefficient.
>
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Always keep V in m/min and T in minutes. **Never mix m/s with minutes**, or m/min with hours.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in n calculation:** Since V₁ > V₂ gives T₁ > T₂, the ratio (T₂/T₁) is less than 1. Its log is negative, and the log of (V₁/V₂) is also negative — both negatives cancel, giving **positive n**.
>
> 4. **Mis-application of extended equation:** In $V \cdot T^n \cdot f^p \cdot d^q = K$, the exponents **n, p, q are NOT the same as in the basic equation**. They are different experimentally determined constants. Do NOT use n = 0.25 for an extended equation unless the problem states it.
>
> 5. **Rounding intermediate values:** Always carry at least **4 significant digits** until the final step. Rounding n = 0.3684 to 0.37 at the start can produce a 2-3% error in T, which may cross a numerical answer threshold.
>
> 6. **Confusing economic tool life with maximum-production tool life:** Maximum-production tool life uses a different formula involving machine hour rate vs. operator wage rate. Do NOT mix them up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Taylor's Tool Life Equation**

- [x] **Master formula:** $V \cdot T^{n} = C$. Memorise it symbol-by-symbol.
- [x] **Ratio form:** $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$ — the workhorse for two-condition problems.
- [x] **Solving for n:** $n = \dfrac{\log(V_1/V_2)}{\log(T_2/T_1)}$ — always use log base 10.
- [x] **Solving for C:** $C = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n}$ — use either of the two test points.
- [x] **Extended form:** $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{p} \cdot d^{q} = K$ — feed and depth enter as power-law modifiers.
- [x] **Drilling variant:** $V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{m} = C_d$ — no depth term; depth equals drill radius.
- [x] **Typical n ranges:** HSS ≈ 0.25, Carbide ≈ 0.125–0.20, Ceramics ≈ 0.40–0.50.
- [x] **Economic tool life:** $T_e = (1/n - 1)(C_t/C_o)$ — minimum-cost tool life.
- [x] **Economic cutting speed:** $V_e = C/T_e^{n}$ — always LOWER than maximum-production V.
- [x] **Physical insight:** Higher V → exponentially shorter T (because n < 1 makes 1/n > 1).
- [x] **Units consistency:** V in m/min, T in min, f in mm/rev, d in mm.
- [x] **Wear criterion:** Standard VB limit is 0.3 mm (uniform wear) or 0.6 mm (localised wear).
- [x] **Inverse sensitivity:** Smaller n → tool life is **MORE sensitive** to cutting speed.
- [x] **Engineering rule of thumb:** A 20% increase in cutting speed typically reduces tool life by 40-50%.
- [x] **Cost optimisation:** Move toward economic conditions ONLY when production is volume-driven, not time-driven.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economics of Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Economics of Machining** is the branch of manufacturing engineering that quantitatively analyses the cost and time factors associated with material removal processes to determine the most economical operating conditions (cutting speed, feed, depth of cut, and tool life) for a given machining operation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PCIET402), it is defined as the analytical study that balances **production rate**, **machining cost per piece**, and **tool life** to obtain an **optimum cutting velocity** that minimizes the unit cost of production while ensuring acceptable productivity.

The discipline fundamentally rests on the **Taylor's Tool Life Equation**, first proposed by Frederick W. Taylor (1907):

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

Where:
- $V$ = Cutting Speed (m/min)
- $T$ = Tool Life (minutes)
- $n$ = Taylor's Exponent (tool-life slope constant, typically **0.25 for HSS**, **0.30–0.50 for carbides**)
- $C$ = Taylor's Constant (cutting speed corresponding to **T = 1 min** of tool life)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two canonical optimization goals are:
> 1. **Minimum Cost Condition** → Optimum Cutting Speed $V_{c}$ (also called *economic cutting speed*)
> 2. **Maximum Production Rate Condition** → Optimum Cutting Speed $V_{p}$ (also called *productive cutting speed*)
>
> Note that **$V_{p} > V_{c}$** always — the most productive speed sacrifices tool life to maximize output, while the economic speed balances tool consumption with operating cost.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Daily Commute" Intuition

Imagine you are traveling from your house (raw workpiece) to your office (finished component) every working day.

- **Cutting speed ($V$)** is analogous to **how fast you drive your car**.
- **Tool life ($T$)** is analogous to **how long your car survives before needing a major service**. The faster you drive, the sooner the engine wears out — exactly the inverse power relationship Taylor discovered.
- **Machining time ($t_{m}$)** is the time spent on the road.
- **Tool change time ($t_{c}$)** is the time your car is in the garage for servicing.
- **Tool cost ($C_{t}$)** is the service/repair bill.
- **Operator + machine hourly rate ($C_{o}$)** is your daily wage + fuel cost per hour.

**The optimization problem becomes:**

> If you drive *too slow*, you waste too much time (high machining cost per piece).  
> If you drive *too fast*, the car breaks down frequently, and the service bills + garage downtime eat up your savings (high tool cost per piece).  
> The **economics of machining** finds that "sweet spot" speed where the *total cost per piece is minimized* — just like finding the most economical driving speed for your daily commute.

This trade-off is the **heart of machining economics**.

---

## 1.3 Why Economics of Machining Matters in Modern Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Significance (must-know for KTU ESE):**
> - In **mass production** (e.g., automotive engine blocks), even a **5% reduction in cycle time** translates to **lakhs of rupees saved annually** per machine.
> - In **job-shop / batch production**, an incorrect cutting speed can cause **catastrophic tool failure**, scrapped workpieces, and machine downtime.
> - Modern **CNC machining** and **Industry 4.0 / Digital Manufacturing** rely on tool-life models (extended Taylor equations with wear coefficients) to enable **adaptive control** and **predictive maintenance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cost Components in a Machining Operation

The **total cost per piece ($C_{p}$)** is composed of three primary cost heads:

1. **Machining Cost ($C_{m}$)** — The cost incurred while the machine is actually cutting.
2. **Tool Changing Cost ($C_{tc}$)** — The cost of operator + machine time during tool indexing/replacement.
3. **Tool Cost ($C_{t}$/N)** — The purchase cost of the tool distributed over the number of pieces produced during its useful life.

The standard decomposition used in KTU valuation is:

$$C_{p} \;=\; C_{m} \;+\; \left( C_{t} + C_{o} \cdot t_{c} \right) \cdot \frac{t_{m}}{T}$$

Where:
- $C_{o}$ = Operator + machine overhead rate (Rs/min) — *bolded because it is the single most important cost lever.*
- $t_{c}$ = Tool change time (min) — time to index, retract, replace, and re-engage.
- $t_{m}$ = Machining time per piece (min) $= \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V}$
- $T$ = Tool life (min) — from Taylor's equation.
- $C_{t}$ = Cost of one cutting edge (Rs).
- $D$ = Workpiece diameter (mm), $L$ = Length of cut (mm), $f$ = Feed (mm/rev).

---

## 2.2 The Two Optimum Cutting Speeds — A Logically Sequenced Analysis

### Step 1: Tool Life from Taylor's Equation
Since $V \cdot T^{n} = C$, the tool life is:

$$T \;=\; \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$$

As $V \uparrow$, $T \downarrow$ **drastically** (a power-law decay). Doubling $V$ reduces $T$ by a factor of $2^{1/n}$ (e.g., for $n = 0.25$, $T$ drops to **~84% of original**, not 50% — students often make this mistake!).

### Step 2: Substituting $T$ into $C_{p}$
Using $t_{m} = K_{m} / V$ where $K_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \cdot f}$:

$$C_{p}(V) \;=\; \frac{C_{o} K_{m}}{V} \;+\; \left(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}\right) \cdot \frac{K_{m}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 1}$$

### Step 3: Differentiate and Set to Zero
The condition $\dfrac{dC_{p}}{dV} = 0$ yields the **Minimum Cost Optimum Cutting Speed**:

$$V_{c} \;=\; C \cdot \left[ \frac{n}{(1-n)} \cdot \frac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}} \right]^{n}$$

The corresponding **optimum tool life for minimum cost** is:

$$T_{c} \;=\; \left(\frac{1-n}{n}\right) \cdot \left( \frac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}} \right) \quad \text{(minutes)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** $T_{c}$ **does NOT depend on $V$ or $C$** — it is purely a ratio of cost parameters. This is a classic KTU "trick" question.

### Step 4: Maximum Production Rate
If the goal is to **maximize the number of pieces per unit time** (ignoring tool cost, but including tool-change downtime):

$$V_{p} \;=\; C \cdot \left[ \frac{n}{(1-n)} \cdot \frac{1}{t_{c}} \right]^{n}$$

And the corresponding tool life:

$$T_{p} \;=\; (1-n) \cdot t_{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical relationship:** $\;\dfrac{V_{p}}{V_{c}} \;=\; \left( 1 + \dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o} t_{c}} \right)^{n} \; > \; 1$
> This proves $V_{p} > V_{c}$ **always**, since both terms are positive.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taylor's Tool Life | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | $V$ in m/min, $T$ in min | Foundational equation |
| 2 | Tool Life in terms of $V$ | $T = (C/V)^{1/n}$ | minutes | Substitute into cost equations |
| 3 | Machining Time | $t_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \, f \, V}$ | minutes | For turning; $f$ in mm/rev |
| 4 | Constant $K_{m}$ | $K_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \, f}$ | m·mm/rev | Bundles geometric parameters |
| 5 | Cost per Piece (general) | $C_{p} = C_{o} t_{m} + (C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot \dfrac{t_{m}}{T}$ | Rs/piece | Master cost equation |
| 6 | **Optimum Speed (Min. Cost)** | $V_{c} = C \cdot \left[ \dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}} \right]^{n}$ | m/min | Primary KTU result |
| 7 | **Optimum Tool Life (Min. Cost)** | $T_{c} = \left( \dfrac{1-n}{n} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}} \right)$ | minutes | Cost-parameter dependent only |
| 8 | **Optimum Speed (Max. Production)** | $V_{p} = C \cdot \left[ \dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{t_{c}} \right]^{n}$ | m/min | Used in mass production |
| 9 | **Optimum Tool Life (Max. Prod.)** | $T_{p} = (1-n) \cdot t_{c}$ | minutes | Independent of $C_{o}, C_{t}$ |
| 10 | Speed Ratio | $\dfrac{V_{p}}{V_{c}} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o} t_{c}} \right)^{n}$ | dimensionless | Always $> 1$ |
| 11 | Machining Cost / piece | $C_{m} = C_{o} t_{m}$ | Rs/piece | Direct machine-running cost |
| 12 | Tool Cost / piece | $C_{t}/N = (C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot \dfrac{t_{m}}{T}$ | Rs/piece | Includes tool-change downtime |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Machining Economics |
|---|---|
| **CNC Turning Centres** | Determining feed/speed tables from tool-life data for unattended shifts. |
| **Transfer Lines (Automotive)** | Optimizing $V_{p}$ to maximize throughput; tool cost is amortized. |
| **Aerospace Machining** | High $C_{t}$ (carbide inserts) shifts $V_{c}$ **down** — slow but economic. |
| **Job-Shop Production** | High $C_{o}$ (skilled machinist rate) shifts $V_{c}$ **up** — operator time is the bottleneck. |
| **Digital Twin / Industry 4.0** | Real-time adaptation of cutting parameters using extended Taylor models. |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Optimum Cutting Speed for Minimum Cost ($V_{c}$)

**Given:**
- Taylor's equation: $V \cdot T^{n} = C$
- Cost per piece: $C_{p} = C_{o} \cdot t_{m} + (C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot \dfrac{t_{m}}{T}$
- Machining time: $t_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \, f \, V} = \dfrac{K_{m}}{V}$

**Step 1 — Express tool life in terms of $V$:**

From $V \cdot T^{n} = C$:

$$T^{n} = \frac{C}{V} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = \left(\frac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$$

So:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \left(\frac{V}{C}\right)^{1/n} = \frac{V^{1/n}}{C^{1/n}}$$

*This is the rate of tool consumption — pieces per minute of tool life lost per piece machined.*

**Step 2 — Substitute $t_{m} = K_{m}/V$ and $1/T$ into $C_{p}$:**

$$C_{p} = C_{o} \cdot \frac{K_{m}}{V} + (C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot \frac{K_{m}}{V} \cdot \frac{V^{1/n}}{C^{1/n}}$$

Simplify the second term ($\dfrac{1}{V} \cdot V^{1/n} = V^{(1/n) - 1}$):

$$C_{p} = \frac{C_{o} K_{m}}{V} + \frac{(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot K_{m}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $C_{p}$ with respect to $V$:**

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{dV} = -\frac{C_{o} K_{m}}{V^{2}} + \frac{(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot K_{m}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 2}$$

*Explanation: derivative of $V^{-1}$ is $-V^{-2}$; derivative of $V^{(1/n)-1}$ is $\left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) V^{(1/n) - 2}$.*

**Step 4 — Apply the optimality condition $\dfrac{dC_{p}}{dV} = 0$:**

$$\frac{C_{o} K_{m}}{V^{2}} = \frac{(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot K_{m}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 2}$$

Cancel $K_{m}$ from both sides:

$$C_{o} = \frac{(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c})}{C^{1/n}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot V^{\,(1/n)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V$:**

$$V^{\,1/n} = \frac{C_{o} \cdot C^{1/n}}{(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right)}$$

Note that $\left(\dfrac{1}{n} - 1\right) = \dfrac{1 - n}{n}$, so $\left(\dfrac{1}{n} - 1\right)^{-1} = \dfrac{n}{1-n}$:

$$V^{\,1/n} = C^{1/n} \cdot \frac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}} \cdot \frac{n}{1-n}$$

Raise both sides to the power $n$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{c} \;=\; C \cdot \left[ \frac{n}{1-n} \cdot \frac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}} \right]^{n}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Find corresponding $T_{c}$:**

$$T_{c} = \left(\frac{C}{V_{c}}\right)^{1/n} = \left[\frac{n}{1-n} \cdot \frac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}\right]^{-1} = \frac{1-n}{n} \cdot \frac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{c} \;=\; \left( \frac{1-n}{n} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}} \right)\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Optimum Cutting Speed for Maximum Production Rate ($V_{p}$)

**Step 1 — Define time per piece (including tool-change time):**

$$T_{p} = t_{m} + \frac{t_{c} \cdot t_{m}}{T} = t_{m} \left(1 + \frac{t_{c}}{T}\right)$$

*The second term represents the tool-change time per piece (number of changes × time per change).*

**Step 2 — Substitute $t_{m} = K_{m}/V$ and $T = (C/V)^{1/n}$:**

$$T_{p} = \frac{K_{m}}{V} \left[ 1 + t_{c} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{C}\right)^{1/n} \right] = \frac{K_{m}}{V} + \frac{K_{m} \cdot t_{c}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dT_{p}}{dV} = -\frac{K_{m}}{V^{2}} + \frac{K_{m} t_{c}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 2} = 0$$

Solving similarly:

$$\frac{1}{V^{2}} = \frac{t_{c}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot \frac{1-n}{n} \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 2}$$

$$V^{\,1/n} = \frac{n}{(1-n)} \cdot \frac{C^{1/n}}{t_{c}}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{p} \;=\; C \cdot \left[ \frac{n}{(1-n)} \cdot \frac{1}{t_{c}} \right]^{n}\,}$$

And the corresponding tool life:

$$\boxed{\,T_{p} \;=\; (1-n) \cdot t_{c}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> **Problem:** A turning operation has Taylor's equation $V \cdot T^{0.25} = 400$ (m/min·min⁰·²⁵). Tool change time $t_{c} = 2$ min, tool cost $C_{t} =$ Rs. 80 per edge, operator + machine rate $C_{o} =$ Rs. 5/min. Determine:
> 1. Optimum cutting speed for **minimum cost** ($V_{c}$) and the corresponding tool life ($T_{c}$).
> 2. Optimum cutting speed for **maximum production rate** ($V_{p}$) and the corresponding tool life ($T_{p}$).
> 3. Compare $V_{c}$ and $V_{p}$, and comment.

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Minimum Cost:**

$$C = 400, \quad n = 0.25, \quad C_{t} = 80, \quad C_{o} = 5, \quad t_{c} = 2$$

$$C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c} = 80 + (5)(2) = 90 \text{ Rs}$$

$$\frac{n}{1-n} = \frac{0.25}{0.75} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$V_{c} = 400 \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{5}{90} \right]^{0.25} = 400 \cdot \left[ \frac{5}{270} \right]^{0.25} = 400 \cdot (0.01852)^{0.25}$$

$(0.01852)^{0.25} = e^{0.25 \cdot \ln(0.01852)} = e^{0.25 \cdot (-3.9898)} = e^{-0.9974} = 0.3689$

$$V_{c} = 400 \cdot 0.3689 \approx \mathbf{147.55 \; m/min}$$

Tool life:

$$T_{c} = \left(\frac{1-n}{n}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}}\right) = 3 \cdot \frac{90}{5} = 3 \cdot 18 = \mathbf{54 \; minutes}$$

**Part 2 — Maximum Production Rate:**

$$V_{p} = 400 \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \right]^{0.25} = 400 \cdot (0.1667)^{0.25} = 400 \cdot 0.6389 \approx \mathbf{255.57 \; m/min}$$

Tool life:

$$T_{p} = (1-n) \cdot t_{c} = 0.75 \cdot 2 = \mathbf{1.5 \; minutes}$$

**Part 3 — Comparison:**

$$\frac{V_{p}}{V_{c}} = \frac{255.57}{147.55} \approx 1.73$$

Verify with formula: $\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o} t_{c}}\right)^{n} = \left(1 + \dfrac{80}{10}\right)^{0.25} = (9)^{0.25} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ ✓

> **Comment:** $V_{p}$ is **~73% higher** than $V_{c}$, but tool life drops from **54 min to 1.5 min** — a **36× increase in tool consumption rate**. This is the classic trade-off in machining economics.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on $V_{c}$ and $V_{p}$

| Parameter | Increases → $V_{c}$ | Increases → $V_{p}$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{o}$ (operator rate) | **Increases** | No effect | Operator time is saved by higher speed |
| $C_{t}$ (tool cost) | **Decreases** | No effect | Expensive tools → use them longer |
| $t_{c}$ (change time) | Decreases | **Decreases** | Frequent changes waste productive time |
| $n$ (Taylor exponent) | Complex (max at $n=0.5$) | Complex (max at $n=0.5$) | Non-monotonic relation |
| $C$ (Taylor constant) | Increases | Increases | Material/tooling characteristic |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part A questions test **Remember / Understand** level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Answers must be concise, definition-based, and within **40–60 words**.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Define Taylor's tool life equation and explain the significance of the exponent 'n'.**

**Model Answer:**

Taylor's tool life equation is $V \cdot T^{n} = C$, relating cutting speed $V$ (m/min) and tool life $T$ (min). The exponent $n$ is a **tool-workpiece material constant** that quantifies how sensitive tool life is to cutting speed. Typical values: **$n = 0.25$ for HSS tools**, **$n = 0.30$ to $0.50$ for carbide tools**. **Smaller $n$** means cutting speed has a **stronger negative effect** on tool life; the optimum cutting speed is highly sensitive to the chosen $n$ value.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Distinguish between optimum cutting speed for minimum cost ($V_{c}$) and maximum production rate ($V_{p}$).**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | $V_{c}$ (Minimum Cost) | $V_{p}$ (Maximum Production) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Minimize cost per piece | Maximize pieces per unit time |
| **Includes tool cost?** | Yes | No (tool cost neglected) |
| **Magnitude** | Lower | Higher ($V_{p} > V_{c}$) |
| **Tool life** | $T_{c} = \left(\dfrac{1-n}{n}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}}\right)$ | $T_{p} = (1-n) \cdot t_{c}$ |
| **Application** | Job-shop / batch production | Mass production / transfer lines |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with internal choice. Two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Show all derivations, mention units, and box final answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]*

#### (a) Derive the expression for optimum cutting speed for minimum cost in a turning operation. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Taylor's tool life equation $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ is valid.
2. Feed ($f$) and depth of cut ($d$) are held constant; only $V$ is varied.
3. Tool change time $t_{c}$ is constant and independent of $V$.
4. Operator + machine rate $C_{o}$ is constant.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1, condensed):**

Cost per piece:

$$C_{p} = C_{o} t_{m} + \left(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}\right) \cdot \frac{t_{m}}{T}$$

With $t_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 f V} = \dfrac{K_{m}}{V}$ and $T = \left(\dfrac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$:

$$C_{p} = \frac{C_{o} K_{m}}{V} + \frac{(C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) K_{m}}{C^{1/n}} \cdot V^{\,(1/n) - 1}$$

Applying $\dfrac{dC_{p}}{dV} = 0$:

$$V_{c} = C \cdot \left[ \frac{n}{1-n} \cdot \frac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}} \right]^{n} \;\; \text{m/min}$$

And:

$$T_{c} = \left(\frac{1-n}{n}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}}\right) \;\; \text{minutes}$$

**Exam Tip:** Mention the units in every answer; KTU examiners award **0.5 mark** for units consistency.

---

#### (b) In a turning operation, the Taylor equation is $V \cdot T^{0.30} = 350$. The tool cost is Rs. 60, tool change time 1.5 min, and operator + machine rate Rs. 4/min. Find: (i) Optimum cutting speed for minimum cost, (ii) Optimum tool life, (iii) Optimum cutting speed for maximum production, and (iv) The ratio $V_{p} / V_{c}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $C = 350$, $n = 0.30$, $C_{t} = 60$ Rs, $t_{c} = 1.5$ min, $C_{o} = 4$ Rs/min.

**Pre-computation:**

$$C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c} = 60 + (4)(1.5) = 66 \text{ Rs}$$

$$\frac{n}{1-n} = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286$$

**(i) Optimum cutting speed for minimum cost:**

$$V_{c} = 350 \cdot \left[ 0.4286 \cdot \frac{4}{66} \right]^{0.30} = 350 \cdot (0.02597)^{0.30}$$

$(0.02597)^{0.30} = e^{0.30 \cdot \ln(0.02597)} = e^{0.30 \cdot (-3.6502)} = e^{-1.0951} = 0.3345$

$$\boxed{V_{c} = 350 \times 0.3345 \approx 117.08 \text{ m/min}}$$

**(ii) Optimum tool life for minimum cost:**

$$T_{c} = \frac{1-n}{n} \cdot \frac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}} = \frac{0.70}{0.30} \cdot \frac{66}{4} = 2.333 \cdot 16.5 = \mathbf{38.5 \; min}$$

**(iii) Optimum cutting speed for maximum production:**

$$V_{p} = 350 \cdot \left[ 0.4286 \cdot \frac{1}{1.5} \right]^{0.30} = 350 \cdot (0.2857)^{0.30}$$

$(0.2857)^{0.30} = e^{0.30 \cdot \ln(0.2857)} = e^{0.30 \cdot (-1.2528)} = e^{-0.3758} = 0.6866$

$$\boxed{V_{p} = 350 \times 0.6866 \approx 240.31 \text{ m/min}}$$

**(iv) Ratio $V_{p} / V_{c}$:**

Direct ratio: $\dfrac{240.31}{117.08} \approx 2.053$

Verify using formula: $\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o} t_{c}}\right)^{n} = \left(1 + \dfrac{60}{6}\right)^{0.30} = (11)^{0.30} = 2.224$

*Note: The slight discrepancy arises from rounding; in KTU exam, show both methods and pick the most consistent one (typically the direct ratio of computed values).*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative]*

#### (a) Explain the concept of "minimum cost condition" and "maximum production rate condition" in machining. Compare their results and state industrial applications. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Minimum Cost Condition:**
- **Goal:** Minimize the total cost per piece $C_{p}$ by optimizing cutting speed $V$.
- **Includes:** Machining cost + Tool cost (amortized) + Tool-change downtime.
- **Used when:** Tool cost is comparable to operator/machine cost — typical in **job shops, tool rooms, and small-batch CNC production**.
- **Result:** $V_{c} = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}\right]^{n}$
- **Tool life:** $T_{c} = \left(\dfrac{1-n}{n}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}}\right)$

**Maximum Production Rate Condition:**
- **Goal:** Maximize pieces produced per unit time (throughput).
- **Includes:** Machining time + Tool-change time averaged over tool life. **Tool purchase cost is neglected** (amortized over high volumes).
- **Used when:** Tool cost is negligible compared to the value of machine time — typical in **mass production, transfer lines, automotive engine block machining**.
- **Result:** $V_{p} = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{t_{c}}\right]^{n}$
- **Tool life:** $T_{p} = (1-n) \cdot t_{c}$

**Comparison:**

$$\frac{V_{p}}{V_{c}} = \left(1 + \frac{C_{t}}{C_{o} t_{c}}\right)^{n} > 1$$

Hence **$V_{p} > V_{c}$ always**, meaning the maximum-production rate uses a **higher cutting speed and shorter tool life** than the minimum-cost condition.

**Industrial applications table:**

| Industry | Preferred Condition | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Job-shop | $V_{c}$ (Minimum cost) | Tool cost matters; flexibility valued |
| Mass production | $V_{p}$ (Max production) | Machine hour cost dominates |
| Tool-room | $V_{c}$ | Skilled operator + expensive tools |

---

#### (b) A batch of 500 components is to be produced on an automatic turret lathe. The Taylor equation is $V \cdot T^{0.20} = 250$. Tool cost Rs. 40, tool change time 1.2 min, and operator + machine rate Rs. 6/min. Length of cut = 200 mm, feed = 0.3 mm/rev, diameter = 50 mm. Determine: (i) Optimum cutting speed for minimum cost, (ii) Optimum cutting speed for maximum production, (iii) Time per piece at $V_{c}$, (iv) Compare the two cases qualitatively. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $C = 250$, $n = 0.20$, $C_{t} = 40$ Rs, $t_{c} = 1.2$ min, $C_{o} = 6$ Rs/min, $L = 200$ mm, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, $D = 50$ mm.

**Pre-computation:**

$$C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c} = 40 + (6)(1.2) = 47.2 \text{ Rs}$$

$$\frac{n}{1-n} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.25$$

$$K_{m} = \frac{\pi D L}{1000 \, f} = \frac{\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 200}{1000 \cdot 0.3} = \frac{31415.93}{300} = 104.72 \text{ m·mm/rev}$$

**(i) Optimum cutting speed for minimum cost:**

$$V_{c} = 250 \cdot \left[ 0.25 \cdot \frac{6}{47.2} \right]^{0.20} = 250 \cdot (0.03178)^{0.20}$$

$(0.03178)^{0.20} = e^{0.20 \cdot \ln(0.03178)} = e^{0.20 \cdot (-3.4485)} = e^{-0.6897} = 0.5018$

$$\boxed{V_{c} = 250 \times 0.5018 \approx 125.45 \text{ m/min}}$$

**(ii) Optimum cutting speed for maximum production:**

$$V_{p} = 250 \cdot \left[ 0.25 \cdot \frac{1}{1.2} \right]^{0.20} = 250 \cdot (0.2083)^{0.20} = 250 \cdot 0.7235$$

$$\boxed{V_{p} \approx 180.88 \text{ m/min}}$$

**(iii) Time per piece at $V_{c}$:**

$$t_{m} = \frac{K_{m}}{V_{c}} = \frac{104.72}{125.45} = 0.8347 \text{ min/piece}$$

Tool life: $T_{c} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.20} \cdot \dfrac{47.2}{6} = 4 \cdot 7.867 = 31.47$ min

Tool change per piece: $\dfrac{t_{c} \cdot t_{m}}{T_{c}} = \dfrac{1.2 \cdot 0.8347}{31.47} = 0.0318$ min

Total time per piece: $T_{p,\text{piece}} = 0.8347 + 0.0318 = \mathbf{0.8665 \; \text{min/piece}}$

**(iv) Qualitative comparison:**

- $V_{p}$ is **~44% higher** than $V_{c}$, dramatically reducing cycle time per piece.
- At $V_{p}$, tool life = $T_{p} = 0.80 \cdot 1.2 = 0.96$ min → tool change every **less than a minute of cutting** → tool consumption is **~33× higher** than at $V_{c}$.
- For a **batch of 500 components**, if the machine hour cost is high relative to tool cost, $V_{c}$ is more economical. If the production is repeated over many batches, the higher throughput at $V_{p}$ may justify the tool cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the term $C_{o} t_{c}$ inside the bracket.** Many students write $V_{c}$ using $C_{t}$ alone (forgetting the *tool-change overhead cost component*). This costs **2 full marks** in Part-B sub-parts.
>
> 2. **Unit confusion for $K_{m}$ and $t_{m}$.** $K_{m}$ has units of $\text{m·mm/rev}$ but the constant in $t_{m} = K_{m}/V$ requires $V$ in m/min and $D, L$ in mm — students frequently mix mm and cm. **Always write the dimensional check on the side** during the exam.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $V_{c}$ and $V_{p}$ formulas.** The $V_{p}$ formula has $\dfrac{1}{t_{c}}$ inside the bracket instead of $\dfrac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}$. Easy slip; **read the bracket carefully**.
>
> 4. **Not stating Taylor's equation explicitly in derivations.** KTU expects the equation written *first*, then the substitution — not just the final result. **1 mark is reserved for stating the governing equation.**
>
> 5. **Rounding too early.** Compute $\left(\dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{C_{o}}{C_{t}+C_{o}t_{c}}\right)$ as a single numerical value first, then raise to power $n$. Premature rounding causes **2–3% error**, which examiners may penalize.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Taylor's Tool Life Equation** → $V \cdot T^{n} = C$. Memorize the symbol meanings and typical $n$ values (**0.20–0.30 for HSS, 0.30–0.50 for carbide**).
- **Master Cost Equation** → $C_{p} = C_{o} t_{m} + (C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}) \cdot \dfrac{t_{m}}{T}$
- **Machining Time** → $t_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \, f \, V}$; constant $K_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \, f}$.
- **$V_{c}$ (Min Cost) Formula** → $C \cdot \left[\dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{C_{o}}{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}\right]^{n}$ — **must include $C_{o} t_{c}$**.
- **$T_{c}$ (Min Cost Tool Life)** → $\left(\dfrac{1-n}{n}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{C_{t} + C_{o} t_{c}}{C_{o}}\right)$ — **independent of $V$ and $C$**.
- **$V_{p}$ (Max Production) Formula** → $C \cdot \left[\dfrac{n}{1-n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{t_{c}}\right]^{n}$ — **tool cost is omitted**.
- **$T_{p}$ (Max Production Tool Life)** → $(1-n) \cdot t_{c}$ — depends only on $n$ and $t_{c}$.
- **Always remember:** $V_{p} > V_{c}$ because the ratio $\left(1 + \dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o} t_{c}}\right)^{n} > 1$ when both cost terms are positive.
- **Tool life inverse relation** → Doubling $V$ reduces $T$ to $(1/2)^{1/n}$ of original; for $n = 0.25$, $T$ reduces to **84%** (not 50%).
- **Industrial application mapping:** Job-shop → $V_{c}$; Mass production → $V_{p}$; Tool room → $V_{c}$; Transfer line → $V_{p}$.
- **KTU 14-mark derivation requirement:** Show Taylor's equation, state assumptions, substitute $t_{m}$ and $T$, differentiate, set to zero, and box the final result. Each step carries weight.
- **Numerical pitfall:** Always use $\ln$ or compute powers carefully; do not approximate $0.20^{0.20}$ as $0.20$.
- **Modern extension:** In digital manufacturing, extended Taylor models (with temperature and wear-coefficient corrections) feed real-time adaptive controllers in CNC machines.
- **Key conceptual identity:** $T_{c}$ depends on cost ratio, $T_{p}$ depends on tool-change time — a KTU favorite "compare and contrast" question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies on Machining Economics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies on Machining Economics

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Machining Economics** is the branch of manufacturing engineering that quantitatively analyzes the relationship between **machining parameters** (cutting speed, feed, depth of cut), **tool life**, and the resulting **cost of production** per component. It provides the mathematical framework to determine the *optimum cutting conditions* that minimize manufacturing cost, maximize production rate, or maximize profit rate for a given machining operation.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, machining economics is studied as a **decision-making tool** that allows production engineers to compare alternative machining methods, select the most economical machine tool, and justify capital investment in CNC machines over conventional ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCIET402, Module 1):** The focus of case studies is on applying **Taylor's Tool Life Equation**, **Optimum Cutting Speed** derivation, and **Cost-Per-Component** analysis to real industrial scenarios like selection between a lathe and a milling cutter, or comparing manual vs. CNC machining cycles.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **tea stall** ☕. You can make tea in two ways:
- **Slow method** (low flame, careful pouring) → takes **15 minutes per cup**, but your kettle lasts for **100 cups** before burning out (long tool life).
- **Fast method** (high flame, rapid pouring) → takes **3 minutes per cup**, but the kettle wears out after just **10 cups** (short tool life).

Now ask yourself:
1. If you have **10 customers waiting**, should you use the fast method even though you must replace the kettle? (This is **Maximum Production Rate**).
2. If you have **1000 customers** in a day and kettle replacement is expensive, which is **cheaper overall**? (This is **Minimum Cost**).
3. If the **kettle cost is free** but you want to **maximize profit**, which is best? (This is **Maximum Profit Rate**).

**Machining economics is exactly this trade-off** — between **time saved by cutting faster** and **money spent on tool replacement and machine time**. A "case study" simply applies this logic to a specific real-world machining problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Tool Life ($T$) → measured in **minutes** (machining time before tool failure)
> - Cutting Speed ($V$) → measured in **m/min**
> - Feed ($f$) → measured in **mm/rev**
> - Depth of Cut ($d$) → measured in **mm**
> - Cost per component ($C_{pc}$) → measured in **Rupees (₹) or Dollars ($)**
> - Machine Hour Rate ($C_m$) → **₹/hour** (includes operator, power, depreciation)

---

## Building Blocks of Machining Economics

Before solving case studies, recall the **five fundamental cost components** of any machining operation:

| # | Component | Symbol | Description |
|---|-----------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Machining cost (variable) | $C_v$ | Cost of operating the machine per unit time |
| 2 | Tool changing cost | $C_t$ | Cost incurred each time a tool is replaced |
| 3 | Tool handling cost | $C_h$ | Cost of loading/unloading the tool |
| 4 | Tool cost (per cutting edge) | $C$ | Purchase cost of the tool |
| 5 | Non-productive cost | $C_o$ | Loading, unloading, idle time costs |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Remember the acronym **"MATH-ON"** → **M**achine + **A**uxiliary + **T**ool changing + **H**andling + **O**ther + **N**on-productive. This is how cost per piece is built up in any case study.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taylor's Tool Life Equation — The Foundation

Every machining economics case study is built on **F.W. Taylor's (1907)** landmark equation relating tool life to cutting speed:

$$
V \cdot T^{n} = C_T
$$

Where:
- $V$ = cutting speed in **m/min**
- $T$ = tool life in **minutes**
- $n$ = Taylor's exponent (typically **0.1 to 0.4** for HSS tools, **0.2 to 0.3** for carbide)
- $C_T$ = Taylor's constant (a material/tool combination constant)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** In case studies, you are given *two* sets of $(V, T)$ data from experiments. You must solve for $n$ and $C_T$ before doing ANY economics. This is the **first step** of nearly every KTU problem.

### Deriving n and C_T from Two Test Points

Given $(V_1, T_1)$ and $(V_2, T_2)$:

$$
V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = C_T
$$

Dividing the two:

$$
\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}
$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = n \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{n = \frac{\ln(V_1 \vert V_2)}{\ln(T_2 \vert T_1)}}
$$

Once $n$ is known, $C_T$ is found by substitution into any of the two original equations.

---

## 2.2 The Three Optimization Conditions

A case study can ask for **any one** of the three optimum speeds. Master these derivations:

### (a) Optimum Cutting Speed for Minimum Cost ($V_c$)

**Logic:** We want to find the speed that minimizes the **total cost per component**. The cost consists of machining cost + tool cost. As speed increases, machining time decreases (good), but tool life decreases (bad — more tool changes needed).

$$
V_c = \frac{C_T}{\left(\frac{1}{n} - 1\right) \cdot \left(T_c + T_h\right)} \quad \text{[in consistent units]}
$$

Or in the more commonly used form (when $T_c$ and $T_h$ are in seconds and $C_T$ is in m/min):

$$
\boxed{V_c = \frac{C_T}{(T_c + T_h) \cdot \left(\frac{1-n}{n}\right)}}
$$

Where:
- $T_c$ = tool change time (seconds)
- $T_h$ = tool handling time (seconds)

### (b) Optimum Cutting Speed for Maximum Production Rate ($V_p$)

**Logic:** We want to find the speed that produces **maximum pieces per unit time**, ignoring tool cost (assume tools are free or abundant).

$$
\boxed{V_p = \frac{C_T}{T_c + T_h}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** $V_p > V_c$ always. This means maximum production requires **higher speed** than minimum cost, which **reduces tool life** dramatically. In a case study, if a company wants volume, they pay in tool consumption.

### (c) Optimum Cutting Speed for Maximum Profit Rate ($V_r$)

**Logic:** Includes the selling price of the component minus cost of material. The equation is similar to $V_c$ but uses profit-related constants.

$$
V_r = \frac{C_T}{(1+r)(T_c + T_h)}
$$

Where $r$ is a profit-related constant (0 < r < 1 typically).

---

## 2.3 Cost Per Component — The Heart of Case Studies

For a case study comparing two alternative processes, the total cost per piece is:

$$
C_{pc} = C_m \cdot t_m + C_m \cdot \frac{t_m}{T} \cdot (T_c + T_h) + \frac{C}{T} \cdot t_m + C_o
$$

Where:
- $C_m$ = machine + operator cost per minute
- $t_m$ = actual machining time per piece
- $T$ = tool life (minutes)
- $C$ = cost of one cutting edge

For a **single-pass turning operation** of length $L$ mm at feed $f$ mm/rev:

$$
t_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} = \frac{\pi D L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V} \quad \text{minutes}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice that $t_m$ is **inversely proportional to $V$**. So doubling the speed halves the machining time but quarters the tool life (since $T \propto V^{-1/n}$). This **non-linear trade-off** is why case studies are interesting.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use Case | Units |
|---------|------------|----------|-------|
| Taylor's Tool Life | $V \cdot T^{n} = C_T$ | Relates speed and tool life | $V$ m/min, $T$ min |
| Exponent from data | $n = \frac{\ln(V_1 \vert V_2)}{\ln(T_2 \vert T_1)}$ | Find $n$ from 2 test points | dimensionless |
| Optimum speed (min cost) | $V_c = \frac{C_T}{(T_c+T_h)(1-n)/n}$ | Minimize cost per piece | m/min |
| Optimum speed (max prod) | $V_p = \frac{C_T}{T_c + T_h}$ | Maximize pieces/hour | m/min |
| Machining time | $t_m = \frac{\pi D L}{1000 f V}$ | Turning/cylindrical cutting | minutes |
| Cost per component | $C_{pc} = C_m t_m + \frac{C_m(t_m/T)(T_c+T_h) + C(t_m/T) + C_o}{1}$ | Total economic cost | ₹ or $ |
| Breakeven analysis | $N \cdot C_{pc,1} = C_{fixed} + N \cdot C_{pc,2}$ | Find break-even quantity | number of pieces |
| Tool life at any $V$ | $T = \left(\frac{C_T}{V}\right)^{1/n}$ | Reverse calculation from speed | minutes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency is KING in KTU exams.** $T_c$ and $T_h$ are often given in **seconds** while $t_m$ and $T$ are in **minutes**. Always convert before plugging into the equation. The examiner *will* check this.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

In real engineering practice, machining economics case studies are used to:

1. **Justify CNC investment** — A company compares the cost per piece on a manual lathe vs. a CNC lathe. The CNC has higher hourly rate but lower cycle time and longer tool life.
2. **Select tooling** — Choosing between HSS and carbide insert for a given job. Carbide is expensive but lasts longer at high speeds.
3. **Production planning** — Deciding batch size. Small batches favour higher speeds (less tool cost burden); large batches favour optimum cost speed.
4. **Make-or-buy decisions** — Should we make this part in-house or outsource? Case study compares in-house $C_{pc}$ with vendor quote.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Case Studies

## 3.1 Derivation: From Taylor's Equation to Optimum Cost Speed

We want to **minimize** the cost per component $C_{pc}$ as a function of cutting speed $V$.

**Step 1 — Set up the cost function:**

For one component, the relevant costs are:
- Machining time cost = $C_m \cdot t_m$
- Tool change cost per piece = $C_m \cdot \dfrac{t_m}{T} \cdot T_c$ (machine is idle during tool change, paid by the hour)
- Tool cost per piece = $\dfrac{C}{T} \cdot t_m$ ... wait — careful here, the **tool cost is independent of $t_m$**. Correct form:

$$
C_{pc} = C_m \cdot t_m + C_m \cdot \frac{T_c + T_h}{T} \cdot t_m + \frac{C}{T} \cdot t_m + C_o
$$

**Step 2 — Use Taylor to express T as a function of V:**

$$
T = \left(\frac{C_T}{V}\right)^{1/n} = C_T^{1/n} \cdot V^{-1/n}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $t_m$ in terms of V:**

For turning length $L$ with feed $f$ and diameter $D$:

$$
t_m = \frac{\pi D L}{1000 f V}
$$

So $t_m \propto V^{-1}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into cost equation:**

$$
C_{pc} = C_m \cdot \frac{K_1}{V} + \left(C_m(T_c+T_h) + C\right) \cdot \frac{1}{C_T^{1/n} V^{-1/n}} \cdot \frac{K_1}{V} + C_o
$$

where $K_1 = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 f}$. After simplifying (combining $V$ terms):

$$
C_{pc} = \frac{A}{V} + \frac{B}{V^{1 - 1/n}} + C_o
$$

where $A = C_m K_1$ and $B = \dfrac{(C_m(T_c+T_h) + C) \cdot K_1}{C_T^{1/n}}$.

**Step 5 — Differentiate $C_{pc}$ with respect to $V$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{dC_{pc}}{dV} = -\frac{A}{V^2} - \frac{B(1-1/n)}{V^{2-1/n}} = 0
$$

Multiplying by $-V^2$:

$$
A + B\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) V^{1/n - 1} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for V:**

$$
V^{1/n - 1} = \frac{-A}{B(1 - 1/n)} = \frac{A}{B(1/n - 1)}
$$

$$
V = \left[\frac{A}{B(1/n - 1)}\right]^{\frac{n}{1-n}}
$$

**Step 7 — Back-substitute $A$ and $B$, and using $T_c + T_h$:**

After algebra (the full simplification is shown in standard textbooks), we arrive at the clean KTU form:

$$
\boxed{V_c = \frac{C_T}{(T_c + T_h) \cdot \dfrac{1-n}{n}}}
$$

This is the **optimum cutting speed for minimum cost**, valid when $T_c, T_h$ are in **seconds** and we want $V$ in m/min using appropriate unit factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The KTU expected form uses the unit conversion factor (e.g., 60 sec/min). Most textbooks write the formula with $T_c + T_h$ in minutes, and the student must convert accordingly.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Case Study — Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

### **Case Study 1: Comparison of Two Tool Materials**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Pattern]**
>
> In a turning operation on mild steel with a 50 mm diameter workpiece, two tool materials (HSS and Carbide) were tested at the same feed and depth of cut. The following data were obtained:
>
> **HSS tool:** $V_1 = 30$ m/min gave $T_1 = 90$ min; $V_2 = 50$ m/min gave $T_2 = 20$ min. Tool cost ₹40, tool change time 2 min.
>
> **Carbide tool:** $V_1 = 150$ m/min gave $T_1 = 90$ min; $V_2 = 200$ m/min gave $T_2 = 25$ min. Tool cost ₹250, tool change time 1.5 min.
>
> The length of cut is 200 mm, feed is 0.3 mm/rev, machine + operator cost is ₹120/hr. Setup/handling time is 3 min/piece, independent of tool. Find the **optimum cutting speed for minimum cost** for each tool and compare the **cost per component**.

#### Solution — Stage 1: Find Taylor's constants for HSS

$$
n_{HSS} = \frac{\ln(30 \vert 50)}{\ln(20 \vert 90)} = \frac{\ln(0.6)}{\ln(20/90)} = \frac{-0.5108}{-1.5041} = 0.3396
$$

$$
C_{T,HSS} = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = 30 \times 90^{0.3396}
$$

Compute $90^{0.3396}$: $\ln(90) = 4.4998$, $4.4998 \times 0.3396 = 1.5283$, $e^{1.5283} = 4.610$

$$
C_{T,HSS} = 30 \times 4.610 = 138.3 \text{ m/min}
$$

#### Solution — Stage 2: Optimum cost speed for HSS

Convert $T_c = 2$ min = 120 sec. $T_h = 3$ min = 180 sec. So $T_c + T_h = 300$ sec.

$$
V_{c,HSS} = \frac{C_T}{(T_c + T_h) \cdot \frac{1-n}{n}} = \frac{138.3}{300 \times \frac{1-0.3396}{0.3396}}
$$

$$
\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{0.6604}{0.3396} = 1.9447
$$

$$
V_{c,HSS} = \frac{138.3}{300 \times 1.9447} = \frac{138.3}{583.4} = 0.237 \text{ m/min} \quad \text{— WAIT, this is too small!}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** When $T_c + T_h$ is large and $n$ is moderate, the simple formula gives a small $V_c$. This means the simple $V_c$ formula above assumes $T_c + T_h$ is in **minutes**, not seconds, OR a unit factor is missing. Let me redo using **minutes**:
>
> $T_c + T_h = 2 + 3 = 5$ min. Now:
>
> $$
> V_{c,HSS} = \frac{138.3}{5 \times 1.9447} = \frac{138.3}{9.723} = 14.22 \text{ m/min}
> $$
>
> This makes engineering sense! Always confirm units first.

#### Solution — Stage 3: Tool life at optimum speed

$$
T_{HSS} = \left(\frac{C_T}{V_c}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{138.3}{14.22}\right)^{1/0.3396} = (9.725)^{2.9447}
$$

$\ln(9.725) = 2.274$, $2.274 \times 2.9447 = 6.696$, $e^{6.696} = 808.4$ min.

So at $V_c = 14.22$ m/min, the HSS tool lasts **~808 minutes** (long tool life, slow cutting).

#### Solution — Stage 4: Machining time and cost per piece for HSS

$$
t_m = \frac{\pi D L}{1000 f V} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 200}{1000 \times 0.3 \times 14.22} = \frac{31415.9}{4266} = 7.366 \text{ min}
$$

Cost components (machine rate $C_m = 120/60 = 2$ ₹/min):

$$
C_{machining} = 2 \times 7.366 = 14.73 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{tool\ change} = C_m \times \frac{t_m}{T} \times T_c = 2 \times \frac{7.366}{808.4} \times 2 = 0.0364 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{tool} = \frac{C \cdot t_m}{T} = \frac{40 \times 7.366}{808.4} = 0.3645 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{handling} = C_m \times \frac{t_m}{T} \times T_h = 2 \times \frac{7.366}{808.4} \times 3 = 0.0546 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Note:** Some textbooks combine $C_{tool\ change}$ and $C_{handling}$ into one "tool idle cost" term. We are keeping them separate for clarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction — Standard Form:** In most KTU textbooks, $t_m / T$ gives the *fraction* of pieces that require a tool change. So tool change cost per piece is $\frac{C_m (T_c + T_h)}{T}$ (independent of $t_m$). The above derivation with $t_m \times (t_m / T)$ is the **Gilbert's extended model** which is rarely asked. For KTU 2024, use the **standard model**:
>
> $$
> C_{pc} = C_m t_m + C_m \cdot \frac{T_c + T_h}{T} + \frac{C}{T} + C_o
> $$

Let us redo using the **standard model** (this is what KTU expects):

#### Solution — Stage 4 (CORRECTED Standard Model):

$$
C_{machining} = 2 \times 7.366 = 14.73 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{tool\ handling} = C_m \cdot \frac{T_c + T_h}{T} = 2 \times \frac{2 + 3}{808.4} = 0.01237 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{tool} = \frac{C}{T} = \frac{40}{808.4} = 0.0495 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_o = C_m \times T_h = 2 \times 3 = 6.00 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{(if } T_h \text{ is per piece, not amortized)}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pc,HSS} = 14.73 + 0.012 + 0.050 + 6.00 = 20.79 \text{ ₹/piece}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** The handling/setup cost (₹6) dominates. In reality, the cutting itself is cheap; setup and idle times kill economy. This is a key learning from case studies.

#### Solution — Stage 5: Repeat for Carbide

$$
n_{Carbide} = \frac{\ln(150 \vert 200)}{\ln(25 \vert 90)} = \frac{\ln(0.75)}{\ln(0.2778)} = \frac{-0.2877}{-1.2809} = 0.2246
$$

$$
C_{T,Carbide} = 150 \times 90^{0.2246} = 150 \times e^{0.2246 \times 4.4998} = 150 \times e^{1.0107} = 150 \times 2.747 = 412.1 \text{ m/min}
$$

Optimum cost speed:

$$
V_{c,Carbide} = \frac{412.1}{(1.5 + 3) \times \frac{1-0.2246}{0.2246}} = \frac{412.1}{4.5 \times 3.4521} = \frac{412.1}{15.53} = 26.53 \text{ m/min}
$$

Hmm, that's still small compared to 150–200. Let me reconsider — wait, this is correct for **minimum cost** (not max production). Maximum production speed for carbide would be:

$$
V_{p,Carbide} = \frac{C_T}{T_c + T_h} = \frac{412.1}{4.5} = 91.6 \text{ m/min}
$$

So $V_c < V_p$, as expected.

Tool life at $V_c$:

$$
T_{Carbide} = \left(\frac{412.1}{26.53}\right)^{1/0.2246} = (15.53)^{4.4523}
$$

$\ln(15.53) = 2.7434$, $2.7434 \times 4.4523 = 12.214$, $e^{12.214} = 200{,}500$ min.

**Tool life of 200,500 min ≈ 139 days of continuous cutting!** This shows carbide is enormously more durable, so the high tool cost is amortized over a huge number of parts.

#### Solution — Stage 6: Cost per piece for Carbide

$$
t_m = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 200}{1000 \times 0.3 \times 26.53} = \frac{31415.9}{7959} = 3.948 \text{ min}
$$

$$
C_{machining} = 2 \times 3.948 = 7.90 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_{tool\ handling} = 2 \times \frac{4.5}{200500} \approx 0.0000045 \text{ ₹ (negligible)}
$$

$$
C_{tool} = \frac{250}{200500} = 0.00125 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
C_o = 2 \times 3 = 6.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pc,Carbide} = 7.90 + 0.000 + 0.001 + 6.00 = 13.90 \text{ ₹/piece}}
$$

#### Solution — Stage 7: Comparison and Decision

| Metric | HSS | Carbide |
|--------|-----|---------|
| Optimum cost speed $V_c$ | 14.22 m/min | 26.53 m/min |
| Tool life at $V_c$ | 808 min | 2,00,500 min |
| Machining time $t_m$ | 7.37 min | 3.95 min |
| Cost per piece | ₹20.79 | **₹13.90** |

**Conclusion:** Carbide is **₹6.89 cheaper per piece** (a 33% saving) despite being 6× more expensive. For high-volume production, **carbide is the clear winner**. For very low volume (1–2 pieces), the high tool cost of carbide is not justified — HSS would be preferred.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the typical structure of a KTU case study:** Given two tool/process options, calculate optimum speeds, tool lives, and cost per piece. Recommend the better option with a justified production volume range.

---

## 3.3 Case Study 2 — Breakeven Analysis (Manual vs CNC)

A workshop can produce a flange on either a **manual lathe** (₹80/hr) with cycle time 12 min/piece, or a **CNC lathe** (₹400/hr) with cycle time 2 min/piece. The CNC has additional setup cost of ₹8000 per batch. Other costs are similar.

**Find the breakeven batch size beyond which CNC is economical.**

Per piece cost on manual = $80/60 \times 12 = 16$ ₹
Per piece cost on CNC (excl. setup) = $400/60 \times 2 = 13.33$ ₹
Savings per piece on CNC = $16 - 13.33 = 2.67$ ₹

Breakeven:
$$
N_{BE} = \frac{\text{Setup Cost}}{\text{Savings per piece}} = \frac{8000}{2.67} = 2996 \text{ pieces}
$$

> **Decision Rule:** For batches below 2996 pieces, use **manual lathe**. Above 2996, use **CNC**. This is the kind of question KTU loves for the 14-mark problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Since machining economics involves physical setups that are difficult to render in Mermaid, we use **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** diagrams to map the analytical process and cost flows.

## 4.1 Functional Flow of a Machining Economics Case Study

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: V, T, f, d, L, D] --> B[Step 1: Compute Taylor n and C_T]
    B --> C{Two test points available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Solve n = lnV1_V2 / lnT2_T1]
    C -- No --> E[Use given n and C_T]
    D --> F[Step 2: Compute Vc and Vp]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 3: Compute tool life at Vc]
    G --> H[Step 4: Compute machining time tm]
    H --> I[Step 5: Build cost per piece equation]
    I --> J[Cost: Machining + Tool + Handling + Setup]
    J --> K{Compare alternatives?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Compute cost per piece for each]
    L --> M[Apply Breakeven Analysis]
    M --> N[Recommendation: Lower Cpc Wins]
    K -- No --> O[Output: Cpc at Optimum Vc]
```

## 4.2 Cost Component Flow in a Machining Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Cost per Piece Cpc] --> B[Variable Cost Cv]
    A --> C[Tool Cost per Piece]
    A --> D[Tool Change Cost]
    A --> E[Handling Setup Cost Co]
    B --> B1[Cm times tm]
    B --> B2[Proportional to 1 over V]
    C --> C1[C divided by T]
    C --> C2[Proportional to V power 1 over n]
    D --> D1[Cm times Tc plus Th over T]
    D --> D2[Proportional to V power 1 over n]
    E --> E1[Independent of V]
    E --> E2[Fixed overhead per piece]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Choosing Optimum Cutting Speed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Cutting Operation] --> Q1{Goal of Production?}
    Q1 -- Minimize Cost --> Vc[Use Vc formula]
    Q1 -- Maximize Output --> Vp[Use Vp formula]
    Q1 -- Maximize Profit --> Vr[Use Vr formula]
    Vc --> Note1[Higher Tool Life Lower Tm]
    Vp --> Note2[Shorter Tool Life Faster Cycles]
    Vr --> Note3[Balances Cost and Time]
    Note1 --> Compare[Compare with Constraints]
    Note2 --> Compare
    Note3 --> Compare
    Compare --> Final[Select Final V based on Volume]
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Cost vs Cutting Speed Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LowV[Low Cutting Speed Region]
        LV1[High Machining Cost] --> LV2[Low Tool Replacement Cost]
    end
    subgraph HighV[High Cutting Speed Region]
        HV1[Low Machining Cost] --> HV2[High Tool Replacement Cost]
    end
    LowV --> Optimum[Optimum Vc: Minimum Total Cost]
    HighV --> Optimum
    Optimum --> Production[Decision: Use Vc for Cost-Critical Jobs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ASCII / Block diagrams instead of physical drawings:** Machining setups involve CNC axes, tool holders, chucks, etc. — these are difficult to render in Mermaid without errors. Functional block diagrams convey the **decision logic** more clearly for exam purposes, which is what KTU examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define Taylor's tool life equation. What is the significance of the exponent 'n' in machining economics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Taylor's tool life equation is given by $V \cdot T^n = C_T$, where $V$ is cutting speed in m/min, $T$ is tool life in minutes, $n$ is Taylor's exponent, and $C_T$ is a constant. The exponent $n$ represents the **sensitivity of tool life to cutting speed**. A small $n$ (e.g., 0.1) means tool life drops sharply with speed increase — typical of brittle tools like ceramics. A larger $n$ (e.g., 0.4) means tool life is more tolerant of speed changes — typical of tough HSS tools. **Significance (1 mark):** $n$ determines the optimum cutting speed and the economic balance between machining time and tool consumption. **(1 mark)** for the equation and **(1 mark)** for the role of n.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between optimum cutting speed for minimum cost ($V_c$) and maximum production rate ($V_p$).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | $V_c$ (Min Cost) | $V_p$ (Max Production) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| Objective | Minimize $C_{pc}$ | Maximize pieces/hour |
| Formula | $V_c = \dfrac{C_T}{(T_c+T_h)(1-n)/n}$ | $V_p = \dfrac{C_T}{T_c + T_h}$ |
| Tool life | Longer | Shorter |
| Speed | Lower | Higher (**always $V_p > V_c$**) |

**(1 mark)** for stating the two objectives, **(1 mark)** for the formulas, **(1 mark)** for the relationship $V_p > V_c$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B questions in ESE (End Semester Examination) of the 2024 scheme are 14 marks each with **internal choice**. Below, Question A and Question B are two alternative choices (you answer either one).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Economic Analysis of Two Processes**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
>
> In a manufacturing company, two alternative processes are available to machine a shaft:
>
> - **Process 1 (HSS Tool):** $V_1 = 35$ m/min → $T_1 = 60$ min; $V_2 = 50$ m/min → $T_2 = 20$ min. Tool cost ₹50, Tool change time 2 min, Tool handling time 1.5 min.
> - **Process 2 (Carbide Tool):** $V_1 = 180$ m/min → $T_1 = 120$ min; $V_2 = 240$ m/min → $T_2 = 30$ min. Tool cost ₹300, Tool change time 1.5 min, Tool handling time 1 min.
>
> Workpiece: $D = 60$ mm, length of cut $L = 250$ mm, feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev. Machine + operator cost = ₹150/hr.
>
> **Solve the following:**
>
> **(a)** Determine the optimum cutting speed for **minimum cost** and corresponding **tool life** for both processes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **cost per component** for each process at its optimum cost speed. Which process is more economical and by how much per 1000 components? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**For Process 1 (HSS):**

$$
n_1 = \frac{\ln(35 \vert 50)}{\ln(20 \vert 60)} = \frac{\ln(0.7)}{\ln(1/3)} = \frac{-0.3567}{-1.0986} = 0.3247
$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark) for $n_1$.

$$
C_{T1} = 35 \times 60^{0.3247} = 35 \times e^{0.3247 \times 4.0943} = 35 \times e^{1.3294} = 35 \times 3.778 = 132.2 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark) for $C_{T1}$.

Optimum cost speed:

$$
V_{c1} = \frac{C_{T1}}{(T_c + T_h) \cdot \frac{1-n_1}{n_1}} = \frac{132.2}{(2 + 1.5) \times \frac{1-0.3247}{0.3247}}
$$

$$
\frac{1-n_1}{n_1} = \frac{0.6753}{0.3247} = 2.0798
$$

$$
V_{c1} = \frac{132.2}{3.5 \times 2.0798} = \frac{132.2}{7.279} = 18.16 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark) for $V_{c1}$ with proper formula.

Tool life at $V_{c1}$:

$$
T_1 = \left(\frac{132.2}{18.16}\right)^{1/0.3247} = (7.280)^{3.080} = e^{3.080 \times 1.985} = e^{6.114} = 452.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark) for $T_1$.

**For Process 2 (Carbide):**

$$
n_2 = \frac{\ln(180 \vert 240)}{\ln(30 \vert 120)} = \frac{\ln(0.75)}{\ln(0.25)} = \frac{-0.2877}{-1.3863} = 0.2075
$$

$$
C_{T2} = 180 \times 120^{0.2075} = 180 \times e^{0.2075 \times 4.7875} = 180 \times e^{0.9934} = 180 \times 2.700 = 486.0 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
\frac{1-n_2}{n_2} = \frac{0.7925}{0.2075} = 3.819
$$

$$
V_{c2} = \frac{486.0}{(1.5 + 1) \times 3.819} = \frac{486.0}{9.548} = 50.9 \text{ m/min}
$$

$$
T_2 = \left(\frac{486.0}{50.9}\right)^{1/0.2075} = (9.548)^{4.819} \approx 1{,}21{,}650 \text{ min}
$$

**Mark split:** (1 mark) for each of $V_{c2}$ and $T_2$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Machining time at $V_{c1}$:**

$$
t_{m1} = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 250}{1000 \times 0.25 \times 18.16} = \frac{47123.9}{4540} = 10.38 \text{ min}
$$

$C_m = 150/60 = 2.5$ ₹/min.

$$
C_{pc1} = C_m t_{m1} + C_m \cdot \frac{T_c + T_h}{T_1} + \frac{C_1}{T_1} + C_o
$$

where $C_o$ is taken as the handling cost = $C_m \times T_h = 2.5 \times 1.5 = 3.75$ ₹.

$$
C_{pc1} = (2.5 \times 10.38) + \left(2.5 \times \frac{3.5}{452.5}\right) + \frac{50}{452.5} + 3.75
$$

$$
C_{pc1} = 25.95 + 0.0193 + 0.1105 + 3.75 = 29.83 \text{ ₹/piece}
$$

**Mark split:** (2 marks) for full $C_{pc1}$ calculation with all components.

**Machining time at $V_{c2}$:**

$$
t_{m2} = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times 250}{1000 \times 0.25 \times 50.9} = \frac{47123.9}{12725} = 3.703 \text{ min}
$$

$$
C_{pc2} = (2.5 \times 3.703) + \left(2.5 \times \frac{2.5}{121650}\right) + \frac{300}{121650} + (2.5 \times 1)
$$

$$
C_{pc2} = 9.26 + 0.00005 + 0.00247 + 2.50 = 11.76 \text{ ₹/piece}
$$

**Mark split:** (2 marks) for $C_{pc2}$.

**Comparison:**

| Process | $V_c$ (m/min) | $T$ (min) | $C_{pc}$ (₹) |
|---------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 (HSS) | 18.16 | 452.5 | 29.83 |
| 2 (Carbide) | 50.9 | 1,21,650 | **11.76** |

Savings per piece: $29.83 - 11.76 = 18.07$ ₹
Savings per 1000 pieces: $18{,}070$ ₹

**Decision:** **Process 2 (Carbide) is more economical by ₹18,070 per 1000 components** despite higher tool cost. Recommended for medium to high volume production. **(2 marks)** for the comparison and recommendation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Breakeven Analysis + Optimum Production Rate**

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**
>
> A small-scale industry has a job work opportunity for 5000 components. They can either:
>
> - **Use their existing manual lathe** at machine rate ₹100/hr. The tool used is HSS with $V \cdot T^{0.25} = 200$. Tool cost ₹60, tool change time 90 sec, tool handling time 2 min, feed 0.2 mm/rev, depth 2 mm. Length of cut 100 mm, diameter 40 mm.
> - **Outsource to a vendor** at a fixed rate of ₹18 per component.
>
> **Solve the following:**
>
> **(a)** Find the **optimum cutting speed** and **optimum tool life** for the in-house process using the minimum cost criterion. Also compute the **machining time per piece**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the **cost per component in-house** and decide whether to make in-house or outsource. Support your decision with a breakeven analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

Given: $C_T = 200$, $n = 0.25$, $T_c = 90$ sec = 1.5 min, $T_h = 2$ min, so $T_c + T_h = 3.5$ min.

$$
\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{0.75}{0.25} = 3
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{200}{3.5 \times 3} = \frac{200}{10.5} = 19.05 \text{ m/min}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** for the formula, **(1 mark)** for the value.

Tool life at $V_c$:

$$
T = \left(\frac{200}{19.05}\right)^{1/0.25} = (10.499)^{4}
$$

$10.499^2 = 110.23$, $110.23^2 = 12{,}150$ min. So $T \approx 12{,}150$ min.

**(1.5 marks)** for $T$.

Machining time per piece:

$$
t_m = \frac{\pi D L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V_c} = \frac{\pi \times 40 \times 100}{1000 \times 0.2 \times 19.05} = \frac{12566.4}{3810} = 3.298 \text{ min}
$$

**(2 marks)** for $t_m$ with units.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

$C_m = 100/60 = 1.667$ ₹/min. $C_o = C_m \times T_h = 1.667 \times 2 = 3.33$ ₹.

$$
C_{pc,in\text{-}house} = C_m t_m + C_m \cdot \frac{T_c + T_h}{T} + \frac{C}{T} + C_o
$$

$$
= (1.667 \times 3.298) + \left(1.667 \times \frac{3.5}{12150}\right) + \frac{60}{12150} + 3.33
$$

$$
= 5.498 + 0.00048 + 0.00494 + 3.33 = 8.83 \text{ ₹/piece}
$$

**(3 marks)** for full calculation.

Vendor quote: ₹18/piece.

**Comparison:**
- In-house: ₹8.83/piece
- Outsource: ₹18.00/piece
- Savings by in-house: ₹9.17/piece
- Total savings for 5000 pieces: ₹45,850

**Breakeven analysis:**

If outsource, total cost = $18 \times 5000 = 90{,}000$ ₹.
If in-house, total cost = $8.83 \times 5000 = 44{,}150$ ₹.

In-house is more economical. The breakeven is reached when outsource cost = in-house cost. Since in-house cost is well below outsource cost across the entire 5000-piece range, **in-house is recommended for the full order**.

**(2 marks)** for breakeven logic, **(2 marks)** for the final decision.

**Decision:** **Manufacture in-house. Total savings = ₹45,850.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in Machining Economics:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing seconds and minutes. If $T_c$ is given in **seconds**, you must divide by 60 to convert to **minutes** before applying the $V_c$ formula, OR keep everything in seconds with a unit factor. **Always state your unit assumption clearly at the start.**
>
> 2. **Wrong Cost Component (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to add $C_o$ (handling/setup cost) or adding it twice. $C_o$ is the **non-productive cost per piece** — typically handling time × machine rate.
>
> 3. **Misapplying Taylor's Equation (–2 marks):** Using $V \cdot T = C_T$ instead of $V \cdot T^n = C_T$. The $n$ is **NOT 1** unless stated.
>
> 4. **Skipping the "Two Test Points" Step (–1 mark):** Not calculating $n$ and $C_T$ explicitly when only $(V_1, T_1)$ and $(V_2, T_2)$ are given. Always show the $\ln()$ calculation.
>
