> 5. **Not converting $T$ to the right form (–1 mark):** Forgetting that $C/T$ gives tool cost per piece assuming one tool is used for $T$ minutes of total machining. If $T < t_m$, the tool cannot even finish one piece — economically infeasible.
>
> 6. **Comparing with wrong baseline (–2 marks):** Comparing $C_{pc}$ at $V_c$ vs. at $V_p$ without stating the goal. **State the goal first, then compute the corresponding speed.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Machining Economics Case Studies**

### **Core Equations to Memorize**

- ✅ **Taylor's Tool Life:** $V \cdot T^n = C_T$
- ✅ **Solve for n:** $n = \dfrac{\ln(V_1 \vert V_2)}{\ln(T_2 \vert T_1)}$
- ✅ **Solve for T at any V:** $T = \left(\dfrac{C_T}{V}\right)^{1/n}$
- ✅ **Optimum cost speed:** $V_c = \dfrac{C_T}{(T_c + T_h)(1-n)/n}$
- ✅ **Optimum production speed:** $V_p = \dfrac{C_T}{T_c + T_h}$
- ✅ **Machining time:** $t_m = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V}$
- ✅ **Cost per piece:** $C_{pc} = C_m t_m + C_m \cdot \dfrac{T_c + T_h}{T} + \dfrac{C}{T} + C_o$
- ✅ **Breakeven batch:** $N_{BE} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost Difference}}{\text{Variable Cost Difference per piece}}$

### **Key Conceptual Points**

- ✅ $V_p > V_c > V_r$ typically (production rate speed is highest, cost speed is moderate, profit rate speed depends on margin).
- ✅ Tool life **decreases** as cutting speed **increases** (inverse power relationship).
- ✅ $n$ is small (≈0.1) for brittle tools (ceramics), moderate (≈0.25) for carbides, larger (≈0.3–0.4) for HSS.
- ✅ HSS is cheaper per tool but wears faster. Carbide is expensive per tool but lasts much longer at high speeds.
- ✅ Total cost per piece has four components: **machining + tool change + tool cost + setup/handling**.
- ✅ For **breakeven** between two processes, the fixed cost difference (e.g., CNC setup) is offset by per-piece savings.
- ✅ Always confirm **units**: $V$ in m/min, $T$ in min, $T_c$ and $T_h$ in the same unit as $T$.
- ✅ Setup/handling time is a **major** cost component in case studies — often dominates the result.
- ✅ Economic batch size > Breakeven quantity → choose the **more automated** process.

### **Common Numerical Values (Memorize for KTU)**

| Tool Material | Typical $n$ | Typical $C_T$ range |
|---------------|-------------|---------------------|
| HSS | 0.3 – 0.4 | 100 – 200 m/min |
| Carbide | 0.2 – 0.3 | 300 – 600 m/min |
| Ceramic | 0.1 – 0.2 | 500 – 1000 m/min |

### **Decision-Making Heuristic (Use in Last-Minute Revision)**

> *"Fast tools save time but cost money. Slow tools waste time but save money. **Optimum cost** speed is the gold standard for high-volume production. **Maximum production** speed is for deadline-driven jobs. **Maximum profit** speed is when tooling is cheap and the product sells well."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lathes Machine - Types of Lathes and Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lathes Machine - Types of Lathes and Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lathe** is a fundamental machine tool designed primarily for **turning operations**, where the workpiece is rotated against a stationary cutting tool to generate cylindrical, conical, or contoured surfaces. It is classified as a **non-portable, power-driven machine tool** and is considered the "mother of all machine tools" because almost all other machine tools evolved from it.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Board Definition**
> A lathe is a machine tool in which the **workpiece is held and rotated about its own axis** while a single-point cutting tool is advanced into the workpiece, with the tool either remaining stationary or moving transversely, longitudinally, or along a profiled path, to remove material and produce an object of desired size and shape.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **potter's wheel (कुम्हार का चाक)**. The potter places a lump of clay on the spinning wheel, and uses his fingers (the "tool") to press, shape, and remove excess material as the wheel rotates. The rotation of the workpiece against a shaping agent is the essence of turning.

Now, replace:
- The potter's wheel with a **heavy, precision-engineered spindle** (running at controlled RPMs)
- The potter's fingers with a **carbide cutting tool** (rigidly held in a tool post)
- The clay with a **metal bar (workpiece)** (clamped in a chuck)

That is a **Lathe** — the industrial, numerically-controllable, and metallically precise cousin of the potter's wheel.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for Students**
> The lathe is the **only major machine tool where the workpiece rotates and the tool is stationary or moves linearly** (in basic turning). This is the **inverse** of drilling and milling, where the tool rotates and the workpiece is fixed.

### 1.3 Fundamental Operating Principle

The primary motion in a lathe is the **rotary motion of the workpiece**, which is called the **Primary (or Cutting) Motion**. The **Secondary (or Feed) Motion** is the linear movement of the tool against the workpiece. The resulting **relative velocity** between the tool edge and the workpiece surface produces the chip.

| Motion Type | Definition | KTU Term |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Motion | Rotation of workpiece at cutting speed | **Cutting Motion** |
| Secondary Motion | Linear feed of the tool | **Feed Motion** |
| Tertiary Motion | Depth of cut applied radially | **Depth of Cut (doc)** |

### 1.4 Standard Size Metrics of a Lathe (Bold Highlights)

The **size** of a lathe is universally specified by three KTU-standard parameters:

- **Swing Diameter (D)** – The largest diameter of workpiece that can be rotated above the bed ways, measured in **millimeters (mm)** or **inches**.
- **Length of Bed (L)** – The maximum length of workpiece that can be mounted between chuck and tailstock center.
- **Height of Centers** – Distance from the bed way surface to the spindle axis, also governing the swing over the cross-slide.

> [!NOTE] **Industry Convention**
> When a workshop says *"We have a 400 × 1500 lathe"*, it means **Swing Diameter = 400 mm** and **Bed Length = 1500 mm**. This is the **KTU standard nomenclature** expected in viva and design problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Lathes (Structured Bulleted Logic)

Lathes are broadly classified by **level of automation**, **size**, and **intended application**. Below is the KTU 2024-aligned taxonomy:

#### A. Based on Design & Drive
- **Speed Lathe**
  - Very low power (~0.5 to 1.5 HP)
  - Used for **wood turning, polishing, spinning, light finishing**
  - No power feed — manually operated tool
- **Bench Lathe**
  - Small-sized lathe mounted on a **workbench**
  - Used for **instrument making, watch parts, small precision jobs**
- **Engine Lathe (The Workhorse)**
  - Power feed in both longitudinal and cross directions
  - Headstock powered by an **electric motor** (via belt or gear drive)
  - Most common type in KTU lab exams and industry
  - Sub-classified as:
    - **Belt-Drive Engine Lathe** (older, variable via stepped pulleys)
    - **Gear-Drive Engine Lathe** (modern, all-geared headstock)
- **Tool Room Lathe**
  - High precision version of engine lathe
  - Used for **die making, gauges, tool fabrication**
  - Tighter tolerances (within **0.005 mm**)
- **Production Lathe**
  - Heavier, more rigid, designed for **mass production**
  - Often equipped with **multi-tool posts** and power feed

#### B. Based on Tool Holding
- **Capstan Lathe** (Turret replaces tailstock)
  - **Capstan head** is a swiveling tool holder
  - Multiple tools pre-set; quick indexing for repeated operations
  - Best for **batch production** of identical parts
- **Turret Lathe** (Heavy-duty capstan)
  - **Ram-type turret** slides on the bed ways
  - Used for **larger, heavier workpieces**
  - More rigid than capstan; longer tool travel
- **Automatic Lathe**
  - All operations (tool indexing, feed, speed change) **automatic**
  - Operated by **cams, camshafts, or numerical control**
  - Sub-classified as:
    - **Screw-type Automatic**
    - **Chucker Automatic** (no bar stock; uses blanks)
    - **Swiss-type Automatic** (sliding headstock for small precision parts)
- **CNC Lathe** (Computer Numerically Controlled)
  - Tool movements governed by **G-codes and M-codes**
  - Highest precision, repeatability (±0.001 mm)
  - Foundation of **modern Industry 4.0 manufacturing**

#### C. Special Purpose Lathes
- **Tracer Lathe** — tool path duplicates a master template (2D profile)
- **Copying Lathe** — 3D reproduction using a stylus-followed model
- **Multi-tool Lathe** — two or more tools cut simultaneously (higher MRR)
- **Duplex (Two-Spindle) Lathe** — two workpieces machined in parallel
- **Vertical Turning Lathe (VTL)** — spindle axis vertical; used for large-diameter short-height components
- **Tire Lathe** — for machining railway wheel tires
- **Roll Lathe** — for grinding/machining rolling mill rolls

### 2.2 Why Each Type Exists — The "Why" Behind the Engineering

- **Speed Lathes** exist because **not all turning needs cutting** — sometimes you only need to spin and polish.
- **Engine Lathes** exist because **manual feed is exhausting and inconsistent** for industrial workloads.
- **Capstan/Turret Lathes** exist because **setup time dominates** in batch production — indexed tools eliminate re-setup.
- **Automatic Lathes** exist because **human attention is a costly, inconsistent input** — a cam can repeat ±0.02 mm forever.
- **CNC Lathes** exist because **cams are inflexible** — software can change geometry instantly.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Mnemonic for Types**
> **"SPEED BENCH ENGINE TOOL PROD CAP TURRET AUTO CNC SPECIAL"**
> (Speed, Bench, Engine, Tool-room, Production, Capstan, Turret, Automatic, CNC, Special-purpose)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes **all critical formulas** for lathe sizing, speed/feed calculations, and material removal rate. These are the **minimum** expected in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Swing Diameter over Bed | $D = 2 \times H$ | mm | $H$ = height of centers |
| 2 | Swing Diameter over Cross-slide | $D_c = D - 2W$ | mm | $W$ = width of cross-slide |
| 3 | Spindle Speed (RPM) | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/min | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 4 | Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in RPM |
| 5 | Linear Feed Rate | $f_L = N \times f$ | mm/min | $f$ in mm/rev |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Z = V_c \times f \times d$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut |
| 7 | MRR (Alternate form) | $Z = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2 \times f \times N}{4 \times 10^6}$ | cm³/min | For cylindrical turning |
| 8 | Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | min | $L$ = length of cut |
| 9 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{F_c \times V_c}{60{,}000}$ | kW | $F_c$ in N |
| 10 | Specific Cutting Force | $K_s = \dfrac{F_c}{f \times d}$ | N/mm² | Material-dependent |

> [!NOTE] **Critical Note on Table Syntax**
> In the table above, **vertical bars are rendered as LaTeX `\vert` equivalents only when needed for absolute values**. Since no formula here uses absolute values, the standard pipe separators are safe to use as column delimiters. **Students writing notes in markdown should mirror this convention.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lathe size and type selection directly impacts:
- **Aerospace** — turning of titanium compressor discs (VTL, heavy-duty)
- **Automotive** — crankshafts, camshafts (CNC turning centers)
- **Medical** — orthopedic implants, bone screws (Swiss-type automatic)
- **Defence** — gun barrels, missile casings (Tracer + CNC hybrid)
- **Watch Industry** — gears, pinions (Bench lathe, Tool-room lathe)

> [!TIP] **Examiner's Tip**
> Always quote **specific numerical examples** in KTU answers. E.g., *"A capstan lathe with 6-station turret can complete a stepped shaft in 45 seconds versus 6 minutes on an engine lathe."* This shows applied understanding and scores high.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed ($V_c$)

The **cutting speed** $V_c$ is defined as the **tangential velocity of the workpiece surface** relative to the tool edge. Since the workpiece rotates with angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s and has instantaneous diameter $D$:

$$
V_c = \omega \times r = \omega \times \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 1:** Express angular velocity in terms of spindle RPM $N$.

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\omega$ into the cutting speed equation.

$$
V_c = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \times \dfrac{D}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the constants.

$$
V_c = \dfrac{\pi N D}{60}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to solve for $N$ when $V_c$ is given (in m/min, $D$ in mm).

$$
N = \dfrac{60 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}
$$

> Since $V_c$ in m/min and $D$ in mm need unit harmonization, we substitute $V_c \text{(m/min)} = 1000 \times V_c \text{(m/s)}$, but a more common working form is:

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}
$$

> Here, $V_c$ is in **m/min** and $D$ is in **mm**. This is the **KTU board-preferred working formula**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Turning

Consider a cylindrical workpiece being turned on a lathe. In one revolution of the workpiece:

- The tool advances by **$f$ mm** (feed per revolution).
- The tool removes a chip of cross-section: **$f \times d$ mm²** where $d$ is the depth of cut.
- The **circumferential length** of the workpiece at diameter $D$ is **$\pi D$ mm**.

**Step 1:** Volume of metal removed in one revolution.

$$
V_{\text{one rev}} = f \times d \times \pi D
$$

**Step 2:** In one minute, the workpiece makes $N$ revolutions. So the total volume removed per minute is:

$$
Z = N \times f \times d \times \pi D
$$

**Step 3:** Express in convenient units (mm³/min when $D, f, d$ in mm and $N$ in rev/min).

$$
Z = \pi D N f d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}
$$

**Step 4:** Alternate form using $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min).

$$
Z = 1000 \times V_c \times f \times d \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}
$$

> This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE — students must memorize both forms.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A 250 mm diameter workpiece is turned on a lathe with cutting speed 30 m/min, feed 0.2 mm/rev, and depth of cut 2 mm. Calculate:
- (a) Spindle speed $N$
- (b) Material removal rate $Z$
- (c) Machining time for a 150 mm length cut

**Given:**
- $D = 250$ mm
- $V_c = 30$ m/min
- $f = 0.2$ mm/rev
- $d = 2$ mm
- $L = 150$ mm

**Step (a):** Calculate $N$.

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 250}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{30{,}000}{785.398} = 38.197 \text{ rev/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 38.2 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**Step (b):** Calculate MRR.

$$
Z = 1000 \times V_c \times f \times d
$$

$$
Z = 1000 \times 30 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} = 12 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}
$$

**Step (c):** Calculate machining time.

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N} = \dfrac{150}{0.2 \times 38.197}
$$

$$
T_m = \dfrac{150}{7.6394} = 19.63 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_m \approx 19.63 \text{ minutes}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Lathe

This matrix is **exam-ready** — it is exactly the type of comparison KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions.

| Parameter | Engine Lathe | Capstan/Turret Lathe | Automatic Lathe | CNC Lathe |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tool Changing | Manual | Quick Indexing (6-station) | Fully Automatic | Programmed |
| Skill Required | High (Operator) | Moderate | Low (Setter) | Low (Programmer) |
| Setup Time | High | Moderate | High | Low (after first setup) |
| Cycle Time | Long | Moderate | Short | Shortest |
| Batch Size | 1–50 | 50–1000 | 1000+ | Any |
| Tolerance | ±0.05 mm | ±0.025 mm | ±0.02 mm | ±0.005 mm |
| Flexibility | Very High | Moderate | Low | Extremely High |
| Capital Cost | Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Operator Cost | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Best Application | Job shop, repair | Batch production | Mass production | Precision + Flexibility |
| KTU 14-Mark Favoritism | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Lathes

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the KTU-mandated taxonomy of lathe types. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to avoid the Mermaid reserved-keyword issue.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[LATHE MACHINE] --> B0[BASED ON DRIVE]
    A0 --> C0[BASED ON TOOL HOLDING]
    A0 --> D0[BASED ON AUTOMATION]
    A0 --> E0[SPECIAL PURPOSE]

    B0 --> B1[Speed Lathe]
    B0 --> B2[Bench Lathe]
    B0 --> B3[Engine Lathe]
    B0 --> B4[Tool Room Lathe]
    B0 --> B5[Production Lathe]

    B3 --> B3a[Belt Drive]
    B3 --> B3b[Gear Drive All Geared]

    C0 --> C1[Capstan Lathe]
    C0 --> C2[Turret Lathe]
    C0 --> C3[Automatic Lathe]

    C3 --> C3a[Screw Type]
    C3 --> C3b[Chucker Type]
    C3 --> C3c[Swiss Type]

    D0 --> D1[CNC Lathe]
    D0 --> D2[DNC Lathe]
    D0 --> D3[Robotic Lathe Cell]

    E0 --> E1[Tracer Lathe]
    E0 --> E2[Copying Lathe]
    E0 --> E3[Multi Tool Lathe]
    E0 --> E4[Duplex Lathe]
    E0 --> E5[Vertical Turning Lathe VTL]
    E0 --> E6[Tire and Roll Lathe]
```

### 4.2 Engine Lathe — Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

The engine lathe is composed of **seven primary subsystems**. Each subsystem has a specific role; together they form a complete kinematic chain from motor to chip.

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1[Electric Motor] --> P1[Belt or Gear Train]
    P1 --> H1[Headstock Spindle]
    H1 --> W1[Workpiece in Chuck]
    W1 --> C1[Cutting Tool on Tool Post]
    C1 --> CH1[Chip Formation Zone]
    H1 --> T1[Tailstock]
    T1 --> W1
    B1[Bed Ways] --> H1
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> T1
    CA1[Carriage Assembly] --> C1
    CA1 --> A1[Apron Feed Mechanism]
    A1 --> P1
    S1[Spindle Speed Selector] --> P1
    F1[Feed Selector Lever] --> A1
```

### 4.3 Capstan Lathe — Tool Indexing Schematic

This block diagram shows the **turret indexing sequence** in a capstan lathe — a recurring KTU viva question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    POS1[Position 1 Drill] --> IDX1[Index Turret 60 deg]
    IDX1 --> POS2[Position 2 Reamer]
    POS2 --> IDX2[Index Turret 60 deg]
    IDX2 --> POS3[Position 3 Boring Bar]
    POS3 --> IDX3[Index Turret 60 deg]
    IDX3 --> POS4[Position 4 Tap]
    POS4 --> IDX4[Index Turret 60 deg]
    IDX4 --> POS5[Position 5 Form Tool]
    POS5 --> IDX5[Index Turret 60 deg]
    IDX5 --> POS6[Position 6 Cutoff Tool]
    POS6 --> ENDX[Return to Position 1]
    ENDX --> POS1
```

### 4.4 Size Specification Diagram (Schematic, Text-Based)

Although a true engineering drawing would use **orthographic projection**, the Mermaid block below captures the **dimensional logic** of lathe sizing in a KTU-friendly format.

```mermaid
graph TB
    HEAD[Headstock] -->|Spindle Axis| SP[Spindle Center Line]
    TAIL[Tailstock] -->|Tailstock Center| SP
    BED[Bed Ways] -->|Width W| H
    SP -->|Distance H from Bed| H[Height of Centers]
    H -->|2H equals| SWING[Swing Diameter over Bed]
    CROSS[Cross Slide] -->|Width Wc| SP
    SP -->|Swing minus 2Wc| SWC[Swing over Cross Slide]
    CHUCK[Chuck Diameter] -->|Maximum less than| SWING
    WORK[Workpiece Maximum Length] -->|Lmax| BEDL[Bed Length]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> - **Swing Diameter = 2 × Height of Centers**
> - **Swing over Cross Slide = Swing Diameter − 2 × Cross-slide Width**
> - **Maximum Workpiece Length ≈ 0.75 × Bed Length** (allowance for chuck and tailstock)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Define a lathe. List any four types of lathes used in industry.**

**Model Answer (Expected Key Points):**

A lathe is a machine tool in which the **workpiece is rotated about its own axis** against a single-point cutting tool, which is fed against the rotating workpiece to produce cylindrical and other surfaces.

**Four types of lathes:**
1. **Engine Lathe** — most common; uses power feed.
2. **Capstan and Turret Lathe** — used for batch production with multiple tools.
3. **Automatic Lathe** — all operations controlled by cams or NC.
4. **CNC Lathe** — controlled by computer programs (G-codes).

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Mistake**
> Many students write *"Lathe is used to make holes."* This is **wrong** — that is a drilling machine. A lathe is for **turning external (and sometimes internal) cylindrical surfaces**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Predicted Pattern]**
**How is the size of a lathe specified? State the meaning of a "300 × 1200 lathe."**

**Model Answer:**

The size of a lathe is specified by **two primary dimensions**:
1. **Swing Diameter (D)** — The maximum diameter of the workpiece that can be rotated above the bed ways.
2. **Length of Bed (L)** — The maximum length of the workpiece that can be accommodated.

A **"300 × 1200 lathe"** means:
- **Swing Diameter = 300 mm** (so the maximum radius above bed = 150 mm)
- **Length of Bed = 1200 mm** (so max workpiece length ≈ 900 mm after chuck allowance)

> [!WARNING] **Pitfall**
> Students often confuse **swing over bed** with **swing over cross slide**. Remember: **Swing over cross slide < Swing over bed**, because the cross-slide consumes part of the swing envelope. Examiners may specifically test this in a 3-mark follow-up.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Predicted Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of an engine lathe**. List its **main parts** and state the function of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between an **engine lathe and a capstan lathe** in terms of construction, tool holding, application, and production rate.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### (a) Working Principle and Main Parts of an Engine Lathe

**Principle:** In an engine lathe, the **workpiece is held in a chuck mounted on the spindle** and rotated at a predetermined **cutting speed ($V_c$)**. A single-point cutting tool, held rigidly in the **tool post** mounted on the **carriage**, is fed against the rotating workpiece along linear paths (longitudinal, cross, or angular). The relative motion between the tool and the workpiece causes **material removal in the form of a continuous chip**, gradually reducing the workpiece diameter to the desired value.

**Main Parts of an Engine Lathe (with Functions):**

| S.No. | Part | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bed** | Rigid base with precision-ground ways; supports headstock, tailstock, and carriage. |
| 2 | **Headstock** | Houses the spindle, gear train, and speed-change levers. Drives the workpiece. |
| 3 | **Tailstock** | Supports the other end of long workpieces via a dead center. Can be slid along the bed. |
| 4 | **Carriage** | Moves the tool along the bed; comprises saddle, cross-slide, compound rest, and tool post. |
| 5 | **Apron** | Attached to carriage; contains feed mechanism, clutch, and half-nuts for lead screw engagement. |
| 6 | **Feed Rod / Lead Screw** | Transmits power from headstock to apron for automatic feed or thread cutting. |
| 7 | **Tool Post** | Holds the cutting tool rigidly in the correct orientation. |

> The sketch of an engine lathe (which students should redraw in the exam) shows these parts in their standard spatial arrangement: **Headstock (left) → Bed → Carriage (middle) → Tailstock (right)**.

#### (b) Engine Lathe vs Capstan Lathe

| Feature | Engine Lathe | Capstan Lathe |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool Holder** | Single tool post (one tool at a time) | **Capstan head** with 6 tool stations |
| **Tool Changing** | Manual; operator must loosen and reset | **Quick indexing** by rotating capstan |
| **Setup Time** | High (re-set for each tool) | **Low** (all tools pre-set) |
| **Production Rate** | Slow (suitable for 1–50 parts) | **Faster** (50–1000 parts) |
| **Workpiece** | Any length, varying diameters | **Short workpieces** (limited by turret stroke) |
| **Skill Required** | High — operator dependent | Moderate — setter pre-configures tools |
| **Repeatability** | Operator-dependent | **High** (tools fixed in turret) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (additional turret mechanism) |
| **Application** | Job shops, repair, prototype | **Batch production** of similar parts |

> **Conclusion:** Capstan lathe is **preferred for batch production** where the same sequence of operations is repeated many times. Engine lathe remains the **versatile workhorse** for varied low-volume work.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a sketch the **construction and working of a turret lathe**. State its advantages over an engine lathe.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mild steel workpiece of **initial diameter 80 mm** is turned on a lathe to a final diameter of **75 mm** in one pass. The cutting speed is **25 m/min**, feed is **0.3 mm/rev**, and the length of cut is **200 mm**. Calculate the **spindle speed, material removal rate, and machining time**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### (a) Construction and Working of a Turret Lathe

**Construction:** A turret lathe is essentially a **heavier, more rigid version of a capstan lathe**. Its main parts are:
- **Headstock** — similar to engine lathe but heavier; houses spindle and gear box.
- **Bed** — wide and long, with separate ways for the carriage and the turret slide.
- **Turret Slide (Ram Type)** — a **rectangular turret** carrying **6 tools** (in standard configuration) is mounted on a **ram** that slides longitudinally on the bed ways. This is the key difference from a capstan lathe (which uses a circular capstan head).
- **Cross Slide with Tool Post** — carries a second tool for simultaneous turning and forming operations.
- **Feed Mechanism** — power feed to both turret and cross slide.

**Working:** The turret is indexed to bring the **first tool** into position, advanced to perform a cut (e.g., facing), then retracted. The turret is **rotated** to the next position, the second tool is brought into play, and so on, until all six tools have completed their respective operations. This sequence is **repeated for every workpiece** in the batch.

**Advantages over Engine Lathe:**
1. **Higher production rate** — no time lost in tool changing.
2. **Less operator skill** required — tools are pre-set.
3. **Consistent quality** — geometry of each part is identical.
4. **Multiple operations in one setup** — drilling, reaming, boring, threading all possible.
5. **Reduced fatigue** for the operator.

#### (b) Numerical Problem Solution

**Given Data:**
- Initial diameter $D_1 = 80$ mm
- Final diameter $D_2 = 75$ mm
- Cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev
- Length of cut $L = 200$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate the mean diameter for spindle speed selection.**

$$
D_m = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \dfrac{80 + 75}{2} = 77.5 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Rationale:** Cutting speed is generally applied at the **average (mean) diameter** during a roughing pass, because the diameter reduces continuously.

**Step 2: Calculate the spindle speed.**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D_m} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 77.5}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{25{,}000}{243.473} = 102.69 \text{ rev/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 103 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the depth of cut.**

$$
d = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{2} = \dfrac{80 - 75}{2} = 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Material Removal Rate (MRR).**

$$
Z = 1000 \times V_c \times f \times d
$$

$$
Z = 1000 \times 25 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 = 18{,}750 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z = 18.75 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Machining Time.**

$$
T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N} = \dfrac{200}{0.3 \times 102.69}
$$

$$
T_m = \dfrac{200}{30.807} = 6.49 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_m \approx 6.49 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** If $V_c$ is given in m/min and $D$ in mm, students often forget the factor of 1000. The correct formula is $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi D}$, **NOT** $N = \dfrac{V_c}{\pi D}$.
> 2. **Mean vs initial diameter:** For multi-pass turning, always use the **current diameter**, not the original. For a single pass, using mean diameter is acceptable, but using $D_1$ throughout gives an error of ~3% — examiners may deduct 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting approach and overtravel:** The actual feed length is $L + L_a + L_o$ where $L_a$ = approach (~2-5 mm) and $L_o$ = overtravel (~2-3 mm). In KTU problems, if not specified, ignore these for simplicity, but **mention this assumption** to earn full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lathe: Types and Size**

- ✅ **Lathe Definition:** Workpiece rotates, tool is stationary or feeds linearly.
- ✅ **Size Specified by:** Swing Diameter × Length of Bed (e.g., "300 × 1200").
- ✅ **Swing Diameter = 2 × Height of Centers.**
- ✅ **Swing over Cross Slide = Swing Diameter − 2 × Cross-slide Width.**
- ✅ **11 Major Lathe Types:** Speed, Bench, Engine, Tool-room, Production, Capstan, Turret, Automatic (Screw/Chucker/Swiss), CNC, Tracer, Copying, Multi-tool, Duplex, VTL.
- ✅ **Engine Lathe = Mother of all machine tools;** the most versatile and the default reference in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Capstan vs Turret:** Capstan = circular capstan head, light work; Turret = ram-type rectangular turret, heavy work.
- ✅ **Automatic Lathe = Operator-less production** (cam or NC driven).
- ✅ **CNC Lathe = Highest flexibility and precision**; backbone of Industry 4.0.
- ✅ **Spindle Speed Formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi D}$
- ✅ **MRR Formula:** $Z = 1000 \times V_c \times f \times d$ (mm³/min)
- ✅ **Machining Time Formula:** $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$
- ✅ **Examiner's Pet Topics:** (1) Engine vs Capstan comparison, (2) CNC vs Conventional, (3) Numerical on MRR and Time.
- ✅ **Always quote units in final answer** (RPM, mm³/min, min) — marks are reserved for this.
- ✅ **Use mean diameter for single-pass turning; current diameter for multi-pass.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification and Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specification and Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Machine Tool Specifications

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **machine tool specification** is a systematically documented set of technical parameters, performance characteristics, geometric dimensions, and operational capabilities that quantitatively and qualitatively define the manufacturing envelope, accuracy, productivity, and functional limits of a machine tool. These specifications are the standardized "identity card" used by designers, manufacturers, and end-users to classify, compare, and select machine tools for specific manufacturing tasks.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), machine tool specifications are classified into two broad categories:

1. **General (Qualitative) Specifications** — describe the *type, construction, and structural features*.
2. **Specific (Quantitative) Specifications** — describe the *size, capacity, range, and accuracy* numerically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU Module 2, students must be able to identify, list, and explain the **main parts (components)** of a machine tool and correlate them with their **functional specifications** such as swing, length of bed, spindle bore, number of speeds/feeds, etc.

---

### 1.2 Definition of "Parts" of a Machine Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **parts (or components)** of a machine tool are the discrete structural, kinematic, and control elements that are assembled together to form the complete machine. Each part performs a specific function — such as supporting the workpiece, transmitting power, guiding motion, holding the tool, or providing rigidity — and together they enable the machine to perform the desired material-removal operation.

These parts are broadly grouped into:

| Functional Group | Role |
|---|---|
| **Structural Parts** | Bed, column, base, housing — provide rigidity, alignment, and vibration damping. |
| **Power Transmission Parts** | Motors, gears, pulleys, lead screws, feed rods — transmit motion and force. |
| **Work-Holding & Tool-Holding Parts** | Chuck, faceplate, tool post, arbor, collet — grip workpiece and tool. |
| **Guiding Parts** | Slides, ways, guideways — provide accurate linear/rotary motion. |
| **Control Parts** | Levers, handwheels, aprons, CNC controllers — govern speed, feed, and direction. |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — The "Car Passport":**
> Think of machine tool specifications exactly like a **car's official brochure**. Before buying a car, you read its *top speed, mileage, engine capacity, seating capacity, fuel type, dimensions, and safety rating*. Similarly, before selecting a machine tool for a workshop, an engineer reads its *swing diameter, length of bed, spindle speed range, feed range, motor power, accuracy class, and floor space*. Specifications = "Brochure of the Machine Tool."

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — The "Human Body":**
> A machine tool is like a **human body**:
> - **Bed/Base** = Skeleton (gives shape & support)
> - **Headstock** = Heart (drives blood/motion through the spindle)
> - **Spindle** = Artery (channel of motion)
> - **Tailstock** = Other hand (supports from opposite end)
> - **Carriage** = Arm (carries the tool)
> - **Tool Post** = Hand (holds the cutting tool)
> - **Apron & Feed Rod** = Nerves (transmit control signals/feed)
> - **Levers/Handwheels** = Brain commands (operator input)
> - **Ways/Guideways** = Joints (allow smooth relative motion)

---

### 1.4 Standard Bodies Governing Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Machine tool specifications are governed by **Indian Standards (IS)** issued by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** and harmonized with **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** norms. Some key standards include:
> - **IS 2063** — General specifications for machine tools.
> - **IS 13855** — Acceptance tests for machine tools (accuracy tests).
> - **IS 12955** — Test code for lathes.
> - **ISO 230** — Test code for machine tools (geometric accuracy).
> - **ISO 841** — Numerical control coordinate system and motion nomenclature.
> - **ISO 513** — Classification and application of hard cutting materials.

The **bold** term **"Geometric Accuracy Tests"** under ISO 230 is mandatory for every machine tool before despatch from the manufacturer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Machine Tool Specifications

Machine tool specifications, as taught in KTU Module 2, are organized into the following hierarchy:

#### A. **General Specifications (Qualitative)**
These describe the *identity and type* of the machine:
- Type of machine (lathe, milling, drilling, grinding, etc.)
- Drive type (belt, gear, hydraulic, CNC, servo)
- Automation level (manual, semi-automatic, automatic, CNC, DNC)
- Construction (column-and-knee, bed-type, planer-type, etc.)
- Number of spindles (single, multi-spindle)
- Coolant system availability
- Lubrication system type

#### B. **Specific Specifications (Quantitative)**
These are numerical and measurable:

| S.No. | Specification | Symbol/Unit | Example (Lathe) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Height of centres | mm | **200 mm** |
| 2 | Swing over bed | mm | **400 mm** |
| 3 | Swing over cross-slide | mm | **250 mm** |
| 4 | Length of bed | mm | **1000 mm** |
| 5 | Spindle bore | mm | **52 mm** |
| 6 | Spindle nose taper | Morse/Metric | **MT 5** |
| 7 | Number of spindle speeds | count | **8 or 16** |
| 8 | Range of spindle speeds | rpm | **30 – 1500 rpm** |
| 9 | Number of feeds | count | **32 or 64** |
| 10 | Range of feeds (longitudinal) | mm/rev | **0.05 – 1.0 mm/rev** |
| 11 | Range of feeds (cross) | mm/rev | **0.025 – 0.5 mm/rev** |
| 12 | Main motor power | kW | **5.5 kW** |
| 13 | Floor space (L × W) | m | **2.0 m × 1.0 m** |
| 14 | Net weight | kg | **1800 kg** |

#### C. **Accuracy Specifications**
- **Positioning Accuracy (Pa)** — maximum deviation between actual and commanded position.
- **Repeatability (R)** — ability to return to the same position.
- **Geometric Accuracy** — straightness, flatness, squareness, parallelism, runout.
- **Surface Finish (Ra)** — achievable in μm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Focus:** Students must remember that **"Swing over bed"** determines the *maximum diameter* of the workpiece that can be rotated, and **"Height of centres"** = **(Swing over bed) / 2**. Both are **bold** key metrics for any lathe problem.

---

### 2.2 Why Specifications Matter — The "Why & How" Logic

> [!TIP]
> **Why are specifications essential?**
> 1. **Machine Selection** — Match the machine to the workpiece size and operation.
> 2. **Workshop Planning** — Decide floor space, foundation, power supply, and material handling.
> 3. **Cost Estimation** — Larger capacity → higher cost, more power, more space.
> 4. **Quality Assurance** — Accuracy specs ensure product quality (tolerances, surface finish).
> 5. **Standardization** — Common IS/ISO specs enable global comparison and procurement.

> [!TIP]
> **How is the specification decided by the manufacturer?**
> - The **workpiece size** decides swing and bed length.
> - The **cutting operations** decide speed range and power.
> - The **tolerance required** decides accuracy class.
> - The **production volume** decides automation level.
> - The **material to be cut** decides rigidity and material of bed (e.g., cast iron, granite, polymer concrete).

---

### 2.3 Main Parts of a Centre Lathe (Detailed)

A **centre lathe** is the most fundamental machine tool and is the most extensively asked topic in KTU Module 2. Its main parts and functions are:

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Bed** | Base of the lathe. Made of **cast iron** (high vibration damping). Has precision **V-ways and flat ways** for sliding the carriage and tailstock. The bed is mounted on a **leg/pedestal** to bring it to operator's working height. |
| **2. Headstock** | Mounted on the left end of the bed. Houses the **main spindle, gear train, and speed-change levers**. Provides various spindle speeds. Also contains the **spindle nose** (for mounting chuck/faceplate) and the **feed gearbox**. |
| **3. Tailstock** | Mounted on the right end of the bed. Can slide along the bed ways. Holds the **dead centre (MT)** to support long workpieces. Has a **quill** that can be advanced/retracted by a handwheel. Used for **drilling, reaming, and tapping** light jobs. |
| **4. Carriage** | Slides on the bed ways. Carries the **cross-slide, compound slide, and tool post**. Provides longitudinal (Z-axis) movement via **saddle** and is driven by the **lead screw** (for threading) or **feed rod** (for general turning). |
| **5. Cross-slide** | Mounted on top of the saddle. Provides **cross (X-axis) movement** for facing and diameter control. Moved by a graduated handwheel. |
| **6. Compound Slide** | Mounted on cross-slide. Has a **swiveling base (graduated in degrees)** for turning tapers and short angles. |
| **7. Tool Post** | Mounted on compound slide. Holds the **cutting tool** (single-point HSS/carbide). Can be **4-way turret type** (manual) or **quick-change type**. |
| **8. Apron** | Attached to the front of the carriage. Houses **feed mechanism, half-nuts, clutches, and levers** for engaging longitudinal/cross feed and threading. |
| **9. Lead Screw** | Long precision screw running along the front of the bed. Engages with **half-nuts on apron** to produce accurate **thread cutting**. |
| **10. Feed Rod** | Runs parallel to lead screw. Provides **positive automatic feed** to the carriage during turning. Driven by the **feed gearbox** in the headstock. |
| **11. Spindle** | Hollow shaft inside headstock. Carries the workpiece (via chuck). Has **Morse taper** for inserting centers. Provides rotation. |
| **12. Chuck / Faceplate** | Work-holding devices mounted on spindle nose. **3-jaw universal (self-centering)**, **4-jaw independent**, or **magnetic/chuck with faceplate**. |
| **13. Legs / Cabinet** | Support the bed and house the **coolant pump, electrical panel, and chip tray**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The relationship **Swing over bed = 2 × Height of centres** is a frequently asked 3-mark question. The **lead screw is used ONLY for thread cutting**, while the **feed rod is used for general turning feeds** — confusing these is a common student error.

---

### 2.4 Main Parts of a Drilling Machine

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Base** | Heavy, ribbed casting — provides stability and supports column. Houses the **coolant tank** in some designs. |
| **Column** | Vertical cylindrical or box-type pillar. Carries the drill head. Has **ways** for vertical movement of the arm/head. |
| **Drill Head (Spindle Head)** | Houses the **drill spindle, feed mechanism, and speed-change gears**. Contains the **quill** for vertical feed. |
| **Spindle** | Rotates the drill. Has **Morse/ISO taper** socket to hold drill chucks. |
| **Drill Chuck** | Self-centering 3-jaw chuck holding straight-shank drills. |
| **Arm** | (Radial drilling machine) — horizontal arm that carries the drill head and can swivel around the column. |
| **Table** | Supports the workpiece. Can be **round table** (radial) or **rectangular slotted table** (with T-slots for clamping). |
| **Feed Handle / Handwheel** | Manually feeds the spindle via rack and pinion. |
| **Power Feed Mechanism** | Automatic feed by clutch and gear arrangement. |

---

### 2.5 Main Parts of a Milling Machine (Column-and-Knee Type)

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Base** | Large casting — provides foundation and rigidity. Houses **coolant and lubrication**. |
| **Column** | Vertical box-type structure. Houses **motor, drive mechanism, and speed-change gearbox**. Has ways for the knee. |
| **Knee** | Vertical sliding member mounted on column ways. Provides **vertical (Y-axis) feed** to the table. Houses **feed mechanism for X and Y axes**. |
| **Saddle** | Mounted on top of the knee. Provides **cross (X-axis) movement** to the table. |
| **Table** | Rectangular casting with **T-slots** for clamping the workpiece. Provides **longitudinal (Z-axis) movement**. |
| **Overarm** | (Horizontal milling) — extends from the column to support the **arbor** at the far end. |
| **Arbor** | Shaft that holds the **milling cutter** between the spindle nose and the overarm support. |
| **Spindle** | Rotates the cutter. Driven through gears or belt. |
| **Swivel Head** | (Universal milling) — spindle head can be swiveled to any angle. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis Convention (ISO 841 / KTU standard):**
> - **X-axis** = Longest table travel (longitudinal).
> - **Y-axis** = Cross travel.
> - **Z-axis** = Spindle axis (parallel to spindle rotation).
> - **W, V, U** = Secondary parallel axes.

---

### 2.6 Main Parts of a Surface Grinding Machine

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Base** | Heavy casting — supports column and table. |
| **Column** | Houses the **wheel head** and its vertical slide. |
| **Wheel Head** | Mounts the **grinding wheel spindle**. Provides **cross feed (Y) and vertical feed (Z)**. |
| **Table** | Reciprocates longitudinally (X) — carries the workpiece mounted on a **magnetic chuck**. |
| **Magnetic Chuck** | Electro-magnetic device holding **ferromagnetic workpieces** for grinding. |
| **Coolant Nozzle** | Directs cutting fluid onto the grinding zone. |

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the most frequently tested in KTU Module 2 numerical problems. All are derived from first principles (see Section 3).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $N$ = rpm, $V_c$ = m/min, $D$ = mm | For turning/drilling |
| 2 | Cutting speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Inverse of (1) |
| 3 | Feed rate (linear) | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | $f_r$ = mm/min, $f$ = mm/rev | For turning |
| 4 | Feed rate (milling) | $f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | $f_z$ = mm/tooth, $z$ = no. of teeth, $N$ = rpm | mm/min |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (turning) | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | $V_c$ = mm/min, $f$ = mm/rev, $d$ = mm | mm³/min |
| 6 | MRR (milling) | $MRR = f_r \cdot a \cdot d$ | $f_r$ = mm/min, $a$ = mm, $d$ = mm | mm³/min |
| 7 | Machining time | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | $L$ = length, $f_r$ = feed rate | minutes |
| 8 | Power | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_s}{60 \cdot 10^6}$ | $P$ = kW, $K_s$ = N/mm² | $K_s$ from tables |
| 9 | Swing / Height of centres | $H_c = \dfrac{D_{swing}}{2}$ | both in mm | Lathe geometry |
| 10 | Pitch of leadscrew | $P_{LS} = \text{lead of thread} \times \text{gear ratio}$ | mm | Thread cutting |
| 11 | Helix angle of cutter | $\sin\beta = \dfrac{P}{\pi \cdot D_c}$ | $P$ = cutter pitch, $D_c$ = cutter diameter | Milling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Never use the absolute value symbol $\vert x \vert$ directly in the above table cells — always write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - In **CNC machine procurement**, specifications like "positioning accuracy ± 0.005 mm" and "repeatability ± 0.003 mm" are decisive for selecting a CNC turning centre for aerospace parts.
> - In **production planning**, MRR calculated from specs decides cycle time, hence cost.
> - In **maintenance**, the leadscrew pitch and feed rod diameter specs are required to order spares.
> - In **ISO 9001-certified industries**, geometric accuracy tests (ISO 230) using dial indicators and laser interferometers are mandatory — directly linked to the **accuracy specifications** of the machine.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed Formula

**Step 1 — Definition of Cutting Speed.**
Cutting speed $V_c$ is defined as the *peripheral (surface) speed* of the workpiece at the cutting zone. If the workpiece rotates at $N$ rpm and has diameter $D$, then in **one revolution** the periphery sweeps a distance equal to the **circumference** $\pi D$.

> **Step explanation:** A rotating cylinder's surface moves a linear distance equal to its circumference every revolution.

**Step 2 — Distance per minute.**
In one revolution, distance = $\pi D$ (mm, if D is in mm).
In $N$ revolutions (per minute), distance = $\pi D N$ (mm/min).

> **Step explanation:** Multiply circumference by the number of revolutions per minute to get distance per minute.

**Step 3 — Convert to standard units (m/min).**
Industry standard expresses $V_c$ in **m/min**, so:

$$
V_c \;\; (\text{m/min}) \;\; = \;\; \frac{\pi \, D \, N}{1000}
$$

> **Step explanation:** Divide mm/min by 1000 to convert to m/min.

**Step 4 — Solve for spindle speed $N$.**
Rearranging the equation for $N$:

$$
N \;\; = \;\; \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \, D}
$$

> **Step explanation:** Multiply both sides by 1000, then divide by $\pi D$.

**Step 5 — Engineering check.**
- If $D$ increases, $N$ decreases (heavier jobs run slower).
- If $V_c$ increases (e.g., aluminum is machined at higher $V_c$ than steel), $N$ increases.
- Units: $\dfrac{(\text{m/min}) \cdot 1000}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{min} \cdot \text{mm}} \cdot 1000 = \text{rev/min}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning

**Step 1 — Definition.**
MRR is the *volume of material removed per unit time* (mm³/min). It is the product of the three "axes" of cut:
- Cutting speed (mm of periphery swept per min) = $V_c \times 1000$ (mm/min, converting m → mm).
- Feed $f$ (mm/rev) — the radial-ish advance per revolution.
- Depth of cut $d$ (mm) — the radial penetration.

> **Step explanation:** In each revolution, a chip of cross-section $f \times d$ is removed, and the periphery moves $\pi D$ mm. So in 1 minute, the volume removed = (periphery/min) × cross-section = $\pi D N \times f \times d$.

**Step 2 — Equating the two expressions for periphery swept.**
From Section 3.1, the periphery swept per minute = $\pi D N$ (mm/min).
But $V_c$ (m/min) is the same quantity in m/min, so:
$$\text{periphery per min (mm)} = 1000 \cdot V_c$$

> **Step explanation:** Use the two equivalent forms.

**Step 3 — Combine to get MRR.**
$$
MRR \;\; = \;\; (\text{periphery per min}) \times f \times d \;\; = \;\; 1000 \, V_c \cdot f \cdot d
$$

> **Step explanation:** Substitute the periphery in mm/min.

**Step 4 — Special compact form.**
Some textbooks use $V_c$ in **mm/min** to avoid the 1000 factor. KTU prefers m/min, so we retain the 1000:

$$
\boxed{MRR \;\; = \;\; 1000 \, V_c \, f \, d \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}}
$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional check.**
$[\text{mm/min}] \times [\text{mm/rev}] \times [\text{mm}] = \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Machining Time $t_m$

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Machining time is the time taken for the tool to traverse the required *effective workpiece length* at the given feed rate.

**Step 2 — Effective length.**
For a turning operation of length $L$ with approach $\ell_a$ and overtravel $\ell_o$:

$$
L_{eff} = L + \ell_a + \ell_o
$$

> **Step explanation:** Tool must travel workpiece length plus entry and exit distances.

**Step 3 — Time = distance / rate.**

$$
t_m \;\; = \;\; \frac{L_{eff}}{f_r} \;\; = \;\; \frac{L_{eff}}{f \cdot N}
$$

> **Step explanation:** Feed rate is mm/min, so dividing length (mm) by feed (mm/min) gives minutes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Specs vs. Parts of Common Machine Tools

| Specification Category | Lathe | Drilling | Milling | Grinding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Size-defining spec** | Swing over bed, length of bed | Max. drilling dia, throat depth | Table size (L × W), travel (X, Y, Z) | Table size, max. height |
| **Power-defining spec** | Main motor kW | Spindle motor kW | Spindle motor kW | Wheel motor kW |
| **Speed-defining spec** | Spindle rpm range | Spindle rpm range | Spindle rpm range | Wheel rpm (fixed) |
| **Accuracy-defining spec** | Spindle runout, bed flatness | Spindle runout, table flatness | Spindle runout, table flatness, squareness | Wheel spindle runout, table flatness |
| **Work-holding part** | Chuck, faceplate, centres | Drill vice, V-block, clamp | Vice, T-slot clamps, fixture | Magnetic chuck, vice |
| **Tool-holding part** | Tool post, tool holder | Drill chuck, sleeve | Arbor, collet, end-mill holder | Wheel flange |
| **Major structural part** | Bed | Column | Column & Knee | Column |
| **Major moving part** | Carriage, cross-slide, compound | Spindle & quill | Knee, saddle, table | Table, wheel head |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A mild steel shaft of diameter **60 mm** is to be turned on a lathe. Recommended cutting speed = **30 m/min**, feed = **0.3 mm/rev**, depth of cut = **2 mm**. Find (a) Spindle speed, (b) Feed rate, (c) MRR, (d) Power if specific cutting force $K_s$ = **2000 N/mm²**.

#### (a) Spindle speed
Using $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \, D}$:

$$
N \;\; = \;\; \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60} \;\; = \;\; \frac{30000}{188.496} \;\; = \;\; 159.15 \;\; \text{rpm}
$$

> **Logic:** Substitute $V_c$ in m/min and $D$ in mm. The 1000 factor converts m → mm.

In practice, choose the **nearest available spindle speed** on the machine, say **160 rpm**.

#### (b) Feed rate
$$
f_r \;\; = \;\; f \times N \;\; = \;\; 0.3 \times 159.15 \;\; = \;\; 47.75 \;\; \text{mm/min}
$$

> **Logic:** Feed per revolution × revolutions per minute = linear feed per minute.

#### (c) MRR
$$
MRR \;\; = \;\; 1000 \, V_c \, f \, d \;\; = \;\; 1000 \times 30 \times 0.3 \times 2 \;\; = \;\; 18000 \;\; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

> **Logic:** 1000 converts $V_c$ from m/min to mm/min; multiply by feed and depth.

#### (d) Power
$$
P \;\; = \;\; \frac{MRR \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6} \;\; = \;\; \frac{18000 \times 2000}{60 \times 10^6} \;\; = \;\; \frac{36 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^6} \;\; = \;\; 0.60 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

> **Logic:** Convert mm³/min to m³/s via $10^6$ and 60. Result in kW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU students:** A safety factor of **1.25 to 1.5** is added to the calculated power to account for transmission losses, idle power, and starting transients.
