## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any six specifications of a centre lathe and explain the importance of "swing over bed" and "length of bed".**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six important specifications of a centre lathe are:
1. Height of centres
2. Swing over bed
3. Length of bed
4. Spindle bore
5. Number and range of spindle speeds
6. Main motor power

**Importance of "swing over bed":**
It is the **largest diameter of a workpiece** that can be rotated above the bed ways without fouling. It determines the *maximum diameter* of a job that the lathe can accommodate and is the primary size-defining specification.

**Importance of "length of bed":**
It is the **maximum length** between the headstock spindle and the tailstock centre. It governs the *longest workpiece* that can be turned between centres.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern:** 6 specs listed (1 mark) + swing over bed explained (1 mark) + length of bed explained (1 mark) = 3 marks.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of (a) headstock, (b) carriage, and (c) tool post in a centre lathe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Headstock (1 mark):**
The headstock is mounted on the left end of the bed and **houses the main spindle, the gear train, and the speed-change mechanism**. It provides the workpiece with a controlled range of rotational speeds through levers and gears. It also supports the chuck/faceplate and the feed gearbox.

**(b) Carriage (1 mark):**
The carriage is mounted on the bed ways and **carries the cutting tool** along the workpiece. It comprises the saddle, cross-slide, compound slide, and apron. It provides **longitudinal (Z-axis), cross (X-axis), and angular** movements for various turning operations.

**(c) Tool Post (1 mark):**
The tool post is mounted on the compound slide and **rigidly holds the single-point cutting tool**. It can be of the *4-way turret type* or *quick-change type* and allows indexing of multiple tools in a single setup.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various classifications of machine tool specifications with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Machine tool specifications are systematically classified into three major categories to comprehensively describe a machine tool:

**1. General Specifications (Qualitative) — 2 marks:**
These describe the identity, type, and construction of the machine. Examples:
- *Type of machine* (e.g., horizontal centre lathe, vertical milling machine, radial drilling machine).
- *Drive type* (e.g., gear-driven, belt-driven, hydraulic, CNC servo).
- *Automation level* (e.g., manual, semi-automatic, automatic, CNC).
- *Construction* (e.g., column-and-knee, bed-type, planer-type, turret-type).
- *Coolant and lubrication system* availability.

**2. Specific Specifications (Quantitative) — 3 marks:**
These are numerical values defining size, capacity, and operational limits. Sub-groups:
- *Size and capacity:* Swing over bed, length of bed, height of centres, spindle bore.
- *Spindle and feed ranges:* Number of spindle speeds, range in rpm, feed range in mm/rev.
- *Power and dimensions:* Main motor power, floor space, net weight.

**3. Accuracy Specifications — 2 marks:**
These define the precision capabilities, tested per ISO 230 / IS 13855:
- *Positioning accuracy* — commanded vs. actual position deviation.
- *Repeatability* — ability to return to a position under same conditions.
- *Geometric accuracy* — straightness, flatness, squareness, parallelism of ways.
- *Surface finish* achievable on standard test pieces.

> **Conclusion:** A complete specification sheet must integrate all three categories so that an engineer can fully evaluate and select the machine for a given application.

---

**(b) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the main parts of a centre lathe and state the function of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A centre lathe is the most fundamental machine tool and consists of the following main parts:

| S.No. | Part | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bed** | Rigid cast-iron base with precision V-ways and flat ways; supports headstock, tailstock, and carriage. | 1 |
| 2 | **Headstock** | Houses spindle, gear train, and feed gearbox; provides variable spindle speeds. | 1 |
| 3 | **Tailstock** | Sliding member on bed; supports long workpieces via dead centre; quill can advance for drilling/reaming. | 1 |
| 4 | **Spindle** | Hollow shaft carrying chuck; rotates workpiece at commanded speed; has Morse taper. | 0.5 |
| 5 | **Carriage** | Slides on bed ways; carries cross-slide, compound slide, tool post; provides Z-axis movement. | 1 |
| 6 | **Cross-slide** | Mounted on saddle; provides X-axis movement for facing and diameter control. | 0.5 |
| 7 | **Compound Slide** | Swivel-mounted on cross-slide for taper turning. | 0.5 |
| 8 | **Tool Post** | Holds single-point cutting tool rigidly; 4-way or quick-change type. | 0.5 |
| 9 | **Apron** | Hangs from carriage front; houses feed clutches, half-nuts, and operating levers. | 0.5 |
| 10 | **Lead Screw & Feed Rod** | Lead screw for thread cutting; feed rod for general automatic feed. | 0.5 |

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to Section 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Centre Lathe — students should reproduce the block diagram in the answer script with proper labelling.)*

> **Conclusion:** Each part has a definite function and together they enable the lathe to perform turning, facing, threading, knurling, and boring operations on cylindrical workpieces.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) What are the general specifications of machine tools? Classify them with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

General specifications (also called qualitative or descriptive specifications) are those specifications that describe the **type, construction, drive, and operational philosophy** of a machine tool, rather than giving numerical values.

**Classification of General Specifications:**

**1. Type and Configuration — 2 marks:**
- *Type of machine* (lathe, milling, drilling, grinding, boring, shaping, planing).
- *Configuration* (e.g., horizontal vs. vertical, plain vs. universal, column-and-knee vs. bed-type).
- *Number of spindles* (single, multi-spindle).
- *Size class* (bench, medium, heavy-duty).

**2. Drive and Power Transmission — 2 marks:**
- *Drive type* (AC motor, DC motor, servo motor, hydraulic motor).
- *Transmission type* (gear train, belt drive, hydrodynamic, hydrostatic).
- *Number of speeds/feeds* (e.g., 8 speeds, 32 feeds).

**3. Automation and Control — 1.5 marks:**
- *Manual / Semi-automatic / Automatic / CNC / DNC level*.
- *Type of control* (mechanical, electrical, electronic, PLC-based, CNC).
- *Feedback system* (open-loop or closed-loop).

**4. Auxiliary Systems — 1.5 marks:**
- *Coolant system* (flood, mist, through-tool).
- *Lubrication* (manual, centralized, auto).
- *Chip removal* (gravity, conveyor, auger).
- *Safety interlocks and guarding* (light curtains, two-hand control, emergency stops).

> **Conclusion:** General specifications help in *initial screening* of machines before comparing them on numerical (specific) parameters.

---

**(b) Explain the constructional features and functions of the headstock, tailstock, and carriage of a centre lathe. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Headstock — 2.5 marks:**
- **Construction:** Heavy cast-iron body bolted to the left end of the bed. Inside it has a multi-step gear train, a back-gear arrangement for low speeds, and a feed gearbox. The front has the **spindle nose** (camlock or threaded type) for chuck/faceplate mounting.
- **Spindle:** Hollow hardened and ground steel shaft, mounted in precision **taper roller bearings**, with a **Morse taper** socket at the front (for live/dead centres) and the rear for back-gear engagement.
- **Function:** (i) Rotates the workpiece at the required cutting speed, (ii) Houses the speed-change and feed-change gear trains, (iii) Provides mounting for the chuck/faceplate.

**(2) Tailstock — 2 marks:**
- **Construction:** Cylindrical or box-type casting that slides on the bed ways. It has a **sleeve (quill)** that moves in/out via a handwheel and a rack-and-pinion. The quill has a **Morse taper** socket at the front to hold dead centres, drill chucks, or reamers. A **graduated scale** indicates quill travel.
- **Function:** (i) Supports the *free end* of long workpieces via a dead centre, (ii) Holds drills/reamers for light axial operations, (iii) Can be **set-over** for turning short tapers.

**(3) Carriage — 2.5 marks:**
- **Construction:** Assembly mounted on the bed ways, comprising the **saddle, cross-slide, compound slide, and tool post**. The apron hangs from the front and contains **feed clutches, half-nuts, and levers**. Engages with the **lead screw (for threading)** and the **feed rod (for general feeds)**.
- **Function:** (i) Carries the tool along the workpiece (Z-axis), (ii) Provides cross movement (X-axis) for facing and diameter control, (iii) Provides angular movement for taper turning, (iv) Transmits feed motion from feed rod/lead screw to the tool.

> **Conclusion:** The headstock provides *rotary motion*, the tailstock provides *support at the far end*, and the carriage provides *linear/angular tool motion* — together they form the kinematic core of the lathe.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **lead screw** (used ONLY for thread cutting) with **feed rod** (used for general turning feed) — losing up to 1 mark.
> 2. Writing **"swing over cross-slide"** when the question asks for **"swing over bed"** — these are different.
> 3. Forgetting the **apron** in the parts list — examiners expect it as a distinct part.
> 4. Wrongly stating that **height of centres = swing over bed** — it is *half* of swing over bed.
> 5. Omitting the **coolant and lubrication** systems from the auxiliary specifications list.
> 6. Failing to mention **Morse taper** when describing the spindle or tailstock quill.
> 7. Confusing **positioning accuracy** with **repeatability** — they are *different* metrics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Specification and Parts**

- [ ] **Specifications = the "brochure" of a machine tool** — three categories: General, Specific, Accuracy.
- [ ] **Swing over bed** = largest diameter that can be rotated above the bed. **Height of centres = Swing over bed / 2**.
- [ ] **Spindle bore** is the largest bar stock diameter that can pass through the hollow spindle.
- [ ] **Number of spindle speeds** is usually 8, 12, 16, or 24 in a manual lathe (geometric progression).
- [ ] **Lead screw = thread cutting only. Feed rod = general turning feed. Half-nuts engage with lead screw.**
- [ ] **Morse taper (MT 1 to MT 7)** is used in lathe spindles, tailstock quills, and drill sockets.
- [ ] **Main parts of a lathe:** Bed, Headstock, Tailstock, Spindle, Chuck, Carriage, Cross-slide, Compound slide, Tool post, Apron, Lead screw, Feed rod, Legs.
- [ ] **Main parts of a drilling machine:** Base, Column, Drill head, Spindle, Quill, Drill chuck, Table, Feed mechanism.
- [ ] **Main parts of a milling machine:** Base, Column, Knee, Saddle, Table, Spindle, Overarm, Arbor.
- [ ] **Main parts of a surface grinder:** Base, Column, Wheel head, Grinding wheel, Reciprocating table, Magnetic chuck.
- [ ] **X-axis = longest table travel; Y-axis = cross travel; Z-axis = spindle axis (ISO 841).**
- [ ] **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D}$.
- [ ] **MRR formula (turning):** $MRR = 1000 \, V_c \, f \, d$ mm³/min.
- [ ] **Power formula:** $P = \dfrac{MRR \times K_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ kW. Apply **safety factor 1.25–1.5**.
- [ ] **Machining time:** $t_m = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{f \cdot N}$ minutes, where $L_{eff} = L + \ell_a + \ell_o$.
- [ ] **Standards to remember:** IS 2063, IS 12955 (lathes), IS 13855, ISO 230, ISO 841, ISO 513.
- [ ] **Material of bed:** **Cast iron** (high damping); modern CNC machines use **polymer concrete** or **granite** for thermal stability.
- [ ] **Coolant and lubrication systems** are part of the *auxiliary general specifications*.
- [ ] **CNC machine tools additionally specify:** Positioning accuracy (e.g., ± 0.005 mm), Repeatability (± 0.003 mm), Rapid traverse rate (m/min), Tool changer capacity, and CNC controller type (Fanuc / Siemens / Mitsubishi).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Holding Devices. Types of Operations in a Lathe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Work Holding Devices. Types of Operations in a Lathe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Work Holding Devices — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Holding Devices (Jigs and Fixtures)**: These are specialized production tools used to locate, support, and hold the workpiece securely in a correct and stable orientation relative to the cutting tool. In a lathe, the device must transmit **rotary motion** from the spindle to the workpiece while resisting **cutting forces** (tangential, radial, and axial).

The **three fundamental functions** of any work holding device are:

1. **Locating** — establishing the correct position of the workpiece relative to the cutting tool.
2. **Clamping** — applying sufficient force to prevent movement during machining.
3. **Supporting** — counteracting the effect of cutting forces and self-weight deflection.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Potter's Wheel + Clamp"

Imagine a **potter's wheel** spinning a lump of clay. To make a uniform cylindrical pot, the potter must:
- **Center** the clay (locating) — like centering a 3-jaw chuck.
- **Press** the clay down so it doesn't fly off (clamping) — like the chuck jaws gripping.
- **Support** the tall pot as it rises (supporting) — like a **steady rest** holding a long slender shaft.

A lathe does the same thing, but with **steel** and **chips** flying off instead of clay.

## 1.3 Classification of Lathe Work Holding Devices

| Category | Examples | Typical Use |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Chucks** | 3-jaw self-centering, 4-jaw independent, Magnetic, Collet chuck | Short, irregular, or batch workpieces |
| **Centers & Mandrels** | Dead center, Live center, Mandrel (expanding, solid, gang) | Long slender shafts and pre-finished bores |
| **Face Plates & Carriers** | Face plate, Driving plate + Dog (Carrier) | Large, irregular, or off-center parts |
| **Rests** | Steady rest, Follow rest | Long, thin, flexible shafts to prevent deflection |
| **Special Fixtures** | V-blocks, special lathe fixtures | Mass production repeatability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The question paper frequently tests the **selection criterion** between a *chuck*, a *between-centers* setup, and a *mandrel* — students must justify the choice based on **workpiece geometry, length-to-diameter ratio (L/D), and required accuracy**.

## 1.4 Types of Operations in a Lathe — Overview

A modern lathe (engine lathe, CNC lathe) can perform a remarkably wide variety of operations by moving the cutting tool in different paths:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lathe Operation** = Any material-removal process where the **workpiece rotates** and the **single-point cutting tool** (or multi-point tool) is fed against it to produce a surface of desired geometry.

The operations include: **Turning, Facing, Threading, Knurling, Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Parting (Cut-off), Grooving, Taper Turning, Forming, Chamfering**, and **Undercutting**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Study of Major Work Holding Devices

### 2.1.1 Three-Jaw Self-Centering Chuck (Universal Chuck)

- **Construction**: Body, back plate, three jaws, scroll (spiral gear) plate, pinion.
- **Working Principle**: When the pinion (driven by a chuck key) is rotated, it engages the scroll plate which has a **spiral groove** (an Archimedean spiral). All three jaws move **simultaneously inwards or outwards** by equal amounts.
- **Self-Centering Accuracy**: Within **0.05 mm TIR** (Total Indicator Reading) for a properly adjusted chuck.
- **Limitation**: Cannot hold square, rectangular, or off-center work.
- **Holding Capacity**: Maximum swing diameter is fixed (e.g., 160 mm, 200 mm, 250 mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why"**: The scroll plate is mathematically an **Archimedean spiral** r = a + b·θ, which guarantees equal radial displacement of all three jaws — hence the name "self-centering."

### 2.1.2 Four-Jaw Independent Chuck

- **Construction**: Four jaws, each moved **independently** by its own adjusting screw.
- **Use**: Holding **square, rectangular, or eccentric** work; for accurate centering using a **dial indicator**.
- **Disadvantage**: Time-consuming to set up; requires skill.

### 2.1.3 Mandrels

A **mandrel** is a precision tool inserted into a previously machined bore to support the workpiece for **external operations** (like turning the OD to size).

| Type | Description | Use |
|------|-------------|-----|
| **Solid Mandrel** | Cylindrical with a slight taper (≈ 0.005–0.01 mm/m) | Light duty, small production |
| **Expanding Mandrel** | Tapered sleeve expands radially | Wider tolerance bores, better grip |
| **Gang Mandrel** | Long mandrel for multiple workpieces in a row | Mass production of rings/bushes |
| **Step Mandrel** | Several diameters on one mandrel | Workpieces of stepped bores |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a slight taper?** A solid mandrel has a **1:2000 to 1:3000** taper so that the workpiece can be slid on/off and wedged into position. The taper is so small that it does not affect the roundness of the workpiece.

### 2.1.4 Dead Center and Live Center

- **Dead Center**: Held stationary in the tailstock. The **workpiece rotates on the point** → high friction and heat. Lubricated with a high-pressure grease.
- **Live Center**: Has **bearings** inside; rotates with the workpiece → low friction, suitable for high speeds.
- **Why use centers?** Provides **two-point support** between headstock and tailstock → best possible concentricity and rigidity for long shafts.

### 2.1.5 Driving Plate and Carrier (Dog)

Used in a between-centers setup to **transmit rotation** from the spindle to the workpiece.
- **Driving plate** is bolted to the spindle nose.
- **Dog (carrier)** is bolted to the workpiece end and its tail engages a slot/pin in the driving plate.
- This arrangement allows the **workpiece to slide axially** during machining (e.g., for turning a long shaft that extends past the tailstock).

### 2.1.6 Steady Rest and Follow Rest

- **Steady Rest**: Fixed to the lathe bed; supports the workpiece at an intermediate point to prevent **sagging** of long slender shafts (L/D > 10:1).
- **Follow Rest**: Attached to the **carriage** and moves with the tool; supports the workpiece **right behind the cutting tool** to prevent **deflection under cutting force**.

### 2.1.7 Face Plate

A circular plate bolted directly to the spindle. **Slots and T-slots** are provided for clamping irregular workpieces using **bolts, straps, and clamps**.

### 2.1.8 Collet Chuck

A **collet** is a cylindrical sleeve with **axial slits** that is drawn into a tapered seating, causing it to contract and grip the workpiece.
- **Holding capacity**: limited to a **narrow size range** (e.g., 1–10 mm or 10–20 mm).
- **Accuracy**: very high (TIR < 0.02 mm), ideal for **small-diameter precision work** (watchmaking, instrument making).

## 2.2 Selection Logic — Which Device When?

```
Workpiece is short & round     →  3-Jaw Chuck
Workpiece is square/irregular  →  4-Jaw Chuck
Workpiece is small bar/rod     →  Collet
Workpiece is long slender shaft →  Between Centers + Dog
Workpiece has finished bore    →  Mandrel
Workpiece is too long (L/D>10) →  + Steady/Follow Rest
Workpiece is very large/odd    →  Face Plate
```

## 2.3 Types of Lathe Operations — Detailed Analysis

| # | Operation | Tool Movement | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------------|---------|
| 1 | **Straight Turning** | Tool moves parallel to axis, radially inward | Reduce diameter of cylindrical workpiece |
| 2 | **Facing** | Tool moves radially inward, parallel to face | Produce flat surface perpendicular to axis |
| 3 | **Taper Turning** | Tool moves at an angle | Produce conical surface |
| 4 | **Thread Cutting** | Tool moves parallel to axis with synchronized feed (feed = pitch) | Cut external/internal threads |
| 5 | **Knurling** | Multi-point tool pressed against surface | Produce roughened pattern for grip |
| 6 | **Drilling** | Axial feed with rotating drill (held in tailstock) | Produce hole |
| 7 | **Boring** | Single-point tool enlarges existing hole | Improve accuracy/size of hole |
| 8 | **Reaming** | Multi-tooth tool (reamer) finishes hole | Improve surface finish and accuracy |
| 9 | **Grooving** | Narrow tool fed radially | Cut shallow groove (for O-ring, snap ring) |
| 10 | **Parting / Cut-off** | Narrow tool fed radially to full depth | Separate workpiece from stock |
| 11 | **Forming** | Tool of specific profile fed radially | Produce contoured profile |
| 12 | **Chamfering** | Tool at 45° at the corner | Remove sharp edge, aid assembly |

### 2.3.1 Taper Turning — Three Methods

1. **Compound Rest Method** — tool set at half-taper angle α; limited to short tapers.
2. **Tailstock Set-Over Method** — tailstock body shifted sideways; very long tapers possible.
3. **Taper Turning Attachment** — linear guide attached to bed; tool moves on a guide bar at the desired angle; most versatile.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these formulas appear in almost every numerical problem on lathe operations.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| **Cutting Speed** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Inverse of above |
| **Feed (linear)** | $f_l = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f$ in mm/rev |
| **Material Removal Rate (Turning)** | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut in mm |
| **MRR in cc/min** | $MRR = \dfrac{f \cdot d \cdot V}{1000}$ | cm³/min | Convenience unit |
| **Machining Time — Turning** | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V}$ | min | $L$ in mm |
| **Machining Time — Facing** | $T_m = \dfrac{(D_0 - D_f)}{2 \cdot f \cdot N}$ | min | Tool moves from $D_0$ to $D_f$ |
| **Taper Ratio** | $T_R = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | unitless | $D$ = large Ø, $d$ = small Ø, $L$ = length |
| **Taper per mm** (TPI mm) | $K = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | mm/mm | Used in compound rest |
| **Half-angle** (compound rest set-over) | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | degrees | α in compound rest |
| **Tailstock Set-Over** | $X = \dfrac{L_t}{2} \cdot \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | mm | $L_t$ = total length of workpiece |
| **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot \text{Specific Energy}}{60}$ | kW | For roughing estimates |
| **Power (Cutting Force)** | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{1000 \cdot 60}$ | kW | $F_c$ in N, $V$ in m/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Absolute Value**: When writing `|D-d|` in any formula in the KTU answer sheet, always use $\vert D - d \vert$ in LaTeX (NOT the vertical bar `|`) to avoid markdown table breaking.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cutting speed $V$** is set from a **machinability chart** based on workpiece material and tool material (HSS, Carbide, Ceramic). For mild steel with HSS: $V \approx 30$ m/min; with Carbide: $V \approx 150$ m/min.
- **Feed $f$** is constrained by **surface roughness requirement** — for a given nose radius $r$, $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8r}$.
- These formulas are the **backbone of CNC G-code programming** — every CNC lathe programmer uses $N = 1000V/(\pi D)$ as the very first calculation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed Formula

**Statement to prove**: 
$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

**Derivation**:

**Step 1 — Define the physical meaning of cutting speed.**
Cutting speed $V$ is the **linear speed at which the cutting edge of the tool sweeps past the workpiece surface**. In one full rotation, a point on the surface traces a **circle of circumference** equal to $\pi D$.

**Step 2 — Express distance per revolution.**
In one revolution of the workpiece:
$$d_{\text{per rev}} = \pi \cdot D \quad \text{(mm/rev)}$$

**Step 3 — Express total distance per minute.**
If the spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then the total distance swept per minute is:
$$V_{\text{raw}} = (\pi \cdot D) \cdot N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert mm to metres.**
Since $1000$ mm $= 1$ m, dividing by 1000 converts mm/min to m/min:
$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Machining Time for Straight Turning

**Given**: Workpiece initial diameter $D_0$, final diameter $D_f$, length to be turned $L$, feed $f$ (mm/rev), spindle speed $N$ (rpm). Note: the cutting speed $V$ is held **constant** by automatic variation of $N$ as $D$ reduces — this is the essence of CSS (Constant Surface Speed) mode.

**Step 1 — Express feed rate in mm/min.**
The carriage feed rate (linear feed) is:
$$f_l = f \cdot N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Step 2 — Define machining time.**
Machining time is the **length to be cut divided by the feed rate**:
$$T_m = \frac{L}{f_l} = \frac{L}{f \cdot N}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of cutting speed.**
Substitute $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$:
$$T_m = \frac{L}{f} \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D}{1000 \cdot V} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V} \quad \text{(min)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Practical note**: When the diameter changes appreciably (e.g., $D_0 = 80$ mm, $D_f = 40$ mm), modern CNC lathes compensate $N$ continuously, and the formula above (with average $D$) gives an excellent engineering estimate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Machining Time for Facing

**Geometry of facing**: A facing tool moves from the **outer diameter $D_0$** radially inwards (or outwards) to the **final diameter $D_f$**, removing a disc-shaped chip.

**Step 1 — Total radial travel of the tool.**
The tool moves a radial distance:
$$\Delta R = \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate in mm/min is constant** (same as turning): $f_l = f \cdot N$.

**Step 3 — Machining time**:
$$T_m = \frac{\Delta R}{f \cdot N} = \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2 \cdot f \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula is **unique to facing** — students often mistakenly use $T_m = L/(fN)$ which is the turning formula. **Examiner's Pitfall**: Wrong formula here = full 7 marks lost.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style

**Problem**: A mild steel shaft of initial diameter 80 mm is to be turned down to 70 mm over a length of 250 mm. Cutting speed $V = 25$ m/min, feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev. Calculate the spindle speed, material removal rate, and machining time.

**Solution**:

**Step 1 — Average diameter for speed calculation (assuming manual non-CNC):**
$$D_{avg} = \frac{80 + 70}{2} = 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 75} = \frac{25000}{235.62}$$
$$N \approx 106.1 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Depth of cut:**
$$d = \frac{80 - 70}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — MRR:**
$$MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V = 0.3 \cdot 5 \cdot 25 = 37.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to cm³/min:
$$MRR = 37.5 \cdot 60 = 2250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 2.25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Machining time (using $D_{avg}$):**
$$T_m = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V} = \frac{\pi \cdot 75 \cdot 250}{1000 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 25}$$
$$T_m = \frac{58904.86}{7500} \approx 7.85 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Note**: The examiner accepts either the $D_{avg}$ approach or the **logarithmic mean diameter** $D_{LM} = (D_0 - D_f)/\ln(D_0/D_f)$. For manual lathe problems, $D_{avg}$ is the standard.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operation vs. Tool vs. Work Holding

| Operation | Work Holding Used | Tool Type | Special Note |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|--------------|
| Straight Turning | Chuck + Tailstock Center (long) / Chuck only (short) | Single-point HSS/Carbide | Constant $V$ requires $N$ change with $D$ |
| Facing | Same | Same | Watch formula — see Section 3.3 |
| Taper Turning | Same | Same (tool tilted or tailstock offset) | Compound rest limited to < 100 mm length |
| Thread Cutting | Between Centers + Dog (rigidity critical) | Thread-cutting tool with thread profile | Feed = pitch of thread (e.g., 1.5 mm/rev for M10) |
| Knurling | Chuck | Multi-wheel knurling tool | Two knurls press against rotating workpiece |
| Drilling | Chuck (workpiece) + Drill chuck in tailstock | Twist drill | Tailstock quill feed for drilling |
| Boring | Chuck or Mandrel | Single-point boring bar | For higher accuracy than drilling |
| Reaming | Chuck | Multi-tooth reamer | Very small feed, no coolant (for HSS reamer in steel) |
| Parting | Chuck | Parting tool (narrow blade) | Highest risk of chatter; tool overhang critical |

## 3.6 Mathematical Note — Surface Roughness $R_a$

Theoretical surface roughness produced by a turning operation is:
$$R_a \approx \frac{f^2}{8 \cdot r_{\varepsilon}}$$

where $r_{\varepsilon}$ is the **nose radius** of the tool insert (typically 0.4, 0.8, or 1.2 mm). This formula is **frequently asked** in Part A as a 3-mark question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight**: To halve the roughness, you must **quarter the feed**. This is why fine finishing passes use very low feed (e.g., 0.05 mm/rev).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Work Holding Device Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START - New Workpiece] --> B{Workpiece Geometry?}
    B -->|Cylindrical, short| C{Length to Diameter Ratio L/D?}
    B -->|Square / Rectangular| D[Use 4-Jaw Independent Chuck]
    B -->|Irregular / Oversize| E[Use Face Plate with Straps]
    B -->|Pre-finished bore exists| F[Use Mandrel - Solid or Expanding]
    B -->|Small diameter bar| G[Use Collet Chuck]

    C -->|L/D less than 4| H[3-Jaw Self-Centering Chuck]
    C -->|L/D 4 to 10| I[Chuck plus Tailstock Center]
    C -->|L/D greater than 10| J[Between Centers plus Dog plus Steady Rest]

    H --> K[Select Tool and Operation]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    D --> K
    E --> K
    F --> K
    G --> K

    K --> L[Required Operation]
    L --> M[Turning Facing Taper Turning]
    L --> N[Threading Knurling Grooving]
    L --> O[Drilling Boring Reaming]
    L --> P[Parting Forming Chamfering]

    M --> Q[Apply Formulas V, N, MRR, Tm]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[END - Machined Component]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Lathe Functional Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain[POWER TRAIN SUBSYSTEM]
        P1[Main Motor] --> P2[Spindle Drive]
        P2 --> P3[Spindle]
        P3 --> P4[Chuck or Driving Plate]
    end

    subgraph WorkHolding[WORK HOLDING SUBSYSTEM]
        W1[3-Jaw Chuck] --> P4
        W2[4-Jaw Chuck] --> P4
        W3[Collet] --> P4
        W4[Face Plate] --> P4
        W5[Driving Plate plus Dog] --> P3
    end

    subgraph ToolHolding[TOOL HOLDING AND FEED SUBSYSTEM]
        T1[Tool Post] --> T2[Cutting Tool]
        T3[Compound Slide] --> T1
        T4[Cross Slide] --> T3
        T5[Carriage] --> T4
        T6[Lead Screw and Feed Rod] --> T5
    end

    subgraph Support[SUPPORT SUBSYSTEM]
        S1[Bed] --> S2[Headstock]
        S1 --> S3[Tailstock]
        S1 --> S4[Carriage Ways]
        S2 --> P3
        S3 --> S5[Dead or Live Center]
    end

    P4 --> WP[Workpiece]
    T2 --> WP
    WP --> CHIP[Chips - Material Removal]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Taper Turning Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Taper Turning Required] --> B{Length of Taper?}
    B -->|Short less than 100 mm| C{Precision Needed?}
    C -->|High| D[Compound Rest Method - Set at Half Angle]
    C -->|Moderate| D
    B -->|Medium 100 to 300 mm| E[Use Taper Turning Attachment on Bed]
    B -->|Long Shaft entire length tapered| F[Tailstock Set-Over Method]

    D --> G[Set tool to angle alpha where tan alpha equals D minus d divided by 2L]
    E --> H[Adjust guide bar angle on attachment]
    F --> I[Offset tailstock by X equals L times D minus d divided by 2L]

    G --> J[Perform Taper Turn]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Measure with Taper Gauge or Sine Bar]
    K --> L{Within Tolerance?}
    L -->|Yes| M[END - Accepted]
    L -->|No| N[Adjust and Recut]
    N --> J
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Between-Centers Setup with Dog and Driving Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HeadstockSide[HEADSTOCK END]
        H1[Spindle Nose] --> H2[Driving Plate Bolted to Spindle]
        H2 --> H3[Slot in Driving Plate]
    end

    subgraph WorkpieceArea[WORKPIECE AREA]
        W1[Workpiece] --> W2[Left End - Engages with Live Center in Headstock OR Chuck]
        W1 --> W3[Right End - Tail of Dog Bolted Here]
        W4[Dog Body] --> W3
        W4 --> W5[Dog Tail - Engages H3 Slot]
    end

    subgraph TailstockSide[TAILSTOCK END]
        T1[Tailstock Quill] --> T2[Dead Center or Live Center]
        T2 --> T3[Pointed Tip Engages Center Hole in W1]
    end

    H3 -.rotational drive transmitted.-> W5
    W1 -.rotates with dog.-> T3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide**: Arrows in the "Between-Centers Setup" show the **rotational drive path** — the driving plate (headstock side) drags the dog, which drags the workpiece, which is also supported axially by the tailstock center. This dual support provides **maximum rigidity and concentricity** for precision turning.
