# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a three-jaw self-centering chuck and a four-jaw independent chuck. State one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer**:

| Feature | 3-Jaw Self-Centering | 4-Jaw Independent |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Number of jaws** | 3, equally spaced 120° apart | 4, equally spaced 90° apart |
| **Jaw movement** | Simultaneous (via scroll plate) | Independent (each jaw has its own screw) |
| **Centering** | Automatic | Manual (with dial indicator) |
| **Accuracy** | ± 0.05 mm TIR | Up to ± 0.01 mm TIR |
| **Speed of clamping** | Fast | Slow |
| **Suitable work** | Round/hexagonal stock | Square, rectangular, eccentric |

**Applications**:
- **3-jaw chuck**: Holding a round bar for facing and straight turning in a production run.
- **4-jaw chuck**: Holding a square casting for turning the OD, or holding an eccentric workpiece.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of a steady rest and a follow rest? When is each used?**

**Model Answer**:
- **Steady Rest**: A fixed support mounted on the **lathe bed** at an intermediate point along a long workpiece. It has three adjustable jaws (or rollers) that contact the workpiece circumference to **prevent sagging due to self-weight** and to damp vibrations.
  - **Used when**: L/D ratio of workpiece exceeds **10:1** and the workpiece cannot be supported by tailstock alone. Also used for grinding long shafts.

- **Follow Rest**: A support **attached to the carriage** that travels with the tool, with two adjustable fingers supporting the workpiece **immediately behind the cutting tool**.
  - **Used when**: Turning long slender shafts where the cutting force itself causes deflection that would otherwise produce a "barrel-shaped" or tapered-out workpiece. It ensures **roundness** along the entire length.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of a **three-jaw self-centering chuck**. List its **two limitations**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **mild steel** workpiece of **60 mm diameter and 300 mm length** is to be turned down to **50 mm** in a single pass. If the recommended cutting speed is **30 m/min** and feed is **0.25 mm/rev**, calculate the **spindle speed, MRR, and machining time**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Construction**: A 3-jaw self-centering chuck consists of:
1. **Body** — the outer cylindrical housing bolted to the spindle nose.
2. **Back plate** — bolts the body to the spindle.
3. **Scroll plate** — a circular plate with an **Archimedean spiral groove** cut on its face.
4. **Three jaws** — slide in radial slots; each has a T-pin or tongue that engages the spiral groove.
5. **Pinion** — engages teeth on the periphery of the scroll plate, turned by a **chuck key**.

**Working**:
1. The chuck key is inserted into the pinion and rotated.
2. The pinion rotates the scroll plate via its gear teeth.
3. The **spiral groove** on the scroll plate forces all three jaws to move **simultaneously inwards (grip) or outwards (release)** by **exactly the same amount**.
4. Because the three jaws are spaced 120° apart and move equally, the workpiece is **automatically centered** on the spindle axis.

**Two Limitations**:
1. **Cannot hold square, rectangular, or irregular workpieces** — the self-centering motion assumes rotational symmetry.
2. **Limited gripping force** compared to an independent chuck; the gripping force decreases as the jaw extends further out (longer workpiece).
3. (Bonus) Wear of the scroll plate reduces centering accuracy over time.

**Diagram (schematic in answer script)**:
```
[ Sketch showing: spindle → back plate → body → 
  three jaws (120° apart) → scroll plate with spiral groove → 
  pinion (with chuck key slot) ]
```

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Average diameter:**
$$D_{avg} = \frac{60 + 50}{2} = 55 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 55} = \frac{30000}{172.79}$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 173.6 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 3 — Depth of cut:**
$$d = \frac{60 - 50}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Material Removal Rate:**
$$MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V = 0.25 \cdot 5 \cdot 30 = 37.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to cc/min (multiply by 60, divide by 1000):
$$MRR = 37.5 \cdot 60 = 2250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 2.25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

Or in SI:
$$\boxed{MRR = 2250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} = 2.25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 5 — Machining time:**
$$T_m = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{avg} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot f \cdot V} = \frac{\pi \cdot 55 \cdot 300}{1000 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 30}$$
$$T_m = \frac{51836.2}{7500}$$
$$\boxed{T_m \approx 6.91 \text{ min}}$$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the **construction and working of a live center**. State the **two situations** in which a live center is preferred over a dead center. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **tapered shaft** of length 200 mm has a **diameter of 50 mm at one end and 40 mm at the other**. Calculate: (i) the **taper ratio**, (ii) the **taper per mm**, and (iii) the **compound rest setting (half-angle)** if taper turning is to be done using the compound rest method. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Construction of a Live Center**:
- A live center is mounted in the **tailstock spindle** and has its point rotating on bearings, not stationary.
- It consists of:
  1. **Tapered shank** (Morse taper, e.g., MT3, MT4) — fits into the tailstock.
  2. **Body** containing **two sets of ball/roller bearings** (one thrust, one radial) supporting the center point.
  3. **Center point (60° included angle)** — hardened and ground, often with carbide tip.
  4. **Bearing housing** — holds the bearings and is mounted on the shank.

**Working**:
1. The live center is inserted into the tailstock.
2. The tailstock quill is advanced to engage the point into the **center hole** of the workpiece.
3. As the workpiece (held in chuck or by dog) rotates, the **center point rotates with it** because the bearings allow free rotation.
4. Therefore, **friction between the center and the workpiece center hole is eliminated** — no lubrication is required.

**Two situations where live center is preferred**:
1. **High spindle speeds** (e.g., above 1000 rpm) — dead center would burn due to friction at the center hole.
2. **Long machining durations** or heavy cuts where heat buildup at the center hole would affect accuracy.

**Sketch in answer** (text representation):
```
  [Tapered shank] → [Bearing 1] → [Bearing 2] → [Center point 60°]
                            ↓
                      [Workpiece rotates here]
```

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given**: $L = 200$ mm, $D = 50$ mm, $d = 40$ mm.

**(i) Taper Ratio** $T_R$:
$$T_R = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{50 - 40}{200} = \frac{10}{200} = \frac{1}{20}$$
$$\boxed{T_R = 1 \text{ in } 20}$$

**(ii) Taper per mm** (or Taper per mm of length):
$$K = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{10}{200} = 0.05 \text{ mm/mm}$$
$$\boxed{K = 0.05 \text{ mm per mm}}$$

This means the diameter reduces by **0.05 mm for every 1 mm of length** along the taper.

**(iii) Compound Rest Half-Angle $\alpha$**:
The compound rest is set to **half** the included taper angle:
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \cdot 200} = \frac{10}{400} = 0.025$$
$$\alpha = \arctan(0.025) = 1.432°$$
$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 1.43°}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> 1. **Confusing Taper Ratio vs Taper Angle**: Taper ratio is a pure number (e.g., 1:20) — it is **NOT** an angle. Many students write "1:20 degrees" and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the half-angle**: The compound rest must be set at **half** the full taper angle, not the full angle. Setting at full angle produces a **double-taper** error.
> 3. **Using wrong formula for tailstock set-over**: Tailstock set-over $X$ uses $L_t/2$ (total length of workpiece, not taper length) in some textbook versions. State which convention you follow.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention average diameter** in $N$ calculation: If you use $D_0$ instead of $D_{avg}$, you lose 1 mark on the spindle speed.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency**: Always carry units through every step — the answer `1/20` is dimensionless, but feed is in mm/rev, cutting speed in m/min. Mismatched units lose 2 marks per question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Work Holding Devices — Quick Recap
- **3-Jaw Chuck** = Self-centering, fast, round/hex work only.
- **4-Jaw Chuck** = Independent jaws, slow but universal shape, high accuracy.
- **Collet** = Small Ø bars, very high accuracy (TIR < 0.02 mm).
- **Mandrel** = For external machining of pre-bored work; **expanding type** gives the best grip.
- **Between Centers** = Long shafts, best concentricity; **Live center mandatory above 1000 rpm**.
- **Face Plate** = For irregular, large, or unbalanced parts.
- **Driving Plate + Dog** = Transmits rotation in between-centers setup, allows axial sliding.
- **Steady Rest** = Bed-mounted intermediate support; use when **L/D > 10:1**.
- **Follow Rest** = Carriage-mounted, supports **right behind tool** to prevent deflection.

### 🎯 Lathe Operations — Quick Recap
- **Turning** reduces diameter; **Facing** produces a flat end.
- **Threading** feed = thread pitch; **Knurling** uses a multi-wheel tool.
- **Taper turning** uses **compound rest, tailstock set-over, or taper attachment**.
- **Drilling/Boring** enlarges holes; **Reaming** finishes them.
- **Parting** uses a narrow blade and is the **most chatter-prone** operation.

### 🎯 Formula Cheat Code (Write This on Your Hand)
```
V    = πDN / 1000          m/min
N    = 1000V / (πD)        rpm
MRR  = f · d · V           mm³/s   (turning)
Tm   = L / (f · N)         min     (turning)
Tm   = (D-d) / (2fN)       min     (facing)
TR   = (D-d) / L           ratio
tanα = (D-d) / 2L          half angle
```

### 🎯 Common Pitfall Checklist Before Exam
- ✅ Did you use **$D_{avg}$** (or specify CSS) when computing $N$?
- ✅ Did you write **half-angle** in the compound rest formula?
- ✅ Did you clearly state **L/D > 10:1** as the trigger for steady rest?
- ✅ Did you mention **mandrel taper of 1:2000 to 1:3000**?
- ✅ Did you convert **mm³/s ↔ cm³/min ↔ cm³/s** correctly?
- ✅ Did you add units to **every numerical answer**?
- ✅ Did you draw a **neat, labeled sketch** for any 7-mark construction question?

### 🎯 High-Yield Theory One-Liners (For 2-Mark Sub-Part Questions)
1. "A **collet chuck** gives the highest gripping accuracy for small-diameter bar stock."
2. "The **Archimedean spiral** on the scroll plate is what enables self-centering in a 3-jaw chuck."
3. "**Between-centers** work holding is preferred for **concentricity and rigidity** on long shafts."
4. "The **driving plate + dog** combination allows the workpiece to **slide axially** while rotating."
5. "**Taper turning by tailstock set-over** can produce a taper over the **entire length** of the workpiece."
6. "**Follow rest** is bolted to the carriage and moves **with** the cutting tool."
7. "**Parting tool** is the most **vibration-prone** operation in a lathe — keep overhang minimal."
8. "**MRR for facing** decreases continuously as the tool approaches the center — chip cross-section reduces."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plain Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plain Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plain Turning** is a fundamental *lathe-based* machining process in which a cylindrical workpiece is rotated at a constant spindle speed about its longitudinal axis while a **single-point cutting tool (SPCT)** is advanced linearly in a direction *parallel*, *perpendicular*, or at a *compound angle* to the axis of rotation. The relative motion between the rotating job and the translating tool causes shearing of the material in the form of chips, thereby generating a new cylindrical surface of reduced diameter.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, plain turning is classified as a **material-removal process governed by orthogonal (2D) cutting mechanics** when the feed is directed along the radius (radial turning), or as an **oblique cutting process** when a standard turning tool (with side cutting edge angle ≠ 90°) is used for straight turning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCIET402 – Module 2):**
> Plain turning is the *baseline* operation upon which taper turning, thread cutting, parting, and grooving are mathematically and kinematically derived. Mastering the kinematic chain, cutting parameters, and tool geometry of plain turning is **mandatory** before progressing to advanced operations.

## 1.2 Key Engineering Terminology

| Term | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | m/min | Surface velocity of workpiece at tool tip |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | rpm | Revolutions per minute of job |
| Feed | $f$ | mm/rev | Tool advance per spindle revolution |
| Feed Rate | $f_n$ | mm/min | Linear tool feed per minute |
| Depth of Cut | $d$ | mm | Radial penetration of tool |
| Workpiece Diameter | $D$ | mm | Initial diameter before pass |
| Finished Diameter | $D_f$ | mm | Final diameter after pass |
| Length of Cut | $L$ | mm | Total axial travel of tool |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Potter's Wheel & Chisel" Intuition

Imagine a **potter's wheel** spinning a lump of clay at a constant rotational speed. The potter holds a flat **chisel (the cutting tool)** and gently presses it against the spinning clay. As the wheel rotates, the chisel continuously shaves off a thin ribbon of clay, gradually reducing the diameter of the pot.

- The **spinning clay** is analogous to the **workpiece** rotating in the lathe chuck.
- The **chisel** is the **single-point cutting tool** held in the tool post.
- The **force applied by the potter's hand** is the **depth of cut ($d$)**.
- The **rate at which the potter moves the chisel sideways** is the **feed ($f$)**.
- The **speed of the wheel surface where the chisel contacts** is the **cutting speed ($V_c$)**.

Just as a potter must coordinate hand movement, wheel speed, and chisel sharpness to obtain a smooth, dimensionally accurate pot, a turner must balance **$V_c$**, **$f$**, and **$d$** to achieve desired **dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and tool life** — the three pillars of any turning operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** *"The wheel spins fast, the tool moves slow-but-steady, the chip peels away."* This captures the relative motion hierarchy of turning.

## 1.4 Kinematic Chain of a Centre Lathe (Brief Context)

The energy flow in plain turning follows this order:

$$ \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Spindle (Job)} \rightarrow \text{Surface at Tool Tip} \rightarrow \text{Cutting Force} \rightarrow \text{Chip Formation} $$

The workpiece receives rotational motion from the **headstock spindle**, while the tool receives translational (feed) motion from the **carriage** (via the **lead screw** or **feed rod**). This **two-motion** system is the defining kinematic signature of all turning operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles – Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Workpiece Rotation (Primary Motion)
The job is held between the **chuck (headstock)** and the **tailstock centre**, and rotated at a programmed spindle speed $N$ (rpm). This creates the **cutting velocity** at the periphery of the workpiece.

$$ V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}] $$

where $D$ is in **mm**. The factor $1000$ converts mm to metres.

### Step 2: Tool Feed (Secondary Motion)
The single-point tool, mounted in the **tool post**, is translated along the axis (longitudinal feed) at a rate $f_n$ (mm/min), which is the product of feed per revolution $f$ (mm/rev) and spindle speed $N$:

$$ f_n = f \times N \quad [\text{mm/min}] $$

### Step 3: Material Removal
The tool penetrates radially to a depth $d$ (mm), initiating **chip formation** through a **shear deformation zone** ahead of the cutting edge. The undeformed chip thickness is approximately equal to the feed $f$ (for plain turning with a sharp tool).

### Step 4: Chip Formation Mechanics
Material in front of the tool is plastically deformed along a **shear plane** inclined at angle $\phi$ (shear angle) to the cutting velocity direction. The relationship is governed by **Merchant's Circle Analysis**:

$$ \tan(\phi) = \frac{r \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{1 - r \cdot \sin(\alpha)} $$

where $r$ is the **chip-thickness ratio** ($r = t_1 / t_2$, with $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness, $t_2$ = chip thickness after cut), and $\alpha$ is the **rake angle**.

### Step 5: Tool Engagement and Exit
As the tool travels the full length of the workpiece, the entire cylindrical surface is regenerated. At the end of the pass, the tool is **rapidly retracted** (using the cross-slide) to avoid dragging and to prepare for the next pass.

## 2.2 Tool Geometry of a Single-Point Turning Tool

A standard turning tool is defined by **seven primary angles** (KTU 2024 syllabus essential):

1. **Back Rake Angle ($\gamma$)** – inclination of the top face in the longitudinal plane.
2. **Side Rake Angle ($\gamma_s$)** – inclination of the top face in the transverse plane.
3. **End Relief (Clearance) Angle ($\alpha$)** – prevents rubbing of the tool flank on the machined surface.
4. **Side Relief Angle ($\alpha_s$)** – prevents flank rubbing on the cylinder being generated.
5. **End Cutting Edge Angle ($\epsilon_r$)** – orientation of the end cutting edge w.r.t. the workpiece surface.
6. **Side Cutting Edge Angle ($\phi_s$)** – orientation of the side cutting edge w.r.t. the feed direction.
7. **Nose Radius ($r_\epsilon$)** – rounded tool tip that improves surface finish and tool strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is tool geometry critical?**
> - **Positive rake** → reduces cutting force, lowers power, suitable for soft materials.
> - **Negative rake** → strengthens cutting edge, used for hard materials and interrupted cuts.
> - **Larger nose radius** → better surface finish ($R_a \propto 1/r_\epsilon$) but higher radial force and chatter tendency.

## 2.3 Types of Chips in Plain Turning

| Chip Type | Formation Mechanism | Material Behaviour | Operator Indication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Chip** | Plastic deformation, no fracture | Ductile (e.g., mild steel, aluminium) | Good finish, ideal for high $V_c$ |
| **Discontinuous Chip** | Periodic fracture along shear plane | Brittle (e.g., cast iron, bronze) | Lower power, granular chips |
| **Built-Up Edge (BUE) Chip** | Workpiece material welds to tool tip | Low $V_c$ + ductile material | Poor finish, requires higher $V_c$ |
| **Serrated (Saw-Tooth) Chip** | Partial fracture with ductile bonding | Titanium, stainless steel | Indicates high cutting temperature |

## 2.4 Tool Wear Mechanisms

Two dominant wear regions govern turning tool life:

- **Crater Wear** – on the **rake face** due to diffusion and abrasion at high temperatures.
- **Flank Wear** – on the **relief (clearance) face** due to rubbing and abrasion.

A tool is considered **end-of-life** when:
1. **Flank wear land width (VB)** $\geq$ **0.3 mm** (ISO 3685 standard).
2. **Crater depth (KT)** exceeds a critical value.
3. Catastrophic failure (chipping, fracture) occurs.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D}$ | Spindle speed | rpm |
| 3 | $f_n = f \times N$ | Feed rate | mm/min |
| 4 | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min |
| 5 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | Cutting power (motor) | kW |
| 6 | $K_c = \dfrac{F_c}{f \cdot d}$ | Specific cutting energy | N/mm² |
| 7 | $F_R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_f^2}$ | Resultant cutting force | N |
| 8 | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_n}$ | Machining time | min |
| 9 | $V_c \cdot T^n = C$ | Taylor's tool life equation | — |
| 10 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 \, r_\epsilon}$ | Theoretical surface roughness | µm |
| 11 | $h = d \times \tan(\beta)$ | Taper rise per unit length | mm |
| 12 | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{D + D_f}{2}$ | Average diameter for long jobs | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, the cutting force is denoted $F_c$ (or sometimes $F_t$ for tangential), the feed force is $F_f$ (or $F_x$ axial), and the radial (deflecting) force is $F_r$ (or $F_y$). The **tangential force $F_c$ governs the power calculation**, while the **radial force $F_r$ governs tool deflection and chatter**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plain turning is the **backbone of mass-production shaft manufacturing**:
- **Automotive crankshafts** — rough and finish turning of journals.
- **Hydraulic piston rods** — precision turning to $\pm 0.01$ mm tolerance.
- **Aerospace turbine shafts** — turning of Inconel and titanium alloys under controlled $V_c$.
- **CNC turned components** — every part that has a rotational feature in a BOM has *originated* on a lathe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cutting Speed from First Principles

The cutting speed $V_c$ is defined as the **linear surface velocity** of the workpiece at the point of contact with the tool tip.

**Step 1:** The workpiece rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. Therefore, in one revolution, the surface point traces a circumference of length $\pi D$.

$$ \text{Distance per revolution} = \pi D \quad [\text{mm}] $$

**Step 2:** In one minute, the surface point completes $N$ revolutions. Therefore, the linear distance travelled in one minute is:

$$ \text{Distance per minute} = \pi D \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}] $$

**Step 3:** Convert mm/min to m/min by dividing by 1000:

$$ V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}] $$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for spindle speed:

$$ N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi D} \quad [\text{rpm}] $$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Determination of Spindle Speed, Feed Rate, and MRR

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A mild steel shaft of diameter **60 mm** is to be rough-turned on a lathe with a depth of cut of **2.5 mm** and a feed of **0.3 mm/rev**. The recommended cutting speed for the tool-workpiece combination is **$V_c$ = 30 m/min**. Calculate:
> (a) The required spindle speed $N$.
> (b) The feed rate $f_n$.
> (c) The material removal rate (MRR).
> (d) The machining time if the length of cut is **150 mm**.

### (a) Spindle Speed

$$ N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60} $$

$$ N = \frac{30000}{188.496} $$

$$ N = 159.15 \quad \text{rpm} \approx 160 \quad \text{rpm} \quad \text{(selected from machine chart)} $$

### (b) Feed Rate

$$ f_n = f \times N = 0.3 \times 160 $$

$$ f_n = 48 \quad \text{mm/min} $$

### (c) Material Removal Rate

$$ MRR = V_c \times f \times d = 30 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 $$

> **Unit Note:** Since $V_c$ is in m/min, we must convert to mm/min: $V_c = 30000$ mm/min.

$$ MRR = 30000 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 = 22500 \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 22.5 \quad \text{cm}^3/\text{min} $$

### (d) Machining Time

$$ t_m = \frac{L}{f_n} = \frac{150}{48} $$

$$ t_m = 3.125 \quad \text{min} \approx 3 \quad \text{min} \, 7.5 \quad \text{s} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the cut length is very long relative to the workpiece diameter, the diameter changes significantly during the cut. For accuracy, use the **average diameter** $D_{avg} = (D + D_f)/2$ in the $V_c$ formula. This is a common KTU exam pitfall.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Cutting Force, Power, and Specific Energy

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** During a turning operation on **EN8 steel** with $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, and $d = 2$ mm, the tangential cutting force is measured as **$F_c = 1200$ N**. Calculate:
> (a) The specific cutting energy $K_c$.
> (b) The cutting power required at the spindle $P_c$.
> (c) The motor power required assuming a mechanical efficiency of **80%**.

### (a) Specific Cutting Energy

$$ K_c = \frac{F_c}{f \cdot d} = \frac{1200}{0.25 \times 2} = \frac{1200}{0.5} $$

$$ K_c = 2400 \quad \text{N/mm}^2 $$

> This value is in the typical range for mild/medium carbon steels (1800–2800 N/mm²).

### (b) Cutting Power at Spindle

The cutting power is the rate of energy consumption at the tool tip.

$$ P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000} $$

Here, $V_c$ in m/min is multiplied by 60 to convert to m/s (since power is in Watts = N·m/s).

$$ P_c = \frac{1200 \times 25}{60000} = \frac{30000}{60000} = 0.5 \quad \text{kW} $$

> **Alternative Method (Using $f_n$):**
> $f_n = 0.25 \times N$, and $N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 60} = 132.6$ rpm,
> $f_n = 33.15$ mm/min.
> $MRR = 30000 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 15000$ mm³/min,
> $P_c = \frac{MRR \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6} = \frac{15000 \times 2400}{6 \times 10^7} = 0.6$ kW.
> (Slight difference due to diameter rounding.)

### (c) Motor Power

$$ P_{motor} = \frac{P_c}{\eta} = \frac{0.5}{0.80} $$

$$ P_{motor} = 0.625 \quad \text{kW} $$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Taylor's Tool Life Equation

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** Tool life tests on a HSS tool during turning of mild steel yield the following data:
> - $V_{c1} = 30$ m/min → $T_1 = 60$ min
> - $V_{c2} = 45$ m/min → $T_2 = 20$ min
>
> Determine: (a) Taylor's tool life equation parameters $n$ and $C$. (b) The tool life at $V_c = 40$ m/min. (c) The cutting speed for a tool life of 30 min.

### (a) Determine $n$ and $C$

Taylor's equation: $V_c \cdot T^n = C$

Take two data points:

$$ 30 \times 60^n = C \quad \text{...(i)} $$

$$ 45 \times 20^n = C \quad \text{...(ii)} $$

Dividing (i) by (ii):

$$ \frac{30 \times 60^n}{45 \times 20^n} = 1 $$

$$ \frac{30}{45} \times \left(\frac{60}{20}\right)^n = 1 $$

$$ \frac{2}{3} \times 3^n = 1 $$

$$ 3^n = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 $$

Taking natural log:

$$ n \ln 3 = \ln 1.5 $$

$$ n = \frac{\ln 1.5}{\ln 3} = \frac{0.4055}{1.0986} $$

$$ n = 0.3691 $$

Substitute in (i):

$$ C = 30 \times (60)^{0.3691} $$

$$ C = 30 \times 4.374 = 131.2 $$

Therefore, **Taylor's Equation:**

$$ V_c \cdot T^{0.369} = 131.2 $$

### (b) Tool Life at $V_c = 40$ m/min

$$ 40 \times T^{0.369} = 131.2 $$

$$ T^{0.369} = 3.28 $$

$$ T = (3.28)^{1/0.369} = (3.28)^{2.71} $$

$$ T = 26.4 \quad \text{min} $$

### (c) Cutting Speed for $T = 30$ min

$$ V_c \times (30)^{0.369} = 131.2 $$

$$ V_c = \frac{131.2}{(30)^{0.369}} = \frac{131.2}{3.674} $$

$$ V_c = 35.7 \quad \text{m/min} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always keep $n$ as an exact fraction. Round only in the final step to avoid error propagation. The value $n \approx 0.3$ to $0.4$ is typical for HSS tools; cemented carbides have $n \approx 0.2$ to $0.25$ (longer life sensitivity).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Turning Calculations

| Given | Find | Key Formula | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D, V_c$ | $N$ | $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$ | Diameter must be in **mm** |
| $N, f$ | $f_n$ | $f_n = f \cdot N$ | $f$ in mm/rev, $f_n$ in mm/min |
| $V_c, f, d$ | $MRR$ | $MRR = 1000 V_c f d$ | Multiply $V_c$ by 1000 for mm/min |
| $F_c, V_c$ | $P_c$ | $P_c = F_c V_c / 60000$ | Use m/s conversion: ×60×1000 |
| $L, f_n$ | $t_m$ | $t_m = L / f_n$ | $L$ in mm, $f_n$ in mm/min |
| Two $(V_c, T)$ pairs | $n, C$ | $\dfrac{V_{c1}}{V_{c2}} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^n$ | Inverse ratio of $T$ |
| $f, r_\epsilon$ | $R_a$ | $R_a \approx f^2 / (8 r_\epsilon)$ | Valid only for sharp tool ideal geometry |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Kinematic & Functional Architecture of Plain Turning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor AC 3-Phase] --> B[Headstock Gearbox]
    B --> C[Spindle Chuck]
    C --> D[Workpiece Rotating Job]
    D --> E[Cutting Zone Shear Plane]
    E --> F[Single-Point Cutting Tool]
    F --> G[Tool Post and Cross Slide]
    G --> H[Carriage with Feed Rod]
    H --> I[Apron and Lead Screw]
    I --> J[Feed Drive Mechanism]
    J --> K[Feed Direction X or Z]
    D --> L[Material Removal as Chip]
    L --> M[Chip Evacuation and Coolant]
    E --> N[Heat Generation Zone]
    N --> O[Coolant Supply from Tail End]
    O --> D
    F --> P[Tool Wear Monitoring]
    P --> Q[Tool Life Indicator]
    Q --> R[Operator Decision or Auto Compensate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Tool Geometry Coordinate System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Tool Reference Plane] --> B[Cutting Edge Geometry]
    B --> C[Back Rake Angle Gamma]
    B --> D[Side Rake Angle Gamma_s]
    B --> E[End Relief Angle Alpha]
    B --> F[Side Relief Angle Alpha_s]
    B --> G[End Cutting Edge Angle Eps_r]
    B --> H[Side Cutting Edge Angle Phi_s]
    B --> I[Nose Radius r_epsilon]
    C --> J[Positive Rake Soft Material]
    C --> K[Negative Rake Hard Material]
    I --> L[Surface Finish Improvement]
    I --> M[Higher Radial Force]
    M --> N[Chatter Risk]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Parameter Selection Procedure for Plain Turning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start New Turning Job] --> B[Identify Workpiece Material]
    B --> C[Select Insert Grade from Tool Catalog]
    C --> D[Choose Recommended Vc from Handbook]
    D --> E[Calculate Spindle Speed N from Vc and D]
    E --> F[Select Feed f from Roughness and MRR Need]
    F --> G[Select Depth of Cut d from Stock Allowance]
    G --> H[Check Machine Power Capacity]
    H --> I{Power OK?}
    I -->|No| J[Reduce Vc or d]
    J --> H
    I -->|Yes| K[Check Tool Deflection and Chatter]
    K --> L{Rigidity OK?}
    L -->|No| M[Reduce d and r_epsilon]
    M --> K
    L -->|Yes| N[Run First Pass and Monitor]
    N --> O{Vibration or Poor Finish?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Adjust Vc upward to avoid BUE]
    P --> N
    O -->|No| Q[Production Run Approved]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Textual Layout of the Cutting Zone

```
                          ↑ Feed direction (f, mm/rev)
                          |
                          |
                Chip →    |    ← Chip (continuous ribbon)
       ___________________|___________________
      /                                      \
     /   ◄——— Shear plane (angle φ) ———►      \
    /__________________________________________\
   |                                            |
   |          WORKPIECE (rotating, Vc)          |
   |____________________________________________|
                          |
                          ▼
                  Tool tip (single point)
            Rake face at angle γ above horizontal
            Relief face at angle α below horizontal
            Nose radius r_epsilon at the tip apex
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **shear plane angle $\phi$** is the most critical parameter in chip formation mechanics. Higher $\phi$ → thinner chips, lower force, better finish. $\phi$ increases with positive rake angle and higher cutting speed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define the term "Cutting Speed" in plain turning. State its unit and write the formula relating it to spindle speed and workpiece diameter.**

**Model Answer:**

**Cutting Speed ($V_c$):** It is the relative velocity between the workpiece surface and the cutting tool at the point of contact, measured at the *cutting edge*. It represents the **linear distance travelled by a point on the workpiece periphery per unit time**.

- **Unit:** m/min (metres per minute) — KTU convention.
- **Formula:**

$$ V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}] $$

where $D$ = workpiece diameter (mm), $N$ = spindle speed (rpm).

Cutting speed is **not** the same as feed rate; it is the speed at which material is sheared, while feed is the rate at which the tool advances per revolution.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a "Built-Up Edge" (BUE) in turning? Under what conditions does it form, and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** is a wedge of *workpiece material* that becomes welded or pressure-bonded to the **rake face of the cutting tool** near the cutting edge, due to high pressure and moderate temperature in the primary shear zone.

**Conditions favouring BUE formation:**
- **Low cutting speeds** (typically $V_c < 60$ m/min for steel).
- **Ductile workpiece materials** (e.g., low-carbon steel, pure aluminium, copper).
- **Insufficient cutting fluid** and high friction at tool-chip interface.
- **Negative or zero rake angles** (concentrate pressure on the tool tip).

**Effects of BUE:** Poor surface finish, increased cutting force, sudden tool failure when BUE breaks off.

**Prevention methods:**
- Increase cutting speed $V_c$ above the BUE threshold.
- Use tools with **positive rake angle** and **sharp cutting edges**.
- Apply **coated tools** (TiN, Al₂O₃ coatings) to reduce adhesion.
- Use **cutting fluid** with good lubricity (e.g., sulphurised oils).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q-A. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]`**
> **(a)** Explain the **kinematic chain of a centre lathe** with a neat block diagram, distinguishing clearly between **primary motion** and **secondary motion**. State the role of the **headstock, feed rod, and lead screw**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** During a plain turning operation on **mild steel (density 7.85 g/cm³)**, the following data is given:
> - Workpiece diameter $D = 80$ mm
> - Finished diameter $D_f = 72$ mm
> - Feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev
> - Depth of cut $d = 4$ mm
> - Cutting speed $V_c = 35$ m/min
> - Length of cut $L = 250$ mm
> - Specific cutting energy $K_c = 2200$ N/mm²
>
> Calculate: (i) Spindle speed $N$, (ii) Feed rate $f_n$, (iii) MRR, (iv) Cutting power $P_c$, (v) Volume and mass of chip removed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Q-A(a)

**Kinematic Chain of a Centre Lathe:**

A centre lathe has two simultaneous motion systems:

1. **Primary Motion (Cutting Motion):** Rotary motion imparted to the workpiece by the headstock spindle. Power flows: **Motor → Belt/Geared Drive → Spindle → Chuck → Workpiece.**

2. **Secondary Motion (Feed Motion):** Linear or transverse motion imparted to the tool. Power flows: **Motor → Feed Gearbox → Feed Rod (or Lead Screw) → Carriage → Cross Slide → Tool Post → Tool.**

**Role of Components:**

- **Headstock:** Houses the spindle, gear train, and chuck. Provides *rotational* primary motion at various speeds.
- **Feed Rod:** Provides *longitudinal and cross* feed motion during turning, facing, and similar operations. Driven by the feed gearbox.
- **Lead Screw:** Specifically provides *thread-cutting* feed (with pitch synchronisation) and also power feed in CNC lathes. Marked with a single groove for identification.

```
   [Motor]──┬──[Belt]──[Headstock Gearbox]──[Spindle]──[Chuck/Job]
            │
            └──[Feed Gearbox]──[Feed Rod/Lead Screw]──[Carriage]──[Tool]
```

#### Model Solution – Q-A(b)

**(i) Spindle Speed (using average diameter for accuracy):**

$$ D_{avg} = \frac{D + D_f}{2} = \frac{80 + 72}{2} = 76 \quad \text{mm} $$

$$ N = \frac{1000 \times 35}{\pi \times 76} = \frac{35000}{238.76} $$

$$ N = 146.6 \quad \text{rpm} \approx 147 \quad \text{rpm} $$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$ f_n = f \times N = 0.3 \times 147 = 44.1 \quad \text{mm/min} $$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**

$$ V_c \text{ in mm/min} = 35000 \quad \text{mm/min} $$

$$ MRR = V_c \times f \times d = 35000 \times 0.3 \times 4 = 42000 \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$

$$ MRR = 42 \quad \text{cm}^3/\text{min} = 42 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{min} $$

**(iv) Cutting Power:**

Tangential cutting force:

$$ F_c = K_c \times f \times d = 2200 \times 0.3 \times 4 = 2640 \quad \text{N} $$

$$ P_c = \frac{F_c \times V_c}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2640 \times 35}{60000} = \frac{92400}{60000} $$

$$ P_c = 1.54 \quad \text{kW} $$

**(v) Volume and Mass of Chip:**

Machining time:

$$ t_m = \frac{L}{f_n} = \frac{250}{44.1} = 5.67 \quad \text{min} $$

Volume of material removed:

$$ V_{chip} = MRR \times t_m = 42000 \times 5.67 = 238140 \quad \text{mm}^3 \approx 238.14 \quad \text{cm}^3 $$

Mass of chip:

$$ m_{chip} = V_{chip} \times \rho = 238.14 \times 7.85 = 1869.4 \quad \text{g} \approx 1.87 \quad \text{kg} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students use the *initial* diameter $D$ instead of $D_{avg}$ in the spindle speed formula, losing 1 mark. The KTU model answer key explicitly states: *"When length of cut is significant, use average diameter."* Also, the $V_c$ value in MRR must be in **mm/min**, not m/min — a common unit-conversion error worth 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q-B. `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 2]`**
> **(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **geometry of a single-point turning tool** and state the effect of **rake angle and nose radius** on cutting performance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Tool life tests on a HSS tool during turning of mild steel yield:
> - Test 1: $V_{c1} = 25$ m/min, $T_1 = 90$ min
> - Test 2: $V_{c2} = 40$ m/min, $T_2 = 25$ min
>
> Determine: (i) Taylor's tool life equation ($V_c T^n = C$), (ii) Cutting speed for a tool life of **45 minutes**, (iii) The effect of doubling the cutting speed on tool life (ratio $T_2/T_1$ if $V_c$ is doubled). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Q-B(a)

**Single-Point Turning Tool Geometry:**

The geometry is defined w.r.t. three reference planes: **rake plane (vertical, longitudinal)**, **clearance plane (perpendicular to rake)**, and **base plane (horizontal, through tool tip)**.

**Key Angles and Their Effects:**

| Angle | Effect on Performance |
|---|---|
| **Positive Rake ($\gamma > 0$)** | Reduces cutting force, lowers power, improves chip flow. Suitable for ductile materials. Tool tip weaker. |
| **Negative Rake ($\gamma < 0$)** | Strengthens cutting edge, suitable for hard materials and interrupted cuts. Higher force, more power. |
| **Larger Nose Radius ($r_\epsilon$)** | Reduces surface roughness ($R_a \propto f^2 / r_\epsilon$), strengthens tip, but increases radial force and chatter tendency. |
| **Smaller Nose Radius** | Lower radial force, but poorer finish and rapid wear at corner. |

**Labelled Diagram (Schematic):**

```
                Top (Rake) Face
                ╱
               ╱  ← Back Rake Angle (γ)
              ╱____________________
             │\                    \
             │ \  ← Side Rake (γs)  \   ← End Cutting Edge
             │  \                    \   Angle (εr)
             │   \____________________\___________
             │    │                            ╲  ← Side Cutting Edge
             │    │  Relief (α)                 ╲   Angle (φs)
             │    │  Clearance Face               \
        _____|____|__________________________________\
                  │◄— Nose Radius (r_ε) —►│
```

#### Model Solution – Q-B(b)

**(i) Taylor's Equation Parameters:**

Using $V_{c1} T_1^n = V_{c2} T_2^n$:

$$ \frac{V_{c1}}{V_{c2}} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^n $$

$$ \frac{25}{40} = \left(\frac{25}{90}\right)^n $$

$$ 0.625 = (0.2778)^n $$

Take natural log:

$$ n \ln(0.2778) = \ln(0.625) $$

$$ n = \frac{\ln(0.625)}{\ln(0.2778)} = \frac{-0.4700}{-1.2809} = 0.367 $$

Now, compute $C$:

$$ C = 25 \times (90)^{0.367} = 25 \times 5.567 = 139.2 $$

$$ \boxed{V_c \cdot T^{0.367} = 139.2} $$

**(ii) Cutting Speed for $T = 45$ min:**

$$ V_c = \frac{139.2}{(45)^{0.367}} = \frac{139.2}{3.940} = 35.3 \quad \text{m/min} $$

**(iii) Effect of Doubling $V_c$:**

If $V_{c2} = 2 V_{c1}$:

$$ 2 V_{c1} \cdot T_2^{0.367} = V_{c1} \cdot T_1^{0.367} $$

$$ \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 2^{1/0.367} = 2^{2.725} $$

$$ T_1 / T_2 = 6.61 \Rightarrow T_2 = T_1 / 6.61 $$

**Tool life reduces by a factor of ~6.6** when cutting speed is doubled. This shows the extreme sensitivity of tool life to $V_c$ — a 2× increase in speed reduces tool life by ~85%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. Students often write $V_{c1}/V_{c2} = (T_1/T_2)^n$ — this is **wrong**. The correct ratio is $V_{c1}/V_{c2} = (T_2/T_1)^n$ (inverse relation).
> 2. Forgetting to take the $n$-th root in step (iii) — they should write $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (V_{c1}/V_{c2})^{1/n}$.
> 3. Lose 1–2 marks by not specifying the **unit of $C$** (m/min · min^n).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plain Turning (PCIET402, Module 2):**

- ☐ **Primary motion** = rotation of job; **Secondary motion** = feed of tool. Two motions define turning.
- ☐ $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min). Always use **average diameter** for long cuts.
- ☐ $f_n = f \times N$ (mm/min). $f$ is mm/rev, $f_n$ is mm/min — different units.
- ☐ **MRR** = $1000 \cdot V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ mm³/min (multiply $V_c$ by 1000 to convert m → mm).
- ☐ **Specific cutting energy** $K_c$ = $F_c / (f \cdot d)$ in N/mm². Mild steel ≈ 2000–2500.
- ☐ **Cutting power** $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c / 60000$ (kW). Multiply motor $P_c$ by 1/η to get motor rating.
- ☐ **Tool life** — Taylor's equation $V_c T^n = C$, with $n$ in 0.2–0.4 range.
- ☐ **Three cutting forces** — $F_c$ (tangential, power), $F_f$ (axial, feed), $F_r$ (radial, chatter).
- ☐ **Surface finish** $R_a \approx f^2 / (8 r_\epsilon)$ — smaller feed and larger nose radius improve finish.
- ☐ **BUE** forms at low $V_c$ with ductile materials — increase $V_c$ to eliminate.
- ☐ **Tool wear** — crater (rake face) and flank (clearance face) — replace at VB = 0.3 mm.
- ☐ **Coolant** — flood for roughing (heat removal), mist for finishing (lubrication).
- ☐ **Tool material selection** — HSS for low $V_c$ / complex tools; Carbide for high $V_c$ / mass production; Ceramics / CBN for hardened steels.
- ☐ **Always** state units explicitly in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
- ☐ **Cutting speed for different tool-workpiece combinations** is given in the Machining Data Handbook (Metcut / Sandvik Coromant) — memorize the **order of magnitude** ($V_c$ for mild steel with HSS ≈ 25–35 m/min, with carbide ≈ 150–250 m/min).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Memory Trick for KTU Viva:**
> *"In turning, $V_c$ is set by the **job**, $f$ is set by the **operator**, and $d$ is set by the **engineer**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taper Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Taper Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taper Turning** is a metal cutting operation performed on a lathe in which a workpiece is machined to produce a **conical (frustum-shaped) external or internal surface** by gradually varying the diameter along the axis of rotation. It is essentially the controlled generation of an **inclined linear profile** (with respect to the axis of rotation) by moving the cutting tool at an angle to the workpiece's longitudinal axis.

A **taper** is mathematically defined as the ratio of the change in diameter (or radius) to the length over which the change occurs.

$$
\text{Taper (T)} = \frac{D - d}{L}
$$

where:
- $D$ = **Larger diameter** of the taper (in mm)
- $d$ = **Smaller diameter** of the taper (in mm)
- $L$ = **Length of the tapered portion** (in mm)

The angle that the tapered surface makes with the axis is called the **half-angle ($\alpha$)**, and the angle subtended at the apex of the cone is the **included angle ($2\alpha$)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sharpened Pencil"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Imagine sharpening a wooden pencil with a knife. The wood surface that is exposed is conical — large at the unsharpened end and small at the tip. Now imagine doing the same on a metal rod mounted in a lathe using a cutting tool. The diameter gradually reduces from one end to the other, just like the pencil. The lathe spindle rotates the rod (workpiece) just as your fingers rotate the pencil, and the tool moves linearly to "shave" the metal. The slope of the cone you produce depends on how fast you push the tool sideways relative to how fast you push it along the length.

**Other everyday analogues:**
- A **traffic cone** (large base, small top).
- A **road gradient sign** showing steepness.
- The **nose of a bullet** or **tip of a missile** — both are tapered for aerodynamic reasons.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance & Applications

Tapered surfaces are encountered everywhere in mechanical assemblies because they provide:
1. **Self-centering action** — A tapered male and female part automatically aligns axially on contact.
2. **Easy assembly/disassembly** — Push-fit / pull-fit action with controlled interference.
3. **Load distribution** — Tapered roller bearings, gear shafts.
4. **Holding force** — Taper shanks transmit torque without slippage.

**Standard Tapers used in industry (Tapering Standards):**
- **Morse Taper (MT)** — used in drill shanks, arbors, and reamers.
- **Brown & Sharpe (B&S) Taper** — used in milling machine spindles.
- **Jarno Taper** — used in some precision tools.
- **Jacobs Taper** — used in drill chucks.
- **American Standard Machine Taper** — used in machine tool spindles.
- **International Standard Taper** — for general engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Taper turning is a high-weightage topic in Module 2. Students must master **all four classical methods** of producing tapers, the associated **formulas**, and the ability to **derive setover/swivel angles** for a given taper specification.

## 1.4 Why is Taper Turning Special?

In **straight turning**, the tool moves purely parallel to the lathe axis, producing a cylinder (constant diameter). To produce a **cone**, the tool's path must trace a **straight line that is inclined** to the axis. This inclined motion can be generated in several ways, which gives rise to the different taper turning methods discussed in the next section.

The **type of taper** generated depends on the **feed direction** combined with the cross-feed:
- **Longitudinal feed only** → Cylinder.
- **Cross feed only** → Facing / Flat surface.
- **Combined longitudinal + cross feed (at an angle)** → **Taper / Cone**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of a Taper — The Foundational Relationships

A taper can be specified in **three equivalent ways**, and the student must fluently interconvert between them.

**1. By Taper (T):**
$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L}
$$
Taper is a **dimensionless ratio**. It can be expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.1), a fraction (e.g., 1:10), or **Taper Per Inch (TPI)** in inch-based systems (e.g., 0.5" per foot, or 0.5/12).

**2. By Half Angle ($\alpha$):**
$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$
$$
\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)
$$

**3. By Included Angle ($2\alpha$):**
$$
2\alpha = 2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of Half-Angle:** Imagine the right triangle formed by the axis of the taper (length $L$) and the slanted conical surface. The vertical leg (perpendicular drop from the large diameter to the axis) is $\frac{D-d}{2}$ (the radial drop), and the horizontal leg (along the axis) is $L$. The half-angle $\alpha$ is opposite to the radial drop. Therefore $\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{(D-d)/2}{L}$.

## 2.2 The Four Classical Methods of Taper Turning

### Method 1 — Form Tool Method (Wide-Tool Method)

A **flat form tool** is ground to the exact shape of the required taper. The tool is fed straight in (radial cross-feed) along a line perpendicular to the lathe axis. The conical shape is generated as the tool's cutting edge sweeps across the rotating workpiece.

- **Used for:** Short tapers (length < ~25 mm) and steep tapers.
- **Advantages:** Simple, fast, no special attachments.
- **Disadvantages:** Long form tools chatter; not suitable for long, gradual tapers; tool wear changes the profile.

**Tool width for through-taper (over-cutting allowed):**
$$
w = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

### Method 2 — Compound Slide (Swivel Slide) Method

The **compound rest** is a small tool-post mounted on the cross-slide that can be **swiveled** around a vertical axis through a graduated dial (0° to 90°). It is set to the **half-angle $\alpha$**, and then the compound slide hand-wheel is fed. The tool now moves in a straight line inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the lathe axis.

- **Used for:** Short to medium tapers, internal tapers, steep tapers.
- **Swivel angle:**
$$
\theta = \alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)
$$

### Method 3 — Tailstock Set-Over Method

The **tailstock** is bodily **shifted laterally (perpendicular to the lathe axis)** by an amount called the **setover ($s$)**. Because the workpiece is now held between centers with its axis inclined to the lathe axis, the cutting tool (fed parallel to the lathe axis) generates a taper.

**Setover formula:**
$$
s = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l}
$$
where $l$ = total length of the workpiece between centers (NOT the taper length $L$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $L$ (taper length) with $l$ (total workpiece length). They are different. If the entire workpiece is tapered, $L = l$.

**Alternative form (using half-angle, valid when setover is small):**
$$
s \approx \frac{l}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha) \quad \text{or more precisely} \quad s = \frac{l \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot L}
$$

- **Used for:** Long, gradual external tapers only (cannot produce internal tapers).
- **Disadvantage:** Reduces available swing clearance; tool may rub the tailstock end.

### Method 4 — Taper Turning Attachment Method

A **taper attachment** is a fixture mounted on the lathe bed (typically on the saddle/ways). It consists of a **guide bar (or sine bar)** that is set at the **half-angle $\alpha$**. The cross-slide is connected to this guide bar via a sliding block; as the carriage moves longitudinally, the cross-slide is automatically deflected sideways in proportion to the sine of the guide-bar angle, producing a taper.

**Guide-bar setting (taper per unit length):**
$$
\text{Set the guide bar so that} \quad \frac{\text{Cross-slide movement}}{\text{Longitudinal movement}} = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \tan(\alpha)
$$

- **Used for:** Long tapers, both internal and external.
- **Advantage:** Does not disturb tailstock alignment; high accuracy.

### Method 5 — Numerical Control (CNC) Method

In CNC lathes, the **X-axis (radial)** and **Z-axis (longitudinal)** are programmed to move in synchronization. The end point coordinates $(x, z)$ are calculated from the taper geometry, and linear interpolation (G01) produces a perfect cone.

- **Used for:** Modern flexible manufacturing; complex tapers, mass production.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it is the **single most important summary** for the KTU board exam.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Method |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Taper (T) | $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | dimensionless | All |
| 2 | Taper Per Inch (TPI) | $\text{TPI} = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ (L in inches) | in/in | All |
| 3 | Half Angle ($\alpha$) | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)$ | degrees | Compound slide & Taper attach. |
| 4 | Included Angle ($2\alpha$) | $2\alpha = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)$ | degrees | All |
| 5 | Form Tool Width ($w$) | $w = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ | mm | Form tool |
| 6 | Tailstock Setover ($s$) | $s = \dfrac{L(D - d)}{2\,l}$ | mm | Tailstock |
| 7 | Compound Slide Swivel ($\theta$) | $\theta = \alpha$ | degrees | Compound slide |
| 8 | Taper Attachment Sine Bar Length | $\sin(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | — | Taper attach. |
| 9 | Morse Taper (TPI table lookup) | Per ISO 296 / DIN 228 | in/ft | Standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The tailstock setover method is widely used in small workshops and tool-rooms to produce long, gentle tapers on shafts without expensive attachments. The taper turning attachment is a standard accessory on most precision lathes (HMT, BFW, etc.) used in Indian industries.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Tailstock Setover Formula

**Given:**
- A workpiece of total length $l$ (between centers) has a taper portion of length $L$, larger diameter $D$, smaller diameter $d$.
- Find the tailstock setover $s$ such that the lathe tool (moving parallel to the bed) produces this taper.

**Step 1 — Establish Geometry.**

The tailstock is shifted laterally by an amount $s$. This means the **workpiece axis is now inclined** to the lathe axis by a small angle. Consider the right triangle formed by:
- The **horizontal axis** of the lathe (length $l$).
- The **inclined axis** of the workpiece (length $l$, but tilted).
- The vertical drop at the tailstock end, which is $s$.

The **angle of inclination** $\beta$ is:
$$
\tan(\beta) = \frac{s}{l}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate $\beta$ to the Taper Geometry.**

Because the tool moves along the **lathe axis** (not the workpiece axis), at a longitudinal position $x$ from the headstock, the radial position of the workpiece surface (relative to the lathe axis) varies linearly.

At the **headstock end** ($x = 0$): the radial offset is $0$ (the workpiece is centered here).
At the **tailstock end** ($x = l$): the radial offset is $s$ (because the workpiece is shifted sideways by $s$).

Therefore, the radial offset of the workpiece surface at any $x$ is:
$$
r(x) = \frac{s \cdot x}{l}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the Radial Drop Over the Taper Length $L$.**

The taper starts at distance $x_1$ from the headstock and ends at $x_2 = x_1 + L$. The radial difference between the two ends of the taper is:
$$
\Delta r = r(x_2) - r(x_1) = \frac{s \cdot (x_1 + L)}{l} - \frac{s \cdot x_1}{l} = \frac{s \cdot L}{l}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate to the Taper Specification.**

By definition, the radial difference over the taper length $L$ is also:
$$
\Delta r = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

Therefore:
$$
\frac{s \cdot L}{l} = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $s$.**

$$
\boxed{\,s = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l}\,}
$$

This is the standard KTU formula. When the **entire workpiece** is tapered, then $L = l$ and the formula reduces to:
$$
s_{\text{fully tapered}} = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If $L = l$, the workpiece is a complete cone. The setover must equal the radial drop $\frac{D-d}{2}$, which is exactly the half-difference between the large and small ends. ✓

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Half-Angle Formula

**Given:** Conical surface with diameters $D$ and $d$ over length $L$.

**Step 1 — Draw the Geometry.**

Consider the longitudinal cross-section of the tapered shaft. We have a trapezoid. Drop a perpendicular from the larger-diameter end to the axis, intersecting the conical surface. This forms a right triangle with:
- Horizontal leg (along the axis) = $L$.
- Vertical leg (perpendicular to axis) = $\dfrac{D - d}{2}$ (the radial drop, since the diameter drops by $D-d$, the radius drops by half that).
- Hypotenuse = the conical surface.
- Angle at the axis (between hypotenuse and axis) = $\alpha$ (the half-angle).

**Step 2 — Apply Trigonometry.**

The angle $\alpha$ is opposite the vertical leg and adjacent to the horizontal leg.
$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{(D-d)/2}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha$.**

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "half"?** The included angle of the cone is $2\alpha$ (the full angle at the apex). $\alpha$ is half of it, measured from the axis. The lathe compound-slide is set to $\alpha$ (NOT $2\alpha$). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 3.3 Worked Example Problem (Comprehensive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A workpiece of total length **200 mm** is to have a **taper of 1 in 10** over a length of **80 mm** from one end. The **larger diameter is 50 mm**. The taper is to be produced by the **(a) tailstock setover method, and (b) compound slide method**. Find the setover and the swivel angle, respectively. Also find the smaller diameter.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
- Total length, $l = 200$ mm
- Taper length, $L = 80$ mm
- Taper ratio, $T = \dfrac{1}{10}$
- Larger diameter, $D = 50$ mm

**Step 2 — Find the smaller diameter $d$ from the taper ratio.**

The taper $T$ is defined as $\dfrac{D - d}{L}$. Therefore:
$$
\frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{1}{10}
$$
$$
D - d = \frac{L}{10} = \frac{80}{10} = 8 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
d = D - 8 = 50 - 8 = 42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Tailstock setover.**

$$
s = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l} = \frac{80 \times (50 - 42)}{2 \times 200} = \frac{80 \times 8}{400} = \frac{640}{400} = 1.6 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{s = 1.6 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compound-slide swivel angle.**

$$
\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{8}{2 \times 80}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{8}{160}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.05)
$$
$$
\alpha = 2.8624^\circ \approx 2^\circ \, 51' \, 45''
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha \approx 2.86^\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative check using TPI:** If taper is "1 in 10", it means 1 mm taper per 10 mm length. Hence $D - d = L/10 = 8$ mm, and $\tan(\alpha) = 0.05$. Consistent ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Method | Best for | Length of Taper | Internal/External | Accuracy | Tool Wear Effect |
|--------|----------|----------------|-------------------|----------|------------------|
| Form Tool | Short, steep | $\le 25$ mm | Both | Moderate | **High** (profile changes) |
| Compound Slide | Short to medium | $25 - 150$ mm | Both | **High** | Low |
| Tailstock Setover | Long, gentle | $100 - 1000$ mm | External only | Moderate | Low |
| Taper Attachment | Long, any slope | $50 - 1500$ mm | Both | **Very High** | Low |
| CNC / NC | Any, repeatable | Any | Both | Highest | Software-controlled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says "long taper" or "gradual taper", default to **Tailstock Setover** or **Taper Attachment**. When it says "short and steep" or "internal taper", default to **Compound Slide**. When it specifies the **form tool width**, it's the **Form Tool Method**.

## 3.5 Effect of Cutting Parameters on Taper Quality

The **surface finish** of a taper is governed by:
- **Feed rate ($f$):** Lower feed → better finish.
- **Spindle speed ($N$):** Higher $N$ → finer finish (within tool limits).
- **Depth of cut ($d$):** Shallow passes → better dimensional accuracy.
- **Tool geometry:** Rake angle, nose radius, and clearance must be appropriate for the workpiece material.
- **Coolant:** Reduces built-up edge and thermal distortion — critical for precision tapers.

The **machining time** for taper turning (using longitudinal feed) is:
$$
t_m = \frac{L + l_a}{f \cdot N} \text{ (minutes)}
$$
where $l_a$ = tool approach length.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Format: 2 compulsory questions, each carrying 3 marks. Total weightage in the ESE module test: 6 marks out of the full module. Answers should be **concise, definition-style** with one supporting formula or diagram.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Define taper. List any four methods of producing a taper on a lathe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Taper** is the uniform and gradual decrease in the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece along its length. Mathematically,
> $$T = \frac{D - d}{L}$$
> where $D$ is the larger diameter, $d$ is the smaller diameter, and $L$ is the length of the taper.
>
> **Four methods of taper turning:**
> 1. **Form tool method** — using a special profile tool fed radially.
> 2. **Compound slide (swivel) method** — swiveling the compound rest to the half-angle.
> 3. **Tailstock setover method** — offsetting the tailstock laterally.
> 4. **Taper turning attachment method** — using a sine-bar guide fixture on the lathe.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for listing the four methods.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]`

**Differentiate between form tool method and compound slide method of taper turning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Form Tool Method | Compound Slide Method |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| Tool shape | Special flat tool ground to taper profile | Standard turning tool |
| Feed direction | Pure cross feed (radial) | Feed along inclined direction |
| Taper length | Short, steep (< 25 mm) | Short to medium (up to ~150 mm) |
| Tool wear effect | **High** — profile changes with wear | Low — only nose wears |
| Suitability | External only usually | Both internal and external |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each method's clear description, 0.5 mark reserved for a clear distinguishing point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, every module's End Semester Examination (ESE) Part B contains at least one question with **internal choice** — meaning students answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total: 14 marks.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a) Derive the formula for tailstock setover for producing a taper on a lathe. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** A workpiece of total length $l$ held between centers has a tapered portion of length $L$, larger diameter $D$, and smaller diameter $d$. The tailstock is offset laterally by an amount $s$.

**Assumptions:**
1. The taper length $L$ is small compared to the total length $l$.
2. The setover $s$ is small (small angle approximation holds).
3. The tool moves parallel to the lathe axis (longitudinal feed only).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** When the tailstock is set over by $s$, the workpiece axis is inclined to the lathe axis. The angle of inclination $\beta$ satisfies:
$$\tan(\beta) = \frac{s}{l}$$

**Step 2:** The radial position $r(x)$ of the workpiece surface relative to the lathe axis at a distance $x$ from the headstock is:
$$r(x) = \frac{s \cdot x}{l}$$

**Step 3:** The radial difference between the two ends of the taper (over length $L$):
$$\Delta r = \frac{s \cdot L}{l}$$

**Step 4:** Equating to the taper specification:
$$\frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{s \cdot L}{l}$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $s$:
$$\boxed{\,s = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l}\,}$$

When the entire workpiece is tapered, $L = l$, hence $s = \frac{D-d}{2}$.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for stating assumptions, 1 mark for geometry setup, 3 marks for the algebraic derivation, 1 mark for solving, 1 mark for the special case and final boxed answer.)*

---

**(b) A shaft of total length 300 mm is to be tapered over a length of 120 mm. The larger diameter is 60 mm and the taper ratio is 1:15. Calculate: (i) the smaller diameter, (ii) the half-angle of the taper, and (iii) the tailstock setover. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $l = 300$ mm, $L = 120$ mm, $D = 60$ mm, $T = 1/15$.

**(i) Smaller diameter $d$:**

$$D - d = L \cdot T = 120 \times \frac{1}{15} = 8 \text{ mm}$$
$$d = D - 8 = 60 - 8 = 52 \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{d = 52 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Half-angle $\alpha$:**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{8}{2 \times 120} = \frac{8}{240} = 0.03333$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.03333) = 1.909^\circ \approx 1^\circ \, 54' \, 34''$$
$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 1.91^\circ}$$

**(iii) Tailstock setover $s$:**

$$s = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l} = \frac{120 \times 8}{2 \times 300} = \frac{960}{600} = 1.6 \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{s = 1.6 \text{ mm}}$$

*(Mark distribution: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2.5 marks, (iii) 2.5 marks.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the compound slide method of taper turning. Why is the compound rest set to the half-angle and not the full included angle? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (described in words for textual answer):**
The lathe bed carries the headstock and tailstock. The cross-slide is mounted on the carriage. On top of the cross-slide is the **compound rest** — a circular swivel base with a graduated dial (0° to 90° in both directions). The tool post sits on the compound rest. The compound rest is rotated by an angle $\theta$ about a vertical pivot, measured from the longitudinal axis. The tool is then fed using the compound-rest hand wheel; the tool moves in a straight line at angle $\theta$ to the lathe axis, generating a taper.

**Working principle:**
1. The compound rest is unlocked from the cross-slide.
2. It is swivelled through the **half-angle $\alpha$** on the graduated dial.
3. It is locked in position.
4. The tool is set on the work center.
5. The compound-rest hand wheel is rotated; the tool moves in a direction inclined at $\alpha$ to the axis.
6. Each pass of the tool removes metal along a line at angle $\alpha$, producing a cone.

**Why half-angle, not full included angle?**
The full included angle $2\alpha$ is the angle at the apex of the cone. The **tool path must be inclined at the half-angle $\alpha$** because the half-angle is measured from the axis to the cone surface. If the tool moves at $2\alpha$ to the axis, the resulting surface will be a steeper cone, and the diameter drop will be twice as much. KTU boards specifically test this concept.

**Mathematical justification:**
$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{D - d}{2L} \implies \text{tool feed direction must be at } \alpha$$

**Suitability:** Internal and external tapers up to about 150 mm length. Cannot produce long tapers due to carriage travel limitations.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for sketch/description, 2 marks for working steps, 1.5 marks for the half-angle justification, 1.5 marks for suitability and limitations.)*

---

**(b) A workpiece 250 mm long has a taper of 0.8 mm per cm over a 100 mm length from the headstock end. The larger diameter is 45 mm. Determine: (i) the smaller diameter, (ii) the angle the taper surface makes with the axis, and (iii) the form-tool width if the form tool method is used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $l = 250$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $D = 45$ mm, taper = 0.8 mm/cm = 0.08 mm/mm.

**(i) Smaller diameter $d$:**

$$D - d = L \times T = 100 \times 0.08 = 8 \text{ mm}$$
$$d = 45 - 8 = 37 \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{d = 37 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Angle with the axis (= half-angle $\alpha$):**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{8}{2 \times 100} = 0.04$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.04) = 2.2906^\circ \approx 2^\circ \, 17' \, 26''$$
$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 2.29^\circ}$$

**(iii) Form-tool width $w$:**

For a through-taper (single-pass form tool), the tool width equals the radial drop:
$$w = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ mm}$$
$$\boxed{w = 4 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The form tool width is **half** of the diameter difference, NOT the full difference. This is because the tool cuts radially — the diameter changes twice the radial cut.

*(Mark distribution: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2.5 marks with explicit $\tan^{-1}$ evaluation, (iii) 2.5 marks with the explanation of why we take half.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Taper Turning questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L$ and $l$** — In the setover formula, $L$ is the **taper length** and $l$ is the **total workpiece length**. Writing $s = \frac{L(D-d)}{2L}$ when only a portion is tapered is the **most common** mistake. **Penalty: full 7 marks lost on that sub-part.**
>
> 2. **Setting compound slide to $2\alpha$ instead of $\alpha$** — Examiners set a "trap" by asking for the swivel angle. Students who write $2\alpha$ lose 1–2 marks. **Always state "the compound rest is set to the HALF-angle $\alpha$".**
>
> 3. **Form tool width = $D-d$** — WRONG. The correct width is $(D-d)/2$ because radial feed changes the radius, and diameter changes by $2\times$ the radial feed. **Penalty: 1.5 marks.**
>
> 4. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Always write **mm** for length, **degrees (or degrees-minutes-seconds)** for angles. **Penalty: 0.5 mark per sub-part.**
>
> 5. **Skipping the assumption list in derivations** — A "Derive the formula" question carries 1 mark for explicitly stating the assumptions. Many students skip this.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the special case $L=l$** — When the entire workpiece is tapered, the formula simplifies. Examiners love testing this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this on your study wall before the exam!**

- **Taper definition:** $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ — dimensionless, expressed as ratio, fraction, or TPI.
- **Half-angle:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D-d}{2L}\right)$ — measured from the **axis** (not from the surface).
- **Included angle:** $2\alpha = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D-d}{2L}\right)$ — full apex angle of the cone.
- **Form tool width:** $w = \dfrac{D-d}{2}$ — only HALF the diameter difference.
- **Tailstock setover:** $s = \dfrac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2 \cdot l}$ — uses **total** workpiece length $l$ in denominator.
- **Compound slide angle:** $\theta = \alpha$ (NOT $2\alpha$ — this is a board-exam trap).
- **Standard tapers:** Morse, Brown & Sharpe, Jarno, Jacobs — memorize the order of taper-per-foot for at least Morse (e.g., MT-1, MT-2 ...).
- **Method selection:** Short-steep → Form tool; Short-medium → Compound slide; Long-gentle-external → Tailstock setover; Long-both → Taper attachment.
- **Tailstock setover limitation:** Only **external** tapers, not internal.
- **Taper attachment advantage:** Works for both internal AND external tapers; high accuracy.
- **CNC method:** Linear interpolation G01 with synchronized X-Z movement — modern industry standard.
- **Tool wear effect:** Most pronounced in form tool method; least in compound slide and taper attachment.
- **Quality control:** Use a **sine bar**, **taper gauge**, **vernier protractor**, or **roller-based measurement** to verify the produced taper.
- **Process parameters:** Lower feed + higher speed → better surface finish; multiple shallow passes → better dimensional accuracy.
- **Formula consistency check:** If $L = l$ (fully tapered workpiece), $s = \dfrac{D-d}{2}$ — useful sanity test.
- **Units check:** Lengths in mm, angles in degrees (or DMS), TPI in in/ft.
- **Common industrial tapers:** Morse taper for drill shanks; Jacobs taper for drill chucks; B&S for milling spindles; ISO/International for general engineering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread Cutting and Facing Operations. Turning Centres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thread Cutting and Facing Operations. Turning Centres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thread Cutting

**Thread cutting** is a precision turning operation performed on a lathe (or turning centre) in which a single-point cutting tool, fed longitudinally in synchronism with the spindle rotation, removes material in the form of a continuous helical groove to produce external or internal threads on a cylindrical workpiece.

**Formal Terminology (KTU 2024 aligned):**
- **Pitch (P):** The axial distance between two adjacent thread crests, measured parallel to the axis.
- **Lead (L):** The axial advance of the thread per spindle revolution. For a single-start thread, **Lead = Pitch**.
- **Helix Angle (α):** The angle the thread helix makes with the axis of the cylinder.
- **Thread Depth (h):** The radial distance from crest to root of the thread.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Thread cutting differs from ordinary turning because the tool feed is **NOT controlled by the operator's hand** but by the **lead screw and half-nut mechanism** (on conventional lathes) or by the **CNC servo loop (on turning centres)**. This synchronization is the heart of thread cutting.

## 1.2 Facing Operation

**Facing** is a turning operation used to produce a flat surface on the end of a rotating workpiece, the surface being generated perpendicular (or at a specified angle) to the axis of rotation. The cutting tool is fed radially inward (or outward) while the workpiece rotates.

## 1.3 Turning Centre

A **Turning Centre** is a computer numerically controlled (CNC) lathe enhanced with additional features such as live tooling, sub-spindles, multi-axis capability (X, Z, C, Y, B), automatic tool changers, and an integrated CNC controller that enables complete machining of complex turned parts in a single setup.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Thread Cutting as a "Cork Screw":** Imagine a carpenter's auger boring into wood. The rotating shavings follow a helical path because the tool advances by exactly one "step" for every revolution. Thread cutting works identically — the tool advances by **one pitch** for every revolution of the workpiece, cutting a perfect helix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Facing as "Peeling an Apple":** Hold an apple on a stick and rotate it. Move a knife steadily from the outer skin toward the core. The flat surface produced is a facing cut. The "feed" is the rate at which the knife moves inward; the "spindle speed" is how fast the apple spins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Turning Centre as a "Swiss Army Knife for Lathes":** A plain lathe can only turn. A turning centre, however, can turn, drill, mill, tap, and perform secondary operations all on one machine — like a Swiss Army knife condensing many tools into one.

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Standard thread included angle: 60°** (ISO metric, UNC, UNF — Unified National).
- **Standard pitch for ACME thread: included angle 29°.**
- **Whitworth (BSW) included angle: 55°.**
- **Truncation factor for ISO metric thread: 0.75** (i.e., actual depth = 0.75 × height of fundamental triangle).
- **Standard cutting speeds for thread cutting: Vc = 15–30 m/min (low-carbon steel)**; **Vc = 8–15 m/min (hardened steel / stainless)**.
- **Standard cutting speed for facing (mild steel): Vc ≈ 100–180 m/min.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thread Geometry — The Five Diameters of a V-Thread

For an external ISO metric V-thread, the geometry is defined by the following parameters:

| Symbol | Parameter | Geometric Meaning |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| D | Major Diameter | Diameter at the thread crest (nominal size, e.g., M20 → 20 mm) |
| D1 | Minor Diameter | Diameter at the thread root |
| D2 | Pitch Diameter | Diameter at which thread thickness equals the gap (mid-flank) |
| P | Pitch | Axial distance between adjacent crests |
| h | Thread Depth | Radial distance from crest to root |

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. **Major diameter D** is the nominal size of the bolt/screw.
2. **Minor diameter D1** lies below the major diameter by a depth of **2h**:
   D1 = D − 2h
3. **Pitch diameter D2** lies midway:
   D2 = D − h
4. **Thread depth h** is calculated from the pitch using the geometry of the 60° V-profile (see Section 3 for derivation).

## 2.2 Principle of Thread Cutting on a Lathe — The 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why?** To produce accurate, repeatable threads that mesh correctly with mating parts.
- **How?**
  1. The workpiece is held in a chuck and rotated at cutting speed N (rev/min).
  2. The threading tool, ground to the correct profile (60° for metric), is set exactly at the centerline height.
  3. The **half-nut lever is engaged** to connect the carriage to the lead screw.
  4. As the lead screw rotates (driven by the spindle through a gear train), the carriage moves longitudinally at a rate equal to the **pitch per spindle revolution**.
  5. Multiple passes are taken, each increasing the depth of cut gradually, until the full thread profile is obtained.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Synchronization Rule:** The longitudinal feed per spindle revolution **MUST equal exactly one pitch**. Any mismatch causes "thread drift" — a thread that runs out or crosses itself over its length.

## 2.3 The Gear-Train Equation (Conventional Lathe)

For a conventional (non-CNC) lathe, the lead screw must rotate at a specific ratio to the spindle:

$$i_{gear} = \frac{N_{leadscrew}}{N_{spindle}} = \frac{L_{job}}{L_{leadscrew}} = \frac{P_{job}}{P_{leadscrew}}$$

Where:
- L_job = lead of the thread to be cut (equal to pitch for single-start)
- L_leadscrew = lead of the lathe lead screw (a fixed value, e.g., 6 mm)

For a multi-start thread with **n** starts: L_job = n × P_job.

## 2.4 Facing Operation — The Underlying Mechanics

Facing is essentially turning in the radial direction:

- The tool moves **perpendicular to the workpiece axis** (X-direction on a CNC).
- Feed per revolution **f_r** is in mm/rev (radial).
- Spindle speed N = 1000 × Vc / (π × D).
- The face is generated by the combined rotation of the workpiece and the radial feed.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the EXAM READY formula bank for Module 2 topics. Memorize these before attempting any numerical.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $h = 0.6495 \times P$ | Thread depth (ISO metric V-thread, 60°) | mm |
| 2 | $D_1 = D - 2h$ | Minor diameter | mm |
| 3 | $D_2 = D - h$ | Pitch diameter | mm |
| 4 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{L}{\pi \times D_2}$ | Helix angle of thread | degrees |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$ | Spindle speed (cutting speed equation) | rev/min |
| 6 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| 7 | $f_{thread} = P \times N$ | Thread cutting feed rate (linear) | mm/min |
| 8 | $f_{face} = f_r \times N$ | Facing feed rate (radial) | mm/min |
| 9 | $i_{gear} = \dfrac{P_{job}}{P_{leadscrew}}$ | Change gear ratio (conventional lathe) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $t_m = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{f \times N}$ | Turning machining time (with approach & overtravel) | min |
| 11 | $t_{face} = \dfrac{(D_o - D_f) / 2}{f_r \times N}$ | Facing machining time | min |
| 12 | $H = \dfrac{P}{2} \times \sqrt{3} \approx 0.866 \times P$ | Height of fundamental V-triangle (sharp V) | mm |
| 13 | $L_{job} = n \times P$ | Lead for multi-start thread | mm |
| 14 | $MRR = V_c \times f \times a_p$ | Material removal rate (facing & turning) | mm³/min |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thread cutting** is foundational to fasteners (bolts, nuts, screws), lead screws in CNC machines, and pipe joints. In high-precision industries (aerospace, medical implants), thread accuracy directly dictates fatigue life and safety.
- **Facing** is the first operation performed on raw stock — it establishes a flat reference datum from which all length measurements are taken (the **datum face**).
