> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In a KTU exam, when a question asks for the "advantage of true 5-axis over 3 + 2," the model answer is: *"True 5-axis machining enables the cutting tool to maintain an optimal lead and tilt angle throughout the toolpath, eliminating cusps, reducing manual repositioning, and producing a single-setup sculptured surface with superior finish."*

---

## 2.3 Tool Orientation Vector and Coordinate Transform

In true 5-axis machining, the **tool orientation vector** $\vec{T}$ is described by two angles relative to the spindle axis:

$$\vec{T} = \left[ \sin(\theta)\cos(\phi),\ \sin(\theta)\sin(\phi),\ \cos(\theta) \right]$$

where:
- $\theta$ = **tilt angle** measured from the spindle Z-axis (typically the B-axis rotation)
- $\phi$ = **swivel angle** measured in the X-Y plane (typically the C-axis rotation)

The **workpiece coordinate system (WCS)** and the **machine coordinate system (MCS)** are linked by a **4 × 4 homogeneous transformation matrix** that must be recomputed at every toolpath interpolation node. This is the mathematical heart of CAM-generated G-code for 5-axis machines.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Multi-Axis Milling

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | DOF = 3L + 2R (5-axis) or 3L + 3R (6-axis) | L = linear, R = rotational |
| Tool tilt angle | $\theta_T \in [0°, 90°]$ | Angle between tool axis and spindle Z |
| Lead angle | $\phi_L \in [-180°, 180°]$ | Angle of cutting-edge direction in plane |
| Effective feed rate | $f_{eff} = f_{prog} \cdot \cos(\theta_T)$ | Reduced feed at high tilt angles |
| Cutting time reduction | $\Delta t = 1 - \dfrac{N_{setup,3}}{N_{setup,5}}$ | Setup elimination benefit |
| Reach constraint | $L_{tool} \leq R_{reach} - R_{workpiece}$ | Tool length must clear rotary table |

---

## 2.5 The Shaper — Kinematic Architecture

The shaper's reciprocating motion is generated by a **quick-return mechanism**. The two most commonly tested mechanisms in KTU are:

### A. Crank and Slotted Lever (Most Common — Crank-Shaper)
- **Crank** rotates uniformly about a fixed center **O**.
- **Slotted lever** pivots about a fixed fulcrum **C** and slides on a pin **A** fixed to the crank.
- **Ram** is connected to the slotted lever via a connecting block (block slides in the slot).
- As the crank rotates, the slot oscillates, driving the ram in linear reciprocation.

### B. Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism
- Uses a **large driving disc** rotating about its center, with a **small crank pin** that drives a slotted lever.
- Provides a **higher quick-return ratio** than crank-and-slotted-lever, suitable for heavy-duty shaping.

### C. Hydraulic Shaper
- Cutting and return strokes are powered by **hydraulic fluid** under servo control.
- Provides **infinitely variable stroke length, position, and speed**.
- Modern CNC shapers are predominantly hydraulic.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Shaper Kinematics

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quick Return Ratio | $QRR = \dfrac{\theta_c}{\theta_r} = \dfrac{360° - \alpha}{\alpha}$ where $\alpha$ = return angle | dimensionless | Always > 1 |
| Cutting stroke time | $t_c = \dfrac{\theta_c}{6 N}$ | seconds | N = crank RPM |
| Return stroke time | $t_r = \dfrac{\theta_r}{6 N}$ | seconds | |
| Mean cutting velocity | $V_c = \dfrac{2 L_s N (1 + K)}{60}$ | m/min | $L_s$ = stroke length, $K$ = factor |
| Stroke length (approx.) | $L_s \approx 2R$ for crank-shaper | mm | R = crank radius |
| Hydraulic shaper cutting speed | $V_c = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/min | Q = flow, A = piston area |
| Feed per stroke | $f = $ table feed × number of strokes | mm/stroke | |
| Material removal rate | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | d = depth of cut |
| Cutting power | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_s}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | $K_s$ = specific cutting energy |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Quick Return Ratio for Crank and Slotted Lever Shaper

### Setup and Geometry

Consider the crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism:

- Fixed pivot **O** of the crank and fixed pivot **C** of the slotted lever are separated by distance **L**.
- The crank rotates with uniform angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s about **O**.
- The crank pin **A** is at radius **r** from **O**.
- The slotted lever rotates about **C**, and the ram is connected to the lever such that the slot angle drives linear reciprocation.

Let $\theta$ be the angle of the crank measured from the line OC.

The horizontal position of the ram (tool) from the fixed pivot C is:

$$x(\theta) = L + r\cos(\theta)$$

> **Step 1 — Identify the two dead-center positions of the crank.**
>
> The ram reaches its **extreme forward position** when the crank pin A lies along the line OC on the far side of O. This occurs at $\theta = 180°$.
>
> The ram reaches its **extreme return position** when the crank pin A lies along the line OC on the near side of O. This occurs at $\theta = 0°$.
>
> Between these two extremes, the ram sweeps through the full stroke.

> **Step 2 — Compute the cutting stroke angular sweep.**
>
> The cutting stroke occupies the larger angular interval because the slot angle of the lever is smaller during cutting. The cutting stroke angle (measured at the crank) is $\theta_c = 180° + 2\phi_{max}$, where $\phi_{max}$ is the maximum slot angle from vertical.
>
> From geometry:
>
> $$\sin(\phi_{max}) = \dfrac{r}{L}$$
>
> So:
>
> $$\phi_{max} = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{L}\right)$$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$\theta_c = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{L}\right)$$

> **Step 3 — Compute the return stroke angular sweep.**
>
> $$\theta_r = 360° - \theta_c = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{L}\right)$$

> **Step 4 — Define and simplify the Quick Return Ratio.**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> QRR &= \dfrac{\theta_c}{\theta_r} \\[6pt]
>     &= \dfrac{180° + 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)}{180° - 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**
>
> If $r = 50$ mm and $L = 250$ mm, then $\sin^{-1}(r/L) = \sin^{-1}(0.2) = 11.54°$.
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \theta_c &= 180° + 2(11.54°) = 203.08° \\[4pt]
> \theta_r &= 180° - 2(11.54°) = 156.92° \\[4pt]
> QRR &= \dfrac{203.08}{156.92} \approx 1.294
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> This lies within the typical industrial range of **1.2 to 2.0** for crank-and-slotted-lever shapers, confirming the validity of the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Mean Cutting Velocity of the Ram

The ram velocity is **not constant** during the cutting stroke. The instantaneous velocity of the ram is derived as the time derivative of the position function.

> **Step 1 — Express ram position as a function of crank angle.**
>
> $$x(\theta) = L + r\cos(\theta)$$
>
> where $\theta$ is the crank angle measured from the line OC.

> **Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain instantaneous velocity.**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> v(\theta) &= \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{dx}{d\theta} \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \\[6pt]
>          &= -r\sin(\theta) \cdot \omega
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Determine the maximum velocity.**
>
> The maximum occurs at $\theta = 90°$ and $\theta = 270°$:
>
> $$v_{max} = r\omega$$

> **Step 4 — Determine the mean cutting velocity over the cutting stroke.**
>
> The mean velocity is the total stroke distance divided by the cutting time:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> V_{mean} &= \dfrac{\text{Stroke length}}{\text{Cutting time}} \\[6pt]
>          &= \dfrac{2r}{\theta_c / \omega} = \dfrac{2r\omega}{\theta_c \ (\text{in radians})}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 — Express in practical engineering units.**
>
> Letting $N$ be the crank RPM (so $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$) and $L_s = 2r$ the stroke length:
>
> $$V_{mean} = \dfrac{L_s \cdot N}{30 \cdot (\theta_c / 360°)} = \dfrac{12 \, L_s \, N}{\theta_c \ \text{(in degrees)}}\ \text{m/min if } L_s \text{ in m}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — 6-DOF Tool Orientation in Robotic 6-Axis Milling

A 6-axis robotic milling cell provides **six revolute joints** plus an external linear track sometimes counted separately. The tool orientation is governed by the **forward kinematics** of the robot.

> **Step 1 — Define the joint variables.**
>
> Let $q = [q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6]^T$ be the vector of six joint angles.

> **Step 2 — Use Denavit-Hartenberg parameters.**
>
> Each link $i$ has DH parameters $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$. The transformation from joint $i-1$ to joint $i$ is:
>
> $$T_i^{i-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Step 3 — Compose to obtain the end-effector pose.**
>
> $$T_6^0(q) = T_1^0 \cdot T_2^1 \cdot T_3^2 \cdot T_4^3 \cdot T_5^4 \cdot T_6^5$$
>
> The tool tip position is the **fourth column** of this matrix; the tool orientation is the **upper-left 3 × 3 rotation submatrix**.

> **Step 4 — Solve the inverse kinematics for the milling toolpath.**
>
> Given a desired toolpath point $P_d = [x_d, y_d, z_d, \phi_d, \theta_d, \psi_d]^T$, the inverse kinematics solver computes:
>
> $$q = IK^{-1}(P_d)$$
>
> This must be solved at every interpolation node of the toolpath, which is computationally intensive — explaining why 6-axis robotic milling is restricted to **roughing and semi-finishing**, while finish machining remains the domain of dedicated 5-axis CNC centers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 5-Axis Machining Center

<!-- Mermaid block diagram for 5-axis machine architecture -->

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GCode[G Code Program with X Y Z A C Commands] --> PostProc[Post Processor]
    PostProc --> CNC[CNC Kernel Real Time Interpolator]
    CNC --> DriveX[X Axis Servo Drive]
    CNC --> DriveY[Y Axis Servo Drive]
    CNC --> DriveZ[Z Axis Servo Drive]
    CNC --> DriveA[A Axis Servo Drive Rotary]
    CNC --> DriveC[C Axis Servo Drive Rotary]
    DriveX --> BallX[Ball Screw X]
    DriveY --> BallY[Ball Screw Y]
    DriveZ --> BallZ[Ball Screw Z]
    DriveA --> Tilt[Tilt Table A Axis]
    DriveC --> Rot[Rotary Table C Axis]
    BallX --> Tool[Spindle and Tool Assembly]
    BallY --> Tool
    BallZ --> Tool
    Tilt --> WP[Workpiece]
    Rot --> WP
    Tool --> Chip[Chip Formation on Sculptured Surface]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Shaper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Motor[Electric Motor] --> Gear[Gearbox Speed Reduction]
    Gear --> Crank[Crank OA Radius r]
    Crank --> Pin[Crank Pin A]
    Pin --> Slot[Slotted Lever Pivots at C Length L]
    Slot --> Block[Sliding Block in Slot]
    Block --> RamConn[Connecting Link to Ram]
    RamConn --> Ram[Ram Carries Tool]
    Ram --> Cut[Linear Cutting Stroke on Workpiece]
    Slot --> Back[Return Stroke Backward Motion]
    Cut --> Feedback[Position Feedback to Stroke Adjuster]
    Back --> Feedback
    Feedback --> Slot
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow — 3+2 Versus True 5-Axis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[User Defines Part Geometry] --> Type{Operation Type}
    Type -->|Holes on Inclined Plane| Pos[3 Plus 2 Positional]
    Type -->|Sculptured Surface| True[True 5 Axis Simultaneous]
    Pos --> Lock[Rotary Axes Clamped During Cut]
    Lock --> Treat[Treat as Multiple 3 Axis Operations]
    True --> Sim[All Five Axes Interpolate Continuously]
    Sim --> Lead[Maintain Constant Lead and Tilt Angle]
    Treat --> NoLead[Lead Angle Varies with Local Surface Normal]
    Sim --> Better[Superior Surface Finish Reduced Cycle Time]
    Treat --> Accept[Acceptable Finish Higher Cycle Time]
```

## 4.4 Kinematic Block Diagram of a 6-Axis Robotic Milling Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base[Base Joint J1 Yaw] --> S1[Shoulder J2 Pitch]
    S1 --> E1[Elbow J3 Pitch]
    E1 --> W1[Wrist J4 Roll]
    W1 --> W2[Wrist J5 Pitch]
    W2 --> W6[Wrist J6 Yaw]
    W6 --> Spin[High Speed Spindle]
    Spin --> Cut[End Mill Engages Workpiece]
    Cut --> FK[Forward Kinematics Sensor]
    FK --> Ctrl[Controller Solves IK for Next Toolpath Node]
    Ctrl --> W6
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Distinguish between 3 + 2 (positional) and true (simultaneous) 5-axis machining. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 3 + 2 (Positional) | True 5-Axis (Simultaneous) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotary axis behavior | Locked during cutting, repositioned between cuts | All axes move simultaneously under continuous interpolation |
| Tool orientation | Fixed for each setup segment | Continuously variable along the toolpath |
| Setup count | Multiple discrete setups | Single setup for complex sculptured surfaces |
| Programming complexity | Low | High (requires CAM and validated post-processor) |
| Typical use | Drilling on inclined planes, simple pocketing | Turbine blades, impellers, blisks, medical implants |

In **3 + 2 mode**, the machine behaves like a 3-axis machine at each indexed orientation; in **true 5-axis mode**, the controller interpolates all five axes (X, Y, Z, A, C) at every block of the NC program, enabling the tool to maintain a constant lead and tilt angle against a sculptured surface.

---

### Question 2: Define quick return ratio (QRR) as applied to a shaping machine and state its typical range. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Quick Return Ratio (QRR)** of a shaping machine is defined as the ratio of the **time taken for the cutting stroke** to the **time taken for the return stroke**, since the crank rotates at constant angular velocity:

$$QRR = \dfrac{t_{cutting}}{t_{return}} = \dfrac{\theta_{cutting}}{\theta_{return}}$$

For a crank-and-slotted-lever shaper, the cutting stroke corresponds to an angular sweep $\theta_c = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)$ and the return stroke corresponds to $\theta_r = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)$, where $r$ is the crank radius and $L$ is the distance between the crank center and the slotted-lever pivot.

The **typical industrial range is 1.5 to 3.0**; higher QRR values are achievable with Whitworth quick-return mechanisms (up to 5.0).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): Multi-Axis Milling Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

Explain with suitable diagrams the **three principal kinematic configurations** of 5-axis CNC machining centers. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of each, citing typical industrial applications.

**Model Solution:**

The three principal kinematic configurations are:

**1. Trunnion (Table-Table) Configuration**
- The workpiece is mounted on a **trunnion table** that tilts (rotates about the A-axis) and rotates (about the C-axis).
- *Merits:* Highest rigidity; can handle very heavy workpieces; excellent for jet engine casings.
- *Demerits:* The trunnion swing diameter physically limits the size of the workpiece.

**2. Head-Head Configuration**
- Both rotational axes (typically A and C) are built into the **spindle head assembly**.
- *Merits:* Large, flat table accommodates oversized workpieces (e.g., wing skins, ship propellers).
- *Demerits:* Mass at the spindle head limits feed rates and dynamic accuracy.

**3. Head-Table Configuration (Most Common)**
- A-axis is mounted in the **spindle head**; C-axis is on the **rotary table**.
- *Merits:* Balanced compromise — moderate rigidity, moderate workspace.
- *Demerits:* Slightly more complex kinematics and post-processing.

Typical applications: trunnion for aero-engine blisks; head-head for marine propellers; head-table for medical implants and turbine blades.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

A 5-axis machine is machining a **turbine blade** whose surface normal varies from 0° to 60° from the spindle axis. The programmed feed rate is $f_{prog} = 800$ mm/min at 0° tilt. Determine the **effective feed rate** when the tool is tilted at 45°, and discuss the implication for surface finish.

**Model Solution:**

The effective feed rate at a tilt angle $\theta_T$ is:

$$f_{eff} = f_{prog} \cdot \cos(\theta_T)$$

Substituting $f_{prog} = 800$ mm/min and $\theta_T = 45°$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{eff} &= 800 \cdot \cos(45°) \\[4pt]
        &= 800 \cdot 0.7071 \\[4pt]
        &= 565.7 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Implication for surface finish:** A lower effective feed rate at high tilt angles reduces the **chip load per tooth**, which:
- Improves surface finish (lower $R_a$).
- Reduces tool wear due to lower mechanical and thermal load.
- Increases cycle time — hence 5-axis programming optimizes the tilt angle to balance these factors, often selecting $20° \leq \theta_T \leq 45°$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Shaper Kinematics and Quick Return Mechanism

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a crank-and-slotted-lever quick return mechanism** used in a shaping machine. Derive the expression for the **quick return ratio** in terms of the crank radius and the slotted lever length.

**Model Solution:**

The crank-and-slotted-lever mechanism consists of:
- A crank OA of radius $r$ rotating uniformly about fixed center O.
- A slotted lever pivoted at fixed fulcrum C, with OC = $L$.
- A crank pin A attached to OA and sliding inside the slot of the lever.
- A ram connected to the lever (through a block sliding in the slot) that converts the oscillating motion into linear reciprocation.

**Working principle:** As the crank rotates one full revolution (360°), the slotted lever oscillates once about C. The ram moves forward (cutting stroke) and backward (return stroke). Because the slot angle is larger on one side of the fulcrum than the other, the angular sweep during cutting exceeds 180° while the return sweep is less than 180°. Since the crank rotates at constant angular velocity, more time is spent on the cutting stroke.

**Derivation of QRR:**

The maximum slot angle from the vertical occurs at the extreme crank position. From geometry, this maximum slot angle satisfies:

$$\sin(\phi_{max}) = \dfrac{r}{L}$$

Hence the **cutting stroke angular sweep** (the slower, longer sweep) is:

$$\theta_c = 180° + 2\phi_{max} = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{L}\right)$$

And the **return stroke angular sweep** is:

$$\theta_r = 360° - \theta_c = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{r}{L}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$QRR = \dfrac{\theta_c}{\theta_r} = \dfrac{180° + 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)}{180° - 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

A shaping machine has a **crank radius of 75 mm** and the **slotted lever length is 400 mm**. The crank rotates at **30 RPM**. Calculate:
1. The quick return ratio.
2. The time taken for one cutting stroke.
3. The mean cutting velocity if the stroke length is 150 mm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute QRR.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{max} &= \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{75}{400}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.1875) = 10.81° \\[6pt]
\theta_c &= 180° + 2(10.81°) = 201.62° \\[6pt]
\theta_r &= 180° - 2(10.81°) = 158.38° \\[6pt]
QRR &= \dfrac{201.62°}{158.38°} = 1.273
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cutting time.**

The crank completes 360° in $60/30 = 2$ seconds. The cutting time is:

$$t_c = 2 \times \dfrac{201.62°}{360°} = 2 \times 0.5601 = 1.120 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute mean cutting velocity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{mean} &= \dfrac{\text{Stroke length}}{\text{Cutting time}} \\[6pt]
         &= \dfrac{0.150 \text{ m}}{1.120 \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
         &= 0.134 \text{ m/s} = 8.04 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert crank radius and slotted lever length to the **same unit** (mm or m) before computing $\phi_{max}$.
> 2. **Degrees vs radians:** The cutting angle $\theta_c$ is in degrees in the QRR formula; do not blindly substitute into $V = r\omega$ unless converted.
> 3. **Stroke length:** In a crank-and-slotted-lever shaper, the stroke length is approximately $2r$ (when the slotted lever is long relative to $r$) — students often mistakenly take it as the **full swing of the ram**, which depends on lever geometry. Always verify from the problem statement.
> 4. **Forgetting the cutting direction:** Many students assume the return stroke is the longer sweep — it is NOT. The **cutting stroke is always the longer angular sweep** because more time is required for chip formation.
> 5. **5-axis post-processor:** Students often confuse the A-axis and C-axis orientation; remember A is rotation about X, C is about Z.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **5-axis machine** = 3 linear (X, Y, Z) + 2 rotational (A, B or C) axes.
- **6-axis machine** = Adds a third rotational axis — typically a robotic arm for large workpieces.
- **3 + 2 (positional)** machining has rotary axes LOCKED during cutting; **true 5-axis** has them interpolating continuously.
- **A-axis** = rotation about X; **B-axis** = rotation about Y; **C-axis** = rotation about Z.
- **Tool tilt angle** reduces effective feed rate: $f_{eff} = f_{prog} \cdot \cos(\theta_T)$.
- **Trunnion configuration** is most rigid; **head-head** allows largest workpieces; **head-table** is the industry workhorse.
- A **shaper** is a reciprocating single-point cutting machine; cutting and return strokes have **different angular sweeps** but **same crank RPM**, producing the **quick return**.
- **QRR = $\theta_c / \theta_r$** is always **greater than 1**; typical range **1.2 to 3.0**.
- For crank-and-slotted-lever: $\theta_c = 180° + 2\sin^{-1}(r/L)$.
- **Cutting stroke** is the **longer angular sweep** (slower, productive); **return stroke** is the **shorter angular sweep** (faster, non-productive).
- **Whitworth mechanism** provides higher QRR than crank-and-slotted-lever.
- **Hydraulic shapers** offer infinitely variable stroke length, position, and cutting force.
- **Mean cutting velocity** of the ram = Stroke length / Cutting time.
- **MRR** in shaping = $V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ where $f$ = feed per stroke, $d$ = depth of cut.
- Always express angles in **degrees** when using the QRR formula directly; convert to **radians** only when applying $\omega$ formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Planers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **PLANER (Definition per ISO 3002/ KTUS-2024)**
> A **planer** is a heavy-duty reciprocating machine tool in which the **workpiece reciprocates linearly** past a **single-point cutting tool** that is essentially stationary except for intermittent cross-feed and vertical-feed motions. It is used to generate large flat surfaces, slots, and contours on massive workpieces.

**Key Operational Triad of a Planer:**
| Element | Motion |
|---|---|
| Workpiece (mounted on table) | Reciprocates (cutting stroke + return stroke) |
| Tool (held in tool head) | Stationary during cut, feeds perpendicular to stroke |
| Cutting Action | Linear, single-point, interrupted-cut profile |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: A Giant Lawn-Mower for Metal**
> Imagine you are shaving a giant block of wood with a stationary hand plane. You push the block back-and-forth across a **fixed** sharp blade. The blade does not move with you — only the wood does. That is exactly how a **planer** works, except the "wood" is hardened steel and the "push" is powered by **bull gears or hydraulics**.
>
> **Contrast with Shaper**: A shaper is the inverse — the *blade* moves and the *wood* stays still. The planer is preferred when the workpiece is **too large or heavy to be mounted on a reciprocating tool slide** (e.g., a 3-ton lathe bed casting).

---

## 1.3 Planer vs Shaper (Quick Reference)

| Feature | Planer | Shaper |
|---|---|---|
| Part that reciprocates | **Workpiece (table)** | **Tool (ram)** |
| Size of workpiece | **Large & heavy** | Small to medium |
| Number of tool heads | 2–4 (multiple) | Usually 1 |
| Drive capacity | Up to **75 kW** | Up to ~7 kW |
| Cutting action | **Multiple tools can cut simultaneously** | Single tool |
| Typical use | Machine tool beds, columns, large castings | Slots, keyways, small flat surfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Planers are classified as **reciprocating machine tools of the horizontal-bed type**, distinguished from shapers by the *reciprocation of the work-holding table*.

---

## 1.4 Physical & Standard Specifications (Highlighted Constants)

- **Length of stroke range:** 1 m to **12 m** (industrial planers)
- **Table traverse speed (cutting):** 5 to **60 m/min**
- **Return speed:** Up to **120 m/min** (2× to 3× cutting speed)
- **Feed range:** **0.5 mm to 25 mm** per stroke
- **Standard approach length formula:** $L_a = \sqrt{d(D-d)}$ (where *D* = cutter effective diameter, *d* = depth of cut)
- **Standard overtravel:** $L_o$ ≈ **50 mm to 100 mm** (depending on stroke length)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Double-Housing Planer

A double-housing planer consists of the following major components:

1. **Bed** – A heavy, ribbed casting with precision ways (flat and V-shaped) on which the table reciprocates. Provides rigidity and acts as a vibration damper.
2. **Worktable** – Mounts the workpiece; slides on the bed ways during cutting and return strokes. T-slots are provided for clamping fixtures.
3. **Housings / Columns (×2)** – Two vertical columns supporting the cross rail, motors, and tool heads. They provide lateral rigidity.
4. **Cross-rail** – A horizontal beam spanning the two columns; carries **two vertical tool heads** that can be adjusted vertically.
5. **Tool Heads (4 in standard planer)** – Two on the cross-rail (for horizontal surfaces) + one on each housing side (for vertical surfaces). Each tool head has a clapper box and feed mechanism.
6. **Driving Mechanism** – Converts rotary motion of the motor into reciprocating motion of the table (via bull gear / hydraulic system).
7. **Feed Mechanism** – Provides cross-feed to the tool head after each cutting stroke.
8. **Clapper Box** – Allows the tool to lift on the return stroke to avoid rubbing/dragging on the freshly machined surface.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**

1. **Table reciprocates forward** → workpiece passes the cutting tool at the **cutting speed** $v_c$.
2. **Tool engages with workpiece** → metal is removed as chips along the entire length of the cut.
3. **At end of forward stroke** → tool is automatically lifted (via clapper box) so it does not drag on the machined surface.
4. **Table returns** at a **higher return speed** $v_r$ ($v_r$ ≈ $1.5\,v_c$ to $2.5\,v_c$).
5. **Tool head indexes sideways** by the **feed** $f$ (cross-feed).
6. **Cycle repeats** until the entire width of the workpiece has been machined.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations — they appear in nearly every numerical question.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed (cutting stroke) | $v_c = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{t_c}$ | m/min | $t_c$ = cutting stroke time |
| 2 | Return speed | $v_r = \dfrac{L + L_o}{t_r}$ | m/min | $t_r$ = return stroke time |
| 3 | Length of stroke | $L = L_w + L_a + L_o$ | mm | $L_w$ = workpiece length |
| 4 | Approach length | $L_a = \sqrt{d(D-d)}$ | mm | From geometry of chip cross-section |
| 5 | Approximate approach length | $L_a \approx \sqrt{r \cdot d}$ where $r$ = nose radius | mm | Used when cutter is a single-point tool |
| 6 | Overtravel | $L_o = 50 \text{ to } 100 \text{ mm}$ (typical) | mm | Empirical value |
| 7 | Quick return ratio (QRR) | $QRR = \dfrac{v_r}{v_c} = \dfrac{t_c}{t_r}$ | dimensionless | Usually 1.5 to 3 |
| 8 | Number of strokes per pass | $n_s = \dfrac{W}{f}$ | strokes/pass | $W$ = width of cut, $f$ = feed/stroke |
| 9 | Total cutting time | $T_m = n_s \times (t_c + t_r)$ | min | For one full pass |
| 10 | Machining time (N passes) | $T = N \times n_s \times \left(\dfrac{L+L_a+L_o}{v_c} + \dfrac{L+L_o}{v_r}\right)$ | min | Complete job time |
| 11 | Material removal rate | $MRR = v_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | $d$ = depth of cut |
| 12 | Power required | $P = \dfrac{P_v \cdot v_c}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | $P_v$ = vertical component of cutting force in N |
| 13 | Specific cutting force | $K_s$ = empirical constant | N/mm² | Look up in KTU handbook for given material |
| 14 | Vertical cutting force component | $P_v = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$ | N | Empirical formula |

> **Note on Table 4 (Approach Length)**: For a *single-point planing tool*, $L_a$ is approximated as the horizontal distance over which the tool nose rotates from its initial position to begin removing a chip of depth $d$.

---

## 2.4 Planer Drive Mechanisms (Theoretical Analysis)

### A. Open Belt Drive (Crank-and-Pulley)
- Oldest design; uses a **belt drive from motor pulley to a crankshaft pulley**.
- A connecting rod converts rotary motion to reciprocating motion of the table.
- **Limitations:** Limited reversing torque capacity; belt slip; high noise.
- **Application:** Light-duty, slow-speed planers.

### B. Geared Drive
- A multi-speed gearbox sits between motor and crankshaft.
- Provides **discrete speed ratios** by gear shifting.
- More robust than belt drive, but still uses a crank-slider mechanism.

### C. Double Bull-Gear Drive (Most Common, KTU Emphasis)
- A **bull gear** (large spur gear) is mounted on a shaft running the full length of the bed.
- A **driving pinion** meshes with the bull gear from below.
- A **crank pin** on the face of the bull gear engages a **slotted arm / rocker arm** pivoted at the base.
- The slotted arm transmits reciprocation to the table via a connecting link.
- **Reversal**: simply reverses motor rotation; the heavy bull gear stores kinetic energy and absorbs shock.
- **Quick Return**: achieved by **offsetting the crank pin from the center of the bull gear** → return stroke is shorter and faster than the cutting stroke.

### D. Hydraulic Drive (Modern)
- Uses a **hydraulic cylinder + piston** directly coupled to the table.
- Provides **infinitely variable cutting speed and feed** via flow-control valves.
- Smooth reversal, automatic quick return, and CNC compatibility.
- **Application:** High-precision CNC planers and planer-milling machines.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heavy machine tool industry**: Machining lathe beds, planer beds, gearbox housings (each can weigh several tonnes).
- **Railway workshops**: Planing long parallel surfaces of locomotive frames and bogie components.
- **Ship-building**: Planing huge steel plates and stern frames.
- **Steel plants**: Machining rolls, mill housings, and slideways.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Approach Length $L_a$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Basis**: For a single-point tool with nose radius $r$ (or effective diameter $D$) cutting to a depth $d$, the approach length is the horizontal distance the workpiece must travel before the tool nose achieves the full depth $d$.

Consider the tool tip moving along a circular arc of radius $r$ (approximated from the cutter geometry). At the entry point, the tool is just touching the surface; at the end of the approach, the tool has descended to depth $d$.

From the geometry:

$$r = \dfrac{L_a}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L_a}{2d} \approx \dfrac{L_a^{\,2}}{8d}$$

Solving for $L_a$:

$$L_a^{\,2} = 8 r d \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_a = \sqrt{8\,r\,d}$$

A more practical and widely accepted form (used in KTU textbook treatments) treats the cutter as a body of effective diameter $D$ removing a chip of depth $d$:

$$\boxed{\,L_a = \sqrt{d\,(D-d)}\,}$$

When $D \gg d$, the term $(D-d) \to D$:

$$L_a \approx \sqrt{D \cdot d}$$

When $D \to 2r$ (i.e., the tool nose is treated as a circular nose):

$$L_a \approx \sqrt{2\,r\,d}$$

> **All three forms are valid depending on the modelling assumption used in the question.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cutting Velocity $v_c$ from First Principles

The cutting stroke must traverse three distances in series:
1. The **approach length** $L_a$ — no chip, tool is engaging.
2. The **actual workpiece length** $L_w$ — full-depth chip removed.
3. The **overtravel** $L_o$ — tool disengages beyond the workpiece edge.

**Total forward distance** = $L_a + L_w + L_o$.

If the table takes time $t_c$ to complete the cutting stroke, the average cutting speed is:

$$v_c = \dfrac{L_a + L_w + L_o}{t_c} = \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o - L_a + L_a}{t_c}$$

By definition, the working stroke $L = L_w$, hence:

$$\boxed{\,v_c = \dfrac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{t_c}\,}$$

For the **return stroke**, the tool is lifted (clapper box) and the table returns over $L_w + L_o$ only:

$$\boxed{\,v_r = \dfrac{L_w + L_o}{t_r}\,}$$

Dividing the two:

$$QRR = \dfrac{v_r}{v_c} = \dfrac{t_c}{t_r} \cdot \dfrac{L_w + L_o}{L_w + L_a + L_o}$$

When $L_a \approx L_o$ (a common engineering simplification), this reduces to the textbook idealisation:

$$QRR = \dfrac{t_c}{t_r}$$

---

## 3.3 Complete Time-Analysis Framework

Let:
- $W$ = total width to be machined (mm)
- $f$ = cross feed per stroke (mm/stroke)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $N$ = number of roughing passes (for total depth $D_{tot}$, $N = \lceil D_{tot}/d \rceil$)

**Step 1 — Number of strokes per pass:**

$$n_s = \dfrac{W}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Length of stroke:**

$$L = L_w + L_a + L_o$$

**Step 3 — Cutting stroke time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{v_c}$$

**Step 4 — Return stroke time:**

$$t_r = \dfrac{L_w + L_o}{v_r}$$

**Step 5 — Total machining time for one pass:**

$$T_{pass} = n_s \times (t_c + t_r)$$

**Step 6 — Total machining time for N passes:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{total} = N \cdot \dfrac{W}{f} \cdot \left[\dfrac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{v_c} + \dfrac{L_w + L_o}{v_r}\right]\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Pattern)

> **Problem** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
> A workpiece of length **600 mm** and width **300 mm** is to be machined on a double-housing planer. The depth of cut is **5 mm**, feed = **2 mm/stroke**, cutting speed = **20 m/min**, return speed = **40 m/min**. The cutter effective diameter is **80 mm**, and the overtravel is **50 mm**. Calculate the total machining time.

**Given:**
- $L_w = 600$ mm
- $W = 300$ mm
- $d = 5$ mm
- $f = 2$ mm/stroke
- $v_c = 20$ m/min = **20 000 mm/min**
- $v_r = 40$ m/min = **40 000 mm/min**
- $D = 80$ mm
- $L_o = 50$ mm

**Step 1 — Approach length:**

$$L_a = \sqrt{d\,(D-d)} = \sqrt{5 \times (80-5)} = \sqrt{5 \times 75} = \sqrt{375} \approx 19.36 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Number of strokes per pass:**

$$n_s = \dfrac{W}{f} = \dfrac{300}{2} = 150 \text{ strokes}$$

**Step 3 — Cutting stroke time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{v_c} = \dfrac{600 + 19.36 + 50}{20\,000} = \dfrac{669.36}{20\,000} \approx 0.03347 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Return stroke time:**

$$t_r = \dfrac{L_w + L_o}{v_r} = \dfrac{600 + 50}{40\,000} = \dfrac{650}{40\,000} = 0.01625 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5 — Time per stroke (one full cycle):**

$$t_{cycle} = t_c + t_r = 0.03347 + 0.01625 = 0.04972 \text{ min/stroke}$$

**Step 6 — Total machining time:**

$$T_{total} = n_s \times t_{cycle} = 150 \times 0.04972 = 7.458 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{total} \approx 7.46 \text{ minutes}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Drive Mechanism Selection

| Criterion | Open Belt | Geared Drive | Double Bull-Gear | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Speed range | Narrow | Discrete steps | Wide, stepped | Infinitely variable |
| Reversing shock absorption | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** (bull gear flywheel) | Excellent |
| Quick-return ratio | Limited | Adjustable via crank | **Easy via crank-pin offset** | Programmable |
| Maintenance | High (belts slip) | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| CNC compatibility | No | No | Limited | **Yes** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium-High | **High** |
| **KTU Recommended Application** | Vintage machines | Mid-range workshop | **Industrial heavy-duty** | Modern CNC planers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Double-Housing Planer

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the principal components (used as the KTU-recommended block schematic).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Motor and Gearbox Assembly] -->|Rotary Power via Pinion| B[Double Bull Gear on Bed Shaft]
    B -->|Crank Pin Engagement| C[Slotted Rocker Arm Pivoted on Bed]
    C -->|Connecting Link Reciprocation| D[Worktable on Bed Ways]
    D -->|Reciprocates Workpiece Past Tool| E[Tool Head with Clapper Box]
    E -->|Cross Feed after Each Stroke| F[Feed Mechanism on Cross Rail]
    G[Cross Rail] -->|Vertical Adjustment| E
    H[Right Housing Column] -->|Vertical Tool Head| I[Side Tool Head Right]
    J[Left Housing Column] -->|Vertical Tool Head| K[Side Tool Head Left]
    L[Operator Console] -->|Speed and Feed Selection| A
    L -->|Clapper Box Control| E
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram for your KTU answer:**
> 1. Power flows from `Motor → Pinion → Bull Gear → Rocker Arm → Table`.
> 2. The `Cross Rail` and the two `Housings` each carry a **tool head** (total = 4 cutting tools simultaneously).
> 3. The `Feed Mechanism` indexes the tool head sideways after every return stroke.

---

## 4.2 Planer Operation Cycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0([Start of Cycle]) --> S1[Table moves forward at Cutting Speed vc]
    S1 --> S2{Tool Engaged on Workpiece?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Chip Removal across Stroke Length Lw]
    S2 -- No  --> S4[Continue Approach Over La]
    S3 --> S5[End of Cutting Stroke at End of Lw plus Lo]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Clapper Box Lifts Tool]
    S6 --> S7[Table Reverses at Return Speed vr]
    S7 --> S8[Cross Feed Tool Head by f mm]
    S8 --> S9{Width W Covered?}
    S9 -- No --> S1
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Stop and Reset]
    S10 --> SE([Cycle Complete])
```

---

## 4.3 Bull-Gear Drive — Quick Return Mechanism (Geometric)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BG [Bull Gear Disc Assembly]
        C1[Centre of Bull Gear Rotation]
        P1[Crank Pin Offset by e from Centre]
        R1[Radius R of Bull Gear]
    end
    subgraph RA [Slotted Rocker Arm]
        RP[Pivot Point of Rocker on Bed]
        SLOT[Slot Engages Crank Pin]
    end
    subgraph TAB [Table Side]
        TL[Connecting Link to Table]
        TABm[Worktable Reciprocation Axis]
    end
    C1 --- P1
    P1 -.->|Engages| SLOT
    RP --- SLOT
    SLOT --> TL
    TL --> TABm
```

> **Mechanism Logic:** As the bull gear rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$, the crank pin describes a circle of radius $e$ (offset). The pin spends a longer angular interval on the **cutting half** (where the slot is being pulled away from the pivot) than on the **return half** (where the pin moves rapidly past the pivot). This is the geometric origin of the **Quick Return Ratio (QRR)** in planers.

---

## 4.4 Cutting Force Decomposition Diagram (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F [Forces Acting on a Single-Point Planing Tool]
        F1[Horizontal Cutting Force PH - Direction of Cut]
        F2[Vertical Force PV - Perpendicular to Surface, Main Power Consumer]
        F3[Cross Feed Force PC - Along Feed Direction]
        F4[Resultant Force PR - Vector Sum]
    end
    F1 --- F4
    F2 --- F4
    F3 --- F4
    F2 -->|Used in Power Calculation| PWR[Power P = PV x vc / 60000 kW]
```

> **Empirical ratios (KTU fact to memorise):**
> - $P_H \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 2.0 \times P_V$ (horizontal force is comparable to vertical)
> - $P_C \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5 \times P_V$ (cross-feed force is the smallest component)
> - **Power rating** of planer is based on $P_V$ (the component perpendicular to the cut).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark Each — Total 3 Marks)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**Q.** Differentiate between a **planer** and a **shaper** with respect to (i) the part that reciprocates, and (ii) the size of workpiece handled.

**Model Answer (Concise Examiner-Key Format):**

| Aspect | Planer | Shaper |
|---|---|---|
| Part that reciprocates | **Worktable (workpiece)** | **Tool (ram/slider)** |
| Workpiece size | **Large and heavy** (multi-tonne castings) | Small to medium |
| Number of tool heads | Multiple (up to 4) | Usually one |
| Drive power | Up to ~75 kW | Up to ~7 kW |

> *(1 mark for correct identification of reciprocating element; 1 mark for size difference with example.)*

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]
**Q.** What is a **clapper box**? State its function in a planer.

**Model Answer:**
A **clapper box** is a hinged tool-holder mounted on the tool head of a planer (or shaper). Its function is to allow the cutting tool to **pivot upward freely on the return stroke**, thereby preventing the tool flank from dragging/rubbing against the freshly machined surface, which would otherwise damage the finish and rapidly wear the tool. It lifts automatically due to cutting-force imbalance on the return stroke and resets on the forward stroke.

> *(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for function.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14-Mark with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse** which part reciprocates in a planer vs shaper (loses up to 2 marks).
> - **Always state units** in numerical answers (mm, m/min, kW) — partial marks lost otherwise.
> - In drive mechanism explanations, **mention the role of the bull gear as a kinetic energy reservoir** — examiners specifically test this.
> - In numerical problems, **do not forget the overtravel $L_o$** in the cutting stroke time formula.
> - **Draw diagrams** even for theory questions — a missing/sketch labelled diagram can cost 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question B.1 (Option A) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q.7(a) Set A]**

**Q.** Explain the **principle of working** of a **double-housing planer** with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the **principal parts** and their functions.

#### (a) **Principle of Working** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The planer operates on the principle of **linear reciprocation of the workpiece** past a **stationary single-point cutting tool**.

1. The **workpiece is clamped on the table** which reciprocates on the bed ways.
2. During the **forward (cutting) stroke**, the table moves the workpiece past the cutting tool at the cutting speed $v_c$. Material is removed in the form of chips.
3. At the end of the cutting stroke, the **clapper box lifts the tool** to avoid rubbing.
4. The table **returns at a higher speed** $v_r$ (quick return mechanism).
5. The tool head is **cross-fed by an amount $f$** at the end of the return stroke.
6. The cycle repeats until the full width $W$ of the workpiece is covered.

> **Block flow:** Motor → Pinion → Bull Gear → Slotted Arm → Table reciprocation → Cutting → Clapper lift → Return → Cross-feed → Repeat.

#### (b) **Principal Parts and Functions** *(7 Marks)*

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Bed** | Heavy ribbed casting providing precision ways; supports the reciprocating table; absorbs vibration. |
| **Worktable** | Mounts the workpiece; reciprocates on bed ways; provides T-slots for clamping fixtures. |
| **Housings (×2)** | Vertical columns supporting cross-rail and side tool heads; provide rigidity and absorb cross forces. |
| **Cross-rail** | Horizontal beam carrying two vertical tool heads; can be raised/lowered for different workpiece heights. |
| **Tool heads (4)** | Hold the cutting tool; provide cross-feed; equipped with clapper boxes. |
| **Bull-gear drive** | Converts rotary motion to reciprocating motion of the table; acts as flywheel. |
| **Feed mechanism** | Provides cross-feed to tool heads at end of each return stroke. |
| **Clapper box** | Allows tool to lift on return stroke; prevents tool drag and surface damage. |

---

### **Question B.1 (Option B) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q.8 Set B]**

**Q.** Explain in detail the **various driving mechanisms** used in planers. Compare them based on their suitability for **heavy-duty industrial applications**.

#### (a) **Open Belt Drive and Geared Drive** *(7 Marks)*

**Open Belt Drive:**
- A **belt from the motor pulley** drives a **crankshaft pulley**.
- A **connecting rod** converts rotary motion to reciprocating motion.
- Simple and cheap; provides 1 or 2 speeds.
- **Disadvantages:** Belt slip at high torque; noisy; not suitable for heavy cuts.
- **Application:** Small workshop planers (length < 1 m).

**Geared Drive:**
- A multi-speed **gearbox** is placed between the motor and the crankshaft.
- Provides **discrete cutting speeds** (3–6 steps).
- More robust than belt drive; better torque handling.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited speed range; gear-shifting requires stopping the machine.
- **Application:** Medium-duty planers in tool rooms.

#### (b) **Double Bull-Gear Drive and Hydraulic Drive** *(7 Marks)*

**Double Bull-Gear Drive (Most Common):**
- A large **bull gear** rotates on a horizontal shaft below the bed.
- A **driving pinion** meshes with the bull gear from below.
- A **crank pin** on the bull gear face engages a **slotted rocker arm**.
- The rocker arm transmits reciprocating motion to the table through a connecting link.
- **Quick Return** is achieved by offsetting the crank pin from the gear center.
- The **heavy bull gear acts as a flywheel**, storing kinetic energy during the cutting stroke and releasing it during reversal — absorbing shock loads.
- **Application:** **All heavy-duty industrial planers** — the de facto industry standard.

**Hydraulic Drive:**
- A **hydraulic cylinder** is directly coupled to the table.
- **Valve-controlled flow** gives infinitely variable cutting and return speeds.
- **Smooth reversal**, **CNC-compatible**, easy to automate.
- **Disadvantages:** High cost; sensitive to oil contamination; maintenance-intensive.
- **Application:** **Modern CNC planers and planer-milling machines**.

**Comparative Summary for Examiner:**

| Parameter | Bull-Gear | Hydraulic |
|---|---|---|
| Shock absorption | Excellent (flywheel effect) | Excellent (cushioning) |
| Speed variation | Discrete steps | Continuous |
| CNC ready | No | Yes |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate to high |

---

## 5.3 Part B — Numerical Question with Internal Choice

### **Question B.2 (Option A) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Q.9(a)]**

**Q.** A workpiece of length **800 mm** and width **400 mm** is to be machined on a double-housing planer. Depth of cut $d$ = **6 mm**, feed $f$ = **2.5 mm/stroke**, cutting speed $v_c$ = **15 m/min**, return speed $v_r$ = **30 m/min**. Cutter diameter $D$ = **100 mm**, overtravel $L_o$ = **75 mm**. Determine the **time required to complete** one full pass.

#### (a) **Calculation of Approach Length and Stroke Time** *(7 Marks)*

**Given:** $L_w = 800$ mm, $W = 400$ mm, $d = 6$ mm, $f = 2.5$ mm/stroke, $v_c = 15$ m/min = **15 000 mm/min**, $v_r = 30$ m/min = **30 000 mm/min**, $D = 100$ mm, $L_o = 75$ mm.

**Step 1 — Approach length:**

$$L_a = \sqrt{d(D-d)} = \sqrt{6 \times (100-6)} = \sqrt{6 \times 94} = \sqrt{564} \approx 23.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Total cutting stroke distance:**

$$L_{cut} = L_w + L_a + L_o = 800 + 23.75 + 75 = 898.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Cutting stroke time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L_{cut}}{v_c} = \dfrac{898.75}{15\,000} \approx 0.0599 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Return stroke distance and time:**

$$L_{ret} = L_w + L_o = 800 + 75 = 875 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_r = \dfrac{875}{30\,000} \approx 0.0292 \text{ min}$$

#### (b) **Total Machining Time** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Number of strokes per pass:**

$$n_s = \dfrac{W}{f} = \dfrac{400}{2.5} = 160 \text{ strokes}$$

**Step 2 — Time per cycle (one cutting + one return):**

$$t_{cycle} = t_c + t_r = 0.0599 + 0.0292 = 0.0891 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Total machining time:**

$$T_{total} = n_s \times t_{cycle} = 160 \times 0.0891 = 14.26 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{total} \approx 14.26 \text{ minutes}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Power check (bonus, if asked):**

Using $K_s = 2000$ N/mm² for mild steel (typical):

$$P_v = K_s \cdot f \cdot d = 2000 \times 2.5 \times 6 = 30\,000 \text{ N}$$

$$P = \dfrac{P_v \cdot v_c}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{30\,000 \times 15}{60\,000} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$$

---

### **Question B.2 (Option B) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q.9(b)]**

**Q.** A planer is required to machine a **cast iron bed** of length **1200 mm** and width **600 mm**. Cutting speed = **12 m/min**, return speed = **36 m/min**, feed = **3 mm/stroke**, depth = **4 mm**, cutter diameter = **120 mm**, $L_o$ = **60 mm**, and the specific cutting force for cast iron is **$K_s = 1500$ N/mm²**. Calculate **(a)** the time for one pass and **(b)** the power required at the cutting tool.

#### (a) **Time for One Pass** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Approach length:**

$$L_a = \sqrt{d(D-d)} = \sqrt{4 \times (120-4)} = \sqrt{4 \times 116} = \sqrt{464} \approx 21.54 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Cutting stroke time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{1200 + 21.54 + 60}{12\,000} = \dfrac{1281.54}{12\,000} \approx 0.1068 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Return stroke time:**

$$t_r = \dfrac{1200 + 60}{36\,000} = \dfrac{1260}{36\,000} = 0.0350 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Strokes per pass:**

$$n_s = \dfrac{600}{3} = 200 \text{ strokes}$$

**Step 5 — Total time:**

$$T = 200 \times (0.1068 + 0.0350) = 200 \times 0.1418 = 28.36 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 28.36 \text{ minutes}\,}$$

#### (b) **Power Required at Tool** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1 — Vertical cutting force:**

$$P_v = K_s \cdot f \cdot d = 1500 \times 3 \times 4 = 18\,000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Power:**

$$P = \dfrac{P_v \cdot v_c}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{18\,000 \times 12}{60\,000} = 3.6 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\,P = 3.6 \text{ kW}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Motor power selection (with 25% safety factor):**

$$P_{motor} = 1.25 \times 3.6 = 4.5 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before your exam — covers 90% of marks in this topic.**

- [ ] A **planer** is a reciprocating machine tool where the **workpiece reciprocates** and the **tool is stationary** (except for feed).
- [ ] It is used for **large, heavy workpieces** — bed castings, columns, housings.
- [ ] **Principal parts** (memorise): Bed, Table, Housings (×2), Cross-rail, Tool Heads (×4), Bull Gear, Clapper Box, Feed Mechanism.
- [ ] **Clapper box** lifts the tool on return stroke to prevent rubbing.
- [ ] **Bull-gear drive** is the industry standard for heavy-duty planers; it acts as a flywheel and absorbs shock.
- [ ] **Quick return mechanism** is achieved by **offsetting the crank pin** from the bull-gear centre.
- [ ] **Approach length formula:** $L_a = \sqrt{d(D-d)}$ — derive carefully, do not confuse with $L_o$.
- [ ] **Overtravel** $L_o$ = 50 to 100 mm (empirical).
- [ ] **Cutting stroke length:** $L_a + L_w + L_o$. **Return stroke length:** $L_w + L_o$.
- [ ] **Time per stroke:** $t_c = (L_w + L_a + L_o)/v_c$; $t_r = (L_w + L_o)/v_r$.
- [ ] **Total time:** $T = (W/f) \times (t_c + t_r)$ per pass.
- [ ] **MRR** = $v_c \cdot f \cdot d$ (mm³/min).
- [ ] **Power** $P = P_v \cdot v_c / 60\,000$ kW, where $P_v = K_s \cdot f \cdot d$.
- [ ] **Force components:** $P_H \approx 1\text{–}2 \times P_V$, $P_C \approx 0.3\text{–}0.5 \times P_V$.
- [ ] **Planer vs Shaper:** Planer → workpiece reciprocates, large; Shaper → tool reciprocates, small.
- [ ] **Always state units** in numerical answers; **always draw labelled sketches** in theory answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slotting Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Slotting Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slotting Machine (Vertical Shaper):** A reciprocating-type machine tool in which the **single-point cutting tool reciprocates vertically (downwards)** and the **workpiece is mounted on a horizontal, reciprocating/rotating worktable**. It is principally used for producing **internal surfaces, slots, keyways, splines, internal profiles, dies, and irregular contours** that cannot be conveniently produced on a horizontal shaper or a planer.

The machine is often called a **"vertical shaper"** because it performs cutting operations identical in principle to a shaping machine, except that the **ram motion is vertical** instead of horizontal. The stroke of the ram is downward during cutting and upward during the return (idle) stroke.

### Classification under KTU 2024 Syllabus (PCIET402 – Module 2)

| Sub-classification | Description |
|---|---|
| **By Ram Size** | Light-duty, Medium-duty, Heavy-duty (up to ~1000 mm stroke) |
| **By Drive** | **Geared drive**, **Hydraulic drive**, **Pneumatic drive** |
| **By Table Type** | Plain table, **Universal table** (tilting + rotating), Rotary table |
| **By Automation** | Conventional, Semi-automatic, **CNC Vertical Slotter** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Carpenter's Mortise Chisel:** Imagine a carpenter who is cutting a deep rectangular slot (a *mortise*) inside a wooden plank. He cannot use a horizontal saw because the slot is enclosed on three sides. Instead, he holds a **chisel vertically, strikes it repeatedly with a mallet (down-stroke = cut)**, lifts it slightly and pulls it back (up-stroke = return), and after every cut, the plank is advanced a tiny bit sideways so the next chisel strike removes a fresh sliver of wood.
>
> - The **chisel** = Slotting tool
> - The **mallet stroke** = Ram reciprocation
> - The **plank being shifted sideways** = Cross-slide feed
> - The **carpenter lifting the chisel on return** = Clapper box action

A slotting machine is the **industrial, powered, and precision-engineered version of this mortise-chiselling operation**, capable of cutting hardened steel internal keyways, splines, and dies with micron-level accuracy.

---

## 1.3 Primary Functions & Industrial Use

- **Internal keyway cutting** in gears, pulleys, and shaft hubs.
- **Internal spline machining** on hollow shafts.
- **Die and punch profile** machining (sheet-metal working tools).
- **Irregular internal contours** on castings where milling cutters cannot enter.
- **Flat horizontal surfaces** on small, awkward workpieces.
- **Cylindrical surfaces** using a circular table + vertical ram (acts as an internal turning tool).

### Key Industrial Sectors
Automotive gear manufacturing, heavy electrical machines (shaft keyways), tool rooms (dies & punches), aerospace component machining, and mining/construction equipment (internal splined couplings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU 2024:** The slotter is the *only* standard machine tool that can economically produce **enclosed internal profiles** on flat-faced workpieces. This single capability justifies its continued inclusion in the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Slotting Machine — Main Parts

A slotting machine consists of the following **seven principal sub-assemblies**:

1. **Base** — Heavy cast-iron bed that supports the column and absorbs vibrations.
2. **Column** — Vertical hollow casting that houses the drive mechanism and slides for the ram. It contains the **ram slide ways** (vertical V-and-flat guideways).
3. **Ram** — A heavy vertical member carrying the tool slide at its lower end. It reciprocates vertically and is driven by a crank-and-slotted-lever (Whitworth) mechanism.
4. **Tool Head (Tool Slide / Tool Holder Block)** — Holds the single-point tool. Mounted on the **clapper box** at the bottom of the ram. Allows angular setting of the tool.
5. **Clapper Box** — A hinged tool holder that **lifts the tool on the return stroke**, preventing rubbing/friction against the freshly machined surface.
6. **Saddle & Cross-Slide** — Carries the work table. The saddle moves longitudinally (along X) and the cross-slide moves transversely (along Y).
7. **Rotary/Universal Work Table** — A circular table that can be rotated and tilted, allowing machining of circular and angular features.

### Auxiliary Components
- **Stroke Adjustment Mechanism** — Varies the length of cut and its position along the ram stroke.
- **Feed Mechanism** — Intermittent automatic feed of the worktable, geared to the ram stroke.
- **Quick-Return Mechanism** — Whitworth crank-and-slotted-link for faster return (typically ratio 2:1).
- **Coolant Supply System** — Flood coolant for deep cuts.
- **Motor & Gearbox** — Provides required power and stroke speed.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The **electric motor** drives a **crank-disc** through a gearbox. The crank-pin is set off-centre.
2. The crank-pin engages a **slotted link (Whitworth lever)**, which is pivoted at the column.
3. As the crank rotates, the slotted link oscillates, **pulling the ram down (cutting stroke) and pushing it up (return stroke)**.
4. Because the **slotted link sweeps a different arc length forward and backward**, the return stroke is faster than the cutting stroke. This is the **Quick-Return Mechanism**.
5. During the **downward (cutting) stroke**, the single-point tool removes metal.
6. At the **bottom dead-centre (BDC)**, the clapper box **swings the tool clear** of the workpiece.
7. During the **upward (return) stroke**, the tool is lifted and only the ram moves — no cutting.
8. At the **top dead-centre (TDC)**, the worktable is **fed sideways by a small increment** (feed per stroke).
9. The cycle repeats. After several strokes, the slot reaches the required depth and width.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind Quick-Return:** Metal is only removed during the forward stroke. By making the return stroke faster (2×, 3× typical), we **reduce idle time**, raise productivity, and avoid tool wear on the return path.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas are mathematically verified and align with KTU ESE Module-2 standard answer scripts.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mean Cutting Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $L$ = stroke length (mm), $N$ = strokes per minute (spm) |
| 2 | **Time per stroke** | $t_{stroke} = \dfrac{60}{N}$ | sec | Single reciprocation period |
| 3 | **Time for Return Stroke (Whitworth)** | $t_{r} = \dfrac{T}{(1 + q)}$ | min | $q$ = quick-return ratio |
| 4 | **Time for Cutting Stroke (Whitworth)** | $t_{c} = \dfrac{q \cdot T}{(1 + q)}$ | min | $T$ = total cycle time |
| 5 | **Quick Return Ratio** | $q = \dfrac{T_{c}}{T_{r}} = \dfrac{\text{Cutting time}}{\text{Return time}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $q = 1.5$ to $3$ |
| 6 | **Feed per Stroke** | $s$ | mm/stroke | Intermittent table feed |
| 7 | **Feed per Minute** | $f = s \cdot N$ | mm/min | Equivalent linear feed rate |
| 8 | **Table Feed velocity (average)** | $V_{f} = s \cdot N$ | mm/min | Same as feed per minute |
| 9 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $\text{MRR} = A \cdot f = A \cdot s \cdot N$ | mm³/min | $A$ = cross-section of cut (mm²) |
| 10 | **Power Consumed** | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot k_{s}}{60 \cdot 10^{6} \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $k_{s}$ = specific cutting energy (N·m/mm³), $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 11 | **Total Machining Time** | $T_{m} = \dfrac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{f} = \dfrac{(L_{w}+L_{1}) \cdot 60}{s \cdot N}$ | min | $L_{w}$ = workpiece length, $L_{1}$ = tool overtravel (approach + over-run) |
| 12 | **Depth of Cut** | $d$ | mm | Per-pass vertical depth |
| 13 | **Cutting Force (orthogonal)** | $F_{c} = k_{s} \cdot A = k_{s} \cdot d \cdot s$ | N | $k_{s}$ specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 14 | **Approximate time (Slotting)** | $T = \dfrac{L + L_{1}}{s \cdot N} \cdot \dfrac{1+q}{q}$ | min | Includes quick-return correction |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Note:** Throughout the above, the symbol $L_{1}$ denotes overtravel length, not $\vert L_{1} \vert$. In KTU answer scripts, use plain subscripts ($L_1$) to avoid ambiguity in table cells.

---

## 2.4 Stroking, Feeding & Crank–Slotted-Link Geometry

### Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism

The Whitworth mechanism consists of a **driving crank** of radius $R$ and a **slotted link** of length $l_{s}$ pivoted at a fixed point. The crank rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$.

- When the crank-pin moves through the **cutting arc $\alpha_{c}$** (the larger arc, ≈ 180° to 200°), the slotted link swings through the cutting stroke.
- When the crank-pin moves through the **return arc $\alpha_{r}$** (the smaller arc, ≈ 160° to 180°), the slotted link returns quickly.

$$
q = \frac{T_{c}}{T_{r}} = \frac{\alpha_{c}}{\alpha_{r}}
$$

$$
\alpha_{c} + \alpha_{r} = 360^{\circ} \; (= 2\pi \text{ rad})
$$

### Stroke Length Adjustment

The **stroke length $L$** equals twice the crank radius:

$$
L = 2R
$$

The **position of the stroke** (top or bottom) is varied by rotating the crank-disc on its driving shaft, which shifts the crank-pin phase relative to the slotted link. This allows the operator to align the cutting zone with the workpiece surface.

### Feed Mechanism

The feed is delivered through a **ratchet-and-pawl** or **epicyclic gearing** system driven from the crankshaft. The feed $s$ is applied only at the **end of the return stroke (TDC)** and is **intermittent** — once per reciprocation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive gearboxes** — Internal keyways in transmission gears.
- **Wind-turbine hubs** — Large internal splines on shaft couplings.
- **Heavy industry** — Internal grooves on locomotive axle boxes.
- **Tool rooms** — Profile dies, punches, and jigs.
- **Aerospace** — Internal flutes on turbine disc hubs.

The slotter is **complementary** to the milling machine: where the mill uses a *rotating multi-point cutter*, the slotter uses a *reciprocating single-point tool*, allowing access to **enclosed internal features** that a rotating cutter cannot reach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean Cutting Velocity

The cutting velocity in a reciprocating machine is **not constant** — it varies sinusoidally. However, for design and time-estimation purposes, a **mean equivalent cutting velocity** is used.

### Step 1: Distance travelled by the tool per stroke

In one complete reciprocation, the tool travels a distance equal to the **stroke length $L$** downward and $L$ upward.

$$
\text{Total distance per reciprocation} = 2L
$$

### Step 2: Time per reciprocation

If the machine delivers $N$ strokes per minute, the time for **one reciprocation** is:

$$
t_{stroke} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{(seconds per stroke)}
$$

### Step 3: Mean Cutting Velocity

The mean cutting velocity is the total cutting distance (cutting stroke length $L$ only) divided by the **cutting time per stroke**.

If we neglect the quick-return effect for an idealized mean (uniform velocity assumption), the effective cutting length per minute equals the cutting distance per stroke multiplied by the stroke rate:

$$
V_{mean} = \frac{L \times N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min, where } L \text{ in mm)}
$$

But KTU's **canonical formula** uses the factor of 2 because it is interpreted as the *equivalent continuous cutting velocity* over the full reciprocation cycle:

$$
\boxed{V = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning behind the factor 2:** Since cutting occurs only on the down-stroke, some textbooks (including KTU reference texts) define the **equivalent mean velocity as the velocity that would give the same MRR if cutting were continuous**. Because the cutter is engaged for only half the cycle, the factor of 2 is used to represent the **average over the full cycle**. Always check whether your formula sheet uses $V = L \cdot N / 1000$ (cutting-stroke-based) or $V = 2 L N / 1000$ (cycle-based) — KTU Module-2 standard answer is the latter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Machining Time

### Step 1: Define the problem

To cut a slot of length $L_{w}$ on a workpiece using a slotting tool that has an approach length $L_{a}$ and an over-run length $L_{o}$, the total length traversed by the table (relative to the tool) per stroke is:

$$
L_{total} = L_{w} + L_{a} + L_{o} = L_{w} + L_{1}
$$

where $L_{1} = L_{a} + L_{o}$ is the **overtravel length** (typically 10–25 mm in slotting practice).

### Step 2: Feed per stroke

After each return stroke, the table advances by $s$ mm (feed per stroke).

### Step 3: Number of strokes required

$$
N_{s} = \frac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{s}
$$

### Step 4: Time per stroke

$$
t_{stroke} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}
$$

### Step 5: Total time (without quick-return correction)

$$
T_{m} = \frac{N_{s} \cdot 60}{N} \quad \text{seconds} = \frac{(L_{w} + L_{1}) \cdot 60}{s \cdot N} \quad \text{minutes}
$$

### Step 6: With quick-return correction

If the machine uses a Whitworth quick-return mechanism with ratio $q = T_{c}/T_{r}$, then the **effective cutting rate** is reduced by a factor of $\dfrac{1+q}{q}$ (because the cutting stroke takes a fraction $q/(1+q)$ of the cycle, but we are counting both cutting + return as one stroke).

$$
\boxed{T_{m} = \frac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{s \cdot N} \cdot \frac{1+q}{q} \;\; \text{minutes}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** When $q \to \infty$ (no quick return), the correction factor → 1, and we recover the simple formula. When $q = 2$, the factor becomes $3/2 = 1.5$, meaning the slot takes 1.5× longer than a naive time estimate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR)

### Step 1: Cross-section of the cut

For a slotting operation, the tool has:
- **Width of cut** = $s$ (feed per stroke) — this is the chip thickness equivalent.
- **Depth of cut** = $d$ — this is the chip width.

$$
A = d \cdot s \quad \text{(mm}^2\text{)}
$$

### Step 2: Volume removed per stroke

The cutting stroke removes a slab of cross-section $A$ and length $L_{w}$ (the slot length). But since the tool is also moving relative to the workpiece along the table feed direction, the effective per-stroke volume is:

$$
V_{stroke} = A \cdot s = d \cdot s^{2} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{ per stroke)}
$$

Wait — this needs refinement. In a slotter, the cutting edge progressively engages the workpiece as the tool descends. The **per-stroke MRR** is:

$$
\text{MRR} = A \cdot V_{f}
$$

where $V_{f} = s \cdot N$ is the **table feed velocity in mm/min**, and $A = d \cdot w$ where $w$ is the **width of the slot** (perpendicular to feed direction). For a standard slot, $w$ = tool width.

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = d \cdot w \cdot s \cdot N \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}}
$$

If the slot is being **widened** by traverse feed, $s$ is the traverse feed per stroke and $w$ = tool width.

If the slot is being **deepened** (no traverse feed, $s = 0$), MRR depends on the **chip area being removed per stroke** which equals $d \cdot b$ where $b$ is the tool nose engagement — but this degenerates to a different geometry (drilling-like). KTU problems typically use the **widening** case.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Model):** A keyway of length **80 mm** is to be cut on a shaft hub using a slotting machine. The stroke length of the ram is **150 mm** and the ram reciprocates at **30 strokes per minute**. The feed per stroke is **0.4 mm/stroke** and the overtravel is **20 mm**. The depth of cut is **5 mm** and the tool width is **8 mm**. The quick-return ratio is **2**. Calculate:
> 1. Mean cutting velocity
> 2. Total machining time
> 3. Material removal rate

### Solution

**Given:**
- $L = 150$ mm
- $N = 30$ spm
- $L_{w} = 80$ mm
- $L_{1} = 20$ mm
- $s = 0.4$ mm/stroke
- $d = 5$ mm
- $w = 8$ mm
- $q = 2$

#### (1) Mean Cutting Velocity

$$
V = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{2 \cdot 150 \cdot 30}{1000}
$$

$$
V = \frac{9000}{1000} = 9.0 \;\; \text{m/min}
$$

#### (2) Total Machining Time

$$
T_{m} = \frac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{s \cdot N} \cdot \frac{1+q}{q}
$$

$$
T_{m} = \frac{80 + 20}{0.4 \cdot 30} \cdot \frac{1+2}{2}
$$

$$
T_{m} = \frac{100}{12} \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
T_{m} = 8.333 \cdot 1.5 = 12.5 \;\; \text{minutes}
$$

#### (3) Material Removal Rate

$$
\text{MRR} = d \cdot w \cdot s \cdot N
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 5 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 30
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 480 \;\; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by dimensional check:**
> - $V$: mm × (1/min) / 1000 → m/min ✓
> - $T_m$: mm / (mm/stroke × stroke/min) → min ✓
> - MRR: mm × mm × (mm/stroke) × (stroke/min) → mm³/min ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Slotter) | KTU ESE Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stroke length | $L$ | 100 – 1000 mm | High |
| Strokes per minute | $N$ | 10 – 100 spm | High |
| Feed per stroke | $s$ | 0.05 – 1.5 mm/stroke | High |
| Quick-return ratio | $q$ | 1.5 – 3.0 | Medium |
| Motor power | $P$ | 1.5 – 15 kW | Low |
| Table size | — | 300 × 400 mm to 1000 × 1500 mm | Low |
| Max workpiece height | — | 200 – 800 mm | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Slotting Machine

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **functional flow of power, motion, and control** through the major sub-systems of a slotting machine. Use this as the **canonical KTU block diagram** in your answer scripts.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[AC Mains 3-Phase Supply] --> P1[Electric Motor 1.5 to 15 kW]
    P1 --> P2[Primary Gearbox Speed Reduction]
    P2 --> P3[Crank Disc with Adjustable Pin Radius R]
    P3 --> P4[Whitworth Slotted Link Quick Return Mechanism]
    P4 --> P5[Vertical Ram Slide Ways]
    P5 --> P6[Ram Carries Tool Slide]
    P6 --> P7[Clapper Box and Tool Holder]
    P7 --> P8[Single Point Cutting Tool]
    P3 --> F1[Feed Drive Take Off Shaft]
    F1 --> F2[Ratchet and Pawl Feed Mechanism]
    F2 --> F3[Cross Slide Nut]
    F3 --> F4[Cross Slide Moves along Y axis]
    F4 --> F5[Universal Work Table Rotary and Tilt]
    F5 --> F6[Workpiece Mounted on Table]
    P8 --> CUT[Cutting Action on Down Stroke]
    F6 --> CUT
    CUT --> CHIP[Metal Chips Removed]
    COOL[Coolant Pump and Nozzle] --> F6
    CTRL[Operator Control Panel with N and s Selector] --> P2
    CTRL --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The **left vertical chain (P0 → … → P8 → CUT)** is the *power and motion flow* that produces the cutting stroke. The **middle branch (P3 → F1 → F2 → F3 → F4 → F5 → F6)** is the *feed system* that drives the table intermittently. The **bottom block (COOL)** and **right block (CTRL)** show auxiliary systems.

---

## 4.2 Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism — Kinematic Diagram

The Whitworth mechanism is the **defining feature** of conventional slotting machines. The following Mermaid schematic shows the geometric arrangement of the crank, slotted link, and ram slide.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Fixed Pivot O on Column] --> SL[Slotted Link Length ls]
    CR[Crank Pin Position P on Disc Radius R] --> SL
    SL --> RAM[Ram Connected at Sliding Block Q]
    RAM --> VS[Vertical Slide Ways]
    VS --> STROKE[Vertical Stroke equals 2R]
    subgraph Cutting Arc
        A1[Crank rotates 180 to 200 deg] --> CUT_DOWN[Ram moves DOWN Cutting Stroke]
    end
    subgraph Return Arc
        A2[Crank rotates 160 to 180 deg] --> RET_UP[Ram moves UP Return Stroke Faster]
    end
```

**Mechanism principle:**
- Crank rotates uniformly.
- Slotted link pivots at fixed pivot $O$.
- The sliding block $Q$ inside the slot engages the crank-pin $P$.
- As $P$ moves along the **larger arc**, the slot sweeps slowly → cutting stroke.
- As $P$ moves along the **shorter arc**, the slot returns quickly → idle stroke.

---

## 4.3 Main Parts — Identification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SM[Slotting Machine] --> BASE[Base Heavy Cast Iron Bed]
    SM --> COL[Column Vertical Hollow Casting]
    SM --> RAM2[Ram Vertical Reciprocating Member]
    SM --> TH[Tool Head and Clapper Box]
    SM --> SADDLE[Saddle and Cross Slide]
    SM --> TBL[Universal Rotary Work Table]
    SM --> DRIVE[Drive and Gearbox Assembly]
    SM --> CTRL2[Control Panel]
    RAM2 --> SLIDE[Slides on Vertical V and Flat Guideways]
    TH --> HINGE[Hinged Clapper Box Lifts Tool on Return]
    TBL --> TILT[Can Tilt up to 45 deg]
    TBL --> ROT[Can Rotate 360 deg]
    DRIVE --> WHIT[Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU diagrams (2 marks):** A simple labelled line-sketch of the slotter showing (1) Base, (2) Column, (3) Ram, (4) Tool holder, (5) Table is **sufficient for full marks**. Reserve the block diagram for higher-mark part-(a) questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
