# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCIET402, Module 2]**
**What is a slotting machine? How is it different from a shaper?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A slotting machine is a **reciprocating-type machine tool** in which the **single-point cutting tool reciprocates vertically (downward)** to remove metal, while the **workpiece is mounted on a horizontal, usually rotary, work table**.

**Key differences from a shaper:**

| Feature | Slotter | Shaper |
|---|---|---|
| Ram motion | Vertical (down) | Horizontal |
| Tool position | At bottom of ram | At front of ram |
| Best suited for | Internal slots, keyways, dies | External flat surfaces, keyways on shafts |
| Workpiece movement | Cross-slide + rotary table | Plain reciprocating table |
| Cutting stroke | Downward | Forward (push) |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the comparison table — KTU 2024 valuation pattern.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCIET402, Module 2]**
**State any three specific applications of a slotting machine in modern industry.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Internal keyway cutting** in gears, pulleys, and shaft hubs (1 mark).
2. **Internal spline machining** on hollow transmission shafts and couplings (1 mark).
3. **Die and punch profile machining** in tool rooms (1 mark).

*(1 mark each. The KTU examiner looks for industry-specific terminology — avoid generic statements like "cutting slots".)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCIET402, Module 2]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a slotting machine**. List its **main parts** and describe the function of the **clapper box** and **quick-return mechanism**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A keyway of length **120 mm** is to be cut on a cast-iron hub using a slotting machine. The stroke length is **200 mm**, the ram speed is **25 strokes per minute**, the feed per stroke is **0.3 mm/stroke**, the overtravel is **25 mm**, and the quick-return ratio is **2**. The depth of cut is **6 mm** and tool width is **10 mm**. Calculate:
1. The mean cutting velocity.
2. The total machining time.
3. The material removal rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A slotting machine consists of the following main parts:
- **Base** — Heavy cast iron bed.
- **Column** — Vertical hollow member housing the drive and ram slide.
- **Ram** — Vertical reciprocating member carrying the tool head.
- **Tool Head & Clapper Box** — Holds the tool; the clapper box is a hinged tool holder.
- **Cross-Slide & Saddle** — Provides table movement along two axes.
- **Universal Work Table** — Can be rotated and tilted.
- **Drive Mechanism** — Motor, gearbox, Whitworth quick-return mechanism.

**Working (2 marks):**
The motor drives a crank disc through a gearbox. The crank pin engages a slotted lever (Whitworth mechanism), which converts rotary motion into reciprocating vertical motion of the ram. On the **downward (cutting) stroke**, the tool removes metal. On the **upward (return) stroke**, the clapper box lifts the tool clear of the workpiece, preventing rubbing. After the return stroke, the worktable is **fed** laterally by a small amount. The cycle repeats until the slot reaches full depth and length.

**Clapper Box Function (1 mark):**
The clapper box is hinged at the ram and carries the tool. During the cutting stroke it remains closed, holding the tool in the cutting position. On the return stroke, the inertia of the tool and the geometry of the hinge cause the box to **swing open**, lifting the tool tip off the freshly cut surface. This prevents rubbing, reduces tool wear, and improves surface finish.

**Quick-Return Mechanism (1 mark):**
The Whitworth quick-return mechanism uses a **crank and slotted link** to produce a slow cutting stroke and a fast return stroke. Because the crank sweeps a larger angular arc during cutting and a smaller arc during return (with the same rotational speed), the **return stroke time is shorter** than the cutting stroke time. The quick-return ratio $q = T_{c}/T_{r}$ is typically between 1.5 and 3. This reduces idle time, increases productivity, and minimizes tool wear.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- $L_{w} = 120$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, $N = 25$ spm
- $s = 0.3$ mm/stroke, $L_{1} = 25$ mm
- $q = 2$, $d = 6$ mm, $w = 10$ mm

**(1) Mean cutting velocity (2 marks):**

$$
V = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{2 \cdot 200 \cdot 25}{1000} = \frac{10000}{1000} = 10 \;\; \text{m/min}
$$

**(2) Total machining time (3 marks):**

$$
T_{m} = \frac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{s \cdot N} \cdot \frac{1+q}{q}
$$

$$
T_{m} = \frac{120 + 25}{0.3 \cdot 25} \cdot \frac{1+2}{2}
$$

$$
T_{m} = \frac{145}{7.5} \cdot 1.5
$$

$$
T_{m} = 19.333 \cdot 1.5 = 29.0 \;\; \text{minutes}
$$

**(3) Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MRR} = d \cdot w \cdot s \cdot N
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 6 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 25
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 450 \;\; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $V = 10$ m/min
- $T_{m} = 29$ min
- $\text{MRR} = 450 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCIET402, Module 2]*

**(a)** Explain the **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** used in a slotting machine with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the **quick-return ratio** in terms of crank angles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a slotting operation, the stroke length is **175 mm**, the ram makes **35 strokes per minute**, the feed is **0.5 mm/stroke**, and the quick-return ratio is **2.5**. The workpiece length is **90 mm** and the overtravel is **15 mm**. Determine:
1. The time required to complete one full slot.
2. The mean cutting velocity.
3. The MRR if the depth of cut is **4 mm** and the tool width is **6 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — Question B**

**Description (2 marks):**
The Whitworth quick-return mechanism consists of a **driving crank disc** of radius $R$ rotating about a fixed axis, and a **slotted link (lever)** pivoted at a fixed point $O$ on the column. The crank pin engages the slot. As the crank rotates, the slot slides on the pin, oscillating the slotted link and driving the ram vertically.

**Sketch — Conceptual (verbal description, 2 marks):**
Draw the crank disc with the pin off-centre. Show the slotted link pivoted at $O$, with the slot enveloping the pin. Indicate the cutting arc (larger, ≈ 200°) and the return arc (smaller, ≈ 160°). Show the ram attached to the slotted link at the far end.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Let $\omega$ be the uniform angular velocity of the crank. The total time for one revolution is:

$$
T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}
$$

Let $\alpha_{c}$ be the **cutting angle** (in radians) swept by the crank during the cutting stroke, and $\alpha_{r}$ be the **return angle** during the return stroke. Then:

$$
\alpha_{c} + \alpha_{r} = 2\pi
$$

The cutting time is:

$$
T_{c} = \frac{\alpha_{c}}{\omega}
$$

The return time is:

$$
T_{r} = \frac{\alpha_{r}}{\omega}
$$

The **quick-return ratio** $q$ is defined as:

$$
\boxed{q = \frac{T_{c}}{T_{r}} = \frac{\alpha_{c}}{\alpha_{r}}}
$$

Using the constraint $\alpha_{c} + \alpha_{r} = 2\pi$, we obtain:

$$
\alpha_{c} = \frac{2\pi \cdot q}{1+q}, \quad \alpha_{r} = \frac{2\pi}{1+q}
$$

The cutting stroke time is:

$$
T_{c} = \frac{2\pi \cdot q}{\omega (1+q)}
$$

The return stroke time is:

$$
T_{r} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega (1+q)}
$$

When $q = 2$, the cutting angle is $\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$ rad (240°) and the return angle is $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ rad (120°). The cutting time is twice the return time, which is the desired effect of the quick-return mechanism.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — Question B**

**Given:**
- $L = 175$ mm, $N = 35$ spm
- $s = 0.5$ mm/stroke, $q = 2.5$
- $L_{w} = 90$ mm, $L_{1} = 15$ mm
- $d = 4$ mm, $w = 6$ mm

**(1) Total machining time (3 marks):**

$$
T_{m} = \frac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{s \cdot N} \cdot \frac{1+q}{q}
$$

$$
T_{m} = \frac{90 + 15}{0.5 \cdot 35} \cdot \frac{1+2.5}{2.5}
$$

$$
T_{m} = \frac{105}{17.5} \cdot \frac{3.5}{2.5}
$$

$$
T_{m} = 6.0 \cdot 1.4 = 8.4 \;\; \text{minutes}
$$

**(2) Mean cutting velocity (2 marks):**

$$
V = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{2 \cdot 175 \cdot 35}{1000} = \frac{12250}{1000} = 12.25 \;\; \text{m/min}
$$

**(3) Material Removal Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MRR} = d \cdot w \cdot s \cdot N
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 4 \cdot 6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 35
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 420 \;\; \text{mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $T_{m} = 8.4$ min
- $V = 12.25$ m/min
- $\text{MRR} = 420 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 2 Slotting):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the quick-return correction factor $(1+q)/q$.** Many students write $T_{m} = (L_{w}+L_{1})/(sN)$ and lose **2 to 3 marks** in part (b) numericals. Always check whether the question specifies a quick-return ratio — if yes, apply the correction.
>
> 2. **Unit mistakes in cutting velocity.** The formula $V = 2 L N / 1000$ gives m/min *only* if $L$ is in mm and $N$ in strokes per minute. Students often forget the **/1000** conversion from mm to m, leading to dimensional errors.
>
> 3. **Confusing slotter with shaper in part (a) theory questions.** A KTU examiner deducts marks for: (a) drawing a horizontal ram, (b) calling the tool motion a "forward stroke" instead of "downward stroke", (c) describing the worktable as a "reciprocating table" (it is rotary/cross-sliding in a slotter, not reciprocating).
>
> 4. **Missing the clapper box explanation.** A full-mark answer must mention that the **clapper box lifts the tool on the return stroke** — students often write only "the clapper box holds the tool", which is incomplete.
>
> 5. **MRR formula confusion.** Some students use $A = d \times s$ (depth × feed) instead of $A = d \times w$ (depth × width). For slotting, the **chip cross-section is depth × tool width** (since feed $s$ is the linear table feed, not the chip thickness). Be careful with the geometry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density, last-minute revision summary for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Slotting Machines:**

- ✅ **Slotter = Vertical Shaper.** Ram moves vertically (down) for cutting, up for return.
- ✅ **Used for internal slots, keyways, splines, dies, and irregular internal profiles** — operations impossible on a horizontal shaper or planer.
- ✅ **Seven main parts:** Base, Column, Ram, Tool Head, Clapper Box, Saddle & Cross-Slide, Universal Work Table.
- ✅ **Clapper Box** — hinged tool holder that **lifts the tool on the return stroke** to prevent rubbing and improve finish.
- ✅ **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism** — crank + slotted link; ratio $q = \alpha_{c}/\alpha_{r}$; typically 1.5 to 3.
- ✅ **Stroke length $L = 2R$** where $R$ is crank radius.
- ✅ **Stroke position adjusted** by rotating the crank disc on its shaft (changes the phase of crank pin).
- ✅ **Feed** is **intermittent**, applied at the **top dead centre** (TDC) at the end of the return stroke.
- ✅ **Feed is delivered** by a ratchet-and-pawl or epicyclic gearbox driven from the crank.
- ✅ **Master Formulas (must memorise):**
    - $V = \dfrac{2 L N}{1000}$ m/min
    - $T_{m} = \dfrac{L_{w} + L_{1}}{s \cdot N} \cdot \dfrac{1+q}{q}$ min
    - $\text{MRR} = d \cdot w \cdot s \cdot N$ mm³/min
- ✅ **Universal table can rotate 360° and tilt up to 45°** — enables machining of circular and angular features.
- ✅ **Slotter vs Shaper:** vertical vs horizontal ram; internal vs external cutting zone; rotary/cross-sliding table vs reciprocating table.
- ✅ **For KTU 2024 ESE:** Part (a) expects a labelled diagram (≥ 6 parts) + working principle + clapper box + quick-return mechanism. Part (b) is a numerical on $V$, $T_m$, and MRR.
- ✅ **Units check is critical:** $L$ in **mm**, $N$ in **spm**, $V$ in **m/min**, MRR in **mm³/min**, $T_m$ in **min**.
- ✅ **Quick-return correction factor $(1+q)/q \geq 1$** — multiplying the naive time by this factor accounts for the fast return stroke.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broaching Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Broaching Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Broaching? (Formal KTU Definition)

**Broaching** is a precision, multiple-tooth, single-pass linear machining process in which material is progressively removed by a hardened, multi-tooth bar (called a *broach*) that slides or is pulled across the workpiece surface. Each tooth on the broach takes a successive, small chip, with each succeeding tooth being slightly larger (in height/diameter) than the preceding one.

A **Broaching Machine** is the specialized production equipment that provides the rigid guidance, controlled linear motion, and high cutting force capacity required to drive the broach through (or past) the workpiece in a single stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Broaching is classified under *Linear Multi-Point Metal Cutting Processes* along with shaping, planing, and sawing. Unlike shaping, broaching is fundamentally a **finishing-to-precision process** producing surface finishes as fine as **0.2 μm Ra** and tolerances of **±0.01 mm**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sequential Filing Process"

Imagine you are filing a square hole into a steel block. A single coarse file removes a lot but leaves a rough surface. To get a smooth, accurate hole, you would file, then file again, then file again, each time with a finer, slightly larger file.

A **broach** does this in *one single stroke*. The broach is essentially **a stack of files welded onto a single rigid bar** — the first tooth (roughing) is small, the last tooth (finishing) is the exact size of the desired hole, and every tooth in between is slightly larger than the one before it. So in **one pass**, you get a rough cut, a semi-finish, and a precision finish simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Single-Pass = High Productivity:** Because the entire profile is generated in one stroke, broaching is **10–30 times faster** than milling or shaping for internal keyways, splines, and irregular profiles. This makes it the *go-to* process for mass production of automotive gears, rifle barrels, and aircraft engine hubs.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Broach** | Multi-point, linear cutting tool with progressively rising teeth. |
| **Broaching** | The machining operation performed by a broach. |
| **Rake Angle (α)** | Angle of the cutting tooth face; usually **15°** (steel), **8–12°** (cast iron), **0–5°** (hardened steel). |
| **Clearance Angle (γ)** | Relief behind the cutting edge; typically **1°–2°**. |
| **Tooth Rise (h)** | Incremental radial increase per tooth (= depth of cut per tooth). |
| **Pitch (p)** | Linear distance between adjacent teeth. |
| **Rabbet** | Recess between teeth to allow chip storage. |
| **Land** | Narrow flat behind the cutting edge providing tooth strength. |
| **Pull End / Push End** | The end of the broach connected to the machine ram. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Broach Tool

A standard broach is a *linear* tool — every tooth is offset by a small increment from the previous one. The broach is divided into **three functional sections**:

### A. Roughing Section (≈ 50–60% of broach length)
- Each tooth removes the **bulk of the stock**.
- Tooth rise (h) is **large** (typically **0.05 mm to 0.20 mm** per tooth on diameter).
- Tooth shape: **Sharp, hooked, or pointed** with deep gullets for chip clearance.
- Pitch is **larger** to handle bulky chips.

### B. Semi-Finishing (Intermediate) Section
- Tooth rise is **reduced** (≈ **0.02–0.05 mm** per tooth).
- Begins to establish the final geometry.
- Usually 3–6 teeth.

### C. Finishing Section
- Each tooth removes only **0.005–0.025 mm** (often the *last 3–5 teeth* are the same size — called **"burnishing teeth"**).
- Produces final dimension and surface finish.
- The last tooth is called the **"sizing tooth"** or **"calibrating tooth"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a numerical problem gives a broach with 30 teeth, assume: **Roughing = first 60% (18 teeth), Semi-finishing = next 25% (≈ 7 teeth), Finishing = last 15% (≈ 5 teeth)** unless otherwise stated.

---

## 2.2 Types of Broaching Machines (KTU High-Yield Classification)

### By Orientation
| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Broaching Machine** | Broach moves horizontally; most common type | Internal keyways, splines |
| **Vertical Broaching Machine** | Broach moves vertically (up or down) | Large workpieces, external surfaces |

### By Broach Motion vs Workpiece
- **Pull-Down** (vertical) — broach pulled down through stationary workpiece.
- **Push-Up / Push-Down** — broach pushed through workpiece (shorter broaches).
- **Surface (Straddle) Broaching** — workpiece moves past a stationary broach (for flat surfaces, e.g., dovetails).

### By Operation Type
1. **Internal Broaching** — Internal shapes (keyways, splines, holes).
2. **External Broaching** — External profiles (gear teeth, dovetails).
3. **Surface Broaching** — Flat or contoured surfaces.
4. **Continuous (Chain) Broaching** — Workpiece clamped to a chain that pulls it continuously past multiple broaches (mass production).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table covers every formula KTU has asked in broaching numericals from 2018–2024.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutting Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/min | $D$ = diameter, $N$ = rpm; usually broach has *linear* speed, not rotary. For linear: $V = L/t$ |
| 2 | **Linear Broach Speed** | $V_b = \dfrac{L_b}{t}$ | m/min | $L_b$ = broach stroke length, $t$ = time per stroke |
| 3 | **Material Removal Rate (per tooth)** | $MRR_t = w \cdot h \cdot V_b$ | mm³/min | $w$ = width of cut, $h$ = tooth rise |
| 4 | **Total MRR (multi-tooth)** | $MRR = n_a \cdot w \cdot h \cdot V_b$ | mm³/min | $n_a$ = number of teeth *simultaneously engaged* |
| 5 | **Cutting Force (per tooth)** | $F_t = K_s \cdot w \cdot h$ | N | $K_s$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 6 | **Total Broaching Force** | $F_b = n_a \cdot K_s \cdot w \cdot h$ | N | Sum of all engaged teeth |
| 7 | **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{F_b \cdot V_b}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | Output power; divide by efficiency for motor power |
| 8 | **Broach Length (Total)** | $L = n \cdot p + L_1 + L_2$ | mm | See derivation in §3 |
| 9 | **Pitch of Teeth** | $p = c \sqrt{L_g}$ | mm | $L_g$ = length of cut, $c = 0.35$ (HSS), $0.5$ for carbide |
| 10 | **Optimum Pitch (Standard Rule)** | $p = \sqrt{L}$ | mm | Simple rule when $L$ in mm; for short broaches |
| 11 | **Number of Engaged Teeth** | $n_a = \dfrac{W}{p}$ | — | $W$ = length of workpiece (or width of cut zone) |
| 12 | **Time per Stroke** | $t_s = \dfrac{L_s}{V_b}$ | min | $L_s$ = stroke length |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** $K_s$ is given in **N/mm²** (NOT N/m²). Typical values: Mild steel → **2000 N/mm²**, Cast iron → **1500 N/mm²**, Aluminium → **700 N/mm²**, Hardened steel → **3000–4000 N/mm²**.

---

## 2.4 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Workpiece is clamped** rigidly on the machine table (or in a fixture).
2. **Broach is mounted** on the ram (pull end for internal broaching).
3. **Ram actuates** (hydraulic or mechanical drive) and pulls/pushes the broach through the workpiece.
4. The **first (roughing) tooth** engages, removes a small chip.
5. The broach **advances by one pitch** — the second tooth (larger by *h*) engages.
6. This process continues for **all n teeth** in a single stroke.
7. The **last tooth** defines the final dimension.
8. **Return stroke** — broach returns to start position (workpiece removed or advanced to next station in continuous broaching).

> **Engineering Utility:** Broaching is the only process capable of producing **internal non-circular shapes (square/hex holes, splines, keyways)** economically in mass production — milling cannot easily produce these without specialized cutters.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Length of a Broach Tool

The total length of a broach $L$ is the sum of the **cutting length**, **approach length**, and **rear (pull-end) length**.

$$
L = L_c + L_a + L_r
$$

Where:
- $L_c$ = length of cutting section = $n \cdot p$
- $n$ = total number of cutting teeth
- $p$ = pitch of teeth
- $L_a$ = approach length (≈ **25–50 mm** for pull end or lead section)
- $L_r$ = rear shank length (≈ **50–100 mm** for pulling / guiding)

Therefore:

$$
L = n \cdot p + L_a + L_r
$$

### Standard KTU Compact Form (for plug-type internal broach):
$$
L = n \cdot p + (2 \text{ to } 3)\,t + t_c
$$

Where $t$ = tooth thickness, $t_c$ = total rise (final tooth oversize).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Style — 7 Mark Standard)

### **Problem:**
A horizontal pull-down broaching machine is used to broach a **cast iron** workpiece of length $W = 75 \text{ mm}$ and width $w = 12 \text{ mm}$. The broach has **25 teeth**, with a **pitch of 8 mm**, and a **tooth rise of 0.05 mm**. Specific cutting force $K_s = 1500 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Broaching speed $V_b = 6 \text{ m/min}$. Calculate:
(a) Number of teeth engaged simultaneously
(b) Total broaching force
(c) Power required
(d) MRR

### **Step 1 — Number of teeth engaged ($n_a$)**

Teeth engaged = length of workpiece / pitch.

$$
n_a = \frac{W}{p} = \frac{75}{8} = 9.375
$$

We take the **ceiling** (whole tooth must engage): $n_a = 10$ teeth.

### **Step 2 — Total Broaching Force ($F_b$)**

$$
F_b = n_a \cdot K_s \cdot w \cdot h
$$

$$
F_b = 10 \times 1500 \times 12 \times 0.05
$$

$$
F_b = 10 \times 900 = 9000 \text{ N} = 9 \text{ kN}
$$

### **Step 3 — Power Required ($P$)**

$$
P = \frac{F_b \cdot V_b}{60 \times 1000} \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P = \frac{9000 \times 6}{60000} = 0.9 \text{ kW}
$$

If machine efficiency $\eta = 0.85$:

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{0.9}{0.85} = 1.058 \text{ kW} \approx 1.06 \text{ kW}
$$

### **Step 4 — Material Removal Rate (MRR)**

$$
MRR = n_a \cdot w \cdot h \cdot V_b
$$

$$
MRR = 10 \times 12 \times 0.05 \times 6 \times 1000 = 36000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 36 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $n_a = 10$, $F_b = 9 \text{ kN}$, $P = 0.9 \text{ kW}$, $MRR = 36 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Pitch

For a broach cutting a length $L_g$, the pitch must be large enough so that **each tooth's chip can be accommodated in the gullet** (rabbet). The empirical rule (widely used in KTU exams):

$$
p = c \sqrt{L_g}
$$

Where:
- $p$ = pitch (mm)
- $L_g$ = length of cut (mm)
- $c$ = constant: **0.35 for HSS, 0.5 for carbide broaches**

**Why the square root?** Chip volume per tooth scales linearly with $L_g$ (longer cut = more material per tooth), and chip cross-section must be cleared — geometry gives a $\sqrt{L_g}$ scaling.

**Example:** $L_g = 100 \text{ mm}$, HSS broach → $p = 0.35 \times \sqrt{100} = 3.5 \text{ mm}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Broach Parameters

| Workpiece Material | Tooth Rise (h) mm | Rake Angle (α) | Clearance (γ) | Pitch Factor (c) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 0.05 – 0.10 | 15° – 20° | 1.5° | 0.35 |
| Cast Iron | 0.05 – 0.15 | 8° – 12° | 1° | 0.35 |
| Hardened Steel | 0.02 – 0.05 | 0° – 5° | 1° | 0.30 |
| Aluminium | 0.05 – 0.20 | 25° – 30° | 2° | 0.40 |
| Brass | 0.05 – 0.15 | 0° – 5° | 1° | 0.35 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** When the question says "a broaching operation on mild steel," examiners expect you to use $K_s = 2000 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and pitch factor $c = 0.35$ — do not assume different values unless stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Broach Tool Anatomy (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pull End - Shank] --> B[Front Pilot / Lead Section]
    B --> C[Roughing Teeth - 60 percent of teeth, large h]
    C --> D[Semi-Finishing Teeth - 25 percent, reduced h]
    D --> E[Finishing Teeth - 10 percent, fine h]
    E --> F[Sizing / Burnishing Teeth - 5 percent, zero rise]
    F --> G[Rear End / Back Shank]

    subgraph RS[Roughing Tooth]
        C1[Crest rises by h] --> C2[Deep gullet - chip storage]
        C2 --> C3[Hook-type geometry]
    end

    subgraph FS[Finishing Tooth]
        E1[Flat crest, narrow land] --> E2[Shallow gullet]
        E2 --> E3[Zero or negative rake option]
    end
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Horizontal Pull-Down Broaching Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Motor 3 phase AC] --> B[Hydraulic Pump Unit]
    B --> C[Control Valves Directional and Flow]
    C --> D[Hydraulic Cylinder Double acting]
    D --> E[Ram - Heavy duty slide]
    E --> F[Broach Tool Mounted on Ram]
    F --> G[Workpiece Clamped on Fixture]
    G --> H[Chip Tray and Coolant Nozzle]
    D --> I[Return Stroke Cylinder]

    subgraph W[Work Holding]
        G1[Floating Fixture] --> G2[Hydraulic Clamp]
        G2 --> G3[Locating Pin]
    end
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Sequential Tooth Engagement

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant W as Workpiece
    participant T1 as Tooth 1 Rough
    participant T2 as Tooth 2 Rough
    participant T3 as Tooth n Finishing
    participant S as Sizing Tooth

    Note over W,S: Broach moves LEFT to RIGHT, single stroke

    T1->>W: Engages, removes 0.10 mm chip
    T2->>W: Engages after pitch p, removes 0.10 mm chip
    T3->>W: Engages, semi-finish, 0.03 mm chip
    S->>W: Final pass, 0.005 mm, defines size and finish

    W-->>W: Final profile = exact size of last tooth crest
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree — Types of Broaching Machines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BM[Broaching Machines]

    BM --> HOR[Horizontal]
    BM --> VER[Vertical]

    HOR --> HPULL[Pull Type Internal]
    HOR --> HSURF[Surface Straddle]

    VER --> VDOWN[Pull Down Internal]
    VER --> VUP[Push Up Internal]
    VER --> VEXT[External Vertical]

    BM --> CONT[Continuous Chain Type]
    BM --> ROT[Rotary Broach]

    VDOWN --> APP1[Gear Splines, Keyways]
    VUP --> APP2[Through Holes Bushings]
    HSURF --> APP3[Dovetails, Flat Slots]
    CONT --> APP4[Mass Production, Auto Parts]
```

---
