<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define broaching. List any four advantages of the broaching process.**

**Model Answer:**

**Broaching** is a multiple-tooth, single-pass, linear metal cutting process in which a hardened multi-tooth tool (the *broach*) is pulled or pushed across a workpiece surface to progressively remove material and produce a desired profile.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **High productivity** — complex profiles produced in a single stroke (10–30× faster than milling).
2. **Excellent surface finish** — up to **0.2 μm Ra** achievable.
3. **High dimensional accuracy** — tolerances of **±0.01 mm** possible.
4. **Capable of internal non-circular shapes** — splines, keyways, square/hex holes that other processes cannot produce economically.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between push broaching and pull broaching (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Push Broaching | Pull Broaching |
|---|---|---|
| **Force capacity** | Lower (broach may buckle) | Higher (broach under tension) |
| **Broach length** | Short (≤ 3× diameter) | Long (up to 1.5 m) |
| **Accuracy** | Lower | Higher (rigid pulling action) |
| **Typical use** | Soft materials, short splines, blind holes | Hard materials, long keyways, through holes |
| **Broach cross-section** | Larger, stouter design needed | Thinner section can be used |
| **Surface finish** | Slightly inferior | Superior |

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a horizontal pull-type broaching machine**. Discuss the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A broach of **20 teeth** is used to cut a **mild steel** workpiece of length **60 mm** and width **15 mm**. The tooth rise is **0.06 mm**, pitch is **6 mm**, and broaching speed is **8 m/min**. Specific cutting force for mild steel is **2000 N/mm²**. Calculate:
   (i) Number of teeth in contact
   (ii) Total cutting force
   (iii) Power required
   (iv) MRR in cm³/min **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Construction & Working**

**Construction (Key Components):**

1. **Bed/Base:** Heavy cast iron base supporting the entire machine; absorbs vibration.
2. **Worktable:** Rigid table on which the workpiece is clamped via hydraulic fixture.
3. **Ram:** A long, heavy slide that holds the broach and moves linearly under high force.
4. **Hydraulic Cylinder:** Provides the pulling force (typically 50–200 kN capacity).
5. **Broach Support / Bushings:** Multiple supports along the ram travel to prevent broach deflection.
6. **Pull End Adapter:** Connects the broach shank to the ram via a quick-release coupling.
7. **Coolant System:** Floods cutting zone with cutting oil (essential in broaching).
8. **Electrical Control Panel:** Stroke length, return speed, auto-cycle timing.

**Working:**
- Workpiece is mounted on the table and aligned with the broach axis.
- Broach is attached to the pull end of the ram.
- Hydraulic cylinder actuates, pulling the broach through the workpiece.
- Each tooth removes a small increment; the last tooth defines final size.
- After completion, the ram returns to the start position (rapid return stroke).
- Coolant flows continuously to flush chips and reduce heat.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $n_{total} = 20$ teeth, $W = 60$ mm, $w = 15$ mm, $h = 0.06$ mm, $p = 6$ mm
- $V_b = 8$ m/min, $K_s = 2000$ N/mm² (mild steel)

**(i) Number of teeth in contact:**

$$
n_a = \frac{W}{p} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ teeth}
$$

**(ii) Total cutting force:**

$$
F_b = n_a \cdot K_s \cdot w \cdot h
$$

$$
F_b = 10 \times 2000 \times 15 \times 0.06
$$

$$
F_b = 10 \times 1800 = 18000 \text{ N} = 18 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Power required:**

$$
P = \frac{F_b \cdot V_b}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{18000 \times 8}{60000} = 2.4 \text{ kW}
$$

With $\eta = 0.85$: $P_{motor} = 2.4 / 0.85 = 2.82$ kW

**(iv) MRR:**

$$
MRR = n_a \cdot w \cdot h \cdot V_b
$$

$$
MRR = 10 \times 15 \times 0.06 \times 8 \times 1000 = 72000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
MRR = 72 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Resit Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of broaching machines** with suitable diagrams. Discuss the applications of each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **vertical pull-down broaching machine** is to be designed to cut a **keyway** in a **cast iron** hub. The keyway length is **40 mm**, width **10 mm**, depth **5 mm**. Calculate:
   (i) The number of cutting teeth required if pitch is **5 mm** and roughing rise is **0.10 mm**.
   (ii) The broach length if the total stock to be removed is **5 mm**.
   (iii) The total cutting force if $K_s = 1500$ N/mm² and **5 teeth** engage simultaneously.
   (iv) The broaching time for a stroke length of **800 mm** at **6 m/min**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Broaching Machines**

**Classification (with applications):**

1. **Horizontal vs Vertical:**
   - *Horizontal:* Most common, for internal broaching of small-medium parts.
   - *Vertical:* For large/heavy workpieces where gravity helps broach alignment.

2. **By Operation:**
   - **Internal broaching** — Keyways, splines, internal gears. Application: gearboxes, automotive hubs.
   - **External broaching** — Gear teeth, serrations, dovetails. Application: machine tool slides, rifle components.
   - **Surface broaching** — Flat/contoured surfaces. Application: dovetail slides, engine blocks.
   - **Continuous (chain) broaching** — High-volume mass production. Application: connecting rods, piston pins.

3. **By Drive Mechanism:**
   - Hydraulic (most common), Mechanical (crank-driven, for small strokes), Pneumatic (low-force applications).

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $L_g = 40$ mm, $w = 10$ mm, $d = 5$ mm, $p = 5$ mm, $h = 0.10$ mm, $K_s = 1500$ N/mm², $n_a = 5$

**(i) Number of cutting teeth:**

Each tooth removes $h = 0.10$ mm. Total stock to remove = **5 mm** (depth).
Allowing some for finishing teeth (assume last 2 teeth do **0.05 mm** each):

$$
\text{Roughing teeth} = \frac{5 - (2 \times 0.05)}{0.10} = 49 \text{ teeth}
$$

Total teeth = $49 + 2 = 51$ (approx., include lead and finishing).

**Simpler KTU interpretation:**

$$
n = \frac{\text{Total rise}}{h} = \frac{5}{0.10} = 50 \text{ teeth} \quad (\text{approx.})
$$

**(ii) Broach length:**

$$
L = n \cdot p + L_a + L_r
$$

Assuming $L_a = 30$ mm (approach) and $L_r = 75$ mm (rear shank):

$$
L = (50 \times 5) + 30 + 75 = 250 + 105 = 355 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Total cutting force:**

$$
F_b = n_a \cdot K_s \cdot w \cdot h = 5 \times 1500 \times 10 \times 0.10 = 7500 \text{ N} = 7.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iv) Broaching time:**

$$
t_s = \frac{L_s}{V_b} = \frac{800 \text{ mm}}{6 \text{ m/min}} = \frac{0.8 \text{ m}}{6 \text{ m/min}} = 0.1333 \text{ min} = 8 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 2–4 marks each:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $V_b$ units** — m/min must be converted to mm/s by ×1000/60 when computing power. Using m/min directly in $\frac{F \times V}{60}$ still gives kW correctly, but slipping up with seconds/minutes is the #1 error.
> 2. **Not taking the ceiling for $n_a$** — if $W/p = 9.3$, you must use **10**, not 9. A partial tooth cannot fully engage.
> 3. **Mixing up pitch and tooth rise** — pitch is *between teeth* (linear spacing); tooth rise is *radial/height increment*. They are different.
> 4. **Using $K_s$ in N/m² instead of N/mm²** — answer will be off by a factor of $10^6$.
> 5. **Omitting the approach and rear shank lengths** in broach length numericals.
> 6. **Not mentioning coolant** in long descriptive answers — KTU examiner's rubric specifically allocates 1 mark for "auxiliary systems."

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the exam.**

- [x] Broaching = **single-pass, multi-tooth, linear** machining process.
- [x] Three sections of a broach: **Roughing (60%), Semi-finishing (25%), Finishing (15%)**.
- [x] **Pull broaching > Push broaching** in force capacity and accuracy.
- [x] **Key formulas (memorize verbatim):**
  - $F_b = n_a \cdot K_s \cdot w \cdot h$
  - $P = F_b \cdot V_b / 60000$ (kW)
  - $n_a = W / p$ (always round **up**)
  - $p = 0.35 \sqrt{L_g}$ (HSS) or $0.5\sqrt{L_g}$ (carbide)
  - $L_{broach} = n \cdot p + L_a + L_r$
- [x] $K_s$ values: Mild steel = **2000**, Cast iron = **1500**, Aluminium = **700**, Hardened steel = **3000–4000** N/mm².
- [x] Surface finish achievable: **0.2–0.8 μm Ra**; tolerance: **±0.01 mm**.
- [x] Major applications: **splines, keyways, internal gears, square/hex holes, dovetails**.
- [x] Broaching is the **only economical process** for internal non-circular profiles.
- [x] Vertical machines handle heavier workpieces; horizontal machines are more common.
- [x] **Continuous (chain) broaching** is used for high-volume automotive mass production.
- [x] HSS is standard broach material; carbide used for **abrasive materials and high production**.
- [x] Coolant is **mandatory** — chips must be flushed continuously from deep gullets.
- [x] Tooth rise (h) order: roughing > semi-finishing > finishing (0.10 > 0.05 > 0.005 mm typical).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uses and Specific Applications. Grinding Machines - Classification - Operations - Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uses and Specific Applications. Grinding Machines - Classification - Operations - Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Grinding

**Grinding** is a material removal process in which a **rotating abrasive wheel** acts as a multi-point cutting tool, removing microscopic chips (swarf) from a workpiece to achieve **high dimensional accuracy**, **superior surface finish (Ra ≤ 0.8 µm)**, and **tight geometric tolerances** (typically **± 0.005 mm**). It belongs to the family of **abrasive machining processes**, characterized by the use of hard, irregularly-shaped abrasive grains bonded together.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Grinding is the most accurate and finishing-oriented machining process. It is the **only practical method** to machine **hardened steels (≥ 60 HRC)** and **brittle materials** like ceramics and glass with acceptable surface integrity.

### 1.2 The "Sandpaper on a Treadmill" Analogy

Imagine dragging a piece of wood across coarse sandpaper (workpiece moving, abrasive fixed). Now imagine spinning the sandpaper at **30 m/s** while dragging the wood slowly under it — that's exactly how a **horizontal spindle surface grinder** works.

- The **sandpaper** = grinding wheel (hundreds of thousands of abrasive grains bonded).
- The **wood** = workpiece.
- The **"scratches"** = chips (each grain acts like a tiny single-point tool).
- The **spin speed** = wheel surface speed ($v_s$), measured in m/s.
- The **drag speed** = table speed ($v_w$), measured in m/min.

### 1.3 Why Use Grinding? — Uses and Specific Applications

| Domain | Specific Application | Why Grinding? |
|---|---|---|
| Tool Room | Sharpening HSS & carbide tools | Edge radius < 5 µm achievable |
| Automotive | Cylinder bore finishing, crankshaft journals | Roundness ≤ 2 µm, Ra ≤ 0.4 µm |
| Bearing Industry | Raceway finishing of ball/roller bearings | Sub-micron form accuracy |
| Die & Mould | Hardened die cavity finishing | Only process for > 60 HRC steels |
| Medical Implants | Hip stem, knee prosthesis spherical surfaces | Mirror finish, no subsurface damage |
| Aerospace | Turbine blade roots, combustor liners | Heat-resistant super-alloys (Inconel) |
| Glass & Ceramics | Edging, lens shaping | Diamond wheels cut non-metallics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific applications of grinding wheel and abrasive tools:**
> - **Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃):** Soft steels, tool steels.
> - **Silicon Carbide (SiC):** Cast iron, non-ferrous, carbide, stone.
> - **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN):** Hardened steels (> 50 HRC).
> - **Diamond:** Ceramics, glass, cemented carbide, stone.

### 1.4 Classification of Grinding Machines (KTU High-Yield)

Grinding machines are classified primarily by the **shape of the workpiece surface** they produce and the **orientation of the grinding wheel spindle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification Table — KTU Favourite**

**A. Based on Workpiece Geometry:**
1. **Surface Grinder** — Flat surfaces.
2. **Cylindrical Grinder** — External cylindrical/ conical surfaces.
3. **Centerless Grinder** — Cylindrical without centres.
4. **Internal Grinder** — Internal bores.
5. **Tool & Cutter Grinder** — Cutting tool geometry.
6. **Thread Grinder** — Threads.
7. **Form Grinder** — Profile grinding.
8. **Jig Grinder** — Holes in hardened jigs.

**B. Based on Spindle Orientation:**
- **Horizontal Spindle (HSG / HSRC):** Wheel axis horizontal.
- **Vertical Spindle (VSG):** Wheel axis vertical.

### 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Must-Know)

- **Wheel surface speed:** $v_s$ = **30 m/s** typical, max **60 m/s** for CBN.
- **Workpiece speed:** $v_w$ = **0.5 – 30 m/min**.
- **Depth of cut:** $a$ (radial infeed) = **0.005 – 0.05 mm/pass**.
- **Spark-out passes:** 2–5 (no infeed) for dimensional stability.
- **Coolant:** Water-based emulsion, flow rate **10–20 L/min**.
- **Specific energy:** **20–100 J/mm³** (10–50× higher than turning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Material Removal in Grinding

Each abrasive grain on the wheel periphery behaves as a **tiny, negatively-raked cutting tool** with a **negative rake angle (γ) ranging from −30° to −60°**. Material removal occurs in three sequential stages:

1. **Rubbing:** Elastic-plastic deformation, no chip formation, generates heat.
2. **Ploughing:** Plastic flow of material to the sides of the groove, no chip.
3. **Cutting:** Actual chip formation occurs only in this stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Three-Zone Model"** — At any instant, only **1–10 %** of active grits are in the **cutting zone**. The rest are in rubbing/ploughing. This is why grinding is **energy-inefficient** but produces superior finish.

### 2.2 Surface Grinding — Operational Anatomy

**Surface grinding** produces a flat surface by feeding the workpiece **past** (or **under**) a rotating grinding wheel. The cross-feed is **perpendicular** to the table traverse.

**Two main configurations:**

| Configuration | Wheel Spindle | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating Table (HSRG / Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Grinder)** | Horizontal | Small to medium workpieces, high accuracy flat surfaces |
| **Rotary Table (HSRG with rotary chuck)** | Horizontal | Mass production of flat parts |
| **Vertical Spindle Reciprocating Grinder (VSRG)** | Vertical | Heavy stock removal, creep-feed grinding |

**Kinematic chain in a surface grinder:**
- **Wheel rotation** ($v_s$) — primary motion.
- **Table traverse** ($v_w$) — feed motion (longitudinal).
- **Cross-feed** ($v_c$) — lateral motion (one tooth of indexing per stroke).
- **Vertical infeed** ($a$) — downfeed per pass.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula you must memorize for KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel surface speed | $v_s = \dfrac{\pi D_s N_s}{60 \times 1000}$ | m/s | $D_s$ = wheel dia (mm), $N_s$ = RPM |
| 2 | Workpiece speed | $v_w = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60 \times 1000}$ | m/min | For cylindrical grinding |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Z_w = v_w \cdot a \cdot b$ | mm³/min | $a$ = depth, $b$ = width (for surface) |
| 4 | MRR (cylindrical traverse) | $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot L \cdot f_r \cdot n_w$ | mm³/min | $f_r$ = radial infeed, $n_w$ = rev/min |
| 5 | Specific Energy | $u = \dfrac{P}{Z_w}$ | J/mm³ | 20–100 J/mm³ for grinding |
| 6 | Grinding Power | $P = u \cdot Z_w$ | W or kW | Multiply by $\eta$ for spindle motor |
| 7 | Tangential Force | $F_t = \dfrac{P}{v_s}$ | N | From power balance |
| 8 | Normal Force | $F_n = k \cdot F_t$ | N | $k \approx 1.5$–$3$ (typically 2) |
| 9 | Grinding Ratio (G-ratio) | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s} = \dfrac{Z_w}{Z_s}$ | dimensionless | Wheel life indicator |
| 10 | Equivalent chip thickness | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{d_s}}$ | mm | Critical for surface finish |
| 11 | Surface Roughness (Ra) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h_{eq}}{4}$ to $\dfrac{h_{eq}}{3}$ | µm | Theoretical model |
| 12 | Equivalent diameter | $d_e = \dfrac{d_s \cdot d_w}{d_s \pm d_w}$ | mm | $+$ for internal, $-$ for external grinding |
| 13 | Number of grits per cm² (approx.) | $C \approx \dfrac{6 \times 10^5}{g^{1.5}}$ | grits/cm² | $g$ = grit size (mesh) |
| 14 | Spark-out time | $t_{so} = \dfrac{L}{v_w}$ for $k$ passes | min | $L$ = table travel |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High G-ratio** ⇒ lower wheel consumption ⇒ lower cost-per-part in **automotive crankshaft grinding** (target G > 100 with CBN).
- **High specific energy** ⇒ grinding is **non-economic for bulk removal**; instead use **turning, milling**.
- **Low $h_{eq}$** ⇒ mirror-like finish; exploited in **bearing raceway manufacturing**.

### 2.5 Concept Reinforcement: Why Negative Rake Causes Heat

In turning, the rake angle is **positive (+5° to +20°)**, allowing the chip to slide off easily. In grinding, the **negative rake** forces the grain to **plough** through the workpiece, with most of the energy converted to **heat** (over **60%** of total energy** = heat, only **10%** = actual chip formation, **20%** = wheel-work friction, **10%** = chip heat).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **scientific justification** for mandatory coolant supply in grinding operations — to prevent **thermal damage**, **residual tensile stresses**, and **white layer formation** on the workpiece surface.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Surface Grinding

**Given:**
- Workpiece width (cross-feed per stroke) = $b$ mm
- Depth of cut per pass = $a$ mm
- Table traverse speed = $v_w$ m/min

**Step 1:** Convert table speed to mm/min.
$$v_w (\text{mm/min}) = v_w (\text{m/min}) \times 1000$$

**Step 2:** Volume removed per minute = (cross-sectional area of cut) × (table travel per minute).

Cross-sectional area of cut (rectangular cross-section of workpiece groove):
$$A_c = a \times b \;\; (\text{mm}^2)$$

**Step 3:** MRR formula:
$$Z_w = A_c \times v_w = a \cdot b \cdot v_w$$

$$\boxed{Z_w = a \cdot b \cdot v_w \;\; (\text{mm}^3/\text{min})}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values (KTU Example): $a = 0.02$ mm, $b = 25$ mm, $v_w = 12$ m/min = 12000 mm/min.
$$Z_w = 0.02 \times 25 \times 12000$$
$$\boxed{Z_w = 6000 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 6 \;\; \text{cc/min}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Chip Thickness ($h_{eq}$)

**Conceptual start:** Each active grain removes a tiny chip of cross-section approximated by a triangle.

**Step 1:** Undeformed chip length per active grit = $l_c$.
By geometry of grain contact arc on the wheel:
$$l_c = \sqrt{a \cdot d_s}$$

where $d_s$ = wheel diameter, $a$ = depth of cut.

**Step 2:** Undeformed chip thickness for a SINGLE grain:
$$h_1 = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot l_c}$$

where:
- $v_w$ = workpiece speed
- $v_s$ = wheel surface speed
- $C$ = number of active grits per unit area on wheel periphery

**Step 3:** Substituting $l_c = \sqrt{a \cdot d_s}$:
$$h_1 = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{a \cdot d_s}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by the perimeter factor to get the **equivalent chip thickness**:
$$h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s} \sqrt{\dfrac{a}{d_s}}$$

$$\boxed{h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{d_s}}}$$

**Numerical example:** $v_w = 12$ m/min = **0.2 m/s**, $v_s = 30$ m/s, $a = 0.02$ mm, $d_s = 200$ mm.
$$h_{eq} = \dfrac{0.2}{30}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.02}{200}}$$
$$h_{eq} = 0.00667 \times \sqrt{0.0001}$$
$$h_{eq} = 0.00667 \times 0.01$$
$$\boxed{h_{eq} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{mm} = 66.7 \;\; \text{nm}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Surface Roughness (Ra) from Equivalent Chip Thickness

**Step 1:** Surface texture in grinding consists of a series of parallel grooves of triangular cross-section. The **peak-to-valley height** $R_t$ is the depth of one groove.

For grinding, geometrically:
$$R_t \approx h_{eq}$$

**Step 2:** For a triangular profile, the **arithmetical mean roughness** $R_a$ relates to $R_t$ as:
$$R_a = \dfrac{R_t}{4}$$

(For pure V-groove geometry; in practice, $R_a \approx R_t / 3$ to $R_t / 5$ due to grit geometry randomness.)

**Step 3:** Combining:
$$\boxed{R_a \approx \dfrac{h_{eq}}{4} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{v_w}{v_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{d_s}}}$$

**Numerical example (continued):**
$$R_a \approx \dfrac{66.7 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 16.7 \times 10^{-6} \;\; \text{mm} = 0.0167 \;\; \mu\text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU real-world correlation: $R_a = 0.0167 \;\mu\text{m}$ is in the **mirror-finish** range (N3 grade). This explains why fine-grinding of bearing raceways can achieve $R_a < 0.05 \;\mu\text{m}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Grinding Power and Specific Energy

**Step 1:** Tangential grinding force $F_t$ is related to MRR and specific energy:
$$P = u \cdot Z_w$$

where $u$ = specific energy (J/mm³). For mild steel grinding, $u \approx 30$ J/mm³.

**Step 2:** Numerical evaluation (using $Z_w = 6000$ mm³/min = 100 mm³/s):
$$P = 30 \;\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \times 100 \;\dfrac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}$$
$$P = 3000 \;\text{W} = 3 \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 3:** Tangential force:
$$F_t = \dfrac{P}{v_s} = \dfrac{3000}{30} = 100 \;\text{N}$$

**Step 4:** Normal force (using $k = 2$):
$$F_n = k \cdot F_t = 2 \times 100 = 200 \;\text{N}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Grinding Ratio (G-ratio)

**Definition:** Volume of material removed from the workpiece per unit volume of wheel wear.
$$G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$$

**Step 1:** Wheel wear volume $V_s$ per unit time = (peripheral area worn) × (width).
$$V_s = \pi D_s \cdot \dot{r}_w \cdot b$$

where $\dot{r}_w$ = radial wheel wear rate (mm³/min·mm).

**Step 2:** For steady-state grinding:
$$\boxed{G = \dfrac{Z_w}{\pi D_s \cdot \dot{r}_w \cdot b}}$$

**Numerical example:** $Z_w = 6000$ mm³/min, $D_s = 200$ mm, $\dot{r}_w = 0.001$ mm/min, $b = 25$ mm.
$$G = \dfrac{6000}{\pi \times 200 \times 0.001 \times 25} = \dfrac{6000}{15.71}$$
$$\boxed{G \approx 382}$$

A G-ratio of **382** is excellent — typical of **CBN grinding** of hardened steel.

---

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

| Problem Type | Key Equation to Apply | Common KTU Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Find wheel RPM for given $v_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{60 \times 1000 \times v_s}{\pi D_s}$ | Unit mismatch: $D_s$ in mm, $v_s$ in m/s |
| Compute MRR in traverse cylindrical grind | $Z_w = \pi D_w L f_r n_w$ | Forgetting factor $\pi$ |
| Surface finish from process params | $R_a \propto \dfrac{v_w}{v_s}\sqrt{a}$ | Confusing $a$ (depth) with $b$ (width) |
| Power estimation | $P = u Z_w / 60$ for kW | $Z_w$ in mm³/s vs mm³/min |
| Wheel selection for HRC > 60 | Use CBN or diamond | Picking Al₂O₃ (wear rate too high) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Grinding Machines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Grinding Machines] --> B[Based on Workpiece Geometry]
    A --> C[Based on Spindle Orientation]
    B --> B1[Surface Grinder]
    B --> B2[Cylindrical Grinder]
    B --> B3[Centerless Grinder]
    B --> B4[Internal Grinder]
    B --> B5[Tool and Cutter Grinder]
    B --> B6[Thread and Form Grinder]
    C --> C1[Horizontal Spindle]
    C --> C2[Vertical Spindle]
    B1 --> D1[Reciprocating Table]
    B1 --> D2[Rotary Table]
    B1 --> D3[Vertical Spindle Surface]
    C1 --> E1[HSRG Most Common]
    C1 --> E2[Plunge Cut Cylindrical]
    C2 --> F1[Rotary Table Surface]
    C2 --> F2[Creep Feed Grinder]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Horizontal Spindle Surface Grinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Train]
        PM[Main Motor 5 to 15 kW] --> WB[Wheel Spindle Bearings]
    end
    subgraph WHEEL[Wheel Assembly]
        WB --> GW[Grinding Wheel 200 to 400 mm]
        GW --> SG[Safety Guard]
    end
    subgraph TABLE[Workpiece Stage]
        TBL[Reciprocating Table] --> CHK[Electromagnetic Chuck]
        CHK --> WP[Workpiece]
    end
    subgraph FEED[Feed Systems]
        HM[Hydraulic Motor] --> TBL
        HFM[Hand Wheel Fine] --> TBL
        VIF[Vertical Infeed Screw] --> SAD[Slide Assembly Down]
        CF[Cross Feed Hand Wheel] --> SAD
    end
    subgraph COOLANT[Coolant System]
        CT[Tank 100 L] --> CP[Coolant Pump]
        CP --> NJ[Nozzle Directed at Contact Zone]
        NJ --> CT
    end
    GW -. contacts .-> WP
    NJ -. sprays at .-> WP
    WP -. swarf chips .-> CT
```

### 4.3 Process Kinematics Flowchart — Surface Grinding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START Machine] --> S2[Mount Wheel and Dress]
    S2 --> S3[True Wheel with Diamond Tool]
    S3 --> S4[Mount Workpiece on EM Chuck]
    S4 --> S5[Set Table Speed vw]
    S5 --> S6[Set Cross Feed per Stroke b]
    S6 --> S7[Engage Coolant Flow]
    S7 --> S8[Start Wheel Rotation]
    S8 --> S9[Lower Wheel to Touch Spark Test]
    S9 --> S10[Set Zero Reference]
    S10 --> S11[Apply Roughing Infeed a1]
    S11 --> S12[Reciprocate Table]
    S12 --> S13[Measure Dimension]
    S13 --> S14{Dimension Reached?}
    S14 -- No --> S15[Apply Finishing Infeed a2]
    S15 --> S16[Continue Reciprocation]
    S16 --> S13
    S14 -- Yes --> S17[Apply Spark Out 2 to 5 Passes]
    S17 --> S18[Retract Wheel]
    S18 --> S19[Stop Table and Wheel]
    S19 --> S20[Remove Workpiece and Inspect Ra]
    S20 --> END[END Cycle]
```

### 4.4 Abrasive Selection Decision Tree (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Workpiece Material} --> Q1[Ferrous Steel]
    Q --> Q2[Cast Iron or Non Ferrous]
    Q --> Q3[Hardened Steel HRC above 50]
    Q --> Q4[Carbide Ceramic Glass Stone]
    Q1 --> R1[Aluminium Oxide Al2O3]
    Q2 --> R2[Silicon Carbide SiC]
    Q3 --> R3[Cubic Boron Nitride CBN]
    Q4 --> R4[Diamond]
    R1 --> Z[Select Bond Vitrified for General Use]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z2[Select Resin or Metal Bond]
    R4 --> Z3[Select Metal or Resin Bond]
```

### 4.5 Wheel Specification Coding (KTU Vital)

Standard marking: **A 60 K 5 V 23**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Abrasive Type A] --> A2[Grit Size 60]
    A2 --> A3[Grade K Medium Soft]
    A3 --> A4[Structure 5 Dense]
    A4 --> A5[Bond Type V Vitrified]
    A5 --> A6[Manufacturer Code 23]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]**

**List and briefly explain any three specific applications of grinding machines in modern industry.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Tool and cutter grinding:** Used for resharpening HSS and carbide cutting tools, drills, milling cutters to restore cutting-edge geometry. Achieves edge radii below 5 µm.
2. **Crankshaft and camshaft grinding in automobiles:** Cylindrical grinding produces journals with roundness ≤ 2 µm and Ra ≤ 0.4 µm, ensuring dynamic balancing and minimal vibration.
3. **Hardened die and mould finishing:** After heat treatment (HRC ≥ 60), grinding is the only practical process that can remove material with the required accuracy. Mirror-finish EDM dies (Ra ≤ 0.05 µm) are produced using fine-grit diamond wheels.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2]**

**Differentiate between horizontal spindle and vertical spindle surface grinding machines.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Horizontal Spindle Surface Grinder (HSRG) | Vertical Spindle Surface Grinder (VSG) |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel axis | Horizontal | Vertical (perpendicular to table) |
| Wheel periphery | Used as cutting edge | Bottom face of cup wheel used |
| Best for | Precision flat surfaces, slots | Heavy stock removal, large area |
| Common operation | Reciprocating table grinding | Rotary table or creep-feed grinding |
| Coolant entry | Side nozzle | Top-down flood |
| Typical application | Tool-room jobs, dies | Mass-production of flat stock removal |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 internal choice is provided below.

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Classify grinding machines based on (i) workpiece geometry and (ii) spindle orientation, with neat sketches. Discuss the **kinematics** and **working principle** of a **horizontal spindle reciprocating table surface grinder**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a surface grinding operation, the following data are given:
- Wheel diameter $D_s$ = 250 mm
- Wheel speed $N_s$ = 1500 rpm
- Table speed $v_w$ = 15 m/min
- Cross-feed per stroke $b$ = 20 mm
- Depth of cut per pass $a$ = 0.025 mm
- Specific energy $u$ = 30 J/mm³
- Wheel wear rate = 0.0012 mm³/mm·min

Calculate: (i) Wheel surface speed $v_s$, (ii) Material removal rate $Z_w$, (iii) Grinding power $P$, (iv) Tangential force $F_t$, and (v) Grinding ratio $G$. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (A-a):

**Classification:**
**Based on workpiece geometry:**
- Surface grinder, cylindrical grinder, centerless grinder, internal grinder, tool and cutter grinder, thread grinder, jig grinder, form/profile grinder.

**Based on spindle orientation:**
- Horizontal spindle and vertical spindle.

**Sketch (HSRG):** Draw labelled diagram showing — wheel, wheel spindle (horizontal), reciprocating table below, electromagnetic chuck, workpiece, longitudinal traverse, cross-feed slide, vertical downfeed hand wheel, coolant nozzle, bed, base.

**Working Principle:**
- The grinding wheel rotates at high speed (primary motion $v_s$) driven by the main motor.
- The workpiece, mounted on the electromagnetic chuck, sits on the reciprocating table.
- The table traverses longitudinally (feed motion $v_w$) by hydraulic power.
- After each stroke, the wheel is cross-fed laterally by $b$ mm.
- Once the wheel covers the entire width, a vertical downfeed of $a$ mm is applied.
- Coolant is sprayed at the wheel-workpiece contact zone to dissipate heat.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (A-b):

**(i) Wheel surface speed:**
$$v_s = \dfrac{\pi D_s N_s}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 250 \times 1500}{60000}$$
$$\boxed{v_s = 19.63 \;\; \text{m/s}}$$

**(ii) Material Removal Rate:**
Convert $v_w$ to mm/min: 15 m/min = 15000 mm/min.
$$Z_w = a \cdot b \cdot v_w = 0.025 \times 20 \times 15000$$
$$\boxed{Z_w = 7500 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 125 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(iii) Grinding Power:**
$$P = u \cdot Z_w = 30 \;\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \times 125 \;\dfrac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}$$
$$\boxed{P = 3750 \;\text{W} = 3.75 \;\text{kW}}$$

**(iv) Tangential Force:**
$$F_t = \dfrac{P}{v_s} = \dfrac{3750}{19.63}$$
$$\boxed{F_t \approx 191 \;\text{N}}$$

**(v) Grinding Ratio:**
$$G = \dfrac{Z_w}{\pi D_s \cdot \dot{r}_w \cdot b} = \dfrac{7500}{\pi \times 250 \times 0.0012 \times 20}$$
$$G = \dfrac{7500}{18.85}$$
$$\boxed{G \approx 398}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> - Students often forget to convert **m/min to mm/min** before multiplying for MRR → lose 1 mark.
> - The formula $Z_w = a \cdot b \cdot v_w$ assumes **mm/min units** for all three variables. Mismatched units cause incorrect answers.
> - For Grinding Ratio, do not confuse **radial wheel wear rate (mm/min)** with **volume wear rate (mm³/min)**. The denominator must use volume.
> - Don't forget to **multiply $\pi$** in the G-ratio denominator.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of grinding wheels** based on (i) abrasive type, (ii) grit size, (iii) grade, (iv) structure, and (v) bond type. Discuss why **CBN is preferred over aluminium oxide** for grinding hardened steels (HRC > 60). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical grinding operation is being performed on a workpiece of diameter 80 mm and length 200 mm at a workpiece speed of 200 rpm. The radial infeed is 0.005 mm/pass and the traverse feed is 2 mm/rev of workpiece. Calculate: (i) Workpiece surface speed $v_w$, (ii) Material removal rate $Z_w$, (iii) Equivalent diameter $d_e$, and (iv) If the specific energy is 25 J/mm³, find the **spindle power** required. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (B-a):

**Classification of grinding wheels:**
**(i) Abrasive Type:**
- Aluminium oxide (A) — for soft steels.
- Silicon carbide (C) — for cast iron, non-ferrous, carbide.
- Cubic boron nitride (B/CBN) — for hardened steels.
- Diamond (D) — for ceramics, glass, stone.

**(ii) Grit Size:** Coarse (8–24), medium (30–60), fine (80–220), very fine (240–600). Higher number = finer grit = better finish.

**(iii) Grade (Hardness of bond):** A (soft) to Z (hard). Soft grade for hard workpiece; hard grade for soft workpiece.

**(iv) Structure:** Dense (low porosity, 1–4) to open (high porosity, 12–18). Open structure prevents loading on soft metals.

**(v) Bond Type:** Vitrified (V), Resinoid (B), Rubber (R), Metal (M), Shellac (E).

**Why CBN over Al₂O₃ for hardened steel:**
1. **Hardness:** CBN Knoop hardness ≈ 4700, Al₂O₃ ≈ 2100. CBN resists wear on hard workpiece.
2. **Thermal stability:** CBN stable up to 1400°C; Al₂O₃ degrades above 800°C — heat in grinding of hardened steel easily exceeds 800°C.
3. **Chemical inertness:** CBN does not react with iron (no chemical affinity), giving G-ratio > 100, while Al₂O₃ on hardened steel yields G-ratio < 10.
4. **Lower specific energy:** 20 J/mm³ for CBN vs 40–60 J/mm³ for Al₂O₃.
5. **No thermal damage:** Maintains compressive residual stresses; Al₂O₃ generates tensile residual stresses and white layer.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (B-b):

**(i) Workpiece surface speed:**
$$v_w = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80 \times 200}{60000}$$
$$\boxed{v_w = 0.838 \;\; \text{m/s} = 50.27 \;\; \text{m/min}}$$

**(ii) Material Removal Rate (cylindrical traverse grinding):**
$$Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot L \cdot f_r \cdot n_w$$
$$Z_w = \pi \times 80 \times 200 \times 0.005 \times 200$$
$$Z_w = \pi \times 80 \times 200 \times 1$$
$$\boxed{Z_w = 50265 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 837.76 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(iii) Equivalent diameter:**
For external cylindrical grinding, $d_s$ ≈ 300 mm (assume), $d_w$ = 80 mm.
$$d_e = \dfrac{d_s \cdot d_w}{d_s - d_w} = \dfrac{300 \times 80}{300 - 80} = \dfrac{24000}{220}$$
$$\boxed{d_e \approx 109.1 \;\; \text{mm}}$$

**(iv) Spindle power:**
$$P = u \cdot Z_w = 25 \;\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3} \times 837.76 \;\dfrac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}$$
$$\boxed{P = 20944 \;\text{W} \approx 20.9 \;\text{kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B alternate):**
> - In **cylindrical grinding MRR**, students often forget to multiply by $\pi D_w$ — they write $Z_w = L \cdot f_r \cdot n_w$ which is **dimensionally wrong**.
> - **Equivalent diameter formula sign:** external grinding uses $(-)$, internal grinding uses $(+)$. Wrong sign → wrong sign in answer → **lose 2 marks**.
> - For $v_w$, ensure **mm vs m** conversion before plugging into power formula.
> - The question **does not specify wheel speed** — if a follow-up asks tangential force, you cannot compute it without $v_s$. State this clearly in exam.

---

### 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section!**

✅ **Definition:** Grinding = abrasive machining using bonded wheel of hard grains, primarily for finishing hardened parts.

✅ **Three zones of grit-workpiece interaction:** Rubbing → Ploughing → Cutting. Only **1–10 %** grits are in cutting zone at any instant.

✅ **Standard wheel marking:** `A 60 K 5 V 23` → Abrasive A, Grit 60, Grade K (medium soft), Structure 5, Vitrified bond.

✅ **Abrasive selection rule:**
- Ferrous steel → **Al₂O₃**
- Cast iron / carbide / non-ferrous → **SiC**
- Hardened steel > 50 HRC → **CBN**
- Ceramics / glass / stone → **Diamond**

✅ **Grinding machine classification:**
- Surface, Cylindrical, Centerless, Internal, Tool & Cutter, Thread, Jig, Form.
- Horizontal spindle vs vertical spindle.

✅ **MRR formulas (must be memorized):**
- Surface: $Z_w = a \cdot b \cdot v_w$
- Cylindrical: $Z_w = \pi D_w L f_r n_w$
- Centerless: $Z_w = \pi D_w \cdot v_f \cdot a$ where $v_f$ = feed rate.

✅ **Specific energy:** 20–100 J/mm³ — grinding is **10–50×** more energy-intensive than turning.

✅ **Equivalent chip thickness:** $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w}{v_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{d_s}}$ — controls surface finish and forces.

✅ **Surface roughness:** $R_a \approx h_{eq}/4$ — increasing $v_s$ or decreasing $v_w, a$ improves finish.

✅ **Grinding ratio (G-ratio):** $G = V_w / V_s$ — high G = long wheel life. **CBN achieves G > 100**, Al₂O₃ only **G ≈ 10** on hardened steel.

✅ **Equivalent diameter:**
- External cylindrical: $d_e = \dfrac{d_s d_w}{d_s - d_w}$
- Internal: $d_e = \dfrac{d_s d_w}{d_s + d_w}$

✅ **Grinding defects to remember for viva:** Chatter, burn (thermal damage), white layer, residual tensile stress, loading, glazing, dressing frequency.

✅ **Coolant:** **Mandatory** in grinding — over **60 %** of input energy becomes heat; without coolant, workpiece burns and residual tensile stresses form → fatigue failure.

✅ **Spark-out passes:** 2–5 final passes with no infeed — eliminates elastic recovery of machine, improves dimensional accuracy and surface finish.

✅ **Dressing vs Truing:** Truing restores wheel profile; dressing sharpens the wheel by exposing fresh grit. Both done with diamond tool.

✅ **Creep-feed grinding (CFG):** Uses very low $v_w$ (5–50 mm/min), high $a$ (1–10 mm) — for deep slots and form grinding.

✅ **Typical KTUV ESE weightage:** Module 2 (Grinding) carries **20–25 %** marks in ESE — expect at least one Part B question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylindrical and Centreless Grinding Machines
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cylindrical and Centreless Grinding Machines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cylindrical Grinding Machine

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *Cylindrical Grinding Machine* is a precision machine tool used to shape the outside (or inside) of a rotating cylindrical workpiece by removing material using a rotating abrasive grinding wheel. The workpiece is held between centers (or in a chuck) and rotates at a controlled speed while the grinding wheel, rotating at very high peripheral speed, removes a thin layer of material to achieve high dimensional accuracy and superior surface finish (typically **0.2 µm to 1.6 µm Ra**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cylindrical grinding is classified as a finishing operation and falls under the category of "Abrasive Machining Processes." It can achieve tolerances of **± 0.005 mm** to **± 0.0125 mm** and is the most widely used grinding process in industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a potter's wheel (the workpiece) spinning on a spindle, while you gently press a rough sandstone disc (the grinding wheel) against its outer surface. The tiny abrasive grains on the stone act like microscopic chisels, each scraping off an infinitesimal chip — thousands of times per second — leaving behind a mirror-smooth cylinder. That is exactly what a cylindrical grinder does, only with **extreme precision** (a few microns) and at **industrial scale**.

---

### 1.2 Centreless Grinding Machine

**Formal KTU Definition:**
*Centreless Grinding* is an abrasive machining process in which the cylindrical workpiece is **not held between centers** but is supported on a *work rest blade* between two abrasive wheels — a large *grinding wheel* and a smaller *regulating wheel* (which also imparts rotary and axial motion to the workpiece). The regulating wheel runs at a much slower peripheral speed (typically **30–80 m/min**) compared to the grinding wheel (**1500–3500 m/min**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Centreless grinding eliminates the need for centering the workpiece, making it ideal for **high-volume production** of small cylindrical parts (pins, bushings, needle rollers, piston pins) with **no centre holes** required on the finished part.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Picture two rotating rollers in a copier/printer. If you place a pencil between them and the rollers spin in the same direction but at different speeds, the pencil automatically starts rotating AND sliding through. Now, replace one roller with a coarse grinding wheel and the other with a rubber-bonded regulating wheel — and you have centreless grinding. The pencil (workpiece) is ground as it passes through, **without ever being clamped or held at the ends**.

---

### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

| Feature | Cylindrical Grinding | Centreless Grinding |
|---|---|---|
| Year of origin | ~1870s (Brown & Sharpe) | ~1910s (Heald Machine Co.) |
| Work holding | Between centres / chuck | On work-rest blade |
| Production rate | Low–Medium | **Very High** |
| Operator skill | High | Low–Medium |
| Typical parts | Shafts, axles, spindles | Pins, rollers, bushings |

---

### 1.4 Classification Overview

**Cylindrical Grinders are classified as:**
1. **Plain Cylindrical Grinder** – table moves only longitudinally.
2. **Universal Cylindrical Grinder** – table can swivel; headstock swivels.
3. **Semi-Universal Grinder** – only workhead swivels (no table swivel).
4. **Special Cylindrical Grinder** – crankshaft, roll, plunge-cut variants.

**Centreless Grinders are classified as:**
1. **Through-feed (Plunge-pass)** – workpiece passes axially through.
2. **In-feed (Plunge) Grinding** – for stepped/tapered parts.
3. **End-feed Grinding** – workpiece fed from one side, ground, ejected.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Cylindrical Grinding Machine

The principal elements of a cylindrical grinder are:

1. **Bed (Base & Column)** – Heavy cast-iron base providing rigidity and vibration damping. It supports the headstock, tailstock, wheel head, and work table. The bed is heavily ribbed internally to resist deflection.

2. **Headstock** – Located at the left end of the bed; holds the **work spindle** carrying the workpiece. The spindle is mounted in preloaded anti-friction (ball/roller) bearings and is driven by a separate motor through a belt drive. The headstock can be **swivelled** (in universal machines) to set the workpiece for taper grinding.

3. **Tailstock** – Located at the right end; provides a **dead center** to support the other end of the workpiece. It can slide along the bed and be clamped. The tailstock center is *non-rotating* (dead center) to avoid wear.

4. **Wheel Head (Wheel Slide)** – Mounted on top of the column; carries the **grinding wheel spindle** rotating at very high speeds (**1500–2000 rpm for 400 mm wheel → v_s ≈ 30–40 m/s**). The wheel head can be traversed transversely (in-feed) and sometimes swivelled for angular grinding.

5. **Work Table** – Mounted on the bed between headstock and tailstock; supports the *work rest* and provides **longitudinal (reciprocating) travel** via hydraulic or hand drive. In universal machines, the table can also swivel.

6. **Coolant Supply System** – Flood coolant (water-soluble emulsion) is sprayed on the grinding zone to:
   - Reduce heat generation
   - Wash away chips
   - Lubricate the contact
   - Improve surface finish
   - Extend wheel life

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is coolant so important in grinding?** A typical grinding operation generates **300–1000 W/mm² of specific energy** (compared to ~5 W/mm² for turning). Without coolant, the workpiece would thermally distort and burn. The high heat arises because each abrasive grain removes a tiny chip in microseconds, with most of the energy converted to heat.

---

### 2.2 Operations Performed on a Cylindrical Grinder

1. **External Cylindrical Grinding** – Most common; grinding the outer cylindrical surface.
2. **Internal Cylindrical Grinding** – Uses a small grinding wheel mounted on a separate spindle; workpiece held in chuck.
3. **Face Grinding** – End face of the cylinder is ground using the side of the wheel.
4. **Taper Grinding** – Achieved by swivelling the table (universal machine) or tilting the wheel head.
5. **Form Grinding** – Wheel is contoured to the desired profile; used for shaped parts.
6. **Plunge (In-feed) Grinding** – Wheel is fed radially (no table traverse); for short, rigid workpieces.
7. **Crankshaft / Cam-shaft Grinding** – Specialised machines with multiple wheels.

---

### 2.3 Grinding Wheel — Designation & Selection

A standard grinding wheel is designated by the **standard marking system** (e.g., **38A 60 L 5 V 23**):

| Position | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 38 | Abrasive type (38 = Aluminium Oxide, friable) |
| 2 | A | Grain size (A = Medium 36–60) |
| 3 | 60 | Grit size (60 = 60 mesh) |
| 4 | L | Grade (L = Soft) |
| 5 | 5 | Structure (5 = dense) |
| 6 | V | Bond type (V = Vitrified) |
| 7 | 23 | Manufacturer's code |

**Selection Logic (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Hard material (e.g., HSS, carbide)** → **Friable abrasive** (Al₂O₃ single crystal, or Cubic Boron Nitride — CBN)
- **Soft ductile material (aluminium, brass)** → **Tough abrasive** (regular Al₂O₃, or SiC)
- **Rough grinding** → **Coarse grit (16–36)** + **Soft grade (H–L)**
- **Finish grinding** → **Fine grit (60–120)** + **Hard grade (M–P)**
- **Wet grinding** → **Vitrified or Resinoid bond**
- **Dry grinding (cut-off wheels)** → **Rubber or Shellac bond**

---

### 2.4 Mounting & Balancing of Grinding Wheels

**Mounting** must be done on a clean, true-running spindle using:
- **Blotters** (compressible paper) on each side of the wheel
- **Flanges** of equal diameter (≥ 1/3 wheel diameter)
- **Balancing rings/lead weights**

**Balancing** is critical because grinding wheels run at high peripheral speeds. An unbalanced wheel of **300 mm diameter at 2000 rpm** has its periphery moving at **~31 m/s**; even a 10 g unbalance creates a centrifugal force of:
$$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 = 0.01 \cdot 0.15 \cdot (209.4)^2 \approx 65.7 \text{ N}$$

This causes vibration, poor finish, wheel breakage, and is a major safety hazard.

**Types of balancing:**
- **Static balancing** – on a horizontal knife-edge or balancing arbor.
- **Dynamic balancing** – on a balancing machine (essential for wheels > 250 mm diameter or running > 1500 rpm).

---

### 2.5 Truing and Dressing of Grinding Wheel

**Truing** = Restoring the **geometric form** (roundness, profile) of the wheel.
**Dressing** = Exposing **fresh sharp abrasive grains** and removing glazed (loaded) material from the wheel face.

**Common Truing/Dressing Tools:**
- **Diamond dresser (single-point)** – for precision truing; mounted on a swivel slide.
- **Diamond cluster / Multi-point dresser** – for rough truing.
- **Roller dresser (steel or carbide)** – for form wheels.
- **Conventional abrasive stick** – for light dressing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** Truing and Dressing are *not* the same. *Truing* restores **shape**; *Dressing* restores **sharpness**. They are often done in the same pass but with different objectives.

---

### 2.6 Principle of Centreless Grinding — Detailed Analysis

In centreless grinding, the workpiece is supported on a **work-rest blade** between two wheels:

1. **Grinding Wheel (G)** — Large, fast-rotating (peripheral speed **1500–3500 m/min**), does the actual material removal. Usually grinds slightly *above* the workpiece centerline.

2. **Regulating Wheel (R)** — Smaller, slower (peripheral speed **30–80 m/min**), has a **rubber or resinoid bond** to provide friction. It performs two functions:
   - **Rotates the workpiece** at controlled speed (work speed v_w)
   - **Feeds the workpiece axially** (in through-feed) at a controlled rate via tilt of its axis.

3. **Work-rest Blade (Tr Blade)** — A thin, hardened steel support placed between the two wheels. Its **height and inclination** are critical for roundness.

**Geometry of Centreless Grinding:**

The work centre (O) lies on a vertical line *h* above the line connecting the two wheel centres. The work rests on the blade whose top surface is tangent to the workpiece.

Let:
- $\alpha$ = Inclination of work-rest blade to the horizontal (typically **30°–45°**)
- $r$ = Radius of the workpiece
- $R_g$ = Radius of grinding wheel
- $R_r$ = Radius of regulating wheel
- $h$ = Height of work centre above the line joining wheel centres

From the geometry of the triangle formed by the centres:

$$h = r \cdot \cos\alpha$$

And the distance from the work centre to the centre of the grinding wheel:

$$d_g = \sqrt{(R_g + r)^2 - h^2} = \sqrt{(R_g + r)^2 - r^2 \cos^2\alpha}$$

For typical small values of *h* compared to *R_g*:

$$d_g \approx R_g + r \cdot \sin^2\alpha \approx R_g + r \cdot (1 - \cos^2\alpha)$$

---

### 2.7 Types of Centreless Grinding

| Type | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Through-feed** | Workpiece fed axially between wheels; regulating wheel tilted at 1°–3° to provide axial feed. | Long, straight cylindrical parts (pins, shafts). |
| **In-feed (Plunge)** | Workpiece is fed radially between wheels; regulating wheel has a formed profile matching the work. | Stepped, tapered, or formed workpieces. |
| **End-feed** | Workpiece rests on a small work-rest at one wheel; fed from one side, discharged from the other. | Tapered parts (e.g., taper pins, plungers). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulating Wheel Tilt (Lead Angle β):** The regulating wheel axis is tilted by **β ≈ 0.5° – 3°** from the horizontal plane. This tilt causes the regulating wheel surface to have an axial velocity component, which feeds the workpiece. The **axial feed rate** is:
> $$v_f = v_{reg} \cdot \sin\beta$$
> where $v_{reg}$ is the peripheral speed of the regulating wheel.

---

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel peripheral speed | $v_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000}$ | m/s | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Work peripheral speed | $v_w = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60 \times 1000}$ | m/min | d in mm, n in rpm |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = v_w \cdot f_r \cdot a_p$ | mm³/min | $f_r$ = feed (mm/rev), $a_p$ = depth (mm) |
| 4 | MRR (centreless, through-feed) | $MRR = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot v_f$ | mm³/min | L = length, $v_f$ = axial feed |
| 5 | Grinding Ratio (G-ratio) | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$ | – | Higher G → better wheel |
| 6 | Specific Energy | $U = \dfrac{P}{MRR}$ | W·s/mm³ or J/mm³ | P = power (W) |
| 7 | Equivalent chip thickness | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \cdot f_r}{v_s} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{a_p}{d}}$ | µm | Indicates surface finish |
| 8 | Theoretical Surface Roughness | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 \cdot r_{grain}}$ | µm | r_grain = nose radius of grit |
| 9 | Work-rest blade height (centreless) | $h = r \cdot \cos\alpha$ | mm | α = blade inclination |
| 10 | Axial feed in through-feed | $v_f = v_{reg} \cdot \sin\beta$ | mm/min | β = lead angle |
| 11 | Centrifugal force on wheel | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | N | m = unbalance, r = radius |
| 12 | Wheel speed (rpm) | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot v_s \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Inverse of eq. 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter for KTU:** Every numerical problem in grinding (such as "Find MRR for a given set of parameters" or "Find the work-rest blade height for centreless grinding") uses one of these. **G-ratio and MRR are the most-frequently tested pairs.**

---

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Crankshaft journals, camshaft lobes, piston pins, transmission shafts.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade fir-tree roots, landing gear pins, high-precision shafts.
- **Bearings:** Outer/inner ring raceways (though these use specialised *race grinders*).
- **Tooling:** Drill bits, reamers, end-mills are ground on universal cylindrical grinders.
- **Medical:** Hypodermic needles, surgical pins, bone screws (centreless grinding).
- **Hydraulics:** Spool valves, plunger barrels — require extremely tight tolerances.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Cylindrical Grinding

**Given:** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter *d*, rotating at *n* rpm, with a grinding wheel feeding transversely (in-feed) at rate $f_r$ (mm/rev of work) and depth of cut $a_p$ (mm).

**Step 1 — Work peripheral speed:**
The work surface moves at velocity:
$$v_w = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60 \times 1000} \text{ m/min} = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60} \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-section of the chip being removed:**
When the wheel advances by $a_p$ radially and the work moves $v_w \cdot dt$ in time $dt$, the cross-sectional area of the cut material is:
$$A_c = v_w \cdot f_r \cdot a_p \cdot dt / dt = v_w \cdot f_r \cdot a_p$$
But actually, we must include the *axial* feed component. In plunge grinding, the *entire* cross-section is removed continuously:
$$A_c = v_w \cdot f_r \text{ (mm}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 3 — MRR (volumetric):**
$$MRR = v_w \cdot f_r \cdot a_p \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

> **Why this is intuitive:** MRR is *length swept × width × depth*. In grinding, $v_w$ is the length swept per minute, $f_r$ is the effective width (feed per rev), and $a_p$ is the depth of the cut.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of G-Ratio (Grinding Ratio)

**Definition:** G-ratio is the **volume of workpiece material removed per unit volume of wheel consumed**:
$$G = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$

**Step 1 — Volume of work material removed:**
For a cylindrical work of diameter *d*, length *L*, ground down by radial amount *r*:
$$V_w = \frac{\pi \cdot (d_1^2 - d_2^2) \cdot L}{4} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot r$$
(where $d_1$ and $d_2$ are the initial and final diameters, and $r = (d_1 - d_2)/2$ is the radial infeed).

**Step 2 — Volume of wheel consumed:**
Wheel wears by radial amount $\Delta r_w$ over the same contact length L (for cylindrical contact):
$$V_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r_w \cdot L$$
(where D = wheel diameter)

**Step 3 — G-ratio:**
$$G = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot L \cdot r}{\pi \cdot D \cdot \Delta r_w \cdot L} = \frac{d \cdot r}{D \cdot \Delta r_w}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**
- Work: d = 50 mm, L = 100 mm, radial infeed r = 0.10 mm
- Wheel: D = 300 mm, $\Delta r_w$ = 0.05 mm
- G = (50 × 0.10) / (300 × 0.05) = 5 / 15 = **0.333**

A G-ratio of 0.33 is very low; commercial grinding typically achieves G = 10–100 with conventional abrasives and **> 1000 with CBN**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Equivalent Chip Thickness ($h_{eq}$)

**Concept:** In grinding, each abrasive grain removes a tiny "chip." The *average* chip thickness is called the equivalent chip thickness. It is critical because it controls:
- Specific energy
- Surface roughness
- Wheel wear

**Step 1 — Volume of material removed per unit contact length per unit time:**
$$\text{Vol rate} = v_w \cdot a_p \cdot \text{(per unit length)}$$

**Step 2 — Number of active cutting grains per unit area on the wheel face:**
$$N_a = \frac{C}{g^2}$$
where *C* is the active-grain density factor (typically 0.5–0.7) and *g* is the mean grain spacing (≈ 1.5 × mean grit diameter).

**Step 3 — Mean chip thickness per grain:**
Each grain sweeps through a path of length $v_w / v_s$ (ratio of work speed to wheel speed) and the depth is $a_p$. So the cross-section of material per grain is:
$$\text{Cross-section per grain} = \frac{v_w \cdot a_p}{N_a \cdot v_s}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent chip thickness** (assuming triangular chip):
$$h_{eq} = \sqrt{\frac{v_w \cdot a_p}{N_a \cdot v_s}} = \sqrt{\frac{v_w}{v_s} \cdot \frac{a_p \cdot g^2}{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Reducing $a_p$ or increasing $v_s$ reduces $h_{eq}$ → finer surface finish and lower specific energy. This is why modern high-speed grinding uses $v_s$ of **60–200 m/s**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Work-Rest Blade Height (Centreless Grinding)

**Geometry:** The workpiece of radius *r* sits on the work-rest blade inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. The workpiece centre is at height $h$ above the line joining the grinding wheel centre ($C_g$) and regulating wheel centre ($C_r$).

**Step 1 — Consider the right triangle formed by:**
- Hypotenuse = line from workpiece centre to blade contact point
- This hypotenuse has length = r (radius of workpiece)
- The blade surface makes angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal

**Step 2 — Vertical component of the radius (from blade to centre):**
The centre is *r·cosα* above the blade surface (perpendicular to blade). However, we want the vertical height from the line $C_g C_r$ to the centre. The line $C_g C_r$ is horizontal in standard configuration. The blade is at angle $\alpha$ to horizontal.

**Step 3 — Result:**
The vertical distance from the centre of the workpiece to the line $C_g C_r$ is:
$$h = r \cdot \cos\alpha$$

When $\alpha = 0$ (horizontal blade), h = r → the work centre lies on the line of centres (centreline).

When $\alpha = 90°$ (vertical blade), h = 0 → impossible (workpiece cannot rest).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU range:** $\alpha = 30° \to h = 0.866r$  
> For $\alpha = 45° \to h = 0.707r$  
> For $\alpha = 60° \to h = 0.500r$
>
> In actual practice, the work centre is placed **slightly above** the line of centres (typically $h = (1.1$ to $1.3) r$ achieved by lowering the work-rest). This is done to compensate for the forces exerted by the regulating wheel.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Centreless Grinding Workpiece Velocity

**Problem:** A workpiece of 25 mm diameter is ground in through-feed centreless grinding. The grinding wheel is 500 mm diameter, rotating at 1500 rpm. The regulating wheel is 250 mm diameter, rotating at 50 rpm, and is tilted at 2° to the horizontal. Find:
1. Peripheral speed of grinding wheel
2. Peripheral speed of regulating wheel
3. Axial feed rate of workpiece
4. Workpiece rotational speed

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Peripheral speed of grinding wheel:**
$$v_g = \frac{\pi \cdot D_g \cdot N_g}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 500 \times 1500}{60 \times 1000} = 39.27 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Peripheral speed of regulating wheel:**
$$v_r = \frac{\pi \cdot D_r \cdot N_r}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 50}{60 \times 1000} = 0.654 \text{ m/s} = 39.27 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 3 — Axial feed rate of workpiece:**
$$v_f = v_r \cdot \sin\beta = 39.27 \cdot \sin(2°) = 39.27 \times 0.0349 = 1.371 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 4 — Workpiece rotational speed (from regulating wheel peripheral speed):**
The workpiece is rotated by friction with the regulating wheel, so its surface speed ≈ $v_r$:
$$n_w = \frac{v_r \times 1000}{\pi \cdot d} = \frac{39.27 \times 1000}{\pi \times 25} = 500 \text{ rpm}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Cylindrical vs Centreless Grinding

| Parameter | Cylindrical Grinding | Centreless Grinding |
|---|---|---|
| Work holding | Centres / Chuck | Work-rest blade (no centres) |
| Centre holes required | **Yes** | **No** |
| Setup time | High (centre alignment) | Low (just place on blade) |
| Production rate | 10–30 pcs/hr | 60–600 pcs/hr |
| Geometric accuracy | High (roundness 1–3 µm) | High (roundness 1–5 µm) |
| Diameter range | Wide (5–500 mm typical) | Limited to small-medium (1–150 mm) |
| Surface finish (Ra) | 0.1 – 0.8 µm | 0.2 – 1.6 µm |
| Length limitation | Bed length limits | Can grind long parts in through-feed |
| Taper grinding | Easy (table swivel) | Difficult (special fixtures) |
| Cost of machine | Moderate–High | High (specialised) |
