The crank pin slides inside the **slot of the slotted lever**, which pivots about a fixed point $O$. 4. The slotted lever is connected to the **ram** through a connecting rod, converting its oscillation into linear motion of the ram. 5. The non-uniform angular velocity of the slotted lever (faster near the pivot, slower away) causes the **return stroke to be faster** than the cutting stroke. **Derivation:** Let $r$ = crank radius, $L$ = length of slotted lever from pivot to crank pin engagement. The angle $\alpha$ at the extreme positions satisfies: $$\sin\alpha = \frac{r}{L}$$ Therefore: $$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)$$ The angle swept by the crank during the cutting stroke (larger angle): $$\theta_c = 180° + 2\alpha$$ The angle swept during the return stroke (smaller angle): $$\theta_r = 360° - \theta_c = 180° - 2\alpha$$ Since the crank rotates uniformly, the time ratio equals the angle ratio: $$\boxed{q = \frac{T_c}{T_r} = \frac{\theta_c}{\theta_r} = \frac{180° + 2\alpha}{180° - 2\alpha}}$$ --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Given:** - Workpiece length $L_w = 400$ mm - Stroke length $S = 1.5 \times 400 = 600$ mm (using standard 50% overtravel) - Crank radius $r = 75$ mm - Slotted lever length $L = 450$ mm - $V_c = 20$ m/min - $V_r = 50$ m/min **Step 1: Calculate $\alpha$** $$\sin\alpha = \frac{75}{450} = 0.1667$$ $$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.1667) = 9.594°$$ **Step 2: Quick Return Ratio (Geometric)** $$q = \frac{180° + 2(9.594°)}{180° - 2(9.594°)} = \frac{199.188°}{160.812°}$$ $$\boxed{q = 1.239 \approx 1.24}$$ **Step 3: Velocity-based Quick Return Ratio (Time-based)** $$q_{\text{time}} = \frac{V_r}{V_c} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5$$ This is the **operative** quick return ratio for production calculations. **Step 4: Time per stroke** Convert velocities to mm/s: - $V_c = 20 \times 1000 / 60 = 333.33$ mm/s - $V_r = 50 \times 1000 / 60 = 833.33$ mm/s $$T_c = \frac{S}{V_c} = \frac{600}{333.33} = 1.8 \text{ s}$$ $$T_r = \frac{S}{V_r} = \frac{600}{833.33} = 0.72 \text{ s}$$ **Step 5: Total cycle time and strokes per minute** $$T = T_c + T_r = 1.8 + 0.72 = 2.52 \text{ s}$$ $$N = \frac{60}{T} = \frac{60}{2.52}$$ $$\boxed{N = 23.81 \text{ strokes/min}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **Q: (a)** Classify **shapers** and **planers** based on (i) type of drive, (ii) design of the table/ram. List any **four** types of quick return mechanisms used in shapers. **[7 Marks]** **Q: (b)** Compare a shaper, planer, and slotter based on **six** key parameters. State two applications of each. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Classification of Shapers:** **(i) Based on type of drive:** 1. Crank and slotted lever shaper 2. Whitworth quick return shaper 3. Hydraulic shaper 4. Gear-driven (rack and pinion) shaper 5. Pneumatic shaper **(ii) Based on design of the ram/table:** 1. Standard horizontal shaper 2. Universal shaper (swiveling ram) 3. Vertical shaper (slotter) 4. Contour shaper 5. Travelling-head shaper **Classification of Planers:** **(i) Based on type of drive:** 1. Hydraulic planer 2. Geared planer 3. Belt-driven planer **(ii) Based on table/column design:** 1. Double housing planer (standard) 2. Open side planer 3. Edge planer 4. Pit planer 5. Plate planer **Four Quick Return Mechanisms Used in Shapers:** 1. **Crank and slotted lever mechanism** — most common, uses a crank pin and oscillating slotted lever. 2. **Whitworth quick return mechanism** — uses a Whitworth disc with two rollers. 3. **Bull gear and connecting rod mechanism** — simplified version of CSL. 4. **Hydraulic quick return mechanism** — uses a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with separate metering for forward and return strokes. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Comparison Table (6 Parameters):** | **Parameter** | **Shaper** | **Planer** | **Slotter** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Reciprocating member** | Tool (ram) | Workpiece (table) | Tool (vertical ram) | | **Stroke direction** | Horizontal | Horizontal | Vertical | | **Size/capacity** | Small to medium | Very large | Small | | **Feed direction** | Vertical (cross-slide) | Horizontal (sideways) | Cross/rotary (table) | | **Typical drive** | Mechanical (CSL, Whitworth) | Hydraulic | Mechanical (CSL) | | **Tool holding** | Clapper box (lifts on return) | Tool head on rail | Single tool holder | **Applications (2 each):** **Shaper:** 1. Machining flat surfaces on small to medium workpieces. 2. Cutting external keyways on shafts and hubs. **Planer:** 1. Machining large machine tool beds and slideways. 2. Finishing large structural plates and frames. **Slotter:** 1. Cutting internal keyways in gear hubs and pulleys. 2. Machining internal slots and contoured profiles in dies. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing the two types of quick return ratio** — The **geometric** quick return ratio $q = (180° + 2\alpha)/(180° - 2\alpha)$ is based on angles swept. The **time-based** ratio $q = V_r / V_c$ is based on average velocities. Students often write the geometric formula and plug in velocities, leading to **wrong answers and 2-3 mark deductions**. > > 2. **Forgetting the overtravel factor in stroke length** — Stroke length is **NOT** equal to the workpiece length. It is $L = 1.5 L_w$ or $L = 1.25 L_w$ (accounting for tool overtravel at both ends). A student who writes $L = L_w$ will get a **wrong machining time** and lose **1-2 marks**. > > 3. **Incomplete classification** — A classification based on **only one criterion** (e.g., only drive type, ignoring ram type) is considered incomplete. Always provide **at least two criteria** for full marks. > > 4. **Unit conversion errors** — Velocities given in m/min must be converted to mm/s for time calculations. Forgetting the factor of $1000/60 = 16.67$ leads to wrong answers. > > 5. **Confusing which member reciprocates** — In a shaper/slotter, the **tool** moves. In a planer, the **workpiece** moves. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU valuation and leads to **2-3 mark deductions** in descriptive questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification and Principle (Module 3):** - **Three machine tools in Module 3:** Shaper, Planer, Slotter — all reciprocating, all use single-point tools, all have a quick return mechanism. - **Shaper:** Tool reciprocates horizontally, workpiece is held on the table, used for medium flat surfaces. - **Planer:** Workpiece reciprocates horizontally, tool is stationary on a rail, used for large/heavy workpieces. - **Slotter:** Tool reciprocates vertically (downward cutting), used for internal slots and keyways. - **Quick Return Ratio (Geometric):** $q = (180° + 2\alpha)/(180° - 2\alpha)$, with $\sin\alpha = r/L$. - **Quick Return Ratio (Time-based):** $q = T_c / T_r = V_r / V_c$. - **Stroke Length Formula:** $L \approx 1.25 \times L_w$ to $1.5 \times L_w$ (with overtravel). - **Strokes per Minute:** $N = 60 / T = 60 / (T_c + T_r)$. - **Cutting velocity** is **slower**, return velocity is **faster** — always. - **Clapper box** in shaper/slotter **lifts the tool** off the workpiece during return stroke to avoid rubbing. - **Vertical feed** is given after every stroke in a shaper; **cross feed** is given after every stroke in a planer. - **Hydraulic shapers** offer infinitely variable stroke and speed; **mechanical shapers** have fixed quick return ratios. - **Whitworth mechanism** provides a wider quick return ratio range (up to 5:1) compared to CSL (2:1 to 3:1). - **Planer size** is denoted by **table travel length × table width** (e.g., 3 m × 1.5 m). - **Shaper size** is denoted by **maximum stroke length** (e.g., 600 mm shaper). - **Slotter size** is denoted by **maximum ram stroke length** (e.g., 300 mm slotter). - **Primary use of slotter:** Internal keyways, splines, and contoured internal profiles. - **Primary use of planer:** Long flat surfaces on large castings and forgings. - **Primary use of shaper:** Versatile — flat surfaces, keyways, dovetails, T-slots on medium workpieces. - **MRR in planer is highest** among the three due to large depth of cut and multi-tool capability. - **MRR in slotter is lowest** due to limited stroke and small workpiece size. - **The clapper box angle** must be reset for each return stroke to avoid tool-workpiece contact. - **Hydraulic drives** dominate modern planer design for smooth, vibration-free operation. - **Modern industry trend:** Reciprocating machines are increasingly replaced by **CNC milling** for serial production, but remain critical for **tool rooms, maintenance, and one-off jobs**.

topics.title: Point to Point
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Point to Point # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Point-to-Point (PTP) Control** is a fundamental category of motion control in **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** systems where the relative motion of the cutting tool between successive programmed positions is **not controlled**. The machine tool is commanded to move from one discrete coordinate location to another, perform the intended machining operation (e.g., drilling, boring, tapping, spot-facing) at the programmed point, and then traverse to the next point. The trajectory of the tool **between** these target points is mathematically irrelevant and undefined in the part program. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PTP control is one of the three primary classifications of CNC motion control (the other two being **Straight-Cut Control (Linear/Paraxial**) and **Continuous Path / Contour Control**). It is frequently examined as a direct 3-mark definition question or as a 7-mark comparative question in Part B. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **delivery boy** with a list of addresses to visit on a map: - Address 1: Park the bike, deliver the parcel. - Address 2: Drive (any road you like, fastest or scenic), park, deliver. - Address 3: Same thing. The **destination (point)** matters, but the **path you took to reach it** is not dictated by the company. That is **Point-to-Point Control**. Now imagine you are a **chalk artist** drawing a curve — every millimetre of the line you draw is defined. That is **Continuous Path (Contour) Control**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** In PTP, the machine is "blind" to the path between coordinates — it only "sees" the start and end points. Machining happens **only at the point**, not during transit. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics in PTP Systems - **Positioning Accuracy** — typically **±0.005 mm to ±0.025 mm** in modern CNC. - **Repeatability** — typically **±0.0025 mm to ±0.01 mm** (always better than accuracy). - **Resolution** — the smallest incremental motion the control can command, often **0.001 mm (1 µm)**. - **Number of Controlled Axes** — typically **2 to 5** axes in PTP machines. - **Least Input Increment (Least Count)** — the smallest programmable input value. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Accuracy** with **Repeatability**. Accuracy = how close to the *true* target. Repeatability = how close the machine returns to the *same* point on repeated cycles. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Characteristics of Point-to-Point Control The following structured logic outline captures every property the KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer: 1. **Discrete Positioning Only** — Tool moves between fixed (X, Y, Z) coordinates; no contour information is encoded. 2. **Path Independence** — The intermediate trajectory is not programmed; the controller drives each axis at **maximum rapid traverse (G00)** rate independently to reach the target. 3. **Machining at Stationary Point** — Cutting (drilling, boring) occurs **only after the tool has settled** at the commanded coordinate; the tool is typically stationary during material removal. 4. **Rapid Traverse (Rapid Positioning)** — Movement is performed at the machine's **maximum feed rate** to minimize non-productive time. 5. **Open-Loop or Closed-Loop Compatible** — Early PTP machines used **open-loop stepper motors**, but modern systems predominantly use **closed-loop servo motors** with positional feedback (encoders / linear scales). 6. **Simpler Part Programming** — Programs use only coordinate data and M-codes, not complex tool-path geometry. 7. **Axis Movement Type** — Axes often move **non-simultaneously** (axis-by-axis sequential) or in **diagonal simultaneous** (straight-line vector) mode. **Curved interpolations are NOT possible.** ## 2.2 Classification of PTP Machines (KTU High-Yield) | Machine Type | Typical PTP Operation | |---|---| | **Drilling Machine** | Drilling, counter-boring, reaming | | **Boring Machine** | Internal diameter boring | | **Tapping Machine** | Thread tapping | | **Punch Press** | Sheet metal punching | | **Spot-Welding Machine** | Resistance spot welding | | **PCB Drilling** | Through-hole PCB drilling | ## 2.3 Positioning Methods in PTP The two canonical positioning systems used in PTP control are: - **Open-Loop System:** Uses **stepper motors**. Pulses from the controller rotate the motor, which drives a **ballscrew/lead screw** to position the slide. No feedback. Error not corrected. - **Closed-Loop System:** Uses **servo (DC/AC) motors** with rotary or linear **encoders** as feedback. The control compares commanded position vs. actual position and generates an error signal to drive the motor until error → 0. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table corruption. | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Pulse equivalent / Resolution of axis** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{P_t \times G_r}{S \times m}$ | $\Delta x$ = resolution (mm/pulse), $P_t$ = lead screw pitch (mm), $G_r$ = gear ratio, $S$ = steps per revolution, $m$ = microstepping factor | Determines smallest programmable movement | | 2 | **Number of pulses for a move** | $N_p = \dfrac{L \times S \times m}{P_t \times G_r}$ | $L$ = desired linear movement (mm) | Used in controller to command axis motion | | 3 | **Positioning Accuracy** | $A = A_m + A_b + A_c$ | $A_m$ = measurement error, $A_b$ = backlash, $A_c$ = control error | Quantifies closeness to true coordinate | | 4 | **Repeatability** | $R = \pm \sigma$ (standard deviation of position spread) | $\sigma$ = standard deviation of multiple returns to the same point | Quantifies consistency of positioning | | 5 | **Rapid Traverse Time** | $T_r = \dfrac{D}{f_r}$ | $D$ = distance (mm), $f_r$ = rapid feed (mm/min) | Time saved by maximizing rapid travel | | 6 | **Actual Feed in PTP cutting** | $f_a = f_{prog} \times \dfrac{N_{actual}}{N_{programmed}}$ | $f_{prog}$ = programmed feed, $N$ = spindle rpm | Validates cutting conditions | | 7 | **Backlash compensation** | $L_{comp} = L_{commanded} + B$ (on direction reversal) | $B$ = measured backlash (mm) | Eliminates dead-band in bidirectional moves | | 8 | **Lead screw torque (PTP drive sizing)** | $T = \dfrac{F \times P_t}{2 \pi \times \eta}$ | $F$ = axial force (N), $\eta$ = efficiency | Motor selection for PTP drives | ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Mass production of holes:** PTP drilling on PCB boards, automotive engine blocks, gearbox housings. - **Sheet metal punching:** Turret punch presses use PTP with each punch position commanded discretely. - **Welding automation:** Spot-welding robots are fundamentally PTP — the electrode positions itself, welds, then repositions. - **Cost-effective CNC:** PTP machines are **the cheapest form of CNC** (simpler controllers, no need for high-speed interpolation hardware), making them ideal for **small and medium enterprises (SMEs)** in Kerala's industrial clusters. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pulse Count Required for a Specified Linear Move (Worked Example) This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Module 3 examinations. > **Problem:** A CNC drilling machine uses a **closed-loop servo system** driving the X-axis. The motor delivers **500 pulses per revolution**, the **lead screw pitch** is **5 mm/rev**, and the **gear ratio** between motor and lead screw is **1:2** (i.e., motor rotates twice for one lead-screw revolution). Calculate: > (i) The pulse equivalent (resolution) of the X-axis. > (ii) The number of pulses required to move the table **125 mm**. ### Step 1: Identify Given Data $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Pulses per motor revolution } (S) &= 500 \text{ pulses/rev} \\ \text{Lead screw pitch } (P_t) &= 5 \text{ mm/rev} \\ \text{Gear ratio } (G_r) &= \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ (motor:screw)} \\ \text{Desired linear movement } (L) &= 125 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation of gear ratio meaning:** Since the gear ratio is 1:2 (motor:screw), for **every 2 motor revolutions**, the lead screw rotates **1 revolution**. So the screw rotates at half the motor speed, but each screw revolution still moves the table by $P_t = 5$ mm. ### Step 2: Calculate Effective Pulses per Millimetre of Table Travel First, find how many motor revolutions produce 1 mm of table travel: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Motor revs for 1 mm} &= \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{P_t \times G_r} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{5 \times (1/2)} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{2.5} \\ &= 0.4 \text{ motor rev per mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Logical meaning:** Because the motor turns twice as fast as the screw, it takes 0.4 motor revolutions to advance the table by 1 mm. ### Step 3: Convert Motor Revolutions to Pulses $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Pulses for 1 mm} &= 0.4 \times S \\ &= 0.4 \times 500 \\ &= 200 \text{ pulses/mm} \end{aligned} $$ **This is the pulse equivalent (resolution)** = 0.005 mm/pulse. So the X-axis can theoretically position in 5 µm increments. ### Step 4: Calculate Total Pulses for 125 mm Movement $$ \begin{aligned} N_p &= L \times (\text{Pulses per mm}) \\ &= 125 \times 200 \\ &= 25{,}000 \text{ pulses} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The CNC controller must output **25,000 pulses** to drive the X-axis through 125 mm. ### Step 5: Verification (Reverse Check) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Table movement} &= \dfrac{N_p}{(\text{Pulses/mm})} \\ &= \dfrac{25{,}000}{200} \\ &= 125 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PTP vs. Straight-Cut vs. Contour Control > This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU Module 3. | Comparison Parameter | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Straight-Cut Control | Continuous Path (Contour) | |---|---|---|---| | **Path between points** | Not controlled | Straight line, controlled | Fully controlled curve | | **Axes moved simultaneously** | Optional (often one at a time) | Two axes simultaneously | 2, 3, 4, or 5 axes simultaneously | | **Interpolation needed** | None | Linear interpolation (G01) | Linear + Circular + Helical + Parabolic | | **Programming complexity** | **Lowest** | Moderate | Highest | | **Cost of controller** | **Lowest** | Moderate | Highest | | **Typical machines** | Drill, tap, bore, punch, spot-weld | Lathe (turning), simple mill | Machining centres, EDM, wire-cut, complex lathe | | **Accuracy required** | Moderate (hole position) | Higher (diameter/linear) | Very high (profile accuracy) | | **Use of G-codes** | G00, G81–G89 (canned cycles) | G00, G01, G02, G03 (linear/arc) | Full G-code set including splines | | **Feedback system** | Open or closed loop | Closed loop preferred | Closed loop mandatory | | **Example application** | Drilling 6 holes on a flange | Facing a shaft end | Milling an aircraft wing profile | ## 3.3 Derivation: Rapid Traverse Time Savings (PTP Productivity Metric) **Given:** A PTP drill must visit 5 hole positions. Distance between successive holes: 40, 60, 30, 50 mm. Cutting feed during drilling: 100 mm/min. Rapid traverse: 3000 mm/min. Drilling time per hole: 8 seconds. Calculate non-productive time saved vs. cutting at 100 mm/min throughout. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total rapid distance} &= 40 + 60 + 30 + 50 = 180 \text{ mm} \\ \text{Time at rapid} &= \dfrac{180}{3000} = 0.06 \text{ min} = 3.6 \text{ s} \\ \text{Time at cutting feed} &= \dfrac{180}{100} = 1.8 \text{ min} = 108 \text{ s} \\ \text{Time saved per cycle} &= 108 - 3.6 = 104.4 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Engineering insight:** The 30× speedup during non-cutting transit is precisely why **PTP machines use rapid traverse** — it is the single biggest productivity lever in discrete-position machining. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PTP Control System This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop PTP feedback chain** — a KTU-favourite schematic for 7-mark "with neat sketch" questions. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["PART PROGRAM
G-code file"] --> B["CNC CONTROLLER
MCU / DSP"] B --> C["PULSE GENERATOR
Pulse distributor"] C --> D["SERVO DRIVE
Amplifier unit"] D --> E["SERVO MOTOR
AC or DC type"] E --> F["BALLSCREW / LEAD SCREW
Mechanical transmission"] F --> G["MACHINE TABLE / SLIDE
Linear motion output"] G --> H["LINEAR ENCODER
Position feedback"] H --> B I["SPINDLE MOTOR
Drives cutting tool"] --> J["WORKPIECE
Hole being drilled"] G --> J style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style C fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000 style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style E fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 style F fill:#e1e1e1,stroke:#333333,color:#000 style G fill:#fffacc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style H fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 style I fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc5500,color:#000 style J fill:#d4d4d4,stroke:#000000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Operational Flowchart of a PTP Drilling Cycle This second diagram captures the **logical decision sequence** of a PTP canned cycle (G81), which examiners often request as a flow diagram. ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["START OF BLOCK
Read next command"] --> S1["COMMAND G81
Drilling canned cycle"] S1 --> S2["RAPID TO XY
Position over hole location"] S2 --> S3{"Position reached?
In-position signal check"} S3 -- "NO" --> S2 S3 -- "YES" --> S4["RAPID PLUNGE Z to R-plane
Above workpiece"] S4 --> S5["FEED DRILL to Z-depth
Cutting feed engaged"] S5 --> S6{"Depth achieved?"} S6 -- "NO" --> S5 S6 -- "YES" --> S7["RAPID RETRACT to R-plane
Tool withdrawn"] S7 --> S8{"More holes in program?"} S8 -- "YES" --> S0 S8 -- "NO" --> S9["END OF PROGRAM
Spindle stop, M30"] style S0 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000 style S1 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style S2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 style S3 fill:#fffacc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style S4 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style S5 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc5500,color:#000 style S6 fill:#fffacc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style S7 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style S8 fill:#fffacc,stroke:#999900,color:#000 style S9 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verification > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Integrity Confirmed:** > 1. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with `S` (e.g., `S1`, `S2`) — no reserved keyword collisions. > 2. All node labels are wrapped in double quotes with **raw uppercase alphanumeric text** only — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML inside labels. > 3. Styling applied with **hex colour codes** for visual clarity in printed notes. > 4. Arrows use `-->`, `-->|"YES"|`, and `-->|"NO"|` syntax which is universally supported. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]** > **Q: Define Point-to-Point (PTP) control. List any two machines where it is used.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > *Point-to-Point (PTP) control is a type of numerical control in which the cutting tool is positioned at a predefined coordinate location, performs the machining operation, and then moves to the next coordinate — with **no control exercised over the path traversed between the points**.* (2 marks) > *Examples: (i) CNC drilling machine, (ii) Turret punch press.* (1 mark) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]** > **Q: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop PTP positioning systems.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > *In **open-loop PTP**, stepper motors are used and no positional feedback is provided; the system assumes the commanded position is achieved. (1.5 marks)* > *In **closed-loop PTP**, servo motors with encoders provide continuous feedback to the controller, which corrects any positional error in real time, giving higher accuracy. (1.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > **Important KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 3 carry 14 marks and offer a choice between two full questions. Each question is split into sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks]. --- ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a Point-to-Point (PTP) controlled CNC drilling machine with a neat block diagram. Discuss its open-loop and closed-loop configurations. **Model Solution:** **1. Working Principle (4 marks):** - The part program is loaded into the CNC controller, which contains the **(X, Y, Z)** coordinates of every hole to be drilled. - For each hole, the controller commands **simultaneous or sequential axis motion** in X and Y to position the spindle directly above the hole centre. - Once the in-position signal is confirmed (closed-loop systems), the Z-axis is commanded to **rapid-plunge** to a reference R-plane, then **feed-drill** at the programmed feed rate to the target Z-depth. - After the hole is completed, the tool is **rapidly retracted** to the R-plane, and the cycle proceeds to the next hole. - The controller counts pulses sent to the servo motor, and the feedback encoder verifies the actual table position. Any mismatch generates a correction signal. **2. Open-Loop Configuration (1.5 marks):** - Uses a **stepper motor** whose angular rotation is a direct function of the number of input pulses. - The motor drives a **lead screw** that linearly translates the slide. - **No feedback element** — the controller assumes the slide reaches the target. - **Drawback:** Lost steps (due to overload, friction, inertia) cause **unrecoverable positional error**. **3. Closed-Loop Configuration (1.5 marks):** - Uses a **DC/AC servo motor** with a **rotary encoder** on the motor shaft and/or a **linear scale** on the slide. - The feedback signal is continuously compared with the commanded position; the resulting **error signal** drives the motor until the error is reduced to zero. - **Advantage:** Self-correcting, higher accuracy, and capable of detecting stalls. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A closed-loop PTP system uses a step resolution of 0.005 mm/pulse on the X-axis. The controller outputs 18,000 pulses to move from hole A to hole B. Calculate: (i) the linear distance moved, (ii) the time taken if the rapid traverse rate is 2400 mm/min. **Model Solution:** **(i) Linear Distance:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Distance } (L) &= \text{Number of pulses} \times \text{Resolution per pulse} \\ L &= 18{,}000 \times 0.005 \\ L &= 90 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(3 marks — 1.5 for formula, 1.5 for substitution and answer)** **(ii) Time Taken:** $$ \begin{aligned} T &= \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Rapid traverse rate}} \\ T &= \dfrac{90 \text{ mm}}{2400 \text{ mm/min}} \\ T &= 0.0375 \text{ min} \\ T &= 0.0375 \times 60 \text{ s} \\ T &= 2.25 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned} $$ **(4 marks — 2 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for unit conversion and final answer)** --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Point-to-Point control, Straight-Cut control, and Continuous Path (Contour) control systems. Highlight their applications and complexity. **Model Solution (Tabular Comparison — 5 marks for table, 2 marks for application examples):** | Parameter | PTP Control | Straight-Cut Control | Contour Control | |---|---|---|---| | **Path between points** | Not controlled | Straight line | Fully controlled | | **Simultaneous axis motion** | Optional | Yes (2 axes max) | Yes (2–5 axes) | | **Programming complexity** | Simple | Moderate | Complex | | **Hardware cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | **Typical machine** | Drill, punch | Lathe, simple mill | Machining centre, EDM | | **Interpolation** | None | Linear only | Linear + Circular + others | | **Feedback** | Open or closed | Closed | Closed mandatory | | **Accuracy required** | Position only | Position + size | Position + size + profile | **Application examples (2 marks):** - **PTP:** Drilling a flange plate with 8 holes. - **Straight-Cut:** Turning a cylindrical shaft on a CNC lathe. - **Contour:** Milling a complex turbine blade profile. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A PTP-controlled CNC machine has a lead screw of 6 mm pitch, driven by a servo motor through a 1:1 gear train. The motor encoder generates 1000 pulses per revolution. Determine: (i) the pulse equivalent, (ii) the number of pulses required to move the slide 240 mm. **Model Solution:** **(i) Pulse Equivalent:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta x &= \dfrac{P_t \times G_r}{S} \\ &= \dfrac{6 \times 1}{1000} \\ &= 0.006 \text{ mm/pulse} \end{aligned} $$ **(3 marks)** **(ii) Number of Pulses for 240 mm:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_p &= \dfrac{L}{\Delta x} \\ &= \dfrac{240}{0.006} \\ &= 40{,}000 \text{ pulses} \end{aligned} $$ **(4 marks)** --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically LOSE marks in PTP questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the gear ratio** in pulse-equivalent derivations — the most common error. Always state the gear ratio explicitly and check if it amplifies or reduces the screw's effective rotation. > 2. **Confusing pulse equivalent** ($\Delta x$ in mm/pulse) with **number of pulses** ($N_p$ in pulses). They are reciprocals. > 3. **Unit mismatch:** Rapid traverse is in **mm/min** — convert to **mm/s** OR convert time to **minutes** consistently. > 4. **Wrong section in comparison answers:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you compare PTP with manual machining instead of with **Straight-Cut or Contour**. > 5. **Omitting the in-position signal** in flow diagrams — it is a **key closed-loop feature** and earns a full mark. > 6. **Mixing up G-codes:** G00 is *rapid positioning* (used in PTP), G01 is *linear interpolation* (Straight-Cut), G02/G03 are *circular interpolation* (Contour). Misassignment is a frequent killer. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **PTP = Position matters, Path does NOT.** - ⭐ **Three control types:** PTP → Straight-Cut → Contour (in order of increasing complexity and cost). - ⭐ **Key formula trio:** $N_p = L / \Delta x$, where $\Delta x = (P_t \times G_r) / S$, and $T = L / f_r$. - ⭐ **PTP applications:** Drilling, boring, tapping, punching, spot-welding — anything where the cut happens at a **stationary point**. - ⭐ **Modern PTP machines are closed-loop** (servo + encoder), but **historical/open-loop stepper systems** still appear in KTU questions. - ⭐ **G00 (rapid traverse)** is the dominant motion command in PTP positioning. - ⭐ **Repeatability is always numerically less than accuracy** — remember the inequality. - ⭐ **Pulse equivalent must be expressed in mm/pulse** — always carry the units through the derivation. - ⭐ **Canned cycles (G81–G89)** automate PTP drilling — a recurring KTU sub-question topic. - ⭐ **Backlash compensation** is essential in PTP bidirectional moves — failure to mention it loses a mark. - ⭐ **Closed-loop PTP = in-position signal + error correction = mandatory terminology in block diagrams.**

topics.title: Straight Cut and Contouring Positioning - Incremental and Absolute Systems - Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems - Parts: Positional Encodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Straight Cut and Contouring Positioning - Incremental and Absolute Systems - Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems - Parts: Positional Encodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Straight Cut and Contouring Positioning (NC Motion Control)

In **Numerical Control (NC)** and **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** machine tools, the path traced by the cutting tool relative to the workpiece is governed by the **motion control strategy** programmed into the controller. Based on the geometry of the tool path, motion control is classified into three fundamental categories:

1. **Point-to-Point (PTP) Positioning** — Tool moves to a defined coordinate, regardless of path.
2. **Straight-Cut Positioning** — Tool moves in a linear, straight-line path between two points (typically along one axis at a time, or at a controlled angle on a single plane).
3. **Contouring Positioning** — Tool moves continuously along a complex curvilinear path with simultaneous multi-axis coordinated motion (2D, 2.5D, 3D, or even 5-axis contouring).

### 1.1.1 Straight-Cut Positioning (Line Milling / End Milling Linear Profile)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Straight-cut positioning* is a type of NC motion in which the tool is required to move in a **straight line** from one programmed point to another while the cutting action is taking place, but with motion restricted essentially to **one axis at a time** (or parallel multi-axis motion without simultaneous angular interpolation between non-collinear axes).

**Key Characteristics:**
- Tool path: Linear, single straight segment.
- Axes involved: Typically **one axis** moves (X or Y or Z), with the others stationary.
- Feed rate: Maintained constant along the path.
- Application: Drilling, straight-line milling, slot cutting.

### 1.1.2 Contouring Positioning (Continuous Path Control)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Contouring positioning*, also called **Continuous Path Control (CPC)** or **Continuous Traverse Control**, is a type of NC motion in which the tool is required to move along a **continuously variable curvilinear path** with **simultaneous coordinated control of two or more axes**, while cutting is in progress.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Tool path: Curves, arcs, splines, free-form surfaces.
- Axes involved: **Two or more axes move simultaneously and in coordination** (e.g., X and Y together to produce an arc via circular interpolation).
- Feed rate: Vector feed rate held constant at the tool tip (tangential velocity).
- Application: Die-mould machining, turbine blades, aerospace structural parts, cam profiles.

### 1.1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Writing with a Pen on Paper:**
> - **Point-to-Point** is like lifting the pen, moving to a new spot, and putting it down (no line drawn in transit).
> - **Straight-Cut** is like drawing one straight line from A to B (only your hand moves along one direction).
> - **Contouring** is like drawing a smooth letter "S" or the outline of a flower — your hand moves in X and Y **at the same time** to follow the curve, with the "feed" (pen speed) being the speed along the curve, not the speed of either axis alone.

The mathematical engine inside the CNC controller that converts a curved path into synchronized axis commands is the **Interpolator** (Linear, Circular, Helical, Spline, or NURBS).

---

## 1.2 Incremental and Absolute Positioning Systems

In CNC programming, every coordinate given to the machine refers to a **reference origin (zero point)**. The way this reference is defined divides NC positioning into two systems:

### 1.2.1 Absolute System (Absolute Coordinate System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** In the **absolute positioning system**, all coordinate values are referenced to a **single fixed origin** (typically the **program zero point** or **workpiece zero**). Each position command specifies the **total distance from the origin**, not from the previous tool location.

**Symbol used in G-code:** `G90` (modal — remains active until canceled)

**Example:**
- To move from (10, 20) to (40, 60), the command is: `N10 G90 X40 Y60`
- The controller calculates: ΔX = +30 mm, ΔY = +40 mm.

### 1.2.2 Incremental System (Incremental / Delta Coordinate System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** In the **incremental positioning system**, each coordinate value specifies the **distance to move from the current (previous) tool position**. The origin is effectively the *last point*, not a fixed reference.

**Symbol used in G-code:** `G91` (modal)

**Example:**
- To move from (10, 20) to (40, 60), the command is: `N10 G91 X30 Y40`
- The controller adds this to the current position.

### 1.2.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Walking on a Number Line:**
> - **Absolute:** "Stand at position 50 m" (measured from the start pole — fixed origin).
> - **Incremental:** "Walk 5 m forward from where you are right now" (measured from current spot).
>
> If you make three moves — A → B → C:
> - **Absolute** gives you the address of A, B, C (e.g., 10, 25, 40).
> - **Incremental** gives you only the *steps* (e.g., +10, +15, +15) — and if you ever lose track of where you are, every subsequent move is wrong.

---

## 1.3 Open Loop and Closed Loop Control Systems

CNC machines require a **servo control system** to drive each axis. The classification depends on whether **feedback** from the actual output (position) is used to correct the input command.

### 1.3.1 Open Loop Control System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An **open-loop control system** is one in which the **control action is independent of the actual output**. The controller sends a command pulse train to the actuator (typically a **stepper motor**), and there is **no feedback sensor** to verify whether the move was executed accurately.

**Block Diagram Concept:** `Input (Pulses) → Controller → Stepper Driver → Stepper Motor → Lead Screw → Table` (no path returning to controller).

**Characteristics:**
- **Simpler, cheaper, more reliable** (fewer components).
- **No accumulation of positioning error** if properly sized (stepper has inherent digital resolution).
- **Cannot detect lost steps** (stalled motor — controller still believes position is correct).
- **Lower torque at high speeds.**
- Used in: Small CNC routers, low-cost engravers, 3D printers (basic), educational machines.

### 1.3.2 Closed Loop Control System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **closed-loop control system** continuously **monitors the actual output position** via a feedback transducer (encoder, resolver, linear scale) and **compares it to the commanded position**. The resulting **error signal** drives the actuator (servo motor) until the error becomes zero (within tolerance).

**Block Diagram Concept:** `Input → Comparator (Σ) → Servo Amplifier → Servo Motor → Lead Screw → Table → Position Sensor → Feedback to Σ`

**Characteristics:**
- **High accuracy and repeatability**.
- **Detects and corrects errors** (load disturbance, backlash, thermal drift).
- **More complex, expensive, prone to instability** (requires tuning of PID gains).
- **Higher torque, higher speeds, higher power**.
- Used in: Industrial CNC machining centres, turning centres, EDM, high-end milling.

### 1.3.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Driving a Car:**
> - **Open Loop:** A person blindfolded pressing the accelerator for a *counted number* of pedal-pushes to reach a destination. No eyes (sensor), no steering correction.
> - **Closed Loop:** A normal driver with eyes (camera/encoder) constantly comparing the road (desired path) to the car's actual lane position (feedback), and continuously turning the steering wheel to keep error = 0.
>
> The "error signal" is the gap between where the steering wheel points and where the road is.

### 1.3.4 Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | **Absent** | **Present** (Position sensor) |
| Motor | **Stepper** (digital) | **Servo** (analog/digital) |
| Accuracy | Limited (~±0.01 mm typical) | High (~±0.001 mm or better) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Error detection | **No** | **Yes** (alarm on following error) |
| Speed | Low to medium | High |
| Reliability | High (simple) | Moderate (tuning required) |
| Application | Low-end CNC, 3D printers | Industrial CNC, precision machines |

---

## 1.4 Parts of Positional Encoders

A **positional encoder** (or simply **encoder**) is a transducer that converts **linear or angular mechanical displacement** into a **digital electrical signal**, which is fed back to the controller in a closed-loop system.

Encoders are broadly classified by their output type and physical principle:

| Type | Output | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Incremental Encoder** | Pulses (counted from a reference) | Spindle speed, basic axis feedback |
| **Absolute Encoder** | Unique binary/gray code per position | Tool magazine, rotary table, axis homing |
| **Linear Encoder** | Linear pulses along a scale | Direct position measurement on slides |
| **Rotary Encoder** | Pulses per revolution on a shaft | Motor shaft, lead screw, spindle |

### 1.4.1 Main Parts of an Optical Encoder (Standard Reference for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A typical incremental/absolute optical encoder consists of five main parts:**
>
> 1. **Light Source** — Usually a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** providing a stable, monochromatic beam.
> 2. **Condenser Lens / Collimator** — Focuses the LED light into a parallel, narrow beam.
> 3. **Code Disc / Scale (Reticle)** — A glass or metal disc (rotary) or strip (linear) on which fine **transparent and opaque lines (gratings)** are etched. The number of lines determines the encoder's **resolution** (e.g., 5000 lines/rev → 20,000 counts/rev with ×4 quadrature).
> 4. **Photodetector / Photodiode Array** — Receives the light pulses passing through (or reflected from) the gratings and converts them into electrical pulses.
> 5. **Signal Conditioning Electronics** — Amplifies, shapes (squares), and outputs the digital pulses (often in **quadrature — A, B, and index Z channels**).

### 1.4.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — A Barcode on a Spinning Wheel:**
> Imagine a pizza wheel spinning under a flashlight, with black-and-white "barcode" stripes painted on it. A sensor (like a barcode reader at a supermarket checkout) sits on the other side. Each time a white stripe passes, the sensor "sees" light and produces a pulse. By *counting* the pulses, the controller knows how far the wheel has turned. An **absolute encoder** uses a *unique* pattern of black/white segments (like a binary number) at every angle — so even after a power-off, the controller instantly knows "we are at position 173°" without counting from zero.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Straight Cut vs Contouring (Step-by-Step)

### 2.1.1 Straight-Cut Positioning Logic

1. **Command Generation:** NC program issues linear move to a target coordinate (e.g., `G01 X100 Y0 F200`).
2. **Interpolator (Linear Interpolator) Output:** Since only one axis is moving (X moves, Y stays at 0), the interpolator generates a single axis command profile (ramp-up, constant feed, ramp-down).
3. **Drive Signal:** Command pulses sent to that single axis servo/stepper driver.
4. **Axis Movement:** X-axis slides linearly, holding Y stationary.
5. **Cutter Engagement:** Cutting tool engages the workpiece along this straight path (e.g., milling a slot, facing, turning a cylinder).

### 2.1.2 Contouring Positioning Logic

1. **Command Generation:** Program specifies start point, end point, arc center (I, J, K) or a continuous curve (e.g., `G02 X50 Y50 I25 J0 F150` for CW arc).
2. **Interpolator (Circular / Spline / NURBS) Output:** The interpolator **simultaneously generates** two (or more) axis command profiles — for example, a sinusoidal X and a co-sinusoidal Y to produce a circular path.
3. **Vector Feedrate Maintenance:** The interpolator constantly recomputes the per-axis feed such that the **tangential vector speed** = $\sqrt{\dot{X}^2 + \dot{Y}^2 + \dot{Z}^2}$ = programmed F.
4. **Multi-axis Drive:** Both X and Y servos receive synchronized command profiles.
5. **Cutter Engagement:** Tool traces a circular/curved profile on the workpiece.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Interpolator** is the heart of contouring — without it, the controller can only do point-to-point or axis-parallel cuts.

## 2.2 Theoretical Logic — Absolute vs Incremental Systems

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Foundation

Let $P_n$ = position after the n-th move, $C_n$ = the command value in move n.

**Absolute System:**
$$P_n = C_n \quad \text{(position is always the command)}$$

**Incremental System:**
$$P_n = P_{n-1} + C_n \quad \text{(position accumulates the command)}$$

### 2.2.2 Why Each System is Used

- **Absolute** is preferred for:
  - Easier debugging (any line is meaningful on its own).
  - Safer (no error propagation if a block is missed).
  - Standard for **most production CNC work**.

- **Incremental** is preferred for:
  - **Subroutines and loops** (e.g., drilling a bolt circle: same ΔX, ΔY repeated).
  - **Mirror imaging** (just negate sign).
  - **Compensation adjustments** (tool length offset, cutter compensation).
  - Old systems with limited memory (each block shorter).

### 2.2.3 Mixing Codes

G90 and G91 are *modal* — they stay in effect until the other is commanded. A single program can switch between them when useful.

## 2.3 Open vs Closed Loop — Control Theory Foundation

### 2.3.1 Open Loop — Stepper Motor Resolution

For a stepper motor driving a lead screw, the **table displacement per pulse** is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{P}{N \cdot s} \quad \text{(mm per pulse)}$$

Where:
- $P$ = lead screw pitch (mm/rev)
- $N$ = number of steps per motor revolution (steps/rev)
- $s$ = microstepping factor (e.g., 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32)

> **Example:** A 1.8° stepper (200 steps/rev) with 5 mm pitch lead screw, 8× microstepping:
> $$\Delta x = \frac{5}{200 \times 8} = 0.003125 \text{ mm/pulse} = 3.125 \text{ μm}$$

### 2.3.2 Closed Loop — Position Error (Following Error)

The **following error** $e(t)$ is the instantaneous difference between **commanded position** $X_c(t)$ and **actual position** $X_a(t)$:

$$e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)$$

The **PID controller** drives this error to zero (or to a small steady-state value). The servo motor torque command is:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $K_p$ = proportional gain (instantaneous response)
- $K_i$ = integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ = derivative gain (damping, anticipates future error)

### 2.3.3 Steady-State Error and Accuracy

For a step input of magnitude $V_c$ (commanded velocity), the steady-state following error in a proportional servo is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{V_c}{K_v} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

Where $K_v$ is the **velocity error constant** (s⁻¹) — a measure of servo stiffness. **Higher $K_v$ → lower error → stiffer, more accurate axis.**

## 2.4 Encoder Resolution and Accuracy

### 2.4.1 Rotary Incremental Encoder

Resolution (pulses per revolution) is set by the **line count** $L$ on the disc. With **quadrature decoding (×4)**, the effective resolution is:

$$R_{eff} = 4L \quad \text{(counts/rev)}$$

**Angular resolution per count:**
$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{R_{eff}} = \frac{90°}{L}$$

**Linear resolution at lead screw** (pitch $P$):
$$\Delta x = \frac{P}{R_{eff}} = \frac{P}{4L}$$

### 2.4.2 Absolute Encoder Output

An $n$-bit absolute encoder can distinguish:
$$N = 2^n \quad \text{unique positions per revolution}$$

For an 8-bit encoder: $2^8 = 256$ positions → $\dfrac{360°}{256} = 1.406°$/step
For a 16-bit encoder: $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ positions → $\approx 0.0055°$/step

**Output code types:** Natural Binary, **Gray Code** (only one bit changes at a time — eliminates transition errors).

### 2.4.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop displacement per pulse | $\Delta x = \dfrac{P}{N \cdot s}$ | mm/pulse | Stepper axis resolution |
| 2 | Following error (closed loop) | $e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)$ | mm | Servo error analysis |
| 3 | PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | V or % | Servo amplifier input |
| 4 | Steady-state error (ramp input) | $e_{ss} = V_c / K_v$ | mm | Servo stiffness |
| 5 | Effective rotary resolution (×4 quad) | $R_{eff} = 4L$ | counts/rev | Encoder feedback |
| 6 | Linear resolution on lead screw | $\Delta x_{enc} = P / R_{eff}$ | mm/count | Axis position feedback |
| 7 | Angular resolution per count | $\theta_{res} = 90° / L$ | °/count | Rotary accuracy |
| 8 | Absolute encoder positions | $N = 2^n$ | — | Number of unique angles |
| 9 | Tangential feed (contouring) | $F_t = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | mm/min | Vector feedrate |
| 10 | Incremental accumulation | $P_n = P_{n-1} + C_n$ | mm | G91 kinematics |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **directly applicable to numerical problems** that appear in KTU end-semester examinations.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Straight-cut and contouring positioning** are the **backbone of modern CAM/CNC machining** — every automotive crankshaft, aircraft wing skin, and mould cavity relies on accurate contouring.
- **Absolute vs Incremental** choice affects **cycle time, file size, and error recovery** in production.
- **Closed-loop control** with **high-resolution encoders** is what enables **sub-micron precision** in semiconductor lithography and optical lens manufacturing.
- **Position encoders** are also the key feedback element in **robotic joints, CNC, 3D printers (advanced), telescopes, and electron microscopes**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stepper Motor Linear Resolution

### Problem Statement
A CNC plasma cutter uses a stepper motor with 1.8° step angle driving a lead screw of 5 mm pitch. The drive uses 16× microstepping. Calculate the linear resolution of the axis in mm per pulse and in μm per pulse.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Step angle: $\theta_s = 1.8°$ per step
- Lead screw pitch: $P = 5$ mm/rev
- Microstepping factor: $s = 16$

**Step 1 — Compute basic steps per revolution (N):**
A full revolution is 360°, so:
$$N = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360}{1.8} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Apply microstepping:**
With 16× microstepping, each "electrical step" is subdivided into 16 microsteps, so:
$$N_{micro} = N \cdot s = 200 \times 16 = 3200 \text{ microsteps/rev}$$

**Step 3 — Linear displacement per microstep (one command pulse):**
The lead screw advances $P$ mm in one full revolution. So per microstep:
$$\Delta x = \frac{P}{N_{micro}} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{3200}$$

$$\Delta x = 0.0015625 \text{ mm/pulse}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to microns:**
$$\Delta x = 0.0015625 \text{ mm} \times 1000 \,\frac{\mu\text{m}}{\text{mm}} = 1.5625 \,\mu\text{m/pulse}$$

> **Conclusion:** The axis has a linear resolution of **0.0015625 mm/pulse** (or **1.5625 μm/pulse**). This is the *smallest* commanded motion the open-loop system can theoretically issue.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Following Error and Servo Stiffness

### Problem Statement
A closed-loop CNC axis has a velocity error constant $K_v = 50 \text{ s}^{-1}$. During a contouring pass, the programmed feed rate is $F = 1500 \text{ mm/min}$. Find the steady-state following error.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $K_v = 50 \,\text{s}^{-1}$
- Programmed feed: $V_c = 1500 \text{ mm/min}$

**Step 1 — Convert feed rate to mm/s:**
$$V_c = 1500 \,\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{min}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}} = 25 \,\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state error formula for a ramp input:**
$$e_{ss} = \frac{V_c}{K_v} = \frac{25 \,\text{mm/s}}{50 \,\text{s}^{-1}}$$

$$e_{ss} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
The actual tool position will **lag** the commanded position by **0.5 mm** during constant velocity cutting. This lag is compensated by the controller, but if it grows beyond the **position tolerance window** (set in machine parameters), the machine will **alarm** ("following error too large").

> **Engineering Note:** For a high-accuracy machine, $K_v$ must be large enough to keep $e_{ss}$ within ±0.01 mm at maximum feed. Hence industrial CNC machines have $K_v$ in the range of 100–1000 s⁻¹.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Encoder Resolution and Effective Axis Resolution

### Problem Statement
A servo motor drives an axis through a 10 mm pitch ball screw. The motor has an incremental rotary encoder with 2500 lines/rev, decoded in quadrature (×4). Find:
(a) Effective counts per revolution of the motor.
(b) Angular resolution per count.
(c) Linear resolution at the table.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Lead screw pitch: $P = 10$ mm/rev
- Encoder line count: $L = 2500$ lines/rev
- Quadrature factor: $Q = 4$

**Step (a) — Effective counts per revolution:**
$$R_{eff} = Q \cdot L = 4 \times 2500 = 10{,}000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step (b) — Angular resolution per count:**
$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{R_{eff}} = \frac{360°}{10{,}000}$$

$$\theta_{res} = 0.036° \text{ per count}$$

**Step (c) — Linear resolution at the table:**
The table advances 10 mm in one full revolution. Per count:
$$\Delta x_{table} = \frac{P}{R_{eff}} = \frac{10}{10{,}000} = 0.001 \text{ mm} = 1 \,\mu\text{m per count}$$

> **Conclusion:** This axis can resolve position changes as small as **1 μm** in the controller. With sub-micron glass or steel linear scales on the slide, resolution can be pushed to **0.1 μm** or better.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Absolute System Coordinate Tracking

### Problem Statement
A CNC mill is in **G90 (Absolute) mode**. Starting position is (0, 0). The program executes:
`N10 G90 X30 Y40`
`N20 G90 X60 Y20`
`N21 G91 X10 Y-5` *(switched to incremental)*
`N30 G90 X80 Y50`

List the actual machine positions after each block.

### Step-by-Step Solution

Let $P_n = (X_n, Y_n)$.

| Block | Mode | Command | Logic | Resulting Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | — | — | $P_0 = (0, 0)$ | **(0, 0)** |
| N10 | G90 | X30 Y40 | $P_1 = (30, 40)$ | **(30, 40)** |
| N20 | G90 | X60 Y20 | $P_2 = (60, 20)$ | **(60, 20)** |
| N21 | G91 | X10 Y-5 | $P_3 = P_2 + (10, -5) = (70, 15)$ | **(70, 15)** |
| N30 | G90 | X80 Y50 | $P_4 = (80, 50)$ | **(80, 50)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mode **G90** or **G91** is **sticky (modal)**. Even after a single block in G91 (N21), the next block (N30) reverts to G90 only because the program explicitly reissued G90. Without it, the G91 would still be active.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for KTU

| If the question says… | The answer involves… |
|---|---|
| "Straight-line milling at a single angle" | **Straight-cut positioning** (single-axis dominant or parallel motion) |
| "Circular interpolation / continuous path / multi-axis simultaneous" | **Contouring positioning** (interpolator) |
| "G90 / coordinates are from a fixed origin" | **Absolute** system |
| "G91 / coordinates are steps from the current point" | **Incremental** system |
| "Stepper motor, no feedback" | **Open loop** |
| "Servo motor, encoder feedback, following error" | **Closed loop** |
| "Part that shines light through a grating to a photodiode" | **Optical encoder** (5 parts: LED, lens, disc, detector, electronics) |
| "Unique binary code per position, power-off memory" | **Absolute encoder** |
| "Pulses counted from a reference, loses position on power-off" | **Incremental encoder** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Open vs Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        A1[NC Program / Pulse Train] --> A2[Pulse Generator / Controller]
        A2 --> A3[Stepper Motor Driver]
        A3 --> A4[Stepper Motor]
        A4 --> A5[Lead Screw and Table]
        A5 -.No Feedback.-> A2
    end

    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        B1[NC Program / Commanded Position] --> B2[Comparator Summation]
        B2 --> B3[PID Controller]
        B3 --> B4[Servo Amplifier]
        B4 --> B5[Servo Motor]
        B5 --> B6[Lead Screw and Table]
        B6 --> B7[Position Encoder / Resolver]
        B7 --> B2
    end
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Parts of an Optical Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Supply] --> B[LED Light Source]
    B --> C[Collimating Lens]
    C --> D[Grating Disc or Linear Scale]
    D --> E[Photodiode Array]
    E --> F[Amplifier and Schmitt Trigger]
    F --> G[Quadrature Decoder A B Z]
    G --> H[CNC Controller Feedback Input]

    subgraph DETAIL["Index Channel and Direction Logic"]
        G --> H1[Index Pulse Z - One per Rev]
        G --> H2[Channel A - Sine pulses]
        G --> H3[Channel B - Cosine pulses - 90 deg phase shift]
    end
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Straight Cut vs Contouring Decision Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([NC Program Block Read]) --> Q1{Path Geometry?}
    Q1 -- Single axis linear move --> SC[Straight Cut Positioning]
    SC --> SC1[Linear Interpolator]
    SC1 --> SC2[Single axis command stream]
    SC2 --> SC3[Axis slides linearly - cutting along path]

    Q1 -- Two or more axes move simultaneously --> CP[Contouring Positioning]
    CP --> CP1{Type of curve?}
    CP1 -- Arc --> CPI[Circular Interpolator G02 G03]
    CP1 -- Line at angle --> CPL[Linear Interpolator with 2 axes]
    CP1 -- Free form --> CPN[NURBS or Spline Interpolator]
    CPI --> CP2[Vector feedrate computed each cycle]
    CPL --> CP2
    CPN --> CP2
    CP2 --> CP3[Synchronized multi axis commands]
    CP3 --> CP4[Tool traces the curve]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Absolute vs Incremental Coordinate Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ABS["ABSOLUTE G90"]
        P1[Program Zero / Work Origin] --> P2[Point A 10 20]
        P1 --> P3[Point B 40 60]
        P1 --> P4[Point C 70 90]
    end

    subgraph INC["INCREMENTAL G91"]
        Q0[Current Tool Position] --> Q1[Step to B: 30 40]
        Q1 --> Q2[Step to C: 30 30]
    end
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop CNC Axis with Encoder Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Operator Console / G code] --> CTRL[CNC Controller]
    CTRL --> CMP[Position Comparator]
    CMP --> PID[PID Servo Algorithm]
    PID --> AMP[Servo Amplifier]
    AMP --> M[Servo Motor]
    M --> LS[Ball Screw]
    LS --> TBL[Machine Table / Spindle]
    TBL --> ENC[Rotary Encoder on Motor Shaft]
    TBL --> LSC[Optional Linear Scale on Slide]
    ENC --> CMP
    LSC --> CMP
    CTRL --> OP
    PID -. Following Error .-> MON[Monitor and Alarm System]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between straight-cut and contouring positioning in NC systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Straight-Cut | Contouring |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Linear, single straight segment | Curvilinear, complex curves |
| Axes | **One axis at a time** (or parallel) | **Two or more axes simultaneously** |
| Interpolator | Linear (single axis) | Circular / Spline / NURBS |
| Example | **Drilling a hole, milling a straight slot** | **Milling a circular pocket, machining a cam profile** |

> **Key point (1 mark):** Contouring requires **simultaneous coordinated multi-axis motion** with a constant tangential feed rate.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the main parts of an optical position encoder and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **LED Light Source** — emits a stable light beam toward the grating (1 mark).
2. **Collimating Lens** — focuses the LED light into a parallel beam for sharp projection (½ mark).
3. **Code Disc / Scale (Grating)** — carries fine transparent/opaque lines whose count sets the resolution (1 mark).
4. **Photodiode / Photodetector Array** — converts the modulated light into electrical pulses (½ mark).
5. **Signal Conditioning Electronics** — amplifies, squares, and outputs the A, B, Z quadrature digital signals (½ mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Q11 or Q12, 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of a **closed-loop CNC positioning system** using a servo motor and incremental encoder. Discuss the role of the **following error** and **PID controller**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling machine axis is driven by a stepper motor with 1.8° step angle, connected to a 6 mm pitch lead screw. The drive uses 8× microstepping. Calculate:
  (i) Linear resolution of the axis in mm/pulse.
  (ii) Number of pulses required to move the table **150 mm**.
  (iii) If a **linear optical scale** with 20 μm grating pitch (×4 quadrature) is added for direct position feedback, what is the new linear resolution? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- (2 marks) **Block diagram and description**: The controller sends a commanded position $X_c(t)$ to a comparator. The actual position $X_a(t)$ is measured by the incremental encoder mounted on the motor (or via linear scale on the slide). The comparator generates an **error signal**:
$$e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)$$
- (2 marks) **PID role**: The error is processed by a PID controller. $K_p$ provides the **proportional push** to reduce error quickly. $K_i$ **accumulates error over time** to eliminate any steady-state offset. $K_d$ acts on the **rate of change of error** to damp out oscillations and provide smooth motion. The combined output drives the servo amplifier, which in turn powers the servo motor.
- (2 marks) **Following error and closed-loop meaning**: The instantaneous difference between commanded and actual position is called the **following error**. In a properly tuned system, $e(t) \to e_{ss}$ (a small constant) during motion, and $e(t) \to 0$ at rest. Closed-loop control **automatically corrects** for load disturbances, friction variation, and thermal drift — capabilities absent in open-loop stepper systems.
- (1 mark) **Sketch** (block diagram with command, comparator, PID, amplifier, motor, encoder feedback).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Linear resolution per pulse (3 marks):**
- Steps per rev: $N = 360° / 1.8° = 200$ steps/rev
- With 8× microstepping: $N_{micro} = 200 \times 8 = 1600$ microsteps/rev
- Linear resolution:
$$\Delta x = \frac{P}{N_{micro}} = \frac{6}{1600} = 0.00375 \text{ mm/pulse} = 3.75 \,\mu\text{m/pulse}$$

**(ii) Pulses for 150 mm (2 marks):**
$$N_{pulses} = \frac{150 \text{ mm}}{0.00375 \text{ mm/pulse}} = 40{,}000 \text{ pulses}$$

**(iii) New resolution with linear scale (2 marks):**
- Grating pitch: $20 \,\mu\text{m} = 0.020$ mm
- With ×4 quadrature:
$$\Delta x_{new} = \frac{0.020 \text{ mm}}{4} = 0.005 \text{ mm/pulse} = 5 \,\mu\text{m/pulse}$$

> **Comparison**: The stepper provides 3.75 μm resolution (command) but the linear scale **verifies** the actual position at 5 μm — a **semi-closed-loop** configuration. Note: linear scale resolution and stepper resolution don't have to match; the controller typically uses the encoder for the *command loop* and the linear scale for the *true position loop* in **dual-loop** architecture.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **absolute optical encoder**. How does it differ from an **incremental encoder**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC lathe is in **G90** mode starting from (0, 0). The following blocks are executed:
```
N10 G90 X30 Z-20 F100
N20 G91 X10 Z-5
N30 G90 X50 Z-35
N40 G91 X-5 Z2
N50 G90 X0 Z0
```
Determine the tool position after **each block**. State which block performs **straight-cut** and which performs **contouring** if any. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- (2 marks) **Construction**: An absolute optical encoder consists of:
  - **LED + lens** (light source + collimator)
  - **Code disc with concentric tracks** — each track has a unique pattern of opaque/transparent segments arranged so that any angular position produces a **unique binary (or Gray) code** at the photodetector.
  - **Photodiode array** — one photodiode per track (e.g., for 8-bit encoder, 8 photodiodes).
  - **Output interface** — produces a parallel binary word.
- (2 marks) **Working**: As the shaft rotates, the disc interrupts the LED light according to its coded pattern. The photodiodes simultaneously read all tracks, producing a **unique N-bit code** for every angle. The controller reads this code in a single bus cycle and knows the exact angle, even after power-off.
- (1 mark) **Gray code preference**: Gray code is used because only **one bit changes** at a time between adjacent codes, eliminating transition errors.
- (2 marks) **Difference from incremental encoder**:

| Feature | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (count from index) | Unique binary word |
| Position after power-off | **Lost** (must re-home) | **Retained** |
| Tracks | 2–3 (A, B, Z) | N tracks (one per bit) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Speed of reading | Continuous count | Single parallel read |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Tracking positions $(X, Z)$:

| Block | Mode | Command | Calculation | Final Position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | — | — | — | (0, 0) |
| N10 | G90 | X30 Z-20 | Direct | **(30, −20)** |
| N20 | G91 | X10 Z-5 | (30+10, −20+−5) = (40, −25) | **(40, −25)** |
| N30 | G90 | X50 Z-35 | Direct | **(50, −35)** |
| N40 | G91 | X-5 Z2 | (50+−5, −35+2) = (45, −33) | **(45, −33)** |
| N50 | G90 | X0 Z0 | Direct | **(0, 0)** |

- **Straight-cut identification (1 mark):** All moves here are single-axis dominant (X and Z on a lathe move independently), and all are along machine axes. Hence **N10, N20, N30, N40, N50 are all straight-cut positioning** (axis-parallel moves).
- **Contouring (1 mark):** None of the blocks is a contouring move — there is no simultaneous multi-axis path with circular or spline interpolation. For contouring, the program would have used **G02 (CW arc)** or **G03 (CCW arc)** with I, K offsets, e.g., `G02 X60 Z-30 I10 K0 F80`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "closed loop" with "absolute encoder"** — Closed/open loop refers to the *control system architecture*, while absolute/incremental refers to the *encoder type* (or coordinate system). They are independent classifications!
> 2. **Forgetting microstepping** in stepper resolution calculations — a 1.8° stepper is 200 steps/rev, but with N× microstepping, multiply by N before dividing pitch.
> 3. **Forgetting the ×4 quadrature factor** for incremental encoders — line count L means **4L counts/rev** when decoded.
> 4. **Mixing G90/G91 errors**: Once G91 is set, the *next* absolute move requires a fresh G90. A common bug is to assume G90 reverts automatically — it does NOT.
> 5. **Writing "contouring" for any multi-axis move** — contouring specifically requires *simultaneous* axis motion with an interpolator; two independent sequential moves are not contouring.
> 6. **Listing encoder parts incorrectly**: KTU expects exactly 5 parts for the optical encoder — LED, lens, disc, photodetector, signal conditioner. Missing any one costs marks.
> 7. **Position vs Resolution confusion**: Resolution is the *smallest measurable* change; accuracy is the *maximum error* from true position. They are not the same.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Three types of NC motion**: Point-to-Point, **Straight-Cut**, **Contouring**.
- **Straight-cut** = single-axis or axis-parallel linear move, linear interpolator, one path segment.
- **Contouring** = simultaneous multi-axis motion along a curve, **interpolator (circular/spline/NURBS)** required, vector feedrate.
- **Interpolator** is the key element enabling contouring.
- **G90 = Absolute**, coordinates measured from a **fixed program origin**.
- **G91 = Incremental**, coordinates are **steps from the current tool position**.
- $P_n = P_{n-1} + C_n$ (incremental recurrence).
- **G90 / G91 are modal** — they remain active until the other is commanded.
- **Open loop** = stepper motor, no feedback, simple, cheap, cannot detect lost steps.
- **Closed loop** = servo motor + feedback sensor (encoder/resolver), **PID control**, high accuracy.
- **Following error** $e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)$ — drives the PID; should approach zero at rest.
- **Steady-state error** $e_{ss} = V_c / K_v$ — increases with feed, decreases with $K_v$ (servo stiffness).
- **PID**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ — P responds, I eliminates offset, D damps.
- **Stepper linear resolution** $\Delta x = P / (N \cdot s)$.
- **Encoder resolution** (rotary, ×4 quad) = $4L$ counts/rev, linear = $P / (4L)$ mm/count.
- **Absolute encoder** has $2^n$ unique positions; outputs **Gray code** (one bit changes at a time).
- **Incremental encoder** outputs A, B, Z channels — needs **homing** to find absolute position.
- **5 parts of an optical encoder**: **LED, collimating lens, code disc/scale (grating), photodetector, signal conditioner**.
- **Linear scales** give direct table position; **rotary encoders** give shaft angle — both used in dual-loop control.
- **Engineering applications**: CNC machining centres, EDM, 3D printers (closed-loop variants), robotics, telescopes, semiconductor lithography.
- **KTU key distinction**: "Closed loop" ≠ "Absolute encoder" — they are independent ideas (axis control vs. coordinate system).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axis and Spindle Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Axis and Spindle Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Axis Drives (Feed Drives) — Formal Definition

**Axis Drives**, also called **Feed Drives** or **Servo Drives**, are the electromechanical/hydraulic subsystems of a CNC machine tool responsible for producing the **controlled linear (or rotary) motion** of the machine's slides, tables, and tool carriers along the programmed axes (X, Y, Z, and auxiliary A, B, C axes). They convert **electrical/ hydraulic command signals** from the CNC controller into **precise mechanical displacement** with the required speed, acceleration, and accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"Axis and Spindle Drives are the power-transmitting elements that drive the linear feed motion of the machine slides and the rotary motion of the spindle, respectively, in response to commands from the CNC unit."*

The primary components of an axis drive are:
1. **Drive Motor** (Servo / Stepper / Linear Motor)
2. **Transmission Element** (Ball Screw, Rack & Pinion, or Leadscrew)
3. **Feedback Device** (Linear/Rotary Encoder, Resolver, Glass Scale)
4. **Position/Speed Controller** (Servo Amplifier / Drive)
5. **Guideways** (Linear Motion Guides)

## 1.2 Spindle Drives — Formal Definition

**Spindle Drives** are the dedicated power systems that rotate the **machine spindle**, which holds the cutting tool (milling/drilling) or the workpiece (turning). They provide the necessary **rotational speed (rpm)** and **torque** demanded by the cutting process. Modern spindle drives operate over a wide constant-power and constant-torque range using AC induction motors or brushless DC servo motors coupled to a **spindle gearbox** or direct-drive spindles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Axis Drive** = Linear motion (mm/min) — Position-controlled
> - **Spindle Drive** = Rotary motion (rpm) — Speed-controlled

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a CNC machine as a **highly skilled carpenter's workshop**:
- The **Spindle Drive** is the **electric drill** (the spinning tool that does the cutting). You control *how fast* it spins.
- The **Axis Drives** are the **worker's hands moving the wood** under the drill — left-right (X), forward-backward (Y), and up-down (Z). You control *where* and *how fast* the wood moves.

The **CNC controller** is the **carpenter's brain** that tells the hands and the drill exactly what to do. The **feedback devices** are the **carpenter's eyes** — constantly checking that the wood is where it should be and the drill is spinning at the right speed.

> [!TIP]
> A simple memory trick: **S**pindle = **S**pin (rotational, like a spinning top 🌀), **A**xis = **A**dvance (linear, like a sliding drawer ➡️).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **standardized** in KTU-board numerical problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle Speed | N | **rev/min (rpm)** | 50 – 30,000 |
| Feed Rate | f | **mm/min** | 10 – 10,000 |
| Cutting Force | Fc | **Newton (N)** | 100 – 50,000 |
| Spindle Power | P | **kW** | 0.5 – 75 |
| Torque | T | **N·m** | 1 – 1,500 |
| Efficiency | η | dimensionless | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Acceleration | a | **m/s²** | up to 1 g |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Drives

### 2.1.1 Electrical Drives (Most Common in Modern CNC)

**A. DC Servo Motors**
- Used in older CNC machines (1970s–1990s).
- Field control or armature control for speed variation.
- Excellent speed regulation, but **brush wear** limits use.
- Speed range: **1 : 10,000** (very wide).

**B. AC Induction Motors (3-Phase Squirrel Cage)**
- **Industry standard** for spindle drives today.
- Combined with **Vector Control (VC)** or **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** for variable speed.
- Robust, maintenance-free, high power.
- Coupled with **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** for speed control.

**C. Brushless DC (BLDC) Servo Motors**
- Used for **high-speed spindles** (up to 100,000 rpm).
- Electronic commutation, no brushes.
- Excellent for direct-drive spindles.

**D. Stepper Motors**
- Open-loop positioning (no feedback).
- Used in low-cost CNC, small machines, and 3D printers.
- Step angle: typically **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.75°** (480 steps/rev).
- Risk of **stalling** under high load.

**E. Linear Motors**
- Direct linear motion (no ball screw required).
- Used in **high-speed machining (HSM)** centers.
- Eliminates backlash, but expensive.

### 2.1.2 Hydraulic Drives
- Used in older machines and heavy-duty applications.
- Hydraulic cylinder + servo valve for axis motion.
- Hydraulic motor for spindle.
- **Stiff and powerful**, but messy and inefficient.

### 2.1.3 Pneumatic Drives
- Limited to **simple on/off** motions (clamping, tool change).
- Not used for precision axis control.

## 2.2 Axis Drive Components in Detail

### 2.2.1 Ball Screw (Most Common Transmission)
A **ball screw** converts rotary motion into linear motion with **>90% efficiency**.

**Lead (L):** Axial distance moved per one revolution.
**Pitch (P):** Distance between adjacent thread grooves.
**Lead vs. Pitch:** Single-start = Lead = Pitch; Multi-start: Lead = n × Pitch.

**Backlash:** Lost motion when reversing direction. Ball screws have backlash of **0.01 mm or less**; preloaded types: **0.001–0.005 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backlash elimination** is critical for bi-directional accuracy. Methods:
> 1. Preloaded ball nut (double-nut type)
> 2. Anti-backlash gearing
> 3. Software backlash compensation

### 2.2.2 Servo Motor Loop (Closed-Loop)

The axis drive operates in a **closed-loop position control** mode:

```
Reference Position (CNC command) → Compare → Error Signal
                                              ↓
                                       Servo Amplifier
                                              ↓
                                       Servo Motor
                                              ↓
                                       Ball Screw
                                              ↓
                                       Linear Table
                                              ↓
                                       Encoder (Feedback) ──┐
                                                          │
                                       (Back to Compare) ←─┘
```

## 2.3 Spindle Drive Power-Torque-Speed Relationship

A spindle motor is rated for:
- **Continuous Power** (Pmax): The maximum power deliverable continuously.
- **Base Speed (Nb):** The speed at which maximum torque begins to drop.
- **Maximum Torque (Tmax):** Available up to base speed.
- **Constant-Torque Region:** Below base speed, motor delivers max torque.
- **Constant-Power Region:** Above base speed, torque decreases as 1/N.

**Mathematically:**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60,000} \quad \text{(kW, with N in rpm, T in N·m)}$$

Rearranging for torque:

$$T = \frac{60,000 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{9550 \cdot P}{N} \quad \text{(N·m)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **9550** appears frequently in KTU numericals. Memorize it!
> **T = 9550 × P / N** (with P in kW, N in rpm, T in N·m)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60,000}$ | Spindle power from torque & speed | kW |
| 2 | $T = \frac{9550 \cdot P}{N}$ | Torque from power & speed | N·m |
| 3 | $P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000 \cdot \eta}$ | Cutting power requirement | kW |
| 4 | $F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f$ | Cutting force (empirical) | N |
| 5 | $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| 6 | $T_m = \frac{F \cdot L}{2 \pi \eta} + T_{preload}$ | Ball screw drive torque | N·m |
| 7 | $f_{rapid} = N_{motor} \cdot L$ | Rapid feed rate | mm/min |
| 8 | $N_{step} = \frac{\theta_{step}}{360}$ | Steps per revolution | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{feed} = \frac{\theta_{motor}}{360} \cdot L$ | Feed resolution | mm/pulse |
| 10 | $T_{acc} = J \cdot \alpha$ | Acceleration torque | N·m |
| 11 | $P_{acc} = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2 \cdot f_{cycle}$ | Acceleration power | W |
| 12 | $V = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF of motor | V |

Where:
- $N$ = spindle speed (rpm), $T$ = torque (N·m)
- $F_c$ = cutting force (N), $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $L$ = lead of ball screw (mm/rev)
- $\eta$ = efficiency (decimal)
- $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $\alpha$ = angular acceleration (rad/s²)
- $K_e$ = motor EMF constant (V·s/rad)

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- The **spindle power equation** is used daily by CAM programmers to select proper cutting parameters and verify that the machine has enough power for a given job.
- The **torque equation** is used in machine selection — a small lathe may have only 50 N·m of torque, so heavy radial cuts would stall it.
- The **ball screw torque equation** is used to size the axis servo motor — under-sizing leads to lost motion; over-sizing wastes money.
- The **acceleration torque** determines how fast a machine can rapid-traverse without losing position — critical for **productivity**.
