<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Power from Cutting Force

**Starting point (KTU Board standard):**

The cutting force $F_c$ acts at the cutting edge moving with velocity $V_c$ (m/min). The instantaneous cutting power $P_c$ (in Watts) is force times velocity.

$$P_c = F_c \cdot v_c \quad \text{(W, with } v_c \text{ in m/s)}$$

**Step 1 — Unit conversion:**
Since $V_c$ is given in **m/min**, convert to m/s by dividing by 60.

$$P_c = F_c \cdot \frac{V_c}{60} \quad \text{(W)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kW:**

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

**Step 3 — Account for drive efficiency:**
Mechanical losses in the spindle (gearbox, bearings, motor) are absorbed by efficiency $\eta$.

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_c}{\eta} = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000 \cdot \eta} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;P_{motor} = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000 \cdot \eta} \; \text{kW}\;}$$

> This is the **most frequently asked KTU numerical formula** for Module 3.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque from Power and Speed

**Starting from fundamental definition:**

Power in Watts = Torque × Angular Velocity:

$$P = T \cdot \omega \quad \text{(W, with } T \text{ in N·m, } \omega \text{ in rad/s)}$$

**Step 1 — Express $\omega$ in rpm:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$P = T \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{(W)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for T:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{30 \cdot P}{\pi N} \quad \text{(N·m, with P in W)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert P to kW (divide by 1000):**

$$T = \frac{30 \cdot (P \times 1000)}{\pi N} = \frac{30{,}000 \cdot P}{\pi N} \quad \text{(N·m, with P in kW)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant:**

$$\frac{30{,}000}{\pi} = \frac{30{,}000}{3.14159} = 9549.3 \approx 9550$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;T = \frac{9550 \cdot P}{N} \quad \text{(N·m, with P in kW, N in rpm)}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Ball Screw Drive Torque (Axis Drive)

**Step 1 — Ideal work balance:**

If the motor delivers torque $T_{ideal}$ and the table moves a distance $L$ (one revolution of screw), the work done is:

$$W_{motor} = T_{ideal} \cdot 2 \pi$$

The work output on the table (force $F$ times distance $L$):

$$W_{table} = F \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Account for efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{W_{table}}{W_{motor}} = \frac{F \cdot L}{T_{ideal} \cdot 2 \pi}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for torque:**

$$T_{ideal} = \frac{F \cdot L}{2 \pi \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 4 — Add preload torque $T_{preload}$:**

$$\boxed{\;T_m = \frac{F \cdot L}{2 \pi \cdot \eta} + T_{preload} \quad \text{(N·m)}\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Template)

### Worked Example 1 — Spindle Power Calculation (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A turning operation on mild steel requires cutting speed $V_c = 150$ m/min, feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, depth of cut $a_p = 2$ mm. The specific cutting force $k_c = 1500$ N/mm². Spindle drive efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Find the **motor power** required.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate cutting force $F_c$:

$$F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f = 1500 \times 2 \times 0.3 = 900 \; \text{N}$$

Step 2 — Apply spindle power formula:

$$P = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000 \cdot \eta} = \frac{900 \times 150}{60{,}000 \times 0.85}$$

Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 900 \times 150 = 135{,}000$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 60{,}000 \times 0.85 = 51{,}000$$

Step 4 — Final division:

$$P = \frac{135{,}000}{51{,}000} = 2.647 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\;P \approx 2.65 \; \text{kW}\;}$$

> A 3 kW motor would be selected. KTU marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for answer.

### Worked Example 2 — Spindle Torque (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Spindle motor rated $P = 7.5$ kW running at $N = 1450$ rpm. Find the **torque**.

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{9550 \cdot P}{N} = \frac{9550 \times 7.5}{1450} = \frac{71{,}625}{1450} = 49.4 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\;T \approx 49.4 \; \text{N·m}\;}$$

### Worked Example 3 — Ball Screw Axis Drive (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A CNC milling machine has a ball screw with lead $L = 10$ mm, driving a table with axial force $F = 4000$ N. Screw efficiency $\eta = 0.90$. Preload torque $T_{preload} = 0.5$ N·m. Find the **motor torque**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the work-conversion term:

$$\frac{F \cdot L}{2 \pi \eta} = \frac{4000 \times 10}{2 \pi \times 0.90} = \frac{40{,}000}{5.6549} = 7074 \; \text{N·mm} = 7.074 \; \text{N·m}$$

Step 2 — Add preload torque:

$$T_m = 7.074 + 0.5 = 7.574 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_m \approx 7.57 \; \text{N·m}\;}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Axis Drive (Feed Drive) — Closed-Loop Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CNC[CNC Controller\nCommand Pulses] --> COMP[Comparator\nSummer Junction]
    FBE[Encoder on Motor\nRotary Feedback] --> COMP
    COMP --> AMP[Servo Amplifier\nPID Controller]
    AMP --> MOT[AC Servo Motor\nBrushless DC]
    MOT --> GBX[Coupling and\nGear Reducer]
    GBX --> BS[Ball Screw\nLead L mm]
    BS --> TAB[Machine Table\nLinear Motion X Y Z]
    TAB --> LSC[Linear Scale\nGlass Encoder]
    LSC --> FBE2[Position Feedback\nSignal]
    FBE2 -.-> COMP
    MOT -.-> FBE
```

## 4.2 Spindle Drive — Power Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MAINS[3 Phase AC Mains\n415 V 50 Hz] --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive\nVFD Inverter]
    VFD --> SPAC[Spindle AC Motor\nInduction or BLDC]
    SPAC --> BELT[Drive Belt and Pulleys\nor Gearbox]
    BELT --> SPIN[Machine Spindle\nTaper ISO 40 or 50]
    SPIN --> TCH[Cutting Tool\nDrill Mill or Turn Insert]
    SPIN --> RES[Resolver or Encoder\nSpindle Feedback]
    RES --> VFD
    TCH --> CHIP[Metal Chips\nMaterial Removal]
```

## 4.3 Axis Drive vs Spindle Drive — Comparison Matrix (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AXIS [AXIS DRIVE SYSTEM]
        A1[Servo Motor] --> A2[Ball Screw]
        A2 --> A3[Linear Table]
        A3 --> A4[Linear Encoder Feedback]
        A4 -.-> A1
    end
    subgraph SPIN [SPINDLE DRIVE SYSTEM]
        S1[VFD Inverter] --> S2[AC Spindle Motor]
        S2 --> S3[Spindle Gearbox]
        S3 --> S4[Spindle and Tool]
        S4 --> S5[Spindle Encoder Feedback]
        S5 -.-> S1
    end
```

## 4.4 Drive Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Need Axis or Spindle Drive] --> Q1{Type of Motion?}
    Q1 -->|Linear| AX[Axis Drive Path]
    Q1 -->|Rotary| SP[Spindle Drive Path]
    AX --> Q2{Power Level?}
    Q2 -->|Less than 2 kW| STEP[Stepper Motor Open Loop]
    Q2 -->|Greater than 2 kW| SERV[AC Servo Motor Closed Loop]
    SP --> Q3{Speed Range?}
    Q3 -->|Up to 15000 rpm| IND[AC Induction Motor with VFD]
    Q3 -->|Above 15000 rpm| BLDC[Brushless DC Direct Drive]
    STEP --> DONE[Select Drive]
    SERV --> DONE
    IND --> DONE
    BLDC --> DONE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between axis drive and spindle drive in a CNC machine tool.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Axis Drive | Spindle Drive |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Drives linear motion of slides | Drives rotary motion of spindle |
| **Motion type** | Linear (X, Y, Z) | Rotary (N rpm) |
| **Control parameter** | Position (mm) and feed (mm/min) | Speed (rpm) and torque (N·m) |
| **Typical motor** | AC servo / stepper / linear motor | AC induction / BLDC spindle motor |
| **Transmission** | Ball screw, rack & pinion, linear motor | Belt-drive, gearbox, direct-drive |
| **Feedback** | Linear/rotary encoder (mandatory) | Resolver / spindle encoder |
| **Loop type** | Position + velocity closed loop | Speed closed loop (mostly open for VFD) |
| **Power range** | 0.5 to 15 kW typical | 1 to 75 kW typical |
| **Key concern** | Backlash, stiffness, positioning accuracy | Constant power range, torque at low speed |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for function, 1 mark for components, 1 mark for control parameter)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any three types of spindle drives used in CNC machines and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **AC Induction Motor with VFD** — Used as the main spindle motor in CNC turning centers and machining centers; provides wide constant-power speed range (e.g., 50 to 6000 rpm). *(1 mark)*

2. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Servo Motor** — Used in direct-drive high-speed spindles for milling and grinding machines; speeds up to 100,000 rpm with low vibration. *(1 mark)*

3. **Hydraulic Spindle Motor** — Used in heavy-duty machines like large horizontal boring machines; provides high torque at low speeds for rough machining. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Past Paper Style]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **closed-loop axis drive system** of a CNC machine tool. Label all components. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC lathe spindle motor has a rated power of **11 kW** and operates at **N = 1450 rpm**. The drive efficiency is **0.9**. Calculate: (i) the spindle torque, and (ii) the maximum cutting force that can be sustained at a cutting speed of **180 m/min**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closed-Loop Axis Drive (7 marks):**

The closed-loop axis drive system of a CNC machine tool consists of the following components (refer to the diagram in Section 4.1):

1. **CNC Controller (Reference Input)** — Issues a stream of command pulses representing the desired position. *(1 mark)*
2. **Comparator / Summer** — Compares the reference position with the actual feedback position and generates an error signal $e(t)$. *(1 mark)*
3. **Servo Amplifier (PID Controller)** — Amplifies the error signal and drives the motor with proportional-integral-derivative control to achieve fast, stable response. *(1 mark)*
4. **Servo Motor (AC Brushless)** — The prime mover; produces torque proportional to the input current. *(1 mark)*
5. **Ball Screw (Transmission)** — Converts the rotary motion of the motor into linear motion of the table with lead $L$ (mm/rev). *(1 mark)*
6. **Linear/Rotary Encoder (Feedback)** — Mounted on the motor shaft or on the table (glass scale); continuously measures the actual position and feeds it back to the comparator. *(1 mark)*
7. **Linear Motion Guide** — Supports the moving table and provides stiffness and accuracy. *(1 mark)*

> The closed loop ensures that any error between commanded and actual position is automatically corrected, giving high accuracy (typically ±0.001 mm) and repeatability.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Spindle Torque (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$T = \frac{9550 \cdot P}{N} = \frac{9550 \times 11}{1450}$$

$$T = \frac{1{,}05{,}050}{1450} = 72.45 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\;T \approx 72.45 \; \text{N·m}\;}$$

**(ii) Maximum Cutting Force (4 marks):**

Start from the cutting power equation:

$$P_{motor} \cdot \eta = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000}$$

Rearrange for $F_c$:

$$F_c = \frac{P_{motor} \cdot \eta \cdot 60{,}000}{V_c}$$

Substitute: $P = 11$ kW, $\eta = 0.9$, $V_c = 180$ m/min.

$$F_c = \frac{11 \times 0.9 \times 60{,}000}{180} = \frac{5{,}94{,}000}{180} = 3300 \; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{\;F_c = 3300 \; \text{N}\;}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Past Paper Style]**
**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **ball screw** used in axis drives. Why is it preferred over a leadscrew? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CNC milling machine axis drive uses a **ball screw with lead L = 8 mm** driven by a servo motor through a gearbox of ratio 1:1. The axial cutting force on the table is **F = 6000 N**. Ball screw efficiency = **0.92**, preload torque = **0.8 N·m**. Calculate: (i) the torque required to drive the ball screw, and (ii) the power required when the feed rate is **480 mm/min**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ball Screw Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- A ball screw consists of a **screw shaft** (with a precision-ground helical groove) and a **ball nut** (with a matching internal groove). *(1 mark)*
- Between the screw and nut, a large number of **hardened steel balls** circulate, acting as the rolling elements. *(1 mark)*
- The balls return from the end of the nut to the starting end through a **return tube** or **deflector**, forming a closed circuit. *(1 mark)*
- A **preloaded double-nut** configuration is used to eliminate backlash and increase stiffness. *(1 mark)*

**Working (2 marks):**
- When the screw rotates, the balls roll between the screw and nut, transmitting force with **rolling friction** instead of sliding friction.
- This converts rotary motion of the screw into linear motion of the nut (or vice versa) with very high efficiency (90–95%).

**Why preferred over leadscrew (1 mark):**
- Efficiency is 5–10 times higher (90% vs 30–40%).
- Lower friction → less heat → no stick-slip → better positioning accuracy.
- Longer life under cyclic loading.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Ball Screw Drive Torque (4 marks):**

$$T_m = \frac{F \cdot L}{2 \pi \cdot \eta} + T_{preload}$$

$$T_m = \frac{6000 \times 8}{2 \pi \times 0.92} + 0.8$$

Compute the first term:

$$\frac{48{,}000}{5.7810} = 8303 \; \text{N·mm} = 8.303 \; \text{N·m}$$

Therefore:

$$T_m = 8.303 + 0.8 = 9.103 \; \text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_m \approx 9.10 \; \text{N·m}\;}$$

**(ii) Power Required (3 marks):**

First, find the spindle rpm equivalent — the feed rate $f$ relates to the screw rpm $N$ by:

$$f = N \cdot L \Rightarrow N = \frac{f}{L} = \frac{480}{8} = 60 \; \text{rpm}$$

Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 60}{60} = 2 \pi = 6.2832 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Power:

$$P = T_m \cdot \omega = 9.103 \times 6.2832 = 57.20 \; \text{W} = 0.0572 \; \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\;P \approx 0.057 \; \text{kW} \approx 57 \; \text{W}\;}$$

This shows axis drive power is much smaller than spindle power — consistent with reality (feed forces are small compared to cutting forces).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch is the #1 reason for losing marks.** Always check: $V_c$ in m/min vs m/s, $P$ in W vs kW, $T$ in N·mm vs N·m. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per unit error.
> 2. **The constant 9550** is valid **only** when P is in **kW** and N is in **rpm**. Memorize: $T = 9550 P / N$.
> 3. **Efficiency is always < 1**, and the motor must supply **more** power than the cutting consumes. So: $P_{motor} = P_{cutting} / \eta$. Students often invert this.
> 4. **In ball screw problems**, do not forget the **preload torque** term — it carries 1 mark in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Stepper motor problems**: For half-stepping, multiply steps by 2; for microstepping (1/10), multiply by 10. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Axis and Spindle Drives**

- ⭐ **Axis Drive** → linear motion; **Spindle Drive** → rotary motion.
- ⭐ Spindle power formula: $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60{,}000 \cdot \eta}$ (kW).
- ⭐ Spindle torque formula: $T = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P}{N}$ (N·m).
- ⭐ Cutting speed: $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ (m/min).
- ⭐ Ball screw torque: $T_m = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{2 \pi \eta} + T_{preload}$.
- ⭐ Feed rate from screw rpm: $f = N_{screw} \cdot L$.
- ⭐ **AC Servo motor** is the industry standard for **axis drives**.
- ⭐ **AC Induction motor with VFD** is the industry standard for **spindle drives**.
- ⭐ **Brushless DC** is used for **high-speed spindles** (>15,000 rpm).
- ⭐ **Stepper motors** are **open-loop**; risk of stalling under load.
- ⭐ **Linear motors** eliminate backlash entirely — used in HSM.
- ⭐ **Backlash** is corrected by **preloaded double nuts** or **software compensation**.
- ⭐ **Closed-loop** is mandatory for precision axis control.
- ⭐ **Resolver** or **encoder** provides spindle speed feedback to VFD.
- ⭐ **Constant-torque region** is below base speed; **constant-power region** is above.
- ⭐ Hydraulic drives are now legacy; electric drives dominate modern CNC.
- ⭐ The **constant 9550** comes from $60{,}000 / (2 \pi)$ — derived from rad/s ↔ rpm conversion.
- ⭐ In axis drives, motor power is **tiny** compared to spindle power (typically 1–5%).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LM Guideways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LM Guideways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Linear Motion (LM) Guideways** are high-precision, preloaded rolling-element bearings designed to provide **unconstrained linear motion** along a single axis with minimal friction, maximum rigidity, and zero backlash. They consist of a hardened steel **rail** and a **carriage (block)** separated by recirculating **balls or rollers**, and form the backbone of all modern CNC machine tool feed drives, replacing the traditional scraping-dependent sliding guideways.

The term **LM** specifically refers to **Linear Motion** — a registered designation used by THK Co. Ltd. (Japan, 1971) which pioneered this technology. The standardized ISO 12090-1, ISO 14728, and JIS B 1192 frameworks govern their design, load ratings, and accuracy classifications.

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Rail (Track)** | Hardened (HRC 58–62) bearing steel, ground to micron-level straightness |
| **Block (Carriage)** | Houses the rolling elements; bolts to the moving table |
| **Rolling Elements** | Balls (4-point contact) or cylindrical rollers (line contact) |
| **End Caps + Retainer** | Force balls back through return path (recirculation) |
| **Seal/Scraper** | Prevents coolant/chip ingress into the raceway |
| **Lubricator** | Supplies oil/grease via nipple or built-in piping |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train on a Perfectly Straight Track"

Imagine a **bullet train gliding on polished steel rails**. The train (carriage) moves effortlessly along fixed tracks (rails) because the wheels (balls) eliminate the metal-to-metal sliding that would otherwise generate enormous friction. Now imagine that this train:
- Never derails even under heavy sideways push (high **rigidity**),
- Maintains the same smooth feel whether it is empty or fully loaded (**preload consistency**),
- Can run at 60 m/min without jerking or play (**zero backlash**),
- Lasts for millions of kilometers before the wheels need replacement (**rated fatigue life**).

This is exactly what an **LM Guideway** does — but at micron-scale precision, inside a CNC machining center or lathe.

---

## 1.3 Conventional Slideway vs. LM Guideway — The Paradigm Shift

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Favourite**
> Examiners **routinely** ask the differences between conventional (sliding) and LM (rolling) guideways. Memorise the table below.

| Parameter | Conventional Slideway | LM Guideway |
|---|---|---|
| Motion Principle | Sliding friction | Rolling friction |
| Coefficient of friction ($\mu$) | **0.05 – 0.20** (dry) | **0.001 – 0.005** |
| Stick-slip (jerky motion) | Severe at low speeds | Virtually eliminated |
| Backlash | Requires scraping to remove | Zero (preloaded) |
| Rigidity | Function of wear & scraping | Built-in (preload selectable) |
| Positioning accuracy | 10–20 µm/m | **2–5 µm/m** (precision grade) |
| Required lubrication | Flood mandatory | Oil film at intervals only |
| Maintenance | Frequent scraping, adjustment | Periodic lubrication only |
| Service life | Unpredictable (wear-based) | Predictable ($L_{10}$ rating) |
| Speed capability | Up to ~15 m/min | Up to **60+ m/min** |

> The shift from scraping to LM guideways is considered as revolutionary in machine tools as the shift from sliding doors to roller-bearing drawers in furniture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Rolling Beats Sliding

When a body slides on another, the frictional resistance is governed by the **normal force × coefficient of friction**. For rolling, the resistance is replaced by a **moment of resistance** proportional to the rolling element diameter. The coefficient of rolling friction ($f_r$) is two to three orders of magnitude smaller than sliding friction ($\mu_s$).

For an LM guideway:

$$F_{f} \;=\; f_{r} \cdot F_{N}$$

where $F_f$ is the friction force, $F_N$ is the normal (vertical) load, and $f_r \approx 0.001$ – $0.005$.

---

## 2.2 The Four-Point Contact Concept (Ball Type)

This is the **geometrical heart** of a ball-type LM guideway. Each ball makes contact with the raceway at **two points on each groove** — hence four contact points per ball. This geometry:

- Absorbs loads in **both vertical (downward) and lateral (sideways)** directions simultaneously.
- Provides a **self-aligning** capability that tolerates minor mounting-surface errors (typically 0.02 mm/m).
- Allows **preloading** by selecting oversized balls, eliminating internal clearance and play.

```
        Lateral Load (Fy)
              ↑
              |
        _____(O)_____   ← Ball
       /     |     \
   Groove   |    Groove
   left     |    right
            ↓
       Vertical Load (Fx)
```

For **roller-type** LM guideways, cylindrical rollers provide **line contact** instead of point contact, increasing load capacity by **~2× to 3×** but requiring more precise alignment.

---

## 2.3 Preload — The Key to Zero Backlash

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Concept**
> **Preload** is a controlled internal stress built into the LM guideway by using balls slightly larger than the free space in the raceway. It eliminates play and increases rigidity but raises friction slightly.

| Preload Class | Designation | Internal Clearance | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Z0 (Light)** | Zero clearance | 0 → +0.01C | Milling machines, low-vibration |
| **Z1 (Medium)** | Preloaded | -0.01C → 0 | Machining centers (most common) |
| **Z2 (Heavy)** | Heavy preload | -0.025C → -0.01C | Heavy cutting, lathes |
| **Z3 (Extra-heavy)** | Maximum preload | -0.05C → -0.025C | Precision grinders |

Where **C** = Basic Dynamic Load Rating (defined in §2.4 below).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] Memorise every row of this table — KTU problems typically test 2–3 of these in a single numerical question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Dynamic Load Rating ($C$) | Manufacturer tabulated | **N** | Load with 90% reliability for 50 km travel |
| 2 | Basic Static Load Rating ($C_0$) | Manufacturer tabulated | **N** | Max allowable static load |
| 3 | Equivalent Load ($P$) | $P = X \cdot F_x + Y \cdot F_y + Z \cdot F_z$ | N | Combined radial + lateral + vertical |
| 4 | **Rated Fatigue Life ($L_{10}$)** | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \cdot 50$ | **km** | For **balls** (exponent = 3) |
| 5 | Rated Fatigue Life (rollers) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{\frac{10}{3}} \cdot 50$ | km | For **rollers** (exponent = 10/3) |
| 6 | Life in hours ($L_h$) | $L_h = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^6}{2 \cdot n_s \cdot 60}$ | h | $n_s$ = strokes/min |
| 7 | Static Safety Factor ($f_s$) | $f_s = \dfrac{C_0}{P}$ | — | Must be $\geq 1$ to **5** (vibration dependent) |
| 8 | Rigidity ($k$) | $k = \dfrac{F}{\delta}$ | N/µm | $\delta$ = elastic deflection under load |
| 9 | Frictional Force ($F_f$) | $F_f = \mu_{app} \cdot F_N$ | N | $\mu_{app} \approx 0.002$ – $0.005$ |
| 10 | Drive Force ($F_d$) | $F_d = F_f + F_{accel} + F_{cut}$ | N | For motor sizing |

> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Always distinguish ball-type ($p = 3$) from roller-type ($p = 10/3$) in the life formula. Marks are routinely lost here.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LM guideways are indispensable in:
- **CNC Machining Centers** — X, Y, Z axes feed motion.
- **CNC Lathes** — Cross-slide and carriage slides.
- **Industrial Robots** — Linear robot axes, pick-and-place.
- **Semiconductor Equipment** — Wafer-handling stages (sub-micron accuracy).
- **Medical Imaging** — CT scanner gantries (smooth, low-vibration motion).
- **3D Printers & Engravers** — Compact, precise, low-cost linear motion.

---

## 2.6 Selection Procedure (Board-Relevant Flow)

A KTU problem may ask: *"Select a suitable LM guideway for the X-axis of a CNC milling machine."* The standard procedure is:

1. **Identify loads** ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) on the block from cutting forces and gravity.
2. **Compute equivalent dynamic load** $P$ from the load combination.
3. **Estimate required life** ($L_{10}$ in km) from duty cycle (e.g., 8 h/day × 250 days × 5 years × average stroke × strokes/min).
4. **Calculate required dynamic load rating** $C_{req}$ using the inverse life formula (see §3).
5. **Select a commercial series** with $C \geq C_{req}$.
6. **Check static safety factor** $f_s \geq$ recommended value.
7. **Verify rigidity** $k$ against the machine's positioning accuracy target.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rated Fatigue Life ($L_{10}$)

The theoretical foundation of LM guideway life is the ** Lundberg-Palmgren fatigue theory**, originally developed for rolling-element bearings and adapted for linear motion systems.

### Step 1 — Hertzian Contact Stress
When a ball of diameter $D$ is pressed against a curved raceway under load $Q$, the maximum contact stress $p_0$ follows Hertz's equation:

$$p_0} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\pi} \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{6 \cdot Q \cdot E^{*2}}{r^{2} \cdot D^{2}}}$$

where $E^{*}$ is the reduced elastic modulus and $r$ is the relative radius of curvature. For steel-on-steel, $E^{*} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa.

### Step 2 — Subsurface Stress and Shear Plane
Maximum alternating shear stress occurs at a depth of $\approx 0.5 a$ below the contact surface (where $a$ is the contact radius). Fatigue initiates here after a finite number of stress cycles.

### Step 3 — Load-Life Exponent
Through dimensional analysis, the number of stress cycles to failure $N$ is related to the load $Q$ by:

$$Q \cdot N^{\frac{1}{3}} \;=\; \text{constant} \;=\; \tau$$

This gives $Q^{3} \cdot N = \tau^{3}$, hence $N \propto Q^{-3}$ — the **third-power law**.

### Step 4 — From Per-Ball to System Load
For $z$ balls per block, each carrying $Q = P / z$ (where $P$ is the total block load), the life in **revolutions (or strokes)** of one ball is:

$$L \;=\; \left(\dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q}\right)^{3} \cdot L_{ref}$$

where $L_{ref} = 1{,}000{,}000$ reference revolutions corresponds to **50 km** of travel (assuming 50 mm/rev nominal stroke).

### Step 5 — Final Rated Life Formula (Balls)
Substituting $Q = P / z$ and normalising against the rated dynamic load $C$:

$$L_{10} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \cdot 50 \quad \text{[km]}$$

This is the equation a KTU student **must** memorise.

### Step 6 — Roller Variation
Because rollers carry load over a **line** (length $\ell$) instead of a point, the contact area is larger and fatigue exponent becomes $10/3$:

$$L_{10,\text{roller}} \;=\; \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{\frac{10}{3}} \cdot 50 \quad \text{[km]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why the exponent differs:** The Hertzian line contact yields a fundamentally different stress-volume relationship under the raceway. Lundberg and Palmgren (1947) derived $p = 10/3$ analytically. Marks are reserved for stating this explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — LM Guideway Life Calculation (KTU-style)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**  
> A vertical axis of a CNC machining centre uses two LM guideway blocks supporting a table of mass $m = 800$ kg. The cutting force during a milling operation is $F_y = 3{,}500$ N (lateral), $F_z = 5{,}000$ N (vertical), and the axis performs $n_s = 20$ strokes/min with a stroke length $s = 0.5$ m. The manufacturer data for the selected LM guideway (ball type) is $C = 38{,}000$ N and $C_0 = 62{,}000$ N. Compute the rated fatigue life in km and in hours, and check the static safety factor.

### Step 1 — List all applied loads

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{x,\text{gravity}} &= 0 \quad \text{(horizontal axis)} \\
F_{y,\text{lateral}} &= 3{,}500 \text{ N} \\
F_{z,\text{vertical}} &= m \cdot g + F_{cut,z} = (800 \times 9.81) + 5{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}848 + 5{,}000 = 12{,}848 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Combine using equivalent load factors

For a four-point contact LM guideway with preload Z1, the equivalent dynamic load factors are typically:
$X = 1.0$, $Y = 0.8$, $Z = 1.0$.

$$P = X \cdot F_{x} + Y \cdot F_{y} + Z \cdot F_{z}$$

$$P = (1.0)(0) + (0.8)(3{,}500) + (1.0)(12{,}848)$$

$$P = 0 + 2{,}800 + 12{,}848 = 15{,}648 \text{ N}$$

> Note: The table weight is supported by **two blocks**; this $P$ is per block (assuming symmetric loading).

### Step 3 — Compute $L_{10}$ in km

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \cdot 50$$

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{38{,}000}{15{,}648}\right)^{3} \cdot 50$$

$$\dfrac{38{,}000}{15{,}648} = 2.4285$$

$$(2.4285)^{3} = 14.322$$

$$L_{10} = 14.322 \times 50 = 716.1 \text{ km}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to hours ($L_h$)

$$L_h = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{2 \cdot n_s \cdot 60 \cdot s \times 1000}$$

$$L_h = \dfrac{716.1 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 20 \times 60 \times 500}$$

$$L_h = \dfrac{716.1 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}200{,}000} = 596.75 \text{ h}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to working years

Assuming **8 h/day, 250 days/year**:

$$L_{\text{years}} = \dfrac{596.75}{8 \times 250} = 0.298 \text{ years}$$

This is too short! ⇒ A larger LM guideway must be selected.

### Step 6 — Required $C$ for target life of 20,000 h

$$20{,}000 \text{ h} \Leftrightarrow L_{10} = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000}{10^{6}} = 24{,}000 \text{ km}$$

$$C_{\text{req}} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_{10}}{50}\right)^{1/3}$$

$$C_{\text{req}} = 15{,}648 \cdot \left(\dfrac{24{,}000}{50}\right)^{1/3} = 15{,}648 \cdot (480)^{1/3}$$

$$\sqrt[3]{480} \approx 7.829$$

$$C_{\text{req}} \approx 122{,}510 \text{ N}$$

### Step 7 — Static safety factor check

$$f_{s} = \dfrac{C_0}{P_{\max}} = \dfrac{62{,}000}{15{,}648} = 3.96$$

Recommended $f_s \geq 3$ for milling machines with moderate vibration ⇒ **PASS** ✅.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Ball vs. Roller LM Guideway

| Criterion | Choose **Ball Type** | Choose **Roller Type** |
|---|---|---|
| Required rigidity | Moderate | High |
| Load magnitude | Light – Medium | Medium – Heavy |
| Positioning accuracy | ≤ 5 µm | ≤ 1 µm (precision grade) |
| Cost sensitivity | High | Lower priority |
| Mounting surface | Tolerates slight errors | Demands flat, precise surface |
| Shock loading | Tolerable | Sensitive (line contact) |
| Typical machine | Drill, light mill, 3D printer | Heavy CNC machining centre, gantry |

> **One-line heuristic:** *Use balls unless rigidity or load demands rollers.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Linear Motion System] --> B[LM Guideway Assembly]

```

```
       Top raceway  ◄—— Balls travel here under load  ——◄
       ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │     ●  ●  ●  ●  ●  ●  ●  ●   ← load zone     │
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define an LM guideway. List any four advantages it offers over conventional slideways.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

An **LM (Linear Motion) guideway** is a precision rolling-element linear bearing consisting of a hardened steel **rail** and a **carriage (block)** that uses recirculating **balls or rollers** to provide low-friction, high-rigidity linear motion with zero backlash.

**Four advantages over conventional slideways:**

1. **Low coefficient of friction** (0.001–0.005 vs. 0.05–0.20), enabling higher feed rates and reduced drive motor power.
2. **No stick-slip phenomenon**, ensuring accurate positioning at very low feed rates (essential for contouring).
3. **Zero backlash** through preloading, eliminating the periodic scraping required in sliding guides.
4. **Predictable fatigue life** ($L_{10}$ rating), enabling condition-based maintenance.

---

### **Q2.** What is meant by "preload" in an LM guideway? Name the standard preload classes.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Preload** is an internal negative clearance introduced into the LM guideway by using rolling elements (balls or rollers) slightly larger than the theoretical free-space between the rail and block raceways. This generates an internal elastic stress that:
- Eliminates internal play (backlash = 0),
- Increases rigidity,
- Improves vibration damping.

**Standard preload classes (per ISO 14728 / JIS B 1192):**

| Class | Description | Clearance |
|---|---|---|
| **Z0** | Light preload / zero clearance | 0 to +0.01 C |
| **Z1** | Medium preload | −0.01 C to 0 |
| **Z2** | Heavy preload | −0.025 C to −0.01 C |
| **Z3** | Extra heavy preload | −0.05 C to −0.025 C |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working principle of a ball-type LM guideway. Highlight the role of recirculation and four-point contact. (7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

A ball-type LM guideway consists of two principal components:

1. **Rail** — A long, hardened (HRC 58–62) bearing-steel bar with two precision-ground **circumferential grooves** running along its length. Mounted on the machine bed via countersunk bolts.

2. **Block (Carriage)** — A rectangular steel housing containing:
   - Two end caps fitted on either side (forming the **return path**),
   - A set of **retained balls** circulating through the load and return zones,
   - A **seal set** at top and bottom to block contaminants,
   - A **grease nipple** or piped lubrication port,
   - Mounting holes on top to bolt to the moving table.

**Working Principle:**

- The block is bolted to the moving table; the rail is bolted to the fixed bed.
- As the table moves, the balls enter the **load zone** (between rail and block raceways), carry the load across the four contact points, and are then deflected into the **return channel** by the end caps.
- The recirculation allows **continuous, indefinite linear motion** without the balls being lost.
- The **four-point contact** per ball (two on each groove) ensures that vertical downward loads, vertical upward (lift) loads, and lateral (sideways) loads are **all simultaneously supported** by the same set of balls.

**Role of recirculation:** Without recirculation, balls would simply exit the load zone, requiring an infinite rail. The end cap deflectors and the return channel keep balls in continuous motion inside the block — enabling **unlimited stroke length**.

**Role of four-point contact:** It allows the **same LM guideway** to resist multi-directional loads (not just downward) — making it ideal for CNC machines where cutting forces come from varied directions.

**Sketch (refer to §4.4 for ASCII version):**

```
   TABLE ──────────────────[ BLOCK ]──────────────────
                              │ ● ● ● ● │  ← load zone (balls)
   [ END CAP ] ←── return path ──→ [ END CAP ]
                              │ ● ● ● ● │  ← return zone
   BED   ═══════════[    RAIL    ]═══════════════
                          (with grooves)
```

---

#### (b) **A CNC machining centre uses four LM guideway blocks (two per rail) on its X-axis. Each block carries an equivalent dynamic load of $P = 12$ kN. The guideway catalog specifies $C = 28$ kN and $C_0 = 52$ kN. The axis reciprocates at 30 strokes/min over a stroke of 0.4 m. Compute: (i) Rated fatigue life in km, (ii) Rated life in hours, (iii) Static safety factor. Comment on suitability for a 2-shift operation (16 h/day × 300 days/year). (7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2022]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rated fatigue life in km (ball type, $p = 3$):**

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \cdot 50$$

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{28{,}000}{12{,}000}\right)^{3} \cdot 50 = (2.333)^{3} \cdot 50$$

$$(2.333)^{3} = 12.703$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} = 12.703 \times 50 = 635.16 \text{ km}}$$

**(ii) Rated life in hours:**

Travel per hour = $2 \times n_s \times s \times 60$ (factor of 2 because each stroke is out-and-back):

$$\text{Travel per hour} = 2 \times 30 \times 0.4 \times 60 = 1{,}440 \text{ m/h} = 1.44 \text{ km/h}$$

$$L_h = \dfrac{L_{10}}{\text{Travel per hour}} = \dfrac{635.16}{1.44}$$

$$\boxed{L_h = 441 \text{ hours}}$$

**(iii) Static safety factor:**

$$\boxed{f_s = \dfrac{C_0}{P} = \dfrac{52{,}000}{12{,}000} = 4.33}$$

**Suitability for 2-shift operation:**

Annual operating hours = $16 \times 300 = 4{,}800$ h/year.

$$L_{\text{years}} = \dfrac{441}{4{,}800} \approx 0.092 \text{ years} \approx 33.7 \text{ days}$$

> [!WARNING] **Comment:** The rated life of only **~441 hours** is **grossly inadequate** for a 2-shift production environment. Required life of, say, **5 years × 4,800 h = 24,000 h**. Required $C$:
>
> $$C_{\text{req}} = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_{10,\text{req}}}{50}\right)^{1/3} = 12{,}000 \cdot \left(\dfrac{24{,}000 \times 1.44}{50}\right)^{1/3}$$
>
> $$C_{\text{req}} = 12{,}000 \cdot (691.2)^{1/3} = 12{,}000 \times 8.84 \approx 106{,}000 \text{ N}$$
>
> The selection is **unsuitable** — must upsize to a series with $C \geq 106$ kN.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### (a) **Compare ball-type and roller-type LM guideways in terms of contact geometry, load capacity, rigidity, accuracy, and cost. State, with justification, which type you would choose for the X-axis of a large gantry-type CNC machining centre used for heavy-duty milling of steel dies. (7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2021]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Ball-Type LM Guideway | Roller-Type LM Guideway |
|---|---|---|
| Contact geometry | **Point contact** (4 points per ball) | **Line contact** (line of length $\ell$) |
| Load capacity (per unit size) | Lower | **2–3× higher** |
| Rigidity | Moderate | **Very high** |
| Friction | Slightly lower | Slightly higher |
| Sensitivity to mounting error | Low (self-aligning) | High (demands flat surface) |
| Accuracy class achievable | Normal, High, Precision | High, Precision, Ultra-precision |
| Cost (per unit load) | Higher | Lower (better load/cost ratio) |
| Shock loading tolerance | Good | Sensitive |
| Typical applications | 3D printers, light mills, automation | Heavy mills, lathes, gantries, grinders |

**Recommendation:** For the **X-axis of a large gantry-type CNC machining centre for heavy steel die milling**, a **roller-type LM guideway** is the correct choice.

**Justification:**
- **Heavy cutting forces** (typically 5–20 kN) demand the **higher load capacity** of rollers.
- The **gantry structure** carries large mass (table + workpiece) and therefore requires **high rigidity** to maintain micron-level accuracy under load.
- The **machine bed is precision-ground**, so the roller-type's sensitivity to mounting error is not an issue.
- The **cost-per-kN-of-load** for rollers is lower, justifying the slightly higher unit price.

Caveat: Use of rollers demands **precision scraping/spot-facing of mounting surfaces** and **strict parallelism** between paired rails on the same axis.

---

#### (b) **With reference to LM guideway selection, derive the relationship between rated dynamic load $C$, equivalent load $P$, and rated life $L_{10}$ for a ball-type system. A horizontal LM guideway block carries a vertical load of $F_z = 8{,}000$ N (including table weight) and a lateral load of $F_y = 2{,}500$ N due to cutting. If the manufacturer catalog lists $C = 40{,}000$ N and $C_0 = 75{,}000$ N, compute the rated life in km and comment on the result. (7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2021]**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (concise):**

Each ball in the LM block carries a load $Q = P / z$, where $z$ is the number of effective load-carrying balls. Based on Lundberg-Palmgren theory, the number of stress cycles to spalling fatigue is:

$$N \;\propto\; Q^{-3} \quad \text{(ball)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q^{3} \cdot N = \text{const.}$$

Normalising against the rated dynamic load $C$ (which corresponds to a reference life $L_{ref} = 50$ km or $10^{6}$ revolutions):

$$\left(\dfrac{C}{Q}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{L}{L_{ref}} = \dfrac{L}{50}$$

Substituting $Q = P / z$ (and noting that $z$ cancels since $C$ is already normalised to the same block):

$$\boxed{L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \cdot 50 \quad \text{[km]}}$$

**Numerical computation:**

Take equivalent load factors $Y = 0.8$ (lateral), $Z = 1.0$ (vertical):

$$P = (0.8)(2{,}500) + (1.0)(8{,}000) = 2{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{40{,}000}{10{,}000}\right)^{3} \cdot 50 = (4)^{3} \cdot 50 = 64 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{L_{10} = 3{,}200 \text{ km}}$$

**Comment:** At a typical feed rate of 10 m/min on a single-stroke (one-way) duty, this corresponds to:

$$L_h = \dfrac{3{,}200 \times 10^{3}}{10 \times 60} \approx 5{,}333 \text{ hours}$$

For **single-shift 8 h/day × 250 days/year = 2,000 h/year**, this is approximately **2.67 years** of life — **acceptable** for a light-to-medium duty application but **not for heavy production** where a longer-life series should be selected.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Board Examiner Insights)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "per block" basis** — When two blocks share the load, students often forget to divide by 2 or apply the correct factor. Always state "load per block" explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong exponent** — Using $p = 3$ for roller-type or $p = 10/3$ for ball-type costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $L_h$** — $L_{10}$ is in **km** but travels-per-hour are in **m**. Convert consistently: $L_{10} \times 10^{3}$ m = total travel in metres.
> 4. **Neglecting gravity on vertical axes** — Vertical axes carry the full mass of the table; horizontal axes do not. Examiners test this routinely.
> 5. **Forgetting the recirculation principle** — A sketch without showing the **return path** is marked incomplete (lose 2 marks).
> 6. **Confusing $C$ and $C_0$** — $C$ is dynamic (life-related); $C_0$ is static (deformation-related). Mixing them up forfeits marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — LM Guideways**

- ☐ **Definition:** Rolling-element linear bearing; rail + block + recirculating balls/rollers.
- ☐ **Function:** Convert sliding friction into rolling friction ($\mu$ drops from 0.1+ to ~0.002).
- ☐ **Four-point contact** allows multi-directional load support with the same ball set.
- ☐ **Recirculation** (end caps + return channel) provides unlimited stroke.
- ☐ **Preload classes:** Z0, Z1, Z2, Z3 — increasing preload → increasing rigidity, slightly increasing friction.
- ☐ **Rated life (ball):** $L_{10} = (C/P)^3 \cdot 50$ **km**.
- ☐ **Rated life (roller):** $L_{10} = (C/P)^{10/3} \cdot 50$ **km**.
- ☐ **Life in hours:** $L_h = L_{10} \times 10^{6} / (2 n_s \cdot s \cdot 60)$ where $s$ in metres.
- ☐ **Static safety factor:** $f_s = C_0 / P \geq 1$ to 5 depending on vibration severity.
- ☐ **Ball vs. Roller:** Balls → versatile, tolerant; Rollers → high load, high rigidity, accurate mounting required.
- ☐ **Advantages over slideways:** low friction, no stick-slip, zero backlash, predictable life, low maintenance, high speeds (60+ m/min).
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** Higher initial cost, sensitive to contamination (needs seals), preload adjustment not field-serviceable.
- ☐ **Selection sequence:** Loads → $P$ → required $L_{10}$ → $C_{req}$ → catalog match → static check → rigidity check.
- ☐ **Standards to remember:** ISO 12090-1, ISO 14728, JIS B 1192.
- ☐ **Accuracy grades:** Normal (N), High (H), Precision (P), Super-precision (SP), Ultra-precision (UP).
- ☐ **Lubrication:** Lithium-soap grease every 6 months or oil via auto-lube at 0.1–0.2 cc per block per day.
- ☐ **Sealing:** Standard end-seal + side-seal + bottom-seal; add scraper for harsh environments.
- ☐ **Common brands (for context):** THK, NSK, Hiwin, Bosch Rexroth, SKF, INA (Schaeffler).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ball Screws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ball Screws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Ball Screw** is a mechanical power-transmission device that converts rotational motion into linear motion (or vice versa) with minimal frictional losses, using a series of hardened steel balls as the rolling elements between the screw shaft and the nut. The balls act as the load-bearing interface, rolling along precision-ground helical raceways in both the screw and nut, thereby substituting the sliding friction of a conventional lead screw with rolling friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme – PCIET402, Module 3):**
> Ball Screws are explicitly listed as a high-weightage sub-topic under the "Feed Drives and Spindle Drives" cluster. Questions frequently appear in Part A (3 marks – definitions, comparison) and Part B (14 marks – derivations and numerical efficiency/torque/life problems).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Marble on a Threaded Ramp":**
> Imagine you are climbing a long spiral staircase. If you walk normally, your feet drag against the steps (sliding friction, like a conventional lead screw). Now imagine tiny, perfectly round marbles rolling in the groove of every step — your feet now glide effortlessly, and the energy you need to climb drops dramatically. That is exactly what a ball screw does inside a CNC machine: instead of sliding metal-on-metal, **steel balls roll in the helical groove**, allowing the machine to position a heavy table with a tiny motor and pinpoint accuracy.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Coefficient of Friction (rolling) | $\mu$ | **0.001 to 0.01** |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | **85% to 95%** |
| Friction Angle (equivalent) | $\phi$ | **5 to 15 arc-minutes** |
| Lead Angle | $\lambda$ | **3° to 25°** |
| Mechanical Back-drivability | — | **Yes (non-self-locking)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Board Exam Favourite):** A ball screw is **not self-locking** when $\lambda > \phi$. This is a direct consequence of replacing sliding friction with rolling friction, and it is a critical design point for vertical-axis CNC machines where counter-balancing / braking becomes mandatory to prevent fall-back during power loss.

### 1.4 Why Ball Screws Are Used in Modern Machine Tools

- **High mechanical efficiency (≈ 90 %)** compared to ≈ 30–50 % for sliding lead screws.
- **High positional accuracy** (1–5 µm achievable) and repeatability.
- **High stiffness and rigidity** when preloaded.
- **Low heat generation** due to minimal friction.
- **Long service life** under proper lubrication and preloading.
- **Reverse efficiency ≈ forward efficiency**, enabling back-driven operation (used in some CNC servo loops).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Ball Screw Assembly

A complete ball-screw feed-drive assembly consists of the following sub-systems:

1. **Screw Shaft (Ball Screw):** A precision-ground shaft carrying a single or multi-start external helical groove (raceway). The raceway profile is a Gothic-arch (concave) profile to maintain ball contact even under load.
2. **Ball Nut:** A matching internally grooved nut, mounted on the machine slide.
3. **Balls:** Hardened chrome-steel (AISI 52100) spheres, typically **grade 10 or higher** for machine-tool duty.
4. **Recirculation System:** Returns balls from the end of the engaged zone to the start, enabling continuous screw rotation.
5. **End Deflectors / Return Tubes / C-shaped Inserts:** The actual hardware that performs recirculation.
6. **Wipers / Seals:** Prevent contamination entry into the raceway.
7. **Bearings (Angular Contact Thrust Bearings):** Support axial, radial, and moment loads at the screw ends.

### 2.2 Types of Ball Screws (Classified by Construction)

| Type | Construction Feature | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Nut (Non-preloaded)** | One ball nut, internal clearance exists | Light-duty positioning |
| **Single Nut (Preloaded)** | Oversized balls or offset-ground raceway | Medium-duty CNC, high stiffness |
| **Double Nut (Preloaded)** | Two ball nuts separated by a spacer shim, pre-tensioned | **High-precision CNC, machine tools** |

### 2.3 Ball Recirculation Methods (Direct KTU Question Pattern)

- **External Recirculation (Deflector Tube Type):** Balls exit the nut at the end of the load zone, pass through a curved return tube mounted externally, and re-enter the nut. Simple and cost-effective.
- **Internal Recirculation (Insert Deflector Type):** A small C-shaped deflector (picking hook) inside the nut channels balls back. Compact design, no external protrusions.
- **End-Cap Recirculation:** A cap at the end of the nut turns the balls 180°. Smooth, low-noise, used in high-speed applications.

### 2.4 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Servomotor applies torque $T$ to the screw shaft.
2. Screw rotates, helical raceway drives the steel balls along the helical path.
3. Balls transfer axial force $F$ to the nut through Hertzian contact (point/elliptical contact at the Gothic arch).
4. Nut moves linearly by one **lead $L$** for every revolution.
5. Balls reaching the end of the loaded zone enter the **return circuit** and circulate back to the start.
6. Continuous rotation yields continuous linear feed.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lead Angle | $\tan \lambda = \dfrac{L}{\pi \, d_{m}}$ | $L$ = lead, $d_{m}$ = pitch (mean ball) diameter |
| 2 | Forward Efficiency | $\eta_{f} = \dfrac{\tan \lambda}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}$ | $\phi = \arctan \mu$ |
| 3 | Reverse Efficiency | $\eta_{r} = \dfrac{\tan(\lambda - \phi)}{\tan \lambda}$ | Self-locking when $\lambda \le \phi$ |
| 4 | Drive Torque (raising load) | $T = \dfrac{F \, L}{2 \pi \, \eta_{f}}$ | $F$ = axial thrust load |
| 5 | Drive Torque (no load) | $T_{0} = \dfrac{F_{p} \, L}{2 \pi \, \eta_{f}} \cdot \text{preload factor}$ | $F_{p}$ = preload force |
| 6 | Mean Rotational Speed | $n_{m} = \dfrac{n_{min} + n_{max}}{2}$ | rev/min |
| 7 | Equivalent Dynamic Load (fluctuating) | $P_{eq} = \sqrt[\leftroot{-1}\uproot{2}\scriptstyle 3]{\dfrac{P_{max}^{\,3} + P_{min}^{\,3}}{2}}$ | For ball-screw (cubic mean) |
| 8 | Basic Rating Life (revolutions) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P_{eq}}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6}$ | $C$ = dynamic load rating |
| 9 | Life in Hours | $L_{h} = \dfrac{L_{10}}{60 \, n_{m}}$ | hours |
| 10 | Static Safety Check | $C_{0a} \ge S_{0} \, P_{max}$ | $S_{0}$ = static safety factor (1 to 3) |
| 11 | Linear Stiffness (axial) | $K = \dfrac{E \, A}{L_{s}}$ | $E = 2.07 \times 10^{5}$ MPa (steel) |
| 12 | Buckling Load | $F_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K_{e} L_{s})^{2}}$ | Euler — check for long, slender screws |
| 13 | Drive Power | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi \, n \, T}{60{,}000}$ | kW (with $n$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Discipline:** Use SI units throughout. Convert lead from mm to m when computing $T$ in N·m, or use the direct shortcut: $T$ (N·m) $= F$ (N) $\times L$ (mm) / $(2\pi \times \eta \times 1000)$. Mixing units is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in numerical problems.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ball screws are the **backbone of linear motion control** in:

- **CNC Machine Tools** — axis drives (X, Y, Z) of machining centres, lathes, EDM.
- **Semiconductor Equipment** — wafer-stage positioning (sub-µm accuracy).
- **Aerospace Actuators** — flaps, slats, landing-gear motion.
- **Robotics** — pick-and-place actuators, SCARA Z-axis.
- **Automotive** — electric power-steering assist units.
- **Press Tools / Injection Moulding** — clamping tie-bar drives.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lead Angle ($\lambda$)

The lead of a screw is the axial advance per one complete revolution. Unrolling the helix of the screw onto a flat plane gives a right-angled triangle:

- **Opposite side** = Lead $L$ (axial advance)
- **Adjacent side** = Circumference of mean diameter $= \pi \, d_{m}$
- **Angle between hypotenuse and adjacent** = Lead angle $\lambda$

$$
\tan \lambda = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{L}{\pi \, d_{m}}
$$

> Hence, $\boxed{\lambda = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L}{\pi d_{m}}\right)}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Ball-Screw Efficiency ($\eta_f$ and $\eta_r$)

**Step 1 — Free-body of a ball rolling up the helical raceway:**
A ball in the loaded zone is wedged between the rotating screw raceway (which moves relative to the ball at lead-angle $\lambda$) and the stationary nut. The relative motion of the screw surface against the ball is along the helix at angle $\lambda$ to the horizontal.

**Step 2 — Force resolution at the ball contact:**
Two force components act on the ball from the screw raceway:
- Normal contact force $N$ (perpendicular to the raceway contact plane).
- Frictional force $F_{f} = \mu N$ (opposing the relative motion along the helix).

**Step 3 — Resolve into axial and tangential components:**
Decomposing along and perpendicular to the axis:
- Axial thrust on nut $= N \sin \lambda + F_{f} \cos \lambda = N(\sin \lambda + \mu \cos \lambda)$
- Tangential force on screw (perpendicular to axis) $= N \cos \lambda - F_{f} \sin \lambda = N(\cos \lambda - \mu \sin \lambda)$

**Step 4 — Torque required on the screw:**
If $d_{m}$ is the pitch diameter (mean ball diameter), the tangential force acts at radius $d_{m}/2$:

$$
T = \frac{d_{m}}{2}\,N(\cos \lambda - \mu \sin \lambda)
$$

**Step 5 — Express $N$ in terms of axial load $F$:**
From the axial equilibrium: $F = N(\sin \lambda + \mu \cos \lambda)$, therefore

$$
N = \frac{F}{\sin \lambda + \mu \cos \lambda}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify using the friction-angle identity** $\mu = \tan \phi$:

$$
T = \frac{F \, d_{m}}{2}\cdot\frac{\cos \lambda - \tan\phi \sin \lambda}{\sin \lambda + \tan\phi \cos \lambda}
$$

$$
T = \frac{F \, d_{m}}{2}\cdot\frac{\cos(\lambda + \phi)}{\sin(\lambda + \phi)} = \frac{F \, d_{m}}{2}\tan(\lambda + \phi)
$$

**Step 7 — Use the lead-angle identity $L = \pi \, d_{m} \tan \lambda$** to express the ideal (frictionless) torque:

$$
T_{ideal} = \frac{F \, L}{2 \pi}
$$

**Step 8 — Define efficiency as the ratio of useful work-out to work-in:**

$$
\eta_{f} = \frac{W_{useful}}{W_{input}} = \frac{F \cdot L}{2\pi \, T} = \frac{F \cdot L}{2\pi \cdot \dfrac{F d_{m}}{2}\tan(\lambda + \phi)}
$$

$$
\eta_{f} = \frac{L}{\pi d_{m} \tan(\lambda + \phi)} = \frac{\tan \lambda}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}
$$

> Hence, $\boxed{\eta_{f} = \dfrac{\tan \lambda}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}}$ — **Forward (driving) efficiency.**

**Step 9 — Reverse (back-driving) efficiency** (when screw is being rotated by an external load, e.g., in servo back-driven condition):

$$
\eta_{r} = \frac{\tan(\lambda - \phi)}{\tan \lambda}
$$

> **Self-locking condition:** If $\lambda \le \phi$, then $\eta_{r} \le 0$, the screw cannot back-drive. For ball screws, $\phi \approx 5$ to 15 arc-minutes, while typical $\lambda$ is several degrees, so **ball screws are normally non-self-locking**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Drive Torque in Terms of Lead

Starting from $\eta_{f} = \dfrac{F L}{2\pi T}$, rearrange:

$$
T = \frac{F \, L}{2 \pi \, \eta_{f}}
$$

> This is the most-used board-exam form. If preload $F_{p}$ is also acting, an additional preload torque $T_{p} = \dfrac{F_{p} \, L}{2 \pi}\left(\dfrac{1 - \eta_{f}^{2}}{\eta_{f}}\right)$ must be added (approximate).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Solved Example (Full 14-mark sub-part style):**
> A CNC vertical machining centre uses a ball screw to drive its X-axis slide. The screw has a mean ball diameter $d_{m} = 40$ mm, lead $L = 10$ mm, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.01$, and is subjected to an axial thrust load $F = 8000$ N. Determine:
> (i) The lead angle $\lambda$
> (ii) The forward drive torque $T$
> (iii) The forward and reverse efficiencies
> (iv) The required drive motor power at 1500 rpm.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Lead angle:**

$$
\tan \lambda = \frac{L}{\pi d_{m}} = \frac{10}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{10}{125.664} = 0.07958
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = \arctan(0.07958) = 4.55^{\circ}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Friction angle:**

$$
\phi = \arctan(\mu) = \arctan(0.01) = 0.5729^{\circ}
$$

**Step (iii) — Forward efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{f} = \frac{\tan(4.55^{\circ})}{\tan(4.55^{\circ} + 0.5729^{\circ})} = \frac{\tan(4.55^{\circ})}{\tan(5.123^{\circ})} = \frac{0.0796}{0.0896}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{f} = 0.888 \approx 88.8\%}
$$

**Step (iv) — Drive torque:**

$$
T = \frac{F \, L}{2 \pi \, \eta_{f}} = \frac{8000 \times 0.010}{2 \pi \times 0.888} = \frac{80}{5.579}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 14.34 \; \text{N·m}}
$$

**Step (v) — Reverse (back-driving) efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{r} = \frac{\tan(\lambda - \phi)}{\tan \lambda} = \frac{\tan(3.977^{\circ})}{\tan(4.55^{\circ})} = \frac{0.0696}{0.0796}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{r} = 0.874 \approx 87.4\%}
$$

**Step (vi) — Drive power:**

$$
P = \frac{2 \pi \, n \, T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 14.34}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 2.252 \; \text{kW}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Life Calculation Example

> **Solved Example (Life of Ball Screw under Fluctuating Load):**
> The dynamic load rating of a ball-screw is $C = 35$ kN. The axial load fluctuates between $P_{min} = 2$ kN and $P_{max} = 8$ kN. The mean speed is $n_{m} = 1200$ rpm. Find (i) equivalent load, (ii) life in revolutions, (iii) life in hours.

**Step (i) — Equivalent dynamic load (cubic mean):**

$$
P_{eq} = \sqrt[\leftroot{-1}\uproot{2}\scriptstyle 3]{\frac{P_{max}^{3} + P_{min}^{3}}{2}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{8000^{3} + 2000^{3}}{2}}
$$

$$
P_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{5.12 \times 10^{11} + 8.0 \times 10^{9}}{2}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{5.20 \times 10^{11}}{2}} = \sqrt[3]{2.60 \times 10^{11}}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{eq} = 6383 \; \text{N} \approx 6.38 \; \text{kN}}
$$

**Step (ii) — Rating life in revolutions:**

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P_{eq}}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6} = \left(\frac{35{,}000}{6383}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
L_{10} = (5.484)^{3} \times 10^{6} = 164.96 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{10} = 1.6496 \times 10^{8} \; \text{revolutions}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Life in hours:**

$$
L_{h} = \frac{L_{10}}{60 \, n_{m}} = \frac{1.6496 \times 10^{8}}{60 \times 1200} = \frac{1.6496 \times 10^{8}}{72{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{h} \approx 2291 \; \text{hours} \approx 0.26 \; \text{years of continuous duty}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a typical single-shift (8 h/day, 250 days/year) CNC machine, this screw would last ≈ 1.15 years — borderline. The designer would either upgrade to a higher $C$ rating or impose load smoothing to extend life.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ball-Screw Functional Architecture (Construction View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Screw_End_A[Screw End A Drive Side]
        A1[Servomotor Coupling]
        A2[Angular Contact Thrust Bearing]
        A3[Lock Nut and Preload Washer]
    end
    subgraph Screw_Body[Screw Shaft Body]
        B1[Helical Raceway Gothic Arch]
        B2[Balls between Screw and Nut]
        B3[Ball Nut with Internal Groove]
        B4[Return Circuit External Tube]
    end
    subgraph Screw_End_B[Screw End B Free Side]
        C1[Support Bearing]
        C2[Wiper and Seal]
        C3[Limit Switch Bumper]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1
    B1 <--> B2
    B2 <--> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B1
    B3 --> C2 --> C1 --> C3
    A2 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The servomotor applies torque through a flexible coupling to **End A** (the "fixed-free" bearing arrangement).
- The screw's helical raceway drives the balls, which transfer force to the nut, which is bolted to the machine slide.
- Balls exit the load zone, pass through the **return circuit**, and re-enter — completing a closed loop.
- **End B** provides axial support and a wiper to keep contaminants out.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Recirc[Ball Recirculation Methods]

    Type1 --> T1c[Simple and Low Cost]
```

### 4.3 Force Resolution on a Single Ball (Free-Body Concept)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Axial Thrust F] --> ResNode[Force Resolution on Ball]
    F2[Normal Reaction N] --> ResNode
    F3[Friction mu times N] --> ResNode
    ResNode --> Out1[Component along Axis N sine lambda]
    ResNode --> Out2[Component Tangent N cosine lambda]
    Out1 --> Eq1[Axial Equilibrium]
    Out2 --> Eq2[Torque Arm d_m divided by 2]
    Eq1 --> Final[Derive T equals F L over 2 pi eta]
    Eq2 --> Final
```

**Reading the diagram:**
This block-level flow mirrors the **derivation sequence in Section 3.2**: input forces (axial, normal, frictional) → resolved components along axis and tangent → fed into axial equilibrium and torque arm equations → final efficiency expression.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** Define a **ball screw**. List any four advantages it has over a conventional sliding lead screw. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Model Answer:**
> A ball screw is a mechanical device that converts rotary motion into linear motion using a train of hardened steel balls as the rolling elements between a helical screw and a mating nut.
>
> **Advantages over a sliding lead screw:**
> 1. **Higher mechanical efficiency** (≈ 90 % vs 30–50 %).
> 2. **Lower frictional losses** — rolling replaces sliding friction.
> 3. **Higher positional accuracy and repeatability** (1–5 µm).
> 4. **Lower driving torque** for the same axial load.
> 5. **Longer life** under preloaded and lubricated conditions.
> 6. **Lower heat generation** during operation.

---

**Q2.** Explain briefly the **three methods of ball recirculation** used in ball screws. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **External Recirculation (Deflector / Return-Tube Type):** Balls exit the nut at the end of the engaged zone, are picked up by a curved tube fitted externally on the nut body, and guided back to the start of the raceway. Simple and economical, but increases the overall nut diameter.
> 2. **Internal Recirculation (Insert-Deflector Type):** A small C-shaped deflector is embedded inside the nut groove itself; the balls follow a smooth, compact path within the nut cross-section. Yields a smaller nut OD and smoother operation.
> 3. **End-Cap Recirculation:** A cap at the end of the nut turns the balls through 180° and feeds them back. Low-noise, high-speed, and suitable for high-precision CNC applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam – May 2024**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **forward efficiency of a ball screw** in terms of the lead angle $\lambda$ and the friction angle $\phi$. State the condition for **self-locking** and explain why a ball screw is normally **not self-locking**. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A CNC horizontal machining centre uses a ball screw with the following specifications: mean ball diameter $d_{m} = 50$ mm, lead $L = 12$ mm, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.008$, axial thrust $F = 12$ kN, speed $n = 1000$ rpm. Calculate:
 (i) Lead angle, (ii) Drive torque, (iii) Forward efficiency, (iv) Reverse efficiency, (v) Motor power in kW. `[7 marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**
> *[Refer to Section 3.2 for the full step-by-step derivation. The final boxed result must be quoted:]* 
> $$\boxed{\eta_{f} = \dfrac{\tan \lambda}{\tan(\lambda + \phi)}}$$
>
> **Self-locking condition:** A screw is self-locking when the reverse efficiency $\eta_{r} \le 0$, i.e., when $\lambda \le \phi$ (the lead angle is at most equal to the friction angle). In this condition, an axial load cannot drive the screw backwards.
>
> **Why a ball screw is not self-locking:** Because the **friction angle $\phi$ is very small (5–15 arc-minutes)** for rolling contact, while the **lead angle $\lambda$ is typically several degrees**. Hence $\lambda \gg \phi$, the reverse efficiency is positive (≈ 85 %), and the screw is free to back-drive. This is why **vertical CNC axes require counter-balance or holding brakes**.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) — Lead angle:**

$$
\tan \lambda = \frac{L}{\pi d_{m}} = \frac{12}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{12}{157.08} = 0.07639
$$

$$
\lambda = \arctan(0.07639) = 4.37^{\circ}
$$

**Step (ii) — Friction angle:**

$$
\phi = \arctan(0.008) = 0.4584^{\circ}
$$

**Step (iii) — Forward efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{f} = \frac{\tan(4.37^{\circ})}{\tan(4.37^{\circ} + 0.4584^{\circ})} = \frac{0.07639}{\tan(4.828^{\circ})} = \frac{0.07639}{0.08451} = 0.904
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{f} \approx 90.4\%}
$$

**Step (iv) — Drive torque:**

$$
T = \frac{F \, L}{2 \pi \, \eta_{f}} = \frac{12000 \times 0.012}{2 \pi \times 0.904} = \frac{144}{5.679} = 25.36 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 25.4 \; \text{N·m}}
$$

**Step (v) — Reverse efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{r} = \frac{\tan(\lambda - \phi)}{\tan \lambda} = \frac{\tan(3.911^{\circ})}{\tan(4.37^{\circ})} = \frac{0.06838}{0.07639} = 0.895
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{r} \approx 89.5\%}
$$

**Step (vi) — Motor power:**

$$
P = \frac{2 \pi \, n \, T}{60{,}000} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 25.36}{60{,}000} = \frac{159{,}340}{60{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 2.656 \; \text{kW}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **construction and working of a ball-screw assembly** used in a CNC machine tool axis. Clearly label the screw, nut, balls, recirculation system, bearings, and seals. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A ball screw has a dynamic load rating $C = 28$ kN. It operates at speeds varying between 800 rpm and 1500 rpm, with corresponding axial loads of 3 kN and 9 kN respectively. Calculate:
 (i) Mean speed, (ii) Equivalent dynamic load, (iii) Basic rating life in revolutions, (iv) Life in hours. `[7 marks]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**
> A CNC axis ball-screw assembly consists of:
> - A **helically grooved screw shaft** supported at both ends by **angular-contact thrust bearings** (fixed at the drive end, supported at the free end).
> - A **ball nut** with a matching internal groove, bolted to the moving slide of the machine.
> - **Steel balls** (typically grade 10 or better) roll between the screw and nut raceways, transmitting axial thrust.
> - A **recirculation system** (external return tube, internal deflector, or end cap) returns balls from the end of the engaged zone to the start.
> - **Wipers/seals** at both nut ends exclude chips and coolant.
> - A **servomotor** drives the screw through a torsionally stiff coupling.
>
> **Working:** When the motor rotates the screw, the helical raceway forces the balls to roll. The balls transmit axial force to the nut, which moves linearly by one lead per revolution. Balls leaving the loaded zone are returned through the recirculation circuit, enabling continuous rotation. *[Refer to Section 4.1 for the labelled block diagram.]*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step (i) — Mean speed:**

$$
n_{m} = \frac{n_{min} + n_{max}}{2} = \frac{800 + 1500}{2} = 1150 \; \text{rpm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Equivalent dynamic load (cubic mean):**

$$
P_{eq} = \sqrt[\leftroot{-1}\uproot{2}\scriptstyle 3]{\frac{P_{max}^{3} + P_{min}^{3}}{2}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{9000^{3} + 3000^{3}}{2}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{7.29 \times 10^{11} + 2.7 \times 10^{10}}{2}}
$$

$$
P_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{7.56 \times 10^{11}}{2}} = \sqrt[3]{3.78 \times 10^{11}} = 7227 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{eq} \approx 7.23 \; \text{kN}}
$$

**Step (iii) — Basic rating life in revolutions:**

$$
L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P_{eq}}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6} = \left(\frac{28{,}000}{7227}\right)^{3} \times 10^{6} = (3.874)^{3} \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
L_{10} = 58.16 \times 10^{6} \; \text{revolutions}
$$

**Step (iv) — Life in hours:**

$$
L_{h} = \frac{L_{10}}{60 \, n_{m}} = \frac{58.16 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1150} = \frac{58.16 \times 10^{6}}{69{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{h} \approx 843 \; \text{hours}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit-Mismatch Disaster (very common):** Forgetting to convert lead from mm to m in the torque formula $T = FL/(2\pi\eta)$. Always use $L$ in metres or apply the direct form $T = F \times L_{mm} / (2000\pi \eta)$.
> 2. **Friction-angle sign error:** Confusing $(\lambda + \phi)$ for forward drive vs $(\lambda - \phi)$ for back-drive. Forward efficiency denominator uses **sum**; reverse uses **difference**.
> 3. **Arithmetic mean vs cubic mean:** For fluctuating loads, students often use $(P_{max}+P_{min})/2$ — the ball-screw standard demands the **cubic mean** $\sqrt[3]{(P_{max}^{3}+P_{min}^{3})/2}$. A linear mean **underestimates** $P_{eq}$ and **overestimates** life.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the self-locking condition** when deriving efficiency — graders deduct 1–2 marks if $\lambda \le \phi$ is not mentioned.
> 5. **Confusing lead with pitch:** Single-start screws have lead = pitch; multi-start screws have lead = $n \times$ pitch. State the number of starts explicitly.

---
