| 8 | $F_{rapid} = 2 \text{ to } 5 \times F_{cutting}$ | Rapid traverse vs cutting feed | Machine commissioning | multiplier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The feed word is the **primary productivity lever** in CNC programming. An optimized F-value balances productivity (high MRR) with tool life and surface finish (low cutting forces). CNC programmers spend considerable time selecting F-values from **machinability databases** (e.g., Sandvik Coromant, Kennametal, ISO 3685).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The feed word is critical in the following industrial domains:

1. **Aerospace Machining** — High-feed programming for titanium and Inconel components where MRR is paramount.
2. **Automotive Production** — Cycle-time optimization through aggressive F-values on transfer lines.
3. **Die and Mold Making** — High-speed milling with carefully tuned F-words for HSC (High-Speed Cutting) of hardened tool steels (HRC > 50).
4. **Medical Implant Manufacturing** — Micro-feed F-words (e.g., F5 mm/min) for titanium implants requiring mirror finish.
5. **Additive-Subtractive Hybrid Manufacturing** — Feed word governs the subtractive finishing pass over additively built features.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Feed per Tooth to F-Word Value (Milling)

### Problem Setup
A CNC vertical machining center is programmed to mill a pocket in mild steel (AISI 1020) using a 4-flute carbide end mill of diameter 20 mm. The recommended feed per tooth from the machinability chart is $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth. The recommended cutting speed for the workpiece-tool combination is $V_c = 80$ m/min.

**Required:** Calculate (i) the spindle speed $N$, and (ii) the **feed word value** `F` to be programmed.

---

### Derivation Step 1 — Compute Spindle Speed $N$

The cutting speed $V_c$ is related to spindle speed and cutter diameter by the standard relation:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$N = \frac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**Textual Explanation:** We multiply the cutting speed (in m/min) by 1000 to convert to mm/min, then divide by the circumference of the cutter ($\pi D$) to obtain revolutions per minute.

**Substituting the values:**

$$N = \frac{80 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$N = \frac{80000}{62.8319}$$

$$N = 1273.24 \text{ rpm}$$

**Textual Explanation:** The calculated spindle speed is non-integer. CNC controls accept integer or decimal spindle speed values, but machine tools typically have a limited spindle speed range. We select the **nearest available spindle speed** on the machine (say, $N = 1200$ rpm) for actual programming.

---

### Derivation Step 2 — Compute the Feed Word (F-Value)

Using the milling feed formula:

$$F_{mm/min} = f_z \times Z_n \times N$$

**Textual Explanation:** The total feed per minute is the product of three factors: how much each tooth advances (feed per tooth), how many teeth engage the cut (number of flutes), and how fast the cutter rotates (spindle speed).

**Substituting the values:**

$$F_{mm/min} = 0.08 \times 4 \times 1200$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 0.32 \times 1200$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 384 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Textual Explanation:** The CNC program block will be written as:
```
N50 G01 X50 Y30 F384
```
Here, `F384` is the **feed word** commanding 384 mm/min linear feed during the G01 linear interpolation.

---

### Derivation Step 3 — Verification via Machining Time

For a tool path length $L = 150$ mm, the machining time is:

$$t_m = \frac{L}{F_{mm/min}}$$

$$t_m = \frac{150}{384}$$

$$t_m = 0.3906 \text{ min} = 23.44 \text{ seconds}$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is a reasonable machining time for a 150 mm pocket, confirming that the calculated F-word is in a practical industrial range.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Feed per Revolution to F-Word Value (Turning)

### Problem Setup
A CNC lathe is to perform a rough turning pass on an EN8 steel workpiece (diameter 60 mm) reduced to 55 mm. The recommended feed per revolution is $f_{rev} = 0.3$ mm/rev, and the recommended cutting speed is $V_c = 150$ m/min.

**Required:** Calculate the feed word value `F` to be programmed.

---

### Derivation Step 1 — Compute Spindle Speed

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

Using the **average diameter** (sometimes the initial, sometimes final, depending on convention — here we use the workpiece diameter during the pass, say $D = 57.5$ mm):

$$N = \frac{150 \times 1000}{\pi \times 57.5}$$

$$N = \frac{150000}{180.642}$$

$$N = 830.3 \text{ rpm}$$

Round to nearest spindle step: $N = 800$ rpm.

---

### Derivation Step 2 — Compute Feed per Minute

$$F_{mm/min} = f_{rev} \times N$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 0.3 \times 800$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 240 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Textual Explanation:** However, in turning, the programmer may directly program in **feed per revolution (G99 mode)** by writing `F0.3` in the program. The controller automatically converts this to per-minute feed internally by multiplying with the active spindle speed.

```
N20 G99 G01 X-1.5 F0.3   (Feed per revolution = 0.3 mm/rev)
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Parameter | Milling (G94) | Turning (G99) | Drilling (G94) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-Word Unit** | mm/min | mm/rev (or mm/min) | mm/min |
| **G-Code** | G94 (modal) | G99 (modal) | G94 (modal) |
| **Calculation Basis** | $f_z \times Z_n \times N$ | $f_{rev} \times N$ | Empirical from table |
| **Typical Range** | 50 – 5000 mm/min | 0.05 – 0.5 mm/rev | 30 – 500 mm/min |
| **Spindle Coupling** | Independent (asynchronous) | Synchronous (G99) | Independent |
| **Override Control** | M-Fn (Feedrate Override) | M-Fn + G99 lock | M-Fn |
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CNC Program Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CNC Program Block N50] --> B[Address Word Parser]
    B --> C[Preparatory Word G01]
    B --> D[Coordinate Words X Y Z]
    B --> E[Feed Word F384]
    B --> F[Spindle Word S1200]
    B --> G[Tool Word T01]
    B --> H[Misc Word M08]
    E --> I{Active G-Code}
    I -->|G94| J[Interpret as mm per min]
    I -->|G99| K[Interpret as mm per rev]
    I -->|G93| L[Interpret as inverse time]
    J --> M[Servo Pulse Generator]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Axis Servo Drives X Y Z]
    N --> O[Tool Moves at F384 mm per min]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The CNC controller parses each program block into discrete **address words**.
- The **Feed Word (F)** is fed into a decision block that interprets its physical meaning based on the **active preparatory G-code**.
- The interpreted feed value is then dispatched to the **servo drives** for physical motion.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Feed Word Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece Material] --> D[Consult Machinability Chart]
    B[Tool Type and Diameter] --> D
    C[Operation Type Milling Turning Drilling] --> D
    D --> E{Determine Cutting Speed Vc}
    E --> F[Compute Spindle Speed N]
    F --> G{Operation Type}
    G -->|Milling| H[Select Feed per Tooth fz]
    G -->|Turning| I[Select Feed per Revolution frev]
    H --> J[Compute F mm per min using fz x Zn x N]
    I --> K[Compute F mm per min using frev x N or set F0.3 for G99]
    J --> L[Program Block with F Word]
    K --> L
    L --> M[CNC Controller Executes Block]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The F-word is **not arbitrarily chosen**; it follows a deterministic engineering workflow.
- Material, tool, and operation data feed into machinability charts, which prescribe cutting speed.
- Cutting speed and operation type jointly determine the appropriate **F-word calculation formula**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Modal Behavior of the F-Word

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Program
    participant C as Controller
    participant T as Tool

    P->>C: N10 G01 X10 F100
    C->>T: Move at 100 mm per min
    T-->>C: Position reached at X10
    P->>C: N20 X20
    C->>T: Move at 100 mm per min MODAL
    T-->>C: Position reached at X20
    P->>C: N30 X30 F200
    C->>T: Move at 200 mm per min UPDATED
    T-->>C: Position reached at X30
    P->>C: N40 X40 F0
    C->>T: STOP - Feed Hold
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Note that in block **N20**, no F-word is specified. The controller **carries over F100** from block N10 because F is **modal**.
- In block **N30**, the F-word is updated to F200, overriding the modal F100.
- In block **N40**, F0 commands a **feed hold** (no movement) — a useful safety feature in CNC programming.
---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Feed Word" in CNC programming. Give a sample program statement and explain its components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Feed Word** is a modal address word in a CNC program block that specifies the rate of relative movement between the cutting tool and the workpiece. It is always denoted by the letter **'F'** followed by a numerical value.

**Example Statement:**
```
N30 G01 X50 Y25 F150
```

**Components Explanation:**
- `N30` — Sequence number (block identifier)
- `G01` — Preparatory function (linear interpolation)
- `X50 Y25` — Coordinate words (target position)
- `F150` — **Feed word** commanding 150 mm/min feed rate in G94 mode (metric)

The F150 value is **modal**, meaning it remains in effect for all subsequent motion blocks (G01, G02, G03) until a new F-word is programmed or the program ends.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Feed per Minute (G94) and Feed per Revolution (G99) modes in CNC programming.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | G94 (Feed per Minute) | G99 (Feed per Revolution) |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | mm/min or in/min | mm/rev or in/rev |
| **Spindle Coupling** | Asynchronous (independent) | Synchronous (coupled) |
| **Application** | Milling, drilling, general machining | Turning, threading, tapping |
| **Formula** | $F = f_z \cdot Z_n \cdot N$ | $F = f_{rev}$ (direct programming) |
| **Example** | `G94 G01 X10 F150` | `G99 G01 X-1.5 F0.25` |
| **Override** | Feedrate override allowed | Feed override locked in some controls |

The choice between G94 and G99 depends on the machine tool type, operation, and synchronization requirements.

---

## 📗 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different types of feed word specifications in CNC programming with suitable examples. Discuss the role of preparatory G-codes (G93, G94, G99) in determining the units and interpretation of the feed word. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The CNC feed word can be specified in three distinct modes based on the active preparatory G-code:

**1. Feed per Minute (G94) — 3 Marks**
- The F-value represents the **linear feed rate per unit time** (mm/min or in/min).
- Used predominantly in **milling and drilling** operations where the feed is independent of spindle rotation.
- **Example:** `G94 G01 X100 Y50 F200` → Tool moves at 200 mm/min along the linear path.
- **Calculation:** $F = f_z \times Z_n \times N$

**2. Feed per Revolution (G99) — 3 Marks**
- The F-value represents the **linear feed per spindle revolution** (mm/rev or in/rev).
- Used in **turning operations** where feed is naturally synchronized with workpiece rotation.
- **Example:** `G99 G01 X-1.5 F0.3` → Tool advances 0.3 mm per spindle revolution.
- The controller internally converts to mm/min using $F_{mm/min} = f_{rev} \times N$.

**3. Inverse Time Feed (G93) — 1 Mark**
- The F-value is specified in **reciprocal minutes (1/min)**.
- Used in **multi-axis contouring** where the feed rate changes with tool path geometry.
- **Example:** `G93 G01 X50 Y50 F0.02` → Tool completes the path in 1/0.02 = 50 seconds.

The role of preparatory G-codes is to establish the **modal framework** within which the F-word is interpreted. They act as **unit and interpretation switches** for all subsequent F-words until overridden.

---

**(b)** A CNC milling operation requires machining an aluminum alloy (Al 6061) slot. The cutter is a 4-flute HSS end mill of diameter 12 mm. The recommended cutting speed is 120 m/min, and the feed per tooth is 0.06 mm/tooth. The slot length is 200 mm. Calculate the **feed word value (F)** to be programmed and the **machining time** for the pass. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Spindle Speed (N)** — 2 Marks

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

$$N = \frac{120 \times 1000}{\pi \times 12}$$

$$N = \frac{120000}{37.699}$$

$$N = 3183.1 \text{ rpm}$$

Round to nearest available spindle step: **N = 3200 rpm**

**Step 2: Compute Feed Word (F)** — 3 Marks

$$F_{mm/min} = f_z \times Z_n \times N$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 0.06 \times 4 \times 3200$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 0.24 \times 3200$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 768 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Program Statement:** `G01 X200 F768`

**Step 3: Compute Machining Time** — 2 Marks

$$t_m = \frac{L}{F_{mm/min}}$$

$$t_m = \frac{200}{768}$$

$$t_m = 0.2604 \text{ min} = 15.625 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** **F = 768 mm/min**, **Machining time ≈ 15.63 seconds**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **factors affecting the selection of feed rate** in CNC machining. How does the feed word interact with the spindle speed word to influence productivity and surface finish? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Factors Affecting Feed Rate Selection:**

**1. Workpiece Material Properties** — 1 Mark
- Harder, tougher materials (e.g., Inconel, titanium) require **lower feed rates** to avoid excessive cutting forces and tool wear.
- Softer, free-machining materials (e.g., Al 6061, brass) allow **higher feed rates**.

**2. Cutting Tool Material and Geometry** — 1 Mark
- Carbide tools tolerate higher feeds than HSS tools.
- More flutes (higher $Z_n$) increase MRR at the same $f_z$.
- Coated tools (TiN, Al₂O₃) permit higher F-values.

**3. Type of Operation** — 1 Mark
- Roughing: High F (productivity-driven).
- Finishing: Low F (surface finish-driven).

**4. Depth and Width of Cut** — 1 Mark
- Heavier cuts ($a_p$, $a_e$ large) require lower F to maintain acceptable cutting forces.

**5. Machine Tool Capability** — 1 Mark
- Spindle power, rigidity, and feed drive limits cap the maximum F.

**6. Surface Finish Requirements** — 1 Mark
- The theoretical surface finish in milling: $R_a \approx \frac{f_z^2}{32 \times r_\epsilon}$ (where $r_\epsilon$ is tool nose radius). Lower $f_z$ → better finish.

**7. Coolant and Chip Evacuation** — 1 Mark
- Deep slots/drills require reduced F for chip evacuation and cooling.

**Interaction with Spindle Speed Word (S-Word):**

The F-word and S-word are **interdependent**. From the formula $F = f_z \times Z_n \times N$, an increase in S (spindle speed) proportionally increases F. However, productivity (MRR) scales linearly with F but nonlinearly with V_c due to heat generation. **Optimal F-S combination** lies on a curve of constant MRR or constant tool life, plotted in **machinability charts**.

---

**(b)** In a turning operation on mild steel, the spindle speed is 1000 rpm and the feed word is programmed as `F0.25` in G99 mode. Calculate: **(i)** the feed per minute, **(ii)** the feed per tooth equivalent if the cutting tool has 2 effective cutting edges (rough analogy), and **(iii)** the machining time for a turning pass of 120 mm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Spindle speed $N = 1000$ rpm
- Feed per revolution $f_{rev} = 0.25$ mm/rev (from `F0.25` in G99)
- Tool cutting edges (analogy) $Z_n = 2$
- Pass length $L = 120$ mm

**(i) Feed per Minute** — 2 Marks

$$F_{mm/min} = f_{rev} \times N$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 0.25 \times 1000$$

$$F_{mm/min} = 250 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(ii) Feed per Tooth Equivalent** — 2 Marks

$$f_z = \frac{f_{rev}}{Z_n}$$

$$f_z = \frac{0.25}{2}$$

$$f_z = 0.125 \text{ mm/tooth}$$

**(Note:** In turning, this is an *analogous* quantity; turning tools technically have a single effective feed per revolution, but the analogy helps in unified machinability analysis.)

**(iii) Machining Time** — 3 Marks

$$t_m = \frac{L}{F_{mm/min}}$$

$$t_m = \frac{120}{250}$$

$$t_m = 0.48 \text{ min} = 28.8 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** $F_{mm/min} = 250$ mm/min, $f_z = 0.125$ mm/tooth, $t_m = 28.8$ s

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often confuse mm/min with mm/rev. Always check whether the question specifies G94 or G99 before plugging into the formula.
> 2. **Forgetting Modal Behavior:** In coding-style questions, students may redundantly program the F-word in every block. This is acceptable but inefficient — KTU examiners may deduct 0.5 mark for "verbose programming" if asked to optimize.
> 3. **Spindle Speed Calculation Errors:** Forgetting to multiply $V_c$ by 1000 to convert m/min to mm/min is the single most common error in feed word calculation questions.
> 4. **No Units in Final Answer:** KTU mandates units in every numerical answer. A bare "384" without "mm/min" may lose 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Inverse Time Misuse:** Students often confuse G93 with G94. G93 is rarely used in basic KTU papers but is a favorite in advanced ESE questions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Feed Word = Address 'F' + Numerical Value** — it is a **modal** address word specifying the cutting feed rate.
- ✅ **Three Modes:** G94 (per minute), G99 (per revolution), G93 (inverse time).
- ✅ **G94 is for milling/drilling**; **G99 is for turning**; **G93 is for multi-axis contouring**.
- ✅ **Milling Formula:** $F_{mm/min} = f_z \times Z_n \times N$ — know this cold.
- ✅ **Turning Formula:** $F_{mm/min} = f_{rev} \times N$ — also memorize.
- ✅ **Spindle Speed Formula:** $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ — used in all feed word calculation problems.
- ✅ **Machining Time:** $t_m = \dfrac{L}{F_{mm/min}}$ — always report in minutes or seconds with units.
- ✅ **Modal Behavior:** F-word persists across blocks; write once, apply many times.
- ✅ **MRR is proportional to F** — higher F = higher productivity, but lower tool life and rougher finish.
- ✅ **Surface Finish** in milling: $R_a \propto f_z^2$ — double the feed per tooth → quadruple the roughness (in theory).
- ✅ **F0 = Feed Hold / Stop** — a safety feature in many CNC controllers.
- ✅ **Feedrate Override (M-Fn / Feedrate % knob)** is an operator control that scales the F-word by a percentage (typically 0% to 200%) without modifying the program.
- ✅ **Always pair F-word with a motion G-code (G00, G01, G02, G03)** — F-word has no effect during a block with no motion.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tool Word
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tool Word

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Computerized Numerical Control (CNC) programming, a **Tool Word** is the part of a programmed instruction block (line) that invokes a specific cutting tool from the machine's automatic tool changer (ATC) or tool magazine and simultaneously (or subsequently) activates its associated offset register. The tool word is designated by the address letter **"T"** followed by a numerical value, conforming to either a **2-digit** or **4-digit** word depending on the controller standard (typically FANUC, Siemens, or HEIDENHAIN).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> A tool word is **not merely a tool number** — it is an integrated command that performs two simultaneous functions: (1) **Tool Selection** (physical tool call) and (2) **Offset Activation** (application of tool geometry/wear data).

The general structure of a CNC program block, standardized by **ISO 6983** and **EIA RS-267**, is shown below:

> **N### G## X### Y### Z### T## [M##]**

Where `T##` represents the tool word. The position of the tool word within the block is flexible but conventionally placed after coordinate words and before miscellaneous functions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Pen Case" Metaphor

Imagine a calligrapher who owns a single desk with **24 numbered pens**, each with a unique ink color, nib thickness, and length. Before writing any line of a letter, the calligrapher must:

1. **Select** the correct pen from the rack (e.g., Pen #07).
2. **Calibrate** its writing offset (since each pen has a slightly different tip-to-hand position, registered in a small notebook: "Pen 07 needs +2 mm vertical lift").
3. **Write** the stroke.

The **Tool Word** does exactly this in CNC:

| Calligrapher Action | CNC Equivalent | Tool Word Element |
|---|---|---|
| Choosing pen #07 from rack | Pulling tool #07 from ATC magazine | First 2 digits of T word (Tool Number) |
| Reading pen offset from notebook | Loading tool length/radius from offset table | Second 2 digits of T word (Offset ID) |
| Writing the stroke | Executing G-code motion block | Subsequent motion commands |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A tool word is the CNC's way of saying, *"Pick the right cutter AND remember its personal size correction — I'll be moving it next."*

## 1.3 Standard Metric Conventions

- **Standard Tool Word Width:** **4 digits** (dominant in modern CNC lathes).
- **Tool Magazine Capacity:** Typically **8 to 40 stations** in machining centers; up to **120+** in advanced production systems.
- **Offset Register Range:** Typically **±999.999 mm** (geometry) with wear offsets added algebraically.
- **Default Safe Position (before T command):** Tool in **home/change position** — controlled by machine PLC.

## 1.4 Two Dominant Dialects — Lathe vs. Machining Center

The format of the tool word differs significantly between turning centers and machining centers due to their kinematic structures.

**A. CNC Lathe (Turning Center) Tool Word:**

$$T_{xy} \quad \text{where } x = \text{Tool Station (1-12 typical)}, \ y = \text{Offset Register (1-32 typical)}$$

Example: `T0304` invokes **Tool Station 03** and activates **Offset Register 04**.

**B. CNC Machining Center (Milling) Tool Word:**

$$T_{xx} \quad \text{where } xx = \text{Tool Number (1-99)}$$

Example: `T05` invokes **Tool 05** from the magazine. The offset register is then called separately using the **H** address (length) or **D** address (diameter/radius) within the same block:

`N40 T05 M06 G43 H05 Z100.0`

Here, `M06` performs the **automatic tool change (ATC)**, and `G43 H05` activates the **positive tool length offset** stored in offset register 05.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Tool Word — Component Breakdown

A fully-resolved tool word in CNC programming comprises **four logical sub-functions**, executed either simultaneously or in sequential commands within the same or adjacent program blocks.

### 2.1.1 Tool Selection (T Code)
- **Function:** Directs the machine to a specific pocket in the tool magazine.
- **Mechanical Action:** Magazine rotates (drum-type) or traverses (chain-type) to align the requested pocket with the ATC arm.
- **Code Standard:** `T##` (2 digits common); some controllers support `T####` (4 digits) for high-capacity magazines.

### 2.1.2 Tool Change Command (M06)
- **Function:** Triggers the **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** mechanism — gripper arm swings, unclamps the spindle, swaps tools, re-clamps.
- **Code:** Almost universally `M06` in ISO/EIA standards.
- **Interlock Behavior:** Many controllers require `M06` to be in the **same block** as the tool selection word, or in the **next block** depending on the post-processor.

### 2.1.3 Tool Length Compensation (G43 / G44 / G49)
- **Purpose:** Corrects the Z-axis position so that the **programmed tool tip (theoretical)** coincides with the **actual physical tool tip** of the loaded cutter.
- **Modes:**
  - `G43` — **Positive length compensation** (move tool down by offset amount) — most common.
  - `G44` — **Negative length compensation** (rarely used).
  - `G49` — **Cancel tool length compensation.**

Mathematically, the compensation is applied as:

$$Z_{actual} = Z_{commanded} + (\text{Geometry Offset}) + (\text{Wear Offset})$$

Or equivalently, using the H register value:

$$Z_{actual} = Z_{commanded} - H_{value}$$

(The sign convention depends on controller, but the effect is the same: the controller offsets the machine's coordinate frame by the length of the loaded tool.)

### 2.1.4 Tool Radius/Diameter Compensation (G41 / G42 / G40)
- **Purpose:** Allows the programmer to write the **part profile** directly; the controller automatically offsets the tool path by the **cutter radius** to either the **left** (G41) or **right** (G42) of the programmed direction.
- **Modes:**
  - `G41` — Cutter compensation **LEFT**.
  - `G42` — Cutter compensation **RIGHT**.
  - `G40` — Cancel cutter compensation.

The offset path equations for a 2D contour are:

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{offset} &= X_{programmed} \pm r \cdot \cos(\theta + 90^\circ) \\
Y_{offset} &= Y_{programmed} \pm r \cdot \sin(\theta + 90^\circ)
\end{aligned}$$

Where `r` is the cutter radius and `θ` is the local tool path tangent angle.

## 2.2 Tool Offset Register — The Two-Layer Memory Model

Modern CNC controllers store **two independent offsets per tool**:

| Offset Type | Function | Typical Range | Adjustability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geometry Offset** | Sets the absolute nominal position of the tool tip relative to the spindle nose (length) or spindle axis (diameter). | ±999.999 mm | Set once per tool setup, rarely changed. |
| **Wear Offset** | Compensates for gradual tool wear during machining — applied **algebraically** to the geometry offset. | ±99.999 mm | Adjusted during production based on actual dimensional measurements. |

The **effective offset** used by the controller is:

$$H_{effective} = H_{geometry} + H_{wear}$$

This two-layer model is critical in production environments because:
- Tools can be **re-sharpened and re-measured** without disturbing the geometry offset.
- **Wear compensation** is updated daily/weekly based on First Article Inspection (FAI) data.

## 2.3 Spindle Speed & Feed Rate — Coupled to the Tool Word

The tool word directly influences the **cutting parameters** (`S` and `F` words) that must accompany it. The governing equations are:

### 2.3.1 Spindle Speed (N)

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Spindle speed in **rev/min (rpm)**
- $V_c$ = Cutting speed in **m/min** (material-dependent constant — e.g., mild steel ≈ **80 m/min**, aluminum ≈ **300 m/min**)
- $D$ = Tool diameter in **mm**

### 2.3.2 Feed Rate (F)

For milling (per tooth feed):

$$F = f_z \times z \times N$$

For turning (per revolution feed):

$$F = f_r \times N$$

Where:
- $F$ = Feed rate in **mm/min**
- $f_z$ = Feed per tooth in **mm/tooth**
- $f_r$ = Feed per revolution in **mm/rev**
- $z$ = Number of effective teeth/cutters on the tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** The tool word, spindle speed, and feed rate are inseparable — they must be programmed as a **coherent set** at the start of every tool change block.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Code | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| 2 | Milling Feed Rate | $F = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_z$ in mm/tooth |
| 3 | Turning Feed Rate | $F = f_r \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_r$ in mm/rev |
| 4 | Effective Tool Length Offset | $H_{eff} = H_{geom} + H_{wear}$ | mm | Algebraic sum |
| 5 | Tool Compensation (Lathe) | $Z_{act} = Z_{cmd} + \text{offset}$ | mm | Sign depends on controller |
| 6 | Cutter Compensation Path | $X_{off} = X_{prog} \pm r \cos(\theta+90^\circ)$ | mm | G41/G42 direction |
| 7 | Material Removal Rate (Milling) | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ depth, $a_e$ width |
| 8 | Tool Life Approximation | $V_c \cdot T^n = C$ (Taylor) | m, min | Empirical constant $C$ |
| 9 | Tool Magazine Index Time | $T_{idx} \propto \Delta N$ | sec | Linear for adjacent pockets |
| 10 | ATC Cycle Time | $T_{ATC} = T_{index} + T_{swap}$ | sec | Sum of magazine + arm motion |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tool word is the **single most critical instruction** in any CNC program because it governs:

- **Machining Accuracy:** Without proper offset compensation, every part is dimensionally wrong from the first cut.
- **Production Flexibility:** Same program can run on different machines with different tool setups by merely re-defining offsets — **no program edit needed**.
- **Tool Life Management:** Wear offsets enable **adaptive correction** during long production runs, maintaining part quality without operator intervention.
- **Multi-Tool Job Execution:** Complex parts (e.g., engine blocks, turbine blades) require 10-30 tool changes per setup — each invoked by a unique tool word.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Context:** In Industry 4.0 smart factories, the tool word is now often linked to **RFID-tagged tools** and **cloud-based offset databases** — the tool word acts as a key to retrieve not just geometry, but also remaining tool life, historical performance, and predictive maintenance alerts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Spindle Speed & Feed Rate Derivation

**Problem Statement [KTU Style]:**
A CNC machining center uses a 4-flute end mill of diameter **D = 50 mm** to machine an aluminum alloy workpiece. The recommended cutting speed for aluminum is **V_c = 300 m/min**, and the feed per tooth is **f_z = 0.15 mm/tooth**. Compute:
(a) The required spindle speed `N` in rpm.
(b) The required feed rate `F` in mm/min.
(c) The corresponding tool word block (assuming the tool is Tool #05, length offset H05, no radius comp).

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 50 \text{ mm} \\
V_c &= 300 \text{ m/min} \\
f_z &= 0.15 \text{ mm/tooth} \\
z &= 4 \text{ (number of flutes)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Spindle Speed Formula**

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$N = \frac{300 \times 1000}{\pi \times 50}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator and Denominator Separately**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 300 \times 1000 = 300{,}000 \\
\text{Denominator} &= \pi \times 50 = 157.0796
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final Spindle Speed**

$$N = \frac{300{,}000}{157.0796} \approx 1909.86 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 6 — Round to Controller Resolution** (typical: integer rpm)

$$\boxed{N \approx 1910 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Feed Rate Formula**

$$F = f_z \times z \times N$$

**Step 8 — Substitute Values**

$$F = 0.15 \times 4 \times 1910$$

**Step 9 — Compute Stepwise**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.15 \times 4 &= 0.60 \\
0.60 \times 1910 &= 1146 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 1146 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step 10 — Construct the CNC Program Block**

```
N50 T05 M06       (Tool change: select Tool 05, execute ATC)
N55 G90 G54 G00 X100.0 Y100.0 Z50.0  (Rapid to safe position)
N60 S1910 M03     (Spindle CW at 1910 rpm)
N65 G43 H05 Z5.0  (Apply tool length offset, approach workpiece)
N70 G01 Z-10.0 F500.0  (Plunge into workpiece at 500 mm/min)
N75 F1146         (Engage calculated feed rate for cutting)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Feed Values (N70 vs N75)?** The plunge feed (500 mm/min) is intentionally **slower** than the cutting feed (1146 mm/min) to prevent end-mill breakage during vertical entry — a critical best practice in CNC programming.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cutter Compensation Path Derivation

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC end mill of radius **r = 10 mm** is programmed to move along a straight contour at a tangent angle **θ = 30°** from the positive X-axis. The programmed endpoint is **(X_prog, Y_prog) = (100.0, 50.0)**. With **cutter compensation LEFT active (G41)**, compute the actual machine coordinates the controller will command.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters**

$$\begin{aligned}
r &= 10 \text{ mm} \\
\theta &= 30^\circ \\
X_{prog} &= 100.0 \text{ mm} \\
Y_{prog} &= 50.0 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Compensation} &= \text{LEFT (G41)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Determine Compensation Direction Vector**

For **LEFT compensation**, the tool is offset to the left of the programmed direction. The normal vector to the left is:

$$\vec{n}_{left} = (-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Normal Vector Components**

$$\begin{aligned}
-\sin(30^\circ) &= -0.500 \\
\cos(30^\circ) &= 0.866
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Compensation Offset Equations**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{offset} &= X_{prog} + r \cdot (-\sin\theta) \\
Y_{offset} &= Y_{prog} + r \cdot (\cos\theta)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Compute**

$$\begin{aligned}
X_{offset} &= 100.0 + 10 \times (-0.500) = 100.0 - 5.0 = 95.0 \text{ mm} \\
Y_{offset} &= 50.0 + 10 \times (0.866) = 50.0 + 8.66 = 58.66 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{(X_{actual}, Y_{actual}) = (95.0 \text{ mm}, \ 58.66 \text{ mm})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the controller **automatically displaces** the tool path by exactly the cutter radius to maintain the **programmed profile** on the workpiece. Without G41/G42, the programmer would have to manually offset every contour — a tedious and error-prone process. This single feature saves hundreds of programming hours in complex mold and die work.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Effective Offset Algebra

**Problem Statement:**
Tool #07 has the following offset data stored in the controller:
- Geometry length offset: **+125.400 mm**
- Wear length offset: **-0.080 mm** (tool has worn slightly shorter)
The programmed Z command is **Z = -25.000 mm**. Compute:
(a) The effective offset value.
(b) The actual Z-axis machine position.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Sum Geometry and Wear Offsets (Algebraic)**

$$H_{effective} = H_{geometry} + H_{wear}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$H_{effective} = (+125.400) + (-0.080) = +125.320 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Compensation to Commanded Z**

$$Z_{actual} = Z_{commanded} - H_{effective}$$

(The negative sign because positive length offset means the tool tip is further from the spindle — the machine must retract less.)

**Step 4 — Substitute and Compute**

$$Z_{actual} = -25.000 - 125.320 = -150.320 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{actual} = -150.320 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Production Insight:** The wear offset of -0.080 mm precisely compensates for the gradual shortening of the tool during its service life, ensuring the **final part dimension remains within ±0.01 mm tolerance** across hundreds of parts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tool Word Decision Flow

| Decision Point | Question | Action | Resulting Tool Word Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Which operation? | Drilling, milling, tapping? | Select tool geometry | First 2 digits (T##) |
| 2. Already loaded? | Check current spindle tool | Skip M06 if match | Conditional branch |
| 3. Coolant needed? | Flood, mist, through-spindle? | Add M08/M07 | M function in same block |
| 4. Length comp active? | New tool? | Apply G43 H## | H address |
| 5. Profile machining? | Following part contour? | Apply G41/G42 | D address |
| 6. Spindle direction? | CW (M03) or CCW (M04)? | Add to block | M function |
| 7. Safety clearance? | Above clamps/fixtures? | Add Z lift motion | Coordinate words |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Program Block Structure — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the hierarchical structure of a CNC program block, with the **Tool Word** highlighted as a critical sub-component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CNC PROGRAM BLOCK N50"] --> B["PREPARATORY FUNCTION G CODE"]
    A --> C["COORDINATE WORDS X Y Z"]
    A --> D["TOOL WORD T ADDRESS"]
    A --> E["SPINDLE SPEED S WORD"]
    A --> F["FEED RATE F WORD"]
    A --> G["MISCELLANEOUS FUNCTION M CODE"]
    D --> D1["TOOL SELECTION T 05"]
    D --> D2["TOOL CHANGE COMMAND M06"]
    D --> D3["LENGTH COMPENSATION G43 H05"]
    D --> D4["RADIUS COMPENSATION G41 G42 D05"]
    D1 --> H["MECHANICAL ACTION MAGAZINE INDEX"]
    D2 --> I["MECHANICAL ACTION ATC SWAP"]
    D3 --> J["SOFT ACTION Z AXIS FRAME OFFSET"]
    D4 --> K["SOFT ACTION XY PATH OFFSET"]
    H --> L["PHYSICAL TOOL NOW IN SPINDLE"]
    I --> L
    J --> M["CONTROLLER READY FOR CUTTING"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Tool Change Sequence — Detailed Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START TOOL CHANGE CYCLE"] --> nodeB["RECEIVE T WORD FROM PROGRAM BLOCK"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TOOL IN SPINDLE EQUALS REQUESTED TOOL"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"MATCH CHECK"}
    nodeD -- YES MATCH --> nodeE["SKIP M06 NO ATC ACTION"]
    nodeD -- NO MISMATCH --> nodeF["RETRACT SPINDLE TO CHANGE POSITION Z"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["ORIENT SPINDLE TO ATC POSITION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["UNCLAMP SPINDLE TOOL"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ATC ARM EXTENDS GRIPS SPINDLE TOOL"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ATC ARM WITHDRAWS TO MAGAZINE POCKET"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["DROPS OLD TOOL INTO MAGAZINE POCKET"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["PICK UP NEW TOOL FROM DESIRED POCKET"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["ATC ARM RETURNS TO SPINDLE"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["INSERT NEW TOOL INTO SPINDLE"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["CLAMP SPINDLE TOOL"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["REORIENT SPINDLE TO NORMAL OPERATION"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["APPLY TOOL LENGTH OFFSET G43 H REGISTER"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["TOOL CHANGE COMPLETE READY FOR CUTTING"]
    nodeE --> nodeR
```

## 4.3 Tool Offset Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["TOOL MAGAZINE POCKET T01 to T40"] --> nodeB["CONTROLLER OFFSET TABLE MEMORY"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["GEOMETRY OFFSET SECTION NON VOLATILE"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["WEAR OFFSET SECTION ADJUSTABLE"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["LENGTH Z VALUE"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["RADIUS XY VALUE"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["LENGTH WEAR Z"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["RADIUS WEAR XY"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["EFFECTIVE H REGISTER VALUE"]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ACTIVE COMPENSATION APPLIED TO MOTION BLOCKS"]
```

## 4.4 Tool Word Selection Decision Matrix — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PART DRAWING ANALYSIS"] --> nodeB["IDENTIFY FEATURES HOLES SLOTS CONTOURS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SELECT CUTTING TOOLS FROM TOOL CATALOG"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ASSIGN TOOL NUMBERS T01 T02 T03"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["MEASURE EACH TOOL ON PRESETTER"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ENTER GEOMETRY OFFSETS INTO CONTROLLER TABLE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["PERFORM FIRST ARTICLE CUT WITH ZERO WEAR OFFSET"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"DIMENSIONAL CHECK"}
    nodeH -- WITHIN TOLERANCE --> nodeI["PROCEED TO PRODUCTION"]
    nodeH -- OUT OF TOLERANCE --> nodeJ["CALCULATE CORRECTION VALUE"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["ENTER WEAR OFFSET INTO CONTROLLER"]
    nodeK --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeL["MONITOR TOOL LIFE UPDATE WEAR OFFSETS PERIODICALLY"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Q: Define a Tool Word in CNC programming. Explain its format in CNC lathes and machining centers with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Tool Word** is the portion of a CNC instruction block, designated by the address letter **T**, that commands the controller to select a specific cutting tool from the magazine and activate its associated offset register.

**Format in CNC Lathe:**
The tool word uses **4 digits** — the first two designate the tool station number, the last two designate the offset register number.

Example: `T0304` invokes **Tool Station 03** with **Offset Register 04**.

**Format in CNC Machining Center:**
The tool word uses **2 digits** for tool number only; the offset register is called separately using the **H** (length) or **D** (diameter) address in the same block.

Example: `T05 M06 G43 H05` invokes **Tool 05**, executes automatic tool change, and activates **positive length compensation from offset register 05**.

The tool word integrates tool selection and offset activation into a single instruction, ensuring dimensional accuracy and enabling multi-tool job execution without manual intervention. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]

**Q: Differentiate between tool geometry offset and tool wear offset. Why is this distinction important in production?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Geometry Offset | Wear Offset |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Stores the absolute nominal size of the tool (length from spindle nose, radius). | Stores the deviation due to gradual wear during machining. |
| **Value** | Set once per tool setup (after measuring on a tool presetter). | Adjusted periodically based on actual dimensional measurements. |
| **Range** | Large (e.g., 50–300 mm typical). | Small (typically ±0.1 mm). |
| **Adjustability** | Rarely changed. | Updated frequently during production runs. |
| **Effect on part** | Defines the baseline tool position. | Fine-tunes the baseline to compensate for wear. |

**Importance in Production:**
This separation allows operators to **re-grind, re-sharpen, or replace** a tool without disturbing its baseline geometry registration. Only the wear offset needs updating based on First Article Inspection (FAI) data. This ensures consistent part quality across long production runs and simplifies tool management in flexible manufacturing systems. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A [Internal Choice Option 1] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the format of the tool word in CNC lathes and machining centers. With neat sketches, describe **tool length compensation (G43/G44)** and **tool radius compensation (G41/G42)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example program, explain the **tool selection, tool change sequence (M06), and offset activation** in a typical CNC vertical machining center. List any **three safety interlocks** associated with tool change operations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Tool Word Format — Lathe vs. Machining Center:**

In CNC lathes, the tool word is **4 digits** because the turning center uses a **turret** with multiple stations, and each station has an independent offset register for X (diameter) and Z (length) axes.

Example: `T0203` → Tool at turret station **2**, using offset register **3**.

In CNC machining centers, the tool word is **2 digits** because the tool magazine contains many tools (up to 40+), and the offsets (length = H, diameter/radius = D) are called separately to provide more programming flexibility.

Example: `T08` → Selects **Tool 8** from the magazine.

**2. Tool Length Compensation (G43 / G44):**

Tool length compensation corrects the Z-axis coordinate so that the **programmed tool tip position** matches the **actual tool tip position** of the loaded tool. This is critical because every tool in the magazine has a different length.

- **G43** = Positive length compensation (add offset value to commanded Z).
- **G44** = Negative length compensation (subtract offset value).
- **G49** = Cancel length compensation.

**Sketch (textual representation):**

```
Spindle Nose ─────────────────┐
                              │ (Geometry Offset = 125.4 mm)
                              │
                              ●─── Tool Tip
                              ▲
                        Programmed Z = 0
                              │
                              └─ Workpiece Top Surface
```

When `G43 H05` is executed, the controller **offsets the Z-axis frame downward** by the value stored in H05, so the tool tip reaches the programmed position regardless of which tool is loaded.

**3. Tool Radius Compensation (G41 / G42):**

Used in **milling** to allow the programmer to write the **part profile** directly. The controller automatically offsets the tool center path by the cutter radius.

- **G41** = Cutter **LEFT** of programmed direction.
- **G42** = Cutter **RIGHT** of programmed direction.
- **G40** = Cancel cutter compensation.

**Sketch (textual representation):**

```
        Programmed Path (Part Contour)
        ────────────────────────────►
                  ↑
        Tool Path (G41 LEFT)
        ──────────────────────►  (offset by radius r)
```

Without G41/G42, the programmer would have to manually offset every line by the cutter radius — impractical for complex contours. **(7 marks — full credit)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Example Program — Tool Selection, Change, and Offset Activation:**

```
O0001 (PROGRAM NAME)
N10 G21 G90 G94 G54       (Metric, Absolute, Feed/min, Work offset 1)
N20 G00 X0 Y0 Z100.0      (Rapid to safe position)
N30 T01 M06               (Select Tool 1, execute ATC)
N40 S1500 M03             (Spindle CW at 1500 rpm)
N50 G43 H01 Z5.0          (Apply length offset, approach)
N60 G01 Z-2.0 F100        (Plunge into workpiece)
N70 F300                  (Cutting feed rate)
N80 X50.0                 (Linear cut)
N90 G00 Z100.0            (Retract)
N100 T02 M06              (Tool change to Tool 2)
N110 S2000 M03            (New spindle speed)
N120 G43 H02 Z5.0         (Apply new length offset)
N130 G01 Z-1.0 F80        (Plunge)
... (continued operations)
N200 M30                  (Program end)
```

**Tool Change Sequence (Step-by-Step):**

1. Machine receives `T02 M06` command.
2. Spindle moves to **tool change position** (Z-axis up, oriented).
3. **Magazine rotates** to bring Tool 2's pocket to the ATC pickup position.
4. **ATC arm extends**, grips Tool 1 (current spindle tool).
5. Arm withdraws, rotates 180°, and inserts Tool 1 into the empty magazine pocket.
6. Arm extends again, grips Tool 2 from the magazine.
7. Arm returns, inserts Tool 2 into the spindle.
8. Spindle **re-clamps** the tool.
9. Controller updates the **active tool number** display.
10. Program continues with new tool parameters.

**Three Safety Interlocks:**

1. **Spindle Orientation Interlock:** Spindle must be oriented to a specific angular position before ATC operation — prevents tool shank damage.
2. **Door/Guard Interlock:** Machine door must be closed during tool change — protects operator from moving ATC arm.
3. **Tool Number Verification:** Controller checks the **actual tool in spindle** against the **requested tool** before starting machining — prevents crash if tool change failed. **(7 marks — full credit)**

---

### QUESTION B [Internal Choice Option 2] — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **concept of tool offset registers** in CNC machines. Discuss the different types of tool offsets (geometry vs. wear; length vs. diameter/radius) with their typical storage locations in the controller memory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC end mill has **diameter D = 50 mm**, **4 cutting flutes**, recommended **cutting speed V_c = 80 m/min** (for mild steel), and **feed per tooth f_z = 0.1 mm/tooth**. Calculate:
(i) The required **spindle speed** in rpm.
(ii) The required **table feed rate** in mm/min.
(iii) The **complete tool change block** with tool word, assuming the tool is **Tool #03**, length offset **H03**, and a safe approach position **Z = 50.0 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Concept of Tool Offset Registers:**

A tool offset register is a **dedicated memory location** in the CNC controller that stores dimensional correction data for each tool in the magazine. When the tool word `T##` (and H/D address) is invoked, the controller **reads the corresponding offset register** and applies the stored values to the coordinate frame in real-time.

**Purpose:**
- Compensate for variations in tool length, diameter, and wear.
- Enable the **same part program** to run on different machines or with different tools — only offsets need updating.

**2. Types of Tool Offsets:**

| Offset Type | Stored Data | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry Length (H)** | Absolute distance from spindle nose to tool tip. | Z-axis position correction. |
| **Geometry Diameter/Radius (D)** | Nominal cutter diameter or radius. | XY path offset (G41/G42). |
| **Wear Length** | Deviation in tool length due to wear/regrinding. | Fine-tune Z position. |
| **Wear Diameter** | Deviation in cutter diameter due to flank wear. | Fine-tune XY path. |

**3. Storage Location in Controller Memory:**

Modern CNC controllers (e.g., FANUC 0i-MF) store offsets in a dedicated **offset table** with separate **Geometry** and **Wear** pages:

- **Geometry Page:** Stores the absolute tool dimensions measured on a **tool presetter** outside the machine. Set once per tool.
- **Wear Page:** Stores the **incremental corrections** based on actual machined part measurements. Updated during production.

Each tool in the magazine has its own **numbered register** (typically 01–99 in machining centers; 01–32 in lathes). The controller **algebraically sums** the geometry and wear values to produce the effective offset used during machining.

**4. Example Offset Table Entry (FANUC-style):**

```
TOOL NO. | GEOM LENGTH | GEOM DIA | WEAR LEN | WEAR DIA
   01    |   125.400   |   50.00  |  -0.020  |   0.00
   02    |    98.750   |   12.00  |   0.000  |  -0.05
   03    |   150.200   |   20.00  |  -0.080  |   0.00
```

When `T03 H03` is invoked, the controller uses the combined offset for Tool 03. **(7 marks — full credit)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 50 \text{ mm} \\
V_c &= 80 \text{ m/min} \\
f_z &= 0.1 \text{ mm/tooth} \\
z &= 4 \text{ flutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (i) — Spindle Speed Calculation:**

Using the formula:

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

Substitute values:

$$N = \frac{80 \times 1000}{\pi \times 50}$$

Compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 80 \times 1000 = 80{,}000 \\
\text{Denominator} &= \pi \times 50 = 157.0796 \\
N &= \frac{80{,}000}{157.0796} = 509.296 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 509 \text{ rpm (rounded to nearest integer)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Table Feed Rate Calculation:**

Using the formula:

$$F = f_z \times z \times N$$

Substitute values:

$$F = 0.1 \times 4 \times 509$$

Compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.1 \times 4 &= 0.4 \\
0.4 \times 509 &= 203.6 \text{ mm/min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 204 \text{ mm/min (rounded up for safety)}}$$

**Part (iii) — Complete Tool Change Block:**

```
N100 T03 M06           (Select Tool 3, execute automatic tool change)
N110 S509 M03          (Spindle CW at 509 rpm)
N120 G90 G00 X0 Y0     (Rapid to workpiece origin in XY)
N130 G43 H03 Z50.0     (Apply positive length offset H03, position to Z50.0)
N140 M08               (Coolant ON — flood coolant for steel)
```

After this block, the next motion block would specify the cutting feed:

```
N150 G01 Z-5.0 F50.0  (Plunge at 50 mm/min)
N160 F204              (Engage cutting feed rate)
N170 ... (cutting motion)
```

**Verification of Units:**
- Spindle speed in **rev/min** ✓
- Feed rate in **mm/min** ✓
- Length offset in **mm** ✓
- All values within typical range for 50 mm cutter in mild steel ✓ **(7 marks — full credit)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion in N and F Formulas:** Many students write $N = V_c / D$ without the **1000 conversion factor**, producing wrong answers off by a factor of 1000. Always remember $V_c$ is in m/min but $D$ is in mm — convert before dividing.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Tool Change Command (M06):** In CNC machining center programs, simply writing `T03` without `M06` **does not execute the tool change** — the tool number is only *registered*. Students lose 1–2 marks for omitting M06.
> 
> 3. **Sign Errors in Offset Application:** The sign convention for G43/G44 varies between controllers. Always state the assumption clearly. On FANUC controllers, G43 with a positive H value **moves the tool down** (closer to workpiece).
> 
> 4. **Mixing Up G41/G42 Direction:** Remember the mnemonic — **G41 = LEFT** (the tool is on the left side of the workpiece as you face the direction of travel). Drawing a quick sketch during the exam prevents errors.
> 
> 5. **Omitting Coolant and Spindle Direction:** A "complete" tool change block includes M03/M04 (spindle direction) and M08/M09 (coolant). Examiners award bonus marks for completeness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tool Word (Module 3)**

- **Tool Word Address:** Always the letter **T**, followed by numerical digits.
- **Lathe Format:** **4 digits** (Txx yy) → tool station + offset register.
- **Machining Center Format:** **2 digits** (Txx) for tool number; offset via **H** (length) or **D** (radius).
- **M06 = Tool Change:** Without this, the T command only *selects* but does not *execute* the physical tool change.
- **G43 = Positive Length Compensation:** Adds offset to Z command. **G44** = negative. **G49** = cancel.
- **G41 = Cutter LEFT, G42 = Cutter RIGHT, G40 = Cancel radius compensation.**
- **Two-Layer Offset Model:** Geometry (baseline, set once) + Wear (incremental, adjusted frequently) = Effective Offset.
- **Spindle Speed Formula:** $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$ — always in **rpm**.
- **Feed Rate Formula (Milling):** $F = f_z \times z \times N$ — always in **mm/min**.
- **Safety Interlocks:** Spindle orientation, door closure, tool number verification — all must be satisfied before ATC operation.
- **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267 Standard:** Defines the program block format with T word as an integral component.
- **Industry 4.0 Connection:** Modern tool words are linked to **RFID-tagged tools** and **cloud offset databases** for predictive tool management.
- **Common Exam Trap:** The tool word does **two jobs** — selection AND offset activation. Never describe only one function.

---

**End of Module 3 — Tool Word Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme PCIET402 — Machine Tools and Digital Manufacturing*
