# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — G81 Drilling Cycle on 4 Holes

**Workpiece:** A 100 mm × 100 mm × 20 mm steel plate, four Ø8 holes at corners (10, 10), (90, 10), (90, 90), (10, 90), depth 15 mm, R-plane at +2 mm above top of plate (Z=0), tool at initial point (50, 50, 100), feed 100 mm/min.

### Step 1 — Initialization (Cancel any active cycle, set work-plane)

```
G90 G54 G17 G80 G21      ; Absolute, work offset 1, XY-plane, cancel cycle, mm mode
G91 G28 Z0               ; Safe Z return
G90                       ; Back to absolute
```

### Step 2 — Position to first hole at rapid

```
G00 X10.0 Y10.0          ; Rapid to first hole center
```

### Step 3 — Activate the G81 cycle

```
G81 Z-15.0 R2.0 F100    ; Drill to Z=-15, retract to R=+2, F=100 mm/min
```

The controller now executes the **6-step motion** for the *first* hole:
1. At (X=10, Y=10), rapid down from initial Z to R-plane (Z=+2).
2. Feed down from Z=+2 to Z=−15 at 100 mm/min.
3. No dwell (G81 has no dwell).
4. Rapid retract from Z=−15 to Z=+2 (R-plane).

### Step 4 — Subsequent holes (cycle modal)

```
X90.0 Y10.0             ; Rapid to hole 2, *automatically* executes the 6-step cycle
X90.0 Y90.0             ; Hole 3
X10.0 Y90.0             ; Hole 4
```

For each of these three lines, the controller repeats the entire canned cycle *automatically* — no additional code needed.

### Step 5 — Cancel the cycle

```
G80                     ; Canned cycle cancel
G00 Z100.0              ; Rapid to safe Z
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to issue `G80` before moving the tool. The controller will think the next X-Y position is *another hole center* and plunge — potentially crashing the tool or workpiece.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough — G71/G70 Turning Cycle on a Stepped Shaft

**Workpiece:** Mild steel bar, Ø50 mm raw stock, 150 mm long.
**Finished profile (between Z=0 and Z=120):**
- Ø50 mm from Z=0 to Z=40
- Tapered reduction from Ø50 → Ø30 between Z=40 and Z=80
- Ø30 mm from Z=80 to Z=120

**Parameters chosen:**
- Depth of cut Δd = 2.0 mm (radius)
- Retract e = 1.0 mm
- Finishing allowance Δu = 0.5 mm (X / radius), Δw = 0.1 mm (Z)
- Tool: T0101 (rough + finish tool)

### Step 1 — Define the *finished profile* with sequence numbers (between P and Q)

```
N100 G00 X30.0 Z2.0        ; First profile block (P point)
N110 G01 Z0 F0.2            ; Approach face
N120 X50.0 F0.3             ; Face to Ø50
N130 Z40.0                   ; Cylindrical Ø50
N140 X80.0 Z80.0            ; Tapered reduction
N150 X90.0                   ; Cylindrical Ø30
N160 Z120.0                  ; Continue to Z=120
N170 X92.0                   ; Pull off (last profile block — Q point)
```

### Step 2 — Issue the G71 roughing command

```
G71 U2.0 R1.0
G71 P100 Q170 U0.5 W0.1 F0.25
```

### Step 3 — Controller's internal logic (what G71 does)

**Controller reasoning (Fanuc algorithm):**
- Read profile between N100–N170.
- Identify it as a **monotonic X-decreasing → X-increasing** profile (Type I, single roughing pass per side).
- Calculate number of roughing passes:
  $n = \left\lceil \dfrac{X_{\text{start}} - X_{\text{end min}}}{2 \cdot \Delta d} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{92.0 - 30.0}{2 \times 2.0} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 15.5 \right\rceil = 16 \text{ passes}$
- For each pass, plunge to the *current* X-radius, traverse along the profile path, and retract.
- Stop 0.5 mm (Δu) before the final X profile, and 0.1 mm (Δw) before final Z.

### Step 4 — Issue the G70 finishing command

```
G70 P100 Q170
```

The controller:
- Re-positions the tool to the start of the finished profile (N100).
- Traverses the entire N100–N170 path *exactly once*, removing the Δu and Δw allowances, at the final finishing feedrate.
- Returns to the start and stops.

### Step 5 — Completion

```
G00 X100.0 Z100.0          ; Retract
M30                         ; Program end / rewind
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough — PLC Ladder Logic: Motor Start-Stop with Seal-In

This is the *classic* "three-wire motor control" ladder — appears in **almost every KTU PLC question**.

**Problem:** A 3-phase motor (M) must be started by pressing the **Start pushbutton (PB1)** and stopped by pressing the **Stop pushbutton (PB2)**. An overload relay contact (OL) must also stop the motor on fault.

### Step 1 — Identify Inputs and Outputs

| Address | Device | Type |
|:-------:|:-------|:-----|
| **I:0/0** | Stop Pushbutton (PB2) — *NC* | Input |
| **I:0/1** | Start Pushbutton (PB1) — *NO* | Input |
| **I:0/2** | Overload Relay (OL) — *NC* | Input |
| **O:0/0** | Motor Contactor (M) coil | Output |

### Step 2 — Ladder Rung (in ASCII representation)

```
      I:0/0        I:0/2         I:0/1         O:0/0          O:0/0
      (NC)         (NC)          (NO)          (NO contact
       |            |             |             in parallel)        |
     Stop          OL          Start          Seal-in              |
     ─┤/├──────────┤/├────────────┤ ├──────────┤ ├─────────────────┤ ├──────( M )──────
                                                                              O:0/0
```

### Step 3 — Logic Analysis Step-by-Step

**Initial state (no buttons pressed):**
- PB1 (NO) = open, so I:0/1 contact is **open** in the rung.
- The rung evaluates as: TRUE AND TRUE AND FALSE AND FALSE = **FALSE**.
- Output O:0/0 (motor) is **OFF**.

**Operator presses Start (PB1):**
- I:0/1 contact **closes** for the duration of the press.
- Rung: TRUE AND TRUE AND TRUE AND FALSE = **TRUE**.
- Output O:0/0 energizes → motor runs.
- Simultaneously, the *seal-in* contact (a NO contact of O:0/0 wired in parallel with PB1) **closes** — latching the rung TRUE even after PB1 is released.

**Operator releases PB1:**
- I:0/1 reopens.
- But the *seal-in* contact (O:0/0 in parallel) is still closed.
- Rung remains TRUE. Motor keeps running. ✅ Seal-in works.

**Operator presses Stop (PB2):**
- I:0/0 (NC) opens → rung breaks.
- O:0/0 de-energizes → motor stops, seal-in contact reopens.
- Rung is now FALSE; releasing Stop does not restart the motor. ✅

**Overload trip:**
- I:0/2 (NC) opens → rung breaks → motor stops, same as Stop. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the canonical "three-wire" control circuit.** It is implemented in ladder logic today, but historically it was built with physical relays. The PLC program *is* the wire diagram.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough — TON (On-Delay Timer) for Conveyor Lubrication

**Problem:** Every time a conveyor starts, a lubrication pump should turn ON **5 seconds after** the conveyor motor energizes, and turn OFF immediately when the conveyor stops.

### Step 1 — Ladder Rung

```
      I:0/0       I:0/1                          T4:1
     Conveyor    Lub.            T4:1          (EN) (DN)        O:0/1
     Motor       Pump            (DN)            |                |
       |         (output)         |            (Preset          Pump
       |           |              |             = 5.0 s)         output
     ─┤ ├──────────┤ ├────────────┤├─────[TON T4:1 Time Base = 1.0 s Preset = 5 ]──( O:0/1 )──
                  ↑              ↑
                Seal-in        Output
                of T4:1/DN     unlatch
                (T4:1/DN)      for pump
```

### Step 2 — Timing Trace Table

| Scan # | I:0/0 (Conv) | T4:1.EN | T4:1.TT | T4:1.DN | T4:1.ET (s) | O:0/1 (Pump) |
|:------:|:------------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-----------:|:------------:|
| 1 | 1 (just ON) | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0.00 | 0 |
| 50 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0.05 | 0 |
| 500 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0.50 | 0 |
| 5000 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 5.00 → 5.0 | 0 |
| 5001 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 5.00 (clamped) | 1 ✅ |
| ... | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 5.00 | 1 |
| 6000 | 0 (Conv OFF) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.00 (reset) | 0 ✅ |

### Step 3 — Logic Reasoning

- **T4:1.EN** (Enable) = TRUE while I:0/0 is TRUE.
- **T4:1.TT** (Timing) = TRUE while ET < PT.
- **T4:1.DN** (Done) = TRUE once ET ≥ PT.
- The pump output is wired directly to the DN bit, so the pump turns ON **only after** 5 s of conveyor run.
- The moment the conveyor stops (I:0/0 = 0), the timer resets and the pump turns OFF immediately.

$$
t_{\text{pump ON}} = t_{\text{conv ON}} + PT = t_{\text{conv ON}} + 5.0 \text{ s}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Walkthrough — TOF (Off-Delay Timer) for Cooling Fan

**Problem:** A cooling fan must keep running for **30 seconds after** a heater turns OFF, to dissipate residual heat.

```
      I:0/0
     Heater             T4:2
     ON                 (EN) (DN)        O:0/2
       |                  |                |
       |                  |               Fan
     ─┤ ├─────────────[TOF T4:2 Time Base = 1.0 s Preset = 30 ]──( O:0/2 )──
```

**Timing trace:**

| Heater (I:0/0) | T4:2.EN | T4:2.TT | T4:2.DN | Fan (O:0/2) |
|:--------------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| 1 (ON) | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 (running) |
| 0 (just OFF) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 (still running) |
| 0 (5 s after) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 (still running) |
| 0 (29 s after) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 (still running) |
| 0 (30 s after) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✅ (stops) |

$$
t_{\text{fan OFF}} = t_{\text{heater OFF}} + PT = t_{\text{heater OFF}} + 30 \text{ s}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canned Cycle — Six-Step Motion Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Canned Cycle G81 Activated] --> B[Step 1: Initial Point to Hole X,Y]
    B --> C[Step 2: Rapid Plunge to R-Plane]
    C --> D[Step 3: Feed Plunge to Z-Depth]
    D --> E{Dwell Required?}
    E -- Yes, G82 / G89 --> F[Step 4: Dwell at Bottom]
    E -- No, G81 / G85 --> G[Step 5: Retract]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Retract Type?}
    H -- Rapid G81, G82, G83 --> I[Rapid Retract to R-Plane]
    H -- Feed G85, G89 --> J[Feed Retract to R-Plane]
    H -- Manual G88 --> K[Manual Retract by Operator]
    I --> L[Step 6: Rapid to Initial Point]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{More Holes in Program?}
    M -- Yes, Next X,Y --> C
    M -- No --> N[G80 Canned Cycle Cancel]
    N --> O[End: Cycle Terminated]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 G71/G70 Roughing + Finishing Cycle Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer defines FINISHED profile N100 to N170] --> B[Issue G71 with depth d, retract e, allow. u,w]
    B --> C[Controller calculates number of passes n]
    C --> D[Pass 1: Plunge d, traverse profile, retract e]
    D --> E{All passes done?}
    E -- No --> F[Increment current X-radius by d] --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Leave finishing allowance u in X and w in Z]
    G --> H[Issue G70 P100 Q170]
    H --> I[Re-trace full profile at finishing feed, removing u and w]
    I --> J[Part complete, retract tool, M30]
    J --> K[End of Program]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 PLC Modular Hardware Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field[FIELD DEVICES]
        SENS[Pushbuttons, Limit Switches, Proximity Sensors, Thermocouples]
        ACTU[Solenoid Valves, Motor Contactors, Indicator Lamps, VFDs]
    end

    subgraph INPUT[INPUT MODULES]
        DI[Digital Input: 24 V DC / 110 V AC]
        AI[Analog Input: 4-20 mA / 0-10 V / RTD]
    end

    subgraph CPU[CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT]
        MIC[Microprocessor]
        MEM[Memory: ROM + RAM + EEPROM]
        SCAN[Scan Logic: Fetch / Decode / Execute]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT MODULES]
        DO[Digital Output: Relay / Transistor / Triac]
        AO[Analog Output: 4-20 mA / 0-10 V]
    end

    subgraph PGM[PROGRAMMING DEVICE]
        PC[PC / HMI with Ladder Editor]
    end

    subgraph POWER[POWER SUPPLY]
        PS[110/230 V AC to +5 V, +15 V, -15 V, +24 V DC]
    end

    SENS --> DI
    SENS --> AI
    DI --> SCAN
    AI --> SCAN
    SCAN --> MIC
    MIC <--> MEM
    PC <-->|Upload/Download/Online Edit| MIC
    PS -.->|DC power| DI
    PS -.->|DC power| AI
    PS -.->|DC power| CPU
    PS -.->|DC power| DO
    PS -.->|DC power| AO
    SCAN --> DO
    SCAN --> AO
    DO --> ACTU
    AO --> ACTU

    style CPU fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style POWER fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PGM fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 PLC Cyclic Scan — Four-Step Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Scan Cycle] --> B[STEP 1: Read all physical INPUTS into Process Image]
    B --> C[STEP 2: Execute Ladder Program Top-to-Bottom, Left-to-Right]
    C --> D[STEP 3: Write all OUTPUTS from Process Image to physical terminals]
    D --> E[STEP 4: Housekeeping - Diagnostics, Comm, Timer/Counter updates]
    E --> F{Total scan time within limit?}
    F -- Yes --> B
    F -- No --> G[Trigger Watchdog Error / Fault]
    G --> H[Outputs go to SAFE State - typically OFF]
    H --> I[Operator Intervention Required]
    I --> B

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Three-Wire Motor Control Ladder Logic (Seal-In Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RUNG1[RUNG 1: MOTOR START-STOP-SEAL]
        L1[L+ Power Rail] --- N1[Stop PB2: NC contact I:0/0]
        N1 --- N2[Overload OL: NC contact I:0/2]
        N2 --- N3[Start PB1: NO contact I:0/1]
        N3 --- N4[Motor Seal-In: NO contact O:0/0]
        N4 --- COIL1[( Motor Contactor O:0/0 )]
        COIL1 --- L2[L- Neutral Rail]
    end

    subgraph RUNG2[RUNG 2: STATUS INDICATOR]
        L1b[L+] --- N1b[Motor Contact: NO contact O:0/0]
        N1b --- COIL2[( Green Run Lamp O:0/1 )]
        COIL2 --- L2b[L-]
    end

    style RUNG1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style RUNG2 fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style COIL1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style COIL2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.6 TON (On-Delay Timer) — Timing Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input goes TRUE] --> B[Timer EN bit = 1]
    B --> C[Internal accumulator ET starts incrementing by Time Base]
    C --> D{ET less than PT?}
    D -- Yes --> E[TT bit = 1, DN bit = 0, Output = 0]
    E --> F[Continue counting]
    F --> D
    D -- No, ET >= PT --> G[TT bit = 0, DN bit = 1, Output = 1]
    G --> H{Input still TRUE?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Hold DN = 1, Output = 1]
    H -- No --> J[Reset: EN = 0, ET = 0, DN = 0, Output = 0]
    J --> K[Timer ready for next cycle]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.7 Functional Block Diagram — Canned Cycle + PLC Integration in a CNC Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CNC[CNC MACHINE]
        CNC_CU[CNC Controller]
        CYC[Canned Cycle Library G81 to G89, G70 to G76]
        SERVO[Servo Drives and Motors]
    end

    subgraph PLC[PLC SYSTEM]
        PLC_CPU[PLC CPU with Ladder Program]
        PLC_IN[Digital and Analog Input Modules]
        PLC_OUT[Digital Output Modules]
    end

    subgraph IO[FIELD I/O]
        PB[Operator Pushbuttons, Selector Switches]
        LS[Limit Switches, Proximity Sensors, Coolant Level]
        SOL[Solenoid Valves, Coolant Pump, Chip Conveyor Motor]
        LMP[Tower Lamp Red Yellow Green, HMI]
    end

    CNC_CU --> CYC
    CNC_CU <-->|M-codes, M-fin, Interlocks| PLC_CPU
    CYC --> SERVO
    PB --> PLC_IN
    LS --> PLC_IN
    PLC_CPU --> PLC_OUT
    PLC_OUT --> SOL
    PLC_OUT --> LMP
    PLC_CPU -.->|Status feedback to HMI| LMP

    style CNC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PLC fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style IO fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define a "Canned Cycle" in CNC programming. List any FOUR canned cycles with their typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Canned Cycle** is a pre-programmed, ready-to-use sequence of machine tool operations (such as rapid positioning, feed plunge, dwell, and retract) stored permanently in the CNC controller's firmware, invoked by a single G-code instruction to perform a common machining task.

Four canned cycles (1/2 mark each):

| G-Code | Application |
|:------:|:------------|
| **G81** | Simple shallow drilling (no dwell). |
| **G82** | Spot-facing or counterboring (with dwell for flat bottom). |
| **G83** | Deep-hole drilling using full retract pecking for chip evacuation. |
| **G85** | Boring cycle with feed-in and feed-out for accurate finish holes. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Definition 1 mark; listing 4 cycles with correct applications 0.5 mark each = 2 marks. Total 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the difference between TON and TOF timers in a PLC with a neat timing diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **TON (On-Delay)** | **TOF (Off-Delay)** |
|:-------:|:------------------:|:-------------------:|
| **Activation** | Output goes ON **after** a time delay *once* the input becomes TRUE. | Output goes OFF **after** a time delay *once* the input becomes FALSE. |
| **Use case** | Delayed motor start, lubrication pump trigger. | Cooling fan run-down, lamp fade-out. |
| **Reset behaviour** | Resets immediately when input goes FALSE. | Maintains output TRUE during timing; resets only after timing completes. |

**Timing diagrams (text representation):**

```
TON:  Input :  ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____
      Output: ________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾  (rises after delay PT)

TOF:  Input :  ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____
      Output: ________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾  (falls after delay PT)
```

(1 mark for tabular comparison + 1 mark for each correct waveform = 3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has an *internal choice* (Question A OR Question B). The two alternatives cover similar cognitive levels but test different sub-topics. Each has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the G71 rough turning cycle and the G70 finishing cycle used in CNC lathe programming. State any four parameters of G71.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**G71 Rough Turning Cycle (3.5 marks):**

The G71 cycle is a **Multiple Repetitive Cycle** used for rough-machining a rotationally symmetric profile on a CNC lathe. The programmer writes the *finished contour* in the program as a sequence of blocks bounded by sequence numbers **P** (start) and **Q** (end), and the controller automatically computes the roughing passes.

**Working principle:**
- The controller reads the final profile (P→Q).
- It plunges the tool to a depth `Δd` (radial).
- It traverses the tool along the profile path.
- It retracts the tool by `e` mm.
- This process repeats **automatically** for `n` passes until the final profile minus the finishing allowance `Δu` (X), `Δw` (Z) is reached.

**General format:**
```
G71 U(Δd) R(e)
G71 P(ns) Q(nf) U(Δu) W(Δw) F__
```

**Four key parameters (1.5 marks):**
1. **U(Δd)** — Depth of cut per pass (radius value, incremental).
2. **R(e)** — Retract amount between passes.
3. **P(ns)** — Sequence number of first profile block.
4. **Q(nf)** — Sequence number of last profile block.

**G70 Finishing Cycle (3.5 marks):**

After G71 roughing leaves Δu and Δw material, **G70** performs a single precise finishing pass along the exact finished profile to clean the surface and meet final dimensional tolerances.

**General format:**
```
G70 P(ns) Q(nf)
```

**Working principle:**
- Tool rapidly repositions to the start of the profile (N ns).
- Traverses the entire N ns → N nf path at the final finishing feedrate.
- Removes the Δu and Δw material allowances.
- Returns and stops.

A labelled diagram showing the G71 roughing passes vs. G70 finishing pass is mandatory for full marks (refer to Section 4.2 mermaid diagram of this note).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the need for a PLC in industrial automation. Describe the PLC scan cycle with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Need for PLC (3.5 marks):**

Before PLCs, industrial control used **hard-wired relay logic panels** containing hundreds of electromechanical relays. Each control change required **physical rewiring** by electricians, taking days of downtime. PLCs solved this by:
1. **Software-based logic** — No rewiring; logic is programmed, not wired.
2. **Compact & rugged** — Replaces hundreds of relays with one small unit.
3. **Easy troubleshooting** — Online monitoring, diagnostics, fault logs.
4. **High reliability** — Solid-state, no moving parts (CPU), Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) > 100,000 hours.
5. **Flexible & modular** — I/O modules can be added/removed easily.
6. **Communication** — Ethernet, Profibus, Modbus for Industry 4.0 integration.

**PLC Scan Cycle (3.5 marks):**

A PLC executes a strict 4-step cyclic scan:

| Step | Function |
|:----:|:---------|
| **1. Input Scan** | Reads all physical input states into the Input Process Image (PII). |
| **2. Program Scan** | Executes ladder program top-to-bottom, left-to-right, using PII and updating the Output Process Image (POI). |
| **3. Output Scan** | Copies POI to physical output terminals. |
| **4. Housekeeping** | Diagnostics, communication, timer/counter updates. |

Total scan time = T_in + T_program + T_out ≈ 5–20 ms. (Refer to Section 4.4 mermaid diagram.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Draw and explain the ladder logic program for a 3-phase motor start-stop control with overload protection using a seal-in contact. Use standard PLC address notation.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Ladder Diagram (3 marks):**

```
RUNG 1:
  L+ ─┤/├──┤/├──┤ ├─────┬──( M )── L-
       │   │   │       │
      PB2  OL  PB1     │
       │   │   │       │
       │   │   │      M (Aux NO contact of M coil)
       │   │   │       │
       └───────────────┘  (Seal-in parallel branch)
```

Where:
- `PB2` (I:0/0) = **Stop** pushbutton (NC contact).
- `OL` (I:0/2) = **Overload relay** (NC contact).
- `PB1` (I:0/1) = **Start** pushbutton (NO contact).
- `M` (O:0/0) = **Motor contactor** output coil.
- The branch in parallel with PB1 is the **seal-in** contact (a NO contact of M wired in parallel with PB1).

**Operating Logic (4 marks):**

| Action | PB1 | PB2 | OL | M (Seal-in) | Motor |
|:-------|:---:|:---:|:--:|:-----------:|:-----:|
| **Idle** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | OFF |
| **Press Start** | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | ON (rung TRUE) |
| **Release Start** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 (latched) | ON (sealed) |
| **Press Stop** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 (broken) | OFF |
| **Overload Trips** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 (broken) | OFF |

**Why the seal-in is needed:** Without it, the motor would only run as long as the operator physically holds the Start button. The seal-in contact latches the rung TRUE after the button is released, providing **memory** of the start command.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain with a neat ladder diagram how a TON (On-Delay Timer) is used to start a lubrication pump 5 seconds after a conveyor motor starts. Draw the timing diagram.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Problem Statement:** A conveyor motor (input I:0/0) drives a conveyor. A lubrication pump (output O:0/1) must turn ON **5 seconds after** the conveyor motor starts, and OFF immediately when the conveyor stops.

**Ladder Diagram (3 marks):**

```
RUNG 1:
  I:0/0 ─────────────┤ ├─────────────────[TON T4:1, Time Base = 1.0 s, Preset = 5]──( O:0/1 )──
  Conveyor                                                   ↑                       Pump
  Motor                                                T4:1/DN
                                                       (Done bit)
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**

- When the conveyor starts, I:0/0 becomes TRUE.
- The TON timer T4:1 begins counting (ET increments by 1.0 s every scan).
- During counting, T4:1/DN (Done bit) = 0; pump O:0/1 is OFF.
- After **5 seconds** (ET ≥ PT = 5), the Done bit goes HIGH.
- The pump output O:0/1 energizes, turning ON the lubrication pump.
- When the conveyor stops (I:0/0 = 0), the timer resets instantly (ET = 0, DN = 0), and the pump turns OFF immediately.

**Timing Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Conveyor Motor : ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____
                              |<-- 5 seconds -->|
Pump Output    : ____________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|____
                              ↑                 ↑
                         T4:1 starts         T4:1/DN = 1
                         ET = 0              ET = 5 s
```

**Key Equation:**
$$
t_{\text{pump ON}} = t_{\text{conveyor ON}} + T_{\text{Preset}} = t_{\text{conveyor ON}} + 5 \text{ s}
$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting G80** after a canned cycle — examiner deducts **1–2 marks** as the next tool move would treat the new X-Y as a hole and crash. Always end a canned cycle section with `G80`.
> 2. **Confusing G83 (full-retract peck) with G73 (short-retract peck)** — G83 is for *deep* holes, G73 is for *high-speed shallow* chip-breaking. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify U(Δu) and W(Δw) in G71** — without these, the roughing cycle will cut right up to the finished profile, leaving NO material for G70. Examiner deducts full 3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Δd (radius) vs diameter** in G71 — G71's U(Δd) parameter is **radius value** in Fanuc, **diameter value** in Siemens. State the controller explicitly. Examiner deducts 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 5. **Drawing ladder logic with wrong symbols** — A NO contact is `―| |―` (no slash); NC is `―|/|―` (with slash). Inverted symbols lose 1 mark each.
> 6. **Forgetting the seal-in contact** in motor start-stop — the motor would only run while Start is held. Examiner deducts **2 marks** for missing latching logic.
> 7. **Mixing up TON and TOF** — TON delays *turning ON*, TOF delays *turning OFF*. Examiner explicitly tests this; wrong identification costs 1–2 marks.
> 8. **Not stating the Time Base** of the timer — Preset is meaningless without it (e.g., Preset = 5 with Time Base 1.0 s ≠ Preset = 5 with Time Base 0.01 s). Deduct 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Canned Cycles, MRCs, and PLCs)**

### Canned Cycles (G81–G89)
- [ ] Canned cycle = one G-code → 6-step motion sequence.
- [ ] **G80** is the cycle CANCEL — always end canned-cycle sections with G80.
- [ ] Format: `G81 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__`.
- [ ] **G81** = simple drill, **G82** = drill with dwell, **G83** = deep-hole peck, **G84** = tapping, **G85** = boring, **G86** = boring w/ spindle stop, **G87** = back bore, **G88** = manual retract, **G89** = boring w/ dwell.
- [ ] Q = peck depth (G83); R = clearance plane; Z = final depth; F = feed.
- [ ] Once active, the cycle repeats for every subsequent X-Y move until G80.

### Multiple Repetitive Cycles (G70–G76, Lathe)
- [ ] G71 = OD/ID rough turning (longitudinal).
- [ ] G72 = face rough turning (transverse).
- [ ] G73 = pattern repeating (for forged/cast blanks).
- [ ] G74 = face grooving / peck drilling.
- [ ] G75 = OD grooving / peck turning.
- [ ] G76 = thread cutting cycle.
- [ ] G70 = finishing cycle — always paired with G71/G72/G73.
- [ ] G71 format: `G71 U(Δd) R(e) / G71 P(ns) Q(nf) U(Δu) W(Δw) F__`.
- [ ] Δu and Δw = finishing allowances; G70 removes them.

### PLC Fundamentals
- [ ] PLC = ruggedized industrial computer replacing relay panels.
- [ ] Components: Power Supply, CPU, Memory, Input Modules, Output Modules, Programming Device.
- [ ] Modular, solid-state, designed for harsh environments (heat, dust, vibration, electrical noise).
- [ ] **Scan Cycle = Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping**.
- [ ] Scan time = 5–20 ms typical; limits max switching frequency.

### Relays
- [ ] Relay = electromagnetic switch: small coil current → large contact switching.
- [ ] **NO** = Normally Open (closes when energized), **NC** = Normally Closed (opens when energized), **CO** = Changeover (SPDT).
- [ ] Provides electrical isolation and power amplification.
- [ ] Types: Latching, Time-Delay, Reed, Solid-State Relay (SSR).

### Ladder Logic
- [ ] Two vertical **rails** (L+ and L−), horizontal **rungs** = logic circuits.
- [ ] PLC scans top-to-bottom, left-to-right.
- [ ] **NO contact `―| |―`** = XIC (Examine If Closed) — contact is closed when the bit is ON.
- [ ] **NC contact `―|/|―`** = XIO (Examine If Open) — contact is closed when the bit is OFF.
- [ ] **OTE `―( )―`** = output coil, **OTL/OTU** = latch/unlatch.
- [ ] **Seal-in (latching) contact** = NO contact of an output wired in parallel with its start input — provides memory of the start command.

### Timers
- [ ] **TON** = On-Delay — output delayed in turning **ON** by Preset time.
- [ ] **TOF** = Off-Delay — output delayed in turning **OFF** by Preset time.
- [ ] **TP** = Pulse — produces fixed-width pulse of Preset time, regardless of input duration.
- [ ] **TONR** = Retentive On-Delay — retains ET across power loss.
- [ ] Timer parameters: **EN** (Enable), **TT** (Timing), **DN** (Done), **ET** (Elapsed), **PT** (Preset), **Time Base** (resolution).
- [ ] Always state **Time Base + Preset** together (e.g., Time Base = 1.0 s, Preset = 5 ⇒ 5 s delay).

### Top Equations to Memorize
- [ ] Number of pecks: $n = \lceil (Z_{\text{total}} - Q) / Q \rceil$.
- [ ] TON activation: $t_{\text{out ON}} = t_{\text{in ON}} + PT$.
- [ ] TOF deactivation: $t_{\text{out OFF}} = t_{\text{in OFF}} + PT$.
- [ ] Spindle speed: $N = (1000 \cdot v_c) / (\pi \cdot D)$.
- [ ] Feedrate: $F = N \cdot f \cdot z$.

> **Final KTU Tip:** In the 14-mark Part B questions, *always* draw a labelled diagram (mermaid-style or ASCII art) — examiners allocate **2–3 marks** purely for a *correct, well-labelled* sketch. A well-organized answer script with **bold keywords, tables, and clear sub-headings** typically scores 8–10% higher than a text-only answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Problems Only
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Problems Only

# Module 3 — Simple Problems: Numerical Foundation for Machining Calculations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Does "Simple Problems" Mean in Module 3?

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PCIET402 — Machine Tools and Digital Manufacturing**, Module 3 typically deals with **gear manufacturing, thread cutting, indexing mechanisms, and milling/cutting kinematics**. The phrase *"Simple Problems Only"* is a study directive: it instructs the learner to master **direct numerical (one-step) calculation problems** that appear in Part A (3-mark) and as sub-parts of Part B (14-mark) university examinations.

These problems do **not** require complex derivations. They test the student's ability to:
- Apply a single formula correctly.
- Substitute known values with correct units.
- Arrive at a numerical answer with units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Context):**
> *Simple problems* refer to single-formula numericals involving **indexing calculations, gear-tooth geometry, cutting speed/feed, machining time, and thread geometry** that are directly solvable without multi-stage analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Baker's Recipe Analogy:**

Imagine a baker wants to bake exactly **40 cookies**. The recipe says "1 batch = 8 cookies, bake for 12 minutes." To find the total time, the baker uses **one simple formula**:

$$\text{Total Time} = \left(\frac{\text{Required Cookies}}{\text{Cookies per Batch}}\right) \times \text{Time per Batch}$$

$$\text{Total Time} = \left(\frac{40}{8}\right) \times 12 = 60 \text{ minutes}$$

A **simple machining problem** works the same way. For example, "Find the spindle speed for a 50 mm diameter mild steel workpiece at 30 m/min cutting speed." Just one formula:

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$

Plug in, solve, done. The **intuition** is: *you are choosing the right recipe (formula) and the right ingredient (variable) for the question asked.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** All cutting speed answers must be in **m/min**, spindle speed in **rpm**, feed in **mm/rev** or **mm/min**, and machining time in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|----------|----------------------|
| Cutting Speed ($V$) | m/min |
| Spindle Speed ($N$) | rpm (revolutions per minute) |
| Feed ($f$) | mm/rev or mm/min |
| Depth of Cut ($d$) | mm |
| Workpiece Diameter ($D$) | mm |
| Tool Diameter ($D_t$) | mm |
| Number of Teeth ($T$ or $Z$) | integer |
| Module ($m$) | mm |
| Pitch Circle Diameter ($PCD$ or $D_p$) | mm |
| Circular Pitch ($p_c$) | mm |
| Lead ($L$) | mm |
| Pitch ($P$) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **module $m$** and **circular pitch $p_c$** are *alternate* ways of specifying gear tooth size. Their relationship is fixed: **$p_c = \pi \times m$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Module 3 "Simple Problems"

Module 3 problems always fall into one (or a combination) of these five categories. Mastering these five pillars guarantees you can solve any simple problem the examiner throws at you.

**Pillar 1 — Cutting Parameters (Speeds & Feeds)**

- These relate *how fast* and *how deep* the tool cuts.
- The three primary parameters are: Cutting Speed $V$, Spindle Speed $N$, and Feed $f$.
- The fundamental link between them is the **rotational motion of the workpiece (turning) or tool (drilling/milling)**.

**Pillar 2 — Machining Time Calculations**

- Once you know $N$ and $f$, finding the time to cut a known length is a direct application.
- The formula differs between turning (rotating workpiece) and milling/drilling (rotating tool), but the underlying logic is identical: **time = length ÷ feed rate**.

**Pillar 3 — Gear Geometry**

- The **module $m$** is the most important parameter — once $m$ is known, the gear's pitch diameter, circular pitch, and addendum/dedendum are all determined.
- Tooth geometry problems are essentially substitution into the standard gear formulas.

**Pillar 4 — Indexing Calculations**

- Used when cutting teeth on a milling machine using a **Dividing Head (Index Head)**.
- Simple indexing uses a single ratio: **turn the crank $N$ turns for $T$ teeth** using a standard index plate.
- The governing relation: **$N = \frac{40}{T}$** for a Brown & Sharpe dividing head with 40:1 worm gear ratio.

**Pillar 5 — Thread Cutting Geometry**

- Threads are specified by **pitch** (metric) or **TPI** (imperial).
- The lead $L$ of a single-start thread equals the pitch $P$.
- For multi-start threads: **$L = n \times P$**, where $n$ is the number of starts.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Logic Behind Each Formula

**Cutting Speed Formula:**
The cutting speed is the *surface velocity* at which the tool engages the material. Since velocity = circumference × rotational speed:

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad \text{(in m/min, when } D \text{ in mm and } N \text{ in rpm)}$$

The factor **1000** converts mm/min to m/min.

**Machining Time Formula:**
In turning, the feed is along the workpiece axis. In one revolution, the tool advances by $f$ mm. To traverse a length $L$, it takes $L/f$ revolutions. Time = revolutions ÷ rpm:

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} \quad \text{(in minutes)}$$

For milling/drilling, the feed per tooth $f_z$ must first be converted to feed per minute:
$$f_m = f_z \times Z \times N \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_m = \frac{L}{f_m}$$

**Gear Geometry:**
The module $m$ is defined as the *pitch diameter divided by the number of teeth*. This single definition cascades into all gear formulas.

**Indexing:**
A dividing head has a worm gear ratio of 40:1, meaning **40 turns of the crank = 1 turn of the workpiece**. To cut $T$ teeth, you need to space them evenly over one full rotation, so each tooth requires $1/T$ of a rotation, which equals $40/T$ crank turns.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Cutting Speed (Turning) | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | $V$ in m/min, $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle RPM | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | Rearrangement of (1) |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_r = f \times N$ | $f_r$ in mm/min, $f$ in mm/rev |
| 4 | Machining Time (Turning) | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | $L$ in mm, result in min |
| 5 | Number of Passes | $n_p = \dfrac{\text{Total Depth}}{d}$ | $d$ = depth of cut per pass |
| 6 | Module (Gear) | $m = \dfrac{D_p}{T}$ | $D_p$ = pitch diameter, $T$ = teeth |
| 7 | Pitch Diameter | $D_p = m \times T$ | Rearrangement of (6) |
| 8 | Circular Pitch | $p_c = \pi \times m$ | Always in mm |
| 9 | Addendum | $a = m$ | Standard full-depth gear |
| 10 | Dedendum | $b = 1.157 \times m$ | Standard full-depth gear (includes clearance) |
| 11 | Whole Depth | $h = 2.157 \times m$ | Sum of addendum + dedendum |
| 12 | Tooth Space / Tooth Thickness | $\dfrac{p_c}{2}$ | Each is half of circular pitch |
| 13 | Simple Indexing | $N = \dfrac{40}{T}$ | 40 = worm gear ratio (B&S head) |
| 14 | Compound Indexing | Uses two gears in series | Required when $40/T$ has no exact hole |
| 15 | Thread Lead | $L = n \times P$ | $n$ = number of starts, $P$ = pitch |
| 16 | Thread Pitch (Metric) | $P = \dfrac{1}{TPI}$ | For inch-based threads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always keep **units consistent**. If $D$ is in mm and $V$ in m/min, the formula needs the **÷1000** factor. If $D$ is in m, no factor is needed.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These five pillars are the daily bread of a **manufacturing engineer**:
- A **CNC programmer** calculates spindle speeds and feeds from material data sheets.
- A **gear designer** chooses the module based on load-carrying capacity.
- A **tool designer** specifies index plates to ensure accurate tooth spacing.
- A **production planner** estimates cycle time using the machining time formula.
- A **quality inspector** verifies thread pitch using the lead formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed Formula

**Starting Concept:** Cutting speed $V$ is the linear velocity at which the tool tip sweeps past the workpiece surface (in turning).

**Step 1 — Express Velocity Geometrically.**
When a cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the *surface point* traces a circle whose circumference is $\pi D$ (in mm, since $D$ is in mm). Per minute, the point sweeps this circumference $N$ times.

$$\text{Linear velocity (mm/min)} = \pi D N$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Standard Unit (m/min).**
Since 1000 mm = 1 m, we divide by 1000 to express velocity in m/min:

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$.**
Multiply both sides by 1000:

$$1000 V = \pi D N$$

Divide both sides by $\pi D$:

$$N = \frac{1000 V}{\pi D} \text{ rpm}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The factor 1000 only exists because we are mixing mm-based diameters with m/min-based cutting speeds. The $\pi$ comes from the circular geometry of rotation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Simple Indexing Formula

**Starting Concept:** A **Brown & Sharpe Dividing Head** has an internal worm-and-wheel gear with **40 teeth** on the wheel and **1 thread** on the worm. Therefore:

$$\text{1 crank turn} = \frac{1}{40} \text{ spindle turn}$$

**Step 1 — Spindle Turn Required per Tooth.**
For a gear with $T$ teeth, the spindle must rotate by exactly $1/T$ of a full revolution between consecutive cuts (so the teeth are equally spaced).

$$\text{Spindle turn per tooth} = \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Spindle Turn to Crank Turn.**
Using the worm ratio (40 crank turns = 1 spindle turn):

$$\text{Crank turn per tooth} = 40 \times \frac{1}{T}$$

$$N = \frac{40}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Real-World Note.**
The index plate provides fixed-hole circles (e.g., 15, 16, 17, ..., 21, 23, ..., 47). The computed $N$ must land *exactly* on a hole circle for **simple indexing** to work. If not, **compound indexing** is needed (not in scope of "simple problems").

> *Conversion logic:* This is a pure ratio problem — converting "spindle rotations needed" into "crank rotations required" via the worm gear ratio of 40:1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Machining Time in Turning

**Starting Concept:** In turning, the tool moves along the workpiece axis at a feed rate of $f$ mm per *spindle revolution*.

**Step 1 — Revolutions Needed.**
To cut a length $L$ mm, the tool must complete $L/f$ revolutions.

$$\text{Revolutions} = \frac{L}{f}$$

**Step 2 — Time Per Revolution.**
Spindle rotates at $N$ rpm. Time per revolution = $1/N$ minutes.

$$\text{Time per rev} = \frac{1}{N} \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Total Machining Time.**

$$T_m = \text{Revolutions} \times \text{Time per rev}$$

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f} \times \frac{1}{N} = \frac{L}{f \times N} \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Include Depth of Cut Passes.**
If the total radial depth $D_{total}$ must be removed in passes of depth $d$:

$$\text{Number of passes} = \frac{D_{total}}{d}$$

$$\text{Total time} = n_p \times \frac{L}{f \times N}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The formula divides "what must be accomplished" by "rate of accomplishment" — the most fundamental dimensional-analysis pattern in manufacturing math.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gear Geometry Cascade

**Starting Concept:** The **module** $m$ is the internationally standardized tooth-size parameter.

**Step 1 — Definition of Module.**

$$m = \frac{D_p}{T}$$

where $D_p$ = pitch circle diameter (mm), $T$ = number of teeth.

**Step 2 — Pitch Circle Diameter (Rearranged).**

$$D_p = m \times T$$

**Step 3 — Circular Pitch.**
Circular pitch is the arc length along the pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth. The pitch circle has circumference $\pi D_p$, and there are $T$ such arcs:

$$p_c = \frac{\pi D_p}{T} = \frac{\pi (m T)}{T} = \pi m$$

**Step 4 — Standard Tooth Proportions (Full-Depth, Stub omitted).**

$$\text{Addendum } a = m$$

$$\text{Dedendum } b = 1.157 \times m$$

$$\text{Whole depth } h = a + b = 2.157 \times m$$

> *Conversion logic:* Every gear formula is just a rearrangement of the single definition $m = D_p / T$. Once you know any two of $\{m, D_p, T\}$, you can find the rest.

---

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Matrix — Quick Pattern Recognition

| If the question asks for… | Given are… | Use Formula | Watch Out For |
|--------------------------|------------|-------------|---------------|
| Spindle speed $N$ | $V, D$ | $N = 1000V/(\pi D)$ | Units of $V$ & $D$ |
| Cutting speed $V$ | $N, D$ | $V = \pi D N / 1000$ | Divide by 1000 for m/min |
| Pitch diameter $D_p$ | $m, T$ | $D_p = mT$ | $T$ is integer |
| Module $m$ | $D_p, T$ | $m = D_p/T$ | Round to standard module |
| Crank turns $N$ | Teeth $T$ | $N = 40/T$ | Check hole availability |
| Machining time $T_m$ | $L, f, N$ | $T_m = L/(fN)$ | Convert min → sec if asked |
| Number of passes | Depth, doc | $n_p = D/d$ | $d$ is per-pass depth |
| Thread pitch $P$ | TPI | $P = 25.4/\text{TPI}$ | 25.4 mm = 1 inch |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Five Pillars of Module 3 Simple Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Module 3 Simple Problem] --> P1[Pillar 1: Cutting Parameters]
    P0 --> P2[Pillar 2: Machining Time]
    P0 --> P3[Pillar 3: Gear Geometry]
    P0 --> P4[Pillar 4: Indexing]
    P0 --> P5[Pillar 5: Thread Geometry]

    P1 --> F1a[V equals pi D N over 1000]
    P1 --> F1b[N equals 1000 V over pi D]

    P2 --> F2a[Tm equals L over f N]
    P2 --> F2b[Number of passes equals total depth over d]

    P3 --> F3a[Dp equals m times T]
    P3 --> F3b[pc equals pi times m]
    P3 --> F3c[Addendum equals m]

    P4 --> F4a[Crank turns equals 40 over T]

    P5 --> F5a[L equals n times P]
    P5 --> F5b[P equals 25 point 4 over TPI]

    F1a --> OUT[Final Numerical Answer with Units]
    F1b --> OUT
    F2a --> OUT
    F2b --> OUT
    F3a --> OUT
    F3b --> OUT
    F3c --> OUT
    F4a --> OUT
    F5a --> OUT
    F5b --> OUT
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Dividing Head Indexing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece with T teeth to be cut] --> B[Each tooth needs 1 over T spindle turn]
    B --> C[Multiply by worm ratio 40]
    C --> D[Crank turns N equals 40 over T]
    D --> E{Does N match a hole on index plate?}
    E -->|YES| F[Simple Indexing Possible]
    E -->|NO| G[Compound Indexing Required]
    F --> H[Move crank to nearest hole circle and lock]
    G --> I[Not in scope of simple problems]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cutting Parameter Interrelationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT1[Workpiece Diameter D] --> COMP[Triangle of Cutting Parameters]
    INPUT2[Cutting Speed V] --> COMP
    INPUT3[Feed f] --> COMP
    COMP --> OUT1[Spindle Speed N]
    COMP --> OUT2[Feed Rate per minute]
    COMP --> OUT3[Machining Time for length L]

    OUT1 --> QC{Check against machine max RPM}
    QC -->|OK| DONE1[Acceptable]
    QC -->|Too High| DONE2[Reduce V or use smaller tool]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Gear Geometry Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Module m] --> D[Pitch Diameter Dp equals m T]
    M --> PC[Circular Pitch pc equals pi m]
    M --> A[Addendum equals m]
    M --> B[Dedendum equals 1 point 157 m]
    M --> H[Whole Depth equals 2 point 157 m]

    D --> GD[Standard Gear Drawing]
    PC --> GD
    A --> GD
    B --> GD
    H --> GD

    T[Number of Teeth T] --> D
    T --> PC
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Thread Cutting Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PITCH[Pitch P in mm] --> CALC1[For single start equals Lead L]
    PITCH --> CALC2[Multi start: L equals n times P]
    CALC1 --> APPL[Single start thread application]
    CALC2 --> APPL
    TPI[TPI threads per inch] --> CALC3[P equals 25 point 4 over TPI]
    CALC3 --> PITCH
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
A mild steel workpiece of **80 mm diameter** is being turned on a lathe. The recommended cutting speed is **25 m/min**. Calculate the **spindle speed** in rpm.

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- Workpiece diameter, $D = 80$ mm
- Cutting speed, $V = 25$ m/min

Formula:

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$$

Substitute:

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \frac{25000}{251.327} \approx 99.47 \text{ rpm}$$

**Answer: $N \approx 99.5$ rpm**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
Define the **module** of a gear. How is it related to the **pitch circle diameter** and the **number of teeth**?

**Model Answer:**

The **module $m$** of a gear is defined as the **ratio of the pitch circle diameter $D_p$ to the number of teeth $T$**.

$$m = \frac{D_p}{T}$$

It is the internationally standardized parameter that specifies *tooth size*. All other gear dimensions (addendum, dedendum, circular pitch) are expressed as multiples of the module. Rearranging:

$$D_p = m \times T \quad \text{or} \quad T = \frac{D_p}{m}$$

**Significance:** Two gears mesh correctly only when they share the *same module*. This makes $m$ the master key for gear design and selection.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** A steel shaft of **120 mm diameter** and **500 mm length** is to be turned on a lathe. The cutting speed is **30 m/min** and the feed is **0.25 mm/rev**. The total depth of cut is **4 mm** and is to be removed in **2 passes**. Calculate the **spindle speed**, **feed rate in mm/min**, and the **total machining time in minutes**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 120}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{376.99} \approx 79.58 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Feed Rate in mm/min:**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.25 \times 79.58 \approx 19.89 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Passes:** Given as 2.

**Step 4 — Total Machining Time:**

For one pass:
$$T_{one} = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{500}{0.25 \times 79.58} = \frac{500}{19.895} \approx 25.13 \text{ min}$$

For two passes:
$$T_{total} = 2 \times 25.13 = 50.26 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Spindle speed: $N \approx 79.6$ rpm
- Feed rate: $f_r \approx 19.9$ mm/min
- Total machining time: $T_{total} \approx 50.3$ min

---

**(b)** A **spur gear** with **48 teeth** and a **module of 3 mm** is to be manufactured on a milling machine using a **Brown & Sharpe dividing head** (worm ratio 40:1). Calculate: **(i)** Pitch circle diameter, **(ii)** Circular pitch, **(iii)** Addendum, **(iv)** Dedendum, and **(v)** Number of crank turns required per tooth on a 15-hole index circle. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Number of teeth, $T = 48$
- Module, $m = 3$ mm

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameter:**

$$D_p = m \times T = 3 \times 48 = 144 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Circular Pitch:**

$$p_c = \pi \times m = \pi \times 3 \approx 9.425 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Addendum:**

$$a = m = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Dedendum:**

$$b = 1.157 \times m = 1.157 \times 3 \approx 3.471 \text{ mm}$$

**(v) Crank Turns per Tooth (Simple Indexing):**

$$N = \frac{40}{T} = \frac{40}{48} = \frac{5}{6}$$

$$N = 0.833 \text{ turns} = 0.833 \times 15 \text{ holes on 15-circle}$$

$$N = \frac{5}{6} \times 15 = 12.5 \text{ holes}$$

Since a fractional hole is not physically possible, the index plate selection must change. Trying **21-hole circle**:

$$N = \frac{5}{6} \times 21 = 17.5 \text{ holes (still fractional)}$$

Trying **27-hole circle**:

$$N = \frac{5}{6} \times 27 = 22.5 \text{ holes (still fractional)}$$

Trying **33-hole circle**:

$$N = \frac{5}{6} \times 33 = 27.5 \text{ holes (still fractional)}$$

For T = 48, since $40/T = 5/6$ yields fractional holes on all standard plates, this requires **compound indexing**. However, for the simple problem we accept the fractional result:

$$N = \frac{40}{48} = \frac{5}{6} \text{ turns} \approx 0.833 \text{ turns per tooth}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> For Part B (b), students often forget that **$T$ is always an integer** in the formula $D_p = mT$. Using a non-standard module (e.g., $m = 3.7$) results in a non-standard gear. Always round module to **standard values** (1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, ...) for full credit.
>
> For Part B (a), **forgetting the factor 1000** in the cutting-speed formula is the most common error, costing 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** A **milling cutter** of **50 mm diameter** has **8 teeth** and rotates at **200 rpm**. The feed per tooth is **0.1 mm/tooth**. Calculate the **table feed rate** in mm/min and the **time required to machine a 300 mm long surface**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Cutter diameter, $D_t = 50$ mm (not directly used in feed calc)
- Number of teeth on cutter, $Z = 8$
- Spindle speed, $N = 200$ rpm
- Feed per tooth, $f_z = 0.1$ mm/tooth
- Length, $L = 300$ mm

**Step 1 — Table Feed Rate (mm/min):**

$$f_m = f_z \times Z \times N = 0.1 \times 8 \times 200 = 160 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2 — Machining Time:**

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f_m} = \frac{300}{160} = 1.875 \text{ min}$$

Converting to seconds:
$$T_m = 1.875 \times 60 = 112.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Table feed rate: $f_m = 160$ mm/min
- Machining time: $T_m \approx 1.875$ min $\approx 112.5$ s

---

**(b)** Explain **simple indexing** with a **worked example**: A gear with **60 teeth** is to be cut using a Brown & Sharpe dividing head with standard index plates. Determine the **number of crank turns and holes** for cutting this gear. Use a **20-hole index circle**. Also calculate the **circular pitch** if the module is **2 mm**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Simple Indexing:**

In simple indexing, a single crank turn ratio is used to advance the workpiece by one tooth space. The dividing head has a worm gear ratio of **40:1**, so:

$$N_{crank} = \frac{40}{T}$$

**Step 1 — Crank Turns for T = 60:**

$$N = \frac{40}{60} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ turns}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Holes on 20-Hole Plate:**

$$\text{Holes} = \frac{2}{3} \times 20 = \frac{40}{3} = 13.33 \text{ holes}$$

Since a fractional hole is not possible, this requires a different plate or compound indexing.

**Alternative — Try a 30-hole index circle** (if available):

$$\text{Holes} = \frac{2}{3} \times 30 = 20 \text{ holes} \checkmark$$

> *Note:* Standard plates come in 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 30, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49, 54 holes.

**Step 3 — Circular Pitch for Module = 2 mm:**

$$p_c = \pi \times m = \pi \times 2 \approx 6.283 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Crank turns: $N = 2/3$ turns
- On 30-hole plate: **20 holes** ✓
- Circular pitch: $p_c \approx 6.283$ mm

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 "Simple Problems" — Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Core formulas to memorize cold:**
  - $V = \pi D N / 1000$ (cutting speed)
  - $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ (spindle speed)
  - $T_m = L / (f \times N)$ (turning time)
  - $T_m = L / (f_z Z N)$ (milling time)
  - $D_p = m \times T$ (pitch diameter)
  - $p_c = \pi m$ (circular pitch)
  - $a = m$ (addendum)
  - $b = 1.157 m$ (dedendum)
  - $h = 2.157 m$ (whole depth)
  - $N_{crank} = 40/T$ (simple indexing)
  - $L = n \times P$ (thread lead)

- **Always convert units consistently:**
  - $V$ in **m/min** needs $D$ in mm and the ÷1000 factor.
  - 1 inch = **25.4 mm** (for TPI ↔ mm pitch conversion).

- **Standard modules:** 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm (AGMA/ISO preferred series).

- **Simple indexing limitation:** Works only when $40/T$ is exactly representable on a standard hole plate. Otherwise, **compound indexing** is needed (out of scope for simple problems).

- **Threads:**
  - **Metric:** pitch in mm (e.g., M10 × 1.5 → pitch = 1.5 mm).
  - **Imperial:** TPI (threads per inch) → pitch = 25.4 / TPI.
  - **Single-start:** lead = pitch. **Multi-start:** lead = $n$ × pitch.

- **Common examiner traps:**
  - Forgetting to convert mm/min to m/min (factor 1000).
  - Mixing $D_p$ with **outside diameter** $D_o = D_p + 2m$.
  - Confusing **module** with **circular pitch** (one is mm, the other is also mm but they differ by $\pi$).
  - Treating $T$ as the denominator directly — always use $40/T$, never $T/40$.

- **Quick sanity check:**
  - For steel: $V \approx 25$–$40$ m/min (turning).
  - For cast iron: $V \approx 15$–$25$ m/min (turning).
  - For aluminum: $V \approx 60$–$120$ m/min (turning).
  - For brass: $V \approx 40$–$80$ m/min (turning).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Final Warning:** In all simple-problem solutions, **always state the given data, write the formula, show substitution, and box the final answer with units**. Partial credit is heavily dependent on showing all four steps. A bare numerical answer without units typically receives **zero** marks even if numerically correct.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Illustration of Digital Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Definition and Illustration of Digital Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Digital Manufacturing (DM)** is the application of a **fully integrated, computer-based system** that encompasses simulation, 3D visualization, analytics, and various collaboration tools to create **product and manufacturing process definitions simultaneously**. It is an **end-to-end, data-driven manufacturing ecosystem** where every stage — from product design and process planning to production, quality control, and supply chain logistics — is digitally modeled, monitored, optimized, and controlled in real time.

According to the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)**, digital manufacturing refers to *"the use of an integrated digital twin / digital thread of manufacturing data to enhance manufacturing and its supply chain operations."*

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**  
> *Digital Manufacturing is an integrated approach to manufacturing that uses digital technologies, computer-aided design (CAD), computer-aided manufacturing (CAM), computer-aided engineering (CAE), and computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) along with modern ICT tools (IoT, Cloud, Big Data, AI) to link all phases of the product lifecycle into a unified digital environment.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍳

Imagine a **modern cloud kitchen** (think Swiggy/Cloud-kitchen model) versus a **traditional roadside tea stall**:

| Aspect | Traditional Tea Stall (Analog Manufacturing) | Cloud Kitchen (Digital Manufacturing) |
|---|---|---|
| Recipe | Written on a dusty blackboard | Stored in a cloud-based recipe management system |
| Order Receiving | Customer shouts across the counter | Mobile app captures order → auto-routed to nearest kitchen |
| Inventory | Owner eyeballs the milk can | IoT sensor on milk can reports exact level in real time |
| Quality | Chef tastes by feel | AI camera checks color, foam height, temperature |
| Delivery | Walk across the road | GPS-tracked drone routing optimized by an algorithm |
| Feedback | Customer grumbles at the counter | NLP engine parses 10,000+ reviews to suggest new blends |

> **The Tea Stall = Traditional Manufacturing** (silos, manual decisions, isolated data).  
> **The Cloud Kitchen = Digital Manufacturing** (every cup, every customer, every gram of sugar is connected, sensed, simulated, and stored as data — decisions are driven by analytics, not gut feel).

The **key idea**: digital manufacturing treats the *factory floor* the way cloud kitchens treat their *delivery zones* — as a **live, networked, software-defined environment**.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]  
> **Three terms you must NEVER confuse in your exam:**
> 
> - **Digital Twin** — A *virtual replica* of a physical asset, process, or system used for simulation and prediction.
> - **Digital Thread** — The *continuous data flow* that connects every stage of the product lifecycle (design → make → use → service).
> - **Digital Manufacturing** — The *overarching umbrella* that uses digital twins, digital threads, CAD/CAM, and smart factory technologies to manufacture products.

---

## 1.4 The Industrial Context — Why Digital Manufacturing?

Before digital manufacturing, factories suffered from the **"islands of automation"** problem — a robotic arm here, a CNC there, but no communication between them. Digital manufacturing **breaks these islands** by linking:

- **Design data** (CAD) ⇆ **Process data** (CAM) ⇆ **Production data** (PLC/CNC) ⇆ **Quality data** (CMM) ⇆ **Logistics data** (ERP).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 – CO Alignment):**  
> This topic maps directly to **CO4** of PCIET402: *"Illustrate the concepts of digital manufacturing and identify the modern digital technologies enabling Industry 4.0."* Bloom's level: **Understand / Illustrate (L2–L3).**

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Digital Manufacturing

Digital manufacturing rests on **five interlocking pillars**. Examiners love testing these as 3-mark or short-answer questions.

### 🔹 Pillar 1 — Design Digitization (CAD/CAE)
- Every component is designed in 3D (e.g., CATIA, SolidWorks, Creo).
- Finite Element Analysis (FEA) virtually tests strength, fatigue, vibration **before** any metal is cut.
- **Real-world utility:** Boeing 787 was 80% designed and 100% simulated before any physical prototype.

### 🔹 Pillar 2 — Process Digitization (CAM/CAPP)
- Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) auto-generates machining steps, tool paths, and G-codes.
- **Real-world utility:** A 5-axis CNC using Mastercam/SolidCAM can machine a turbine blade from a single CAD file with zero manual drafting.

### 🔹 Pillar 3 — Production Digitization (CIM / CNC / Robotics)
- Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) interlinks CNC machines, robots, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and PLCs.
- **Real-world utility:** Tesla's Gigafactory body shop uses 1,000+ robots orchestrated by a single digital MES (Manufacturing Execution System).

### 🔹 Pillar 4 — Quality Digitization (CMM / Vision Systems)
- Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM) and AI-based machine vision inspect 100% of parts, not just samples.
- **Real-world utility:** Bosch uses in-line optical inspection to detect cracks invisible to the human eye.

### 🔹 Pillar 5 — Supply Chain Digitization (ERP / SCM / Blockchain)
- Digital links tie raw material suppliers, in-house production, and end-customers.
- **Real-world utility:** Amazon's "anticipatory shipping" uses demand-simulation models to ship goods before you order them.

---

## 2.2 Enabling Technology Stack (Industry 4.0 Substrate)

The **technology stack** that powers digital manufacturing is a layered cake:

```
Layer 5 (Top)    │  AI / Machine Learning / Generative Design
Layer 4          │  Big Data Analytics / Cloud / Edge Computing
Layer 3          │  IoT Sensors / CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems)
Layer 2          │  Connectivity (5G / OPC-UA / MQTT / Industrial Ethernet)
Layer 1 (Bottom) │  Physical Machines (CNC, Robots, AGVs, 3D Printers)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this stack matters for KTU exams:**  
> When a question asks *"List the enabling technologies of digital manufacturing"* — this is the structure to write. **Memorize the bottom-up logic:** Physical → Connectivity → Sensing → Computing → Intelligence.

---

## 2.3 The Digital Thread — The Backbone of DM

The **digital thread** is a concept often tested as a 7-mark question. It refers to the *unbroken, bidirectional data flow* that connects:

$$\text{Design} \leftrightarrow \text{Engineering} \leftrightarrow \text{Manufacturing} \leftrightarrow \text{Quality} \leftrightarrow \text{Service} \leftrightarrow \text{End-of-Life}$$

Mathematically, we can represent the digital thread as a **continuous data set** $D(t)$ evolving over time:

$$
D(t) = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \big[ C_i(t) \,\|\, P_i(t) \,\|\, Q_i(t) \,\|\, L_i(t) \big]
$$

Where:
- $C_i(t)$ = Computer-aided Design data for part *i* at time *t*
- $P_i(t)$ = Process planning & production data
- $Q_i(t)$ = Quality inspection data
- $L_i(t)$ = Logistics & supply-chain data
- $\|$ denotes concatenation (data-fusion operator)
- $n$ = total number of components in the product

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Traditional Manufacturing | Digital Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|
| Design Media | 2D paper drawings | 3D CAD + virtual simulation |
| Process Planning | Manual, experience-based | CAPP, generative AI |
| Production Control | Manual supervisory | MES, IIoT, real-time |
| Quality Inspection | Sampling (5–10%) | 100% in-line inspection |
| Data Storage | Paper logs, isolated PCs | Cloud-based unified database |
| Decision Making | Reactive (after defect) | Predictive (before defect) |
| Lead Time | Weeks–Months | Days–Hours |
| Changeover Cost | High (retooling) | Low (software reconfig) |
| Energy Management | Sub-metered only | Per-machine, per-cycle, AI-optimized |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | 55–65% (world avg.) | 85%+ achievable |
| Core Standards | ISO 9001 | ISO 9001 + ISO 23247 (Digital Twin) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **OEE is calculated as:**  
> $$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$  
> where each factor is expressed as a percentage (0–100%). DM systems push OEE higher by reducing downtime and defects.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Automotive:** Toyota's MONOZUKURI system uses digital twins to simulate body welding lines before physical commissioning.
- **Aerospace:** Airbus A350 — 100% of structural parts are defined digitally and 50% are 3D-printed.
- **Medical:** Stryker uses DM to mass-customize knee implants to each patient's CT scan data.
- **Education/Research Labs:** KTU-affiliated mechanical engineering labs use CNC + CAD/CAM as the foundational DM toolkit.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Insight:** Digital manufacturing is **not** about replacing humans; it is about **augmenting** human decisions with data-driven insights so that engineers can focus on creativity, while machines handle repetition.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Worked Example

> [!NOTE]  
> Digital Manufacturing is conceptual, not computational — so the "derivation" below is a **structured logical walkthrough** of how a digital manufacturing workflow transforms an idea into a finished, traceable product.

---

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Digital Manufacturing Cycle

We can model the DM cycle in **seven sequential steps**:

### **Step 1 — Customer Demand Capture**
- Customer requirements (specs, volume, delivery date) are entered into a **CRM (Customer Relationship Management)** portal.
- A formal Product Requirement Specification (PRS) document $S$ is generated:
$$
S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k\}
$$
where each $s_i$ is an individual specification (e.g., *tensile strength ≥ 400 MPa*, *mass ≤ 2 kg*).

> 💡 **Why:** All downstream decisions trace back to $S$. This is the "single source of truth."

### **Step 2 — Digital Design (CAD + CAE)**
- Designer creates a **3D parametric model** $M$ in CAD software.
- CAE simulation (FEA / CFD) verifies $M$ against every $s_i \in S$.
- If failure occurs, **design parameters are modified automatically** by an optimization loop (e.g., topology optimization):
$$
\min_{x} \; W(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sigma(x) \leq \sigma_{allow}, \; u(x) \leq u_{allow}
$$
where:
- $W(x)$ = weight of the structure (objective)
- $\sigma(x)$ = maximum stress under load
- $u(x)$ = maximum displacement
- $x$ = vector of geometric design variables

> 💡 **Why:** Eliminates ~70% of design flaws before any material is purchased.

### **Step 3 — Process Planning (CAPP + CAM)**
- The validated CAD model $M$ is fed into CAM software.
- Tool selection, spindle speed $N$, feed rate $f$, and depth of cut $d$ are auto-computed.
- Cutting speed $V$ is constrained by:
$$
V = \pi D N
$$
where:
- $V$ = cutting speed in m/min
- $D$ = tool/workpiece diameter in mm
- $N$ = spindle speed in rpm
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$

### **Step 4 — Digital Twin Commissioning**
- A **virtual replica** of the CNC machine is created in software (e.g., Siemens NX MCD, DELMIA).
- The G-code is **simulated first** on this digital twin:
- Collision check ✓
- Cycle-time estimation ✓
- Tool-path optimization ✓

> 💡 **Why:** A 30-second G-code error can crash a ₹50 lakh spindle. Digital twin catches it *before* the physical machine runs.

### **Step 5 — Physical Production (CNC / Robot / 3D Printer)**
- The validated G-code is transmitted via **MTConnect / OPC-UA** to the physical CNC.
- Real-time sensor data (vibration, current, temperature) is streamed back to a **dashboard**.
- The physical process $P$ generates:
$$
P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_m\}
$$
where each $p_j$ is a produced part with a unique **digital birth certificate** (timestamp, operator ID, machine ID, raw material batch).

### **Step 6 — In-line Quality Assurance (CMM + Vision)**
- 100% of parts are inspected by CMM and machine vision.
- Each part $p_j$ gets a measured dimensional vector $d_j$:
$$
d_j = [d_{j1}, d_{j2}, \dots, d_{jL}]
$$
where $L$ = number of critical dimensions.
- Tolerance check is performed:
$$
|d_{jl} - d_{l}^{nom}| \leq \tau_l \quad \forall \, l \in \{1, 2, \dots, L\}
$$
where $d_{l}^{nom}$ = nominal dimension, $\tau_l$ = tolerance band.

> If all constraints hold → **part accepted** and linked to its digital birth certificate.  
> If any fails → **automated root-cause analysis** is triggered.

### **Step 7 — Digital Thread Storage & Feedback**
- All data $\{S, M, \text{G-code}, P, D\}$ is archived in a **cloud database**.
- AI models continuously retrain on this data to:
- Predict machine failures (predictive maintenance)
- Optimize energy consumption
- Suggest design improvements for next-generation products

> 💡 **Why:** This step **closes the loop**, turning every manufactured part into *future knowledge*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Inputs vs Outputs at Each Stage

| Stage | Primary Input | Primary Output | Enabling Tech |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Demand | Customer order | PRS document $S$ | CRM, ERP |
| 2. Design | $S$ | 3D model $M$ + CAE report | CAD / CAE |
| 3. Process | $M$ | G-code, tool list, fixtures | CAM / CAPP |
| 4. Digital Twin | G-code | Validated simulation | DELMIA, Siemens MCD |
| 5. Production | Validated G-code | Physical parts $P$ | CNC, Robotics, 3DP |
| 6. Quality | Parts $P$ | Pass/Fail + dimensional vector $d_j$ | CMM, Vision |
| 7. Thread | All data | AI insights, traceability | Cloud, Big Data, AI |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Mini-Example (for Numerical Bearings)

> **Problem:** A CNC milling operation is part of a digital manufacturing cell. The tool diameter $D = 50$ mm, spindle speed $N = 600$ rpm, and the recommended cutting speed for mild steel is $V = 90$ m/min. Verify if the chosen spindle speed is consistent with the recommended cutting speed.

**Solution:**

$$
V_{actual} = \pi D N
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_{actual} = \pi \times 50 \times 600
$$

$$
V_{actual} = 3.14159 \times 50 \times 600
$$

$$
V_{actual} = 3.14159 \times 30{,}000
$$

$$
V_{actual} = 94{,}247.78 \;\text{mm/min}
$$

Converting to m/min (divide by 1000):

$$
V_{actual} = \frac{94{,}247.78}{1000} = 94.25 \;\text{m/min}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
V_{actual} = 94.25 \;\text{m/min} \quad \text{vs} \quad V_{recommended} = 90 \;\text{m/min}
$$

**Percentage deviation:**

$$
\Delta V\,(\%) = \frac{94.25 - 90}{90} \times 100 = 4.72\,\%
$$

Since the deviation is **less than 5%**, the chosen spindle speed is **acceptable** for the digital manufacturing workflow. ✅

> 💡 **Engineering takeaway:** In a DM environment, such calculations are **performed automatically** by CAM software, and any deviation beyond tolerance is flagged back to the engineer **before** a single chip is cut.

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]`

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Definition, Architecture & Benefits (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 Marks):**

Digital Manufacturing (DM) is a holistic, **end-to-end digital paradigm** that integrates design, process planning, production, quality control, and supply chain management through a unified digital platform. It converts every physical activity into a digital data point, enabling simulation, prediction, and real-time optimization across the entire product lifecycle.

**Architecture Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Customer Requirement Input Layer] --> B[Product Design and Engineering Layer]
    B --> C[Process Planning and Simulation Layer]
    C --> D[Production Execution Layer]
    D --> E[Quality Assurance Layer]
    E --> F[Supply Chain and Logistics Layer]
    F --> G[Digital Thread and Cloud Database]
    G --> A
    G --> B
    G --> C
    G --> D
    G --> E
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FF6347,color:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

**Six Key Benefits (½ mark each — 3 Marks total):**

1. **Reduced time-to-market** — Concurrent engineering via digital simulation.
2. **Lower production cost** — Predictive maintenance reduces downtime by 30–50%.
3. **Improved product quality** — 100% inspection and AI-driven defect detection.
4. **Design flexibility** — Easy customization through parametric CAD models.
5. **Better traceability** — Every part carries a digital birth certificate.
6. **Energy efficiency** — Real-time energy monitoring reduces consumption by 10–20%.

---

#### **Part (b) — Digital Thread, Digital Twin & Automotive Example (7 Marks)**

**Digital Twin (2 Marks):**

A Digital Twin is a **dynamic virtual replica** of a physical asset, process, or system. It receives real-time sensor data from its physical counterpart and uses physics-based models + AI to predict behavior.

Examples:
- A virtual model of a CNC spindle running in parallel with the real spindle.
- A simulated factory layout that updates as robots move.

**Digital Thread (2 Marks):**

A Digital Thread is the **continuous, connected data layer** that links every stakeholder, tool, and lifecycle stage. It carries data from the customer's voice → CAD → CAM → MES → ERP → service and back, without manual re-entry.

**How they together form the DM backbone (1 Mark):**

- Digital Twin = *the body* (instantiates one specific physical asset).  
- Digital Thread = *the nervous system* (connects all bodies into one organism).

Without the twin, you can't simulate. Without the thread, you can't coordinate.

**Automotive Industry Example (2 Marks):**

Consider **Tesla's Model 3 production line**:
- The **digital twin** of each welding robot simulates weld-pool quality before the robot fires.
- The **digital thread** ensures that any design change uploaded by the engineer in California is auto-propagated to the Gigafactory in Shanghai within minutes — toolpaths, robot programs, and inspection routines all update themselves.
- Result: Tesla shortened Model 3 development time by ~30% compared to traditional OEMs.

---

**(a) Explain in detail the five pillars of Digital Manufacturing. For each pillar, give one real-world industrial example. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Describe the role of the Internet of Things (IoT) and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) in enabling digital manufacturing. How does the OPC-UA protocol support machine-to-machine communication in a smart factory? (7 Marks)**
