# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question Paper]**
**Define Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV). List any four types of AGVs based on their function.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)** is a self-driven, computer-controlled material handling vehicle that follows a predefined path marked on or embedded in the factory floor, used to transport materials between workstations without human intervention in a Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS).

The four functional types of AGVs are:
1. **Driverless trains / Tow vehicles** — pull multiple trailers.
2. **Unit load carriers** — carry individual unit loads.
3. **Pallet trucks** — lift and transport pallets.
4. **Fork trucks** — have on-board forks for self-loading.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest Paper]**
**Compare wire guidance and laser guidance methods of AGVs on any three parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Wire Guidance | Laser Guidance |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±10 mm | ±10 mm |
| Installation cost | Very high (floor cutting) | High (retro-reflectors on walls) |
| Flexibility | Very low (path fixed in concrete) | Very high (reprogrammable) |
| Floor modification | Required | Not required |
| Suitable environment | Clean indoor, fixed routes | Modern FMS, frequently reconfigured |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part B question must be answered with internal choice. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 ESE — Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the major subsystems of an Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV). Discuss the role of the central host computer in coordinating a fleet of AGVs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An AGV has wheelbase $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$, track width $W = 0.9 \text{ m}$, and maximum permissible steering angle at the front inner wheel $\delta_i = 35^\circ$. Determine the minimum turning radius and verify whether the steering linkage satisfies the Ackermann condition. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Subsystems of an AGV:**

1. **Vehicle chassis & drive system:** Differential drive with two independently driven wheels + 2–4 castor/idler wheels for support.
2. **Guidance system:** Path sensors such as inductive coils (wire guidance), Hall-effect sensors (magnetic tape), or rotating laser scanners (laser guidance). These detect the lateral deviation of the AGV from the reference path.
3. **On-board controller:** A microcontroller or PLC running the path-tracking algorithm (e.g., Stanley controller). It computes steering correction every 10–50 ms.
4. **Communication system:** Radio-frequency (RF) or Wi-Fi module for two-way communication with the host.
5. **Power system:** Rechargeable battery (24 V or 48 V) with a Battery Management System (BMS).
6. **Payload handling:** Lifting platform, roller conveyor, or fork assembly.
7. **Safety system:** Bumper switches, emergency stop, audible/visual alarms, light curtains.

**Role of the Central Host Computer:**

- **Fleet management:** Maintains a global map of all AGV positions, battery states, and loads.
- **Dispatch logic:** Assigns tasks to AGVs using static, dynamic, or auction-based algorithms.
- **Traffic management:** Implements zone control and prevents collisions at intersections.
- **Data logging:** Records every trip for OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) calculation.
- **Integration with MES/ERP:** Receives production schedules and updates WIP inventory.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Minimum Turning Radius**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\min} &= \dfrac{L}{\tan(\delta_i)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.5}{\tan(35^\circ)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{1.5}{0.7002} \\[6pt]
&= 2.142 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Ackermann Check — Outer Wheel Angle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_o &= \delta_i + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 35^\circ + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.9}{1.5}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 35^\circ + \arctan(0.6) \\[6pt]
&= 35^\circ + 30.96^\circ \\[6pt]
&= 65.96^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

A steering angle of 65.96° is mechanically infeasible. Therefore, the steering linkage *cannot* be a simple rigid tie-rod; it must use a **trapezoidal Ackermann linkage** so that steering the inner wheel by 35° automatically produces the correct outer wheel angle of approximately 65.96°. Since most AGVs use **differential steering** instead, the Ackermann geometry is only relevant for steered-wheel AGVs such as AGV fork trucks.

**Final Answer:** $R_{\min} = 2.142 \text{ m}$; Outer wheel angle required $= 65.96^\circ$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 ESE — Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare the following AGV guidance methods in a tabular form: Wire, Magnetic Tape, Laser, and Vision guidance. State at least four comparative parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An AGV has the following duty cycle:
- Driving motor: $P_d = 350 \text{ W}$ (continuous)
- Lift motor: $P_L = 700 \text{ W}$ (active 20% of time)
- Controller and sensors: $P_c = 60 \text{ W}$ (continuous)

If the AGV operates on a **48 V** battery with overall efficiency $\eta = 0.90$ for a continuous run-time of $t_h = 10$ hours, determine: (i) the required battery ampere-hour capacity, and (ii) the average current drawn. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Comparative Table — AGV Guidance Methods**

| Parameter | Wire | Magnetic Tape | Laser | Vision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | ±10 mm | ±10 mm | ±10 mm | ±20 mm |
| Installation cost | Very high | Low | High | High |
| Flexibility | Very low | Low | Very high | Very high |
| Floor modification | Required | Required | None | None |
| Sensor type | Inductive coil | Hall effect | Rotating laser | CCD camera |
| Maintenance | Very low | Medium (tape wear) | Low | Medium (lens cleaning) |
| Susceptible to dust/oil | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Typical application | Old FMS, automotive | Warehouses | Modern FMS, semiconductor | Pharma, food |

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Average Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{avg}} &= P_d + (P_L \times 0.20) + P_c \\[6pt]
&= 350 + (700 \times 0.20) + 60 \\[6pt]
&= 350 + 140 + 60 \\[6pt]
&= 550 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= P_{\text{avg}} \cdot t_h \\[6pt]
&= 550 \times 10 \\[6pt]
&= 5500 \text{ Wh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Battery Ampere-Hour Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Ah} &= \dfrac{E_b}{V \cdot \eta} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{5500}{48 \times 0.90} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{5500}{43.2} \\[6pt]
&= 127.31 \text{ Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average Current Drawn**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{P_{\text{avg}}}{V \cdot \eta} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{550}{48 \times 0.90} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{550}{43.2} \\[6pt]
&= 12.73 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_{Ah} \approx 128 \text{ Ah}$, $I_{\text{avg}} \approx 12.73 \text{ A}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use $P_{\text{avg}} = P_d + P_L + P_c$ without multiplying by duty cycle. This gives an oversized battery and costs **3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to divide by efficiency $\eta$ while computing $C_{Ah}$. This gives an undersized battery and costs **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $\tan^{-1}$ instead of $\tan$ in the Ackermann formula, or using degrees where the formula expects radians. **Always** verify the units.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing "traffic management" with "collision avoidance." They are *different*: traffic management is *centralized*; collision avoidance is *on-board*.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing "AGV uses GPS" for indoor FMS — GPS does **not** work indoors. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> This is your rapid-revision checklist for the **last 30 minutes** before the KTU ESE exam.

- [ ] **AGV = Automated Guided Vehicle** — driverless, path-bound, battery-powered material handler.
- [ ] **AGV ≠ Conveyor** — AGV has *selectable* path; conveyor has *fixed* path.
- [ ] **AGV ≠ AMR** — AGV follows predefined path; AMR uses SLAM/free navigation.
- [ ] **Six vehicle types:** Tow, Pallet, Unit Load, Fork, Light Load, Assembly.
- [ ] **Six guidance methods:** Wire, Magnetic Tape, Optical, Laser, Inertial, Vision.
- [ ] **Laser guidance accuracy:** ±10 mm with rotating scanner + retro-reflectors.
- [ ] **Wire guidance frequency:** 5–15 kHz, 100–500 mA AC.
- [ ] **Ackermann formula:** $R_{\min} = L / \tan(\delta_{\max})$.
- [ ] **Stanley steering law:** $\delta = \theta_p + \Delta\psi + \arctan(k e / v)$.
- [ ] **Battery capacity:** $C_{Ah} = (P_{\text{avg}} \cdot t_h) / (V \cdot \eta)$.
- [ ] **Average power formula:** $P_{\text{avg}} = P_{\text{continuous}} + \Sigma (P_i \cdot \text{duty}_i)$.
- [ ] **Number of AGVs:** $N = (\lambda \cdot T_c) / \eta_u$ (Little's law variant).
- [ ] **Cycle time:** $T_c = 2 L_p / v + T_{load} + T_{unload}$.
- [ ] **Differential drive** allows zero-radius turning; preferred for indoor AGVs.
- [ ] **Central host functions:** Dispatch, traffic, fleet, data logging, MES/ERP integration.
- [ ] **Battery voltages:** 24 V or 48 V DC standard.
- [ ] **Operating speed:** 0.5–1.5 m/s indoor; up to 3 m/s outdoor.
- [ ] **Payload range:** 500 kg to 50,000 kg.
- [ ] **Stop accuracy:** ±10 mm position; ±0.5° orientation.
- [ ] **Always include duty cycle** in battery calculation — common 3-mark error.
- [ ] **Always divide by efficiency $\eta$** — common 2-mark error.
- [ ] **GPS does NOT work indoors** — guaranteed 2-mark deduction if used in FMS answer.
- [ ] **Ackermann linkage** is required only for *steered-wheel* AGVs; differential drive bypasses it.
- [ ] **Industry 4.0 link:** AGVs are nodes on the IoT network of a smart factory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** In a 14-mark Part B question, allocate approximately **3 marks** for diagrams (functional block + flowchart), **4 marks** for descriptive content, and **7 marks** for numerical derivation. Always conclude with a **practical example** (e.g., "Used by Tata Motors for engine delivery") — this often nudges a +1 mark from the examiner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (ASRS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (ASRS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS):** A computer-controlled, often total-enclosed combination of equipment (Storage Racks, Storage/Retrieval (S/R) machines, Pick stations, Conveyors, and Warehouse Management Software) used to automatically store and retrieve materials, parts, tools, and finished goods from defined storage locations with high density and minimum human intervention.

In the context of **PCIET402 – Machine Tools and Digital Manufacturing**, AS/RS is classified under **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** work-in-process (WIP) handling and is considered the *backbone of automated material flow* in modern digital factories. It directly enables the **Industry 4.0** vision of *unmanned, lights-out* production.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of an AS/RS as a **"robotic, multi-storey library for parts"**. Imagine the largest library in the world where, instead of you walking aisle to aisle looking for a book, an **elevator-like crane** whizzes horizontally along the aisle, simultaneously moves vertically to the correct shelf, grabs the exact bin, and delivers it to a checkout counter — all in less than a minute. The librarian (human) is replaced by an algorithm, and the elevator is the S/R machine.

**Real-world analogy:** Amazon's fulfilment centres use mini-load AS/RS-like systems behind the scenes — your order is fetched by a robotic shuttle from a towering rack, brought to a human picker, packed, and shipped, often in under 15 minutes. KTU factories integrate AS/RS with CNC machines so that raw billets are dispatched to machining centres without any manual touch.

### 1.3 Primary Justification for AS/RS in KTU Context

| Justification Metric | Manual System | AS/RS (Benchmark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Space Utilisation (Floor-to-Ceiling)** | ~**35%** of warehouse height | Up to **90%** (rack height **30 m** typical) |
| **Pick Accuracy** | ~**97%** (human error ~3%) | **>99.99%** (computer-tracked) |
| **Inventory Visibility** | Delayed / paper-based | **Real-time**, RFID/WMS integrated |
| **Labour Dependency** | High (3 shifts) | Single shift supervision only |

### 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of an AS/RS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to remember these four quantitative KPIs:
> 1. **Throughput** — loads moved per hour (loads/h)
> 2. **Storage Density** — m³ stored per m² of floor area
> 3. **S/R Machine Cycle Time ($T_{sc}$)** — seconds per single command cycle
> 4. **Reliability / Availability** — typically **>95%** (MTBF in hours)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture of a Modern AS/RS

The system is built on **four interacting subsystems** that must be studied as a single coordinated unit:

1. **Storage Sub-system** — The fixed racks/aisles.
2. **S/R Machine (Crane/Shuttle)** — The mechanical extractor.
3. **Pick/Deposit Station (P/D station)** — The human-machine interface (HMI).
4. **Material Handling Interfacing** — Conveyors, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), or robotic transfer arms connecting the AS/RS to the manufacturing cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024):** The **S/R machine is the heart of the AS/RS**. Its acceleration, deceleration, and path-optimisation profile dictate **80%** of the system's performance.

### 2.2 Classification of AS/RS (High-Yield for KTU Exams)

| Class | Storage Unit | Typical Payload | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit-Load AS/RS** | Pallet / tote | **500 – 2000 kg** | Raw materials, finished goods |
| **Mini-Load AS/RS** | Tote / bin / carton | **≤ 100 kg** | Spare parts, small components, tools |
| **Vertical Lift Module (VLM)** | Tray | **≤ 500 kg** | Tools, tooling, slow-moving parts |
| **Horizontal/Vertical Carousel** | Bins on rotating frame | **≤ 50 kg/bin** | Small parts, pharmaceutical, e-commerce |
| **Cube-Based / Shuttle AS/RS** | Mobile robotic shuttle in rack | **≤ 50 kg** | High-throughput e-commerce fulfilment |

### 2.3 Coordinate Positioning Principle

Every storage location in an AS/RS is identified by a unique **triple (X, Y, Z)** coordinate:
- **X** → Aisle number (1 to $N_a$)
- **Y** → Bay (column position along the aisle) → 1 to $N_b$
- **Z** → Shelf level (height) → 1 to $N_l$

The S/R machine must execute a **simultaneous 2-axis motion** (Y + Z) — never one axis at a time — to minimise cycle time. This is known as the **"Chebyshev travel"** principle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these six equations — they form **>60%** of ESE numerical questions on AS/RS.

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{sc} = T_t + T_s + T_f$ | $T_{sc}$: single command cycle time, $T_t$: travel, $T_s$: simultaneous settle, $T_f$: fork/dwell | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $T_{sc} = T_h + T_v + T_f$ | $T_h$: horizontal travel, $T_v$: vertical travel | s |
| 3 | $T_h = \dfrac{D_x}{V_x}$ | $D_x$: horizontal distance, $V_x$: horizontal velocity | s |
| 4 | $T_v = \dfrac{D_y}{V_y}$ | $D_y$: vertical distance, $V_y$: vertical velocity | s |
| 5 | $T_{dc} = 2(T_{sc})$ | $T_{dc}$: dual command cycle (store + retrieve in one trip) | s |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{sc}}$ | Loads per hour per S/R machine | loads/h |
| 7 | $V = L \times W \times H$ | Volume of one bin (length × width × height) | m³ |
| 8 | $S_{cap} = N \times V$ | $N$: total bin count, $S_{cap}$: storage capacity | m³ |
| 9 | $S_d = \dfrac{S_{cap}}{A_{floor}}$ | $S_d$: storage density, $A_{floor}$: footprint | m³/m² |
| 10 | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{T_h + T_v}{2} + T_f$ | Approx. effective cycle (Chebyshev average) | s |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **strictly penalise** forgetting to convert the throughput from **seconds** to **hours**. The factor is always **3600 s/h**. Many students write "throughput = $1/T_{sc}$" — that gives loads **per second**, which is wrong for board exam answers.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

In modern Industry 4.0 factories, AS/RS is the **physical anchor** of the **Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS)**:
- The WMS (Warehouse Management System) **talks** to the MES (Manufacturing Execution System) using **OPC-UA / MQTT** protocols.
- A **Just-In-Time (JIT)** AS/RS issues the *exact* quantity of raw material to the *exact* CNC machine *exactly* when needed — **zero WIP inventory** on the shop floor.
- **Predictive maintenance** sensors on S/R machines (vibration, current, temperature) push the **MTBF** above **20,000 hours**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Single Command Cycle Time ($T_{sc}$)

A **single command cycle** is defined as the S/R machine performing **either** a storage **or** a retrieval (not both) — i.e., one round trip between the P/D station and one storage location.

**Step 1 — Decompose the motion into orthogonal components:**

The crane starts at the P/D station (origin) and travels to a target bin located at coordinate $(X_i, Y_j, Z_k)$.

- **Horizontal travel distance** along the aisle: $D_x = \vert X_i - X_0 \vert$
- **Vertical travel distance**: $D_y = \vert Z_k - Z_0 \vert$

where $X_0$ and $Z_0$ are the home/P&D coordinates.

**Step 2 — Calculate the time for each axis (assuming constant velocity):**

$$
T_h = \frac{D_x}{V_x}
$$

$$
T_v = \frac{D_y}{V_y}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simultaneous (Chebyshev) travel principle:**

Because the modern S/R crane moves on **two axes simultaneously**, the total **travel time** is **not** $T_h + T_v$, but the **maximum** of the two (longer of the two journeys):

$$
T_t = \max(T_h, \, T_v)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *Justification:* The crane begins both motions at $t=0$ and waits for the **slower** axis to complete its motion. Whichever axis finishes later determines the total travel time.

**Step 4 — Add the fork/extraction dwell time:**

After arriving at the bin, the crane needs time to **extend the fork**, **grip the load**, and **retract the fork**. This fixed overhead is the **fork time** $T_f$.

$$
T_f = T_{ext} + T_{grip} + T_{ret}
$$

Typical values: $T_{ext} = 1.5$ s, $T_{grip} = 1.0$ s, $T_{ret} = 1.5$ s $\Rightarrow T_f \approx 4$ s.

**Step 5 — Add the return trip to the P/D station:**

The S/R machine must return to the P/D station. Assuming a similar Chebyshev return trip:

$$
T_{return} = \max(T_h', \, T_v') = T_t \text{ (by symmetry, average case)}
$$

**Step 6 — Total single command cycle:**

$$
\boxed{T_{sc} = 2 \, T_t + 2 \, T_f}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **2** accounts for the **outbound + return** motion and the **outbound + return** fork operation. This is the most common form tested in KTU ESE.

### 3.2 Alternative Closed-Form (Average Cycle for Random Storage Policy)

When storage locations are accessed **randomly** (no fixed-classification), the expected distances are:

$$
E[D_x] = \dfrac{L}{3}, \quad E[D_y] = \dfrac{H}{3}
$$

where $L$ = aisle length, $H$ = rack height. Substituting:

$$
E[T_{sc}] = 2 \, \max\!\left(\frac{L}{3 V_x}, \, \frac{H}{3 V_y}\right) + 2 T_f
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A unit-load AS/RS has **$L = 80$ m** aisle length and **$H = 20$ m** rack height. Horizontal crane speed $V_x = 2$ m/s, vertical lift speed $V_y = 1$ m/s, fork time $T_f = 4$ s. The crane must retrieve a load from a bin at $X_i = 60$ m, $Z_k = 15$ m (P/D at $X_0 = 0$, $Z_0 = 0$). Calculate the **single command cycle time** and the **throughput in loads/h**.

**Step 1 — Calculate horizontal travel time:**

$$
D_x = \vert 60 - 0 \vert = 60 \text{ m}
$$

$$
T_h = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate vertical travel time:**

$$
D_y = \vert 15 - 0 \vert = 15 \text{ m}
$$

$$
T_v = \frac{15}{1} = 15 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chebyshev travel:**

$$
T_t = \max(30, \, 15) = 30 \text{ s}
$$

> *Interpretation:* The horizontal travel is the bottleneck; the crane waits 15 s after the vertical axis has already reached the target.

**Step 4 — Compute single command cycle:**

$$
T_{sc} = 2(30) + 2(4) = 60 + 8 = 68 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{3600}{T_{sc}} = \frac{3600}{68} \approx 52.94 \text{ loads/h}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU Board Pattern: Always box the final numerical answer. Showing unit cancellation earns half-mark.

### 3.4 Storage Capacity & Density Derivation

**Given:** $N_a$ aisles, $N_b$ bays/aisle, $N_l$ shelf levels, bin volume $V_b$.

$$
N = N_a \times N_b \times N_l
$$

$$
S_{cap} = N \times V_b
$$

$$
S_d = \frac{S_{cap}}{L \times W_{aisle} \times N_a}
$$

**Numerical:** $N_a = 6$, $N_b = 80$, $N_l = 12$, $V_b = 1.2 \text{ m}^3$, aisle width = 1.5 m.

$$
N = 6 \times 80 \times 12 = 5760 \text{ bins}
$$

$$
S_{cap} = 5760 \times 1.2 = 6912 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
S_d = \frac{6912}{80 \times 1.5 \times 6} = \frac{6912}{720} = 9.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **storage density of 9.6 m³/m²** is **4–5× higher** than a manual warehouse, which is why AS/RS is a standard KTU-favoured numerical question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AS/RS

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows the **complete functional flow** of an AS/RS — from the upstream MES (Manufacturing Execution System) down to the physical storage rack — and is the **expected KTU board sketch** for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MES Manufacturing Execution System] --> B[WMS Warehouse Management Software]
    B --> C{ASRS Control Computer PLC}
    C --> D1[Conveyor Interface]
    C --> D2[S R Machine Controller]
    C --> D3[P D Station HMI]
    D1 --> E1[Inbound Conveyor]
    D1 --> E2[Outbound Conveyor]
    D2 --> F1[Horizontal Drive Motor]
    D2 --> F2[Vertical Lift Motor]
    D2 --> F3[Extraction Fork]
    E1 --> G[P D Station Pick Deposit]
    E2 --> G
    G --> H1[Storage Rack Aisle 1]
    G --> H2[Storage Rack Aisle 2]
    G --> H3[Storage Rack Aisle N]
    F3 --> H1
    F3 --> H2
    F3 --> H3
    H1 --> I1[Level 1 to Level 12 Bins]
    H2 --> I2[Level 1 to Level 12 Bins]
    H3 --> I3[Level 1 to Level 12 Bins]
    B -.Inventory Update.-> A
```

### 4.2 Single Command Cycle — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IdlePD
    IdlePD --> TravelOut: Receive Store Command
    TravelOut --> ForkExtract: Reached Target Bin
    ForkExtract --> TravelReturn: Load Extracted
    TravelReturn --> IdlePD: Load Deposited at P D
    TravelOut --> ForkExtract2: Receive Retrieve Command
    ForkExtract2 --> TravelReturn2: Load Picked
    TravelReturn2 --> IdlePD: Load at P D
    IdlePD --> [*]
```

### 4.3 S/R Machine Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Drive Motor Horizontal] --> G1[Gear Reducer]
    G1 --> W1[Wheels along Aisle Floor]
    M2[Drive Motor Vertical] --> G2[Hoist Drum]
    G2 --> C1[Cable and Pulley]
    C1 --> FM[Fork Mast Telescopic]
    FM --> FK[Telescopic Fork Extractor]
    FB[Fork Barrel Rollers] --> FM
    SS1[Position Encoder X axis] --> CTRL[PLC Controller]
    SS2[Position Encoder Y axis] --> CTRL
    SS3[Fork In Out Sensor] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> M1
    CTRL --> M2
    CTRL --> FB
```

### 4.4 Comparative Performance Diagram — AS/RS vs Manual Warehouse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAN[Manual Warehouse]
        MA1[Picker Walks 2 to 4 m s]:::bad
        MA2[Floor space use 35 percent]:::bad
        MA3[Error rate 2 to 3 percent]:::bad
    end
    subgraph ASR[Automated ASRS]
        AS1[Crane Speed 2 m s plus 1 m s]:::good
        AS2[Floor space use 90 percent]:::good
        AS3[Error rate less than 0.01 percent]:::good
    end
    classDef bad fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    classDef good fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600
```

> [!TIP]
> Always label **all** key sub-systems (Conveyor, S/R Machine, P/D Station, Rack, WMS) in your board diagrams. KTU examiners award **2 marks** for a clean, well-labelled block diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define an Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS). List **any four** advantages of AS/RS over a conventional manual warehouse.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An AS/RS is a computer-controlled, often enclosed, combination of equipment (storage racks, S/R machine, conveyors, P/D station) used to automatically store and retrieve materials with minimum human intervention.
- **Advantages (2 marks — any four):**
  1. Higher **storage density** (up to 90% of vertical space used).
  2. Improved **inventory accuracy** (>99.99%).
  3. **Labour cost reduction** (3-shift manual → 1-shift supervised).
  4. **Faster throughput** with deterministic cycle time.
  5. Better **worker safety** (no forklift traffic in aisles).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Unit-Load AS/RS** and **Mini-Load AS/RS** in terms of payload, application, and storage unit.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Unit-Load AS/RS | Mini-Load AS/RS |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload capacity** | **500 – 2000 kg** | **≤ 100 kg** |
| **Storage unit** | Pallet / tote | Tote / carton / bin |
| **Typical application** | Raw materials, finished goods | Spare parts, small components |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

> **(a)** A unit-load AS/RS has aisle length $L = 100$ m, rack height $H = 24$ m. Horizontal crane velocity $V_x = 2.5$ m/s, vertical lift velocity $V_y = 1.2$ m/s, fork time $T_f = 5$ s. Using the **Chebyshev travel principle** and **random storage policy**, compute:
> 1. The expected single command cycle time $E[T_{sc}]$.
> 2. The throughput in **loads per hour**.
> 3. The total storage capacity if each bin has dimensions $1.2 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.0$ m, with **6 aisles**, **100 bays/aisle**, and **12 shelf levels**.

> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of an AS/RS. List **any four** key performance indicators (KPIs) of the system. **[(a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks]**

##### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Expected distances (random storage, 1 mark):**

$$
E[D_x] = \frac{L}{3} = \frac{100}{3} \approx 33.33 \text{ m}
$$

$$
E[D_y] = \frac{H}{3} = \frac{24}{3} = 8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected single-axis travel times (1 mark):**

$$
E[T_h] = \frac{33.33}{2.5} = 13.33 \text{ s}
$$

$$
E[T_v] = \frac{8}{1.2} \approx 6.67 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chebyshev (1 mark):**

$$
E[T_t] = \max(13.33, \, 6.67) = 13.33 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected single command cycle (1 mark):**

$$
E[T_{sc}] = 2 E[T_t] + 2 T_f = 2(13.33) + 2(5) = 26.66 + 10 = 36.66 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{3600}{36.66} \approx 98.2 \text{ loads/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Storage capacity (2 marks):**

$$
N = 6 \times 100 \times 12 = 7200 \text{ bins}
$$

$$
V_b = 1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 1.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
S_{cap} = 7200 \times 1.2 = 8640 \text{ m}^3
$$

##### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** Draw a labelled functional block diagram showing:
**MES → WMS → AS/RS Control PLC → {S/R Machine, Conveyor, P/D Station} → Storage Racks → Feedback to WMS.** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full schematic.)

**Four KPIs (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Throughput** — loads/h
2. **Storage Density** — m³/m²
3. **S/R Machine Cycle Time $T_{sc}$** — seconds
4. **Reliability / MTBF** — hours (typically > 20,000 h)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Students frequently write the **storage density** formula incorrectly as $S_d = S_{cap} / N$ — that gives **m³/bin**, not m³/m². The correct denominator is the **floor footprint** ($L \times W \times N_a$), which **always** carries the aisle count.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain the **classification of AS/RS** with neat sketches. Compare **Carousel systems** and **Vertical Lift Modules (VLMs)** on the basis of mechanism, space efficiency, and typical application.

> **(b)** Derive the **single command cycle time** $T_{sc}$ for an S/R machine operating under the **Chebyshev travel principle**. State the assumptions clearly. **[(a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks]**

##### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification (3 marks):** Enumerate the five major types (Unit-Load, Mini-Load, VLM, Carousel, Cube/Shuttle AS/RS) with one-line definitions.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Carousel System | Vertical Lift Module (VLM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Rotating frame (horizontal or vertical) carries bins past a fixed picker | Lift moves up/down inside a vertical column to bring the correct tray to the picker |
| **Space Efficiency** | Moderate (~50–60%) | High (~70–80%) |
| **Footprint** | Wide horizontal carousel = larger floor footprint | Small floor footprint, taller column |
| **Typical Application** | Small parts, pharma, e-commerce | Tools, slow-movers, machine tooling |

##### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Crane moves on **two orthogonal axes simultaneously** (Y horizontal, Z vertical).
2. **Constant velocity** profile (no acceleration/deceleration accounted for).
3. **Symmetric** outbound and return paths.
4. Fork operation is **non-overlapping** with travel.

**Derivation (5 marks):** The complete derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final boxed equation is:

$$
\boxed{T_{sc} = 2 \, \max\!\left(\frac{\vert D_x \vert}{V_x}, \frac{\vert D_y \vert}{V_y}\right) + 2 T_f}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning — Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write $T_{sc} = T_h + T_v + 2T_f$ — this is the **sequential** (not Chebyshev) form. **KTU 2024 specifically tests the Chebyshev form.**
> 2. The factor **2** in front of $T_f$ and $T_t$ is **not optional** — it represents the **outbound + return** trip. Many students lose **1 full mark** by writing $T_{sc} = T_t + T_f$ (one-way only).
> 3. Always state the **assumptions** explicitly before deriving — KTU board examiners award **2 marks** specifically for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Memorise Before Exam):**

- **AS/RS** = *Storage Racks* + *S/R Machine* + *P/D Station* + *Conveyor/AGV* + *WMS Software*.
- **Five main types:** Unit-Load, Mini-Load, **VLM**, Carousel, Cube/Shuttle.
- **Coordinate system:** Every bin has a unique **(Aisle, Bay, Level)** = (X, Y, Z) identifier.
- **Chebyshev Travel:** $T_t = \max(T_h, T_v)$ — both axes move *simultaneously*.
- **Single Command Cycle (out-and-return):** $T_{sc} = 2 T_t + 2 T_f$.
- **Dual Command Cycle (one trip, store + retrieve):** $T_{dc} = 2 T_{sc}$ (worst case) or $T_{dc} = T_t + T_f$ (best case, perfect pair).
- **Throughput conversion factor:** Always **3600 s/h** — never forget!
- **Random Storage:** $E[D] = L/3$ and $E[H] = H/3$.
- **Storage Capacity:** $N \times V_b$ (in m³).
- **Storage Density:** $S_{cap} / (L \times W \times N_a)$ (in m³/m²).
- **AS/RS benchmarks:** ~**90%** vertical space, >**99.99%** pick accuracy, >**20,000 h** MTBF.
- **Industry 4.0 hook:** AS/RS is the **physical anchor** of the **Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS)**; WMS ↔ MES communication uses **OPC-UA / MQTT**.
- **Key term in KTU syllabus:** *"Simultaneous 2-axis motion of the S/R machine minimises cycle time"* — this is the **Chebyshev principle**, guaranteed to appear in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Robots - Major Configurations and Applications. PLM and Its Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Robots - Major Configurations and Applications. PLM and Its Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Robots — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (per ISO 8373:2021 / KTU Standard):**
> An **Industrial Robot** is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multi-purpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes (degrees of freedom), which can be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications.*

Three keywords govern this definition in every KTU valuation key:

1. **Automatically Controlled** — operates without continuous human intervention (servo-driven, not hand-guided).
2. **Reprogrammable** — its motion trajectories can be rewritten without physically altering the mechanism.
3. **Multi-purpose & ≥ 3 Axes** — distinguishes a robot from a fixed automation machine (e.g., a single-axis transfer line is *not* a robot).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Robot is a Mechanical Human Arm"

Think of an industrial robot as the **steel equivalent of your own arm**:

| Human Body Part | Robot Equivalent | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| Shoulder | Base + first joint | **Base joint (J1)** |
| Upper arm | Vertical link | **Link 1** |
| Elbow | Mid-joint | **Joint 2 (J2)** |
| Forearm | Connecting link | **Link 2** |
| Wrist (pitch/yaw/roll) | Wrist assembly | **Wrist joints (J4–J6)** |
| Palm/fingers | Tool flange | **End Effector (EE)** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as your hand can reach only a *bounded 3D bubble* around your body, a robot's reach is restricted to a calculable **Workspace Volume** — this becomes critical for KTU design problems.

---

## 1.2 Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** is a strategic, integrated, *information-driven* business approach that orchestrates the **people, processes, business systems, and information** across the entire product lifecycle — from *conception* (ideation/requirement capture) through *design*, *manufacturing*, *service*, and finally *retirement/disposal*.

### Conceptual Analogy — "PLM is the Product's Medical File"

Imagine every product carries a **passport that grows with it**:

- 🟢 At **birth** (Conceive) — the passport is opened with the product idea.
- 🔵 During **childhood** (Design) — every CAD model, every dimension, every material decision is stamped on its pages.
- 🟡 At **adolescence** (Manufacture) — its actual birth serial numbers, supplier parts, test results are logged.
- 🟠 In **adulthood** (Service/Use) — every maintenance event, warranty repair, field-failure update is added.
- 🔴 At **end-of-life** (Retire) — recycling instructions, environmental compliance, and lessons learned are filed.

> This *single, versioned, traceable digital passport* — shared across design, shop-floor, sales, and field-service teams — **is PLM**.

---

## 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| **Degrees of Freedom** | **DOF** | **3 to 6** (most KTU exam = **6 DOF**) |
| **Payload (Rated Load)** | $P$ | **3 kg – 1000 kg** |
| **Repeatability** | $R_{rep}$ | **±0.02 mm – ±0.1 mm** |
| **Reach** | $r_{max}$ | **0.5 m – 3.0 m** |
| **Number of Axes** | $n$ | **= DOF for serial robots** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hot Tip:** A robot's **Number of Axes = Number of Independent Servo Joints**, NOT the number of physical links. The most common KTU answer is **6 axes** (3 for positioning + 3 for orientation of the wrist).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Architecture of an Industrial Robot

Every industrial robot — irrespective of configuration — contains **four mandatory sub-systems** (KTU marks will be awarded for naming all four):

1. **Mechanical Structure** — links, joints, bearings, drive mechanisms.
2. **Drive (Actuation) Subsystem** — servo motors, hydraulic actuators, pneumatic cylinders, harmonic drives (RV reducers).
3. **Sensing Subsystem** — encoders (rotary/linear), force/torque sensors, vision systems, proximity switches.
4. **Control Subsystem** — PLC/CNC/PC-based controller executing the kinematic model (forward & inverse kinematics).

### 2.1.1 Joint Types — The Two Primitive Building Blocks

| Joint Type | Symbol | Motion | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | `R` | Rotation about a fixed axis | Elbow, shoulder |
| **Prismatic (P)** | `P` | Linear translation along an axis | Telescopic antenna |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Any industrial robot configuration can be uniquely described by a **joint-type string** read from the **base toward the end-effector**. Example: **RRR** = Articulated; **PPP** = Cartesian gantry.

---

## 2.2 Major Robot Configurations — The KTU 5-Configuration Table

The five configurations below appear in **≥ 90% of KTU questions** on this module. Memorize the configuration string, the workspace shape, and at least two applications.

### 2.2.1 Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — `PPP`

- **Structure:** Three mutually perpendicular prismatic joints.
- **Workspace:** **Rectangular cuboid** (box-shaped volume).
- **KTU Pros/Cons:**
  - ✅ Highest rigidity, best accuracy, easiest programming.
  - ❌ Requires large floor/ceiling space; only translational DOF.

### 2.2.2 Cylindrical Robot — `RPP`

- **Structure:** One revolute (base rotation) + two prismatic (vertical lift + radial reach).
- **Workspace:** **Hollow cylindrical** volume (like a thick-walled pipe standing up).

### 2.2.3 Spherical (Polar) Robot — `RRP`

- **Structure:** Two revolute + one prismatic.
- **Workspace:** **Spherical sector** (portion of a ball).

### 2.2.4 SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) — `RRR` (vertical axis)

- **Structure:** Two parallel revolute joints for horizontal motion + one prismatic for vertical, with a *passive* compliance joint.
- **Workspace:** **Circular cylindrical**.
- **Key feature:** Rigid in the vertical (Z) direction but **compliant** in horizontal (X-Y) — perfect for *vertical insertion* tasks (PCB assembly).

### 2.2.5 Articulated (Revolute / Jointed-Arm) Robot — `RRR(RRR)`

- **Structure:** All revolute joints. Typically 6 in total.
- **Workspace:** **Quasi-spherical** (irregular blob-like envelope, closest to a human arm's reach).
- **Most flexible configuration** — used in welding, painting, palletizing.

### 2.2.6 Parallel (Delta) Robot — Bonus Configuration

- **Structure:** A moving platform suspended by **3 to 6 parallel limbs** (each limb = R + P).
- **Workspace:** A **flat cylindrical dome** above the base.
- **Used for:** Ultra-high-speed pick-and-place (e.g., chocolate packing at >120 picks/min).

---

## 2.3 The Master Configuration Comparison Table (KTU Favourite)

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the pipe `|` symbol inside table cells** — KTU expects you to reproduce this in the answer script. Use `\vert` or write "absolute value" verbally.

| Config | Joint String | Workspace Shape | DOF | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian** | PPP | Rectangular cuboid | 3 | CNC loading, pick-place |
| **Cylindrical** | RPP | Hollow cylinder | 3 | Die casting, machine tending |
| **Spherical/Polar** | RRP | Spherical sector | 3 | Forging, arc welding (older) |
| **SCARA** | RRP (compliant) | Flat circular disc | 4 | PCB assembly, screw insertion |
| **Articulated** | RRR (RRR) | Irregular sphere | 6 | Welding, painting, palletizing |
| **Parallel/Delta** | 3 or 6 parallel limbs | Dome (flat) | 3 or 6 | High-speed packaging |

---

## 2.4 Industrial Robot Applications — The KTU Six-Domain Classification

1. **Material Handling & Palletizing** — pick-and-place, loading CNC, palletizing boxes.
2. **Welding Operations** — spot welding (automotive), arc welding (fabrication).
3. **Painting & Coating** — auto-body spray paint (always articulated with hollow wrist).
4. **Assembly Operations** — screwing, inserting, snap-fitting (SCARA dominates).
5. **Machining & Processing** — drilling, deburring, waterjet cutting, polishing.
6. **Inspection & Quality Control** — vision-guided gauging, dimensional measurement.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** **"H-W-P-A-M-I"** → **H**andling, **W**elding, **P**ainting, **A**ssembly, **M**achining, **I**nspection.

---

## 2.5 PLM — Theoretical Framework

### 2.5.1 The Four Pillars of PLM (KTU 2024 Standard)

| Pillar | What It Contains | KTU Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | Cross-functional teams (Design, Mfg, Sales, Service) | Project portals |
| **Process** | Stage-Gate, APQP, Lean workflows | BPM software |
| **Product (Information)** | 3D CAD, BOM, specifications, documents | PDM, Teamcenter |
| **Systems (Technology)** | PLM software stack + ERP + MES integration | SAP PLM, PTC Windchill |

### 2.5.2 PLM Lifecycle Phases

The standard KTU lifecycle has **5 (or 7) phases**:

```
Conceive → Design → Manufacture → Service → Retire
   (BPL)  →(EPCD)  →(MP)       →(SO)    →(DR)
```

Where: **BPL** = Business Planning & Launch, **EPCD** = Engineering Product & Component Design, **MP** = Manufacturing Production, **SO** = Service Operations, **DR** = Disposal & Recycle.

### 2.5.3 PLM vs. PDM vs. ERP — The KTU Distinction

| System | Scope | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| **PDM** (Product Data Management) | CAD files, BOM, revisions | Design/Engineering |
| **PLM** | Whole product lifecycle (uses PDM inside) | Multi-department |
| **ERP** (Enterprise Resource Planning) | Finance, procurement, supply chain | Business/Operations |
| **MES** (Manufacturing Execution System) | Real-time shop-floor control | Production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLM ⊃ PDM.** PLM *contains* PDM as its data backbone but extends it to manufacturing, service, and disposal. This is a **2-mark differentiator** question in every KTU exam.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degrees of Freedom (serial robot)** | $DOF = n$ | integer |
| 2 | **Workspace volume (Cartesian)** | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 3 | **Cylindrical robot reach (radial)** | $r_{min} \le r \le r_{max}$ | m |
| 4 | **Spherical robot reach (radial)** | $r_{min} \le \rho \le r_{max}$ | m |
| 5 | **Repeatability error** | $R_{rep} = \pm 3\sigma$ | mm |
| 6 | **Payload rating equation (static)** | $\tau_i = \sum_{j=i}^{n} m_j g \, L_{ij}$ | N·m |
| 7 | **PLM cost-of-quality (CoQ) reduction** | $\Delta CoQ = CoQ_{before} - CoQ_{after}$ | currency |
| 8 | **ROI on PLM (years)** | $T_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{PLM}}{S_{annual}}$ | years |
| 9 | **Kinematic chain degrees (Kutzbach/Gruebler)** | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | integer |
| 10 | **Resolving power at end-effector** | $\delta_e = \dfrac{e_{encoder} \cdot r_{gear}}{N_{bits}}$ | rad/pulse |

> **Notation used:** $n$ = number of links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF lower pairs, $j_2$ = 2-DOF lower pairs, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $L_{ij}$ = moment arm from joint $i$ to mass $j$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Workspace Volume of a Cartesian Robot

A Cartesian robot has three independent prismatic joints with strokes $L_x$, $L_y$, and $L_z$ along the X, Y, and Z axes respectively. The **end-effector can occupy any point $(x, y, z)$** within the Cartesian product of these three ranges.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: } 0 \le x \le L_x, \quad 0 \le y \le L_y, \quad 0 \le z \le L_z \\
&\text{Therefore the workspace is the 3-D box formed by:} \\
&\qquad W_{cartesian} = \left\{ (x, y, z) \;\big\vert\; 0 \le x \le L_x,\; 0 \le y \le L_y,\; 0 \le z \le L_z \right\} \\
&\text{Volume:} \\
&\qquad V_{box} = \int_{0}^{L_x}\int_{0}^{L_y}\int_{0}^{L_z} dz\,dy\,dx \\
&\qquad \phantom{V_{box}} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z \\
&\text{Hence, } \boxed{V_{box} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z \;\; [\text{m}^3]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The volume grows *linearly* with each axis stroke. Doubling any one stroke doubles the workspace — useful when sizing a machine-tending cell.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workspace Volume of a Spherical (Polar) Robot

A spherical robot has a radial stroke $\rho$ (prismatic), a vertical (elevation) angle $\phi$, and a horizontal (azimuth) rotation $\theta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Spherical coordinates: } \\
&\qquad x = \rho \sin\phi \cos\theta \\
&\qquad y = \rho \sin\phi \sin\theta \\
&\qquad z = \rho \cos\phi \\
&\text{Bounds: } \rho_{min} \le \rho \le \rho_{max}, \quad 0 \le \phi \le \phi_{max}, \quad 0 \le \theta \le 2\pi \\
&\text{Jacobian determinant of (}\rho, \phi, \theta\text{) transformation:} \\
&\qquad J = \rho^{2} \sin\phi \\
&\text{Workspace volume:} \\
&\qquad V_{sphere} = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\phi_{max}}\!\!\int_{\rho_{min}}^{\rho_{max}} \rho^{2}\sin\phi\; d\rho\, d\phi\, d\theta \\
&\qquad \phantom{V_{sphere}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \cdot \int_{0}^{\phi_{max}} \sin\phi\, d\phi \cdot \int_{\rho_{min}}^{\rho_{max}} \rho^{2}\, d\rho \\
&\qquad \phantom{V_{sphere}} = 2\pi \cdot \big[-\cos\phi\big]_{0}^{\phi_{max}} \cdot \left[\frac{\rho^{3}}{3}\right]_{\rho_{min}}^{\rho_{max}} \\
&\qquad \phantom{V_{sphere}} = \frac{2\pi}{3}\,(1 - \cos\phi_{max})\,(\rho_{max}^{3} - \rho_{min}^{3}) \\
&\text{Hence, } \boxed{V_{sphere} = \frac{2\pi}{3}\,(1 - \cos\phi_{max})\,(\rho_{max}^{3} - \rho_{min}^{3}) \;\; [\text{m}^3]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** If $\phi_{max} = \pi$ (full hemisphere) and $\rho_{min} = 0$, we recover the well-known hemisphere volume $\frac{2}{3}\pi \rho_{max}^{3}$. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kutzbach–Gruebler DOF Formula (Planar)

For a planar mechanism with $n$ links (excluding ground) and $j$ 1-DOF joints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Kutzbach equation (planar):} \\
&\qquad M = 3(n-1) - 2j \\
&\text{Apply to a 4-bar linkage: } n = 3 \text{ moving links, } j = 4 \text{ revolute joints.} \\
&\qquad M = 3(3-1) - 2(4) = 3(2) - 8 = 6 - 8 = -2 \\
&\text{Negative } \Rightarrow \text{ statically indeterminate (a structure, not mechanism).} \\
&\text{Apply to a slider-crank: } n = 3 \text{ links, } j = 4 \text{ (3R + 1P).} \\
&\qquad M = 3(3-1) - 2(4) = 6 - 8 = -2 \\
&\text{Reduce one link: actually } n=3 \text{ moving links, joint 1 is the slider pair (P) which counts as 2 constraints.} \\
&\text{Re-corrected with proper identification: slider-crank DOF} = 1 \;\; \text{(standard result).} \\
&\text{Hence, } \boxed{M_{planar} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \;\; \text{(integer)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **spatial mechanism**, KTU uses the spatial form: $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$. A 6-DOF articulated robot must satisfy this with the right number of links/joints.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Robot Payload Torque on Base Joint (Static)

For a 6-DOF articulated robot, the static torque on the base joint (J1) supporting all subsequent links and payload is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let masses: } m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n \text{ (links) and } m_p \text{ (payload).} \\
&\text{Moment arms from J1: } r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n, r_p. \\
&\text{In worst-case (arm fully extended horizontally, gravity } g \text{):} \\
&\qquad \tau_{J1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g\, r_i + m_p g\, r_p \\
&\text{This must be } \le \tau_{rated,J1} \text{ for safe operation.} \\
&\text{With } m_p = 50 \text{ kg, } r_p = 1.5 \text{ m, and link equivalent mass } M_{eq} = 80 \text{ kg at } \bar{r} = 0.6 \text{ m, } g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2\text{:} \\
&\qquad \tau_{J1} = (80)(9.81)(0.6) + (50)(9.81)(1.5) \\
&\qquad \phantom{\tau_{J1}} = 471.0 + 735.8 \\
&\qquad \phantom{\tau_{J1}} = 1206.8 \;\text{N·m} \\
&\text{Hence, } \boxed{\tau_{J1} = g \!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i + m_p r_p\right) \;\; [\text{N·m}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — PLM Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } C_{PLM} = \text{total cost of PLM software + implementation.} \\
&\text{Let } S_{annual} = \text{annual savings from PLM (reduced rework, faster NPI, lower warranty).} \\
&\text{Payback period:} \\
&\qquad T_{payback} = \frac{C_{PLM}}{S_{annual}} \;\; [\text{years}] \\
&\text{Net Present Value (NPV) over N years at discount rate } r\text{:} \\
&\qquad NPV = -C_{PLM} + \sum_{t=1}^{N}\frac{S_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} \\
&\text{Decision rule: Accept PLM if } NPV > 0 \text{ and } T_{payback} < T_{threshold} \text{ (typically 3–5 years).} \\
&\text{Hence, } \boxed{NPV = -C_{PLM} + \sum_{t=1}^{N}\frac{S_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Selection Criteria

| Selection Criterion | Cartesian | Cylindrical | Spherical | SCARA | Articulated | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Payload** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low | High | Low |
| **Reach** | Limited by cell size | Medium | Medium | Small | Large | Small/flat |
| **Accuracy** | Highest | High | Medium | High (in plane) | High | Medium |
| **Speed** | Slow | Medium | Medium | Fast | Medium | Fastest |
| **Footprint** | Large | Medium | Medium | Small | Small | Smallest |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| **Best Application** | CNC tending | Die casting | Welding | Assembly | Welding/Paint | Packaging |
| **Recommended DOF** | 3 | 3 | 3 | 4 | 6 | 3–4 |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Required Robot Configuration for a Given Task

> **Problem:** A factory needs a robot to load 5 kg engine blocks into a vertical CNC chuck from a conveyor belt located **1.2 m horizontally and 0.4 m vertically below the chuck centerline**. The robot will work in a **clean aisle with limited floor space**, cycle time **≤ 6 seconds per load**, and the **insertion must be vertical (Z-axis only).** Recommend the optimal configuration and compute the minimum reach.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: payload } m = 5 \text{ kg, reach } r = \sqrt{1.2^2 + 0.4^2} \text{ m.} \\
&\text{Step 1 — Compute reach: } \\
&\qquad r = \sqrt{(1.2)^2 + (0.4)^2} = \sqrt{1.44 + 0.16} = \sqrt{1.60} \\
&\qquad r \approx 1.265 \text{ m} \\
&\text{Step 2 — Add safety margin (10\%)}: \\
&\qquad r_{min} = 1.265 \times 1.10 = 1.391 \text{ m} \\
&\text{Step 3 — Configuration selection matrix:}\\
&\qquad \text{(a) Payload 5 kg } \Rightarrow \text{all configs OK except very small delta robots.} \\
&\qquad \text{(b) Vertical insertion (Z-only)} \Rightarrow \text{SCARA is IDEAL (rigid in Z).} \\
&\qquad \text{(c) Limited floor space} \Rightarrow \text{Cartesian/SCARA preferred over cylindrical.} \\
&\qquad \text{(d) Cycle } \le 6 \text{ s} \Rightarrow \text{SCARA cycle times are 0.4–0.8 s for 1 kg; OK for 5 kg.} \\
&\text{Step 4 — Decision: } \boxed{\text{SCARA robot with reach } r_{min} \approx 1.4 \text{ m and payload } \ge 5 \text{ kg}.}
\end{aligned}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks

> Define an industrial robot as per ISO 8373. List any **four essential characteristics** that distinguish a robot from a conventional fixed automation machine.

**Model Answer:**

An **industrial robot**, per **ISO 8373**, is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multi-purpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes*, used in industrial automation.

Four distinguishing characteristics:

1. **Automatic control** — operates via programmed instructions without continuous human guidance.
2. **Reprogrammability** — task sequences can be altered through software, not by mechanical retooling.
3. **Multi-purpose versatility** — the same robot can perform different tasks (welding → painting → assembly) by changing the end-effector.
4. **Multi-axis capability** — provides **≥ 3 degrees of freedom** (typically 6 for full spatial positioning + orientation).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–1 mark):** Writing "multi-purpose" *without* mentioning **"reprogrammable"** or **"≥ 3 axes"**. Both keywords are **mandatory** in the ISO definition.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks

> What is **PLM**? Differentiate between **PLM and PDM** in one sentence each.

**Model Answer:**

**Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** is a strategic business approach that integrates people, processes, and information systems to manage a product from conception through retirement across the entire enterprise.

**Difference in one line each:**

- **PDM** (Product Data Management) → Manages the **engineering data** (CAD, BOM, revisions) primarily during the **design phase**.
- **PLM** → Extends PDM across the **full product lifecycle**, integrating manufacturing, service, and end-of-life information across the enterprise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–2 marks):** Many students write "PLM is a software" — this loses marks. PLM is a **business strategy/process** supported by software tools.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

#### (a) With neat sketches, explain the **FIVE major configurations of industrial robots**. Compare their **workspace shapes, joint types, DOF, and suitable applications** in a single comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five major configurations of industrial robots, classified by joint arrangement and resulting workspace geometry, are:

**1. Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — Joint String: P-P-P**

- **Structure:** Three mutually perpendicular prismatic (linear) joints, one each along X, Y, Z axes.
- **Workspace:** A rectangular cuboid (box-shaped envelope).
- **DOF:** 3 (positioning only; orientation via wrist if added).
- **Application:** CNC machine loading/unloading, pick-and-place from conveyor, 3D printing, pallet stacking in a fixed grid.

**2. Cylindrical Robot — Joint String: R-P-P**

- **Structure:** One revolute base joint (rotation about vertical axis), one prismatic for radial reach, one prismatic for vertical lift.
- **Workspace:** A hollow cylindrical volume (like a thick-walled pipe standing upright).
- **DOF:** 3.
- **Application:** Die-casting unload, injection-moulding machine tending, spot-welding simple jigs.

**3. Spherical (Polar) Robot — Joint String: R-R-P**

- **Structure:** Base rotation (azimuth), shoulder pivot (elevation angle), and one prismatic radial extension.
- **Workspace:** A spherical sector — portion of a hollow ball.
- **DOF:** 3.
- **Application:** Forging, heat-resistant furnace tending (older Unimate-style lines), gas tungsten arc welding on large fabrications.

**4. SCARA Robot — Joint String: R-R-P (with selective compliance)**

- **Structure:** Two parallel revolute joints for planar X-Y motion; one prismatic for vertical Z motion; a passive compliance mechanism in X-Y.
- **Workspace:** A flat circular disc (cylindrical with small height).
- **DOF:** 4 (3 + Z rotation).
- **Application:** Vertical insertion tasks — PCB component placement, screw insertion, small-parts assembly at high speed.

**5. Articulated (Jointed-Arm / Revolute) Robot — Joint String: R-R-R (R-R-R)**

- **Structure:** All revolute joints — base yaw, shoulder pitch, elbow pitch, plus 3 wrist joints (roll-pitch-yaw).
- **Workspace:** A quasi-spherical irregular envelope (closest to the human arm).
- **DOF:** 6.
- **Application:** Arc welding, spot welding, painting (with hollow wrist for hose routing), palletizing, complex assembly, machine tending.

**6. (Optional Bonus) Parallel / Delta Robot**

- **Structure:** A moving top platform suspended by 3 (or 6) parallel limbs, each limb = R + P.
- **Workspace:** A flat dome above the base.
- **DOF:** 3 translational (or 6 with rotational variants).
- **Application:** Ultra-high-speed packaging (>120 picks/min in food/pharma industry).

**Master Comparison Table:**

| Config | Joints | Workspace | DOF | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | PPP | Rectangular box | 3 | CNC loading |
| Cylindrical | RPP | Hollow cylinder | 3 | Die casting |
| Spherical | RRP | Spherical sector | 3 | Forging |
| SCARA | RRP | Flat disc | 4 | PCB assembly |
| Articulated | RRR-RRR | Irregular sphere | 6 | Welding/paint |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–2 marks):** Writing joint string **without capital letters** (e.g., "rpp" instead of "RPP") or omitting the **wrist joints** in articulated configuration. KTU strictly expects **6 joints = 6 DOF** in articulated robots.

---

#### (b) Define **PLM**. List and briefly explain the **FIVE phases of the product lifecycle** as adopted by KTU 2024. Also write **three benefits** of implementing PLM in an Indian manufacturing SME. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of PLM:**

> **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** is an integrated, information-driven business strategy that manages all product-related data and processes across the entire product lifecycle — from initial concept through design, manufacture, service, and retirement — by coordinating people, processes, business systems, and information.

**The Five Phases of the Product Lifecycle (KTU 2024 Standard):**

1. **Conceive (Idea / Business Planning Phase)**
   - Market need identification, feasibility study, project approval, high-level concept generation.
   - Outputs: market requirements document (MRD), project charter, preliminary BOM.

2. **Design (Engineering Product & Component Design Phase)**
   - Detailed CAD modelling, CAE simulation, prototype manufacture, design validation.
   - Outputs: 3D models, detailed drawings, engineering BOM (EBOM), DFMEA.

3. **Manufacture (Production Phase)**
   - Process planning, tooling design, CNC program generation, assembly line setup, quality inspection.
   - Outputs: manufacturing BOM (MBOM), routings, work instructions, as-built data.

4. **Service (Operations & After-Sales Phase)**
   - Installation, commissioning, customer support, warranty claims, field service, predictive maintenance.
   - Outputs: service BOM (SBOM), maintenance logs, RMA records, service bulletins.

5. **Retire (End-of-Life / Disposal Phase)**
   - Product recall management, recycling, remanufacturing, environmental compliance, lessons-learned archival.
   - Outputs: disposal records, environmental compliance reports, knowledge base updates for next-generation products.

**Three Benefits for an Indian Manufacturing SME:**

1. **Reduction in Time-to-Market:** PLM parallelizes design with manufacturing planning, often reducing the New Product Introduction (NPI) cycle by **20–40 %**, critical for Indian MSMEs competing on agility.

2. **Lower Cost of Quality (CoQ):** A single source of truth for BOM eliminates rework caused by drawing/BOM mismatches. Studies (CIMdata, Aberdeen) show PLM reduces engineering change orders by **30 %**, saving both material and labour cost.

3. **Regulatory & Traceability Compliance:** With GST-era digital record-keeping and growing BIS/AIS compliance needs, PLM provides **versioned, audit-ready documentation** across the entire lifecycle — essential for auto-components and pharma exports.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–2 marks):** Confusing **PLM with ERP**. PLM manages the *product* (design → retire). ERP manages the *business* (finance, procurement, payroll). They *integrate*, but are **not the same**.

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

#### (a) Explain in detail the **anatomy of an industrial robot**, identifying all **four major sub-systems** and listing the **types of joints, drives, and sensors** commonly used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An industrial robot, despite configuration differences, is composed of **four interconnected sub-systems**:

**1. Mechanical Structure (Manipulator)**

- Comprises **rigid links** connected by **joints** (axes of motion).
- **Joint types:**
  - **Revolute (R):** rotation about a fixed axis (most common).
  - **Prismatic (P):** linear sliding along an axis.
  - **Helical/Screw:** rare; used in lead-screw actuator joints.
- Link materials: cast steel (heavy), cast aluminium (medium), carbon-fibre composites (high-speed delta arms).

**2. Drive (Actuation) Sub-system**

- **Electric Servo Drives (AC brushless):** most common in modern robots; paired with harmonic or RV (cycloidal) reducers. Used in 6-axis articulated arms.
- **Hydraulic Actuators:** used for very high payloads (>500 kg), e.g., forging robots. Provide high power-to-weight ratio.
- **Pneumatic Actuators:** low-cost, limited to pick-and-place or simple 3-DOF applications.
- **Reducer types:**
  - **Harmonic Drive:** high reduction ratio (50:1 to 320:1), zero backlash, used in wrist joints.
  - **RV Reducer (Cycloidal):** high torque capacity, robust, used in base/shoulder/elbow.

**3. Sensing Sub-system**

- **Encoders (rotary/linear):** measure joint angles with 17–23 bit resolution.
- **Force/Torque Sensors:** mounted at wrist for assembly force-feedback control.
- **Vision Systems (2D/3D cameras):** guide bin-picking and weld-seam tracking.
- **Proximity & Limit Switches:** home-position safety.
- **Tactile Sensors:** on gripper fingers for delicate part handling.

**4. Control Sub-system**

- **Controller architecture:**
  - **PLC-based** for simple pick-place.
  - **CNC/PC-based controllers** for 6-axis arms (e.g., FANUC R-30iB, ABB IRC5, KUKA KR-C5).
- **Functions:**
  - **Forward kinematics:** joint angles → Cartesian pose.
  - **Inverse kinematics:** Cartesian pose → joint angles.
  - **Trajectory interpolation:** linear, circular, splined paths between taught points.
  - **HMI via Teach Pendant:** manual lead-through or pose-by-pose programming.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–2 marks):** Listing **only** the mechanical structure and controller — *missing drives and sensors*. KTU strictly requires **all four sub-systems** for full 7 marks.

---

#### (b) Discuss the **step-by-step implementation roadmap for PLM** in a manufacturing organization. List at least **four common challenges** faced during PLM implementation and how each is mitigated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**PLM Implementation Roadmap — Seven Stages (KTU Recommended):**

| Stage | Activity | Duration (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Strategy & Vision** | Define PLM goals, ROI targets, executive sponsorship | 1–2 months |
| **2. Process Mapping** | Document current as-is workflows; design to-be | 2–3 months |
| **3. Data Cleanup & Standardization** | Harmonize part numbering, BOM structure, file-naming | 3–6 months |
| **4. Tool Selection** | Evaluate (PTC Windchill, Siemens Teamcenter, Dassault Enovia) | 1–3 months |
| **5. Pilot Rollout** | Deploy on one product line/factory as proof-of-concept | 6–9 months |
| **6. Enterprise-wide Rollout** | Phased rollout across all divisions | 12–18 months |
| **7. Continuous Improvement** | KPI tracking, user training, system upgrades | Ongoing |

**Four Common Challenges and Mitigations:**

1. **Resistance to Change (People Challenge)**
   - *Symptom:* Engineers reluctant to migrate from legacy CAD folders to a centralized vault.
   - *Mitigation:* Top-management mandate + incentive-linked adoption KPIs + champion-based training.

2. **Poor Data Quality (Information Challenge)**
   - *Symptom:* Legacy BOMs contain duplicate part numbers, inconsistent revisions.
   - *Mitigation:* Mandatory **data cleansing phase** before go-live; introduce **part-numbering policy** and **classification scheme** (e.g., GTIN-based).

3. **Integration Complexity with ERP/MES (System Challenge)**
   - *Symptom:* BOM/Item master conflicts between PLM and SAP/Oracle ERP.
   - *Mitigation:* Define **clear system-of-record boundaries** — PLM owns design data, ERP owns commercial data. Use certified **middleware/integration platform**.

4. **High Implementation Cost & ROI Uncertainty (Financial Challenge)**
   - *Symptom:* Capital expenditure of ₹2–10 Cr for a mid-sized enterprise with unclear payback.
   - *Mitigation:* Adopt **phased SaaS PLM** (e.g., cloud-based Arena, Autodesk Fusion Manage) for lower upfront cost; track **KPI dashboard** (engineering-change-order cycle time, NPI duration, scrap rate).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (–1 mark):** Writing only the **technical** steps (install software → train users → go live) and **missing the people/process strategy** stages. KTU expects the **business-side** approach (Stage-1 strategy → Stage-2 process mapping).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read 3 Times Before Exam**

- [ ] **ISO 8373 Definition:** *Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multi-purpose, ≥ 3 axes*.
- [ ] **Four Sub-systems of any robot:** Mechanical, Drive, Sensing, Control.
- [ ] **Two joint primitives:** Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P).
- [ ] **Five configurations:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), SCARA (RRP-compliant), Articulated (RRR-RRR).
- [ ] **Workspace shapes in order:** Box → Hollow cylinder → Spherical sector → Flat disc → Irregular sphere.
- [ ] **Most common industrial robot:** Articulated 6-DOF.
- [ ] **Fastest robot:** Delta (parallel).
- [ ] **Best for PCB assembly:** SCARA.
- [ ] **Best for paint:** Articulated with hollow wrist (so hose routing doesn't snag).
- [ ] **DOF for serial robot = number of joints**; verify via Kutzbach–Gruebler formula.
- [ ] **PLM = Strategy, not software.** PDM ⊂ PLM ⊂ Enterprise system landscape.
- [ ] **Five PLM phases:** Conceive → Design → Manufacture → Service → Retire.
- [ ] **Four pillars of PLM:** People, Process, Product (Information), Systems (Technology).
- [ ] **PLM Implementation:** 7-stage roadmap, pilot-first approach.
- [ ] **Key PLM KPIs:** Time-to-market reduction, engineering-change-order (ECO) cycle time, scrap/rework cost, BOM accuracy %.
- [ ] **Mnemonics to retain:**
  - Configurations → **CCSSA** (Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, SCARA, Articulated)
  - Robot applications → **H-W-P-A-M-I**
  - PLM phases → **C-D-M-S-R** (Conceive, Design, Manufacture, Service, Retire)
  - PLM pillars → **3P + S** (People, Process, Product, Systems)
- [ ] **Numerical sanity:** Workspace volume of Cartesian = $L_x L_y L_z$; Spherical = $\frac{2\pi}{3}(1 - \cos\phi_{max})(\rho_{max}^{3} - \rho_{min}^{3})$.
- [ ] **Payback Period for PLM:** $T_{payback} = C_{PLM}/S_{annual}$ — typical acceptable threshold = **3–5 years**.
- [ ] **Examiner red flags to avoid:**
  - Don't write "PLM is a software."
  - Don't confuse PLM with ERP.
  - Don't claim articulated robot has 3 DOF — it has **6**.
  - Don't omit joint types (R/P) in configuration diagrams.
  - Don't use the phrase "robot is a machine" without elaborating *reprogrammability* and *multi-purpose* features.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Industrial Robots & PLM**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND DIGITAL MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
