# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is a 3-mark short-answer — KTU expects **definition + 2 application points** (or 1 short derivation) within **60–80 words**.

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define Industry 4.0. List any two key enabling technologies.**

**Model Answer:**

**Industry 4.0** is the fourth industrial revolution characterized by the automation and data exchange in manufacturing technologies, integrating **cyber-physical systems (CPS), the Internet of Things (IoT), cloud computing, big data analytics, and artificial intelligence** to create smart, self-organizing factories.

**Two enabling technologies:**
1. **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Connects machines, sensors, and controllers to enable real-time data exchange and remote monitoring of shop-floor equipment.
2. **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):** Embedded systems that integrate physical processes with computation, networking, and actuation (e.g., a modern smart CNC controller such as Siemens SINUMERIK ONE).

*(Word count: 72 — within KTU 3-mark limit)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Distinguish between Industry 3.0 and Industry 4.0 in terms of automation and decision-making.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Industry 3.0 | Industry 4.0 |
|---|---|---|
| **Automation** | Programmed automation (PLC, CNC) — fixed sequences | Smart, adaptive, self-optimizing automation |
| **Control** | Centralized (SCADA, PLC) | Decentralized (CPS + Edge AI) |
| **Decision-Making** | Human-driven, reactive | AI-driven, predictive and prescriptive |
| **Data** | Batch, offline | Real-time, continuous streaming via IIoT |
| **Connectivity** | Standalone machines, ERP integration | Fully connected value chain (5G, OPC UA) |

In essence, **Industry 3.0 automated the *body*** (mechanization of work), while **Industry 4.0 automates the *brain*** (decision-making and self-optimization).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Pattern:** Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**. KTU requires *both* theory (definitions, diagrams) *and* numerical/analytical depth.

---

### **Q3(A).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] 

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **nine key enabling technologies (pillars)** of Industry 4.0. How do they collectively transform a conventional machine shop into a smart factory?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A smart CNC cell operates for **one shift of 8 hours (480 min)**. Sensor data shows: **downtime = 60 min, ideal cycle time = 0.8 min/piece, parts produced = 500, good parts = 470.** Calculate **OEE** and recommend one Industry 4.0 intervention for the biggest loss category.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q3(A)(a):**

**Introduction:**
Industry 4.0 rests on **nine enabling technologies (WEF 2016)** that integrate physical, digital, and biological systems to create smart, self-organizing manufacturing ecosystems.

**The Nine Pillars:**

1. **Big Data & Analytics:** Massive structured/unstructured datasets from shop-floor sensors (vibration, thermal, acoustic) are processed using **descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive** analytics. Used for **predictive maintenance, demand forecasting, and process optimization**.

2. **Autonomous Robots:** Self-learning, flexible robots that perform tasks without human intervention. **Cobots** (e.g., KUKA LBR iiwa) work safely alongside humans. Applications: machine tending, welding, assembly, bin-picking.

3. **Simulation (Digital Twin Foundation):** 3D virtual replicas of products, processes, or factories used for **virtual commissioning, operator training, and what-if analysis**. Examples: Siemens Tecnomatix, Dassault DELMIA.

4. **Horizontal & Vertical System Integration:** Seamless data flow across the value chain — **horizontal** (supplier ↔ manufacturer ↔ distributor) and **vertical** (sensors ↔ PLC ↔ MES ↔ ERP). Eliminates data silos.

5. **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT):** A network of connected sensors, devices, and machines exchanging data over the internet using **OPC UA, MQTT, HTTP** protocols. Enables real-time telemetry and OEE tracking.

6. **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS):** Embedded systems with tight physical-computational integration forming a **sense-compute-communicate-act** feedback loop. Every modern smart CNC is a CPS.

7. **Cybersecurity:** With increased connectivity comes increased attack surface. **IEC 62443** standardizes protection of industrial networks. Critical for plant safety.

8. **Cloud Computing:** On-demand, scalable compute/storage for **centralized analytics, global factory aggregation, and AI model training**. Migration: Cloud → Fog → Edge.

9. **Additive Manufacturing (AM):** Layer-by-layer 3D printing (FDM, SLM, DMLS) enabling **mass customization, lightweight structures, and on-demand spare parts**.

**Transformation of a Conventional Machine Shop into a Smart Factory:**
- **Before:** Standalone CNC machines, paper-based scheduling, reactive maintenance.
- **After:** All machines networked via IIoT → data flows to MES → AI predicts tool wear → cobots auto-load parts → digital twin simulates new jobs before physical tryout → OEE pushed to >85%.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q3(A)(b):**

**Given:** $T_p = 480\ \text{min}$, $T_d = 60\ \text{min}$, $T_c = 0.8\ \text{min/piece}$, $N_t = 500$, $N_g = 470$.

**Step 1: Operating Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_o &= 480 - 60 = 420\ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Performance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{0.8 \times 500}{420} = \dfrac{400}{420} = 0.9524 = 95.24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{470}{500} = 0.94 = 94.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: OEE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= 0.875 \times 0.9524 \times 0.94 = 0.7834 = 78.34\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** OEE of **78.34%** lies in the *typical* band. The **biggest loss is Availability (87.5%)** — the machine spent **12.5%** of its time idle or down.

**Recommended Industry 4.0 Intervention:**
- Deploy **IIoT-enabled predictive maintenance** using vibration and thermal sensors on spindle and ballscrews, integrated with **AI-based anomaly detection** on the Edge. This will reduce unplanned downtime by **30–50%** and lift OEE above the 85% world-class benchmark.

---

### **Q3(B). Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(If Part (a) of Q3(A) is asked, this is the alternate choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **four design principles of Industry 4.0** as outlined in the Hannover Messe framework. Illustrate each with one manufacturing example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CNC turning machine has recorded **3 failures** in **6 months**, with **total uptime = 4320 hours** and **total repair time = 18 hours**. Compute **MTBF, MTTR, steady-state availability**, and the **predictive maintenance trigger threshold** (80% of MTBF).

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q3(B)(a):**

The four design principles of Industry 4.0 (**Hannover Messe 2011**) define the *behavior* expected from a smart, connected factory.

1. **Interoperability:** The ability of machines, devices, sensors, and people to **connect and communicate** with each other seamlessly.
   - *Example:* A **DMG MORI CELOS** controller communicating with a **Siemens MES** via the **OPC UA** protocol, sharing cycle time, tool offsets, and alarms in real time.

2. **Information Transparency:** Massive volumes of sensor data are **collected, aggregated, and visualized** to create a digital model of the physical world.
   - *Example:* Vibration, acoustic, and temperature sensors on a CNC spindle streaming data to a **Power BI dashboard**, displaying the real-time health of all 50 machines on the shop floor.

3. **Technical Assistance:** Smart systems **assist humans** in decision-making, problem-solving, and physically demanding tasks.
   - *Example:* A maintenance engineer wearing **AR glasses (Microsoft HoloLens)** sees **digital work instructions overlaid** on a CNC machine, with step-by-step guidance for replacing the spindle.

4. **Decentralized Decisions:** **Cyber-Physical Systems make autonomous decisions** without relying on a central controller.
   - *Example:* A self-driving AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) that **independently reroutes** to avoid a blocked path using onboard LiDAR and edge AI, without human or central command.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Q3(B)(b):**

**Given:** $N_{failures} = 3$, $T_{up} = 4320\ \text{hours}$, $T_{rep} = 18\ \text{hours}$.

**Step 1: MTBF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= \dfrac{4320}{3} = 1440\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: MTTR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \dfrac{18}{3} = 6\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{1440}{1440 + 6} = \dfrac{1440}{1446} = 0.9958 = 99.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Predictive Maintenance Trigger**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{trigger} &= 0.80 \times 1440 = 1152\ \text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** Configure the IIoT dashboard to trigger an **automated service reminder** to the maintenance team when the CNC's cumulative runtime crosses **1152 hours**, allowing proactive bearing/sensor inspection before failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Many students write $OEE = A + P + Q$ (sum) instead of **product**. KTU deducts **1 mark** for this error.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *Quality* (good parts/total parts) with *First Pass Yield* (good parts/parts started). Always read the question carefully.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $MTBF + MTTR$ in the denominator for $A$ — correctly, $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$, not $\dfrac{MTBF}{T_{up} + T_{rep}}$.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In 14-mark theory questions, students **list** the nine pillars without **explaining application to machine tools** — KTU requires both. Always tie every pillar to a **manufacturing/engineering use case**.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Skipping the **diagram** in 7-mark theory answers. Always include at least one **hand-drawn / printed** block diagram (e.g., ISA-95 levels, RAMI 4.0 axes). It is worth **1–2 marks** by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read 2 hours before the exam.**

- ✅ **Industry 4.0** = 4th industrial revolution; fusion of physical, digital, and biological systems.
- ✅ **Coined:** Hannover Messe, **Germany, 2011**; popularized by **Klaus Schwab (WEF, 2016)**.
- ✅ **Four Design Principles:** **Interoperability, Information Transparency, Technical Assistance, Decentralized Decisions**.
- ✅ **Nine Pillars (WEF):** **Big Data, Autonomous Robots, Simulation, System Integration, IIoT, CPS, Cybersecurity, Cloud, Additive Manufacturing**.
- ✅ **Core Enablers:** IoT, CPS, AI/ML, Big Data, Cloud, 5G, Digital Twin.
- ✅ **Reference Architecture:** **RAMI 4.0** (3 axes: Hierarchy, Life Cycle, Layers).
- ✅ **Communication Standards:** **OPC UA, MQTT, 5G URLLC** (latency < 1 ms).
- ✅ **Key Numerical Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (multiplicative, not additive).
- ✅ **KPI Formulas:** $MTBF = T_{up} / N_{failures}$, $MTTR = T_{rep} / N_{failures}$, $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ✅ **World-class benchmark:** $OEE \geq 85\%$.
- ✅ **Smart Factory Layers (ISA-95):** Level 0 (process) → Level 1 (control) → Level 2 (supervisory) → Level 3 (MES) → Level 4 (ERP) → Cloud.
- ✅ **CPS Loop:** Sense → Compute → Communicate → Act (continuous, real-time).
- ✅ **Industry 4.0 vs. Industry 3.0:** Smart + Connected + Autonomous vs. Programmed + Centralized + Reactive.
- ✅ **Digital Twin:** Virtual replica of a physical asset for simulation, monitoring, and optimization.
- ✅ **Predictive Maintenance Trigger:** $0.80 \times MTBF$ (rule of thumb).
- ✅ **Indian Context:** Make in India 2025; Industry 4.0 readiness ~25–30% (NASSCOM 2023); Kerala's Kinfra/KSIDC industrial hubs adopting IIoT.
- ✅ **Real-world references for exam:** Siemens Amberg (Germany), FANUC (Japan), Bosch (Germany), Tata Steel (India).
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always combine **theory + numerical + diagram** in 14-mark answers for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the 9 Pillars — "BASIC-I-CA":**
> **B**ig Data, **A**utonomous Robots, **S**imulation, **I**ntegration, **C**yber-Physical Systems, **I**IoT, **C**ybersecurity, **A**dditive Manufacturing, **(C)loud** Computing.
